AI.result: All three strategies show significant performance gains; safety-first yields ROI 3.91 and 152% better performance than alternatives, with 31% higher investment; system achieves 95.66% operational stability and is presented as readily applicable to industrial environments.
AI.conclusion: Proposes a novel distributional RL CBM framework incorporating aging factors and multiple operational strategies, delivering improved cost efficiency and reliability with demonstrated training performance and practical applicability.

title: Factored Reasoning with Inner Speech and Persistent Memory for Evidence-Grounded Human-Robot Interaction
summary: Dialogue-based human-robot interaction requires robot cognitive assistants to maintain persistent user context, recover from underspecified requests, and ground responses in external evidence, while keeping intermediate decisions verifiable. In this paper we introduce JANUS, a cognitive architecture for assistive robots that models interaction as a partially observable Markov decision process and realizes control as a factored controller with typed interfaces. To this aim, Janus (i) decomposes the overall behavior into specialized modules, related to scope detection, intent recognition, memory, inner speech, query generation, and outer speech, and (ii) exposes explicit policies for information sufficiency, execution readiness, and tool grounding. A dedicated memory agent maintains a bounded recent-history buffer, a compact core memory, and an archival store with semantic retrieval, coupled through controlled consolidation and revision policies. Models inspired by the notion of inner speech in cognitive theories provide a control-oriented internal textual flow that validates parameter completeness and triggers clarification before grounding, while a faithfulness constraint ties robot-to-human claims to an evidence bundle combining working context and retrieved tool outputs. We evaluate JANUS through module-level unit tests in a dietary assistance domain grounded on a knowledge graph, reporting high agreement with curated references and practical latency profiles. These results support factored reasoning as a promising path to scalable, auditable, and evidence-grounded robot assistance over extended interaction horizons.

AI.tldr: JANUS is a cognitive architecture for assistive robots that uses a partially observable Markov decision process and a factored, module-based controller to maintain context, ground responses in evidence, and ensure verifiability. It includes specialized modules for perception and dialogue, a memory system with bounded history and semantic retrieval, an inner-speech mechanism for internal validation, and a faithfulness constraint linking claims to evidence. Evaluated via module-level tests in a dietary-assistance domain with a knowledge graph, showing high agreement to references and practical latency, suggesting scalable, auditable, evidence-grounded robot assistance over extended interactions.
AI.motivation: To enable dialogue-based human-robot interaction with persistent user context, robust recovery from underspecified requests, grounding in external evidence, and verifiability of intermediate decisions during extended interactions.
AI.method: Model interaction as a partially observable Markov decision process and implement a factored controller with typed interfaces. Decompose behavior into modules: scope detection, intent recognition, memory, inner speech, query generation, outer speech. Introduce a memory agent with bounded recent-history, compact core memory, and archival semantic retrieval, coupled with consolidation/revision policies. Use a control-oriented inner speech for parameter validation and clarification triggers. Enforce a faithfulness constraint tying robot claims to evidence bundles (working context + retrieved tool outputs). Evaluate via module-level unit tests in a knowledge-graph-grounded dietary-assistance domain.
AI.result: Demonstrates high agreement with curated references and practical latency profiles for JANUS’s module-level components, supporting factored reasoning as scalable, auditable, and evidence-grounded for long-horizon robot assistance.
AI.conclusion: Factored reasoning that combines modular controls, memory and inner-speech validation with evidence-grounded claims offers a promising path to scalable, auditable robot assistance over extended interactions.

title: Position: Agentic Evolution is the Path to Evolving LLMs
summary: As Large Language Models (LLMs) move from curated training sets into open-ended real-world environments, a fundamental limitation emerges: static training cannot keep pace with continual deployment environment change. Scaling training-time and inference-time compute improves static capability but does not close this train-deploy gap. We argue that addressing this limitation requires a new scaling axis-evolution. Existing deployment-time adaptation methods, whether parametric fine-tuning or heuristic memory accumulation, lack the strategic agency needed to diagnose failures and produce durable improvements. Our position is that agentic evolution represents the inevitable future of LLM adaptation, elevating evolution itself from a fixed pipeline to an autonomous evolver agent. We instantiate this vision in a general framework, A-Evolve, which treats deployment-time improvement as a deliberate, goal-directed optimization process over persistent system state. We further propose the evolution-scaling hypothesis: the capacity for adaptation scales with the compute allocated to evolution, positioning agentic evolution as a scalable path toward sustained, open-ended adaptation in the real world.

AI.tldr: Proposes A-Evolve: a general framework for deployment-time adaptation of LLMs via agentic evolution, introducing an evolution axis and the evolution-scaling hypothesis to enable open-ended, durable adaptation.
AI.motivation: Static training cannot keep pace with continually changing deployment environments; existing deployment-time methods (fine-tuning, memory) lack strategic agency and durability; a scalable, autonomous approach to adaptation is needed.
AI.method: Introduces A-Evolve, treating deployment-time improvement as a deliberate, goal-directed optimization over persistent system state using an evolver agent; posits that adaptation scales with compute allocated to evolution (the evolution-scaling hypothesis).
AI.result: Conceptual theoretical contribution: a framework and hypothesis for autonomous, agentic adaptation; no empirical results reported.
AI.conclusion: Agentic evolution represents a scalable, durable path toward sustained real-world adaptation; evolution should be treated as a tractable separate scaling axis alongside training and inference.

title: TextBFGS: Quasi-Newton Optimization for Discrete Executable Text via Gradient-Operator Retrieval
summary: Optimizing discrete executable text such as prompts and code has recently been framed as a gradient-based process, effectively translating backpropagation concepts to the semantic space. However, existing methods predominantly operate as first-order optimizers akin to Stochastic Gradient Descent, which are suffering from slow convergence and instability because they neglect the semantic curvature of the optimization landscape. To bridge this gap, we introduce TextBFGS, a second-order framework to implement a Quasi-Newton optimization method for discrete text. Unlike traditional memory-based approaches that retrieve similar textual instances, TextBFGS approximates the inverse Hessian matrix by retrieving Gradient-Operators from the memory of pre-learned successful trajectories. Specifically, given a textual gradient feedback, TextBFGS identifies historical correction patterns from the optimization knowledge base and tries to apply these abstract operators to the current variable. This mechanism enables a One-Pass Update, combining feedback generation and second-order correction into a single inference step. Empirical evaluations on code optimization across diverse domains (e.g., HumanEval, MBPP) demonstrate that TextBFGS significantly outperforms first-order baselines. It achieves superior pass rates with fewer model calls and exhibits strong cross-task transferability, thus establishes a mathematically grounded paradigm for efficient, memory-aware text optimization.

AI.tldr: A second-order, memory-augmented optimization method (TextBFGS) for discrete text like prompts and code, using a quasi-Newton style inverse-Hessian approximation built from gradient-operator memories to enable one-pass updates, outperforming first-order baselines on code-generation tasks.
AI.motivation: First-order gradient-based methods for discrete text optimization suffer from slow convergence and instability due to neglecting semantic curvature; a second-order, memory-aware approach could capture curvature and improve efficiency and stability.
AI.method: TextBFGS adopts a quasi-Newton framework. It does not store concrete past texts; instead it retrieves historical gradient-correction operators from a memory of successful trajectories. Given a current gradient, it selects and applies abstract gradient-operators to approximate the inverse Hessian, enabling a one-pass update that combines feedback generation and second-order correction in a single inference step.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on code optimization benchmarks (e.g., HumanEval, MBPP) shows TextBFGS achieves higher pass rates with fewer model calls than first-order baselines and exhibits cross-task transferability.
AI.conclusion: Provides a mathematically grounded paradigm for efficient, memory-aware text optimization in discrete domains, with promising transferability across tasks and potential applicability beyond code prompts.

title: Toward Reliable Sim-to-Real Predictability for MoE-based Robust Quadrupedal Locomotion
summary: Reinforcement learning has shown strong promise for quadrupedal agile locomotion, even with proprioception-only sensing. In practice, however, sim-to-real gap and reward overfitting in complex terrains can produce policies that fail to transfer, while physical validation remains risky and inefficient. To address these challenges, we introduce a unified framework encompassing a Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) locomotion policy for robust multi-terrain representation with RoboGauge, a predictive assessment suite that quantifies sim-to-real transferability. The MoE policy employs a gated set of specialist experts to decompose latent terrain and command modeling, achieving superior deployment robustness and generalization via proprioception alone. RoboGauge further provides multi-dimensional proprioception-based metrics via sim-to-sim tests over terrains, difficulty levels, and domain randomizations, enabling reliable MoE policy selection without extensive physical trials. Experiments on a Unitree Go2 demonstrate robust locomotion on unseen challenging terrains, including snow, sand, stairs, slopes, and 30 cm obstacles. In dedicated high-speed tests, the robot reaches 4 m/s and exhibits an emergent narrow-width gait associated with improved stability at high velocity.

AI.tldr: Mixture-of-Experts locomotion with RoboGauge improves sim-to-real transfer and robustness for quadruped locomotion across unseen terrains using proprioception alone.
AI.motivation: Address the sim-to-real gap, reward overfitting, and risky/inefficient physical validation in complex terrains; enable robust, terrain-diverse locomotion with minimal sensing.
AI.method: Introduce a gated Mixture-of-Experts policy to decompose latent terrain and command modeling; integrate RoboGauge, a multi-dimensional proprioception-based sim-to-sim assessment suite (terrain variety, difficulty levels, domain randomizations) to predict transferability and guide robust MoE policy selection without extensive physical trials.
AI.result: Unitree Go2 experiments show robust locomotion on unseen terrains (snow, sand, stairs, slopes, 30 cm obstacles); high-speed tests reach 4 m/s with an emergent narrow-width gait that enhances stability at high velocity.
AI.conclusion: The unified MoE+RoboGauge framework yields improved robustness and generalization for proprioception-only locomotion across diverse terrains, with RoboGauge enabling reliable policy selection and reducing reliance on physical trials.

title: POET: Protocol Optimization via Eligibility Tuning
summary: Eligibility criteria (EC) are essential for clinical trial design, yet drafting them remains a time-intensive and cognitively demanding task for clinicians. Existing automated approaches often fall at two extremes either requiring highly structured inputs, such as predefined entities to generate specific criteria, or relying on end-to-end systems that produce full eligibility criteria from minimal input such as trial descriptions limiting their practical utility. In this work, we propose a guided generation framework that introduces interpretable semantic axes, such as Demographics, Laboratory Parameters, and Behavioral Factors, to steer EC generation. These axes, derived using large language models, offer a middle ground between specificity and usability, enabling clinicians to guide generation without specifying exact entities. In addition, we present a reusable rubric-based evaluation framework that assesses generated criteria along clinically meaningful dimensions. Our results show that our guided generation approach consistently outperforms unguided generation in both automatic, rubric-based and clinician evaluations, offering a practical and interpretable solution for AI-assisted trial design.

AI.tldr: A guided generation framework for eligibility criteria (EC) using interpretable semantic axes (Demographics, Laboratory Parameters, Behavioral Factors) derived from LLMs, plus a rubric-based evaluation framework. It steers EC generation without fixed entities and outperforms unguided generation across automatic, rubric-based, and clinician assessments.
AI.motivation: Drafting EC is time-consuming and cognitively demanding. Existing automated approaches are either highly structured (requiring predefined entities) or end-to-end with minimal input, limiting practicality. A middle-ground approach with interpretable axes aims to balance specificity and usability for clinicians.
AI.method: Introduce guided generation with semantic axes (Demographics, Laboratory Parameters, Behavioral Factors) derived via large language models. Clinicians steer generation without specifying exact entities. Develop a reusable rubric-based evaluation framework to assess generated criteria along clinically meaningful dimensions. Compare guided generation against unguided generation using automatic metrics, rubric-based scores, and clinician evaluations.
AI.result: Guided generation consistently outperforms unguided generation across automatic, rubric-based, and clinician assessments.
AI.conclusion: The framework offers a practical and interpretable solution for AI-assisted trial design by providing steerable EC generation through interpretable axes and a standardized rubric-based evaluation framework.

title: SCPL: Enhancing Neural Network Training Throughput with Decoupled Local Losses and Model Parallelism
summary: Adopting large-scale AI models in enterprise information systems is often hindered by high training costs and long development cycles, posing a significant managerial challenge. The standard end-to-end backpropagation (BP) algorithm is a primary driver of modern AI, but it is also the source of inefficiency in training deep networks. This paper introduces a new training methodology, Supervised Contrastive Parallel Learning (SCPL), that addresses this issue by decoupling BP and transforming a long gradient flow into multiple short ones. This design enables the simultaneous computation of parameter gradients in different layers, achieving superior model parallelism and enhancing training throughput. Detailed experiments are presented to demonstrate the efficiency and effectiveness of our model compared to BP, Early Exit, GPipe, and Associated Learning (AL), a state-of-the-art method for decoupling backpropagation. By mitigating a fundamental performance bottleneck, SCPL provides a practical pathway for organizations to develop and deploy advanced information systems more cost-effectively and with greater agility. The experimental code is released for reproducibility. https://github.com/minyaho/scpl/

AI.tldr: SCPL decouples backpropagation in large-scale models by transforming long gradient flows into multiple short ones, enabling simultaneous gradient computation across layers for improved model parallelism and training throughput; it outperforms BP, Early Exit, GPipe, and AL in experiments, with code released at GitHub.
AI.motivation: To reduce training costs and development cycles for large enterprise AI systems by addressing the inefficiencies of end-to-end backpropagation. The bottleneck is the sequential gradient flow across many layers that hampers throughput and scalability.
AI.method: Introduce Supervised Contrastive Parallel Learning (SCPL) that decouples backpropagation into shorter gradient paths and enables parallel gradient computation across layers. Use supervised contrastive learning to guide representations. Compare against standard BP, Early Exit, GPipe, and Associated Learning (AL). Provide experimental results and release code for reproducibility.
AI.result: SCPL yields superior model parallelism and higher training throughput compared to BP, Early Exit, GPipe, and AL, demonstrating efficiency and effectiveness. Experimental code is released for reproducibility at the provided GitHub URL.
AI.conclusion: SCPL offers a practical, scalable training paradigm to make large-scale enterprise AI development more cost-effective and agile by mitigating backpropagation bottlenecks, with empirical evidence and open-source code supporting its adoption.

title: Learning to Accelerate Vision-Language-Action Models through Adaptive Visual Token Caching
summary: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have demonstrated remarkable generalization capabilities in robotic manipulation tasks, yet their substantial computational overhead remains a critical obstacle to real-world deployment. Improving inference efficiency is therefore essential for practical robotic applications. Existing acceleration methods often rely on heuristic or static strategies--such as rule-based token caching or pruning--that are decoupled from task objectives and fail to adapt to dynamic scene changes. In this work, we reformulate inference acceleration as a learnable policy optimization problem and propose a novel framework that integrates a dynamic, task-aware decision-making process directly into the VLA model. At its core are two lightweight, cooperative modules: a Cached Token Selector, which determines which tokens should be reused, and a Cache Ratio Predictor, which controls how many tokens to reuse. Training these modules is non-trivial due to their discrete decisions. We address this by adopting a differentiable relaxation that allows gradient-based end-to-end optimization. Extensive experiments on the LIBERO and SIMPLER benchmarks, as well as real-robot evaluations, show that our method achieves a 1.76x wall-clock inference speedup while simultaneously improving the average success rate by 1.9 percentage points (from 75.0% to 76.9%) on LIBERO and by 5.0 percentage points on real-world tasks, significantly outperforming existing baselines. This work highlights the potential of learning task-aware computational allocation policies, paving the way for VLA models that are both powerful and efficient.

AI.tldr: A learnable, task-aware inference acceleration framework for Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models, introducing two lightweight modules that dynamically reuse tokens to speed up inference while improving task success rates.
AI.motivation: Inference efficiency is critical for real-world robotic deployment of VLA models. Existing acceleration methods are heuristic, static, and fail to adapt to changing scenes, requiring a task-aware, adaptive solution.
AI.method: Introduce two cooperative modules: (1) Cached Token Selector to choose which tokens to reuse, (2) Cache Ratio Predictor to determine how many tokens to reuse. Use differentiable relaxation to enable gradient-based end-to-end training and integrate these modules into the VLA pipeline for dynamic, task-aware token caching during inference.
AI.result: Achieves 1.76x wall-clock inference speedup. Improves average success rate from 75.0% to 76.9% on LIBERO (1.9 percentage point gain) and yields a 5.0 percentage point improvement on real-world tasks; benchmarks include LIBERO, SIMPLER, and real-robot evaluations, outperforming existing baselines.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates the viability of learning task-aware computational allocation policies for VLA models, enabling systems that are both computationally efficient and more effective in manipulation tasks.

title: KEPO: Knowledge-Enhanced Preference Optimization for Reinforcement Learning with Reasoning
summary: Reinforcement learning (RL) has emerged as a promising paradigm for inducing explicit reasoning behaviors in large language and vision-language models. However, reasoning-oriented RL post-training remains fundamentally challenging due to sparse trajectory-level rewards, leading to ambiguous credit assignment and severe exploration failures that can trap the policy in a ``learning cliff.'' Recent on-policy distillation methods introduce dense teacher supervision to stabilize optimization, but apply it uniformly across all generated trajectories. We argue that such uniform distillation is ill-suited for reasoning-intensive tasks, as low-quality on-policy trajectories often originate from early logical errors, and distillation under flawed contexts injects noisy and misaligned gradients. To address these challenges, we propose Knowledge-Enhanced Preference Optimization (KEPO), a unified post-training framework that integrates: (i) a quality-gated on-policy distillation objective that selectively applies dense teacher guidance only to high-quality trajectories, and (ii) a knowledge-enhanced exploration strategy that leverages hints learned from a teacher model to rejectively sample reward-positive on-policy trajectories for RL, thereby mitigating exploration collapse. Evaluated on a challenging medical visual question answering benchmark under single-source generalization, KEPO demonstrates improved training stability, more coherent reasoning behaviors, and superior out-of-distribution performance over reinforcement learning and on-policy distillation baselines.

AI.tldr: KEPO offers a unified RL post-training framework that uses quality-gated distillation and knowledge-guided exploration to stabilize reasoning in LLM/VLMs; it improves training stability and OOD performance on medical VQA versus RL and on-policy distillation baselines.
AI.motivation: Address the core challenges of reasoning-oriented RL post-training: sparse trajectory-level rewards, ambiguous credit assignment, and exploration failures that can trap the policy in a learning cliff; standard uniform dense supervision can worsen performance if applied to low-quality, flawed trajectories.
AI.method: 1) quality-gated on-policy distillation: apply dense teacher guidance only to high-quality trajectories; 2) knowledge-enhanced exploration: use hints learned from a teacher model to rejectively sample reward-positive on-policy trajectories, mitigating exploration collapse.
AI.result: On a challenging medical visual question answering benchmark with single-source generalization, KEPO improves training stability, yields more coherent reasoning, and achieves superior out-of-distribution performance compared with reinforcement learning and on-policy distillation baselines.
AI.conclusion: KEPO provides a robust post-training framework for reasoning-focused RL in vision-language models, combining selective supervision and teacher-guided exploration to address credit assignment and exploration issues; results suggest strong potential but require broader validation and consideration of computational cost and biases from teacher guidance.

title: The Impact of Machine Learning Uncertainty on the Robustness of Counterfactual Explanations
summary: Counterfactual explanations are widely used to interpret machine learning predictions by identifying minimal changes to input features that would alter a model's decision. However, most existing counterfactual methods have not been tested when model and data uncertainty change, resulting in explanations that may be unstable or invalid under real-world variability. In this work, we investigate the robustness of common combinations of machine learning models and counterfactual generation algorithms in the presence of both aleatoric and epistemic uncertainty. Through experiments on synthetic and real-world tabular datasets, we show that counterfactual explanations are highly sensitive to model uncertainty. In particular, we find that even small reductions in model accuracy - caused by increased noise or limited data - can lead to large variations in the generated counterfactuals on average and on individual instances. These findings underscore the need for uncertainty-aware explanation methods in domains such as finance and the social sciences.

AI.tldr: Counterfactual explanations are highly sensitive to model uncertainty; even small reductions in model accuracy due to noise or data scarcity can cause large changes in the generated counterfactuals, indicating instability of current methods under uncertainty.
AI.motivation: To address the gap that counterfactual explanations are typically evaluated assuming fixed models, this work tests robustness of common model–counterfactual pipelines under both aleatoric and epistemic uncertainty on synthetic and real-world tabular data.
AI.method: Empirical evaluation across multiple ML models and counterfactual generation algorithms on synthetic and real-world tabular datasets, while varying levels of uncertainty (noise and limited data) to observe changes in counterfactuals.
AI.result: Counterfactuals exhibit high sensitivity to model uncertainty; modest accuracy degradation leads to substantial variations in generated counterfactuals, both on average and for individual instances.
AI.conclusion: There is a need for uncertainty-aware counterfactual explanation methods. The findings have implications for high-stakes domains like finance and the social sciences and motivate future work on robust or uncertainty-calibrated counterfactual generation.

title: USS-Nav: Unified Spatio-Semantic Scene Graph for Lightweight UAV Zero-Shot Object Navigation
summary: Zero-Shot Object Navigation in unknown environments poses significant challenges for Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs) due to the conflict between high-level semantic reasoning requirements and limited onboard computational resources. To address this, we present USS-Nav, a lightweight framework that incrementally constructs a Unified Spatio-Semantic scene graph and enables efficient Large Language Model (LLM)-augmented Zero-Shot Object Navigation in unknown environments. Specifically, we introduce an incremental Spatial Connectivity Graph generation method utilizing polyhedral expansion to capture global geometric topology, which is dynamically partitioned into semantic regions via graph clustering. Concurrently, open-vocabulary object semantics are instantiated and anchored to this topology to form a hierarchical environmental representation. Leveraging this hierarchical structure, we present a coarse-to-fine exploration strategy: LLM grounded in the scene graph's semantics to determine global target regions, while a local planner optimizes frontier coverage based on information gain. Experimental results demonstrate that our framework outperforms state-of-the-art methods in terms of computational efficiency and real-time update frequency (15 Hz) on a resource-constrained platform. Furthermore, ablation studies confirm the effectiveness of our framework, showing substantial improvements in Success weighted by Path Length (SPL). The source code will be made publicly available to foster further research.

title: RobustDebias: Debiasing Language Models using Distributionally Robust Optimization
summary: Pretrained language models have been shown to exhibit biases and social stereotypes. Prior work on debiasing these models has largely focused on modifying embedding spaces during pretraining, which is not scalable for large models. Fine-tuning pretrained models on task-specific datasets can both degrade model performance and amplify biases present in the fine-tuning data. We address bias amplification during fine-tuning rather than costly pretraining, focusing on BERT models due to their widespread use in language understanding tasks. While Empirical Risk Minimization effectively optimizes downstream performance, it often amplifies social biases during fine-tuning. To counter this, we propose \textit{RobustDebias}, a novel mechanism which adapts Distributionally Robust Optimization (DRO) to debias language models during fine-tuning. Our approach debiases models across multiple demographics during MLM fine-tuning and generalizes to any dataset or task. Extensive experiments on various language models show significant bias mitigation with minimal performance impact.

AI.tldr: Proposes RobustDebias, a DRO-based method to mitigate bias during MLM fine-tuning of BERT, achieving substantial bias reduction with little to no performance loss.
AI.motivation: Bias amplification during fine-tuning is a scalable problem not addressed by pretraining-only debiasing; needs a method that works across datasets and tasks for large models.
AI.method: Adapts Distributionally Robust Optimization to debias MLM fine-tuning, enforcing fairness across multiple demographic groups and generalizing to any dataset/task.
AI.result: Experiments across language models show significant bias mitigation with minimal impact on accuracy or other performance metrics.
AI.conclusion: RobustDebias offers an effective, scalable debiasing approach for fine-tuning pretrained language models, especially BERT, with broad applicability.

title: SPGCL: Effective Graph Contrastive Learning via SVD-Guided Structural Perturbation
summary: Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) can be highly sensitive to structural noise, including spurious or missing edges caused by adversarial attacks or non-adversarial imperfections. Existing graph contrastive learning methods typically rely on either random perturbations (e.g., edge dropping) to generate diverse views or purely spectral augmentations (e.g., SVD) to preserve global structural priors. However, random perturbations are structure-agnostic and may remove critical edges, while SVD-based views often become dense and lack sufficient diversity. To bridge this gap, we propose SPGCL, a robust graph contrastive learning framework via SVD-guided structural perturbation. SPGCL couples lightweight stochastic edge removal with an SVD-guided refinement step that can recover mistakenly removed informative edges and introduce semantically meaningful missing links while avoiding graph densification through sparse top-ranked edge selection and merging. By balancing edge removal and recovery rates, SPGCL explicitly controls structural discrepancy between views so that contrastive signals reflect semantic structural differences rather than edge-count gaps. We further incorporate a contrastive fusion module regularized by a global similarity constraint to better align the two views. Extensive experiments on ten benchmark datasets demonstrate that SPGCL consistently improves robustness and accuracy of base GNNs, outperforming state-of-the-art graph contrastive learning and structure learning methods.

AI.tldr: SPGCL combines SVD-guided structural perturbation with stochastic edge removal to create robust, semantically diverse graph views for contrastive learning, plus a fusion module with global similarity regularization; it yields improved robustness and accuracy across 10 datasets.
AI.motivation: Graph data are fragile to structural noise (attacks or imperfections). Existing augmentations either rely on random perturbations (which can remove important edges) or spectral methods (which produce dense, less diverse views). A method that preserves semantics while enabling diverse views and robustness is needed.
AI.method: Apply lightweight stochastic edge removal paired with an SVD-guided refinement to recover informative edges and introduce meaningful missing links without graph densification. Use sparse top-ranked edge selection and merging to maintain sparsity. Include a contrastive fusion module regularized by a global similarity constraint to align two views.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on ten benchmark datasets shows SPGCL consistently improves robustness and accuracy of base GNNs, outperforming state-of-the-art graph contrastive learning and structure learning methods.
AI.conclusion: SPGCL enables explicit control of structural discrepancy between contrastive views, ensuring that learned representations reflect semantic structure rather than edge-count differences, and offers a robust, scalable framework for graph contrastive learning.

title: SA-VLA: Spatially-Aware Flow-Matching for Vision-Language-Action Reinforcement Learning
summary: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models exhibit strong generalization in robotic manipulation, yet reinforcement learning (RL) fine-tuning often degrades robustness under spatial distribution shifts. For flow-matching VLA policies, this degradation is closely associated with the erosion of spatial inductive bias during RL adaptation, as sparse rewards and spatially agnostic exploration increasingly favor short-horizon visual cues. To address this issue, we propose \textbf{SA-VLA}, a spatially-aware RL adaptation framework that preserves spatial grounding during policy optimization by aligning representation learning, reward design, and exploration with task geometry. SA-VLA fuses implicit spatial representations with visual tokens, provides dense rewards that reflect geometric progress, and employs \textbf{SCAN}, a spatially-conditioned annealed exploration strategy tailored to flow-matching dynamics. Across challenging multi-object and cluttered manipulation benchmarks, SA-VLA enables stable RL fine-tuning and improves zero-shot spatial generalization, yielding more robust and transferable behaviors. Code and project page are available at https://xupan.top/Projects/savla.

AI.tldr: SA-VLA: a spatially-aware RL adaptation framework for VLA policies that preserves spatial grounding during fine-tuning, improving robustness to spatial distribution shifts and zero-shot generalization via geometry-aligned representation learning, dense rewards, and SCAN exploration.
AI.motivation: RL fine-tuning of flow-matching VLA policies erodes spatial inductive bias due to sparse rewards and exploration that favors short-horizon visual cues, hindering robustness under spatial distribution shifts.
AI.method: Integrate implicit spatial representations with visual tokens; design dense, geometry-reflecting rewards; employ SCAN, a spatially-conditioned annealed exploration strategy tailored to flow-matching dynamics, aligning representation learning, reward design, and exploration with task geometry.
AI.result: On challenging multi-object and cluttered manipulation benchmarks, SA-VLA achieves stable RL fine-tuning and improved zero-shot spatial generalization, yielding more robust and transferable behaviors.
AI.conclusion: Spatially-aware RL adaptation (SA-VLA) effectively preserves spatial grounding during policy optimization, enhancing robustness and transferability of VLA policies in robotic manipulation.

title: PolarMem: A Training-Free Polarized Latent Graph Memory for Verifiable Multimodal Agents
summary: As multimodal agents evolve from passive observers to long-horizon decision-makers, they require memory systems that provide not just information availability but logical verifiability. A fundamental limitation of current architectures is the epistemic asymmetry inherent in probabilistic vision-language models and dense associative memories: they conflate semantic affinity with factual existence and structurally fail to encode negative constraints. To this end, we introduce PolarMem, a training-free Polarized Latent Graph Memory designed to ground agent reasoning in verifiable evidence. PolarMem transforms fuzzy perceptual likelihoods into discrete logical constraints through non-parametric distributional partitioning. Furthermore, it employs a polarized graph topology with orthogonal inhibitory connections to explicitly store verified negation as a primary cognitive state. At inference time, we enforce a logic-dominant retrieval paradigm, suppressing hallucinatory patterns that violate negative constraints. Extensive evaluation across eight frozen Vision--Language Models and six benchmarks demonstrates that PolarMem functions as a robust cognitive system, establishing a foundation for verifiable multimodal agents. Our code is available at https://github.com/czs-ict/PolarMem.

AI.tldr: PolarMem is a training-free memory module that grounds multimodal reasoning in verifiable evidence by converting fuzzy perceptual likelihoods into discrete logical constraints via non-parametric partitioning and by storing verified negation in a polarized inhibitory graph for logic-dominant retrieval, improving verifiability across multiple frozen VLMs and benchmarks; code released.
AI.motivation: Current probabilistic vision-language models and dense associative memories suffer from epistemic asymmetry: they conflate semantic affinity with factual existence and cannot encode negative constraints, hindering verifiable long-horizon reasoning in multimodal agents.
AI.method: 1) Transform probabilistic percepts into discrete logical constraints using non-parametric distributional partitioning. 2) Use a polarized graph topology with orthogonal inhibitory connections to store verified negation as a primary cognitive state. 3) At inference, apply a logic-dominant retrieval that suppresses patterns violating negative constraints. 4) Do not require training (training-free) and operate with frozen vision-language models.
AI.result: Extensive evaluation across eight frozen Vision–Language Models and six benchmarks demonstrates that PolarMem functions as a robust cognitive system, improving verifiability and reducing hallucinations by grounding reasoning in verifiable evidence.
AI.conclusion: PolarMem provides a foundation for verifiable multimodal agents by coupling memory-grounded inference with a logic-centric structure that explicitly encodes negation, enabling reliable retrieval with frozen LMs and non-parametric grounding; code is publicly available.

title: Modality as Heterogeneity: Node Splitting and Graph Rewiring for Multimodal Graph Learning
summary: Multimodal graphs are gaining increasing attention due to their rich representational power and wide applicability, yet they introduce substantial challenges arising from severe modality confusion. To address this issue, we propose NSG (Node Splitting Graph)-MoE, a multimodal graph learning framework that integrates a node-splitting and graph-rewiring mechanism with a structured Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architecture. It explicitly decomposes each node into modality-specific components and assigns relation-aware experts to process heterogeneous message flows, thereby preserving structural information and multimodal semantics while mitigating the undesirable mixing effects commonly observed in general-purpose GNNs. Extensive experiments on three multimodal benchmarks demonstrate that NSG-MoE consistently surpasses strong baselines. Despite incorporating MoE -- which is typically computationally heavy -- our method achieves competitive training efficiency. Beyond empirical results, we provide a spectral analysis revealing that NSG performs adaptive filtering over modality-specific subspaces, thus explaining its disentangling behavior. Furthermore, an information-theoretic analysis shows that the architectural constraints imposed by NSG reduces mutual information between data and parameters and improving generalization capability.

AI.tldr: NSG-MoE introduces a Node Splitting Graph and a structured Mixture-of-Experts to disentangle multimodal information in graphs, achieving state-of-the-art results on three benchmarks with competitive efficiency, and providing spectral and information-theoretic justifications for its behavior.
AI.motivation: Multimodal graphs suffer severe modality confusion, where heterogeneous modalities get mixed in GNNs, degrading representation quality. There is a need to preserve modality-specific semantics and structural information while mitigating unwanted mixing.
AI.method: Each node is decomposed into modality-specific components via node-splitting and graph-rewiring. A structured MoE with relation-aware experts handles heterogeneous message flows, enabling modality-aware processing and adaptive subspace filtering. The approach includes spectral analysis to explain disentangling and an information-theoretic analysis to link architectural constraints with generalization.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on three multimodal benchmarks shows NSG-MoE consistently surpasses strong baselines. Despite MoE’s typical computational demands, the method maintains competitive training efficiency.
AI.conclusion: NSG-MoE effectively disentangles multimodal signals while preserving structure and semantics, supported by spectral evidence of adaptive subspace filtering and an information-theoretic rationale for improved generalization.

title: Physics-informed Diffusion Mamba Transformer for Real-world Driving
summary: Autonomous driving systems demand trajectory planners that not only model the inherent uncertainty of future motions but also respect complex temporal dependencies and underlying physical laws. While diffusion-based generative models excel at capturing multi-modal distributions, they often fail to incorporate long-term sequential contexts and domain-specific physical priors. In this work, we bridge these gaps with two key innovations. First, we introduce a Diffusion Mamba Transformer architecture that embeds mamba and attention into the diffusion process, enabling more effective aggregation of sequential input contexts from sensor streams and past motion histories. Second, we design a Port-Hamiltonian Neural Network module that seamlessly integrates energy-based physical constraints into the diffusion model, thereby enhancing trajectory predictions with both consistency and interpretability. Extensive evaluations on standard autonomous driving benchmarks demonstrate that our unified framework significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines in predictive accuracy, physical plausibility, and robustness, thereby advancing safe and reliable motion planning.

AI.tldr: A diffusion-based multi-modal trajectory model enhanced with long-range context and physics priors via a Diffusion Mamba Transformer and a Port-Hamiltonian Neural Network, achieving improved accuracy, plausibility, and robustness for autonomous driving trajectories.
AI.motivation: Autonomous driving requires capturing multi-modal future motions while respecting temporal dependencies and physical laws. Diffusion models alone struggle with long-term context and domain priors.
AI.method: Introduce a Diffusion Mamba Transformer that integrates Mamba and attention mechanisms into the diffusion process to better aggregate sequential sensor/context data; couple this with a Port-Hamiltonian Neural Network module that enforces energy-based physical constraints within the diffusion model.
AI.result: Extensive evaluations on standard autonomous driving benchmarks show substantial gains in predictive accuracy, physical plausibility, and robustness compared with state-of-the-art baselines.
AI.conclusion: The unified framework advances safe and reliable motion planning by combining probabilistic, context-rich forecasting with interpretable physical priors.

title: Do Latent-CoT Models Think Step-by-Step? A Mechanistic Study on Sequential Reasoning Tasks
summary: Latent Chain-of-Thought (Latent-CoT) aims to enable step-by-step computation without emitting long rationales, yet its mechanisms remain unclear. We study CODI, a continuous-thought teacher-student distillation model, on strictly sequential polynomial-iteration tasks. Using logit-lens decoding, linear probes, attention analysis, and activation patching, we localize intermediate-state representations and trace their routing to the final readout. On two- and three-hop tasks, CODI forms the full set of bridge states that become decodable across latent-thought positions, while the final input follows a separate near-direct route; predictions arise via late fusion at the end-of-thought boundary. For longer hop lengths, CODI does not reliably execute a full latent rollout, instead exhibiting a partial latent reasoning path that concentrates on late intermediates and fuses them with the last input at the answer readout position. Ablations show that this partial pathway can collapse under regime shifts, including harder optimization. Overall, we delineate when CODI-style latent-CoT yields faithful iterative computation versus compressed or shortcut strategies, and highlight challenges in designing robust latent-CoT objectives for sequential reasoning.

AI.tldr: CODI shows that latent-CoT in sequential polynomial-iteration tasks can produce a full chain of decodable intermediate states for short chains with late fusion at the end, but for longer chains it relies on a partial latent path that blends late intermediates with the final input; this reveals when latent-CoT is faithful versus shortcut and exposes robustness challenges.
AI.motivation: To clarify the mechanisms of latent-CoT and understand how a continuous-thought teacher-student distillation (CODI) performs sequential reasoning, identifying when the model actually computes intermediate steps versus using compressed shortcuts.
AI.method: Continuous-thought teacher-student distillation (CODI); logit-lens decoding; linear probes; attention analysis; activation patching; analysis on strictly sequential polynomial-iteration tasks (2-3 hops) with ablations and regime-shift experiments for longer hops.
AI.result: On 2-3 hop tasks, CODI forms the full bridge-state set decodable across latent positions; the final input travels a separate near-direct route; predictions arise via late fusion at the end-of-thought. With longer hops, CODI does not execute a full latent rollout, instead a partial latent path concentrates on late intermediates and fuses with the last input at the answer readout. Ablations show this partial pathway can collapse under regime shifts (harder optimization).
AI.conclusion: Delineate when CODI-style latent-CoT yields faithful iterative computation versus compressed/shortcut strategies, and highlight challenges in designing robust latent-CoT objectives for sequential reasoning.

title: Generative AI-enhanced Probabilistic Multi-Fidelity Surrogate Modeling Via Transfer Learning
summary: The performance of machine learning surrogates is critically dependent on data quality and quantity. This presents a major challenge, as high-fidelity (HF) data is often scarce and computationally expensive to acquire, while low-fidelity (LF) data is abundant but less accurate. To address this data scarcity problem, we develop a probabilistic multi-fidelity surrogate framework based on generative transfer learning. We employ a normalizing flow (NF) generative model as the backbone, which is trained in two phases: (i) the NF is first pretrained on a large LF dataset to learn a probabilistic forward model; (ii) the pretrained model is then fine-tuned on a small HF dataset, allowing it to correct for LF-HF discrepancies via knowledge transfer. To relax the dimension-preserving constraint of standard bijective NFs, we integrate surjective (dimension-reducing) layers with standard coupling blocks. This architecture enables learned dimension reduction while preserving the ability to train with exact likelihoods. The resulting surrogate provides fast probabilistic predictions with quantified uncertainty and significantly outperforms LF-only baselines while using fewer HF evaluations. We validate the approach on a reinforced concrete slab benchmark, combining many coarse-mesh (LF) simulations with a limited set of fine-mesh (HF) simulations. The proposed model achieves probabilistic predictions with HF accuracy, demonstrating a practical path toward data-efficient, generative AI-driven surrogates for complex engineering systems.

AI.tldr: A probabilistic multi-fidelity surrogate using a normalizing-flow backbone with two-phase LF pretraining and HF fine-tuning, augmented with dimension-reducing surjective layers, yielding data-efficient, uncertainty-aware HF predictions validated on a coarse vs fine-mesh reinforced concrete slab benchmark.
AI.motivation: High-fidelity data are scarce and expensive to obtain, while low-fidelity data are abundant but inaccurate. There is a need for data-efficient surrogates that deliver accurate predictions with quantified uncertainty for complex engineering systems.
AI.method: A normalizing-flow generative model pretrained on large LF data to learn a probabilistic forward model, then fine-tuned on limited HF data to correct LF-HF discrepancies. The architecture adds surjective (dimension-reducing) layers within standard coupling blocks to enable learned dimension reduction while preserving exact likelihood training.
AI.result: The surrogate achieves probabilistic predictions with HF accuracy, outperforms LF-only baselines, and requires fewer HF evaluations, validated on a reinforced concrete slab benchmark combining many coarse-mesh (LF) with a limited set of fine-mesh (HF) simulations.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates a practical, data-efficient path toward generative AI–driven surrogates for complex engineering systems by integrating transfer learning and dimension-reducing normalizing flows for uncertainty-aware predictions.

title: SyNeT: Synthetic Negatives for Traversability Learning
summary: Reliable traversability estimation is crucial for autonomous robots to navigate complex outdoor environments safely. Existing self-supervised learning frameworks primarily rely on positive and unlabeled data; however, the lack of explicit negative data remains a critical limitation, hindering the model's ability to accurately identify diverse non-traversable regions. To address this issue, we introduce a method to explicitly construct synthetic negatives, representing plausible but non-traversable, and integrate them into vision-based traversability learning. Our approach is formulated as a training strategy that can be seamlessly integrated into both Positive-Unlabeled (PU) and Positive-Negative (PN) frameworks without modifying inference architectures. Complementing standard pixel-wise metrics, we introduce an object-centric FPR evaluation approach that analyzes predictions in regions where synthetic negatives are inserted. This evaluation provides an indirect measure of the model's ability to consistently identify non-traversable regions without additional manual labeling. Extensive experiments on both public and self-collected datasets demonstrate that our approach significantly enhances robustness and generalization across diverse environments. The source code and demonstration videos will be publicly available.

AI.tldr: Introduces explicit synthetic negatives for vision-based traversability learning within PU/PN frameworks, improving robustness and generalization without changing inference, plus an object-centric FPR evaluation for non-traversable regions.
AI.motivation: Self-supervised traversability learning suffers from the absence of explicit negative samples, limiting the model's ability to identify diverse non-traversable areas.
AI.method: Generate synthetic negative samples that are plausible yet non-traversable and integrate them into training under both PU and PN setups without altering the inference architecture. Propose an object-centric FPR evaluation by analyzing predictions in regions where negatives are inserted.
AI.result: Empirical results on public and self-collected datasets show enhanced robustness and generalization across diverse outdoor environments, indicating the approach effectively teaches the model to recognize non-traversable regions.
AI.conclusion: Explicitly constructed synthetic negatives can bolster self-supervised traversability learning, with a simple training strategy compatible with PU/PN frameworks and a novel evaluation metric; code and demonstrations are to be released.

title: Cross-Modal Memory Compression for Efficient Multi-Agent Debate
summary: Multi-agent debate can improve reasoning quality and reduce hallucinations, but it incurs rapidly growing context as debate rounds and agent count increase. Retaining full textual histories leads to token usage that can exceed context limits and often requires repeated summarization, adding overhead and compounding information loss. We introduce DebateOCR, a cross-modal compression framework that replaces long textual debate traces with compact image representations, which are then consumed through a dedicated vision encoder to condition subsequent rounds. This design compresses histories that commonly span tens to hundreds of thousands of tokens, cutting input tokens by more than 92% and yielding substantially lower compute cost and faster inference across multiple benchmarks. We further provide a theoretical perspective showing that diversity across agents supports recovery of omitted information: although any single compressed history may discard details, aggregating multiple agents' compressed views allows the collective representation to approach the information bottleneck with exponentially high probability.

AI.tldr: DebateOCR compresses long textual debate histories into compact image representations consumed by a vision encoder, achieving >92% token reduction and faster inference, with theory showing multi-agent diversity enables recovery of omitted information.
AI.motivation: Multi-agent debates suffer from rapid token growth and context limits; need scalable history representations that preserve essential information.
AI.method: Convert textual debate traces into cross-modal image representations; use a dedicated vision encoder to condition subsequent rounds; aggregate multiple agents' compressed views to approximate the information bottleneck.
AI.result: Token usage reduced by over 92%; lower compute cost and faster inference across benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Cross-modal compression via DebateOCR enables scalable multi-agent debate with theoretical justification that diverse agent views allow recovery of omitted details, mitigating information loss.

title: Dimensional Peeking for Low-Variance Gradients in Zeroth-Order Discrete Optimization via Simulation
summary: Gradient-based optimization methods are commonly used to identify local optima in high-dimensional spaces. When derivatives cannot be evaluated directly, stochastic estimators can provide approximate gradients. However, these estimators' perturbation-based sampling of the objective function introduces variance that can lead to slow convergence. In this paper, we present dimensional peeking, a variance reduction method for gradient estimation in discrete optimization via simulation. By lifting the sampling granularity from scalar values to classes of values that follow the same control flow path, we increase the information gathered per simulation evaluation. Our derivation from an established smoothed gradient estimator shows that the method does not introduce any bias. We present an implementation via a custom numerical data type to transparently carry out dimensional peeking over C++ programs. Variance reductions by factors of up to 7.9 are observed for three simulation-based optimization problems with high-dimensional input. The optimization progress compared to three meta-heuristics shows that dimensional peeking increases the competitiveness of zeroth-order optimization for discrete and non-convex simulations.

AI.tldr: A variance reduction technique called dimensional peeking for gradient estimation in discrete, simulation-based optimization; lifts sampling granularity to classes of values sharing the same control flow, is unbiased, implemented via a custom data type in C++, and yields up to 7.9x variance reduction with improved zeroth-order optimization performance.
AI.motivation: High-dimensional discrete optimization with unavailable derivatives; stochastic gradient estimates exhibit high variance leading to slow convergence.
AI.method: Dimensional peeking: group values by the same control flow path to increase information per simulation evaluation; derived from an established smoothed gradient estimator; implemented as a transparent custom numeric data type that propagates through C++ programs; preserves unbiasedness.
AI.result: Variance reduction factors up to 7.9x observed across three simulation-based, high-dimensional problems; optimization progress with dimensional peeking rivals or surpasses three meta-heuristics, improving zeroth-order optimization for discrete and non-convex simulations.
AI.conclusion: Dimensional peeking is a practical, unbiased variance reduction technique for gradient estimation in discrete simulation optimization, enhancing zeroth-order methods and enabling easier integration via a custom data type.

title: Ocean Current-Harnessing Stage-Gated MPC: Monotone Cost Shaping and Speed-to-Fly for Energy-Efficient AUV Navigation
summary: Autonomous Underwater Vehicles (AUVs) are a highly promising technology for ocean exploration and diverse offshore operations, yet their practical deployment is constrained by energy efficiency and endurance. To address this, we propose Current-Harnessing Stage-Gated MPC, which exploits ocean currents via a per-stage scalar which indicates the "helpfulness" of ocean currents. This scalar is computed along the prediction horizon to gate lightweight cost terms only where the ocean currents truly aids the control goal. The proposed cost terms, that are merged in the objective function, are (i) a Monotone Cost Shaping (MCS) term, a help-gated, non-worsening modification that relaxes along-track position error and provides a bounded translational energy rebate, guaranteeing the shaped objective is never larger than a set baseline, and (ii) a speed-to-fly (STF) cost component that increases the price of thrust and softly matches ground velocity to the ocean current, enabling near zero water-relative "gliding". All terms are C1 and integrate as a plug-and-play in MPC designs. Extensive simulations with the BlueROV2 model under realistic ocean current fields show that the proposed approach achieves substantially lower energy consumption than conventional predictive control while maintaining comparable arrival times and constraint satisfaction.

AI.tldr: MPC for AUVs that leverages ocean currents via a per-stage 'helpfulness' gate, using a monotone cost shaping term and a speed-to-fly term to reduce energy while preserving arrival times and constraints.
AI.motivation: Enhance energy efficiency and endurance of AUV missions by exploiting ambient ocean currents within a predictive control framework.
AI.method: Stage-gated MPC where a horizon-wide scalar indicates the usefulness of currents; integrates two terms into the objective: (i) Monotone Cost Shaping (MCS) a help-gated, non-worsening revision that relaxes along-track error and provides a bounded translational energy rebate; (ii) Speed-to-Fly (STF) term that increases thrust cost and gently aligns ground velocity with the current; both terms are C1 and plug-and-play in MPC.
AI.result: Simulations with the BlueROV2 model under realistic current fields show substantial energy reduction compared to conventional MPC, with arrival times and constraint satisfaction remaining comparable.
AI.conclusion: The proposed current-harnessing MPC achieves energy efficiency gains without sacrificing performance, and its MCS and STF terms offer a modular, monotone, and differentiable augmentation suitable for MPC implementations.

title: Benchmarking Agents in Insurance Underwriting Environments
summary: As AI agents integrate into enterprise applications, their evaluation demands benchmarks that reflect the complexity of real-world operations. Instead, existing benchmarks overemphasize open-domains such as code, use narrow accuracy metrics, and lack authentic complexity. We present UNDERWRITE, an expert-first, multi-turn insurance underwriting benchmark designed in close collaboration with domain experts to capture real-world enterprise challenges. UNDERWRITE introduces critical realism factors often absent in current benchmarks: proprietary business knowledge, noisy tool interfaces, and imperfect simulated users requiring careful information gathering. Evaluating 13 frontier models, we uncover significant gaps between research lab performance and enterprise readiness: the most accurate models are not the most efficient, models hallucinate domain knowledge despite tool access, and pass^k results show a 20% drop in performance. The results from UNDERWRITE demonstrate that expert involvement in benchmark design is essential for realistic agent evaluation, common agentic frameworks exhibit brittleness that skews performance reporting, and hallucination detection in specialized domains demands compositional approaches. Our work provides insights for developing benchmarks that better align with enterprise deployment requirements.

AI.tldr: UNDERWRITE is an expert-driven, multi-turn underwriting benchmark that reveals enterprise-specific evaluation gaps across 13 frontier models, highlighting realism factors and the misalignment between lab performance and deployment readiness.
AI.motivation: Current AI benchmarks for enterprise agents overemphasize open-domain tasks and narrow metrics, failing to capture real-world enterprise complexity such as proprietary business knowledge, noisy tool interfaces, and imperfect user behavior.
AI.method: Developed in collaboration with domain experts, UNDERWRITE incorporates realism factors (proprietary knowledge, noisy tools, imperfect user simulators) and evaluates models on realistic multi-turn underwriting tasks, using real-world enterprise scenarios.
AI.result: Evaluations of 13 frontier models show substantial gaps between lab performance and enterprise readiness: accuracy does not imply efficiency, models hallucinate domain knowledge despite tool access, and pass^k performance declines by about 20%. Expert-driven design reveals brittleness in common agent frameworks and the need for compositional hallucination-detection in specialized domains.
AI.conclusion: Expert involvement is essential for realistic benchmarks; current agent frameworks are brittle in enterprise contexts, and robust evaluation requires compositional methods to detect domain-specific hallucinations. The work offers guidance for benchmarking aligned with deployment needs.

title: Automated univariate time series forecasting with regression trees
summary: This paper describes a methodology for automated univariate time series forecasting using regression trees and their ensembles: bagging and random forests. The key aspects that are addressed are: the use of an autoregressive approach and recursive forecasts, how to select the autoregressive features, how to deal with trending series and how to cope with seasonal behavior. Experimental results show a forecast accuracy comparable with well-established statistical models such as exponential smoothing or ARIMA. Furthermore, a publicly available software implementing all the proposed strategies has been developed and is described in the paper.

AI.tldr: Univariate time series forecasting via regression trees and ensembles (bagging, random forests) using autoregressive features and recursive forecasts; addresses feature selection, trend, seasonality; results competitive with exponential smoothing or ARIMA; public software provided.
AI.motivation: Improve forecast accuracy for univariate series by applying tree-based regression ensembles to autoregressive features, while tackling common issues like feature selection, trend, and seasonality; also provide accessible software to practitioners.
AI.method: Construct autoregressive features (lags) as input to regression trees and ensembles. Use bagging and random forests with recursive multi-step forecasting. Discuss AR-feature selection strategies and techniques to handle trend and seasonal behavior.
AI.result: Forecast accuracy comparable to well-established models (exponential smoothing or ARIMA). A publicly available software implementing the proposed strategies is described.
AI.conclusion: Tree-based regression ensembles are viable for univariate forecasting when coupled with thoughtful AR feature design and handling of trend/seasonality; the accompanying software facilitates practical application.

title: Safe Stochastic Explorer: Enabling Safe Goal Driven Exploration in Stochastic Environments and Safe Interaction with Unknown Objects
summary: Autonomous robots operating in unstructured, safety-critical environments, from planetary exploration to warehouses and homes, must learn to safely navigate and interact with their surroundings despite limited prior knowledge. Current methods for safe control, such as Hamilton-Jacobi Reachability and Control Barrier Functions, assume known system dynamics. Meanwhile existing safe exploration techniques often fail to account for the unavoidable stochasticity inherent when operating in unknown real world environments, such as an exploratory rover skidding over an unseen surface or a household robot pushing around unmapped objects in a pantry. To address this critical gap, we propose Safe Stochastic Explorer (S.S.Explorer) a novel framework for safe, goal-driven exploration under stochastic dynamics. Our approach strategically balances safety and information gathering to reduce uncertainty about safety in the unknown environment. We employ Gaussian Processes to learn the unknown safety function online, leveraging their predictive uncertainty to guide information-gathering actions and provide probabilistic bounds on safety violations. We first present our method for discrete state space environments and then introduce a scalable relaxation to effectively extend this approach to continuous state spaces. Finally we demonstrate how this framework can be naturally applied to ensure safe physical interaction with multiple unknown objects. Extensive validation in simulation and demonstrative hardware experiments showcase the efficacy of our method, representing a step forward toward enabling reliable widespread robot autonomy in complex, uncertain environments.

AI.tldr: Introduces Safe Stochastic Explorer (S.S.Explorer): a GP-based, probabilistic framework for safe, goal-directed exploration under stochastic dynamics, with discrete-to-continuous state-space extension, validated in simulation and hardware.
AI.motivation: Bridge the gap between traditional safe-control methods (requiring known dynamics) and safe exploration under real-world stochasticity, enabling reliable autonomy in unstructured environments.
AI.method: Online learning of an unknown safety function via Gaussian Processes; use predictive uncertainty to guide exploration actions that balance safety with information gain; starts with discrete state spaces and introduces a scalable relaxation for continuous spaces; extends to safe interaction with unknown objects.
AI.result: Simulation studies and demonstrative hardware experiments show the framework effectively balances safety and information gathering, reducing uncertainty about safety and enabling safe physical interaction.
AI.conclusion: S.S.Explorer provides a probabilistic, scalable approach to safe exploration under stochastic dynamics, offering safety guarantees via GP-based uncertainty and a discrete-to-continuous extension suitable for real-world robotic autonomy.

title: Dual Latent Memory for Visual Multi-agent System
summary: While Visual Multi-Agent Systems (VMAS) promise to enhance comprehensive abilities through inter-agent collaboration, empirical evidence reveals a counter-intuitive "scaling wall": increasing agent turns often degrades performance while exponentially inflating token costs. We attribute this failure to the information bottleneck inherent in text-centric communication, where converting perceptual and thinking trajectories into discrete natural language inevitably induces semantic loss. To this end, we propose L$^{2}$-VMAS, a novel model-agnostic framework that enables inter-agent collaboration with dual latent memories. Furthermore, we decouple the perception and thinking while dynamically synthesizing dual latent memories. Additionally, we introduce an entropy-driven proactive triggering that replaces passive information transmission with efficient, on-demand memory access. Extensive experiments among backbones, sizes, and multi-agent structures demonstrate that our method effectively breaks the "scaling wall" with superb scalability, improving average accuracy by 2.7-5.4% while reducing token usage by 21.3-44.8%. Codes: https://github.com/YU-deep/L2-VMAS.

AI.tldr: L2-VMAS introduces dual latent memories and entropy-driven triggering to overcome the VMAS scaling wall, enhancing performance and reducing token costs in a model-agnostic way.
AI.motivation: Text-centric inter-agent communication causes semantic loss and bottlenecks; scaling VMAS with more turns leads to performance degradation and higher token costs.
AI.method: Proposes L2-VMAS framework with dual latent memories that separate perception and thinking; dynamic synthesis of memories; entropy-driven proactive triggering enabling on-demand memory access; model-agnostic; extensive cross-backbone experiments.
AI.result: Across backbones/sizes/structures, accuracy improves by 2.7–5.4% and token usage decreases by 21.3–44.8%; demonstrates removal of the scaling wall.
AI.conclusion: L2-VMAS effectively breaks the scaling wall, offering scalable, efficient collaboration in VMAS; code released.

title: Lossless Embedding Compression via Spherical Coordinates
summary: We present a lossless compression method for unit-norm embeddings that achieves 1.5$\times$ compression, 25\% better than the best prior method. The method exploits that spherical coordinates of high-dimensional unit vectors concentrate around $π/2$, causing IEEE 754 exponents to collapse to a single value and enabling entropy coding. Evaluation across 26 configurations spanning text, image, and multi-vector embeddings confirms consistent improvement. The method requires no training and is fully lossless within float32 precision.

AI.tldr: A training-free, lossless compression scheme for unit-norm embeddings that achieves 1.5x compression (≈25% better than the prior best) by exploiting that high-dimensional unit vectors’ spherical coordinates concentrate near π/2, which collapses IEEE 754 exponent values and enables entropy coding; validated on 26 configurations (text, image, multi-vector embeddings); lossless within float32.
AI.motivation: Reduce storage and bandwidth for embedding payloads while preserving exact reconstruction, particularly for high-dimensional unit-norm embeddings. Leverage a geometric property of the sphere to enable efficient, training-free lossless coding.
AI.method: Exploit the concentration of spherical coordinates around π/2 in high dimensions, causing IEEE 754 exponents to collapse to a single value. Use entropy coding on the resulting representation. No training required; the approach remains lossless within float32 precision.
AI.result: Achieves 1.5× compression, about 25% better than the best prior method. Evaluation across 26 configurations spanning text, image, and multi-vector embeddings shows consistent improvement and full losslessness within float32.
AI.conclusion: The method provides a training-free, lossless compression pathway for unit-norm embeddings by leveraging a concentration phenomenon in high-dimensional spherical coordinates, with practical storage and bandwidth benefits and broad empirical validation.

title: Learning When to Jump for Off-road Navigation
summary: Low speed does not always guarantee safety in off-road driving. For instance, crossing a ditch may be risky at a low speed due to the risk of getting stuck, yet safe at a higher speed with a controlled, accelerated jump. Achieving such behavior requires path planning that explicitly models complex motion dynamics, whereas existing methods often neglect this aspect and plan solely based on positions or a fixed velocity. To address this gap, we introduce Motion-aware Traversability (MAT) representation to explicitly model terrain cost conditioned on actual robot motion. Instead of assigning a single scalar score for traversability, MAT models each terrain region as a Gaussian function of velocity. During online planning, we decompose the terrain cost computation into two stages: (1) predict terrain-dependent Gaussian parameters from perception in a single forward pass, (2) efficiently update terrain costs for new velocities inferred from current dynamics by evaluating these functions without repeated inference. We develop a system that integrates MAT to enable agile off-road navigation and evaluate it in both simulated and real-world environments with various obstacles. Results show that MAT achieves real-time efficiency and enhances the performance of off-road navigation, reducing path detours by 75% while maintaining safety across challenging terrains.

AI.tldr: Introduces Motion-aware Traversability (MAT) to model terrain traversability as velocity-dependent costs using Gaussian functions; enables real-time planning by predicting Gaussian parameters from perception and updating costs for new velocities; demonstrated to reduce detours and maintain safety in off-road tasks.
AI.motivation: Existing traversability representations often ignore robot motion dynamics and treat terrain cost as a static scalar or fixed velocity, which can be unsafe in off-road scenarios where speed and motion affect traversability (e.g., ditch crossing). There is a need for motion-aware traversability that couples terrain cost with actual dynamics.
AI.method: MAT represents each terrain region as a Gaussian function of velocity. Online planning decomposes terrain cost computation into two stages: (1) predict terrain-dependent Gaussian parameters from perception in a single forward pass, (2) efficiently update terrain costs for new velocities inferred from current dynamics by evaluating these Gaussian functions without repeated inference. The system integrates MAT into the planning pipeline for agile off-road navigation.
AI.result: MAT achieves real-time efficiency and improves off-road navigation performance, reducing path detours by 75% while maintaining safety across challenging terrains in both simulated and real-world environments with various obstacles.
AI.conclusion: Motion-aware traversability enables agile, safe off-road navigation by explicitly modeling terrain costs as velocity-dependent functions, paving the way for dynamic-aware path planning and broader applicability to diverse terrains and platforms.

title: Replacing Parameters with Preferences: Federated Alignment of Heterogeneous Vision-Language Models
summary: VLMs have broad potential in privacy-sensitive domains such as healthcare and finance, yet strict data-sharing constraints render centralized training infeasible. FL mitigates this issue by enabling decentralized training, but practical deployments face challenges due to client heterogeneity in computational resources, application requirements, and model architectures. We argue that while replacing data with model parameters characterizes the present of FL, replacing parameters with preferences represents a more scalable and privacy-preserving future. Motivated by this perspective, we propose MoR, a federated alignment framework based on GRPO with Mixture-of-Rewards for heterogeneous VLMs. MoR initializes a visual foundation model as a KL-regularized reference, while each client locally trains a reward model from local preference annotations, capturing specific evaluation signals without exposing raw data. To reconcile heterogeneous rewards, we introduce a routing-based fusion mechanism that adaptively aggregates client reward signals. Finally, the server performs GRPO with this mixed reward to optimize the base VLM. Experiments on three public VQA benchmarks demonstrate that MoR consistently outperforms federated alignment baselines in generalization, robustness, and cross-client adaptability. Our approach provides a scalable solution for privacy-preserving alignment of heterogeneous VLMs under federated settings.

AI.tldr: Proposes MoR, a federated alignment framework for heterogeneous vision-language models using Mixture-of-Rewards and GRPO to fuse client rewards, enabling privacy-preserving VLM alignment under federation; shows improvements in generalization, robustness, and cross-client adaptability on VQA benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Privacy-sensitive domains hinder centralized VLM training; federated learning with heterogeneous clients requires robust aggregation of subjective evaluation signals (rewards) rather than shared data.
AI.method: KL-regularized reference VLM is initialized; each client trains a local reward model from preferences; routing-based fusion aggregates client reward signals; server performs GRPO with the mixed rewards to optimize the base VLM.
AI.result: MoR outperforms federated alignment baselines on three public VQA benchmarks in generalization, robustness, and cross-client adaptability.
AI.conclusion: Offers scalable privacy-preserving alignment for heterogeneous VLMs in FL; demonstrates potential of preferences-based federation.

title: Why LoRA Resists Label Noise: A Theoretical Framework for Noise-Robust Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning
summary: Parameter-efficient fine-tuning methods like Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) have become the dominant paradigm for adapting large pretrained models. We present a theoretical framework explaining an underexplored property: LoRA's inherent resistance to label noise. Our analysis reveals three key insights. First, we prove that rank-$r$ LoRA cannot memorize all possible label assignments once the sample size exceeds $O(r(d+k-r))$, limiting its capacity to fit arbitrary noise. Second, we derive an optimal rank balancing approximation bias and noise-induced variance, showing it decreases with noise rate. Third, we establish temporal separation: clean patterns are learned early while noise memorization occurs later. We propose RACT (Rank-Aware Curriculum Training), leveraging rank discrepancy for noise detection. Experiments validate our predictions, with RACT achieving 91.1% F1 for noise detection on AG News while maintaining 91.46% accuracy, competitive with baselines that lack noise detection capability.
