AI.tldr: The work offers a theoretical account for why LoRA is inherently resistant to label noise, derives sample-complexity limits for memorizing noisy labels, identifies an optimal rank-variance trade-off, and introduces a rank-aware curriculum (RACT) to detect noise; validated by experiments showing strong noise-detection and competitive accuracy.
AI.motivation: To explain and leverage LoRA's apparent robustness to label noise, by characterizing the capacity of rank-r LoRA, the dynamics of learning under noise, and providing a practical method to detect noisy labels during fine-tuning.
AI.method: 1) Theoretical analysis proving rank-r LoRA cannot memorize all label assignments once the sample size exceeds O(r(d+k−r)). 2) Derivation of an optimal rank that balances approximation bias and noise-induced variance, with the balance improving as noise rate increases. 3) Demonstration of temporal separation: clean patterns learned early, noise memorized later. 4) Proposal of RACT (Rank-Aware Curriculum Training) that uses rank discrepancy to detect noise. 5) Empirical validation on datasets (e.g., AG News) showing noise-detection F1 of 91.1% and accuracy of 91.46%, competitive with baselines without noise-detection capability.
AI.result: Theoretical bounds on LoRA’s memorization capacity under label noise; a rank-based bias-variance trade-off that improves with higher noise; demonstration of a clean-vs-noise learning timeline; a practical noise-detection algorithm (RACT) with strong empirical results (92%-ish metrics on AG News and competitive accuracy).
AI.conclusion: The study provides a principled explanation for LoRA’s resistance to label noise and introduces a viable noise-detection curriculum grounded in rank analysis, with empirical results supporting its effectiveness and practicality.

title: RoDiF: Robust Direct Fine-Tuning of Diffusion Policies with Corrupted Human Feedback
summary: Diffusion policies are a powerful paradigm for robotic control, but fine-tuning them with human preferences is fundamentally challenged by the multi-step structure of the denoising process. To overcome this, we introduce a Unified Markov Decision Process (MDP) formulation that coherently integrates the diffusion denoising chain with environmental dynamics, enabling reward-free Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) for diffusion policies. Building on this formulation, we propose RoDiF (Robust Direct Fine-Tuning), a method that explicitly addresses corrupted human preferences. RoDiF reinterprets the DPO objective through a geometric hypothesis-cutting perspective and employs a conservative cutting strategy to achieve robustness without assuming any specific noise distribution. Extensive experiments on long-horizon manipulation tasks show that RoDiF consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, effectively steering pretrained diffusion policies of diverse architectures to human-preferred modes, while maintaining strong performance even under 30% corrupted preference labels.

AI.tldr: A unified MDP formulation integrates diffusion denoising with environmental dynamics to enable reward-free Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) for diffusion policies, and RoDiF robustly handles corrupted human preferences via a geometric hypothesis-cutting approach within a conservative strategy.
AI.motivation: Fine-tuning diffusion-based robotic policies with human preferences is hindered by the multi-step denoising process and unreliable feedback. A unified framework that combines diffusion dynamics with environment dynamics and a robust preference optimization objective can improve sample efficiency and robustness to noisy labels.
AI.method: 1) Propose Unified Markov Decision Process to coherently couple the diffusion denoising chain with environmental dynamics for reward-free DPO. 2) Introduce RoDiF (Robust Direct Fine-Tuning), which reinterprets the DPO objective through geometric hypothesis-cutting and applies a conservative cutting strategy to reject corrupted preferences without assuming a noise distribution.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on long-horizon manipulation tasks shows RoDiF consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, effectively steering pretrained diffusion policies to human-preferred modes, and maintaining strong performance even with up to 30% corrupted preference labels.
AI.conclusion: The unified MDP and RoDiF provide robust, preference-based fine-tuning for diffusion policies, enabling reliable adaptation across architectures and resilience to substantial label corruption in real-world settings.

title: PCBSchemaGen: Constraint-Guided Schematic Design via LLM for Printed Circuit Boards (PCB)
summary: Printed Circuit Board (PCB) schematic design plays an essential role in all areas of electronic industries. Unlike prior works that focus on digital or analog circuits alone, PCB design must handle heterogeneous digital, analog, and power signals while adhering to real-world IC packages and pin constraints. Automated PCB schematic design remains unexplored due to the scarcity of open-source data and the absence of simulation-based verification. We introduce PCBSchemaGen, the first training-free framework for PCB schematic design that comprises LLM agent and Constraint-guided synthesis. Our approach makes three contributions: 1. an LLM-based code generation paradigm with iterative feedback with domain-specific prompts. 2. a verification framework leveraging a real-world IC datasheet derived Knowledge Graph (KG) and Subgraph Isomorphism encoding pin-role semantics and topological constraints. 3. an extensive experiment on 23 PCB schematic tasks spanning digital, analog, and power domains. Results demonstrate that PCBSchemaGen significantly improves design accuracy and computational efficiency.

AI.tldr: Training-free PCBSchemaGen: LLM-driven PCB schematic design with constraint-guided synthesis and KG-based verification; achieves higher accuracy and efficiency across digital, analog, and power tasks.
AI.motivation: Automated PCB schematic design lags due to mixed signal domains, real-world IC package/pin constraints, data scarcity, and lack of verification; need a framework that combines LLM-driven generation with rigorous constraint verification.
AI.method: Iterative LLM-based code generation using domain-specific prompts; verification via a Knowledge Graph derived from real IC datasheets and Subgraph Isomorphism encoding for pin-role semantics and topological constraints.
AI.result: Extensive evaluation on 23 PCB schematic tasks spanning digital, analog, and power; significant gains in design accuracy and computational efficiency.
AI.conclusion: First training-free framework for PCB schematic design; demonstrates feasibility of LLM-assisted design with constraint-guided synthesis and datasheet-driven verification; potential for broader adoption and extension.

title: CARE-RFT: Confidence-Anchored Reinforcement Finetuning for Reliable Reasoning in Large Language Models
summary: Reinforcement finetuning (RFT) has emerged as a powerful paradigm for unlocking reasoning capabilities in large language models. However, we identify a critical trade-off: while unconstrained RFT achieves strong reasoning performance, it severely compromises model trustworthiness by amplifying hallucination and worsening calibration; conversely, RKL-constrained RFT preserves trustworthiness but limits reasoning gains due to its unbounded penalty on exploratory deviations. To resolve this tension, we introduce CARE-RFT (Confidence-Anchored Regularized Reinforcement Finetuning), a novel method that replaces standard reverse KL regularization with a skew reverse KL divergence. CARE-RFT provides a confidence-sensitive penalty: it is bounded for confident, consistently rewarded explorations to enable reasoning, while unbounded elsewhere to preserve calibration. Extensive experiments across multiple model scales and RFT algorithms show that CARE-RFT achieves a superior balance, matching the reasoning performance of unconstrained RFT while recovering the trustworthiness and calibration of the base model. Our work establishes that careful, confidence-aware regularization is key to building both capable and trustworthy reasoning models.

AI.tldr: CARE-RFT leverages confidence-anchored skew reverse KL to balance exploration and calibration in reinforcement finetuning, achieving strong reasoning while preserving trustworthiness.
AI.motivation: Unconstrained RFT yields strong reasoning but harms model trustworthiness (hallucination, miscalibration); RKL-constrained RFT preserves trust but suppresses reasoning; a regularizer that adapts to confidence is needed to reconcile these aims.
AI.method: Replace standard reverse KL regularization with a skew reverse KL divergence that is bounded for confident exploration (rewarded consistently) and unbounded elsewhere, yielding a confidence-sensitive penalty that enables reasoning without sacrificing calibration; validated across model scales and RFT algorithms.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation across multiple model sizes and RFT algorithms shows CARE-RFT matches the reasoning performance of unconstrained RFT while restoring the base model’s trustworthiness and calibration, outperforming conventional RKL-constrained approaches.
AI.conclusion: Confidence-aware regularization is essential for building LLMs that are both capable in reasoning and trustworthy in behavior during reinforcement finetuning.

title: UniMorphGrasp: Diffusion Model with Morphology-Awareness for Cross-Embodiment Dexterous Grasp Generation
summary: Cross-embodiment dexterous grasping aims to generate stable and diverse grasps for robotic hands with heterogeneous kinematic structures. Existing methods are often tailored to specific hand designs and fail to generalize to unseen hand morphologies outside the training distribution. To address these limitations, we propose \textbf{UniMorphGrasp}, a diffusion-based framework that incorporates hand morphological information into the grasp generation process for unified cross-embodiment grasp synthesis. The proposed approach maps grasps from diverse robotic hands into a unified human-like canonical hand pose representation, providing a common space for learning. Grasp generation is then conditioned on structured representations of hand kinematics, encoded as graphs derived from hand configurations, together with object geometry. In addition, a loss function is introduced that exploits the hierarchical organization of hand kinematics to guide joint-level supervision. Extensive experiments demonstrate that UniMorphGrasp achieves state-of-the-art performance on existing dexterous grasp benchmarks and exhibits strong zero-shot generalization to previously unseen hand structures, enabling scalable and practical cross-embodiment grasp deployment.

AI.tldr: Diffusion-based UniMorphGrasp unifies cross-embodiment dexterous grasp generation by mapping diverse hands to a canonical pose and conditioning on hand kinematics graphs with object geometry; introduces hierarchical supervision; achieves state-of-the-art results and strong zero-shot generalization.
AI.motivation: Current dexterous grasp methods are tailored to specific hand designs and fail to generalize to unseen hand morphologies. A unified representation across embodiments is needed for scalable cross-embodiment grasp synthesis.
AI.method: A diffusion-based framework that maps grasps to a unified human-like canonical hand pose. Grasp generation is conditioned on graph-encoded hand kinematics derived from hand configurations and object geometry. A hierarchical joint-level supervision loss leverages the kinematic structure to guide learning.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on existing dexterous grasp benchmarks and shows strong zero-shot generalization to unseen hand structures, enabling scalable cross-embodiment deployment.
AI.conclusion: The work demonstrates that a unified canonical representation and structure-aware diffusion learning enable robust cross-embodiment grasp synthesis with strong generalization and practical applicability.

title: Diagnosing the Reliability of LLM-as-a-Judge via Item Response Theory
summary: While LLM-as-a-Judge is widely used in automated evaluation, existing validation practices primarily operate at the level of observed outputs, offering limited insight into whether LLM judges themselves function as stable and reliable measurement instruments. To address this limitation, we introduce a two-phase diagnostic framework for assessing reliability of LLM-as-a-Judge, grounded in Item Response Theory (IRT). The framework adopts Graded Response Model (GRM) of IRT and formalizes reliability along two complementary dimensions: (1) intrinsic consistency, defined as the stability of measurement behavior under prompt variations, and (2) human alignment, capturing correspondence with human quality assessments. We empirically examine diverse LLM judges with this framework, and show that leveraging IRT-GRM yields interpretable signals for diagnosing judgments systematically. These signals provide practical guidance for verifying reliablity of LLM-as-a-Judge and identifying potential causes of unreliability.

AI.tldr: Introduces an IRT-GRM based two-phase diagnostic framework to assess the reliability of LLM-based judges, separating intrinsic consistency from human alignment and providing actionable diagnostics.
AI.motivation: Current validation of LLM judges focuses on observed outputs rather than treating the judge as a measurement instrument; there is a need for reliability diagnostics to ensure judgments are stable and align with human quality assessments.
AI.method: A two-phase framework using the Graded Response Model (GRM) from Item Response Theory to quantify (i) intrinsic consistency (stability under prompt variations) and (ii) human alignment (correlation with human judgments) across diverse LLM judges.
AI.result: The framework yields interpretable reliability signals via IRT-GRM that can diagnose judgments systematically, offering practical guidance to verify LLM-as-a-Judge reliability and identify potential unreliability causes.
AI.conclusion: IRT-GRM provides a principled, interpretable approach to evaluating LLM-based judges, enabling systematic diagnosis and remediation of reliability issues.

title: ECCO: Evidence-Driven Causal Reasoning for Compiler Optimization
summary: Compiler auto-tuning faces a dichotomy between traditional black-box search methods, which lack semantic guidance, and recent Large Language Model (LLM) approaches, which often suffer from superficial pattern matching and causal opacity. In this paper, we introduce ECCO, a framework that bridges interpretable reasoning with combinatorial search. We first propose a reverse engineering methodology to construct a Chain-of-Thought dataset, explicitly mapping static code features to verifiable performance evidence. This enables the model to learn the causal logic governing optimization decisions rather than merely imitating sequences. Leveraging this interpretable prior, we design a collaborative inference mechanism where the LLM functions as a strategist, defining optimization intents that dynamically guide the mutation operations of a genetic algorithm. Experimental results on seven datasets demonstrate that ECCO significantly outperforms the LLVM opt -O3 baseline, achieving an average 24.44% reduction in cycles.

AI.tldr: ECCO introduces an interpretable, LLM-guided framework for compiler auto-tuning that merges causal reasoning with combinatorial search, achieving notable gains over LLVM -O3.
AI.motivation: Address the gap between traditional black-box search and LLM pattern matching by enabling the model to learn the causal logic of optimization decisions through an interpretable chain-of-thought dataset.
AI.method: Reverse-engineer a Chain-of-Thought dataset linking static code features to verifiable performance evidence; use the interpretable prior to guide a genetic algorithm via an LLM acting as a strategist who defines optimization intents that steer mutation operations.
AI.result: Evaluated on seven datasets, ECCO outperforms the LLVM -O3 baseline, achieving an average 24.44% reduction in cycles.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates that combining interpretable reasoning with collaborative LLM-guided search can surpass traditional compiler optimizations, offering a generalizable approach for causal, interpretable auto-tuning.

title: Green-VLA: Staged Vision-Language-Action Model for Generalist Robots
summary: We introduce Green-VLA, a staged Vision-Language-Action (VLA) framework for real-world deployment on the Green humanoid robot while maintaining generalization across diverse embodiments. Green-VLA follows a five stage curriculum: (L0) foundational VLMs, (L1) multimodal grounding, (R0) multi-embodiment pretraining, (R1) embodiment-specific adaptation, and (R2) reinforcement-learning (RL) policy alignment. We couple a scalable data-processing pipeline (3,000 hours of demonstrations) with temporal alignment and quality filtering, and use a unified, embodiment-aware action interface enabling a single policy to control humanoids, mobile manipulators, and fixed-base arms. At inference, the VLA controller is enhanced with episode-progress prediction, out-of-distribution detection, and joint-prediction-based guidance to improve safety and precise target selection. Experiments on Simpler BRIDGE WidowX and CALVIN ABC-D, as well as real-robot evaluations, show strong generalization and performance gains from RL alignment in success rate, robustness, and long-horizon efficiency.

AI.tldr: Green-VLA is a five-stage Vision-Language-Action framework for cross-embodiment robotics, combining a scalable 3,000-hour demo pipeline with RL-aligned policy and an embodiment-aware interface, achieving strong generalization and improved long-horizon performance in real-world tests.
AI.motivation: Enable a single, generalizable VLA policy that works across heterogeneous robotic embodiments, addressing data efficiency and safety for real-world deployment.
AI.method: Five-stage curriculum: L0 foundational VLMs; L1 multimodal grounding; R0 multi-embodiment pretraining; R1 embodiment-specific adaptation; R2 RL policy alignment. Scalable data processing (3k demonstrations) with temporal alignment and quality filtering; unified embodiment-aware action interface; inference-time enhancements: episode-progress prediction, OOD detection, joint-prediction guidance.
AI.result: Demonstrates strong generalization and performance gains from RL alignment in success rate, robustness, and long-horizon efficiency, on Simpler BRIDGE WidowX and CALVIN ABC-D, plus real-robot evaluations.
AI.conclusion: RL-aligned, embodiment-aware VLA policy enables cross-embodiment real-world deployment with improved safety, accuracy, and efficiency.

title: How Far Are LLMs from Professional Poker Players? Revisiting Game-Theoretic Reasoning with Agentic Tool Use
summary: As Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly applied in high-stakes domains, their ability to reason strategically under uncertainty becomes critical. Poker provides a rigorous testbed, requiring not only strong actions but also principled, game-theoretic reasoning. In this paper, we conduct a systematic study of LLMs in multiple realistic poker tasks, evaluating both gameplay outcomes and reasoning traces. Our analysis reveals LLMs fail to compete against traditional algorithms and identifies three recurring flaws: reliance on heuristics, factual misunderstandings, and a "knowing-doing" gap where actions diverge from reasoning. An initial attempt with behavior cloning and step-level reinforcement learning improves reasoning style but remains insufficient for accurate game-theoretic play. Motivated by these limitations, we propose ToolPoker, a tool-integrated reasoning framework that combines external solvers for GTO-consistent actions with more precise professional-style explanations. Experiments demonstrate that ToolPoker achieves state-of-the-art gameplay while producing reasoning traces that closely reflect game-theoretic principles.

AI.tldr: LLMs underperform in poker; reveal heuristics, factual errors, and a knowing-doing gap; ToolPoker integrates external solvers for optimal actions with explanations, achieving state-of-the-art gameplay and improved reasoning.
AI.motivation: Assess LLMs' capacity for strategic reasoning under uncertainty in high-stakes domains; poker as a rigorous testbed.
AI.method: Systematic evaluation of LLMs across realistic poker tasks, analysis of gameplay outcomes and reasoning traces; investigation of behavior cloning and step-level RL; development and evaluation of ToolPoker with external solvers and professional-style explanations.
AI.result: LLMs lag traditional algorithms; three recurring flaws identified; ToolPoker attains state-of-the-art gameplay and better-aligned, traceable reasoning.
AI.conclusion: Tool integration with external solvers and guided explanations can bridge the gap between reasoning and action in strategic domains; encourages future work on tool-assisted reasoning and explainable AI.

title: From Numbers to Prompts: A Cognitive Symbolic Transition Mechanism for Lightweight Time-Series Forecasting
comment: 16 pages, 5 figures. Submitted to ACM Transactions on Intelligent Systems and Technology
summary: Large language models have achieved remarkable success in time series prediction tasks, but their substantial computational and memory requirements limit deployment on lightweight platforms. In this paper, we propose the Symbolic Transition Mechanism (STM) a novel framework that bridges numeric time series data and language models through symbolic abstraction and prompt engineering. STM transforms continuous time series values into symbol tokens with quantization techniques based on human cognitive structures, and captures temporal dynamics through structured transformations of symbols, enabling fast engineering based predictions in which language models focus on critical parts of time series data. STM is a general purpose mechanisms that ensure the integrity of backbone language models, but they significantly improve their efficiency by inferring the dynamic and structured patterns inherent in time series data. We evaluated STM on various time series datasets, paired with four small language models (SLM) with limited computational environments. For all models, STM achieves error reductions of up to 69% in MAE and 90% in MSE compared to the default backbone SLM without STM. These results demonstrate the potential of STM as an efficient, adaptable layer for symbol-driven time series prediction using foundation models. The accuracy improvements were made at negligible resource costs, with maximum GPU memory of the base model increasing by approximately 0.06% and latency overhead increasing by only 0.64%.

AI.tldr: STM converts time series to symbolic tokens to allow language models to predict time series efficiently, achieving substantial error reductions with minimal overhead.
AI.motivation: Deploying language models for time series prediction is computationally expensive; need an efficient approach for lightweight platforms. STM uses symbolic abstraction and prompt engineering to bridge numeric data with LMs.
AI.method: Symbolic Transition Mechanism (STM) quantizes continuous time series into symbolic tokens guided by human cognitive-inspired structures, and applies structured symbolic transformations to capture temporal dynamics. It serves as an adaptable layer that preserves backbone LM integrity while focusing predictions on critical data parts. Evaluated with four small language models on multiple datasets; shows low overhead.
AI.result: Across models/datasets, STM achieves up to 69% MAE reduction and 90% MSE reduction compared to the backbone LM without STM. Base model GPU memory up by ~0.06% and latency up by ~0.64%.
AI.conclusion: STM is a general-purpose, efficient layer for symbol-driven time series prediction with foundation models, enabling significant accuracy gains with negligible resource costs.

title: SanD-Planner: Sample-Efficient Diffusion Planner in B-Spline Space for Robust Local Navigation
summary: The challenge of generating reliable local plans has long hindered practical applications in highly cluttered and dynamic environments. Key fundamental bottlenecks include acquiring large-scale expert demonstrations across diverse scenes and improving learning efficiency with limited data. This paper proposes SanD-Planner, a sample-efficient diffusion-based local planner that conducts depth image-based imitation learning within the clamped B-spline space. By operating within this compact space, the proposed algorithm inherently yields smooth outputs with bounded prediction errors over local supports, naturally aligning with receding-horizon execution. Integration of an ESDF-based safety checker with explicit clearance and time-to-completion metrics further reduces the training burden associated with value-function learning for feasibility assessment. Experiments show that training with $500$ episodes (merely $0.25\%$ of the demonstration scale used by the baseline), SanD-Planner achieves state-of-the-art performance on the evaluated open benchmark, attaining success rates of $90.1\%$ in simulated cluttered environments and $72.0\%$ in indoor simulations. The performance is further proven by demonstrating zero-shot transferability to realistic experimentation in both 2D and 3D scenes. The dataset and pre-trained models will also be open-sourced.

AI.tldr: A diffusion-based, sample-efficient local planner (SanD-Planner) uses depth-image imitation in a clamped B-spline space with an ESDF safety checker. It achieves high success with very limited demonstrations and shows zero-shot transfer to real 2D/3D scenes, with open-sourced data/models.
AI.motivation: Reliable local planning in highly cluttered and dynamic environments is hindered by data-hungry learning; obtaining large-scale expert demonstrations across diverse scenes and improving learning efficiency with limited data remain bottlenecks.
AI.method: SanD-Planner performs depth-image-based imitation learning within a compact, clamped B-spline space, yielding smooth local plans with bounded errors and natural fit for receding-horizon execution. A safety checker based on an ESDF (with explicit clearance and time-to-completion metrics) reduces reliance on value-function learning for feasibility assessment.
AI.result: Trains on 500 episodes (0.25% of demonstration scale of the baseline) and achieves state-of-the-art performance: 90.1% success in simulated cluttered environments, 72.0% in indoor simulations. Demonstrates zero-shot transfer to realistic 2D/3D experiments. Open-sourcing dataset and pre-trained models.
AI.conclusion: The approach delivers strong data efficiency and transferability for local planning in cluttered/dynamic settings, achieving high success rates with minimal demonstrations and enabling reproducibility through open resources.

title: Uncovering Latent Communication Patterns in Brain Networks via Adaptive Flow Routing
summary: Unraveling how macroscopic cognitive phenotypes emerge from microscopic neuronal connectivity remains one of the core pursuits of neuroscience. To this end, researchers typically leverage multi-modal information from structural connectivity (SC) and functional connectivity (FC) to complete downstream tasks. Recent methodologies explore the intricate coupling mechanisms between SC and FC, attempting to fuse their representations at the regional level. However, lacking fundamental neuroscientific insight, these approaches fail to uncover the latent interactions between neural regions underlying these connectomes, and thus cannot explain why SC and FC exhibit dynamic states of both coupling and heterogeneity. In this paper, we formulate multi-modal fusion through the lens of neural communication dynamics and propose the Adaptive Flow Routing Network (AFR-Net), a physics-informed framework that models how structural constraints (SC) give rise to functional communication patterns (FC), enabling interpretable discovery of critical neural pathways. Extensive experiments demonstrate that AFR-Net significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines. The code is available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/DIAL-F0D1.

AI.tldr: AFR-Net is a physics-informed, adaptive flow-routing approach that explains how structural connectivity constrains functional communication, yielding interpretable neural pathways and outperforming baselines.
AI.motivation: Understanding how macroscopic cognitive phenotypes emerge from microscopic wiring remains unresolved. Existing SC-FC fusion methods lack neuroscientific insight into latent regional interactions and dynamic coupling/heterogeneity between connectomes.
AI.method: Adaptive Flow Routing Network (AFR-Net), a physics-informed multi-modal fusion framework that models how structural constraints (SC) shape functional communication (FC) via adaptive routing of neural flow, enabling interpretable discovery of critical pathways.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show AFR-Net significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines.
AI.conclusion: AFR-Net provides an interpretable mechanism linking SC and FC, explaining dynamic coupling and heterogeneity and revealing key neural pathways; code is available at the provided URL.

title: Interpreting and Controlling Model Behavior via Constitutions for Atomic Concept Edits
summary: We introduce a black-box interpretability framework that learns a verifiable constitution: a natural language summary of how changes to a prompt affect a model's specific behavior, such as its alignment, correctness, or adherence to constraints. Our method leverages atomic concept edits (ACEs), which are targeted operations that add, remove, or replace an interpretable concept in the input prompt. By systematically applying ACEs and observing the resulting effects on model behavior across various tasks, our framework learns a causal mapping from edits to predictable outcomes. This learned constitution provides deep, generalizable insights into the model. Empirically, we validate our approach across diverse tasks, including mathematical reasoning and text-to-image alignment, for controlling and understanding model behavior. We found that for text-to-image generation, GPT-Image tends to focus on grammatical adherence, while Imagen 4 prioritizes atmospheric coherence. In mathematical reasoning, distractor variables confuse GPT-5 but leave Gemini 2.5 models and o4-mini largely unaffected. Moreover, our results show that the learned constitutions are highly effective for controlling model behavior, achieving an average of 1.86 times boost in success rate over methods that do not use constitutions.

AI.tldr: Black-box interpretability through atomic concept edits (ACEs) to learn a verifiable natural-language constitution mapping prompt edits to model behavior, enabling controllable and generalizable understanding across tasks.
AI.motivation: Provide a verifiable, causal, and generalizable framework to understand how prompt-level concepts steer model behavior, and to enable reliable control without peeking into model internals.
AI.method: Apply targeted ACEs (add/remove/replace interpretable concepts in prompts), observe effects on behavior across tasks (math reasoning, text-to-image alignment), learn a causal mapping from edits to outcomes, derive a natural-language constitution, and validate its predictive/control power.
AI.result: Constitutions yield deep insights into model behavior; observed task- and model-specific focuses (GPT-Image on grammatical adherence, Imagen 4 on atmospheric coherence); distractor variables differentially affect models; learned constitutions substantially improve control (average ~1.86x boost in success rate vs non-constitution baselines).
AI.conclusion: A verifiable, generalizable interpretability framework that can both explain and steer model behavior through a learned constitutional map from prompt edits to outcomes, with strong empirical gains across tasks.

title: Minimal Footprint Grasping Inspired by Ants
summary: Ants are highly capable of grasping objects in clutter, and we have recently observed that this involves substantial use of their forelegs. The forelegs, more specifically the tarsi, have high friction microstructures (setal pads), are covered in hairs, and have a flexible under-actuated tip. Here we abstract these features to test their functional advantages for a novel low-cost gripper design, suitable for bin-picking applications. In our implementation, the gripper legs are long and slim, with high friction gripping pads, low friction hairs and single-segment tarsus-like structure to mimic the insect's setal pads, hairs, and the tarsi's interactive compliance. Experimental evaluation shows this design is highly robust for grasping a wide variety of individual consumer objects, with all grasp attempts successful. In addition, we demonstrate this design is effective for picking single objects from dense clutter, a task at which ants also show high competence. The work advances grasping technology and shed new light on the mechanical importance of hairy structures and tarsal flexibility in insects.

AI.tldr: A bio-inspired, low-cost gripper emulating ant tarsal pads and hairy microstructures achieves robust grasping of diverse objects and effective clutter pickup.
AI.motivation: To evaluate whether hairy structures (setal pads and hairs) and tarsal-like compliant, single-segment designs offer functional advantages for low-cost grasping in clutter and bin-picking tasks, inspired by ants.
AI.method: Abstracts ant foreleg features into a gripper architecture: long slim legs, high-friction gripping pads, low-friction hairs, and a single-segment tarsus-like structure to mimic setal pads, hairs, and tarsal compliance. Implemented as a low-cost gripper and tested on grasping diverse consumer objects and extracting single items from dense clutter.
AI.result: The design showed high robustness, with successful grasp attempts across a wide variety of objects and effective performance in dense clutter, suggesting the mechanical value of hairy structures and tarsal flexibility.
AI.conclusion: The work advances grasping technology by incorporating insect-inspired hairy and compliant tarsal features, highlighting their functional importance for robust, clutter-resilient grasping and bin-picking; it supports the potential for low-cost, bio-inspired grippers.

title: Unmasking Reasoning Processes: A Process-aware Benchmark for Evaluating Structural Mathematical Reasoning in LLMs
summary: Recent large language models (LLMs) achieve near-saturation accuracy on many established mathematical reasoning benchmarks, raising concerns about their ability to diagnose genuine reasoning competence. This saturation largely stems from the dominance of template-based computation and shallow arithmetic decomposition in existing datasets, which underrepresent reasoning skills such as multi-constraint coordination, constructive logical synthesis, and spatial inference. To address this gap, we introduce ReasoningMath-Plus, a benchmark of 150 carefully curated problems explicitly designed to evaluate structural reasoning. Each problem emphasizes reasoning under interacting constraints, constructive solution formation, or non-trivial structural insight, and is annotated with a minimal reasoning skeleton to support fine-grained process-level evaluation. Alongside the dataset, we introduce HCRS (Hazard-aware Chain-based Rule Score), a deterministic step-level scoring function, and train a Process Reward Model (PRM) on the annotated reasoning traces. Empirically, while leading models attain relatively high final-answer accuracy (up to 5.8/10), HCRS-based holistic evaluation yields substantially lower scores (average 4.36/10, best 5.14/10), showing that answer-only metrics can overestimate reasoning robustness.

AI.tldr: Introduces ReasoningMath-Plus, a 150-problem benchmark targeting structural reasoning beyond template-based computation, plus HCRS step-level scoring and a Process Reward Model; final-answer accuracy may be high while holistic reasoning evaluation remains low.
AI.motivation: To counter saturation on math-reasoning benchmarks caused by template-driven computation and shallow arithmetic decomposition, and to evaluate deeper reasoning skills (multi-constraint coordination, constructive synthesis, spatial inference) using annotated reasoning skeletons for process-level evaluation.
AI.method: Curate 150 problems emphasizing interacting constraints, constructive solution formation, and non-trivial structural insight; annotate with a minimal reasoning skeleton; propose Hazard-aware Chain-based Rule Score (HCRS) for deterministic step-level evaluation; train a Process Reward Model (PRM) on annotated traces; evaluate using both final answers and process-level scores.
AI.result: Leading models reach up to 5.8/10 final-answer accuracy, but HCRS-based holistic scores average 4.36/10 (best 5.14/10); indicates answer-only metrics overestimate genuine reasoning robustness.
AI.conclusion: Structural reasoning benchmarks with process-level signals (HCRS, PRM) reveal gaps in reasoning that final-answer metrics miss, underscoring the need for process-aware evaluation and training to improve true reasoning competence.

title: Trade-offs Between Individual and Group Fairness in Machine Learning: A Comprehensive Review
summary: Algorithmic fairness has become a central concern in computational decision-making systems, where ensuring equitable outcomes is essential for both ethical and legal reasons. Two dominant notions of fairness have emerged in the literature: Group Fairness (GF), which focuses on mitigating disparities across demographic subpopulations, and Individual Fairness (IF), which emphasizes consistent treatment of similar individuals. These notions have traditionally been studied in isolation. In contrast, this survey examines methods that jointly address GF and IF, integrating both perspectives within unified frameworks and explicitly characterizing the trade-offs between them. We provide a systematic and critical review of hybrid fairness approaches, organizing existing methods according to the fairness mechanisms they employ and the algorithmic and mathematical strategies used to reconcile multiple fairness criteria. For each class of methods, we examine their theoretical foundations, optimization mechanisms, and empirical evaluation practices, and discuss their limitations. Additionally, we discuss the challenges and identify open research directions for developing principled, context-aware hybrid fairness methods. By synthesizing insights across the literature, this survey aims to serve as a comprehensive resource for researchers and practitioners seeking to design hybrid algorithms that provide reliable fairness guarantees at both the individual and group levels.

AI.tldr: Survey of hybrid fairness methods that jointly address Group Fairness (GF) and Individual Fairness (IF), with taxonomy, theory, optimization, evaluation practices, limitations, and open directions.
AI.motivation: To unify GF and IF, address their trade-offs, and provide principled guidance for designing algorithms that guarantee fairness at both group and individual levels in decision-making systems.
AI.method: Systematic literature review and synthesis that classifies existing hybrid methods by the fairness mechanisms they employ (e.g., pre-, in-, post-processing) and the optimization/regularization strategies used to balance multiple fairness criteria; critique of theoretical foundations and empirical practices.
AI.result: Provides a comprehensive taxonomy of hybrid GF+IF methods, analyzes their theoretical underpinnings, optimization approaches, and evaluation practices, identifies trade-offs and limitations, and outlines open challenges and future directions for context-aware, principled hybrid fairness algorithms.
AI.conclusion: Hybrid GF+IF methods offer promise for simultaneous group- and individual-level fairness guarantees, but require principled trade-off management, robust evaluation, and context-aware design; the survey outlines a roadmap with open research directions and benchmarks.

title: CLAMP: Contrastive Learning for 3D Multi-View Action-Conditioned Robotic Manipulation Pretraining
summary: Leveraging pre-trained 2D image representations in behavior cloning policies has achieved great success and has become a standard approach for robotic manipulation. However, such representations fail to capture the 3D spatial information about objects and scenes that is essential for precise manipulation. In this work, we introduce Contrastive Learning for 3D Multi-View Action-Conditioned Robotic Manipulation Pretraining (CLAMP), a novel 3D pre-training framework that utilizes point clouds and robot actions. From the merged point cloud computed from RGB-D images and camera extrinsics, we re-render multi-view four-channel image observations with depth and 3D coordinates, including dynamic wrist views, to provide clearer views of target objects for high-precision manipulation tasks. The pre-trained encoders learn to associate the 3D geometric and positional information of objects with robot action patterns via contrastive learning on large-scale simulated robot trajectories. During encoder pre-training, we pre-train a Diffusion Policy to initialize the policy weights for fine-tuning, which is essential for improving fine-tuning sample efficiency and performance. After pre-training, we fine-tune the policy on a limited amount of task demonstrations using the learned image and action representations. We demonstrate that this pre-training and fine-tuning design substantially improves learning efficiency and policy performance on unseen tasks. Furthermore, we show that CLAMP outperforms state-of-the-art baselines across six simulated tasks and five real-world tasks.

AI.tldr: CLAMP is a 3D pre-training framework for robotic manipulation that uses point clouds and action-conditioned contrastive learning with multi-view re-rendered observations, plus diffusion-policy initialization, achieving improved sample efficiency and generalization across six simulated and five real-world tasks.
AI.motivation: 2D image representations lack essential 3D geometric information needed for precise manipulation; a geometry-aware pre-training approach could improve generalization and sim-to-real transfer in robotics.
AI.method: From RGB-D and camera extrinsics, a merged point cloud is used to re-render multi-view four-channel observations including depth and 3D coordinates (with dynamic wrist views). Encoders are trained via contrastive learning to align 3D geometry with robot action patterns using large-scale simulated trajectories. During pre-training, a Diffusion Policy is also pre-trained to initialize policy weights for fine-tuning. Fine-tuning is performed on limited task demonstrations using the learned image and action representations.
AI.result: The proposed pre-training + fine-tuning pipeline substantially improves learning efficiency and policy performance on unseen tasks and outperforms state-of-the-art baselines across six simulated tasks and five real-world tasks.
AI.conclusion: CLAMP demonstrates that 3D geometry-aware pretraining with action-conditioned contrastive learning and diffusion-policy initialization yields improved sample efficiency and generalization for robotic manipulation, effective in both simulation and real-world tasks.

title: Learning Modal-Mixed Chain-of-Thought Reasoning with Latent Embeddings
summary: We study how to extend chain-of-thought (CoT) beyond language to better handle multimodal reasoning. While CoT helps LLMs and VLMs articulate intermediate steps, its text-only form often fails on vision-intensive problems where key intermediate states are inherently visual. We introduce modal-mixed CoT, which interleaves textual tokens with compact visual sketches represented as latent embeddings. To bridge the modality gap without eroding the original knowledge and capability of the VLM, we use the VLM itself as an encoder and train the language backbone to reconstruct its own intermediate vision embeddings, to guarantee the semantic alignment of the visual latent space. We further attach a diffusion-based latent decoder, invoked by a special control token and conditioned on hidden states from the VLM. In this way, the diffusion head carries fine-grained perceptual details while the VLM specifies high-level intent, which cleanly disentangles roles and reduces the optimization pressure of the VLM. Training proceeds in two stages: supervised fine-tuning on traces that interleave text and latents with a joint next-token and latent-reconstruction objective, followed by reinforcement learning that teaches when to switch modalities and how to compose long reasoning chains. Extensive experiments across 11 diverse multimodal reasoning tasks, demonstrate that our method yields better performance than language-only and other CoT methods. Our code will be publicly released.

AI.tldr: Proposes modal-mixed CoT to handle multimodal reasoning by interleaving text with compact visual sketches represented as latent embeddings; uses a VLM as encoder to reconstruct its own vision embeddings; adds a diffusion-based latent decoder controlled by a special token; trained in supervised fine-tuning and reinforcement learning; reports improvements across 11 tasks.
AI.motivation: Standard chain-of-thought (CoT) works well for textual reasoning but struggles with vision-heavy problems where intermediate states are inherently visual. A multimodal CoT that integrates visual latent representations can better capture intermediate perceptual steps without losing the VLM's capabilities.
AI.method: Interleave textual tokens with compact visual sketches as latent embeddings. Use the VLM as an encoder and train the language backbone to reconstruct its own intermediate vision embeddings to align modalities. Attach a diffusion-based latent decoder invoked by a special control token and conditioned on VLM hidden states. Training proceeds in two stages: (i) supervised fine-tuning with joint next-token and latent-reconstruction objective on text+latents traces; (ii) reinforcement learning to learn when to switch modalities and how to compose long reasoning chains.
AI.result: Extensive experiments across 11 diverse multimodal reasoning tasks show better performance than language-only and other CoT methods.
AI.conclusion: Modal-mixed CoT effectively extends CoT to multimodal reasoning by cleanly separating high-level intent (VLM) and fine-grained perceptual detail (diffusion head), reducing optimization pressure on the VLM; code will be released.

title: Gauss-Newton Natural Gradient Descent for Shape Learning
comment: 16 Pages, 9 Figures, submitted to Computer-Aided Design
summary: We explore the use of the Gauss-Newton method for optimization in shape learning, including implicit neural surfaces and geometry-informed neural networks. The method addresses key challenges in shape learning, such as the ill-conditioning of the underlying differential constraints and the mismatch between the optimization problem in parameter space and the function space where the problem is naturally posed. This leads to significantly faster and more stable convergence than standard first-order methods, while also requiring far fewer iterations. Experiments across benchmark shape optimization tasks demonstrate that the Gauss-Newton method consistently improves both training speed and final solution accuracy.

AI.tldr: Gauss-Newton optimization improves shape-learning tasks by addressing ill-conditioning and space mismatch, achieving faster convergence and higher accuracy with fewer iterations.
AI.motivation: Shape-learning problems with implicit neural surfaces and geometry-informed networks suffer from ill-conditioned differential constraints and a mismatch between parameter-space optimization and the natural function-space formulation; first-order methods struggle.
AI.method: Apply Gauss-Newton to the optimization problem by framing residuals of constraints and linearizing via Gauss-Newton; address ill-conditioning; apply to shape-learning settings including implicit surfaces and geometry-informed nets; compare with standard first-order methods.
AI.result: Demonstrates significantly faster and more stable convergence, fewer iterations; training speed and final solution accuracy improve on benchmark shape-optimization tasks.
AI.conclusion: Gauss-Newton is effective for shape-learning optimization, offering robustness to ill-conditioning and space-mismatch; beneficial for implicit geometry representations and geometry-aware networks.

title: Meanshift Shape Formation Control Using Discrete Mass Distribution
summary: The density-distribution method has recently become a promising paradigm owing to its adaptability to variations in swarm size. However, existing studies face practical challenges in achieving complex shape representation and decentralized implementation. This motivates us to develop a fully decentralized, distribution-based control strategy with the dual capability of forming complex shapes and adapting to swarm-size variations. Specifically, we first propose a discrete mass-distribution function defined over a set of sample points to model swarm formation. In contrast to the continuous density-distribution method, our model eliminates the requirement for defining continuous density functions-a task that is difficult for complex shapes. Second, we design a decentralized meanshift control law to coordinate the swarm's global distribution to fit the sample-point distribution by feeding back mass estimates. The mass estimates for all sample points are achieved by the robots in a decentralized manner via the designed mass estimator. It is shown that the mass estimates of the sample points can asymptotically converge to the true global values. To validate the proposed strategy, we conduct comprehensive simulations and real-world experiments to evaluate the efficiency of complex shape formation and adaptability to swarm-size variations.

AI.tldr: A fully decentralized, discrete mass-distribution framework enables complex shape formation and adaptation to swarm size without relying on continuous densities, using a mean-shift control law and decentralized mass estimation with proven asymptotic convergence.
AI.motivation: Overcome practical limits of the continuous density-distribution approach in swarm robotics: difficulty representing complex shapes and implementing decentralized control, while needing robustness to varying swarm sizes.
AI.method: 1) Define a discrete mass-distribution over sample points to model swarm formation. 2) Develop a decentralized mean-shift control law that aligns the swarm's global distribution to the sample-point distribution using mass feedback. 3) Implement a decentralized mass estimator so all agents can obtain the masses of sample points. 4) Prove asymptotic convergence of sample-point mass estimates to true global values.
AI.result: Simulation and real-world experiments validate that the method achieves complex shape formation and adapts to swarm-size variations, with mass estimates converging to global values.
AI.conclusion: The proposed discrete, fully decentralized distribution-based control effectively enables complex shape formation and robustness to swarm-size changes, avoiding the difficulties of defining continuous density functions.

title: Small Shifts, Large Gains: Unlocking Traditional TSP Heuristic Guided-Sampling via Unsupervised Neural Instance Modification
summary: The Traveling Salesman Problem (TSP) is one of the most representative NP-hard problems in route planning and a long-standing benchmark in combinatorial optimization. Traditional heuristic tour constructors, such as Farthest or Nearest Insertion, are computationally efficient and highly practical, but their deterministic behavior limits exploration and often leads to local optima. In contrast, neural-based heuristic tour constructors alleviate this issue through guided-sampling and typically achieve superior solution quality, but at the cost of extensive training and reliance on ground-truth supervision, hindering their practical use. To bridge this gap, we propose TSP-MDF, a novel instance modification framework that equips traditional deterministic heuristic tour constructors with guided-sampling capability. Specifically, TSP-MDF introduces a neural-based instance modifier that strategically shifts node coordinates to sample multiple modified instances, on which the base traditional heuristic tour constructor constructs tours that are mapped back to the original instance, allowing traditional tour constructors to explore higher-quality tours and escape local optima. At the same time, benefiting from our instance modification formulation, the neural-based instance modifier can be trained efficiently without any ground-truth supervision, ensuring the framework maintains practicality. Extensive experiments on large-scale TSP benchmarks and real-world benchmarks demonstrate that TSP-MDF significantly improves the performance of traditional heuristics tour constructors, achieving solution quality comparable to neural-based heuristic tour constructors, but with an extremely short training time.

AI.tldr: TSP-MDF: an instance-modification framework that equips deterministic heuristics with guided sampling via a neural-based modifier. It trains without ground-truth supervision and maps modified tours back to the original instance, achieving neural-like quality with very short training times.
AI.motivation: Traditional heuristics are fast but prone to local optima; neural methods offer better solutions but require extensive supervision and training. The goal is a practical approach that blends exploration of neural methods with the efficiency of deterministic heuristics.
AI.method: Introduce a neural-based instance modifier that shifts node coordinates to generate multiple modified instances. The base traditional heuristic constructs tours on these modified instances, which are then mapped to the original instance. The modifier is trained efficiently without any ground-truth supervision.
AI.result: On large-scale and real-world TSP benchmarks, TSP-MDF substantially improves traditional heuristics, achieving solution quality comparable to neural-based methods while requiring extremely short training times.
AI.conclusion: The framework effectively aggregates the strengths of both paradigms, delivering practical, scalable improvements for TSP solving by enabling guided exploration of traditional scouts without heavy supervision.

title: THDC: Training Hyperdimensional Computing Models with Backpropagation
summary: Hyperdimensional computing (HDC) offers lightweight learning for energy-constrained devices by encoding data into high-dimensional vectors. However, its reliance on ultra-high dimensionality and static, randomly initialized hypervectors limits memory efficiency and learning capacity. Therefore, we propose Trainable Hyperdimensional Computing (THDC), which enables end-to-end HDC via backpropagation. THDC replaces randomly initialized vectors with trainable embeddings and introduces a one-layer binary neural network to optimize class representations. Evaluated on MNIST, Fashion-MNIST and CIFAR-10, THDC achieves equal or better accuracy than state-of-the-art HDC, with dimensionality reduced from 10.000 to 64.

AI.tldr: Trainable Hyperdimensional Computing (THDC) enables end-to-end HDC via backpropagation by learning embeddings and a simple one-layer binary neural network, reducing dimensionality from 10,000 to 64 while maintaining or improving accuracy on MNIST, Fashion-MNIST, and CIFAR-10.
AI.motivation: Overcomes memory inefficiency and limited learning capacity in traditional HDC caused by ultra-high-dimensional, statically initialized hypervectors.
AI.method: Replace random hypervectors with trainable embeddings and integrate a one-layer binary neural network to optimize class representations within an end-to-end training framework for HDC.
AI.result: THDC achieves equal or better accuracy than state-of-the-art HDC on MNIST, Fashion-MNIST and CIFAR-10, with a dramatic reduction in hypervector dimensionality (10,000 down to 64).
AI.conclusion: THDC makes hyperdimensional computing more practical for energy-constrained devices by enabling backpropagation-based training and substantial dimensionality reduction while preserving or improving performance.

title: Geometry-Aware Sampling-Based Motion Planning on Riemannian Manifolds
comment: Submitted to WAFR 2026 (17th World Symposium on the Algorithmic Foundations of Robotics (WAFR))
summary: In many robot motion planning problems, task objectives and physical constraints induce non-Euclidean geometry on the configuration space, yet many planners operate using Euclidean distances that ignore this structure. We address the problem of planning collision-free motions that minimize length under configuration-dependent Riemannian metrics, corresponding to geodesics on the configuration manifold. Conventional numerical methods for computing such paths do not scale well to high-dimensional systems, while sampling-based planners trade scalability for geometric fidelity. To bridge this gap, we propose a sampling-based motion planning framework that operates directly on Riemannian manifolds. We introduce a computationally efficient midpoint-based approximation of the Riemannian geodesic distance and prove that it matches the true Riemannian distance with third-order accuracy. Building on this approximation, we design a local planner that traces the manifold using first-order retractions guided by Riemannian natural gradients. Experiments on a two-link planar arm and a 7-DoF Franka manipulator under a kinetic-energy metric, as well as on rigid-body planning in $\mathrm{SE}(2)$ with non-holonomic motion constraints, demonstrate that our approach consistently produces lower-cost trajectories than Euclidean-based planners and classical numerical geodesic-solver baselines.

AI.tldr: Proposes a sampling-based planner that operates on configuration-space Riemannian manifolds, using a fast midpoint-based geodesic distance approximation with third-order accuracy to compute locally geodesic-like paths under configuration-dependent metrics, enabling lower-cost trajectories than Euclidean-planner baselines.
AI.motivation: Euclidean distance ignores configuration-space geometry induced by task constraints and physical laws; existing planners either ignore geometry (scaling with high dimension) or suffer from poor foresight. A scalable method that respects Riemannian geometry is needed to minimize path length under configuration-dependent metrics.
AI.method: Introduce a midpoint-based approximation of Riemannian geodesic distance with third-order accuracy. Develop a local planner that traces the manifold via first-order retractions guided by Riemannian natural gradients. Integrate this into a sampling-based planning framework that operates directly on Riemannian manifolds. Validate on a two-link planar arm and a 7-DoF Franka manipulator under a kinetic-energy metric, and SE(2) rigid-body planning with non-holonomic constraints.
AI.result: Consistently yields lower-cost trajectories than Euclidean-based planners and classical numerical geodesic solvers baselines across tested scenarios, demonstrating improved geometric fidelity and planning efficiency in high-dimensional, configuration-dependent metric spaces.
AI.conclusion: A practical bridge between sampling-based planning and Riemannian geometry: a fast geodesic-distance approximation and a manifold-aware local planner enable scalable, lower-cost planning under non-Euclidean configuration-space metrics.

title: Exploring Information Seeking Agent Consolidation
summary: Information-seeking agents have emerged as a powerful paradigm for solving knowledge-intensive tasks. Existing information-seeking agents are typically specialized for open web, documents, or local knowledge bases, which constrains scalability and cross-domain generalization. In this work, we investigate how to consolidate heterogeneous information-seeking agents into a single foundation agentic model. We study two complementary consolidation strategies: data-level consolidation, which jointly trains a unified model on a mixture of domain-specific datasets, and parameter-level consolidation, which merges independently trained agent models at the parameter level. Our analysis compares these approaches in terms of performance retention, cross-domain generalization, and interference across information-seeking behaviors. Our results show that data-level consolidation remains a strong and stable baseline, while parameter-level consolidation offers a promising, efficient alternative but suffers from interference and robustness challenges. We further identify key design factors for effective agent consolidation at the parameter level, including fine-grained merging granularity, awareness of task heterogeneity, and principled consensus strategy.

AI.tldr: Two consolidation strategies for information-seeking agents are compared: data-level (joint training on mixed domain datasets) and parameter-level (merging independently trained agents). Data-level consolidation is robust and strong; parameter-level offers efficiency but suffers from interference and robustness issues; design factors for effective parameter-level consolidation include fine-grained merging granularity, task heterogeneity awareness, and a principled consensus strategy.
AI.motivation: To enable a single foundation model to perform across diverse information-seeking tasks across domains, reducing fragmentation and improving cross-domain generalization.
AI.method: Evaluate data-level vs parameter-level consolidation; data-level trains a unified model on mixed datasets; parameter-level merges independently trained models at the parameter level; analyze performance retention, cross-domain generalization, interference across behaviors; identify design factors.
AI.result: Data-level consolidation provides a strong, stable baseline; parameter-level consolidation is efficient but prone to interference and robustness challenges; certain design factors mitigate issues but not fully resolve them.
AI.conclusion: Effective consolidation requires sensitive merging granularity, task heterogeneity awareness, and a principled consensus mechanism, with data-level consolidation serving as a reliable baseline and parameter-level consolidation as a promising but challenging alternative.

title: Predicting Mortgage Default with Machine Learning: AutoML, Class Imbalance, and Leakage Control
comment: 12 pages, 4 figures. An extended and pedagogical version will appear as a book chapter
summary: Mortgage default prediction is a core task in financial risk management, and machine learning models are increasingly used to estimate default probabilities and provide interpretable signals for downstream decisions. In real-world mortgage datasets, however, three factors frequently undermine evaluation validity and deployment reliability: ambiguity in default labeling, severe class imbalance, and information leakage arising from temporal structure and post-event variables. We compare multiple machine learning approaches for mortgage default prediction using a real-world loan-level dataset, with emphasis on leakage control and imbalance handling. We employ leakage-aware feature selection, a strict temporal split that constrains both origination and reporting periods, and controlled downsampling of the majority class. Across multiple positive-to-negative ratios, performance remains stable, and an AutoML approach (AutoGluon) achieves the strongest AUROC among the models evaluated. An extended and pedagogical version of this work will appear as a book chapter.

AI.tldr: Comparison of ML models for mortgage default prediction with leakage control and imbalance handling; AutoGluon performs best in AUROC under temporal split and downsampling; results stable across class ratios; pedagogical extension planned.
AI.motivation: Address evaluation validity in real-world mortgage data by mitigating default-label ambiguity, severe class imbalance, and information leakage from temporal structure and post-event variables; provide robust, leakage-aware modeling guidance for risk management.
AI.method: Leakage-aware feature selection; strict temporal split restricting origination and reporting periods; controlled downsampling of the majority class; evaluation across multiple positive-to-negative ratios; comparison of multiple ML approaches using a real-world loan-level dataset; AutoML via AutoGluon.
AI.result: Performance remains stable across varying class ratios; AutoGluon achieves the strongest AUROC among evaluated models.
AI.conclusion: An extended and pedagogical version of this work is planned for a book chapter.

title: HERMES: A Holistic End-to-End Risk-Aware Multimodal Embodied System with Vision-Language Models for Long-Tail Autonomous Driving
summary: End-to-end autonomous driving models increasingly benefit from large vision--language models for semantic understanding, yet ensuring safe and accurate operation under long-tail conditions remains challenging. These challenges are particularly prominent in long-tail mixed-traffic scenarios, where autonomous vehicles must interact with heterogeneous road users, including human-driven vehicles and vulnerable road users, under complex and uncertain conditions. This paper proposes HERMES, a holistic risk-aware end-to-end multimodal driving framework designed to inject explicit long-tail risk cues into trajectory planning. HERMES employs a foundation-model-assisted annotation pipeline to produce structured Long-Tail Scene Context and Long-Tail Planning Context, capturing hazard-centric cues together with maneuver intent and safety preference, and uses these signals to guide end-to-end planning. HERMES further introduces a Tri-Modal Driving Module that fuses multi-view perception, historical motion cues, and semantic guidance, ensuring risk-aware accurate trajectory planning under long-tail scenarios. Experiments on the real-world long-tail dataset demonstrate that HERMES consistently outperforms representative end-to-end and VLM-driven baselines under long-tail mixed-traffic scenarios. Ablation studies verify the complementary contributions of key components.

AI.tldr: HERMES is a holistic, risk-aware end-to-end multimodal driving framework that injects explicit long-tail risk cues into trajectory planning, using a foundation-model-assisted annotation pipeline and a Tri-Modal Driving Module to fuse perception, motion history, and semantic guidance, achieving superior performance on long-tail mixed-traffic data.
AI.motivation: Current end-to-end driving models struggle under long-tail mixed-traffic conditions with rare but safety-critical scenarios. There is a need to systematically capture hazard-centric cues and planning intents to ensure safe, accurate trajectories in uncertain environments.
AI.method: Proposes HERMES with (1) a foundation-model-assisted annotation pipeline to construct Long-Tail Scene Context and Long-Tail Planning Context (hazard cues, maneuver intent, safety preferences); (2) a Tri-Modal Driving Module that fuses multi-view perception, historical motion cues, and semantic guidance to guide end-to-end planning; and (3) training and inference pipelines that integrate these signals into trajectory planning.
AI.result: Experiments on a real-world long-tail dataset show HERMES consistently outperforms representative end-to-end and VLM-driven baselines in long-tail mixed-traffic scenarios; ablations confirm the complementary contributions of key components.
AI.conclusion: HERMES enables risk-aware, accurate trajectory planning under long-tail conditions by explicitly injecting hazard-centric cues and leveraging multimodal signals, improving safety and reliability in complex driving environments.

title: DockSmith: Scaling Reliable Coding Environments via an Agentic Docker Builder
summary: Reliable Docker-based environment construction is a dominant bottleneck for scaling execution-grounded training and evaluation of software engineering agents. We introduce DockSmith, a specialized agentic Docker builder designed to address this challenge. DockSmith treats environment construction not only as a preprocessing step, but as a core agentic capability that exercises long-horizon tool use, dependency reasoning, and failure recovery, yielding supervision that transfers beyond Docker building itself. DockSmith is trained on large-scale, execution-grounded Docker-building trajectories produced by a SWE-Factory-style pipeline augmented with a loop-detection controller and a cross-task success memory. Training a 30B-A3B model on these trajectories achieves open-source state-of-the-art performance on Multi-Docker-Eval, with 39.72% Fail-to-Pass and 58.28% Commit Rate. Moreover, DockSmith improves out-of-distribution performance on SWE-bench Verified, SWE-bench Multilingual, and Terminal-Bench 2.0, demonstrating broader agentic benefits of environment construction.

AI.tldr: DockSmith reframes environment construction as a core agentic capability and achieves state-of-the-art performance on Docker-based evaluation benchmarks using a large, execution-grounded 30B model trained on trajectory data.
AI.motivation: Reliable, scalable environment construction is a bottleneck for execution-grounded training of software engineering agents; current work treats it as preprocessing and limits long-horizon tool use and failure recovery.
AI.method: Train a 30B-A3B model on execution-grounded Docker-building trajectories derived from a SWE-Factory-style pipeline, enhanced with a loop-detection controller and a cross-task success memory to support long-horizon reasoning and recovery.
AI.result: Open-source state-of-the-art on Multi-Docker-Eval with 39.72% Fail-to-Pass and 58.28% Commit Rate; notable out-of-distribution gains on SWE-bench Verified, SWE-bench Multilingual, and Terminal-Bench 2.0.
AI.conclusion: Environment construction can be a generalizable, agentic capability that yields broader benefits beyond Docker building, enabling improved tool use, dependency reasoning, and failure recovery across tasks.

title: MiniTensor: A Lightweight, High-Performance Tensor Operations Library
summary: We present MiniTensor, an open source tensor operations library that focuses on minimalism, correctness, and performance. MiniTensor exposes a familiar PyTorch-like Python API while it executes performance critical code in a Rust engine. The core supports dense $n$ dimensional tensors, broadcasting, reductions, matrix multiplication, reverse mode automatic differentiation, a compact set of neural network layers, and standard optimizers. In this paper, we describe the design of MiniTensor's architecture, including its efficient memory management, dynamic computation graph for gradients, and integration with Python via PyO3. We also compare the install footprint with PyTorch and TensorFlow to demonstrate that MiniTensor achieves a package size of only a few megabytes, several orders of magnitude smaller than mainstream frameworks, while preserving the essentials needed for research and development on CPUs. The repository can be found at https://github.com/neuralsorcerer/minitensor

AI.tldr: MiniTensor is a minimal, correct, high-performance tensor library with a PyTorch-like Python API backed by a Rust engine; it supports dense tensors, broadcasting, reductions, matrix multiplication, reverse-mode automatic differentiation, a compact set of neural network layers, and standard optimizers, with a tiny install footprint and Python integration via PyO3, aimed at CPU-based research.
AI.motivation: There is a need for a lightweight, correct, and easy-to-use tensor framework that preserves essential features for research while minimizing install size, bridging Python usability with a high-performance Rust backend.
AI.method: Design and implement a Rust-backed engine for core tensor operations, expose a PyTorch-like Python API via PyO3, implement dynamic computation graphs for gradients, efficient memory management, and a compact set of neural network components and optimizers, then compare install footprint with PyTorch and TensorFlow.
AI.result: A new library, MiniTensor, providing core tensor operations, autograd, simple neural network layers, and optimizers with a much smaller install footprint (a few MBs) compared to mainstream frameworks, suitable for CPU-based research and development.
AI.conclusion: MiniTensor delivers a lightweight, correct, and performant tensor framework with essential features for CPU-centric research, achieving a substantially smaller footprint while maintaining usability and extensibility.

title: Offline Discovery of Interpretable Skills from Multi-Task Trajectories
summary: Hierarchical Imitation Learning is a powerful paradigm for acquiring complex robot behaviors from demonstrations. A central challenge, however, lies in discovering reusable skills from long-horizon, multi-task offline data, especially when the data lacks explicit rewards or subtask annotations. In this work, we introduce LOKI, a three-stage end-to-end learning framework designed for offline skill discovery and hierarchical imitation. The framework commences with a two-stage, weakly supervised skill discovery process: Stage one performs coarse, task-aware macro-segmentation by employing an alignment-enforced Vector Quantized VAE guided by weak task labels. Stage two then refines these segments at a micro-level using a self-supervised sequential model, followed by an iterative clustering process to consolidate skill boundaries. The third stage then leverages these precise boundaries to construct a hierarchical policy within an option-based framework-complete with a learned termination condition beta for explicit skill switching. LOKI achieves high success rates on the challenging D4RL Kitchen benchmark and outperforms standard HIL baselines. Furthermore, we demonstrate that the discovered skills are semantically meaningful, aligning with human intuition, and exhibit compositionality by successfully sequencing them to solve a novel, unseen task.

AI.tldr: LOKI is a three-stage offline hierarchical imitation learning framework that discovers reusable skills from long-horizon, multi-task data without rewards, via (1) alignment-guided VQ-VAE macro-segmentation with weak labels, (2) self-supervised micro-segmentation and iterative clustering, and (3) an option-based hierarchical policy with learned termination. It achieves strong results on D4RL Kitchen and shows semantically meaningful, composable skills.
AI.motivation: To address the difficulty of extracting reusable, semantically meaningful skills from long-horizon, multi-task offline demonstrations when explicit rewards or subtask annotations are absent.
AI.method: Stage 1: coarse macro-segmentation using an alignment-enforced Vector Quantized VAE guided by weak task labels. Stage 2: micro-segmentation via a self-supervised sequential model with iterative clustering to refine skill boundaries. Stage 3: construct a hierarchical policy in an option-based framework with a learned termination condition beta for explicit skill switching. The approach is end-to-end learned.
AI.result: LOKI achieves high success rates on the D4RL Kitchen benchmark and outperforms standard HIL baselines. The discovered skills are semantically meaningful, align with human intuition, and demonstrate compositionality by solving a novel, unseen task.
AI.conclusion: LOKI provides a practical, end-to-end solution for offline skill discovery and hierarchical imitation by combining weakly supervised macro-segmentation, self-supervised micro-segmentation, and an explicit, learned termination-based option framework, enabling reusable, composable skills.

title: Scalable Generative Game Engine: Breaking the Resolution Wall via Hardware-Algorithm Co-Design
summary: Real-time generative game engines represent a paradigm shift in interactive simulation, promising to replace traditional graphics pipelines with neural world models. However, existing approaches are fundamentally constrained by the ``Memory Wall,'' restricting practical deployments to low resolutions (e.g., $64 \times 64$). This paper bridges the gap between generative models and high-resolution neural simulations by introducing a scalable \textit{Hardware-Algorithm Co-Design} framework. We identify that high-resolution generation suffers from a critical resource mismatch: the World Model is compute-bound while the Decoder is memory-bound. To address this, we propose a heterogeneous architecture that intelligently decouples these components across a cluster of AI accelerators. Our system features three core innovations: (1) an asymmetric resource allocation strategy that optimizes throughput under sequence parallelism constraints; (2) a memory-centric operator fusion scheme that minimizes off-chip bandwidth usage; and (3) a manifold-aware latent extrapolation mechanism that exploits temporal redundancy to mask latency. We validate our approach on a cluster of programmable AI accelerators, enabling real-time generation at $720 \times 480$ resolution -- a $50\times$ increase in pixel throughput over prior baselines. Evaluated on both continuous 3D racing and discrete 2D platformer benchmarks, our system delivers fluid 26.4 FPS and 48.3 FPS respectively, with an amortized effective latency of 2.7 ms. This work demonstrates that resolving the ``Memory Wall'' via architectural co-design is not merely an optimization, but a prerequisite for enabling high-fidelity, responsive neural gameplay.

AI.tldr: Hardware-algorithm co-design enables high-resolution real-time neural world generation by decoupling compute-bound world models from memory-bound decoders across accelerators, achieving 720x480 real-time generation with substantially higher throughput and low latency.
AI.motivation: Real-time neural gameplay at high resolution is blocked by the Memory Wall; current approaches cap at low-res; a scalable architecture and techniques are needed to hide latency.
AI.method: Proposed a heterogeneous, clustered accelerator architecture with (1) asymmetric resource allocation under sequence parallelism, (2) memory-centric operator fusion to minimize off-chip bandwidth, (3) manifold-aware latent extrapolation to exploit temporal redundancy; decouples World Model and Decoder across accelerators.
AI.result: Achieves real-time generation at 720x480, ~50x pixel throughput over prior baselines; 26.4 FPS and 48.3 FPS on continuous 3D racing and discrete 2D platformer; amortized latency 2.7 ms; validated on a programmable AI accelerator cluster.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates that resolving the Memory Wall via hardware-algorithm co-design is essential to enable high-fidelity, responsive neural gameplay; the proposed approach makes high-resolution neural simulations feasible in real time.

title: ALIGN: Aligned Delegation with Performance Guarantees for Multi-Agent LLM Reasoning
summary: LLMs often underperform on complex reasoning tasks when relying on a single generation-and-selection pipeline.
