Using Gemini 3.5 Pro, SkySim translates user commands (e.g., "Form a circle") into spatial waypoints, informed by real-time drone states. An Artificial Potential Field (APF) safety filter applies minimal adjustments for collision avoidance, kinematic limits, and geo-fencing, ensuring feasible execution at 20 Hz. Experiments with swarms of 3, 10, and 30 Crazyflie drones validate spatial reasoning accuracy (100% across tested geometric primitives), real-time collision prevention, and scalability. SkySim empowers non-experts to iteratively refine behaviors, bridging AI cognition with robotic safety for dynamic environments. Future work targets hardware integration.

AI.tldr: SkySim is a ROS2/Gazebo framework that uses LLM-based high-level planning for UAV swarm control, with a real-time APF safety filter to enforce collision avoidance and constraints; demonstrates safe, feasible trajectories at 20 Hz in simulations up to 30 drones.
AI.motivation: To enable non-expert users to specify swarm behaviors through natural language while ensuring physical safety and feasibility, addressing the gap between AI planning and robotic safety.
AI.method: Decouples high-level planning from low-level safety. An LLM (Gemini 3.5 Pro) translates commands into spatial waypoints based on real-time drone states. An Artificial Potential Field safety filter enforces collision avoidance, kinematic limits, and geo-fencing, updating at 20 Hz within a ROS2/Gazebo framework using Crazyflie swarms.
AI.result: Experiments with 3, 10, and 30 drones show 100% spatial reasoning accuracy for tested geometric primitives, real-time collision prevention, and scalability.
AI.conclusion: SkySim enables non-experts to iteratively refine swarm behaviors by marrying AI planning with safety guarantees, bridging cognition and robotic safety in dynamic environments; future work will focus on hardware integration.

title: Neuro-symbolic AI for Predictive Maintenance (PdM) -- review and recommendations
summary: In this document we perform a systematic review the State-of-the-art in Predictive Maintenance (PdM) over the last five years in industrial settings such as commercial buildings, pharmaceutical facilities, or semi-conductor manufacturing. In general, data-driven methods such as those based on deep learning, exhibit higher accuracy than traditional knowledge-based systems. These systems however, are not without significant limitations. The need for large labeled data sets, a lack of generalizibility to new environments (out-of-distribution generalization), and a lack of transparency at inference time are some of the obstacles to adoption in real world environments. In contrast, traditional approaches based on domain expertise in the form of rules, logic or first principles suffer from poor accuracy, many false positives and a need for ongoing expert supervision and manual tuning. While the majority of approaches in recent literature utilize some form of data-driven architecture, there are hybrid systems which also take into account domain specific knowledge. Such hybrid systems have the potential to overcome the weaknesses of either approach on its own while preserving their strengths. We propose taking the hybrid approach even further and integrating deep learning with symbolic logic, or Neuro-symbolic AI, to create more accurate, explainable, interpretable, and robust systems. We describe several neuro-symbolic architectures and examine their strengths and limitations within the PdM domain. We focus specifically on methods which involve the use of sensor data and manually crafted rules as inputs by describing concrete NeSy architectures. In short, this survey outlines the context of modern maintenance, defines key concepts, establishes a generalized framework, reviews current modeling approaches and challenges, and introduces the proposed focus on Neuro-symbolic AI (NESY).

AI.tldr: A five-year systematic review of predictive maintenance (PdM) in industrial settings shows data-driven methods generally outperform traditional rule-based approaches but face data, generalization, and transparency limitations. Hybrid and neuro-symbolic AI (NESY) methods are highlighted as promising to combine accuracy with interpretability and robustness. The paper defines a generalized framework, surveys current approaches, and advocates NESY as a focus to advance PdM.
AI.motivation: Address the limitations of purely data-driven PdM (data hunger, OOD generalization, lack of interpretability) and the poor performance of traditional rule-based approaches. Propose NESY as a means to achieve accurate, explainable, robust PdM in real-world environments.
AI.method: Systematic literature review of the state-of-the-art PdM literature from the last five years in industrial settings. Describes and analyzes neuro-symbolic architectures that use sensor data and manually crafted rules, and proposes a generalized framework for NESY in PdM.
AI.result: Finds that deep learning-based PdM offers higher accuracy but struggles with data requirements, generalization, and transparency; traditional approaches have lower accuracy and require ongoing supervision. Hybrid systems are common and NESY is presented as a path to improve explainability, interpretability, and robustness. The survey defines key concepts, provides a generalized framework, reviews current modeling approaches and challenges, and introduces NESY as the focal point for future PdM research.
AI.conclusion: Neuro-symbolic AI integration is a promising direction to enhance PdM by combining data-driven accuracy with symbolic reasoning for explainability and robustness. The paper calls for further exploration of NESY architectures, framework development, and assessment of their practical viability in industrial PdM deployments.

title: The Illusion of Forgetting: Attack Unlearned Diffusion via Initial Latent Variable Optimization
summary: Although unlearning-based defenses claim to purge Not-Safe-For-Work (NSFW) concepts from diffusion models (DMs), we reveals that this "forgetting" is largely an illusion. Unlearning partially disrupts the mapping between linguistic symbols and the underlying knowledge, which remains intact as dormant memories. We find that the distributional discrepancy in the denoising process serves as a measurable indicator of how much of the mapping is retained, also reflecting the strength of unlearning. Inspired by this, we propose IVO (Initial Latent Variable Optimization), a concise and powerful attack framework that reactivates these dormant memories by reconstructing the broken mappings. Through Image Inversion}, Adversarial Optimization and Reused Attack, IVO optimizes initial latent variables to realign the noise distribution of unlearned models with their original unsafe states. Extensive experiments across 8 widely used unlearning techniques demonstrate that IVO achieves superior attack success rates and strong semantic consistency, exposing fundamental flaws in current defenses. The code is available at anonymous.4open.science/r/IVO/. Warning: This paper has unsafe images that may offend some readers.

AI.tldr: IVO reveals that unlearning in diffusion models leaves dormant memories; it reactivates them via latent optimization, beating 8 defenses with higher attack success and semantic coherence.
AI.motivation: Unlearning-based defenses aim to purge NSFW concepts from diffusion models, but evidence suggests forgetting is incomplete. There is a need to evaluate robustness and develop stronger attacks/defenses.
AI.method: IVO: Initial Latent Variable Optimization. It combines Image Inversion, Adversarial Optimization, and Reused Attack to optimize initial latent variables, re-aligning the noise distribution of unlearned models with their original unsafe states. It reconstructs broken mappings to reactivate dormant memories.
AI.result: IVO achieves superior attack success rates and strong semantic consistency across eight unlearning techniques, exposing fundamental flaws in current defenses.
AI.conclusion: Current unlearning defenses are insufficient to purge NSFW concepts from diffusion models; robust defenses or detection methods are required to address dormant memory mappings.

title: Reinforcement Learning for Active Perception in Autonomous Navigation
comment: Accepted to the IEEE International Conference on Robotics and Automation (ICRA) 2026
summary: This paper addresses the challenge of active perception within autonomous navigation in complex, unknown environments. Revisiting the foundational principles of active perception, we introduce an end-to-end reinforcement learning framework in which a robot must not only reach a goal while avoiding obstacles, but also actively control its onboard camera to enhance situational awareness. The policy receives observations comprising the robot state, the current depth frame, and a particularly local geometry representation built from a short history of depth readings. To couple collision-free motion planning with information-driven active camera control, we augment the navigation reward with a voxel-based information metric. This enables an aerial robot to learn a robust policy that balances goal-directed motion with exploratory sensing. Extensive evaluation demonstrates that our strategy achieves safer flight compared to using fixed, non-actuated camera baselines while also inducing intrinsic exploratory behaviors.

AI.tldr: End-to-end RL for active perception in aerial navigation; jointly learns goal-directed motion and onboard camera control using depth input and a voxel-based information gain reward.
AI.motivation: Active perception in unknown, cluttered environments requires balancing safe navigation with information gathering; camera-actuation can enhance situational awareness beyond fixed cameras.
AI.method: An end-to-end reinforcement learning framework where observations include robot state, depth frames, and a short history-based local geometry; policy also controls the onboard camera; navigation reward is augmented with a voxel-based information gain metric to couple planning with sensing.
AI.result: The learned policy achieves safer flights than fixed, non-actuated camera baselines and exhibits intrinsic exploratory sensing behaviors.
AI.conclusion: Information-aware, end-to-end policies can effectively balance goal-directed motion with active sensing, improving safety and situational awareness in autonomous aerial navigation.

title: Engineering AI Agents for Clinical Workflows: A Case Study in Architecture,MLOps, and Governance
comment: 9 pages, 5 figures 2026 IEEE/ACM 5th International Conference on AI Engineering - Software Engineering for AI}{April 12--13, 2026}{Rio de Janeiro, Brazil
summary:
The integration of Artificial Intelligence (AI) into clinical settings presents a software engineering challenge, demanding a shift from isolated models to robust, governable, and reliable systems. However, brittle, prototype-derived architectures often plague industrial applications and a lack of systemic oversight, creating a ``responsibility vacuum'' where safety and accountability are compromised. This paper presents an industry case study of the ``Maria'' platform, a production-grade AI system in primary healthcare that addresses this gap.
  Our central hypothesis is that trustworthy clinical AI is achieved through the holistic integration of four foundational engineering pillars. We present a synergistic architecture that combines Clean Architecture for maintainability with an Event-driven architecture for resilience and auditability. We introduce the Agent as the primary unit of modularity, each possessing its own autonomous MLOps lifecycle. Finally, we show how a Human-in-the-Loop governance model is technically integrated not merely as a safety check, but as a critical, event-driven data source for continuous improvement. We present the platform as a reference architecture, offering practical lessons for engineers building maintainable, scalable, and accountable AI-enabled systems in high-stakes domains.

AI.tldr: A case-study-driven blueprint for trustworthy clinical AI (Maria) built on four pillars: Clean Architecture, Event-driven design, Agent-level autonomous MLOps, and Human-in-the-Loop governance to ensure safety, auditability, and accountability in healthcare AI.
AI.motivation: Address the software-engineering gap in production AI for healthcare by moving from brittle prototypes to robust, governable, and auditable systems, mitigating a “responsibility vacuum” in high-stakes domains.
AI.method: Industry case study and architectural proposal outlining a reference architecture. The paper describes the integration of Clean Architecture with an Event-driven pattern, introduces Agent as the unit of modularity with autonomous MLOps lifecycles, and embeds Human-in-the-Loop governance as an event-driven data source for continuous improvement.
AI.result: A reference architecture and practical lessons showing how to build maintainable, scalable, and accountable AI-enabled healthcare systems by combining the four pillars into a cohesive design.
AI.conclusion: Trustworthy clinical AI arises from the holistic integration of the four pillars; the Maria platform provides a blueprint for engineering practice in high-stakes domains and underscores the role of governance and lifecycle autonomy in continuous improvement.

title: How Understanding Forecast Uncertainty Resolves the Explainability Problem in Machine Learning Models
summary: For applications of machine learning in critical decisions, explainability is a primary concern, and often a regulatory requirement. Local linear methods for generating explanations, such as LIME and SHAP, have been criticized for being unstable near decision boundaries. In this paper, we explain that such concerns reflect a misunderstanding of the problem. The forecast uncertainty is high at decision boundaries, so consequently, the explanatory instability is high. The correct approach is to change the sequence of events and questions being asked. Nonlinear models can be highly predictive in some regions while having little or no predictability in others. Therefore, the first question is whether a usable forecast exists. When there is a forecast with low enough uncertainty to be useful, an explanation can be sought via a local linear approximation. In such cases, the explanatory instability is correspondingly low. When no usable forecast exists, the decision must fall to a simpler overall model such as traditional logistic regression. Additionally, these results show that some methods that purport to be explainable everywhere, such as ReLU networks or any piecewise linear model, have only an illusory explainability, because the forecast uncertainty at the segment boundaries is too high to be useful. Explaining an unusable forecast is pointless.

AI.tldr: Explainability should be conditioned on forecast usability: local explanations are meaningful only where forecast uncertainty is low; near decision boundaries uncertainty is high, so explanatory instability is expected; if no usable forecast exists, revert to simpler global models; many so-called universally explainable models (e.g., ReLU networks) have illusory explainability due to boundary uncertainty.
AI.motivation: Address regulatory demands for explainability and common criticisms of local explanation methods (LIME/SHAP) by reframing explainability as contingent on forecast usefulness and uncertainty; provide a practical decision framework.
AI.method: Conceptual/theoretical analysis that partitions problem space by forecast uncertainty; proposes a workflow: first assess whether a usable forecast exists; if yes, apply local linear explanations; if not, adopt a simpler global model; critique of piecewise-linear, ReLU-based approaches as providing illusory explanations.
AI.result: Clarifies that instability of local explanations (LIME/SHAP) reflects intrinsic forecast uncertainty near decision boundaries; when usefully forecastable regions exist, local explanations are stable; when not, explanations are moot and a simpler model is preferred; challenges claims of universal explainability of piecewise-linear models.
AI.conclusion: Explainability is not universal; it should be conditional on forecast usefulness. Use local explanations only where the forecast uncertainty is sufficiently low, and fallback to simpler global models otherwise. ReLU/PLN explanations at boundaries are not truly explanatory due to high uncertainty.

title: TriphiBot: A Triphibious Robot Combining FOC-based Propulsion with Eccentric Design
summary: Triphibious robots capable of multi-domain motion and cross-domain transitions are promising to handle complex tasks across diverse environments. However, existing designs primarily focus on dual-mode platforms, and some designs suffer from high mechanical complexity or low propulsion efficiency, which limits their application. In this paper, we propose a novel triphibious robot capable of aerial, terrestrial, and aquatic motion, by a minimalist design combining a quadcopter structure with two passive wheels, without extra actuators. To address inefficiency of ground-support motion (moving on land/seabed) for quadcopter based designs, we introduce an eccentric Center of Gravity (CoG) design that inherently aligns thrust with motion, enhancing efficiency without specialized mechanical transformation designs. Furthermore, to address the drastic differences in motion control caused by different fluids (air and water), we develop a unified propulsion system based on Field-Oriented Control (FOC). This method resolves torque matching issues and enables precise, rapid bidirectional thrust across different mediums. Grounded in the perspective of living condition and ground support, we analyse the robot's dynamics and propose a Hybrid Nonlinear Model Predictive Control (HNMPC)-PID control system to ensure stable multi-domain motion and seamless transitions. Experimental results validate the robot's multi-domain motion and cross-mode transition capability, along with the efficiency and adaptability of the proposed propulsion system.

AI.tldr: A minimalist triphibious quadcopter with two passive wheels enabling aerial, terrestrial, and aquatic motion via eccentric CoG for ground efficiency, a unified FOC propulsion for air/water, and Hybrid Nonlinear Model Predictive Control (HNMPC)-PID for stable multi-domain control; experiments confirm cross-domain transitions and efficiency.
AI.motivation: Address the need for true triphibious robots with low mechanical complexity and high propulsion efficiency, overcoming limitations of existing dual-mode designs that rely on complex mechanisms or suffer inefficiencies.
AI.method: Design a quadcopter-based frame with two passive wheels, implement an eccentric center of gravity to align thrust with motion for ground motion without extra actuators, use a unified field-oriented control (FOC) propulsion system to operate across air and water, and employ a Hybrid Nonlinear Model Predictive Control (HNMPC) plus PID loop to ensure stable multi-domain motion and seamless transitions.
AI.result: Experimental results validate multi-domain motion and cross-mode transitions; the propulsion system demonstrates efficiency and adaptability across air and water.
AI.conclusion: The study presents a minimalistic, efficient triphibious platform capable of aerial, terrestrial, and aquatic operation with seamless cross-domain transitions, enabled by an eccentric CoG design, unified FOC propulsion, and HNMPC-PID control.

title: Environment-Aware Adaptive Pruning with Interleaved Inference Orchestration for Vision-Language-Action Models
summary: While Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models hold promise in embodied intelligence, their large parameter counts lead to substantial inference latency that hinders real-time manipulation, motivating parameter sparsification. However, as the environment evolves during VLA execution, the optimal sparsity patterns change accordingly. Static pruning lacks the adaptability required for environment dynamics, whereas fixed-interval dynamic layer pruning suffers from coarse granularity and high retraining overheads. To bridge this gap, we propose EcoVLA, a training-free, plug-and-play adaptive pruning framework that supports orthogonal combination with existing VLA acceleration methods. EcoVLA comprises two components: Environment-aware Adaptive Pruning (EAP) and Interleaved Inference Orchestration ($I^2O$). EAP is a lightweight adaptive channel pruning method that incorporates the temporal consistency of the physical environment to update sparsity patterns. $I^2O$ leverages the FLOPs bubbles inherent in VLA inference to schedule the pruning method in parallel, ensuring negligible impact on latency. Evaluated on diverse VLA models and benchmarks, EcoVLA delivers state-of-the-art performance, achieving up to 1.60$\times$ speedup with only a 0.4% drop in success rate, and further reaches 2.18$\times$ speedup with only a 0.5% degradation when combined with token pruning. We further validate the effectiveness of EcoVLA on real-world robots.

AI.tldr: EcoVLA introduces a training-free, adaptive pruning framework for Vision-Language-Action models to handle environment dynamics with two components: Environment-aware Adaptive Pruning (EAP) and Interleaved Inference Orchestration (I^2O). It achieves substantial latency reductions with minimal accuracy loss and is compatible with existing acceleration methods and real-world robotic deployments.
AI.motivation: VLA models have large parameter counts causing high inference latency. Static pruning or coarse-grained dynamic pruning fail to adapt to evolving environments during execution, necessitating a training-free, adaptable pruning approach that can be integrated with current acceleration techniques.
AI.method: EAP is a lightweight, environment-consistent adaptive channel pruning method that updates sparsity patterns with temporal environment information. I^2O exploits FLOPs bubbles in VLA inference to interleave pruning scheduling in parallel, minimizing latency impact. The framework is designed to be plug-and-play and training-free, and orthogonal to existing VLA acceleration methods.
AI.result: EcoVLA delivers up to 1.60× speedup with only a 0.4% drop in success rate, and up to 2.18× speedup with a 0.5% degradation when combined with token pruning, validated on diverse VLA models and benchmarks and demonstrated on real-world robots.
AI.conclusion: EcoVLA provides a training-free, adaptable pruning solution for VLA that preserves latency while maintaining performance, is compatible with other acceleration techniques, and is validated in simulation and real-world robotic tasks.

title: GEPC: Group-Equivariant Posterior Consistency for Out-of-Distribution Detection in Diffusion Models
summary: Diffusion models learn a time-indexed score field $\mathbf{s}_θ(\mathbf{x}_t,t)$ that often inherits approximate equivariances (flips, rotations, circular shifts) from in-distribution (ID) data and convolutional backbones. Most diffusion-based out-of-distribution (OOD) detectors exploit score magnitude or local geometry (energies, curvature, covariance spectra) and largely ignore equivariances. We introduce Group-Equivariant Posterior Consistency (GEPC), a training-free probe that measures how consistently the learned score transforms under a finite group $\mathcal{G}$, detecting equivariance breaking even when score magnitude remains unchanged. At the population level, we propose the ideal GEPC residual, which averages an equivariance-residual functional over $\mathcal{G}$, and we derive ID upper bounds and OOD lower bounds under mild assumptions. GEPC requires only score evaluations and produces interpretable equivariance-breaking maps. On OOD image benchmark datasets, we show that GEPC achieves competitive or improved AUROC compared to recent diffusion-based baselines while remaining computationally lightweight. On high-resolution synthetic aperture radar imagery where OOD corresponds to targets or anomalies in clutter, GEPC yields strong target-background separation and visually interpretable equivariance-breaking maps. Code is available at https://github.com/RouzAY/gepc-diffusion/.

AI.tldr: GEPC is a training-free probe that detects equivariance breaking in diffusion models by checking how the learned time-indexed score transforms under a finite group; it can detect violations even when score magnitude is unchanged, provides theoretical ID/OOD bounds, and yields interpretable maps; it is competitive on OOD benchmarks and useful on high-resolution SAR data; code available.
AI.motivation: Diffusion models inherit approximate equivariances from in-distribution data and convolutional backbones; existing OOD detectors focus on score magnitude or local geometry and overlook equivariance structure; there is a need for a training-free, interpretable method to quantify equivariance-consistency and localize violations.
AI.method: Define GEPC as a training-free equivariance-consistency probe. For a finite group G, compute an equivariance-residual functional that measures how the score s_theta(x_t,t) fails to transform under group actions. Form the ideal GEPC residual by averaging this residual over G. Derive population-level bounds: ID upper bounds and OOD lower bounds under mild assumptions. GEPC requires only score evaluations, produces interpretable equivariance-breaking maps, and yields a residual-based visualization.
AI.result: On image OOD benchmarks, GEPC is competitive or improves AUROC relative to recent diffusion-based baselines while remaining computationally lightweight. On high-resolution synthetic aperture radar imagery, where OOD corresponds to targets/anomalies in clutter, GEPC achieves strong target-background separation and provides visually interpretable equivariance-breaking maps.
AI.conclusion: GEPC offers a principled, training-free tool to detect equivariance-breaking behavior in diffusion models, complementing magnitude/geometry-based OOD detectors. It provides interpretable maps, theoretical bounds, and practical performance gains, with open-source code.

title: Instance-Guided Unsupervised Domain Adaptation for Robotic Semantic Segmentation
summary: Semantic segmentation networks, which are essential for robotic perception, often suffer from performance degradation when the visual distribution of the deployment environment differs from that of the source dataset on which they were trained. Unsupervised Domain Adaptation (UDA) addresses this challenge by adapting the network to the robot's target environment without external supervision, leveraging the large amounts of data a robot might naturally collect during long-term operation. In such settings, UDA methods can exploit multi-view consistency across the environment's map to fine-tune the model in an unsupervised fashion and mitigate domain shift. However, these approaches remain sensitive to cross-view instance-level inconsistencies. In this work, we propose a method that starts from a volumetric 3D map to generate multi-view consistent pseudo-labels. We then refine these labels using the zero-shot instance segmentation capabilities of a foundation model, enforcing instance-level coherence. The refined annotations serve as supervision for self-supervised fine-tuning, enabling the robot to adapt its perception system at deployment time. Experiments on real-world data demonstrate that our approach consistently improves performance over state-of-the-art UDA baselines based on multi-view consistency, without requiring any ground-truth labels in the target domain.

AI.tldr: A self-supervised domain adaptation method for semantic segmentation that uses a volumetric 3D map to generate multi-view pseudo-labels, refines them with a foundation model's zero-shot instance segmentation to enforce instance-level coherence, and then fine-tunes the model at deployment time without target labels, outperforming multi-view UDA baselines.
AI.motivation: To mitigate performance degradation caused by domain shift in robotic perception by leveraging abundant unlabeled data and multi-view consistency, while addressing cross-view instance-level inconsistencies.
AI.method: Extract a volumetric 3D map to generate multi-view consistent pseudo-labels; refine these labels using zero-shot instance segmentation from a foundation model to enforce instance-level coherence; use the refined annotations for self-supervised fine-tuning during deployment.
AI.result: Real-world experiments show consistent improvements over state-of-the-art UDA baselines that rely on multi-view consistency, without requiring ground-truth labels in the target domain.
AI.conclusion: The approach enables deployment-time adaptation of semantic segmentation by combining 3D-map-based pseudo-labeling with foundation-model refinement to improve robustness under domain shift without labeled target data.

title: World Models as an Intermediary between Agents and the Real World
summary: Large language model (LLM) agents trained using reinforcement learning has achieved superhuman performance in low-cost environments like games, mathematics, and coding. However, these successes have not translated to complex domains where the cost of interaction is high, such as the physical cost of running robots, the time cost of ML engineering, and the resource cost of scientific experiments. The true bottleneck for achieving the next level of agent performance for these complex and high-cost domains lies in the expense of executing actions to acquire reward signals. To address this gap, this paper argues that we should use world models as an intermediary between agents and the real world. We discuss how world models, viewed as models of dynamics, rewards, and task distributions, can overcome fundamental barriers of high-cost actions such as extreme off-policy learning and sample inefficiency in long-horizon tasks. Moreover, we demonstrate how world models can provide critical and rich learning signals to agents across a broad set of domains, including machine learning engineering, computer use, robotics, and AI for science. Lastly, we identify the challenges of building these world models and propose actionable items along dataset curation, architecture design, scaling, and evaluation of world models.

AI.tldr: World models as an intermediary between agents and the real world to enhance data efficiency and enable long-horizon, high-cost-domain RL; outlines benefits, challenges, and actionable items across domains.
AI.motivation: The bottleneck in achieving advanced agent performance in high-cost domains arises from the expense of actions and reward signals. The abstract argues that world models can bridge the gap by providing dynamics, rewards, and task distributions to improve sample efficiency and off-policy learning.
AI.method: Conceptual/analytic framework: define world models as representations of environment dynamics, rewards, and task distributions; analyze how such models can mitigate high-cost action barriers; discuss cross-domain applicability and provide actionable directions for dataset curation, architecture design, scaling, and evaluation.
AI.result: Argument that world models can offer rich learning signals and improve performance in domains with costly interactions; presents cross-domain applicability (ML engineering, computing, robotics, AI for science) and outlines practical challenges and guidance for implementation.
AI.conclusion: World models offer a promising middle layer to overcome data and interaction costs in high-stakes domains; further work is needed in dataset curation, architectural design, scaling, and robust evaluation to realize their potential.

title: Reducing Memorisation in Generative Models via Riemannian Bayesian Inference
summary: Modern generative models can produce realistic samples, however, balancing memorisation and generalisation remains an open problem. We approach this challenge from a Bayesian perspective by focusing on the parameter space of flow matching and diffusion models and constructing a predictive posterior that better captures the variability of the data distribution. In particular, we capture the geometry of the loss using a Riemannian metric and leverage a flexible approximate posterior that adapts to the local structure of the loss landscape. This approach allows us to sample generative models that resemble the original model, but exhibit reduced memorisation. Empirically, we demonstrate that the proposed approach reduces memorisation while preserving generalisation. Further, we provide a theoretical analysis of our method, which explains our findings. Overall, our work illustrates how considering the geometry of the loss enables effective use of the parameter space, even for complex high-dimensional generative models.

AI.tldr: A geometry-aware Bayesian posterior over flow matching and diffusion-model parameters using a Riemannian metric on the loss landscape yields samples with less memorisation while retaining generalisation; theory and empirical validation are provided.
AI.motivation: To address the memorisation-generalisation trade-off in modern high-dimensional generative models by using the geometry of the loss to better characterize the predictive posterior.
AI.method: Define a Riemannian metric on parameter space induced by the loss landscape to capture local geometry; construct a flexible approximate posterior that adapts to this structure; apply to flow matching and diffusion models to sample models with reduced memorisation; provide theoretical analysis.
AI.result: Empirically, memorisation is reduced without sacrificing generalisation; a theoretical framework explains these findings.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating loss-geometry into Bayesian treatment of generative model parameters can improve generalisation while mitigating memorisation in high-dimensional models; supports the utility of geometry-aware posterior inference.

title: Sem-NaVAE: Semantically-Guided Outdoor Mapless Navigation via Generative Trajectory Priors
summary: This work presents a mapless global navigation approach for outdoor applications. It combines the exploratory capacity of conditional variational autoencoders (CVAEs) to generate trajectories and the semantic segmentation capabilities of a lightweight visual language model (VLM) to select the trajectory to execute. Open-vocabulary segmentation is used to score and select the generated trajectories based on natural language, and a state-of-the-art local planner executes velocity commands. One of the key features of the proposed approach is its ability to generate a large variability of trajectories and to select them and navigate in real-time. The approach was validated through real-world outdoor navigation experiments, achieving superior performance compared to state-of-the-art methods. A video showing an experimental run of the system can be found in https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=i3R5ey5O2yk.

AI.tldr: Generates diverse CVAE-based trajectories for mapless outdoor navigation and uses open-vocabulary semantic segmentation via a lightweight VLM to select a trajectory based on natural language, with a local planner executing velocity commands; validated in real-world outdoors with superior performance.
AI.motivation: Need robust mapless navigation that leverages semantic understanding and diverse candidate trajectories for outdoor environments where maps are unavailable or outdated.
AI.method: CVAE-based trajectory generation to produce many candidate trajectories; open-vocabulary segmentation with lightweight VLM to score trajectories according to natural language; selection based on language-guided scoring; local planner executes velocity commands; real-time pipeline.
AI.result: Real-world outdoor experiments show superior performance vs state-of-the-art baselines; video available.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates feasibility of semantically guided, real-time mapless navigation with diverse trajectory generation; potential limitations to address and broader validation.

title: MissMAC-Bench: Building Solid Benchmark for Missing Modality Issue in Robust Multimodal Affective Computing
summary: As a knowledge discovery task over heterogeneous data sources, current Multimodal Affective Computing (MAC) heavily rely on the completeness of multiple modalities to accurately understand human's affective state. However, in real-world scenarios, the availability of modality data is often dynamic and uncertain, leading to substantial performance fluctuations due to the distribution shifts and semantic deficiencies of the incomplete multimodal inputs. Known as the missing modality issue, this challenge poses a critical barrier to the robustness and practical deployment of MAC models. To systematically quantify this issue, we introduce MissMAC-Bench, a comprehensive benchmark designed to establish fair and unified evaluation standards from the perspective of cross-modal synergy. Two guiding principles are proposed, including no missing prior during training, and one single model capable of handling both complete and incomplete modality scenarios, thereby ensuring better generalization. Moreover, to bridge the gap between academic research and real-world applications, our benchmark integrates evaluation protocols with both fixed and random missing patterns at the dataset and instance levels. Extensive experiments conducted on 3 widely-used language models across 4 datasets validate the effectiveness of diverse MAC approaches in tackling the missing modality issue. Our benchmark provides a solid foundation for advancing robust multimodal affective computing and promotes the development of multimedia data mining.

AI.tldr: MissMAC-Bench is a benchmark for evaluating robustness to missing modalities in multimodal affective computing (MAC), proposing a single-model solution that handles both complete and incomplete inputs. It uses fixed and random missing patterns at dataset and instance levels, validated across 3 language models and 4 datasets, to establish fair, cross-modal evaluation standards.
AI.motivation: In real-world MAC, modalities are often incomplete, causing distribution shifts and degraded performance. There is a need for fair, unified evaluation to quantify the impact of missing modalities and to promote robust, generalizable models.
AI.method: Introduce MissMAC-Bench with two guiding principles: (1) no missing priors during training; (2) a single model capable of handling both complete and incomplete modalities. Implement evaluation protocols incorporating fixed and random missing patterns at dataset and instance levels, and assess across multiple language models and datasets.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on 3 widely-used language models across 4 datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of various MAC approaches in mitigating missing-modality issues, validating the benchmark’s utility for robust MAC research.
AI.conclusion: MissMAC-Bench provides a solid, unified foundation for evaluating and advancing robust multimodal affective computing, bridging research and real-world multimedia data mining.

title: Reducing Class-Wise Performance Disparity via Margin Regularization
summary: Deep neural networks often exhibit substantial disparities in class-wise accuracy, even when trained on class-balanced data, posing concerns for reliable deployment. While prior efforts have explored empirical remedies, a theoretical understanding of such performance disparities in classification remains limited. In this work, we present Margin Regularization for Performance Disparity Reduction (MR$^2$), a theoretically principled regularization for classification by dynamically adjusting margins in both the logit and representation spaces. Our analysis establishes a margin-based, class-sensitive generalization bound that reveals how per-class feature variability contributes to error, motivating the use of larger margins for hard classes. Guided by this insight, MR$^2$ optimizes per-class logit margins proportional to feature spread and penalizes excessive representation margins to enhance intra-class compactness. Experiments on seven datasets, including ImageNet, and diverse pre-trained backbones (MAE, MoCov2, CLIP) demonstrate that MR$^2$ not only improves overall accuracy but also significantly boosts hard class performance without trading off easy classes, thus reducing performance disparity. Code is available at: https://github.com/BeierZhu/MR2

AI.tldr: MR^2 introduces dynamic, per-class margin regularization in logit and representation spaces to reduce class-wise accuracy disparities; it is grounded in a margin-based, class-sensitive generalization bound that ties per-class feature spread to error. The method increases margins for hard classes based on feature spread while penalizing excess representation margins to foster intra-class compactness. Empirically, MR^2 improves overall accuracy and markedly boosts hard-class performance across seven datasets (including ImageNet) and multiple backbones (MAE, MoCov2, CLIP) without hurting easy classes; code released.
AI.motivation: Despite training on balanced data, classifiers show substantial class-wise accuracy gaps. A theoretical understanding of these disparities is limited, hindering principled remedies. The authors seek a margin-based regularization that adapts to per-class difficulty and feature variability to reduce disparities.
AI.method: Develop Margin Regularization for Performance Disparity Reduction (MR^2). Derive a margin-based, class-sensitive generalization bound linking per-class feature spread to error. Implement dynamic per-class logit margins proportional to feature spread and penalize excessive representation margins to improve intra-class compactness. Optimize these margins during training to balance performance across classes.
AI.result: MR^2 yields higher overall accuracy and substantially improves hard-class performance across seven datasets, including ImageNet, with diverse backbones (MAE, MoCov2, CLIP). It reduces performance disparity without compromising easy classes.
AI.conclusion: A principled, margin-based regularization framework can mitigate class-wise performance disparities by aligning logit and representation margins with per-class variability. MR^2 demonstrates broad effectiveness and compatibility with multiple backbones and datasets, and provides a publicly available implementation.

title: Towards a Novel Wearable Robotic Vest for Hemorrhage Suppression
summary: This paper introduces a novel robotic system designed to manage severe bleeding in emergency scenarios, including unique environments like space stations. The robot features a shape-adjustable "ring mechanism", transitioning from a circular to an elliptical configuration to adjust wound coverage across various anatomical regions. We developed various arms for this ring mechanism with varying flexibilities to improve adaptability when applied to non-extremities of the body (abdomen, back, neck, etc.). To apply equal and constant pressure across the wound, we developed an inflatable ring and airbag balloon that are compatible with this shape-changing ring mechanism. A series of experiments focused on evaluating various ring arm configurations to characterize their bending stiffness. Subsequent experiments measured the force exerted by the airbag balloon system using a digital scale. Despite its promising performance, certain limitations related to coverage area are identified. The shape-changing effect of the device is limited to scenarios involving partially inflated or deflated airbag balloons, and cannot fully conform to complex anatomical regions. Finally, the device was tested on casualty simulation kits, where it successfully demonstrated its ability to control simulated bleeding.

AI.tldr: A shape-changing ring-based compression device with inflatable airbag for uniform wound pressure, adaptable to various body regions, tested on bending stiffness and force, with success in casualty simulations but limited coverage for complex anatomy.
AI.motivation: Develop a versatile, space-ready bleeding control device that can conform to diverse anatomical regions and environments where conventional tourniquets are insufficient.
AI.method: Design a circular-to-elliptical ring mechanism with multiple ring-arm configurations of varying flexibility; integrate an inflatable ring and airbag balloon that adapt to the ring's shape; characterize bending stiffness of ring-arm configurations; measure output force with a digital scale; test on casualty simulation kits.
AI.result: Certain ring-arm configurations showed defined bending stiffness; airbag system produced measurable, controllable pressure; device demonstrated bleeding control in casualty simulations; limitations identified: partial inflation/deflation constraints reduce conformability to complex anatomy; overall promising performance.
AI.conclusion: The device offers a promising approach to universal external hemorrhage control across multiple body regions and environments, but requires improvements in coverage for complex anatomies and more robust validation before real-world deployment.

title: Resource-Efficient Reinforcement for Reasoning Large Language Models via Dynamic One-Shot Policy Refinement
summary: Large language models (LLMs) have exhibited remarkable performance on complex reasoning tasks, with reinforcement learning under verifiable rewards (RLVR) emerging as a principled framework for aligning model behavior with reasoning chains. Despite its promise, RLVR remains prohibitively resource-intensive, requiring extensive reward signals and incurring substantial rollout costs during training. In this work, we revisit the fundamental question of data and compute efficiency in RLVR. We first establish a theoretical lower bound on the sample complexity required to unlock reasoning capabilities, and empirically validate that strong performance can be achieved with a surprisingly small number of training instances. To tackle the computational burden, we propose Dynamic One-Shot Policy Refinement (DoPR), an uncertainty-aware RL strategy that dynamically selects a single informative training sample per batch for policy updates, guided by reward volatility and exploration-driven acquisition. DoPR reduces rollout overhead by nearly an order of magnitude while preserving competitive reasoning accuracy, offering a scalable and resource-efficient solution for LLM post-training. This approach offers a practical path toward more efficient and accessible RL-based training for reasoning-intensive LLM applications.

AI.tldr: RLVR is resource-intensive; the paper offers DoPR, an uncertainty-aware RL strategy with single informative per-batch sample updates, plus a theoretical lower bound on sample complexity.
AI.motivation: Address data and compute inefficiency in RLVR for LLM reasoning; reduce rollout costs while maintaining reasoning performance.
AI.method: Establish a theoretical lower bound on sample complexity for unlocking reasoning in RLVR; introduce Dynamic One-Shot Policy Refinement (DoPR), which selects a single informative training sample per batch based on reward volatility and exploration-driven acquisition.
AI.result: Empirical results show DoPR reduces rollout overhead by ~10x while preserving competitive reasoning accuracy; strong performance achieved with a surprisingly small number of training instances.
AI.conclusion: DoPR provides a scalable, resource-efficient path for RL-based training of reasoning-intensive LLM applications and broadens practicality of RLVR for post-training alignment.

title: Analyzing Shapley Additive Explanations to Understand Anomaly Detection Algorithm Behaviors and Their Complementarity
summary: Unsupervised anomaly detection is a challenging problem due to the diversity of data distributions and the lack of labels. Ensemble methods are often adopted to mitigate these challenges by combining multiple detectors, which can reduce individual biases and increase robustness. Yet building an ensemble that is genuinely complementary remains challenging, since many detectors rely on similar decision cues and end up producing redundant anomaly scores. As a result, the potential of ensemble learning is often limited by the difficulty of identifying models that truly capture different types of irregularities. To address this, we propose a methodology for characterizing anomaly detectors through their decision mechanisms. Using SHapley Additive exPlanations, we quantify how each model attributes importance to input features, and we use these attribution profiles to measure similarity between detectors. We show that detectors with similar explanations tend to produce correlated anomaly scores and identify largely overlapping anomalies. Conversely, explanation divergence reliably indicates complementary detection behavior. Our results demonstrate that explanation-driven metrics offer a different criterion than raw outputs for selecting models in an ensemble. However, we also demonstrate that diversity alone is insufficient; high individual model performance remains a prerequisite for effective ensembles. By explicitly targeting explanation diversity while maintaining model quality, we are able to construct ensembles that are more diverse, more complementary, and ultimately more effective for unsupervised anomaly detection.

AI.tldr: SHAP-based attribution profiling for detectors; explanation-diverse ensembles improve unsupervised anomaly detection, but base model quality is essential.
AI.motivation: Unsupervised anomaly detection suffers from data distribution diversity and lack of labels; ensembles help but require complementary detectors; explanations can reveal decision cues to gauge complementarity.
AI.method: Compute SHAP attributions for each detector; derive attribution profiles; measure similarity/diversity between detectors; analyze correlation with anomaly scores; propose explanation-diversity criterion for model selection; validate that high base performance is necessary; construct ensembles with explanation diversity.
AI.result: Detectors with similar explanations yield correlated scores and overlapping anomalies; explanation divergence signals complementary behavior; explanation-driven model selection differs from raw-score based selection; diversification alone is insufficient; by targeting explanation diversity while preserving performance, ensembles become more diverse, complementary, and effective.
AI.conclusion: Explanation-driven diversity is beneficial for ensemble construction but must be coupled with strong individual detectors; the proposed approach improves unsupervised anomaly detection by leveraging SHAP-based decision explanations.

title: TreeLoc: 6-DoF LiDAR Global Localization in Forests via Inter-Tree Geometric Matching
comment: An 8-page paper with 7 tables and 8 figures, accepted to ICRA 2026
summary: Reliable localization is crucial for navigation in forests, where GPS is often degraded and LiDAR measurements are repetitive, occluded, and structurally complex. These conditions weaken the assumptions of traditional urban-centric localization methods, which assume that consistent features arise from unique structural patterns, necessitating forest-centric solutions to achieve robustness in these environments. To address these challenges, we propose TreeLoc, a LiDAR-based global localization framework for forests that handles place recognition and 6-DoF pose estimation. We represent scenes using tree stems and their Diameter at Breast Height (DBH), which are aligned to a common reference frame via their axes and summarized using the tree distribution histogram (TDH) for coarse matching, followed by fine matching with a 2D triangle descriptor. Finally, pose estimation is achieved through a two-step geometric verification. On diverse forest benchmarks, TreeLoc outperforms baselines, achieving precise localization. Ablation studies validate the contribution of each component. We also propose applications for long-term forest management using descriptors from a compact global tree database. TreeLoc is open-sourced for the robotics community at https://github.com/minwoo0611/TreeLoc.

AI.tldr: TreeLoc is a LiDAR-based forest localization framework that uses a two-stage descriptor (TDH and a 2D triangle descriptor) constructed from tree stems and DBH to enable robust place recognition and 6-DoF pose estimation, outperforming baselines on forest benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Forest environments challenge localization due to degraded GPS signals, repeated occlusions and structural complexity, making urban-centric localization assumptions ineffective; a forest-centric global localization approach leveraging stable tree-based structures is needed for robust navigation and long-term forest management.
AI.method: Scenes are represented by tree stems and DBH, aligned to a common reference frame via their axes. A Tree Distribution Histogram (TDH) is used for coarse place recognition, followed by fine matching with a 2D triangle descriptor. Pose estimation is achieved through a two-step geometric verification process. The framework also includes a compact global tree database for long-term forest management and is open-sourced.
AI.result: TreeLoc achieves precise localization and outperforms baselines on diverse forest benchmarks. Ablation studies validate the contribution of each component, demonstrating robustness of place recognition and 6-DoF pose estimation in forest environments.
AI.conclusion: TreeLoc presents a forest-centric LiDAR localization framework that combines stable tree-based structural features with a two-stage descriptor and geometric verification to enable reliable global localization and practical applications in forest management, with open-source availability.

title: Optimizing Agentic Reasoning with Retrieval via Synthetic Semantic Information Gain Reward
summary: Agentic reasoning enables large reasoning models (LRMs) to dynamically acquire external knowledge, but yet optimizing the retrieval process remains challenging due to the lack of dense, principled reward signals. In this paper, we introduce InfoReasoner, a unified framework that incentivizes effective information seeking via a synthetic semantic information gain reward. Theoretically, we redefine information gain as uncertainty reduction over the model's belief states, establishing guarantees, including non-negativity, telescoping additivity, and channel monotonicity. Practically, to enable scalable optimization without manual retrieval annotations, we propose an output-aware intrinsic estimator that computes information gain directly from the model's output distributions using semantic clustering via bidirectional textual entailment. This intrinsic reward guides the policy to maximize epistemic progress, enabling efficient training via Group Relative Policy Optimxization (GRPO). Experiments across seven question-answering benchmarks demonstrate that InfoReasoner consistently outperforms strong retrieval-augmented baselines, achieving up to 5.4% average accuracy improvement. Our work provides a theoretically grounded and scalable path toward agentic reasoning with retrieval.

AI.tldr: InfoReasoner introduces a synthetic information-gain reward to guide external-knowledge retrieval in large reasoning models, defining information gain as uncertainty reduction with guarantees; it uses an output-aware intrinsic estimator based on semantic clustering via bidirectional entailment, trained with Group Relative Policy Optimxization (GRPO).
AI.motivation: The retrieval optimization for agentic reasoning is hampered by the lack of dense, principled reward signals and the need for scalable, annotation-free training to encourage effective information seeking.
AI.method: Propose InfoReasoner framework with a synthetic semantic information gain reward. Theoretically redefine information gain as uncertainty reduction over belief states, establishing non-negativity, telescoping additivity, and channel monotonicity. Practically, implement an output-aware intrinsic estimator that computes information gain from model outputs using semantic clustering via bidirectional textual entailment, and train with Group Relative Policy Optimxization (GRPO).
AI.result: InfoReasoner outperforms strong retrieval-augmented baselines across seven QA benchmarks, achieving up to 5.4% average accuracy improvement.
AI.conclusion: The work provides a theoretically grounded, scalable approach to agentic reasoning with retrieval, linking information-goal signals to practical, annotation-free optimization.

title: Dispersion Loss Counteracts Embedding Condensation and Improves Generalization in Small Language Models
summary: Large language models (LLMs) achieve remarkable performance through ever-increasing parameter counts, but scaling incurs steep computational costs. To better understand LLM scaling, we study representational differences between LLMs and their smaller counterparts, with the goal of replicating the representational qualities of larger models in the smaller models. We observe a geometric phenomenon which we term $\textbf{embedding condensation}$, where token embeddings collapse into a narrow cone-like subspace in some language models. Through systematic analyses across multiple Transformer families, we show that small models such as $\texttt{GPT2}$ and $\texttt{Qwen3-0.6B}$ exhibit severe condensation, whereas the larger models such as $\texttt{GPT2-xl}$ and $\texttt{Qwen3-32B}$ are more resistant to this phenomenon. Additional observations show that embedding condensation is not reliably mitigated by knowledge distillation from larger models. To fight against it, we formulate a dispersion loss that explicitly encourages embedding dispersion during training. Experiments demonstrate that it mitigates condensation, recovers dispersion patterns seen in larger models, and yields performance gains across 10 benchmarks. We believe this work offers a principled path toward improving smaller Transformers without additional parameters.

AI.tldr: Defines embedding condensation in small LMs, shows distillation does not reliably fix it, and introduces dispersion loss to enforce embedding dispersion, yielding performance gains without extra parameters.
AI.motivation: Understand how scaling affects representations and enable smaller LMs to emulate larger models' representations without increasing parameters.
AI.method: Systematic empirical analysis across multiple Transformer families (e.g., GPT2, Qwen3) to characterize embedding geometry; compare small vs large models; evaluate knowledge distillation; propose and train with a dispersion loss; assess on 10 benchmarks.
AI.result: Small models exhibit severe embedding condensation; larger models resist it; knowledge distillation does not reliably mitigate condensation; dispersion loss mitigates condensation, restores dispersion patterns akin to larger models, and yields performance gains across 10 benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: A dispersion-based training objective offers a principled path to improve small Transformers without adding parameters by promoting embedding dispersion and mitigating condensation.

title: RAPT: Model-Predictive Out-of-Distribution Detection and Failure Diagnosis for Sim-to-Real Humanoid Robots
summary: Deploying learned control policies on humanoid robots is challenging: policies that appear robust in simulation can execute confidently in out-of-distribution (OOD) states after Sim-to-Real transfer, leading to silent failures that risk hardware damage. Although anomaly detection can mitigate these failures, prior methods are often incompatible with high-rate control, poorly calibrated at the extremely low false-positive rates required for practical deployment, or operate as black boxes that provide a binary stop signal without explaining why the robot drifted from nominal behavior. We present RAPT, a lightweight, self-supervised deployment-time monitor for 50Hz humanoid control. RAPT learns a probabilistic spatio-temporal manifold of nominal execution from simulation and evaluates execution-time predictive deviation as a calibrated, per-dimension signal. This yields (i) reliable online OOD detection under strict false-positive constraints and (ii) a continuous, interpretable measure of Sim-to-Real mismatch that can be tracked over time to quantify how far deployment has drifted from training. Beyond detection, we introduce an automated post-hoc root-cause analysis pipeline that combines gradient-based temporal saliency derived from RAPT's reconstruction objective with LLM-based reasoning conditioned on saliency and joint kinematics to produce semantic failure diagnoses in a zero-shot setting. We evaluate RAPT on a Unitree G1 humanoid across four complex tasks in simulation and on physical hardware. In large-scale simulation, RAPT improves True Positive Rate (TPR) by 37% over the strongest baseline at a fixed episode-level false positive rate of 0.5%. On real-world deployments, RAPT achieves a 12.5% TPR improvement and provides actionable interpretability, reaching 75% root-cause classification accuracy across 16 real-world failures using only proprioceptive data.

AI.tldr: RAPT: a lightweight, self-supervised deployment monitor for 50Hz humanoid control that detects out-of-distribution execution, tracks Sim-to-Real drift, and provides interpretable root-cause analysis. Demonstrates improved detection performance (37% TPR gain in simulation; 12.5% in real hardware) and 75% accuracy for zero-shot fault diagnosis using proprioception data.
AI.motivation: Deploying learned policies on humanoids suffers from silent, high-risk failures when deployed outside training distribution. Existing anomaly detectors struggle with high-rate control, ultra-low false-positive rates, and explainability.
AI.method: Train a probabilistic spatio-temporal manifold of nominal behavior from simulation. At runtime, compute per-dimension predictive deviation as calibrated signals for anomaly detection. Provide online OOD detection and a continuous drift metric. Add an automated post-hoc root-cause analysis using gradient-based temporal saliency from the reconstruction objective and LLM-based reasoning conditioned on saliency and joint kinematics to yield semantic failure diagnoses in zero-shot.
AI.result: RAPT achieves reliable online OOD detection under strict FPR constraints. In large-scale simulation, TPR improves by 37% over strongest baseline at 0.5% FPR. On real hardware, TPR improves by 12.5% and achieves 75% accuracy in root-cause classification across 16 failures using only proprioceptive data.
AI.conclusion: RAPT provides a calibrated, per-dimension, interpretable deployment monitor for high-rate humanoid control, enabling both detection of deployment drift and actionable, zero-shot root-cause diagnoses, with strong empirical improvements in both simulated and real-world tests.

title: Persuasion Propagation in LLM Agents
summary: Modern AI agents increasingly combine conversational interaction with autonomous task execution, such as coding and web research, raising a natural question: what happens when an agent engaged in long-horizon tasks is subjected to user persuasion? We study how belief-level intervention can influence downstream task behavior, a phenomenon we name \emph{persuasion propagation}. We introduce a behavior-centered evaluation framework that distinguishes between persuasion applied during or prior to task execution. Across web research and coding tasks, we find that on-the-fly persuasion induces weak and inconsistent behavioral effects. In contrast, when the belief state is explicitly specified at task time, belief-prefilled agents conduct on average 26.9\% fewer searches and visit 16.9\% fewer unique sources than neutral-prefilled agents. These results suggest that persuasion, even in prior interaction, can affect the agent's behavior, motivating behavior-level evaluation in agentic systems.

AI.tldr: Belief-level interventions can influence long-horizon agent behavior; on-the-fly persuasion has weak effects, while pre-specified beliefs lead to substantial reductions in search and source visits.
AI.motivation: To understand how user persuasion affects autonomous agents during long-horizon tasks and to develop a behavior-centered evaluation framework that separates persuasion during vs prior to task execution.
AI.method: Empirical study across web research and coding tasks comparing: (1) on-the-fly persuasion during task execution, (2) belief-state manipulation with belief-prefilled vs neutral-prefilled agents; metrics include number of searches and unique sources visited.
AI.result: On-the-fly persuasion yields weak and inconsistent effects. Belief-prefilled agents perform more conservatively, conducting 26.9% fewer searches and visiting 16.9% fewer unique sources than neutral-prefilled agents on average.
AI.conclusion: Persuasion can affect agent behavior even when applied before execution; a behavior-level evaluation framework is necessary to capture such effects in agentic systems.

title: GRIP2: A Robust and Powerful Deep Knockoff Method for Feature Selection
summary: Identifying truly predictive covariates while strictly controlling false discoveries remains a fundamental challenge in nonlinear, highly correlated, and low signal-to-noise regimes, where deep learning based feature selection methods are most attractive. We propose Group Regularization Importance Persistence in 2 Dimensions (GRIP2), a deep knockoff feature importance statistic that integrates first-layer feature activity over a two-dimensional regularization surface controlling both sparsity strength and sparsification geometry. To approximate this surface integral in a single training run, we introduce efficient block-stochastic sampling, which aggregates feature activity magnitudes across diverse regularization regimes along the optimization trajectory. The resulting statistics are antisymmetric by construction, ensuring finite-sample FDR control. In extensive experiments on synthetic and semi-real data, GRIP2 demonstrates improved robustness to feature correlation and noise level: in high correlation and low signal-to-noise ratio regimes where standard deep learning based feature selectors may struggle, our method retains high power and stability. Finally, on real-world HIV drug resistance data, GRIP2 recovers known resistance-associated mutations with power better than established linear baselines, confirming its reliability in practice.

AI.tldr: GRIP2 provides a two-dimensional regularization-based deep knockoff feature importance statistic with block-stochastic surface integral, achieving finite-sample FDR control and robust performance under high correlation and low SNR; validated on HIV data.
AI.motivation: Identify truly predictive covariates under nonlinear, highly correlated, and low signal-to-noise regimes while strictly controlling false discoveries, where standard deep learning feature selectors struggle.
AI.method: GRIP2 integrates first-layer feature activity over a two-dimensional regularization surface controlling sparsity strength and sparsification geometry. It uses efficient block-stochastic sampling to approximate the surface integral along the optimization trajectory, producing antisymmetric statistics that guarantee finite-sample FDR control.
AI.result: GRIP2 demonstrates improved robustness to feature correlation and noise in synthetic and semi-real data, maintaining high power and stability in challenging regimes; on real HIV drug resistance data, GRIP2 recovers known resistance-associated mutations with higher power than established linear baselines.
AI.conclusion: GRIP2 advances deep knockoff feature selection by integrating regularization geometry and surface-trajectory information to achieve finite-sample FDR control in nonlinear, correlated, low-SNR settings, with practical validation on real data; potential extensions include broader datasets and architectures.

title: Co-Design of Rover Wheels and Control using Bayesian Optimization and Rover-Terrain Simulations
summary: While simulation is vital for optimizing robotic systems, the cost of modeling deformable terrain has long limited its use in full-vehicle studies of off-road autonomous mobility. For example, Discrete Element Method (DEM) simulations are often confined to single-wheel tests, which obscures coupled wheel-vehicle-controller interactions and prevents joint optimization of mechanical design and control. This paper presents a Bayesian optimization framework that co-designs rover wheel geometry and steering controller parameters using high-fidelity, full-vehicle closed-loop simulations on deformable terrain. Using the efficiency and scalability of a continuum-representation model (CRM) for terramechanics, we evaluate candidate designs on trajectories of varying complexity while towing a fixed load. The optimizer tunes wheel parameters (radius, width, and grouser features) and steering PID gains under a multi-objective formulation that balances traversal speed, tracking error, and energy consumption. We compare two strategies: simultaneous co-optimization of wheel and controller parameters versus a sequential approach that decouples mechanical and control design. We analyze trade-offs in performance and computational cost. Across 3,000 full-vehicle simulations, campaigns finish in five to nine days, versus months with the group's earlier DEM-based workflow. Finally, a preliminary hardware study suggests the simulation-optimized wheel designs preserve relative performance trends on the physical rover. Together, these results show that scalable, high-fidelity simulation can enable practical co-optimization of wheel design and control for off-road vehicles on deformable terrain without relying on prohibitively expensive DEM studies. The simulation infrastructure (scripts and models) is released as open source in a public repository to support reproducibility and further research.

AI.tldr: Bayesian optimization framework co-designs rover wheel geometry and steering controller using high-fidelity CRM-based full-vehicle simulations on deformable terrain; demonstrates scalable, efficient joint optimization and open-source tooling.
AI.motivation: Deformable terrain modeling is costly and DEM-based studies are typically restricted to single-wheel tests, hindering joint optimization of mechanical design and control for off-road mobility.
AI.method: Use a continuum-representation model (CRM) to enable full-vehicle simulations on deformable terrain and perform multi-objective Bayesian optimization of wheel parameters (radius, width, grouser features) and steering PID gains. Compare simultaneous vs sequential co-design strategies, run 3,000 simulations with varying trajectory complexity and fixed load, and include a preliminary hardware validation. The simulation infrastructure is released as open source.
AI.result: Achieves 5–9 day turnaround for 3,000 simulations (versus months with DEM); reveals trade-offs between simultaneous and sequential optimization and quantifies computational costs; wheel designs preserve relative performance trends on hardware; results support practical co-optimization and reproducibility through open-source tools.
AI.conclusion: Scalable, high-fidelity simulation enables practical co-optimization of wheel design and control for deformable-terrain mobility and provides a reproducible, open-source workflow.

title: Position: Human-Centric AI Requires a Minimum Viable Level of Human Understanding
summary: AI systems increasingly produce fluent, correct, end-to-end outcomes. Over time, this erodes users' ability to explain, verify, or intervene. We define this divergence as the Capability-Comprehension Gap: a decoupling where assisted performance improves while users' internal models deteriorate. This paper argues that prevailing approaches to transparency, user control, literacy, and governance do not define the foundational understanding humans must retain for oversight under sustained AI delegation. To formalize this, we define the Cognitive Integrity Threshold (CIT) as the minimum comprehension required to preserve oversight, autonomy, and accountable participation under AI assistance. CIT does not require full reasoning reconstruction, nor does it constrain automation. It identifies the threshold beyond which oversight becomes procedural and contestability fails. We operatinalize CIT through three functional dimensions: (i) verification capacity, (ii) comprehension-preserving interaction, and (iii) institutional scaffolds for governance. This motivates a design and governance agenda that aligns human-AI interaction with cognitive sustainability in responsibility-critical settings.

AI.tldr: Introduces Cognitive Integrity Threshold (CIT) to preserve human oversight under AI delegation; operationalizes with three functional dimensions and a governance-forward agenda.
AI.motivation: As AI systems achieve fluent, end-to-end performance, users' internal models can deteriorate, creating a Capability-Comprehension Gap that undermines oversight and accountability.
AI.method: Conceptual framework defining CIT, its three functional dimensions (verification capacity, comprehension-preserving interaction, institutional governance scaffolds), and a design/governance agenda for responsible AI deployment.
AI.result: A theoretical construct with clear criteria for evaluating human-AI alignment, and guidance for practical design and governance—no empirical results provided but actionable directions.
AI.conclusion: To sustain oversight and accountability in responsibility-critical settings, design and governance must maintain a minimal level of human cognitive integrity without unduly constraining automation.

title: Green-NAS: A Global-Scale Multi-Objective Neural Architecture Search for Robust and Efficient Edge-Native Weather Forecasting
summary: We introduce Green-NAS, a multi-objective NAS (neural architecture search) framework designed for low-resource environments using weather forecasting as a case study. By adhering to 'Green AI' principles, the framework explicitly minimizes computational energy costs and carbon footprints, prioritizing sustainable deployment over raw computational scale. The Green-NAS architecture search method is optimized for both model accuracy and efficiency to find lightweight models with high accuracy and very few model parameters; this is accomplished through an optimization process that simultaneously optimizes multiple objectives. Our best-performing model, Green-NAS-A, achieved an RMSE of 0.0988 (i.e., within 1.4% of our manually tuned baseline) using only 153k model parameters, which is 239 times fewer than other globally applied weather forecasting models, such as GraphCast. In addition, we also describe how the use of transfer learning will improve the weather forecasting accuracy by approximately 5.2%, in comparison to a naive approach of training a new model for each city, when there is limited historical weather data available for that city.

AI.tldr: Green-NAS is a multi-objective NAS framework aimed at low-resource weather forecasting, optimizing accuracy and energy efficiency; Green-NAS-A achieves RMSE 0.0988 with 153k parameters (239x fewer than GraphCast); transfer learning can improve accuracy by ~5.2% in data-scarce cities.
AI.motivation: Reduce computational energy costs and carbon footprint of NAS-driven weather forecasting; enable sustainable deployment in resource-constrained environments, using weather forecasting as a case study.
AI.method: Multi-objective neural architecture search (NAS) that optimizes accuracy and efficiency; lightweight architectures; transfer learning to boost performance when historical data per city is limited.
AI.result: Best model Green-NAS-A: RMSE 0.0988 (within 1.4% of a manually tuned baseline); 153k parameters, ~239x fewer than GraphCast; transfer learning yields ~5.2% accuracy gain over naive city-by-city training.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates viability of Green AI-driven NAS for scalable, energy-efficient weather forecasting; highlights trade-offs between accuracy and resource use; transfer learning mitigates data scarcity.

title: UniDWM: Towards a Unified Driving World Model via Multifaceted Representation Learning
summary: Achieving reliable and efficient planning in complex driving environments requires a model that can reason over the scene's geometry, appearance, and dynamics. We present UniDWM, a unified driving world model that advances autonomous driving through multifaceted representation learning. UniDWM constructs a structure- and dynamic-aware latent world representation that serves as a physically grounded state space, enabling consistent reasoning across perception, prediction, and planning. Specifically, a joint reconstruction pathway learns to recover the scene's structure, including geometry and visual texture, while a collaborative generation framework leverages a conditional diffusion transformer to forecast future world evolution within the latent space. Furthermore, we show that our UniDWM can be deemed as a variation of VAE, which provides theoretical guidance for the multifaceted representation learning. Extensive experiments demonstrate the effectiveness of UniDWM in trajectory planning, 4D reconstruction and generation, highlighting the potential of multifaceted world representations as a foundation for unified driving intelligence. The code will be publicly available at https://github.com/Say2L/UniDWM.

AI.tldr: Unified driving world model (UniDWM) learns a structure- and dynamic-aware latent state space for perception, prediction, and planning by joint reconstruction of geometry/texture and diffusion-based forecasting of future latent evolution, framed as a VAE-like objective; demonstrated in trajectory planning and 4D reconstruction with code release.
AI.motivation: Need for a cohesive, physically grounded latent representation that unifies perception, prediction, and planning in complex driving environments.
AI.method: A joint reconstruction pathway recovers scene structure (geometry and visual texture); a conditional diffusion transformer forecasts future latent world states; the framework is interpreted as a VAE variant to guide multifaceted representation learning.
AI.result: Empirically improves trajectory planning, 4D reconstruction, and generation; shows promising unified driving intelligence; code will be released.
AI.conclusion: Multifaceted latent representations can underpin unified driving intelligence; UniDWM provides a foundation for integrated perception, prediction, and planning in autonomous driving.

title: Multi-Head Attention Is a Multi-Player Game
summary: Modern transformer attention is internally multi-agent -- heads compete and coordinate -- yet we train it as if it were a monolithic optimizer. We formalize this gap: cross-entropy training induces an implicit potential game among heads, and gradient descent converges to Nash equilibria with potentially unbounded inefficiency due to unpriced externalities (redundancy, correlated errors). Our main result bounds the Price of Anarchy by $Γ(G)$, the off-diagonal mass of a head interaction matrix capturing weight and gradient coupling. Under mild smoothness assumptions, we prove that both \emph{excess hallucination probability} and \emph{excess head redundancy} scale with PoA, unifying two distinct failure modes into a single mechanism.
