The bound is prescriptive: regularization that reduces $Γ(G)$ provably tightens PoA. We instantiate this as GAME-LoRA, combining Barlow Twins decorrelation with log-determinant coordination pressure. Experiments validate the theory: $Γ(G)$ predicts hallucination ($p{<}0.05$), emergent coalitions exhibit selective coordination, and GAME-LoRA achieves up to 18\% hallucination reduction (8\% average) with no knowledge degradation -- a Pareto improvement inaccessible to methods ignoring the game structure.

AI.tldr: Treat transformer attention as a multi-agent game among heads; the training objective creates externalities that induce inefficiency. A bound on the Price of Anarchy (PoA) is given by the off-diagonal mass Γ(G) of head interaction, linking hallucination and head redundancy; regularization reducing Γ(G) tightens PoA. GAME-LoRA (Barlow Twins decorrelation + log-determinant coordination) empirically reduces hallucination while preserving accuracy; up to 18% reduction overall.
AI.motivation: There is a mismatch between standard monolithic training of transformer attention and the inherently multi-agent interactions among attention heads. Externalities like redundancy and correlated errors lead to inefficiencies (PoA). A formal game-theoretic treatment can quantify this inefficiency and guide regularization.
AI.method: Model attention heads as players in a potential game derived from cross-entropy training. Define a head interaction matrix G whose off-diagonal mass Γ(G) captures weight and gradient coupling. Prove that excess hallucination probability and excess head redundancy scale with PoA. Show that regularization reducing Γ(G) tightens PoA. Propose GAME-LoRA combining Barlow Twins decorrelation with log-determinant coordination pressure. Validate with experiments linking Γ(G) to hallucination and demonstrating coalitions and Pareto-improvement in accuracy vs hallucination.
AI.result: Analytical bound: PoA ≤ Γ(G) under mild smoothness assumptions; both excess hallucination probability and excess head redundancy scale with PoA. Γ(G) predicts hallucination (p<0.05). Emergent coalitions show selective coordination. GAME-LoRA achieves up to 18% hallucination reduction (8% average) with no knowledge degradation, a Pareto improvement unattainable by methods ignoring the game structure.
AI.conclusion: Regularization that reduces Γ(G) tightens the PoA bound, linking multi-head interactions to practical improvements. The game-theoretic lens explains two failure modalities (hallucination and redundancy) as manifestations of externalities, and GAME-LoRA provides a concrete, Pareto-improving method grounded in this framework.

title: TABES: Trajectory-Aware Backward-on-Entropy Steering for Masked Diffusion Models
summary: Masked Diffusion Models (MDMs) have emerged as a promising non-autoregressive paradigm for generative tasks, offering parallel decoding and bidirectional context utilization. However, current sampling methods rely on simple confidence-based heuristics that ignore the long-term impact of local decisions, leading to trajectory lock-in where early hallucinations cascade into global incoherence. While search-based methods mitigate this, they incur prohibitive computational costs ($O(K)$ forward passes per step). In this work, we propose Backward-on-Entropy (BoE) Steering, a gradient-guided inference framework that approximates infinite-horizon lookahead via a single backward pass. We formally derive the Token Influence Score (TIS) from a first-order expansion of the trajectory cost functional, proving that the gradient of future entropy with respect to input embeddings serves as an optimal control signal for minimizing uncertainty. To ensure scalability, we introduce \texttt{ActiveQueryAttention}, a sparse adjoint primitive that exploits the structure of the masking objective to reduce backward pass complexity. BoE achieves a superior Pareto frontier for inference-time scaling compared to existing unmasking methods, demonstrating that gradient-guided steering offers a mathematically principled and efficient path to robust non-autoregressive generation. We will release the code.

AI.tldr: Backward-on-Entropy (BoE) Steering for Masked Diffusion Models uses a gradient-guided, single backward pass to approximate infinite-horizon lookahead, with Token Influence Score and ActiveQueryAttention to improve efficiency; yields better inference-time scalability.
AI.motivation: Solve trajectory lock-in in non-autoregressive masked diffusion models (MDMs) caused by early local decisions, and reduce the computational cost of long-horizon search-based unmasking.
AI.method: Derive Token Influence Score (TIS) from a first-order expansion of trajectory cost. Use the gradient of future entropy with respect to input embeddings as the optimal control signal. Introduce ActiveQueryAttention, a sparse adjoint primitive leveraging masking structure to reduce backward pass complexity. Implement BoE as a gradient-guided inference framework that implicitly performs infinite-horizon lookahead with a single backward pass.
AI.result: BoE achieves a superior Pareto frontier for inference-time scaling compared to existing unmasking methods, demonstrating improved robustness and efficiency in non-autoregressive generation.
AI.conclusion: Gradient-guided steering via BoE provides a principled, efficient approach to robust non-autoregressive generation in MDMs, with practical scalability improvements and a plan to release code.

title: A Closed-Form Geometric Retargeting Solver for Upper Body Humanoid Robot Teleoperation
summary: Retargeting human motion to robot poses is a practical approach for teleoperating bimanual humanoid robot arms, but existing methods can be suboptimal and slow, often causing undesirable motion or latency. This is due to optimizing to match robot end-effector to human hand position and orientation, which can also limit the robot's workspace to that of the human. Instead, this paper reframes retargeting as an orientation alignment problem, enabling a closed-form, geometric solution algorithm with an optimality guarantee. The key idea is to align a robot arm to a human's upper and lower arm orientations, as identified from shoulder, elbow, and wrist (SEW) keypoints; hence, the method is called SEW-Mimic. The method has fast inference (3 kHz) on standard commercial CPUs, leaving computational overhead for downstream applications; an example in this paper is a safety filter to avoid bimanual self-collision. The method suits most 7-degree-of-freedom robot arms and humanoids, and is agnostic to input keypoint source. Experiments show that SEW-Mimic outperforms other retargeting methods in computation time and accuracy. A pilot user study suggests that the method improves teleoperation task success. Preliminary analysis indicates that data collected with SEW-Mimic improves policy learning due to being smoother. SEW-Mimic is also shown to be a drop-in way to accelerate full-body humanoid retargeting. Finally, hardware demonstrations illustrate SEW-Mimic's practicality. The results emphasize the utility of SEW-Mimic as a fundamental building block for bimanual robot manipulation and humanoid robot teleoperation.

AI.tldr: SEW-Mimic is a fast, closed-form orientation-based retargeting method that aligns a robot arm to a human's SEW (shoulder–elbow–wrist) orientations for teleoperation, enabling broad applicability to 7-DOF arms with high accuracy and low latency.
AI.motivation: Traditional retargeting optimizes end-effector pose, which can reduce workspace, incur latency, and produce suboptimal motion. By focusing on orientation alignment with SEW keypoints, SEW-Mimic aims for faster, more robust retargeting with optimality guarantees.
AI.method: Compute target robot arm orientation by aligning its upper and lower arm directions to the human's SEW orientations derived from shoulder, elbow, and wrist keypoints. This yields a closed-form geometric solution with an optimality guarantee. The approach is input-keypoint-agnostic and applicable to most 7-DOF humanoid arms, with a high inference rate (~3 kHz) on standard CPUs.
AI.result: Outperforms existing retargeting methods in computation time and accuracy. Demonstrates improvements in teleoperation task success in a pilot user study. Preliminary data suggest smoother trajectories improve downstream policy learning. Serves as a drop-in for full-body retargeting and is supported by hardware demonstrations indicating practicality.
AI.conclusion: SEW-Mimic provides a fast, general, and reliable orientation-based retargeting abstraction that enhances bimanual manipulation and humanoid teleoperation, and can serve as a foundational component for downstream control and learning pipelines.

title: Foundation CAN LM: A Pretrained Language Model For Automotive CAN Data
summary: The Controller Area Network (CAN) bus provides a rich source of vehicular signals increasingly leveraged for applications in automotive and auto insurance domains, including collision detection, predictive maintenance, and driver risk modeling. Despite this potential, existing pipelines largely train isolated task-specific models on raw CAN data, with only limited efforts exploring decoded signals. Such fragmentation prevents shared representation learning and limits cross-task generalization. By contrast, natural language processing (NLP) and computer vision (CV) have been transformed by the foundation model paradigm: large-scale pretraining followed by task-specific adaptation. In this work, we introduce the foundation CAN model that demonstrates multi-objective downstream generalization using a single pretrained backbone. Our approach treats CAN data as a language: we pretrain on large-scale, unlabeled decoded CAN signals and fine-tune across heterogeneous auto insurance tasks. To enable this, we propose a unified tokenization scheme for mixed discrete-continuous signals and address challenges of temporal complexity and trip-specific variability. Our results show that one pretrained CAN model can adapt effectively to diverse predictive tasks, validating that the foundation modeling paradigm, proven in NLP and CV, also holds for CAN data. This establishes a new direction for generalizable representation learning in automotive AI.

AI.tldr: Pretraining decoded CAN signals as a language yields a single backbone that generalizes across auto insurance tasks via a unified tokenization scheme for mixed discrete-continuous data.
AI.motivation: Fragmented task-specific CAN models hinder cross-task learning; a foundation-model approach could enable shared representations and better generalization in automotive AI.
AI.method: Treat CAN data as language, pretrain on large-scale unlabeled decoded CAN signals, develop a unified tokenization for mixed data types, address temporal complexity and trip-specific variability, fine-tune on heterogeneous auto insurance tasks with one backbone.
AI.result: A single pretrained CAN model adapts to diverse predictive tasks, demonstrating cross-task generalization and validating foundation modeling for CAN data.
AI.conclusion: Foundation modeling can extend to automotive CAN data, enabling generalizable, multi-task representation learning in automotive AI.

title: VoxServe: Streaming-Centric Serving System for Speech Language Models
comment: The code is available at https://github.com/vox-serve/vox-serve
summary: Deploying modern Speech Language Models (SpeechLMs) in streaming settings requires systems that provide low latency, high throughput, and strong guarantees of streamability. Existing systems fall short of supporting diverse models flexibly and efficiently. We present VoxServe, a unified serving system for SpeechLMs that optimizes streaming performance. VoxServe introduces a model-execution abstraction that decouples model architecture from system-level optimizations, thereby enabling support for diverse SpeechLM architectures within a single framework. Building on this abstraction, VoxServe implements streaming-aware scheduling and an asynchronous inference pipeline to improve end-to-end efficiency. Evaluations across multiple modern SpeechLMs show that VoxServe achieves 10-20x higher throughput than existing implementations at comparable latency while maintaining high streaming viability. The code of VoxServe is available at https://github.com/vox-serve/vox-serve.

AI.tldr: VoxServe is a unified streaming-serving system for SpeechLMs that uses a model-execution abstraction to decouple model architecture from system-level optimizations, enabling streaming-aware scheduling and asynchronous inference, achieving 10–20x higher throughput at similar latency across multiple SpeechLMs; code released.
AI.motivation: The streaming deployment of Speech Language Models requires low latency, high throughput, and strong streamability, but existing systems poorly support diverse model architectures in a flexible, efficient manner.
AI.method: Introduce a model-execution abstraction that decouples model architecture from system optimizations, implement streaming-aware scheduling, and build an asynchronous inference pipeline within a unified VoxServe framework to support diverse SpeechLM architectures.
AI.result: Empirical evaluations show VoxServe achieves 10–20x higher throughput than existing implementations at comparable latency across multiple modern SpeechLMs, with strong streaming viability.
AI.conclusion: Decoupling model architecture from system optimizations and employing streaming-aware scheduling enables efficient, flexible streaming deployment of SpeechLMs within a single framework; VoxServe is open-source (GitHub).

title: AgenticLab: A Real-World Robot Agent Platform that Can See, Think, and Act
summary: Recent advances in large vision-language models (VLMs) have demonstrated generalizable open-vocabulary perception and reasoning, yet their real-robot manipulation capability remains unclear for long-horizon, closed-loop execution in unstructured, in-the-wild environments. Prior VLM-based manipulation pipelines are difficult to compare across different research groups' setups, and many evaluations rely on simulation, privileged state, or specially designed setups. We present AgenticLab, a model-agnostic robot agent platform and benchmark for open-world manipulation. AgenticLab provides a closed-loop agent pipeline for perception, task decomposition, online verification, and replanning. Using AgenticLab, we benchmark state-of-the-art VLM-based agents on real-robot tasks in unstructured environments. Our benchmark reveals several failure modes that offline vision-language tests (e.g., VQA and static image understanding) fail to capture, including breakdowns in multi-step grounding consistency, object grounding under occlusion and scene changes, and insufficient spatial reasoning for reliable manipulation. We will release the full hardware and software stack to support reproducible evaluation and accelerate research on general-purpose robot agents.

AI.tldr: AgenticLab provides a model-agnostic, hardware-in-the-loop benchmark for open-world robot manipulation, revealing failure modes not captured by offline tests and enabling reproducible evaluation of VLM-based agents in real-world, unstructured environments.
AI.motivation: To address the gap between impressive open-vocabulary perception in large vision-language models and robust long-horizon manipulation in unstructured real-world settings; existing evaluations are often non-standardized, simulated, or rely on privileged data.
AI.method: Introduce AgenticLab as a closed-loop robot agent platform with perception, task decomposition, online verification, and replanning. Benchmark state-of-the-art VLM-based agents on real-robot tasks in unstructured environments and analyze failure modes beyond offline VQA/image understanding tests. Commit to releasing hardware/software for reproducibility.
AI.result: Identification of failure modes not captured by offline tests: breakdowns in multi-step grounding consistency, object grounding under occlusion and scene changes, and insufficient spatial reasoning for manipulation. Demonstrates gaps between offline metrics and real-robot performance. Provides a reproducible benchmark and open-source stack.
AI.conclusion: AgenticLab enables standardized, reproducible evaluation of general-purpose robot agents in real-world settings and highlights critical deficiencies in current VLM-based manipulation approaches, guiding future research toward more robust grounding, spatial reasoning, and planning in open-world contexts.

title: Beyond Output Critique: Self-Correction via Task Distillation
summary: Large language models (LLMs) have shown promising self-correction abilities, where iterative refinement improves the quality of generated responses. However, most existing approaches operate at the level of output critique, patching surface errors while often failing to correct deeper reasoning flaws. We propose SELF-THOUGHT, a framework that introduces an intermediate step of task abstraction before solution refinement. Given an input and an initial response, the model first distills the task into a structured template that captures key variables, constraints, and problem structure. This abstraction then guides solution instantiation, grounding subsequent responses in a clearer understanding of the task and reducing error propagation. Crucially, we show that these abstractions can be transferred across models: templates generated by larger models can serve as structured guides for smaller LLMs, which typically struggle with intrinsic self-correction. By reusing distilled task structures, smaller models achieve more reliable refinements without heavy fine-tuning or reliance on external verifiers. Experiments across diverse reasoning tasks demonstrate that SELF-THOUGHT improves accuracy, robustness, and generalization for both large and small models, offering a scalable path toward more reliable self-correcting language systems.

AI.tldr: SELF-THOUGHT introduces a task-abstraction step before self-correction; a structured template distilled from the input guides solution refinement and can transfer from larger to smaller models to improve robustness without fine-tuning.
AI.motivation: Patch-based critique often misses deeper reasoning flaws; need a structured task representation that clarifies variables, constraints, and structure, enabling scalable, cross-model improvements.
AI.method: From input and initial response, distill a structured task template capturing key variables, constraints, and problem structure; use this abstraction to guide solution instantiation; transfer templates generated by larger models to smaller models to improve self-correction; evaluate across diverse reasoning tasks.
AI.result: Improved accuracy, robustness, and generalization for both large and small models; smaller models benefit from transfer without heavy fine-tuning or external verifiers.
AI.conclusion: Task-abstraction-based self-correction is scalable and reduces error propagation; cross-model transfer expands reliability of self-correcting LLMs.

title: Sample Complexity Analysis for Constrained Bilevel Reinforcement Learning
summary: Several important problem settings within the literature of reinforcement learning (RL), such as meta-learning, hierarchical learning, and RL from human feedback (RL-HF), can be modelled as bilevel RL problems. A lot has been achieved in these domains empirically; however, the theoretical analysis of bilevel RL algorithms hasn't received a lot of attention. In this work, we analyse the sample complexity of a constrained bilevel RL algorithm, building on the progress in the unconstrained setting. We obtain an iteration complexity of $O(ε^{-2})$ and sample complexity of $\tilde{O}(ε^{-4})$ for our proposed algorithm, Constrained Bilevel Subgradient Optimization (CBSO). We use a penalty-based objective function to avoid the issue of primal-dual gap and hyper-gradient in the context of a constrained bilevel problem setting. The penalty-based formulation to handle constraints requires analysis of non-smooth optimization. We are the first ones to analyse the generally parameterized policy gradient-based RL algorithm with a non-smooth objective function using the Moreau envelope.

AI.tldr: Constrained Bilevel Subgradient Optimization (CBSO) achieves O(ε^-2) iteration complexity and Õ(ε^-4) sample complexity for constrained bilevel RL via a penalty-based objective and Moreau envelope to handle non-smoothness; first analysis of parameterized policy gradient RL with non-smooth objective.
AI.motivation: Bilevel RL captures settings like meta-learning, hierarchical RL, and RL from human feedback. Theoretical analysis is lacking for bilevel RL; this work seeks sample-efficient guarantees under constraints.
AI.method: Proposes CBSO with a penalty-based objective to avoid primal-dual gaps in constrained bilevel problems; analyzes non-smooth optimization using Moreau envelope within a policy-gradient RL framework.
AI.result: Derives iteration complexity O(ε^-2) and sample complexity Õ(ε^-4) for CBSO; extends RL theory to non-smooth objectives via Moreau envelope; provides the first such analysis for parameterized policy gradient RL with non-smooth objectives.
AI.conclusion: Shows penalty-based constrained bilevel RL can be theoretically tractable with concrete complexity guarantees; introduces Moreau envelope-based analysis in RL, suggesting directions for practical and theoretical extensions in bilevel RL settings.

title: Towards Autonomous Instrument Tray Assembly for Sterile Processing Applications
comment: 7 pages, 9 figures, 2026 International Symposium on Medical Robotics
summary: The Sterile Processing and Distribution (SPD) department is responsible for cleaning, disinfecting, inspecting, and assembling surgical instruments between surgeries. Manual inspection and preparation of instrument trays is a time-consuming, error-prone task, often prone to contamination and instrument breakage. In this work, we present a fully automated robotic system that sorts and structurally packs surgical instruments into sterile trays, focusing on automation of the SPD assembly stage. A custom dataset comprising 31 surgical instruments and 6,975 annotated images was collected to train a hybrid perception pipeline using YOLO12 for detection and a cascaded ResNet-based model for fine-grained classification. The system integrates a calibrated vision module, a 6-DOF Staubli TX2-60L robotic arm with a custom dual electromagnetic gripper, and a rule-based packing algorithm that reduces instrument collisions during transport. The packing framework uses 3D printed dividers and holders to physically isolate instruments, reducing collision and friction during transport. Experimental evaluations show high perception accuracy and statistically significant reduction in tool-to-tool collisions compared to human-assembled trays. This work serves as the scalable first step toward automating SPD workflows, improving safety, and consistency of surgical preparation while reducing SPD processing times.

AI.tldr: An automated SPD packaging system uses vision-based detection/classification and a 6-DOF robot with a dual gripper plus a rule-based packing algorithm to sort and pack surgical instruments, reducing collisions and processing time versus manual assembly.
AI.motivation: Manual inspection and preparation of instrument trays is slow, error-prone, and susceptible to contamination and instrument damage, motivating automation to improve safety, consistency, and efficiency in SPD workflows.
AI.method: A hybrid perception pipeline using YOLO12 for instrument detection and a cascaded ResNet for fine-grained classification is trained on a dataset of 31 instruments (6,975 images). A calibrated vision module guides a Staubli TX2-60L 6-DOF robotic arm with a custom dual electromagnetic gripper. A rule-based packing algorithm with 3D-printed dividers/holders mitigates instrument collisions during transport.
AI.result: High perception accuracy achieved; the system significantly reduces tool-to-tool collisions in comparison to human-assembled trays, indicating improved safety and consistency and potential reductions in processing time.
AI.conclusion: This work demonstrates a scalable, first-step automation of SPD workflows by integrating vision, manipulation, and packing planning to enhance safety, consistency, and throughput in sterile instrument preparation.

title: Synapse Compendium Aware Federated Knowledge Exchange for Tool Routed LLMs
summary: Collaborative learning among LLM-based agents under federated learning faces challenges, including communication costs, heterogeneity in data, and tool-usage, limiting their effectiveness. We introduce Synapse, a framework that trains a shared global knowledge model of tool-usage behavior. Client agents with fixed LLMs learn tool-usage patterns locally, and transmit artifacts for federated aggregation through coordinators. A global tool compendium is updated and redistributed, enabling convergence toward stable tool selection. Synapse uses templated representations, embedding retrieval with LLM reranking, and adaptive masking to maintain utility while limiting information leakage. The framework supports heterogeneous data and quantifies performance improvements. Results show that Synapse improves tool-usage effectiveness and reduces communication overhead compared with weight or prompt-sharing approaches in multi-agent LLM systems.

AI.tldr: Synapse introduces a federated framework for LLM-based agents to learn tool-usage behavior by training a shared global knowledge model, reducing communication and leakage while handling heterogeneous data.
AI.motivation: To overcome communication costs, data heterogeneity, and tool-usage coordination challenges in collaborative LLM agent systems under federated learning.
AI.method: Clients with fixed LLMs learn tool-usage locally and send artifacts to coordinators; a global tool compendium is updated and redistributed; representations are templated; embedding retrieval with LLM reranking; adaptive masking to limit leakage; supports heterogeneous data.
AI.result: Compared with weight-sharing or prompt-sharing baselines, Synapse improves tool-usage effectiveness and reduces communication overhead.
AI.conclusion: Synapse enables convergence toward stable tool selection and scalable coordination in multi-agent LLM systems, with privacy-preserving features and adaptability to heterogeneity.

title: Generation Order and Parallel Decoding in Masked Diffusion Models: An Information-Theoretic Perspective
summary: Masked Diffusion Models (MDMs) significantly accelerate inference by trading off sequential determinism. However, the theoretical mechanisms governing generation order and the risks inherent in parallelization remain under-explored. In this work, we provide a unified information-theoretic framework to decouple and analyze two fundamental sources of failure: order sensitivity and parallelization bias. Our analysis yields three key insights: (1) The benefits of Easy-First decoding (prioritizing low-entropy tokens) are magnified as model error increases; (2) factorized parallel decoding introduces intrinsic sampling errors that can lead to arbitrary large Reverse KL divergence, capturing "incoherence" failures that standard Forward KL metrics overlook; and (3) while verification can eliminate sampling error, it incurs an exponential cost governed by the total correlation within a block. Conversely, heuristics like remasking, though computationally efficient, cannot guarantee distributional correctness. Experiments on a controlled Block-HMM and large-scale MDMs (LLaDA) for arithmetic reasoning validate our theoretical framework.

AI.tldr: Unified information-theoretic analysis of Masked Diffusion Models reveals how order sensitivity and parallelization bias drive failures. Easy-First decoding helps more when model error is high; factorized parallel decoding introduces sampling errors causing potentially large Reverse KL divergence; verification removes sampling error but with exponential cost in block total correlation; remasking cannot guarantee distributional correctness. Empirical validation on Block-HMM and LLaDA MDMs for arithmetic reasoning supports the framework.
AI.motivation: To understand the theoretical mechanisms governing generation order and parallelization risks in Masked Diffusion Models (MDMs) and to provide a unified framework that decouples order sensitivity from parallelization bias, enabling quantitative analysis of trade-offs and failure modes.
AI.method: Develop a unified information-theoretic framework to separate order sensitivity and parallelization bias in MDMs; derive insights about easy-first decoding, parallel decoding sampling errors (Reverse KL divergence), and verification costs; analyze the exponential cost tied to total correlation within a block; compare with remasking heuristics; validate with experiments on a controlled Block-HMM and large-scale MDMs (LLaDA) on arithmetic reasoning tasks.
AI.result: Three key insights: (1) Easy-First decoding yields greater benefits as model error increases. (2) Factorized parallel decoding introduces intrinsic sampling errors that can cause arbitrarily large Reverse KL divergence, capturing incoherence not reflected by Forward KL. (3) Verification can eliminate sampling error but incurs exponential cost governed by the total correlation within a block. Remasking is computationally efficient but cannot guarantee distributional correctness. Empirical validation on a controlled Block-HMM and LLaDA MDMs for arithmetic reasoning corroborates the theory.
AI.conclusion: MDMs offer fast inference at the cost of fundamental trade-offs between order sensitivity, parallelization bias, and distributional correctness. The proposed information-theoretic lens clarifies when and why certain decoding strategies work or fail, highlights the prohibitive cost of exact verification under high total correlation, and explains why simple heuristics like remasking may be insufficient. The work motivates principled decoding design and efficient approximations to manage sampling error while balancing computational budgets.

title: GSR: Learning Structured Reasoning for Embodied Manipulation
summary: Despite rapid progress, embodied agents still struggle with long-horizon manipulation that requires maintaining spatial consistency, causal dependencies, and goal constraints. A key limitation of existing approaches is that task reasoning is implicitly embedded in high-dimensional latent representations, making it challenging to separate task structure from perceptual variability. We introduce Grounded Scene-graph Reasoning (GSR), a structured reasoning paradigm that explicitly models world-state evolution as transitions over semantically grounded scene graphs. By reasoning step-wise over object states and spatial relations, rather than directly mapping perception to actions, GSR enables explicit reasoning about action preconditions, consequences, and goal satisfaction in a physically grounded space. To support learning such reasoning, we construct Manip-Cognition-1.6M, a large-scale dataset that jointly supervises world understanding, action planning, and goal interpretation. Extensive evaluations across RLBench, LIBERO, GSR-benchmark, and real-world robotic tasks show that GSR significantly improves zero-shot generalization and long-horizon task completion over prompting-based baselines. These results highlight explicit world-state representations as a key inductive bias for scalable embodied reasoning.

AI.tldr: GSR introduces structured reasoning over grounded scene graphs to model world-state evolution for long-horizon manipulation, distinguishing task structure from perception. It pairs GSR with a large-scale Manip-Cognition-1.6M dataset and shows improved zero-shot generalization and long-horizon task completion across benchmarks, arguing explicit world-state representations as a key inductive bias.
AI.motivation: Existing embodied agents rely on latent, high-dimensional representations where task structure is entangled with perceptual variability, hindering separation of preconditions and consequences needed for long-horizon planning.
AI.method: Propose Grounded Scene-graph Reasoning (GSR) that reasons step-wise over semantically grounded object states and spatial relations to infer action preconditions, consequences, and goal satisfaction in a physically grounded state space. Create Manip-Cognition-1.6M to jointly supervise world understanding, action planning, and goal interpretation. Evaluate on RLBench, LIBERO, GSR-benchmark, and real-world tasks.
AI.result: GSR significantly improves zero-shot generalization and long-horizon task completion compared with prompting-based baselines, across multiple benchmarks and real-world experiments.
AI.conclusion: Explicit, world-state representations via scene-graph reasoning serve as a stronger inductive bias for scalable embodied reasoning, enabling clearer separation between perception and task structure and better generalization.

title: Supervised sparse auto-encoders as unconstrained feature models for semantic composition
summary: Sparse auto-encoders (SAEs) have re-emerged as a prominent method for mechanistic interpretability, yet they face two significant challenges: the non-smoothness of the $L_1$ penalty, which hinders reconstruction and scalability, and a lack of alignment between learned features and human semantics. In this paper, we address these limitations by adapting unconstrained feature models-a mathematical framework from neural collapse theory-and by supervising the task. We supervise (decoder-only) SAEs to reconstruct feature vectors by jointly learning sparse concept embeddings and decoder weights. Validated on Stable Diffusion 3.5, our approach demonstrates compositional generalization, successfully reconstructing images with concept combinations unseen during training, and enabling feature-level intervention for semantic image editing without prompt modification.

AI.tldr: Supervised decoder-only sparse auto-encoders guided by unconstrained feature models improve reconstruction and semantic alignment, enabling compositional generalization and feature-level semantic editing without prompt changes.
AI.motivation: Address two core issues in SAEs: (1) non-smooth L1 sparsity that hurts reconstruction and scalability, and (2) misalignment between learned features and human semantics, hindering interpretability.
AI.method: Adapt unconstrained feature models from neural collapse theory and supervise decoder-only SAEs to reconstruct feature vectors by jointly learning sparse concept embeddings and decoder weights.
AI.result: On Stable Diffusion 3.5, demonstrate compositional generalization to unseen concept combinations and enable feature-level interventions for semantic image editing without modifying prompts.
AI.conclusion: The approach improves mechanistic interpretability by aligning sparse features with human semantics and enabling controllable semantic edits via learned concept embeddings and decoder parameters.

title: Self-Attention at Constant Cost per Token via Symmetry-Aware Taylor Approximation
comment: For source code and replication instructions, see https://github.com/glassroom/sata_attention. 12 pages, 6 figures (main); 4 pages, 2 figures (appendix)
summary: The most widely used artificial intelligence (AI) models today are Transformers employing self-attention. In its standard form, self-attention incurs costs that increase with context length, driving demand for storage, compute, and energy that is now outstripping society's ability to provide them. To help address this issue, we show that self-attention is efficiently computable to arbitrary precision with constant cost per token, achieving orders-of-magnitude reductions in memory use and computation. We derive our formulation by decomposing the conventional formulation's Taylor expansion into expressions over symmetric chains of tensor products. We exploit their symmetry to obtain feed-forward transformations that efficiently map queries and keys to coordinates in a minimal polynomial-kernel feature basis. Notably, cost is fixed inversely in proportion to head size, enabling application over a greater number of heads per token than otherwise feasible. We implement our formulation and empirically validate its correctness. Our work enables unbounded token generation at modest fixed cost, substantially reducing the infrastructure and energy demands of large-scale Transformer models. The mathematical techniques we introduce are of independent interest.

AI.tldr: Transformers can achieve constant-time per-token self-attention with respect to context length by a Taylor-expansion-based decomposition over symmetric tensor products, mapping queries/keys into a fixed polynomial-kernel feature basis; this enables unbounded token generation with substantial memory and compute savings.
AI.motivation: Mitigate rising storage, compute, and energy costs of self-attention at long context lengths to enable scalable, energy-efficient large-scale transformers.
AI.method: Decompose the standard self-attention (via Taylor expansion) into expressions over symmetric chains of tensor products; leverage symmetry to derive feed-forward transforms that project queries and keys into coordinates in a minimal polynomial-kernel feature basis; make the cost fixed inversely to the number of heads, allowing more heads per token; implement and empirically validate the formulation.
AI.result: Empirical validation confirms correctness; achieves unbounded token generation at fixed cost with substantial reductions in memory and computation, and therefore energy demands; the mathematical techniques are of independent interest.
AI.conclusion: Offers a principled, scalable alternative to traditional self-attention with fixed per-token cost, enabling larger-scale models and longer context without prohibitive resource use; the approach introduces novel symmetric-tensor and kernel-feature techniques that could influence future work in attention mechanisms.

title: Tilt-Ropter: A Novel Hybrid Aerial and Terrestrial Vehicle with Tilt Rotors and Passive Wheels
summary: In this work, we present Tilt-Ropter, a novel hybrid aerial-terrestrial vehicle (HATV) that combines tilt rotors with passive wheels to achieve energy-efficient multi-mode locomotion. Unlike existing under-actuated HATVs, the fully actuated design of Tilt-Ropter enables decoupled force and torque control, greatly enhancing its mobility and environmental adaptability. A nonlinear model predictive controller (NMPC) is developed to track reference trajectories and handle contact constraints across locomotion modes, while a dedicated control allocation module exploits actuation redundancy to achieve energy-efficient control of actuators. Additionally, to enhance robustness during ground contact, we introduce an external wrench estimation algorithm that estimates environmental interaction forces and torques in real time. The system is validated through both simulation and real-world experiments, including seamless air-ground transitions and trajectory tracking. Results show low tracking errors in both modes and highlight a 92.8% reduction in power consumption during ground locomotion, demonstrating the system's potential for long-duration missions across large-scale and energy-constrained environments.

AI.tldr: A fully actuated hybrid aerial-terrestrial vehicle (Tilt-Ropter) uses tilt rotors plus wheels to enable energy-efficient, multi-mode locomotion; it employs NMPC for trajectory tracking and contact constraints, with a redundancy-based control allocation and real-time external wrench estimation, validated in simulation and experiments, achieving seamless transitions and a 92.8% power reduction on ground locomotion.
AI.motivation: Address energy efficiency and environmental adaptability in hybrid aerial-terrestrial systems by enabling decoupled force/torque control and robust ground contact handling.
AI.method: Design a fully actuated HATV with tilt rotors and passive wheels; develop NMPC for tracking and contact constraints across modes; implement a control allocation module to exploit actuation redundancy for energy efficiency; introduce real-time external wrench estimation for robust ground contact; validate via simulations and real-world experiments including air-ground transitions and trajectory tracking.
AI.result: Low tracking errors in air and ground modes; seamless air-ground transitions; 92.8% reduction in power consumption during ground locomotion; evidence of energy-efficient long-duration operation.
AI.conclusion: This work demonstrates the feasibility of energy-efficient, robust multi-mode locomotion for HATVs and highlights potential for long-duration, large-scale missions in energy-constrained environments.

title: Learning Abstractions for Hierarchical Planning in Program-Synthesis Agents
summary: Humans learn abstractions and use them to plan efficiently to quickly generalize across tasks -- an ability that remains challenging for state-of-the-art large language model (LLM) agents and deep reinforcement learning (RL) systems. Inspired by the cognitive science of how people form abstractions and intuitive theories of their world knowledge, Theory-Based RL (TBRL) systems, such as TheoryCoder, exhibit strong generalization through effective use of abstractions. However, they heavily rely on human-provided abstractions and sidestep the abstraction-learning problem. We introduce TheoryCoder-2, a new TBRL agent that leverages LLMs' in-context learning ability to actively learn reusable abstractions rather than relying on hand-specified ones, by synthesizing abstractions from experience and integrating them into a hierarchical planning process. We conduct experiments on diverse environments, including BabyAI, Minihack and VGDL games like Sokoban. We find that TheoryCoder-2 is significantly more sample-efficient than baseline LLM agents augmented with classical planning domain construction, reasoning-based planning, and prior program-synthesis agents such as WorldCoder. TheoryCoder-2 is able to solve complex tasks that the baselines fail, while only requiring minimal human prompts, unlike prior TBRL systems.

AI.tldr: TheoryCoder-2 enables LLM-based agents to learn reusable abstractions from experience and integrate them into hierarchical planning, achieving superior sample efficiency and task-solving on BabyAI, MiniHack, and Sokoban-like VGDL games compared to baselines.
AI.motivation: Humans form abstractions to generalize; current Theory-Based RL (TBRL) relies on hand-crafted abstractions, hindering scalability. Automatic abstraction learning is needed to improve generalization and sample efficiency in RL/AI agents.
AI.method: Present TheoryCoder-2, a TBRL agent that uses LLMs' in-context learning to actively synthesize reusable abstractions from experience and integrate them into a hierarchical planning process, reducing reliance on hand-specified abstractions.
AI.result: TheoryCoder-2 is significantly more sample-efficient than baseline LLM agents augmented with traditional planning/domain-construction approaches and prior program-synthesis agents (e.g., WorldCoder); it solves complex tasks that baselines fail and requires only minimal human prompts.
AI.conclusion: LLM-driven abstraction learning within a hierarchical planner can yield strong generalization and sample efficiency in TBRL, reducing the need for hand-crafted abstractions and enabling broader task generalization across diverse environments.

title: From Observations to States: Latent Time Series Forecasting
summary: Deep learning has achieved strong performance in Time Series Forecasting (TSF). However, we identify a critical representation paradox, termed Latent Chaos: models with accurate predictions often learn latent representations that are temporally disordered and lack continuity. We attribute this phenomenon to the dominant observation-space forecasting paradigm. Most TSF models minimize point-wise errors on noisy and partially observed data, which encourages shortcut solutions instead of the recovery of underlying system dynamics. To address this issue, we propose Latent Time Series Forecasting (LatentTSF), a novel paradigm that shifts TSF from observation regression to latent state prediction. Specifically, LatentTSF employs an AutoEncoder to project observations at each time step into a higher-dimensional latent state space. This expanded representation aims to capture underlying system variables and impose a smoother temporal structure. Forecasting is then performed entirely in the latent space, allowing the model to focus on learning structured temporal dynamics. Theoretical analysis demonstrates that our proposed latent objectives implicitly maximize mutual information between predicted latent states and ground-truth states and observations. Extensive experiments on widely-used benchmarks confirm that LatentTSF effectively mitigates latent chaos, achieving superior performance. Our code is available in https://github.com/Muyiiiii/LatentTSF.

AI.tldr: LatentTSF proposes forecasting in latent space via AutoEncoder to mitigate latent chaos in time-series forecasting, achieving improved performance and offering code release.
AI.motivation: Observation-space forecasting optimizes pointwise errors on noisy/partial data, which encourages temporally disordered latent representations and hinders recovery of true system dynamics.
AI.method: Train an AutoEncoder to map observations at each time step to a higher-dimensional latent state; perform forecasting entirely in latent space; latent objectives aim to preserve information about ground-truth states.
AI.result: Theoretical analysis shows latent objectives implicitly maximize mutual information with ground-truth and observations; empirical benchmarks demonstrate reduced latent chaos and improved forecasting performance.
AI.conclusion: LatentTSF introduces a latent-space forecasting paradigm that promotes smoother, more informative latent dynamics and superior TSF performance, with code available.

title: Uncertainty-Aware Non-Prehensile Manipulation with Mobile Manipulators under Object-Induced Occlusion
comment: 8 pages, 7 figures, Accepted to ICRA 2026, Webpage: https://jiw0o.github.io/cura-ppo/
summary: Non-prehensile manipulation using onboard sensing presents a fundamental challenge: the manipulated object occludes the sensor's field of view, creating occluded regions that can lead to collisions. We propose CURA-PPO, a reinforcement learning framework that addresses this challenge by explicitly modeling uncertainty under partial observability. By predicting collision possibility as a distribution, we extract both risk and uncertainty to guide the robot's actions. The uncertainty term encourages active perception, enabling simultaneous manipulation and information gathering to resolve occlusions. When combined with confidence maps that capture observation reliability, our approach enables safe navigation despite severe sensor occlusion. Extensive experiments across varying object sizes and obstacle configurations demonstrate that CURA-PPO achieves up to 3X higher success rates than the baselines, with learned behaviors that handle occlusions. Our method provides a practical solution for autonomous manipulation in cluttered environments using only onboard sensing.

AI.tldr: CURA-PPO is a reinforcement learning framework that handles occlusion in non-prehensile manipulation by modeling collision as a distribution under partial observability, using uncertainty to drive active perception and confidence maps for safer, onboard-sensing navigation; it achieves up to 3x higher success rates versus baselines.
AI.motivation: Non-prehensile manipulation with onboard sensing suffers from occluded sensor views when the object being manipulated occludes the field of view, causing unseen collisions. A method that explicitly handles uncertainty and promotes information gathering is needed for safe autonomous manipulation in cluttered environments.
AI.method: CURA-PPO extends PPO by explicitly modeling collision probability as a distribution under partial observability. It uses the predicted distribution to separate risk and uncertainty, where the uncertainty term encourages active perception and information gathering. Confidence maps representing observation reliability are integrated to improve safety, enabling simultaneous manipulation and perception. The framework is evaluated in varying object sizes and obstacle configurations.
AI.result: Empirical results show CURA-PPO achieves up to 3× higher success rates than baselines and develops learned behaviors that cope with occlusions. The approach enables safe navigation and manipulation using only onboard sensing in cluttered environments.
AI.conclusion: Modeling uncertainty under partial observability and integrating active perception through the RL objective yields safer, more robust autonomous manipulation with onboard sensing in occluded scenarios.

title: The Keyhole Effect: Why Chat Interfaces Fail at Data Analysis
summary: Chat has become the default interface for AI-assisted data analysis. For multi-step, state-dependent analytical tasks, this is a mistake. Building on Woods (1984) Keyhole Effect, the cognitive cost of viewing large information spaces through narrow viewports, I show that chat interfaces systematically degrade analytical performance through five mechanisms: (1) constant content displacement defeats hippocampal spatial memory systems; (2) hidden state variables exceed working memory capacity (approximately 4 chunks under load); (3) forced verbalization triggers verbal overshadowing, degrading visual pattern recognition; (4) linear text streams block epistemic action and cognitive offloading; (5) serialization penalties scale with data dimensionality. I formalize cognitive overload as O = max(0, m - v - W) where m is task-relevant items, v is visible items, and W is working memory capacity. When O > 0, error probability increases and analytical biases (anchoring, confirmation, change blindness) amplify. Eight hybrid design patterns address these failures: Generative UI, Infinite Canvas, Deictic Interaction, State Rail, Ghost Layers, Mise en Place, Semantic Zoom, and Probabilistic UI. Each pattern targets specific cognitive bottlenecks while preserving natural language for intent specification and synthesis. Well-scaffolded conversational systems that encode expert priors may reduce load for guided tasks; the framework applies most strongly to open-ended exploration. The paper concludes with falsifiable hypotheses and experimental paradigms for empirical validation.

AI.tldr: Chat as default interface degrades multi-step data analysis due to cognitive bottlenecks; proposes a formal overload model and eight hybrid design patterns to mitigate it, with hypotheses for empirical validation.
AI.motivation: To address cognitive costs of exploring large information spaces via narrow viewports and chat in AI-assisted analysis, building on Woods' Keyhole Effect; aims to preserve analytical performance and enable effective open-ended exploration.
AI.method: Grounded in cognitive psychology and human-computer interaction; identifies five mechanisms by which chat hinders analysis (content displacement, hidden states, verbalization, serial text streams, dimensionality-related penalties). Formalizes overload as O = max(0, m - v - W); proposes eight design patterns (Generative UI, Infinite Canvas, Deictic Interaction, State Rail, Ghost Layers, Mise en Place, Semantic Zoom, Probabilistic UI) to target bottlenecks while preserving natural language for intent and synthesis. Argues for well-scaffolded conversational systems and expert priors to reduce load; outlines experimental paradigms and falsifiable hypotheses for validation.
AI.result: A cohesive theoretical framework combining cognitive load theory with UI design patterns for AI-assisted analysis. The overload metric and identified bottlenecks provide a basis for measurement and comparison. The eight patterns map to specific cognitive bottlenecks and are intended to reduce error-prone heuristics, especially in open-ended exploration.
AI.conclusion: Offers falsifiable hypotheses and experimental paradigms to empirically validate the framework. The contribution is primarily theoretical, with practical UI patterns proposed to mitigate cognitive overload and preserve NL-based specification and synthesis.

title: Agentic Framework for Epidemiological Modeling
summary: Epidemic modeling is essential for public health planning, yet traditional approaches rely on fixed model classes that require manual redesign as pathogens, policies, and scenario assumptions evolve. We introduce EPIAGENT, an agentic framework that automatically synthesizes, calibrates, verifies, and refines epidemiological simulators by modeling disease progression as an iterative program synthesis problem. A central design choice is an explicit epidemiological flow graph intermediate representation that links scenario specifications to model structure and enables strong, modular correctness checks before code is generated. Verified flow graphs are then compiled into mechanistic models supporting interpretable parameter learning under physical and epidemiological constraints. Evaluation on epidemiological scenario case studies demonstrates that EPIAGENT captures complex growth dynamics and produces epidemiologically consistent counterfactual projections across varying vaccination and immune escape assumptions. Our results show that the agentic feedback loop prevents degeneration and significantly accelerates convergence toward valid models by mimicking professional expert workflows.

AI.tldr: EPIAGENT automates epidemiological model synthesis and refinement via an agentic, program-synthesis workflow centered on an explicit epidemiological flow-graph IR, enabling verified, constrained mechanistic models with interpretable parameter learning and robust counterfactuals.
AI.motivation: Traditional epidemiological modeling relies on fixed model classes that struggle to adapt to evolving pathogens, policies, and scenario assumptions. There is a need for automated, verifiable, and interpretable model synthesis that links scenario specifications to model structure and supports rapid iteration.
AI.method: Introduce an explicit epidemiological flow graph intermediate representation that ties scenario specs to model structure. Treat disease progression as an iterative program synthesis problem. Use verification on flow graphs before code generation, then compile verified graphs into mechanistic models that support constrained parameter learning under physical/epidemiological constraints. Employ an agentic feedback loop to mimic expert workflows and prevent degeneration.
AI.result: EPIAGENT captures complex growth dynamics and yields epidemiologically consistent counterfactual projections across varying vaccination and immune-escape assumptions. The agentic loop accelerates convergence toward valid models and improves stability by avoiding degeneration.
AI.conclusion: The framework delivers automated, verifiable, and interpretable epidemiological modeling that adapts to changing conditions and supports robust counterfactual analysis, with potential to generalize beyond traditional fixed-model approaches.

title: RFS: Reinforcement learning with Residual flow steering for dexterous manipulation
summary: Imitation learning has emerged as an effective approach for bootstrapping sequential decision-making in robotics, achieving strong performance even in high-dimensional dexterous manipulation tasks. Recent behavior cloning methods further leverage expressive generative models, such as diffusion models and flow matching, to represent multimodal action distributions. However, policies pretrained in this manner often exhibit limited generalization and require additional fine-tuning to achieve robust performance at deployment time. Such adaptation must preserve the global exploration benefits of pretraining while enabling rapid correction of local execution errors. We propose Residual Flow Steering(RFS), a data-efficient reinforcement learning framework for adapting pretrained generative policies. RFS steers a pretrained flow-matching policy by jointly optimizing a residual action and a latent noise distribution, enabling complementary forms of exploration: local refinement through residual corrections and global exploration through latent-space modulation. This design allows efficient adaptation while retaining the expressive structure of the pretrained policy. We demonstrate the effectiveness of RFS on dexterous manipulation tasks, showing efficient fine-tuning in both simulation and real-world settings when adapting pretrained base policies. Project website:https://weirdlabuw.github.io/rfs.

AI.tldr: A data-efficient RL framework (RFS) for adapting pretrained generative policies (flow matching) via residual actions and latent noise modulation, enabling local refinement and global exploration for dexterous manipulation.
AI.motivation: Pretrained generative policies (e.g., diffusion models, flow matching) can model multimodal actions but often generalize poorly and require fine-tuning; there is a need to preserve global exploration while correcting local execution errors at deployment.
AI.method: Residual Flow Steering (RFS) jointly optimizes a residual action added to the pretrained policy output and a learned latent noise distribution. This dual optimization enables local refinements through residual corrections and global exploration via latent-space modulation, preserving the expressive structure of the pretrained policy while enabling data-efficient adaptation.
AI.result: Demonstrates efficient fine-tuning on dexterous manipulation tasks in both simulation and real-world settings when adapting pretrained base policies.
AI.conclusion: RFS provides a practical, data-efficient approach to adapt pretrained generative policies by combining local residual corrections with global latent exploration, retaining the policy’s expressive structure and enabling robust deployment in robotics.

title: MindGuard: Guardrail Classifiers for Multi-Turn Mental Health Support
summary: Large language models are increasingly used for mental health support, yet their conversational coherence alone does not ensure clinical appropriateness. Existing general-purpose safeguards often fail to distinguish between therapeutic disclosures and genuine clinical crises, leading to safety failures. To address this gap, we introduce a clinically grounded risk taxonomy, developed in collaboration with PhD-level psychologists, that identifies actionable harm (e.g., self-harm and harm to others) while preserving space for safe, non-crisis therapeutic content. We release MindGuard-testset, a dataset of real-world multi-turn conversations annotated at the turn level by clinical experts. Using synthetic dialogues generated via a controlled two-agent setup, we train MindGuard, a family of lightweight safety classifiers (with 4B and 8B parameters). Our classifiers reduce false positives at high-recall operating points and, when paired with clinician language models, help achieve lower attack success and harmful engagement rates in adversarial multi-turn interactions compared to general-purpose safeguards. We release all models and human evaluation data.

AI.tldr: Clinically grounded safety framework for mental-health LLMs using MindGuard: a 4B/8B-parameter classifier trained on real and synthetic data with a dedicated risk taxonomy; improves safety against adversarial interactions and reduces false positives, with released models and evaluation data.
AI.motivation: General safety guards fail to distinguish therapeutic disclosures from clinical crises in mental-health AI. A clinically grounded risk taxonomy co-developed with psychologists aims to identify actionable harm while preserving safe therapeutic content.
AI.method: Develop a clinically grounded risk taxonomy; create MindGuard-testset with expert-annotated real-world multi-turn conversations; generate synthetic dialogues via a controlled two-agent setup; train lightweight classifiers (4B/8B) using these data; evaluate at high-recall points and against adversarial multi-turn scenarios; pair with clinician-language models for improved safety; release models and human evaluation data.
AI.result: MindGuard reduces false positives at high-recall operating points; when used with clinician LMs, achieves lower attack success and harmful engagement rates in adversarial multi-turn interactions compared to general-purpose safeguards.
AI.conclusion: Provides a clinically grounded framework and resources (taxonomy, testset, models, and evaluation data) to improve safety of mental-health LLMs and facilitate safer deployment.

title: Neural Ising Machines via Unrolling and Zeroth-Order Training
summary: We propose a data-driven heuristic for NP-hard Ising and Max-Cut optimization that learns the update rule of an iterative dynamical system. The method learns a shared, node-wise update rule that maps local interaction fields to spin updates, parameterized by a compact multilayer perceptron with a small number of parameters. Training is performed using a zeroth-order optimizer, since backpropagation through long, recurrent Ising-machine dynamics leads to unstable and poorly informative gradients. We call this approach a neural network parameterized Ising machine (NPIM). Despite its low parameter count, the learned dynamics recover effective algorithmic structure, including momentum-like behavior and time-varying schedules, enabling efficient search in highly non-convex energy landscapes. Across standard Ising and neural combinatorial optimization benchmarks, NPIM achieves competitive solution quality and time-to-solution relative to recent learning-based methods and strong classical Ising-machine heuristics.

AI.tldr: A data-driven neural update rule for Ising/Max-Cut optimization (NPIM) learned via zeroth-order optimization; competitive with baselines on standard benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Merge learning flexibility with Ising dynamics to improve search in NP-hard combinatorial landscapes while avoiding unstable backpropagation through long recurrent dynamics.
AI.method: Learn a shared, node-wise update rule parameterized by a compact MLP that maps local interaction fields to spin updates; train with a zeroth-order optimizer due to unstable gradients in recurrent Ising-machine dynamics.
AI.result: NPIM achieves competitive solution quality and time-to-solution on standard Ising and neural combinatorial optimization benchmarks, relative to recent learning-based methods and strong classical Ising-machine heuristics.
AI.conclusion: Compact learned dynamics can recover algorithmic structure (e.g., momentum-like behavior, time-varying schedules) and enable efficient search in challenging non-convex energy landscapes despite a small parameter footprint.

title: From Knowing to Doing Precisely: A General Self-Correction and Termination Framework for VLA models
comment: Accepted to 2026 IEEE International Conference on Acoustics, Speech, and Signal Processing (ICASSP 2026)
summary: While vision-language-action (VLA) models for embodied agents integrate perception, reasoning, and control, they remain constrained by two critical weaknesses: first, during grasping tasks, the action tokens generated by the language model often exhibit subtle spatial deviations from the target object, resulting in grasp failures; second, they lack the ability to reliably recognize task completion, which leads to redundant actions and frequent timeout errors. To address these challenges and enhance robustness, we propose a lightweight, training-free framework, VLA-SCT. This framework operates as a self-correcting control loop, combining data-driven action refinement with conditional logic for termination. Consequently, compared to baseline approaches, our method achieves consistent improvements across all datasets in the LIBERO benchmark, significantly increasing the success rate of fine manipulation tasks and ensuring accurate task completion, thereby promoting the deployment of more reliable VLA agents in complex, unstructured environments.

AI.tldr: VLA-SCT is a lightweight, training-free self-correcting loop that refines action tokens and adds termination logic to improve robustness of vision-language-action agents, achieving higher grasp success and correct task completion on LIBERO benchmarks.
AI.motivation: VLA agents suffer from (i) subtle spatial deviations in action tokens during grasping leading to failures, and (ii) poor recognition of task completion causing redundant actions and timeouts.
AI.method: A self-correcting control loop that combines data-driven action refinement with conditional termination logic. The framework is lightweight and training-free, designed to augment existing VLA pipelines without additional training.
AI.result: Consistent improvements across all LIBERO benchmark datasets, with higher success rates in fine manipulation tasks and more reliable task completion relative to baselines.
AI.conclusion: Enhances the robustness and reliability of vision-language-action agents in unstructured environments, facilitating deployment in complex manipulation scenarios.

title: R-HTN: Rebellious Online HTN Planning for Safety and Game AI
summary: We introduce online Hierarchical Task Network (HTN) agents whose behaviors are governed by a set of built-in directives \D. Like other agents that are capable of rebellion (i.e., {\it intelligent disobedience}), our agents will, under some conditions, not perform a user-assigned task and instead act in ways that do not meet a user's expectations. Our work combines three concepts: HTN planning, online planning, and the directives \D, which must be considered when performing user-assigned tasks. We investigate two agent variants: (1) a Nonadaptive agent that stops execution if it finds itself in violation of \D~ and (2) an Adaptive agent that, in the same situation, instead modifies its HTN plan to search for alternative ways to achieve its given task. We present R-HTN (for: Rebellious-HTN), a general algorithm for online HTN planning under directives \D. We evaluate R-HTN in two task domains where the agent must not violate some directives for safety reasons or as dictated by their personality traits. We found that R-HTN agents never violate directives, and aim to achieve the user-given goals if feasible though not necessarily as the user expected.

AI.tldr: Introduces R-HTN, an online HTN planning framework with directives D; analyzes Nonadaptive and Adaptive variants; guarantees directive compliance and attempts feasible user goals via the R-HTN algorithm.
AI.motivation: Address intelligent disobedience by embedding safety/personality directives into online HTN planning; reconcile user tasks with constraints.
AI.method: Proposes R-HTN algorithm for online HTN planning under directives D; defines two agent variants (Nonadaptive and Adaptive); evaluates in two task domains.
AI.result: R-HTN agents never violate directives; aim to fulfill user goals when feasible, though results may differ from user expectations.
AI.conclusion: R-HTN provides a general framework for compliant online HTN planning and demonstrates directive adherence in constrained domains.

title: Beyond the Loss Curve: Scaling Laws, Active Learning, and the Limits of Learning from Exact Posteriors
summary: How close are neural networks to the best they could possibly do? Standard benchmarks cannot answer this because they lack access to the true posterior p(y|x). We use class-conditional normalizing flows as oracles that make exact posteriors tractable on realistic images (AFHQ, ImageNet). This enables five lines of investigation. Scaling laws: Prediction error decomposes into irreducible aleatoric uncertainty and reducible epistemic error; the epistemic component follows a power law in dataset size, continuing to shrink even when total loss plateaus. Limits of learning: The aleatoric floor is exactly measurable, and architectures differ markedly in how they approach it: ResNets exhibit clean power-law scaling while Vision Transformers stall in low-data regimes. Soft labels: Oracle posteriors contain learnable structure beyond class labels: training with exact posteriors outperforms hard labels and yields near-perfect calibration. Distribution shift: The oracle computes exact KL divergence of controlled perturbations, revealing that shift type matters more than shift magnitude: class imbalance barely affects accuracy at divergence values where input noise causes catastrophic degradation. Active learning: Exact epistemic uncertainty distinguishes genuinely informative samples from inherently ambiguous ones, improving sample efficiency. Our framework reveals that standard metrics hide ongoing learning, mask architectural differences, and cannot diagnose the nature of distribution shift.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces class-conditional normalizing flows as oracle posteriors to quantify how close neural networks are to the best possible performance, revealing scaling laws, limits of learning, benefits of soft labels, distribution-shift sensitivities, and gains from active learning.
AI.motivation: Standard benchmarks only measure end-task performance without access to the true posterior p(y|x). By making posteriors tractable, the study can decompose error into irreducible (aleatoric) and reducible (epistemic) components and assess learning dynamics across architectures and data regimes on realistic images.
AI.method: Train class-conditional normalizing flows as oracle posteriors for realistic image datasets (AFHQ, ImageNet). Use these exact posteriors to (i) decompose error and study scaling laws, (ii) quantify the aleatoric floor across architectures, (iii) compare training with soft vs hard labels, (iv) measure exact KL divergences under controlled distribution shifts, and (v) evaluate active learning using exact epistemic uncertainty.
AI.result: Key findings: (1) Epistemic error follows a power law with dataset size and decreases even after total loss plateaus, (2) The aleatoric floor is measurable; ResNets achieve clean power-law scaling while Vision Transformers stall in low-data regimes, (3) Exact posteriors enable better calibration; soft-training with oracle posteriors outperforms hard labels, (4) Shift type matters more than magnitude for performance degradation as measured by KL divergence, with class imbalance having little impact at high divergence, (5) Exact epistemic uncertainty improves sample efficiency in active learning.
AI.conclusion: Standard benchmarks obscure ongoing learning, mask architectural differences, and fail to reveal the true nature of distribution shifts; using oracle posteriors provides a framework to diagnose and understand learning dynamics and model robustness more faithfully.

title: Concept-Based Dictionary Learning for Inference-Time Safety in Vision Language Action Models
summary: Vision Language Action (VLA) models close the perception action loop by translating multimodal instructions into executable behaviors, but this very capability magnifies safety risks: jailbreaks that merely yield toxic text in LLMs can trigger unsafe physical actions in embodied systems. Existing defenses alignment, filtering, or prompt hardening intervene too late or at the wrong modality, leaving fused representations exploitable. We introduce a concept-based dictionary learning framework for inference-time safety control. By constructing sparse, interpretable dictionaries from hidden activations, our method identifies harmful concept directions and applies threshold-based interventions to suppress or block unsafe activations. Experiments on Libero-Harm, BadRobot, RoboPair, and IS-Bench show that our approach achieves state-of-the-art defense performance, cutting attack success rates by over 70\% while maintaining task success. Crucially, the framework is plug-in and model-agnostic, requiring no retraining and integrating seamlessly with diverse VLAs. To our knowledge, this is the first inference-time concept-based safety method for embodied systems, advancing both interpretability and safe deployment of VLA models.

AI.tldr: Proposes an inference-time, concept-based dictionary learning framework for safety in Vision-Language-Action models that detects harmful concept directions from hidden activations and applies threshold-based gating to suppress unsafe actions; achieves strong defense with minimal task impact across multiple benchmarks.
AI.motivation: VLA systems create a perception-action loop that can be exploited by jailbreak-like prompts, leading to unsafe physical actions. Existing defenses either intervene too late or on the wrong modality, leaving fused representations vulnerable. An interpretable, plug-in, inference-time solution is needed to improve safety without retraining.
AI.method: Construct sparse, interpretable dictionaries from hidden activations to identify harmful concept directions. Apply threshold-based interventions to suppress or block unsafe activations. The approach is model-agnostic and plug-in, requiring no retraining and integrating with diverse VLAs.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art defense performance, reducing attack success rates by over 70% while maintaining task success across Libero-Harm, BadRobot, RoboPair, and IS-Bench.
AI.conclusion: First inference-time concept-based safety method for embodied VLA systems, enhancing interpretability and safe deployment by enabling plug-in suppression of unsafe activations without retraining.

title: Small-Margin Preferences Still Matter-If You Train Them Right
summary: Preference optimization methods such as DPO align large language models (LLMs) using paired comparisons, but their effectiveness can be highly sensitive to the quality and difficulty of preference pairs. A common heuristic treats small-margin (ambiguous) pairs as noisy and filters them out. In this paper, we revisit this assumption and show that pair difficulty interacts strongly with the optimization objective: when trained with preference-based losses, difficult pairs can destabilize training and harm alignment, yet these same pairs still contain useful supervision signals when optimized with supervised fine-tuning (SFT). Motivated by this observation, we propose MixDPO, a simple yet effective difficulty-aware training strategy that (i) orders preference data from easy to hard (a curriculum over margin-defined difficulty), and (ii) routes difficult pairs to an SFT objective while applying a preference loss to easy pairs. This hybrid design provides a practical mechanism to leverage ambiguous pairs without incurring the optimization failures often associated with preference losses on low-margin data. Across three LLM-judge benchmarks, MixDPO consistently improves alignment over DPO and a range of widely-used variants, with particularly strong gains on AlpacaEval~2 length-controlled (LC) win rate.

AI.tldr: MixDPO introduces a curriculum-based, difficulty-aware training for preference-based LLM alignment by routing hard pairs to SFT and easy pairs to a preference loss, improving alignment over DPO on three benchmarks, especially LC win rate on AlpacaEval2.
AI.motivation: DPO performance is highly sensitive to preference pair quality and difficulty. Small-margin (ambiguous) pairs are often treated as noise, but they still carry useful supervision signals that can destabilize training if misused. A method that exploits difficult pairs without destabilizing optimization is needed.
AI.method: Construct a margin-defined easy-to-hard curriculum over preference data. Apply the preference-based loss to easy pairs and route difficult pairs to a supervised fine-tuning (SFT) objective; combine both losses in a hybrid training strategy (MixDPO). Evaluated on three LLM-judge benchmarks including AlpacaEval2 LC.
AI.result: MixDPO consistently outperforms DPO and common variants across the benchmarks, with particularly strong gains on AlpacaEval2 length-controlled win rate.
AI.conclusion: Difficulty-aware curriculum with a hybrid objective enables leveraging ambiguous pairs for signal while avoiding instability from hard pairs, providing a practical improvement for alignment via preference data.

title: Optimal Transport-Guided Adversarial Attacks on Graph Neural Network-Based Bot Detection
summary:
The rise of bot accounts on social media poses significant risks to public discourse. To address this threat, modern bot detectors increasingly rely on Graph Neural Networks (GNNs). However, the effectiveness of these GNN-based detectors in real-world settings remains poorly understood. In practice, attackers continuously adapt their strategies as well as must operate under domain-specific and temporal constraints, which can fundamentally limit the applicability of existing attack methods. As a result, there is a critical need for robust GNN-based bot detection methods under realistic, constraint-aware attack scenarios.
  To address this gap, we introduce BOCLOAK to systematically evaluate the robustness of GNN-based social bot detection via both edge editing and node injection adversarial attacks under realistic constraints. BOCLOAK constructs a probability measure over spatio-temporal neighbor features and learns an optimal transport geometry that separates human and bot behaviors. It then decodes transport plans into sparse, plausible edge edits that evade detection while obeying real-world constraints. We evaluate BOCLOAK across three social bot datasets, five state-of-the-art bot detectors, three adversarial defenses, and compare it against four leading graph adversarial attack baselines. BOCLOAK achieves up to 80.13% higher attack success rates while using 99.80% less GPU memory under realistic real-world constraints. Most importantly, BOCLOAK shows that optimal transport provides a lightweight, principled framework for bridging the gap between adversarial attacks and real-world bot detection.

AI.tldr: Presents BOCLOAK, a constraint-aware adversarial framework using optimal transport to evaluate and attack GNN-based social bot detectors via edge edits, achieving high attack success with low resource use.
AI.motivation: GNN-based bot detectors can be brittle under realistic constraints; attackers operate under domain-specific and temporal limits, necessitating robust, constraint-aware evaluation and attack methods.
AI.method: BOCLOAK constructs a probability measure over spatio-temporal neighbor features and learns an optimal transport geometry that separates human and bot behaviors; it decodes transport plans into sparse, plausible edge edits that evade detection while complying with real-world constraints; evaluated across multiple datasets, detectors, defenses, and baselines.
AI.result: BOCLOAK achieves up to 80.13% higher attack success rates and uses 99.80% less GPU memory compared to baselines; demonstrates strong effectiveness across diverse settings and positions optimal transport as a lightweight bridge between adversarial attacks and real-world bot detection.
