AI.conclusion: Optimal transport provides a principled, lightweight framework for constraint-aware adversarial evaluation of GNN-based bot detectors; BOCLOAK offers a scalable attack and evaluation pipeline to study robustness under realistic conditions.

title: Vision-only UAV State Estimation for Fast Flights Without External Localization Systems: A2RL Drone Racing Finalist Approach
comment: Visit our webpage for more details: https://mrs.fel.cvut.cz/papers/vision-only-uav-state-estimation
summary: Fast flights with aggressive maneuvers in cluttered GNSS-denied environments require fast, reliable, and accurate UAV state estimation. In this paper, we present an approach for onboard state estimation of a high-speed UAV using a monocular RGB camera and an IMU. Our approach fuses data from Visual-Inertial Odometry (VIO), an onboard landmark-based camera measurement system, and an IMU to produce an accurate state estimate. Using onboard measurement data, we estimate and compensate for VIO drift through a novel mathematical drift model. State-of-the-art approaches often rely on more complex hardware (e.g., stereo cameras or rangefinders) and use uncorrected drifting VIO velocities, orientation, and angular rates, leading to errors during fast maneuvers. In contrast, our method corrects all VIO states (position, orientation, linear and angular velocity), resulting in accurate state estimation even during rapid and dynamic motion. Our approach was thoroughly validated through 1600 simulations and numerous real-world experiments. Furthermore, we applied the proposed method in the A2RL Drone Racing Challenge 2025, where our team advanced to the final four out of 210 teams and earned a medal.

AI.tldr: Monocular UAV state estimation in GNSS-denied clutter using fused VIO, onboard landmark-based camera measurements and IMU with a novel drift model to correct VIO drift; achieves accurate state estimates during fast maneuvers, validated via extensive simulations and real flights, and performed well in Drone Racing Challenge 2025.
AI.motivation: Enable fast, reliable UAV navigation under GNSS-denied clutter where VIO drift and limited sensing degrade performance, especially during aggressive maneuvers, while avoiding heavier sensors like stereo cameras or rangefinders.
AI.method: Fuse Visual-Inertial Odometry (VIO), onboard landmark-based camera measurements, and IMU data using a novel mathematical drift model to estimate and compensate VIO drift, correcting all VIO states (position, orientation, linear and angular velocity) onboard.
AI.result: Extensive validation including 1600 simulations and numerous real-world experiments; achieved strong competition performance by reaching the final four of 210 teams and earning a medal in the A2RL Drone Racing Challenge 2025.
AI.conclusion: The proposed onboard drift-corrected fusion of VIO, landmarks, and IMU provides accurate, robust state estimation for high-speed UAV flight in GNSS-denied environments, outperforming approaches that leave VIO drift uncorrected and often rely on heavier hardware.

title: Reasoning and Tool-use Compete in Agentic RL:From Quantifying Interference to Disentangled Tuning
summary: Agentic Reinforcement Learning (ARL) focuses on training large language models (LLMs) to interleave reasoning with external tool execution to solve complex tasks. Most existing ARL methods train a single shared model parameters to support both reasoning and tool use behaviors, implicitly assuming that joint training leads to improved overall agent performance. Despite its widespread adoption, this assumption has rarely been examined empirically. In this paper, we systematically investigate this assumption by introducing a Linear Effect Attribution System(LEAS), which provides quantitative evidence of interference between reasoning and tool-use behaviors. Through an in-depth analysis, we show that these two capabilities often induce misaligned gradient directions, leading to training interference that undermines the effectiveness of joint optimization and challenges the prevailing ARL paradigm. To address this issue, we propose Disentangled Action Reasoning Tuning(DART), a simple and efficient framework that explicitly decouples parameter updates for reasoning and tool-use via separate low-rank adaptation modules. Experimental results show that DART consistently outperforms baseline methods with averaged 6.35 percent improvements and achieves performance comparable to multi-agent systems that explicitly separate tool-use and reasoning using a single model.

AI.tldr: Jointly training reasoning and tool-use in Agentic Reinforcement Learning often causes gradient interference, reducing performance. The authors quantify this interference with LEAS, and propose DART to disentangle updates via separate low-rank adapters, achieving ~6.35% gains and rivaling multi-agent setups.
AI.motivation: Question the default assumption that end-to-end joint training of reasoning and tool-use in ARL improves performance; investigate whether gradient interference undermines benefits of joint optimization; provide quantitative evidence and a practical solution.
AI.method: Introduce LEAS (Linear Effect Attribution System) to quantify interference between reasoning and tool-use; conduct in-depth gradient analysis showing misaligned directions; propose DART (Disentangled Action Reasoning Tuning) using separate low-rank adapters to decouple parameter updates for reasoning and tool-use; compare with baselines on standard tasks.
AI.result: LEAS reveals frequent misalignment of gradient directions between reasoning and tool-use; DART consistently outperforms baselines with an average of 6.35% improvement and achieves performance comparable to explicit multi-agent designs that separate components.
AI.conclusion: Interference between reasoning and tool-use challenges joint optimization in ARL; disentangled training via DART mitigates interference and can match multi-agent performance while using a single model.

title: Harvest: Opportunistic Peer-to-Peer GPU Caching for LLM Inference
summary: Large Language Model (LLM) inference is increasingly constrained by GPU memory capacity rather than compute throughput, driven by growing model sizes and the linear growth of the key-value (KV) cache during autoregressive decoding. Existing approaches mitigate memory pressure by offloading model state and KV tensors to host memory, but incur substantial latency due to limited PCIe bandwidth. We present Harvest, an opportunistic GPU cache management framework that exploits high-bandwidth peer-to-peer GPU interconnects to dynamically place model weights and KV cache in unused GPU memory. Harvest treats peer GPU memory as a transient cache tier, preserving correctness while reducing data movement overhead under dynamic memory availability. We demonstrate significant throughput speedup of more than 2 times by using Harvest to accelerate the retrieval of two widely-used inference components: expert layer weights and KV cache entries.

AI.tldr: Harvest uses peer GPU memory as a dynamic, transient cache to store model weights and KV caches during LLM inference, reducing data movement and memory pressure, achieving >2× throughput improvements.
AI.motivation: LLM inference is increasingly memory-bound due to growing model sizes and the linearly growing KV cache during autoregressive decoding. Offloading to host memory via PCIe adds high latency, becoming a bottleneck.
AI.method: Treat peer GPU memory as a transient cache tier and opportunistically place weights and KV cache in unused GPU memory using high-bandwidth GPU interconnects. The system dynamically adapts to memory availability while preserving correctness.
AI.result: Significant throughput improvement, achieving more than 2× speedup by accelerating retrieval of expert layer weights and KV cache entries.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates that exploiting high-bandwidth peer-to-peer GPU interconnects for an opportunistic cache can substantially mitigate memory bottlenecks in LLM inference without sacrificing correctness.

title: Error Taxonomy-Guided Prompt Optimization
summary: Automatic Prompt Optimization (APO) is a powerful approach for extracting performance from large language models without modifying their weights. Many existing methods rely on trial-and-error, testing different prompts or in-context examples until a good configuration emerges, often consuming substantial compute. Recently, natural language feedback derived from execution logs has shown promise as a way to identify how prompts can be improved. However, most prior approaches operate in a bottom-up manner, iteratively adjusting the prompt based on feedback from individual problems, which can cause them to lose the global perspective. In this work, we propose Error Taxonomy-Guided Prompt Optimization (ETGPO), a prompt optimization algorithm that adopts a top-down approach. ETGPO focuses on the global failure landscape by collecting model errors, categorizing them into a taxonomy, and augmenting the prompt with guidance targeting the most frequent failure modes. Across multiple benchmarks spanning mathematics, question answering, and logical reasoning, ETGPO achieves accuracy that is comparable to or better than state-of-the-art methods, while requiring roughly one third of the optimization-phase token usage and evaluation budget.

AI.tldr: ETGPO proposes a top-down, error taxonomy-guided prompt optimization that aggregates global model failures to guide prompt improvements, achieving comparable/better accuracy with ~1/3 the optimization-token and evaluation budget compared to state-of-the-art methods.
AI.motivation: Current APO methods are often bottom-up and cost-intensive, focusing on per-instance feedback and losing a global view of failure modes. There is a need for a global, data-efficient approach that leverages common error patterns.
AI.method: Collects model errors across tasks, builds a taxonomy of failure modes, and augments prompts with guidance targeting the most frequent failures in a top-down manner, evaluating across mathematics, QA, and logical reasoning benchmarks.
AI.result: ETGPO achieves accuracy comparable to or better than SOTA methods while reducing optimization-phase token usage and evaluation budget by roughly one third.
AI.conclusion: A top-down error taxonomy-guided framework effectively concentrates optimization on prevalent failure modes, offering data- and compute-efficient improvements across diverse reasoning tasks; future work could refine the taxonomy and examine robustness to noisy or evolving failure patterns.

title: In-Run Data Shapley for Adam Optimizer
summary: Reliable data attribution is essential for mitigating bias and reducing computational waste in modern machine learning, with the Shapley value serving as the theoretical gold standard. While recent "In-Run" methods bypass the prohibitive cost of retraining by estimating contributions dynamically, they heavily rely on the linear structure of Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD) and fail to capture the complex dynamics of adaptive optimizers like Adam. In this work, we demonstrate that data attribution is inherently optimizer-dependent: we show that SGD-based proxies diverge significantly from true contributions under Adam (Pearson $R \approx 0.11$), rendering them ineffective for modern training pipelines. To bridge this gap, we propose Adam-Aware In-Run Data Shapley. We derive a closed-form approximation that restores additivity by redefining utility under a fixed-state assumption and enable scalable computation via a novel Linearized Ghost Approximation. This technique linearizes the variance-dependent scaling term, allowing us to compute pairwise gradient dot-products without materializing per-sample gradients. Extensive experiments show that our method achieves near-perfect fidelity to ground-truth marginal contributions ($R > 0.99$) while retaining $\sim$95\% of standard training throughput. Furthermore, our Adam-aware attribution significantly outperforms SGD-based baselines in data attribution downstream tasks.

AI.tldr: Adam-aware in-run data Shapley: accounts for optimizer dynamics in data attribution, achieving near-perfect fidelity to true contributions with strong throughput, outperforming SGD-based proxies.
AI.motivation: Data attribution via Shapley is theoretically optimal but highly sensitive to optimizer dynamics. SGD-based in-run proxies struggle under adaptive optimizers like Adam, risking biased or inaccurate attribution.
AI.method: Derive a closed-form approximation under a fixed-state assumption to restore additivity. Introduce Linearized Ghost Approximation to linearize the variance-dependent scaling term, enabling computation of pairwise gradient dot-products without materializing per-sample gradients.
AI.result: Experiments show near-perfect fidelity to ground-truth marginal contributions (Pearson R > 0.99) and about 95% training throughput relative to standard training. Adam-aware attribution also outperforms SGD-based baselines on downstream data-attribution tasks.
AI.conclusion: Optimizer-aware data attribution substantially improves reliability in modern training pipelines and provides a scalable, accurate method compatible with adaptive optimizers like Adam.

title: Multimodal Large Language Models for Real-Time Situated Reasoning
comment: Submitted to the interactivity track of the 21st ACM/IEEE International Conference on Human-Robot Interaction on December 2025, accepted January 2026
summary: In this work, we explore how multimodal large language models can support real-time context- and value-aware decision-making. To do so, we combine the GPT-4o language model with a TurtleBot 4 platform simulating a smart vacuum cleaning robot in a home. The model evaluates the environment through vision input and determines whether it is appropriate to initiate cleaning. The system highlights the ability of these models to reason about domestic activities, social norms, and user preferences and take nuanced decisions aligned with the values of the people involved, such as cleanliness, comfort, and safety. We demonstrate the system in a realistic home environment, showing its ability to infer context and values from limited visual input. Our results highlight the promise of multimodal large language models in enhancing robotic autonomy and situational awareness, while also underscoring challenges related to consistency, bias, and real-time performance.

AI.tldr: Multimodal LLMs (GPT-4o) guide a domestic robot (TurtleBot 4) in real-time, using vision to decide when to clean, factoring context and user values; demonstrates potential for enhanced autonomy and situational awareness with challenges in consistency, bias, and latency.
AI.motivation: To advance robotic autonomy in household settings by enabling value-aware, context-sensitive decision-making through multimodal LLMs that can reason about domestic activities, social norms, and user preferences.
AI.method: Integrate GPT-4o with a TurtleBot 4 vacuum platform, use vision input to assess the environment, and have the model determine whether initiating cleaning is appropriate. Demonstrate in a realistic home environment and analyze the model's ability to infer context and values from limited visual input.
AI.result: The system showcases context- and value-aware decision-making in real time, reasoning about domestic activities, social norms, and user preferences, and inferring context from limited visual cues. Demonstrates enhanced robotic autonomy and situational awareness, while highlighting challenges in consistency, bias, and real-time performance.
AI.conclusion: Multimodal LLMs hold promise for improving autonomy and safety in service robotics by aligning robot actions with human values, but require advances to address reliability, bias, and latency to be robust in real-world deployments.

title: How RLHF Amplifies Sycophancy
summary: Large language models often exhibit increased sycophantic behavior after preference-based post-training, showing a stronger tendency to affirm a user's stated or implied belief even when this conflicts with factual accuracy or sound judgment. We present a formal analysis of how alignment from human feedback can increase this failure mode by identifying an explicit amplification mechanism that causally links optimization against a learned reward to bias in the human preference data used for alignment. We show that the direction of behavioral drift is determined by a covariance under the base policy between endorsing the belief signal in the prompt and the learned reward, and that the first-order effect reduces to a simple mean-gap condition. We then analyze reward learning from pairwise comparisons under random utility models like Bradley-Terry and characterize when bias in human annotators' preferences induces this reward gap. Next, we propose a training-time intervention designed to neutralize the amplification mechanism itself. Among all post-trained policies that prevent sycophantic behavior from increasing, we characterize the unique policy closest in KL divergence to the unconstrained post-trained policy, and derive the corresponding minimal reward correction as a closed-form agreement penalty. Computational experiments find that reward gaps are common and cause behavioral drift in all the configurations considered.

AI.tldr: Identifies an amplification mechanism: alignment via human feedback can increase sycophantic bias in LLMs; formalizes via covariance between endorsing beliefs in prompts and learned rewards, and a mean-gap condition; analyzes reward-learning under pairwise comparisons (Bradley–Terry); proposes a training-time penalty to neutralize the mechanism and derives the minimal KL-constrained corrective policy; experiments show reward gaps and drift are widespread.
AI.motivation: Understand why alignment through human feedback may inadvertently amplify sycophantic behavior and provide a concrete mitigation strategy.
AI.method: Theoretical analysis of optimization against a learned reward; derivation of a covariance-based amplification mechanism and a first-order mean-gap condition; modeling reward learning from pairwise comparisons with random utility models (e.g., Bradley–Terry); formulation of a training-time intervention to neutralize the amplification, and identification of the unique KL-minimal policy under a constraint with a closed-form penalty; empirical computation to illustrate reward gaps and drift.
AI.result: Reveals that a reward-gap bias in human preferences can amplify sycophantic behavior during post-training alignment; presents a KL-divergence-minimizing penalty approach and a closed-form reward correction; shows through experiments that reward gaps are common and cause behavioral drift across configurations.
AI.conclusion: To prevent amplified sycophancy, apply a minimal, KL-constrained reward correction (agreement penalty) during training; the proposed policy is the closest in KL to the unconstrained post-trained policy while neutralizing the amplification mechanism; human-bias in preferences is a critical source of drift, warranting careful design of reward models and data collection.

title: Prototype-based Explainable Neural Networks with Channel-specific Reasoning for Geospatial Learning Tasks
summary: Explainable AI (XAI) is essential for understanding machine learning (ML) decision-making and ensuring model trustworthiness in scientific applications. Prototype-based XAI methods offer an intrinsically interpretable alternative to post-hoc approaches which often yield inconsistent explanations. Prototype-based XAI methods make predictions based on the similarity between inputs and learned prototypes that represent typical characteristics of target classes. However, existing prototype-based models are primarily designed for standard RGB image data and are not optimized for the distinct, variable-specific channels commonly found in geoscientific image and raster datasets. In this study, we develop a prototype-based XAI approach tailored for multi-channel geospatial data, where each channel represents a distinct physical environmental variable or spectral channel. Our approach enables the model to identify separate, channel-specific prototypical characteristics sourced from multiple distinct training examples that inform how these features individually and in combination influence model prediction while achieving comparable performance to standard neural networks. We demonstrate this method through two geoscientific case studies: (1) classification of Madden Julian Oscillation phases using multi-variable climate data and (2) land-use classification from multispectral satellite imagery. This approach produces both local (instance-level) and global (model-level) explanations for providing insights into feature-relevance across channels. By explicitly incorporating channel-prototypes into the prediction process, we discuss how this approach enhances the transparency and trustworthiness of ML models for geoscientific learning tasks.

AI.tldr: A prototype-based XAI tailored for multi-channel geospatial data using channel-specific prototypes to provide local and global explanations while maintaining competitive accuracy; demonstrated on Madden-Julian Oscillation phase classification and multispectral land-use classification.
AI.motivation: To address the gap where existing prototype-based XAI methods are designed for RGB data and neglect channel-specific interpretability in geospatial raster datasets, enabling more transparent and trustworthy ML for multi-channel Earth science tasks.
AI.method: Introduce channel-aware prototypes that capture channel-specific and cross-channel patterns; integrate into prediction via similarity to prototypes across channels; train on multi-variable climate data and multispectral imagery; derive instance-level and model-level explanations highlighting channel contributions.
AI.result: Achieves comparable performance to standard neural networks; provides channel-specific explanations and combined feature influence; yields local and global explanations; demonstrated through two geoscience case studies.
AI.conclusion: Channel-prototype XAI enhances transparency and trust in geoscientific ML, enabling interpretable insights across channels and improving usefulness for decision-making in Earth system science.

title: Path Tracking with Dynamic Control Point Blending for Autonomous Vehicles: An Experimental Study
summary: This paper presents an experimental study of a path-tracking framework for autonomous vehicles in which the lateral control command is applied to a dynamic control point along the wheelbase. Instead of enforcing a fixed reference at either the front or rear axle, the proposed method continuously interpolates between both, enabling smooth adaptation across driving contexts, including low-speed maneuvers and reverse motion. The lateral steering command is obtained by barycentric blending of two complementary controllers: a front-axle Stanley formulation and a rear-axle curvature-based geometric controller, yielding continuous transitions in steering behavior and improved tracking stability. In addition, we introduce a curvature-aware longitudinal control strategy based on virtual track borders and ray-tracing, which converts upcoming geometric constraints into a virtual obstacle distance and regulates speed accordingly. The complete approach is implemented in a unified control stack and validated in simulation and on a real autonomous vehicle equipped with GPS-RTK, radar, odometry, and IMU. The results in closed-loop tracking and backward maneuvers show improved trajectory accuracy, smoother steering profiles, and increased adaptability compared to fixed control-point baselines.

AI.tldr: A unified path-tracking framework for autonomous vehicles that uses a dynamic control point along the wheelbase to blend front-axle Stanley control with a rear-axle curvature-based geometric controller, enabling smooth transitions across driving contexts (including low-speed and reverse) via barycentric interpolation, complemented by a curvature-aware longitudinal strategy based on virtual track borders and ray-tracing. Validated in simulation and on a real GPS/IMU/radar/odometry-equipped vehicle, showing improved trajectory accuracy, smoother steering, and greater adaptability versus fixed-control-point baselines.
AI.motivation: To overcome limitations of fixed front- or rear-axle references in vehicle path tracking, enabling smooth adaptation across diverse driving conditions, including reverse and low speed, and to unify lateral and longitudinal control within a single framework for improved stability and feasibility.
AI.method: Introduce a dynamic control point along the wheelbase and perform barycentric (linear) blending of two complementary lateral controllers: a front-axle Stanley controller and a rear-axle curvature-based geometric controller. The longitudinal controller incorporates curvature awareness via virtual track borders and ray-tracing to convert upcoming geometric constraints into a virtual obstacle distance, guiding speed. All components are integrated into a unified control stack and validated in both simulation and a real vehicle equipped with GPS-RTK, radar, odometry, and IMU.
AI.result: The approach yields improved closed-loop trajectory accuracy, smoother steering profiles, and enhanced adaptability, particularly during backward maneuvers, relative to fixed-control-point baselines.
AI.conclusion: Dynamic control-point blending between front and rear axle controllers, together with curvature-aware longitudinal control, provides a robust, adaptable path-tracking framework for autonomous vehicles across a range of driving contexts, validated through simulation and real-vehicle experiments.

title: HalluHard: A Hard Multi-Turn Hallucination Benchmark
summary: Large language models (LLMs) still produce plausible-sounding but ungrounded factual claims, a problem that worsens in multi-turn dialogue as context grows and early errors cascade. We introduce $\textbf{HalluHard}$, a challenging multi-turn hallucination benchmark with 950 seed questions spanning four high-stakes domains: legal cases, research questions, medical guidelines, and coding. We operationalize groundedness by requiring inline citations for factual assertions. To support reliable evaluation in open-ended settings, we propose a judging pipeline that iteratively retrieves evidence via web search. It can fetch, filter, and parse full-text sources (including PDFs) to assess whether cited material actually supports the generated content. Across a diverse set of frontier proprietary and open-weight models, hallucinations remain substantial even with web search ($\approx 30\%$ for the strongest configuration, Opus-4.5 with web search), with content-grounding errors persisting at high rates. Finally, we show that hallucination behavior is shaped by model capacity, turn position, effective reasoning, and the type of knowledge required.

AI.tldr: Hallucinations persist in large language models even when web-grounded; a new HalluHard benchmark (950 seed questions) across four high-stakes domains enforces inline citations; a web-search judging pipeline checks evidence to verify support; strongest model with web search still shows ~30% hallucinations; factors shaping hallucination include model capacity, turn position, reasoning, and knowledge type.
AI.motivation: Address the pervasive problem of groundedness in multi-turn LLMs, especially in high-stakes domains, by creating a rigorous benchmark and an evidence-based evaluation pipeline that requires explicit citations and verifiable sources.
AI.method: Introduce HalluHard: a challenging multi-turn hallucination benchmark with 950 seed questions across legal, scientific, medical, and coding domains, requiring inline citations for factual claims. Develop a judging pipeline that iteratively retrieves evidence via web search, capable of fetching, filtering, and parsing full-text sources (including PDFs) to determine whether cited material supports the generated content.
AI.result: Hallucinations remain substantial across frontier models even with web search; approximately 30% of cases still hallucinate in the strongest configuration (Opus-4.5 with web search); content-grounding errors persist at high rates despite evidence retrieval.
AI.conclusion: Web-based grounding reduces but does not eliminate hallucinations. Model capacity, turn position, reasoning effectiveness, and the type of knowledge required shape hallucination behavior, highlighting the need for improved grounding strategies and evaluation frameworks.

title: Efficient and accurate steering of Large Language Models through attention-guided feature learning
summary: Steering, or direct manipulation of internal activations to guide LLM responses toward specific semantic concepts, is emerging as a promising avenue for both understanding how semantic concepts are stored within LLMs and advancing LLM capabilities. Yet, existing steering methods are remarkably brittle, with seemingly non-steerable concepts becoming completely steerable based on subtle algorithmic choices in how concept-related features are extracted. In this work, we introduce an attention-guided steering framework that overcomes three core challenges associated with steering: (1) automatic selection of relevant token embeddings for extracting concept-related features; (2) accounting for heterogeneity of concept-related features across LLM activations; and (3) identification of layers most relevant for steering. Across a steering benchmark of 512 semantic concepts, our framework substantially improved steering over previous state-of-the-art (nearly doubling the number of successfully steered concepts) across model architectures and sizes (up to 70 billion parameter models). Furthermore, we use our framework to shed light on the distribution of concept-specific features across LLM layers. Overall, our framework opens further avenues for developing efficient, highly-scalable fine-tuning algorithms for industry-scale LLMs.

AI.tldr: An attention-guided steering framework markedly improves steerability of semantic concepts in LLMs, addressing key extraction and architecture questions; it nearly doubles the number of steerable concepts on a 512-concept benchmark and reveals layer-wise distribution of concept features, enabling scalable fine-tuning for large models.
AI.motivation: To understand how semantic concepts are stored in LLMs and to overcome brittleness in existing steering techniques that make some concepts steerable only under specific extraction choices.
AI.method: Introduce an attention-guided steering framework that (i) automatically selects relevant token embeddings for concept-related features, (ii) accounts for heterogeneity of concept-related features across activations, and (iii) identifies the layers most relevant for steering.
AI.result: On a steering benchmark of 512 semantic concepts, the approach substantially outperforms prior state-of-the-art, nearly doubling the number of successfully steered concepts across model architectures and sizes up to 70B parameters; it also elucidates how concept-specific features distribute across LLM layers.
AI.conclusion: The framework enables more efficient and scalable fine-tuning for industry-scale LLMs and opens avenues for deeper understanding of concept feature distribution across layers.

title: Multi-Task Learning for Robot Perception with Imbalanced Data
summary: Multi-task problem solving has been shown to improve the accuracy of the individual tasks, which is an important feature for robots, as they have a limited resource. However, when the number of labels for each task is not equal, namely imbalanced data exist, a problem may arise due to insufficient number of samples, and labeling is not very easy for mobile robots in every environment. We propose a method that can learn tasks even in the absence of the ground truth labels for some of the tasks. We also provide a detailed analysis of the proposed method. An interesting finding is related to the interaction of the tasks. We show a methodology to find out which tasks can improve the performance of other tasks. We investigate this by training the teacher network with the task outputs such as depth as inputs. We further provide empirical evidence when trained with a small amount of data. We use semantic segmentation and depth estimation tasks on different datasets, NYUDv2 and Cityscapes.

AI.tldr: Semi-supervised multi-task learning that handles missing labels by exploiting inter-task interactions, using a teacher network that incorporates outputs (e.g., depth) to guide segmentation and depth estimation on NYUDv2 and Cityscapes, effective with limited data.
AI.motivation: Address imbalanced and missing task labels in multi-task learning for robots with limited data and labeling difficulty; uncover how tasks influence each other to improve overall performance.
AI.method: Proposes a teacher-student framework where the teacher is fed task outputs (such as depth) as inputs to learn tasks even when ground-truth labels are unavailable for some tasks. Includes analysis of task interactions to identify beneficial task relationships. Evaluates on semantic segmentation and depth estimation across NYUDv2 and Cityscapes, with experiments using small data.
AI.result: Demonstrates that inter-task interactions can be harnessed to improve performance, even with scarce labeled data. Provides a methodology to identify which tasks enhance others and shows empirical evidence of gains when data is limited across standard datasets.
AI.conclusion: The approach enables learning under incomplete supervision, reveals meaningful task interactions, and is effective with limited data on established benchmarks (NYUDv2, Cityscapes), highlighting the utility of using task outputs to guide learning in a multi-task setting.

title: Discovering Process-Outcome Credit in Multi-Step LLM Reasoning
summary: Reinforcement Learning (RL) serves as a potent paradigm for enhancing reasoning capabilities in Large Language Models (LLMs), yet standard outcome-based approaches often suffer from reward sparsity and inefficient credit assignment. In this paper, we propose a novel framework designed to provide continuous reward signals, which introduces a Step-wise Marginal Information Gain (MIG) mechanism that quantifies the intrinsic value of reasoning steps against a Monotonic Historical Watermark, effectively filtering out training noise. To ensure disentangled credit distribution, we implement a Decoupled Masking Strategy, applying process-oriented rewards specifically to the chain-of-thought (CoT) and outcome-oriented rewards to the full completion. Additionally, we incorporate a Dual-Gated SFT objective to stabilize training with high-quality structural and factual signals. Extensive experiments across textual and multi-modal benchmarks (e.g., MATH, Super-CLEVR) demonstrate that our approach consistently outperforms baselines such as GRPO in both sample efficiency and final accuracy. Furthermore, our model exhibits superior out-of-distribution robustness, demonstrating promising zero-shot transfer capabilities to unseen and challenging reasoning tasks.

AI.tldr: Introduces Step-wise Marginal Information Gain with a Monotonic Historical Watermark and a Decoupled Masking Strategy to provide continuous RL rewards for LLM reasoning, achieving better sample efficiency, accuracy, and OOD transfer; outperforms baselines like GRPO on MATH and Super-CLEVR.
AI.motivation: Tackle reward sparsity and credit assignment in RL for LLM-based reasoning; provide continuous, disentangled rewards and noise-robust signals.
AI.method: Define Step-wise Marginal Information Gain (MIG) evaluated against a Monotonic Historical Watermark; apply a Decoupled Masking Strategy that assigns CoT process rewards separately from final outcome rewards; use a Dual-Gated SFT objective for stability.
AI.result: Empirical results on textual and multi-modal benchmarks (MATH, Super-CLEVR) show improved sample efficiency and final accuracy over GRPO; improved out-of-distribution robustness and zero-shot transfer.
AI.conclusion: Continuous reward signals via MIG with watermarking and decoupled credit improves RL for reasoning in LLMs and offers robust, transferable performance.

title: Adaptive Momentum and Nonlinear Damping for Neural Network Training
summary: We propose a continuous-time scheme for large-scale optimization that introduces individual, adaptive momentum coefficients regulated by the kinetic energy of each model parameter. This approach automatically adjusts to local landscape curvature to maintain stability without sacrificing convergence speed. We demonstrate that our adaptive friction can be related to cubic damping, a suppression mechanism from structural dynamics. Furthermore, we introduce two specific optimization schemes by augmenting the continuous dynamics of mSGD and Adam with a cubic damping term. Empirically, our methods demonstrate robustness and match or outperform Adam on training ViT, BERT, and GPT2 tasks where mSGD typically struggles. We further provide theoretical results establishing the exponential convergence of the proposed schemes.

AI.tldr: Adaptive kinetic-energy–driven momentum with cubic damping for large-scale optimization; improves stability and convergence in nonconvex training, demonstrated on transformers with exponential convergence guarantees.
AI.motivation: To address instability and sensitivity of momentum-based optimizers in deep learning, particularly under varying curvature and large-scale models; provide a principled continuous-time formulation with local damping.
AI.method: Define continuous-time dynamics where each parameter has an individual, adaptively scaled momentum coefficient regulated by parameter kinetic energy; introduce a cubic damping term analogous to structural dynamic damping; derive two schemes by augmenting mSGD and Adam; provide discretized implementations; analyze stability.
AI.result: Empirical experiments on ViT, BERT, GPT2 show robustness and performance comparable or superior to Adam, where mSGD struggles; the methods match or outperform Adam in training large models; theoretical results prove exponential convergence under certain assumptions.
AI.conclusion: Adaptive cubic damping in continuous-time optimization yields stable, fast convergence and broad applicability to SGD-like methods; provides theoretical guarantees and practical schemes for large-scale DL training.

title: ForSim: Stepwise Forward Simulation for Traffic Policy Fine-Tuning
summary: As the foundation of closed-loop training and evaluation in autonomous driving, traffic simulation still faces two fundamental challenges: covariate shift introduced by open-loop imitation learning and limited capacity to reflect the multimodal behaviors observed in real-world traffic. Although recent frameworks such as RIFT have partially addressed these issues through group-relative optimization, their forward simulation procedures remain largely non-reactive, leading to unrealistic agent interactions within the virtual domain and ultimately limiting simulation fidelity. To address these issues, we propose ForSim, a stepwise closed-loop forward simulation paradigm. At each virtual timestep, the traffic agent propagates the virtual candidate trajectory that best spatiotemporally matches the reference trajectory through physically grounded motion dynamics, thereby preserving multimodal behavioral diversity while ensuring intra-modality consistency. Other agents are updated with stepwise predictions, yielding coherent and interaction-aware evolution. When incorporated into the RIFT traffic simulation framework, ForSim operates in conjunction with group-relative optimization to fine-tune traffic policy. Extensive experiments confirm that this integration consistently improves safety while maintaining efficiency, realism, and comfort. These results underscore the importance of modeling closed-loop multimodal interactions within forward simulation and enhance the fidelity and reliability of traffic simulation for autonomous driving. Project Page: https://currychen77.github.io/ForSim/

AI.tldr: ForSim introduces a stepwise closed-loop forward simulation to improve traffic simulation fidelity for autonomous driving. It selects stepwise agent trajectories that best match reference paths through physics-based dynamics, preserving multimodal diversity while maintaining intra-modality consistency, and updates all agents stepwise for coherent interactions. When coupled with RIFT's group-relative optimization, it enhances safety without sacrificing efficiency, realism, or comfort.
AI.motivation: Current traffic simulation faces covariate shift from open-loop imitation learning and limited capacity to reflect real-world multimodal traffic. Existing forward simulation frameworks like RIFT are largely non-reactive, yielding unrealistic agent interactions and lower fidelity.
AI.method: At each virtual timestep, each traffic agent proposes a candidate trajectory that best spatiotemporally matches the reference trajectory under physically grounded motion dynamics. This stepwise propagation preserves multimodal behavioral diversity and intra-modality consistency. Other agents are updated with stepwise predictions to ensure coherent, interaction-aware evolution. The approach is integrated with RIFT’s group-relative optimization to fine-tune traffic policies.
AI.result: Extensive experiments demonstrate that combining ForSim with RIFT improves safety while maintaining efficiency, realism, and comfort, enhancing the fidelity and reliability of traffic simulation for autonomous driving.
AI.conclusion: Modeling closed-loop, multimodal interactions in forward simulation is crucial for high-fidelity traffic simulation. ForSim advances the realism and reliability of autonomous-driving traffic models and complements group-relative policy optimization in RIFT.

title: SetPO: Set-Level Policy Optimization for Diversity-Preserving LLM Reasoning
summary: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards has shown notable effectiveness in enhancing large language models (LLMs) reasoning performance, especially in mathematics tasks. However, such improvements often come with reduced outcome diversity, where the model concentrates probability mass on a narrow set of solutions. Motivated by diminishing-returns principles, we introduce a set level diversity objective defined over sampled trajectories using kernelized similarity. Our approach derives a leave-one-out marginal contribution for each sampled trajectory and integrates this objective as a plug-in advantage shaping term for policy optimization. We further investigate the contribution of a single trajectory to language model diversity within a distribution perturbation framework. This analysis theoretically confirms a monotonicity property, proving that rarer trajectories yield consistently higher marginal contributions to the global diversity. Extensive experiments across a range of model scales demonstrate the effectiveness of our proposed algorithm, consistently outperforming strong baselines in both Pass@1 and Pass@K across various benchmarks.

AI.tldr: Proposes a kernelized, set-level diversity objective for RL with verifiable rewards in LLMs, using leave-one-out contributions as a plug-in advantage shaping term; proves rarer trajectories contribute more to global diversity; shows empirical gains (Pass@1/Pass@K) across model scales.
AI.motivation: Mitigate reduced outcome diversity that accompanies RL-based reasoning improvements for LLMs, by explicitly optimizing diversity at the set/trajectory level while maintaining performance.
AI.method: Introduce a kernelized diversity objective evaluated over sampled trajectories; derive a leave-one-out marginal contribution for each trajectory to the diversity measure; integrate this as a plug-in advantage shaping term in policy optimization; analyze diversity contribution under distribution perturbations to establish monotonicity with rarer trajectories.
AI.result: Empirically, the method improves diversity and outperforms strong baselines in Pass@1 and Pass@K across multiple benchmarks and model scales.
AI.conclusion: A principled framework that balances reasoning performance and response diversity via a diversity-aware advantage term; theoretical monotonicity shows rarer trajectories enhance global diversity; demonstrated gains suggest broad applicability to LLM RLHF settings.

title: Planning with Language and Generative Models: Toward General Reward-Guided Wireless Network Design
summary: Intelligent access point (AP) deployment remains challenging in next-generation wireless networks due to complex indoor geometries and signal propagation. We firstly benchmark general-purpose large language models (LLMs) as agentic optimizers for AP planning and find that, despite strong wireless domain knowledge, their dependence on external verifiers results in high computational costs and limited scalability. Motivated by these limitations, we study generative inference models guided by a unified reward function capturing core AP deployment objectives across diverse floorplans. We show that diffusion samplers consistently outperform alternative generative approaches. The diffusion process progressively improves sampling by smoothing and sharpening the reward landscape, rather than relying on iterative refinement, which is effective for non-convex and fragmented objectives. Finally, we introduce a large-scale real-world dataset for indoor AP deployment, requiring over $50k$ CPU hours to train general reward functions, and evaluate in- and out-of-distribution generalization and robustness. Our results suggest that diffusion-based generative inference with a unified reward function provides a scalable and domain-agnostic foundation for indoor AP deployment planning.

AI.tldr: Diffusion-based generative inference with a unified reward function enables scalable indoor AP deployment planning, outperforming LLM-based optimizers and other generative methods; introduces a large real-world dataset with extensive training costs.
AI.motivation: Address the challenge of intelligent AP deployment in complex indoor geometries and the limitations of general-purpose LLMs as optimizers, aiming for scalable, domain-agnostic planning.
AI.method: Benchmark general-purpose LLMs as agents for AP planning; develop diffusion-sampler-based generative inference guided by a unified reward function for AP deployment objectives across diverse floorplans; construct a large-scale real-world indoor AP deployment dataset and train general reward functions; evaluate in- and out-of-distribution generalization and robustness.
AI.result: Diffusion samplers consistently outperform alternative generative approaches by smoothing/sharpening the reward landscape rather than iterative refinement; large-scale dataset reveals substantial training cost (~50k CPU hours) and demonstrates scalable, domain-agnostic planning with diffusion-based inference.
AI.conclusion: Diffusion-based generative inference with a unified reward function provides a scalable, domain-agnostic foundation for indoor AP deployment planning, capable of handling non-convex and fragmented objectives across diverse floorplans.

title: LIEREx: Language-Image Embeddings for Robotic Exploration
comment: This preprint has not undergone peer review or any post-submission improvements or corrections. The Version of Record of this article is published in KI - Künstliche Intelligenz, and is available online at https://doi.org/10.1007/s13218-026-00902-6
summary: Semantic maps allow a robot to reason about its surroundings to fulfill tasks such as navigating known environments, finding specific objects, and exploring unmapped areas. Traditional mapping approaches provide accurate geometric representations but are often constrained by pre-designed symbolic vocabularies. The reliance on fixed object classes makes it impractical to handle out-of-distribution knowledge not defined at design time. Recent advances in Vision-Language Foundation Models, such as CLIP, enable open-set mapping, where objects are encoded as high-dimensional embeddings rather than fixed labels. In LIEREx, we integrate these VLFMs with established 3D Semantic Scene Graphs to enable target-directed exploration by an autonomous agent in partially unknown environments.

AI.tldr: Open-set semantic mapping by integrating Vision-Language Foundation Models with 3D Semantic Scene Graphs to support target-directed exploration in partially unknown environments (LIEREx).
AI.motivation: Traditional mapping relies on fixed object vocabularies, limiting handling of out-of-distribution knowledge; open-set, flexible representations are needed for robust navigation and exploration.
AI.method: Integrate Vision-Language Foundation Models (e.g., CLIP) with established 3D Semantic Scene Graphs to encode objects as high-dimensional embeddings rather than fixed labels, enabling target-directed exploration by an autonomous agent in partially unknown environments.
AI.result: The abstract proposes the approach and its integration; no empirical results are reported in the abstract.
AI.conclusion: Open-set semantic mapping via VLFMs and 3D Semantic Scene Graphs enables flexible, target-driven navigation in partially unknown environments.

title: ConvexBench: Can LLMs Recognize Convex Functions?
summary: Convex analysis is a modern branch of mathematics with many applications. As Large Language Models (LLMs) start to automate research-level math and sciences, it is important for LLMs to demonstrate the ability to understand and reason with convexity. We introduce \cb, a scalable and mechanically verifiable benchmark for testing \textit{whether LLMs can identify the convexity of a symbolic objective under deep functional composition.} Experiments on frontier LLMs reveal a sharp compositional reasoning gap: performance degrades rapidly with increasing depth, dropping from an F1-score of $1.0$ at depth $2$ to approximately $0.2$ at depth $100$. Inspection of models' reasoning traces indicates two failure modes: \textit{parsing failure} and \textit{lazy reasoning}. To address these limitations, we propose an agentic divide-and-conquer framework that (i) offloads parsing to an external tool to construct an abstract syntax tree (AST) and (ii) enforces recursive reasoning over each intermediate sub-expression with focused context. This framework reliably mitigates deep-composition failures, achieving substantial performance improvement at large depths (e.g., F1-Score $= 1.0$ at depth $100$).

AI.tldr: A scalable convexity benchmark for LLMs (cb) reveals a depth-dependent reasoning gap; an agentic divide-and-conquer approach with external parsing and recursive sub-expression reasoning closes the gap, achieving perfect F1 at depth 100.
AI.motivation: To evaluate LLMs' ability to reason about convexity under deep functional composition and to push toward reliable math reasoning in AI systems.
AI.method: Introduce cb benchmark; test frontier LLMs; identify failure modes (parsing failure, lazy reasoning); propose agentic divide-and-conquer: external parser to build AST; recursive reasoning over sub-expressions with focused context.
AI.result: Baseline accuracy degrades with depth (F1 from 1.0 at depth 2 to ~0.2 at depth 100); the proposed framework yields high performance, achieving F1=1.0 at depth 100.
AI.conclusion: A tool-assisted, divide-and-conquer reasoning framework robustly mitigates deep-composition failures in convexity reasoning tasks; supports scalable, verifiable evaluation of LLMs on mathematical properties.

title: Leveraging Textual-Cues for Enhancing Multimodal Sentiment Analysis by Object Recognition
summary: Multimodal sentiment analysis, which includes both image and text data, presents several challenges due to the dissimilarities in the modalities of text and image, the ambiguity of sentiment, and the complexities of contextual meaning. In this work, we experiment with finding the sentiments of image and text data, individually and in combination, on two datasets. Part of the approach introduces the novel `Textual-Cues for Enhancing Multimodal Sentiment Analysis' (TEMSA) based on object recognition methods to address the difficulties in multimodal sentiment analysis. Specifically, we extract the names of all objects detected in an image and combine them with associated text; we call this combination of text and image data TEMS. Our results demonstrate that only TEMS improves the results when considering all the object names for the overall sentiment of multimodal data compared to individual analysis. This research contributes to advancing multimodal sentiment analysis and offers insights into the efficacy of TEMSA in combining image and text data for multimodal sentiment analysis.

AI.tldr: TEMSA proposes TEMS by fusing image object-name cues with text for multimodal sentiment analysis; evaluated on two datasets, TEMS outperforms single-modality baselines.
AI.motivation: Address challenges in multimodal sentiment analysis arising from modality dissimilarities, sentiment ambiguity, and contextual meaning by leveraging object-level visual cues linked to textual data.
AI.method: Two-dataset evaluation; introduce Textual-Cues for Enhancing Multimodal Sentiment Analysis (TEMSA); extract object names detected in images and combine with corresponding text to form TEMS; compare against individual modality analyses.
AI.result: Using all object-name cues, TEMS improves overall multimodal sentiment performance relative to single-modality analyses; TEMSA demonstrates efficacy of integrating image-derived object cues with text for sentiment analysis.
AI.conclusion: The work advances multimodal sentiment analysis by validating TEMSA and highlighting the value of object-name cues in aligning image and text for better sentiment inference.

title: Towards Exploratory and Focused Manipulation with Bimanual Active Perception: A New Problem, Benchmark and Strategy
summary: Recently, active vision has reemerged as an important concept for manipulation, since visual occlusion occurs more frequently when main cameras are mounted on the robot heads. We reflect on the visual occlusion issue and identify its essence as the absence of information useful for task completion. Inspired by this, we come up with the more fundamental problem of Exploratory and Focused Manipulation (EFM). The proposed problem is about actively collecting information to complete challenging manipulation tasks that require exploration or focus. As an initial attempt to address this problem, we establish the EFM-10 benchmark that consists of 4 categories of tasks that align with our definition (10 tasks in total). We further come up with a Bimanual Active Perception (BAP) strategy, which leverages one arm to provide active vision and another arm to provide force sensing while manipulating. Based on this idea, we collect a dataset named BAPData for the tasks in EFM-10. With the dataset, we successfully verify the effectiveness of the BAP strategy in an imitation learning manner. We hope that the EFM-10 benchmark along with the BAP strategy can become a cornerstone that facilitates future research towards this direction. Project website: EFManipulation.github.io.

AI.tldr: Proposes Exploratory and Focused Manipulation (EFM) to address visual occlusion in manipulation. Introduces the EFM-10 benchmark (4 task categories, 10 tasks), the Bimanual Active Perception (BAP) strategy using one arm for active vision and the other for force sensing, and the BAPData dataset. Demonstrates imitation-learning effectiveness and positions EFM-10/BAP as a foundation for future research.
AI.motivation: Visual occlusion from head-mounted cameras results in missing task-relevant information. The work reframes this as a fundamental information-collection problem (EFM) requiring exploration or focused sensing during manipulation.
AI.method: Define the EFM problem, construct the EFM-10 benchmark with four task categories (10 tasks total), propose the BAP strategy that partitions sensing and manipulation across two arms, collect the BAPData dataset, and validate the approach via imitation learning.
AI.result: Shows feasibility of EFM-10 and effectiveness of the BAP strategy in imitation learning; provides a dataset and benchmark to support future research.
AI.conclusion: EFM-10 and BAP offer a potential cornerstone for exploratory/focused manipulation research; includes a project website for access.

title: AutoHealth: An Uncertainty-Aware Multi-Agent System for Autonomous Health Data Modeling
summary: LLM-based agents have demonstrated strong potential for autonomous machine learning, yet their applicability to health data remains limited. Existing systems often struggle to generalize across heterogeneous health data modalities, rely heavily on predefined solution templates with insufficient adaptation to task-specific objectives, and largely overlook uncertainty estimation, which is essential for reliable decision-making in healthcare. To address these challenges, we propose \textit{AutoHealth}, a novel uncertainty-aware multi-agent system that autonomously models health data and assesses model reliability. \textit{AutoHealth} employs closed-loop coordination among five specialized agents to perform data exploration, task-conditioned model construction, training, and optimization, while jointly prioritizing predictive performance and uncertainty quantification. Beyond producing ready-to-use models, the system generates comprehensive reports to support trustworthy interpretation and risk-aware decision-making. To rigorously evaluate its effectiveness, we curate a challenging real-world benchmark comprising 17 tasks across diverse data modalities and learning settings. \textit{AutoHealth} completes all tasks and outperforms state-of-the-art baselines by 29.2\% in prediction performance and 50.2\% in uncertainty estimation.

AI.tldr: AutoHealth introduces an uncertainty-aware multi-agent system that autonomously models health data and assesses model reliability, showing strong cross-modal generalization and improved uncertainty quantification.
AI.motivation: Targets core challenges in health-ML: generalization across heterogeneous data modalities, limited adaptability beyond predefined templates, and insufficient uncertainty estimation for safe clinical decision-making.
AI.method: Five specialized agents engage in data exploration, task-conditioned model construction, training, and optimization within a closed-loop coordination framework; jointly optimize predictive performance and uncertainty quantification and generate interpretable reports.
AI.result: On a real-world benchmark of 17 tasks across diverse data modalities, AutoHealth completes all tasks and outperforms state-of-the-art baselines by 29.2% in prediction performance and 50.2% in uncertainty estimation.
AI.conclusion: AutoHealth demonstrates strong potential for autonomous health ML with uncertainty-aware modeling and interpretable reporting, advancing reliable decision-making and providing a challenging benchmark.

title: Quantum Generator Kernels
summary: Quantum kernel methods offer significant theoretical benefits by rendering classically inseparable features separable in quantum space. Yet, the practical application of Quantum Machine Learning (QML), currently constrained by the limitations of Noisy Intermediate-Scale Quantum (NISQ) hardware, necessitates effective strategies to compress and embed large-scale real-world data like images into the constrained capacities of existing quantum devices or simulators. To this end, we propose Quantum Generator Kernels (QGKs), a generator-based approach to quantum kernels, comprising a set of Variational Generator Groups (VGGs) that merge universal generators into a parameterizable operator, ensuring scalable coverage of the available quantum space. Thereby, we address shortcomings of current leading strategies employing hybrid architectures, which might prevent exploiting quantum computing's full potential due to fixed intermediate embedding processes. To optimize the kernel alignment to the target domain, we train a weight vector to parameterize the projection of the VGGs in the current data context. Our empirical results demonstrate superior projection and classification capabilities of the QGK compared to state-of-the-art quantum and classical kernel approaches and show its potential to serve as a versatile framework for various QML applications.

AI.tldr: Introduces Quantum Generator Kernels (QGKs), a generator-based quantum kernel framework using Variational Generator Groups (VGGs) to create scalable, learnable projections that better embed large real-world data into quantum spaces, improving classification performance over existing quantum and classical kernels.
AI.motivation: Overcome NISQ data embedding constraints and fixed intermediate embeddings in hybrid quantum-classical kernels by learning a flexible projection into quantum feature space aligned with the target domain.
AI.method: Design VGGs as universal generators combined into parameterizable operators. Learn a weight vector to parametrically project data via VGGs, optimizing kernel alignment to the task. Demonstrate via experiments that QGK yields superior projection and classification compared to benchmarks.
AI.result: Empirical results show improved projection and classification performance against state-of-the-art quantum and classical kernel baselines, indicating QGKs' effectiveness and potential as a general QML framework.
AI.conclusion: QGKs provide a scalable, adaptable kernel framework for QML on NISQ devices, enabling better data embedding and broader applicability across tasks; future work may explore broader datasets, hardware realizations, and theoretical guarantees.

title: A Unified Control Architecture for Macro-Micro Manipulation using a Active Remote Center of Compliance for Manufacturing Applications
comment: 17 pages, 14 figures, submitted to Robotics and Computer-Integrated Manufacturing (RCIM)
summary: Macro-micro manipulators combine a macro manipulator with a large workspace, such as an industrial robot, with a lightweight, high-bandwidth micro manipulator. This enables highly dynamic interaction control while preserving the wide workspace of the robot. Traditionally, position control is assigned to the macro manipulator, while the micro manipulator handles the interaction with the environment, limiting the achievable interaction control bandwidth. To solve this, we propose a novel control architecture that incorporates the macro manipulator into the active interaction control. This leads to a increase in control bandwidth by a factor of 2.1 compared to the state of the art architecture, based on the leader-follower approach and factor 12.5 compared to traditional robot-based force control. Further we propose surrogate models for a more efficient controller design and easy adaptation to hardware changes. We validate our approach by comparing it against the other control schemes in different experiments, like collision with an object, following a force trajectory and industrial assembly tasks.

AI.tldr: A macro-micro manipulator framework integrates the macro robot into active interaction control, doubling and increasing bandwidth; uses surrogate models; validated against collision, force tracking, and assembly tasks.
AI.motivation: To overcome the bandwidth limitation of traditional separation where the macro handles position and micro handles interaction, constraining interaction control bandwidth; aim to boost bandwidth and simplify design.
AI.method: Introduce a control architecture that embeds the macro manipulator into active interaction control; develop surrogate models for efficient controller design and easy hardware adaptation; compare against leader-follower and traditional force control baselines in experiments.
AI.result: Bandwidth increases: 2.1x over state-of-the-art architecture (leader-follower); 12.5x over traditional robot-based force control; surrogate models enable efficient controller design and easy adaptation; experiments validate performance across collision, force tracking, and industrial assembly tasks.
AI.conclusion: Integrating macro manipulators into active interaction control substantially improves interaction bandwidth and performance; surrogate models aid design and adaptation; demonstrated gains across representative tasks.

title: EvoOpt-LLM: Evolving industrial optimization models with large language models
summary: Optimization modeling via mixed-integer linear programming (MILP) is fundamental to industrial planning and scheduling, yet translating natural-language requirements into solver-executable models and maintaining them under evolving business rules remains highly expertise-intensive. While large language models (LLMs) offer promising avenues for automation, existing methods often suffer from low data efficiency, limited solver-level validity, and poor scalability to industrial-scale problems. To address these challenges, we present EvoOpt-LLM, a unified LLM-based framework supporting the full lifecycle of industrial optimization modeling, including automated model construction, dynamic business-constraint injection, and end-to-end variable pruning. Built on a 7B-parameter LLM and adapted via parameter-efficient LoRA fine-tuning, EvoOpt-LLM achieves a generation rate of 91% and an executability rate of 65.9% with only 3,000 training samples, with critical performance gains emerging under 1,500 samples. The constraint injection module reliably augments existing MILP models while preserving original objectives, and the variable pruning module enhances computational efficiency, achieving an F1 score of ~0.56 on medium-sized LP models with only 400 samples. EvoOpt-LLM demonstrates a practical, data-efficient approach to industrial optimization modeling, reducing reliance on expert intervention while improving adaptability and solver efficiency.

AI.tldr: EvoOpt-LLM is a data-efficient LLM-based framework for end-to-end industrial MILP modeling, including automated model construction, constraint injection, and variable pruning, achieving high generation and executability rates with modest training data.
AI.motivation: Translate natural-language requirements into solver-executable MILP models and maintain them under evolving business rules, addressing data efficiency, solver validity, and scalability challenges in industrial optimization.
AI.method: A unified framework built on a 7B-parameter LLM with LoRA fine-tuning, comprising automated model construction, dynamic constraint injection that preserves objectives, and end-to-end variable pruning, forming a complete MILP modeling lifecycle.
AI.result: Generation rate 91% and MILP executability rate 65.9% with ~3,000 training samples; notable gains with fewer than 1,500 samples. Constraint injection reliably augments MILP models while preserving objectives. Variable pruning yields an F1 score of ~0.56 on medium-sized LPs with only 400 samples.
AI.conclusion: EvoOpt-LLM offers a practical, data-efficient path for industrial optimization modeling, reducing expert dependence and enhancing adaptability and solver efficiency.

title: Post-Training Probability Manifold Correction via Structured SVD Pruning and Self-Referential Distillation
summary:
Large language models are expensive to deploy. We introduce Sparse Knowledge Distillation (SparseKD), a post-training method that compresses transformer models by combining structured SVD pruning with self-referential knowledge distillation. The key insight is simple: instead of using an external teacher, the model teaches itself by matching its own probability distribution from before compression. This self-referential setup enables surprisingly strong quality recovery after aggressive pruning.
  Our experiments reveal an unexpected finding: self-referential distillation alone, applied post-training under an identical objective and fixed calibration dataset, improves model quality by 39% relative to the original converged checkpoint. When combined with structured pruning, SparseKD achieves 15-65% parameter reduction with acceptable quality trade-offs. Kernel profiling shows that speedups arise entirely from reduced dense matrix multiplication in feed-forward layers while attention remains unchanged, making this approach complementary to attention optimizations.
  We validate across two model families (0.6B and 3.8B parameters) with multi-seed experiments confirming high reproducibility. SparseKD requires no external super-teacher, no architectural changes, and no custom inference kernels, making it immediately deployable with existing infrastructure.

AI.tldr: SparseKD compresses transformers with post-training structured SVD pruning plus self-referential distillation, achieving 15-65% parameter reduction with acceptable quality loss, and unexpectedly 39% relative quality gain from self-distillation alone. Validated on 0.6B and 3.8B models with reproducibility across seeds. No external teacher or architectural changes; speedups stem from FFN dense matmul reductions; complementary to attention optimizations; readily deployable on existing infra.
AI.motivation: Reduce deployment cost of large language models by enabling post-training compression without external teachers or architectural changes, while maintaining or improving quality and preserving compatibility with existing inference infrastructure.
AI.method: Apply structured singular value decomposition (SVD) pruning to transformer layers after training, combined with self-referential knowledge distillation where the model learns from its own pre-compression probability distribution under an identical objective and fixed calibration dataset.
AI.result: Self-distillation alone yields a 39% relative improvement over the original converged checkpoint. When paired with structured pruning, the method achieves 15-65% parameter reduction with acceptable quality trade-offs. Speedups originate from reduced dense matrix multiplications in feed-forward layers; attention computations remain unchanged. Demonstrated across two model families (0.6B and 3.8B) with multi-seed reproducibility.
AI.conclusion: SparseKD offers a practical, immediately deployable post-training compression method that is free from external teachers and architectural changes, complementary to attention optimizations, and reproducible across seeds and model scales.

title: Reformulating AI-based Multi-Object Relative State Estimation for Aleatoric Uncertainty-based Outlier Rejection of Partial Measurements
comment: Accepted for publication at ICRA 2026, Vienna, Austria
summary:
Precise localization with respect to a set of objects of interest enables mobile robots to perform various tasks. With the rise of edge devices capable of deploying deep neural networks (DNNs) for real-time inference, it stands to reason to use artificial intelligence (AI) for the extraction of object-specific, semantic information from raw image data, such as the object class and the relative six degrees of freedom (6-DoF) pose. However, fusing such AI-based measurements in an Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) requires quantifying the DNNs' uncertainty and outlier rejection capabilities.
  This paper presents the benefits of reformulating the measurement equation in AI-based, object-relative state estimation. By deriving an EKF using the direct object-relative pose measurement, we can decouple the position and rotation measurements, thus limiting the influence of erroneous rotation measurements and allowing partial measurement rejection. Furthermore, we investigate the performance and consistency improvements for state estimators provided by replacing the fixed measurement covariance matrix of the 6-DoF object-relative pose measurements with the predicted aleatoric uncertainty of the DNN.

AI.tldr: EKF-based object-relative 6-DoF pose estimation with decoupled translation/rotation and uncertainty-aware measurements improves robustness and consistency in AI-assisted localization.
AI.motivation: Edge DNNs enable semantic 6-DoF pose estimation from images, but fusing these measurements in an EKF requires reliable uncertainty quantification and outlier rejection to ensure robust localization.
AI.method: Derive an EKF using direct object-relative pose measurements, decoupling position and rotation to reduce impact of rotation errors and allow partial measurement rejection; replace fixed 6-DoF covariance with the DNN's predicted aleatoric uncertainty.
AI.result: Demonstrates improved consistency and robustness of state estimation by the proposed measurement reformulation and uncertainty-aware covariance, enabling better handling of erroneous measurements and outliers.
AI.conclusion: Object-relative EKF with aleatoric-uncertainty-based measurements provides more robust, consistent localization for AI-assisted perception in robotics, through decoupled measurements and adaptive uncertainty handling.

title: MedBeads: An Agent-Native, Immutable Data Substrate for Trustworthy Medical AI
comment: 19 pages, 5 figures. Code available at https://github.com/medbeads/medbeads
summary: Background: As of 2026, Large Language Models (LLMs) demonstrate expert-level medical knowledge. However, deploying them as autonomous "Clinical Agents" remains limited. Current Electronic Medical Records (EMRs) and standards like FHIR are designed for human review, creating a "Context Mismatch": AI agents receive fragmented data and must rely on probabilistic inference (e.g., RAG) to reconstruct patient history. This approach causes hallucinations and hinders auditability. Methods: We propose MedBeads, an agent-native data infrastructure where clinical events are immutable "Beads"--nodes in a Merkle Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG)--cryptographically referencing causal predecessors. This "write-once, read-many" architecture makes tampering mathematically detectable. We implemented a prototype with a Go Core Engine, Python middleware for LLM integration, and a React-based visualization interface. Results: We successfully implemented the workflow using synthetic data. The FHIR-to-DAG conversion transformed flat resources into a causally-linked graph. Our Breadth-First Search (BFS) Context Retrieval algorithm traverses relevant subgraphs with O(V+E) complexity, enabling real-time decision support. Tamper-evidence is guaranteed by design: any modification breaks the cryptographic chain. The visualization aids clinician understanding through explicit causal links. Conclusion: MedBeads addresses the "Context Mismatch" by shifting from probabilistic search to deterministic graph traversal, and from mutable records to immutable chains, providing the substrate for "Trustworthy Medical AI." It guarantees the context the AI receives is deterministic and tamper-evident, while the LLM determines interpretation. The structured Bead format serves as a token-efficient "AI-native language." We release MedBeads as open-source software to accelerate agent-native data standards.

AI.tldr: MedBeads proposes an immutable, Merkle DAG-based representation of clinical events (Beads) to provide deterministic, tamper-evident context for AI-driven clinical agents, replacing mutable EMR-centric workflows with a write-once, read-many substrate and BFS-based subgraph retrieval; prototype implemented and open-sourced.
AI.motivation: Solve the context mismatch between LLM-based clinical agents and fragmented EMR/FHIR data, reduce hallucinations, and provide auditable, provenance-rich data for trustworthy medical AI.
AI.method: Introduce Beads as nodes in a Merkle DAG referencing causal predecessors; convert FHIR resources to a causally linked graph; implement a BFS context retrieval with O(V+E) complexity; build a Go core engine, Python middleware, and a React visualization; assess using synthetic data; ensure tamper-evidence via cryptographic chaining.
AI.result: Prototype implemented with synthetic data; FHIR-to-DAG conversion achieved; BFS retrieves relevant subgraphs efficiently; tamper-evidence guaranteed by design; visualization clarifies explicit causal links; architecture exhibits linearithmic feasibility for real-time decision support.
AI.conclusion: MedBeads reframes context provisioning from probabilistic retrieval to deterministic graph traversal, and from mutable records to immutable chains, enabling trustworthy AI. The Bead format offers an AI-native, token-efficient substrate. Open-sourcing aims to accelerate adoption of agent-native data standards.

title: MATRIX: A Multimodal Benchmark and Post-Training Framework for Materials Science
summary: Scientific reasoning in materials science requires integrating multimodal experimental evidence with underlying physical theory. Existing benchmarks make it difficult to assess whether incorporating visual experimental data during post-training improves mechanism-grounded explanation reasoning beyond text-only supervision. We introduce MATRIX, a multimodal benchmark for materials science reasoning that evaluates foundational theory, research-level reasoning, and the interpretation of real experimental artifacts across multiple characterization modalities. Using MATRIX as a controlled diagnostic, we isolate the effect of visual grounding by comparing post-training on structured materials science text alone with post-training that incorporates paired experimental images. Despite using relatively small amounts of multimodal data, visual supervision improves experimental interpretation by 10-25% and yields 5-16% gains on text-only scientific reasoning tasks. Our results demonstrate that these improvements rely on correct image-text alignment during post-training, highlighting cross-modal representational transfer. We also observe consistent improvements on ScienceQA and PubMedQA, demonstrating that the benefits of structured multimodal post-training extend beyond materials science. The MATRIX dataset is available at https://huggingface.co/datasets/radical-ai/MATRIX and the model at https://huggingface.co/radical-ai/MATRIX-PT.

AI.tldr: MATRIX introduces a multimodal benchmark to assess whether visual data during post-training improves materials science reasoning, showing gains in both multimodal interpretation and text-only reasoning, contingent on proper image-text alignment, with benefits extending to other scientific tasks.
AI.motivation: Address the gap in assessing how visual experimental data supports mechanism-grounded reasoning in materials science, enabling controlled evaluation of cross-modal transfer and generalization beyond text-only supervision.
AI.method: Construct MATRIX with multimodal materials science data across multiple characterization modalities; perform controlled post-training comparisons between text-only and text+paired images; evaluate on foundational theory, research-level reasoning, and experimental artifact interpretation; test cross-domain transfer on ScienceQA and PubMedQA; analyze the role of image-text alignment.
AI.result: Visual supervision yields 10–25% improvements in experimental interpretation and 5–16% gains on text-only scientific reasoning; benefits rely on correct image-text alignment and show cross-modal transfer; improvements generalize to ScienceQA and PubMedQA beyond materials science.
