AI.conclusion: Structured multimodal post-training can meaningfully enhance scientific reasoning and interpretability; MATRIX provides a controlled diagnostic and dataset/model resources for broad evaluation of multimodal learning in science.

title: Synchronized Online Friction Estimation and Adaptive Grasp Control for Robust Gentle Grasp
summary: We introduce a unified framework for gentle robotic grasping that synergistically couples real-time friction estimation with adaptive grasp control. We propose a new particle filter-based method for real-time estimation of the friction coefficient using vision-based tactile sensors. This estimate is seamlessly integrated into a reactive controller that dynamically modulates grasp force to maintain a stable grip. The two processes operate synchronously in a closed-loop: the controller uses the current best estimate to adjust the force, while new tactile feedback from this action continuously refines the estimation. This creates a highly responsive and robust sensorimotor cycle. The reliability and efficiency of the complete framework are validated through extensive robotic experiments.

AI.tldr: A unified, closed-loop framework for gentle robotic grasping that combines real-time friction estimation via a particle filter with vision-based tactile sensing and adaptive grasp control, validated through extensive experiments.
AI.motivation: To enable stable and delicate grasping under uncertain friction by tightly coupling perception and control in real time.
AI.method: Particle filter-based real-time estimation of the friction coefficient using vision-based tactile sensors, integrated with a reactive controller that adjusts grip force. The estimation and control run synchronously in a closed loop, with tactile feedback continuously refining the estimate.
AI.result: Demonstrated reliability and efficiency of the framework through extensive robotic experiments, indicating a highly responsive and robust sensorimotor cycle.
AI.conclusion: The framework enables resilient, real-time adaptation to varying friction in gentle grasping and illustrates the effectiveness of tightly coupled perception–control loops for robotic manipulation.

title: Hard Constraints Meet Soft Generation: Guaranteed Feasibility for LLM-based Combinatorial Optimization
summary: Large language models (LLMs) have emerged as promising general-purpose solvers for combinatorial optimization (CO), yet they fundamentally lack mechanisms to guarantee solution feasibility which is critical for real-world deployment. In this work, we introduce FALCON, a framework that ensures 100\% feasibility through three key innovations: (i) \emph{grammar-constrained decoding} enforces syntactic validity, (ii) a \emph{feasibility repair layer} corrects semantic constraint violations, and (iii) \emph{adaptive Best-of-$N$ sampling} allocates inference compute efficiently. To train the underlying LLM, we introduce the Best-anchored Objective-guided Preference Optimization (BOPO) in LLM training, which weights preference pairs by their objective gap, providing dense supervision without human labels. Theoretically, we prove convergence for BOPO and provide bounds on repair-induced quality loss. Empirically, across seven NP-hard CO problems, FALCON achieves perfect feasibility while matching or exceeding the solution quality of state-of-the-art neural and LLM-based solvers.

AI.tldr: FALCON guarantees 100% feasibility for combinatorial optimization using grammar-constrained decoding, a feasibility repair layer, and adaptive Best-of-N sampling, with BOPO training that converges. It achieves perfect feasibility across seven NP-hard CO problems while matching or exceeding state-of-the-art solvers.
AI.motivation: LLMs solving CO often produce infeasible solutions, limiting real-world deployment. A unified framework that enforces feasibility with theoretical guarantees while preserving solution quality is needed.
AI.method: Three innovations: (i) grammar-constrained decoding to enforce syntactic validity; (ii) a feasibility repair layer to fix semantic constraint violations; (iii) adaptive Best-of-N sampling to allocate inference compute efficiently. Training uses Best-anchored Objective-guided Preference Optimization (BOPO) that weights preference pairs by their objective gap for dense supervision without human labels, with theoretical convergence guarantees and bounds on repair-induced quality loss.
AI.result: Empirically validated across seven NP-hard CO problems, achieving perfect feasibility and solution quality that matches or surpasses state-of-the-art neural and LLM-based solvers.
AI.conclusion: FALCON offers a theoretically grounded and practically effective framework for guaranteeing feasibility in LLM-based CO solvers, combining grammatical, repair, and sampling strategies with a convergence-guaranteed training objective.

title: RePaint-Enhanced Conditional Diffusion Model for Parametric Engineering Designs under Performance and Parameter Constraints
summary: This paper presents a RePaint-enhanced framework that integrates a pre-trained performance-guided denoising diffusion probabilistic model (DDPM) for performance- and parameter-constraint engineering design generation. The proposed method enables the generation of missing design components based on a partial reference design while satisfying performance constraints, without retraining the underlying model. By applying mask-based resampling during inference process, RePaint allows efficient and controllable repainting of partial designs under both performance and parameter constraints, which is not supported by conventional DDPM-base methods. The framework is evaluated on two representative design problems, parametric ship hull design and airfoil design, demonstrating its ability to generate novel designs with expected performance based on a partial reference design. Results show that the method achieves accuracy comparable to or better than pre-trained models while enabling controlled novelty through fixing partial designs. Overall, the proposed approach provides an efficient, training-free solution for parameter-constraint-aware generative design in engineering applications.

AI.tldr: A training-free RePaint-based framework repaints partial designs using a pre-trained performance-guided DDPM to satisfy performance and parameter constraints, demonstrated on hull and airfoil design.
AI.motivation: Enable generation of constraint-aware designs without retraining large diffusion models.
AI.method: Mask-based inference-time repainting (RePaint) applied to a pre-trained performance-guided DDPM; use of a partial reference design to generate missing components while enforcing performance and parameter constraints; no retraining.
AI.result: Achieves accuracy comparable to or better than pre-trained models; enables controlled novelty by fixing partial designs; validated on parametric ship hull and airfoil design.
AI.conclusion: Offers an efficient, training-free solution for parameter-constraint-aware generative design in engineering.

title: Bandwidth-Efficient Multi-Agent Communication through Information Bottleneck and Vector Quantization
comment: Accepted at the 2026 IEEE International Conference on Robotics and Automation (ICRA 2026), Vienna, Austria. 9 pages, 4 figures, 6 tables
summary: Multi-agent reinforcement learning systems deployed in real-world robotics applications face severe communication constraints that significantly impact coordination effectiveness. We present a framework that combines information bottleneck theory with vector quantization to enable selective, bandwidth-efficient communication in multi-agent environments. Our approach learns to compress and discretize communication messages while preserving task-critical information through principled information-theoretic optimization. We introduce a gated communication mechanism that dynamically determines when communication is necessary based on environmental context and agent states. Experimental evaluation on challenging coordination tasks demonstrates that our method achieves 181.8% performance improvement over no-communication baselines while reducing bandwidth usage by 41.4%. Comprehensive Pareto frontier analysis shows dominance across the entire success-bandwidth spectrum with area-under-curve of 0.198 vs 0.142 for next-best methods. Our approach significantly outperforms existing communication strategies and establishes a theoretically grounded framework for deploying multi-agent systems in bandwidth-constrained environments such as robotic swarms, autonomous vehicle fleets, and distributed sensor networks.

AI.tldr: Proposes a principled framework that merges information bottleneck and vector quantization to enable bandwidth-efficient, selective inter-agent communication in multi-agent RL; includes a gated communication mechanism and achieves strong performance gains with reduced bandwidth, outperforming existing methods.
AI.motivation: Real-world multi-agent robotics face severe communication constraints that degrade coordination; there is a need for principled, bandwidth-aware communication that preserves task-critical information.
AI.method: Integrates information bottleneck theory with vector quantization to compress and discretize messages; optimizes for preserving task-relevant information; introduces a gating mechanism to decide when to communicate based on context and agent states.
AI.result: Shows 181.8% improvement over no-communication baselines and 41.4% bandwidth reduction; Pareto frontier analysis yields area-under-curve of 0.198 vs 0.142 for competitors, indicating broad dominance across success-bandwidth spectrum.
AI.conclusion: The approach provides a theoretically grounded framework for deploying multi-agent systems in bandwidth-constrained settings (robotic swarms, autonomous fleets, distributed sensors) and outperforms existing communication strategies.

title: Probing RLVR training instability through the lens of objective-level hacking
summary: Prolonged reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) has been shown to drive continuous improvements in the reasoning capabilities of large language models, but the training is often prone to instabilities, especially in Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architectures. Training instability severely undermines model capability improvement, yet its underlying causes and mechanisms remain poorly understood. In this work, we introduce a principled framework for understanding RLVR instability through the lens of objective-level hacking. Unlike reward hacking, which arises from exploitable verifiers, objective-level hacking emerges from token-level credit misalignment and is manifested as system-level spurious signals in the optimization objective. Grounded in our framework, together with extensive experiments on a 30B MoE model, we trace the origin and formalize the mechanism behind a key pathological training dynamic in MoE models: the abnormal growth of the training-inference discrepancy, a phenomenon widely associated with instability but previously lacking a mechanistic explanation. These findings provide a concrete and causal account of the training dynamics underlying instabilities in MoE models, offering guidance for the design of stable RLVR algorithms.

AI.tldr: A principled framework shows RLVR instability in MoE LLMs stems from objective-level hacking, not reward hacking, caused by token-level credit misalignment leading to system-level spurious optimization signals; experiments on a 30B MoE model reveal the mechanism behind training-inference discrepancy growth and provide guidance for stable RLVR design.
AI.motivation: Diagnose and mitigate instabilities that limit performance gains in prolonged RL with verifiable rewards (RLVR) for mixture-of-experts (MoE) language models; current explanations focus on reward hacking only, neglecting objective-level dynamics.
AI.method: Propose an objective-level hacking framework and conduct extensive experiments on a 30B MoE model to trace the origin of instabilities. Analyze the abnormal growth of the training–inference discrepancy and link token-level credit misalignment to system-level optimization signals.
AI.result: Establishes a causal account of MoE RLVR instability, formalizes the mechanism behind abnormal growth of the training–inference discrepancy, and demonstrates that objective-level misalignment drives instability. Provides empirical validation and a basis for designing stable RLVR algorithms.
AI.conclusion: Offers a concrete, causal explanation for RLVR instabilities in MoE models and actionable guidance for designing stable RLVR algorithms.

title: A Fragile Guardrail: Diffusion LLM's Safety Blessing and Its Failure Mode
summary: Diffusion large language models (D-LLMs) offer an alternative to autoregressive LLMs (AR-LLMs) and have demonstrated advantages in generation efficiency. Beyond the utility benefits, we argue that D-LLMs exhibit a previously underexplored safety blessing: their diffusion-style generation confers intrinsic robustness against jailbreak attacks originally designed for AR-LLMs. In this work, we provide an initial analysis of the underlying mechanism, showing that the diffusion trajectory induces a stepwise reduction effect that progressively suppresses unsafe generations. This robustness, however, is not absolute. We identify a simple yet effective failure mode, termed context nesting, where harmful requests are embedded within structured benign contexts, effectively bypassing the stepwise reduction mechanism. Empirically, we show that this simple strategy is sufficient to bypass D-LLMs' safety blessing, achieving state-of-the-art attack success rates across models and benchmarks. Most notably, it enables the first successful jailbreak of Gemini Diffusion, to our knowledge, exposing a critical vulnerability in commercial D-LLMs. Together, our results characterize both the origins and the limits of D-LLMs' safety blessing, constituting an early-stage red-teaming of D-LLMs.

AI.tldr: Diffusion LLMs show an intrinsic safety robustness against jailbreaks due to diffusion-driven stepwise reduction of unsafe content, but a simple failure mode—context nesting—can bypass the mechanism. The paper demonstrates a Gemini Diffusion jailbreak, indicating both origins and limits of the safety blessing and advocating early red-teaming of D-LLMs.
AI.motivation: Investigate whether diffusion-based generation confers safety advantages over autoregressive LLMs and identify potential vulnerabilities and failure modes that could enable jailbreaks.
AI.method: Theoretical analysis of diffusion trajectories; empirical evaluation across diffusion-based LLMs and standard benchmarks; introduction and testing of a context-nesting jailbreak strategy; case study on Gemini Diffusion.
AI.result: The diffusion process induces a stepwise reduction of unsafe content but is not foolproof. Context nesting can bypass the safety mechanism, achieving high attack success rates and enabling the first recorded jailbreak of Gemini Diffusion.
AI.conclusion: D-LLMs exhibit a safety blessing that is real but limited. Vulnerabilities exist (notably context nesting), necessitating red-teaming and more robust defense strategies for diffusion-based models.

title: Frictional Contact Solving for Material Point Method
summary: Accurately handling contact with friction remains a core bottleneck for Material Point Method (MPM), from reliable contact point detection to enforcing frictional contact laws (non-penetration, Coulomb friction, and maximum dissipation principle). In this paper, we introduce a frictional-contact pipeline for implicit MPM that is both precise and robust. During the collision detection phase, contact points are localized with particle-centric geometric primitives; during the contact resolution phase, we cast frictional contact as a Nonlinear Complementarity Problem (NCP) over contact impulses and solve it with an Alternating Direction Method of Multipliers (ADMM) scheme. Crucially, the formulation reuses the same implicit MPM linearization, yielding efficiency and numerical stability. The method integrates seamlessly into the implicit MPM loop and is agnostic to modeling choices, including material laws, interpolation functions, and transfer schemes. We evaluate it across seven representative scenes that span elastic and elasto-plastic responses, simple and complex deformable geometries, and a wide range of contact conditions. Overall, the proposed method enables accurate contact localization, reliable frictional handling, and broad generality, making it a practical solution for MPM-based simulations in robotics and related domains.

AI.tldr: Frictional-contact pipeline for implicit MPM using an NCP over contact impulses solved with ADMM; integrates with MPM linearization for efficiency; general across materials and transfer schemes.
AI.motivation: Frictional contact handling is a core bottleneck in MPM, requiring accurate detection and stable friction laws within an implicit solver.
AI.method: Collision detection uses particle-centric geometric primitives; frictional contact is formulated as a nonlinear complementarity problem over contact impulses and solved via ADMM; reuses the implicit MPM linearization; agnostic to material laws, interpolation functions, and transfer schemes.
AI.result: Evaluation across seven scenes showing accuracy in contact localization, robustness in frictional handling, and broad applicability to elastic and elasto-plastic responses with diverse geometries and contact conditions.
AI.conclusion: Presents a practical, general frictional-contact pipeline for implicit MPM suitable for robotics and related domains.

title: Transforming Vehicle Diagnostics: A Multimodal Approach to Error Patterns Prediction
summary: Accurately diagnosing and predicting vehicle malfunctions is crucial for maintenance and safety in the automotive industry. While modern diagnostic systems primarily rely on sequences of vehicular Diagnostic Trouble Codes (DTCs) registered in On-Board Diagnostic (OBD) systems, they often overlook valuable contextual information such as raw sensory data (e.g., temperature, humidity, and pressure). This contextual data, crucial for domain experts to classify vehicle failures, introduces unique challenges due to its complexity and the noisy nature of real-world data. This paper presents BiCarFormer: the first multimodal approach to multi-label sequence classification of error codes into error patterns that integrates DTC sequences and environmental conditions. BiCarFormer is a bidirectional Transformer model tailored for vehicle event sequences, employing embedding fusions and a co-attention mechanism to capture the relationships between diagnostic codes and environmental data. Experimental results on a challenging real-world automotive dataset with 22,137 error codes and 360 error patterns demonstrate that our approach significantly improves classification performance compared to models that rely solely on DTC sequences and traditional sequence models. This work highlights the importance of incorporating contextual environmental information for more accurate and robust vehicle diagnostics, hence reducing maintenance costs and enhancing automation processes in the automotive industry.

AI.tldr: BiCarFormer is the first multimodal bidirectional Transformer for multi-label sequence classification that fuses DTC sequences with environmental conditions to predict error patterns in vehicles; it outperforms DTC-only baselines on a real-world dataset.
AI.motivation: DTC sequences alone miss valuable contextual data (e.g., temperature, humidity, pressure) that domain experts use to classify failures; real-world data is noisy and multimodal, necessitating a model that can integrate heterogeneous signals.
AI.method: A bidirectional Transformer tailored for vehicle event sequences, using embedding fusion to combine DTC and environmental inputs, and a co-attention mechanism to capture cross-modal relationships; supports multi-label sequence classification of error patterns.
AI.result: On a real-world automotive dataset with 22,137 error codes and 360 error patterns, BiCarFormer significantly improves classification performance over models that rely solely on DTC sequences and traditional sequence models.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating contextual environmental information with DTC data yields more accurate and robust vehicle diagnostics, potentially reducing maintenance costs and improving automation in the automotive industry.

title: Localized, High-resolution Geographic Representations with Slepian Functions
summary: Geographic data is fundamentally local. Disease outbreaks cluster in population centers, ecological patterns emerge along coastlines, and economic activity concentrates within country borders. Machine learning models that encode geographic location, however, distribute representational capacity uniformly across the globe, struggling at the fine-grained resolutions that localized applications require. We propose a geographic location encoder built from spherical Slepian functions that concentrate representational capacity inside a region-of-interest and scale to high resolutions without extensive computational demands. For settings requiring global context, we present a hybrid Slepian-Spherical Harmonic encoder that efficiently bridges the tradeoff between local-global performance, while retaining desirable properties such as pole-safety and spherical-surface-distance preservation. Across five tasks spanning classification, regression, and image-augmented prediction, Slepian encodings outperform baselines and retain performance advantages across a wide range of neural network architectures.

AI.tldr: Introduces a region-focused geographic encoder using spherical Slepian functions, plus a hybrid Slepian-SH encoder for global context; demonstrates superior performance on five tasks across architectures.
AI.motivation: Geographic data is inherently local; standard global location encodings waste capacity and underperform at fine resolutions; need localized representations that can scale and preserve geometry.
AI.method: Develops a Slepian-based geographic location encoder concentrating capacity inside a region-of-interest; introduces a hybrid Slepian-Spherical Harmonic encoder to balance local-global performance; emphasizes pole-safety and preservation of spherical surface distance; tested across five tasks including classification, regression, and image-augmented prediction.
AI.result: Slepian encodings outperform baselines across tasks and architectures; performance gains persist across varying network types.
AI.conclusion: Slepian encodings are an effective approach for geographic data, enabling high-resolution regional representations while maintaining efficient global connectivity and desirable geometric properties.

title: FD-VLA: Force-Distilled Vision-Language-Action Model for Contact-Rich Manipulation
summary: Force sensing is a crucial modality for Vision-Language-Action (VLA) frameworks, as it enables fine-grained perception and dexterous manipulation in contact-rich tasks. We present Force-Distilled VLA (FD-VLA), a novel framework that integrates force awareness into contact-rich manipulation without relying on physical force sensors. The core of our approach is a Force Distillation Module (FDM), which distills force by mapping a learnable query token, conditioned on visual observations and robot states, into a predicted force token aligned with the latent representation of actual force signals. During inference, this distilled force token is injected into the pretrained VLM, enabling force-aware reasoning while preserving the integrity of its vision-language semantics. This design provides two key benefits: first, it allows practical deployment across a wide range of robots that lack expensive or fragile force-torque sensors, thereby reducing hardware cost and complexity; second, the FDM introduces an additional force-vision-state fusion prior to the VLM, which improves cross-modal alignment and enhances perception-action robustness in contact-rich scenarios. Surprisingly, our physical experiments show that the distilled force token outperforms direct sensor force measurements as well as other baselines, which highlights the effectiveness of this force-distilled VLA approach.

AI.tldr: FD-VLA distills a force signal into a token via a Force Distillation Module (FDM) conditioned on vision and state, injects it into a pretrained Vision-Language Model, enabling force-aware manipulation without physical force sensors; achieves superior robustness and lower hardware cost.
AI.motivation: Enable force-aware perception-action in contact-rich manipulation without expensive/fragile force sensors; leverage cross-modal alignment to improve robustness.
AI.method: Introduce FDM that maps a learnable query token, conditioned on visual observations and robot states, into a predicted force token aligned with latent force representations. During inference, inject the distilled force token into the pretrained VLM to enable force-aware reasoning while preserving vision-language semantics. The training distills force from sensor signals to align latent representations and facilitate hardware-agnostic deployment.
AI.result: Physical experiments show distilled force token outperforms direct sensor force measurements and baselines.
AI.conclusion: Force distillation into VLA frameworks reduces hardware requirements and enhances cross-modal alignment and robustness in contact-rich manipulation; the distilled force token can surpass direct sensor measurements in practice.

title: Lyapunov Stability-Aware Stackelberg Game for Low-Altitude Economy: A Control-Oriented Pruning-Based DRL Approach
summary: With the rapid expansion of the low-altitude economy, Unmanned Aerial Vehicles (UAVs) serve as pivotal aerial base stations supporting diverse services from users, ranging from latency-sensitive critical missions to bandwidth-intensive data streaming. However, the efficacy of such heterogeneous networks is often compromised by the conflict between limited onboard resources and stringent stability requirements. Moving beyond traditional throughput-centric designs, we propose a Sensing-Communication-Computing-Control closed-loop framework that explicitly models the impact of communication latency on physical control stability. To guarantee mission reliability, we leverage the Lyapunov stability theory to derive an intrinsic mapping between the state evolution of the control system and communication constraints, transforming abstract stability requirements into quantifiable resource boundaries. Then, we formulate the resource allocation problem as a Stackelberg game, where UAVs (as leaders) dynamically price resources to balance load and ensure stability, while users (as followers) optimize requests based on service urgency. Furthermore, addressing the prohibitive computational overhead of standard Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL) on energy-constrained edge platforms, we propose a novel and lightweight pruning-based Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) algorithm. By integrating a dynamic structured pruning mechanism, the proposed algorithm significantly compresses the neural network scale during training, enabling the UAV to rapidly approximate the game equilibrium with minimal inference latency. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed scheme effectively secures control loop stability while maximizing system utility in dynamic low-altitude environments.

AI.tldr: A Lyapunov-constrained, Stackelberg-driven resource allocation framework for UAV-based low-altitude networks, complemented by a lightweight, pruning-based PPO for edge efficiency, achieving control stability and high utility in dynamic environments.
AI.motivation: Traditional throughput-centric designs fail to ensure control stability under latency-constrained, resource-limited, heterogeneous UAV networks. There is a need for an integrated sensing-communication-computing-control loop that explicitly links communication latency to physical stability and supports reliable mission performance.
AI.method: 1) Use Lyapunov stability theory to map control state evolution into quantifiable communication/resource constraints and convert stability requirements into resource boundaries. 2) Formulate a Stackelberg game with UAVs as leaders setting prices to balance load and maintain stability, and users as followers optimizing requests based on urgency. 3) Propose a lightweight pruning-based PPO algorithm with dynamic structured pruning to compress the neural network during training, reducing inference latency on energy-constrained edge devices. 4) Validate via simulations in dynamic low-altitude environments to assess stability and system utility.
AI.result: The framework preserves control loop stability while maximizing system utility. The pruning-based PPO significantly reduces model size and inference latency, enabling rapid approximation of the game equilibrium under dynamic conditions; simulations show improved stability guarantees and resource efficiency compared with baseline approaches.
AI.conclusion: An integrated Sensing–Communication–Computing–Control framework with Lyapunov-based constraints and Stackelberg pricing, augmented by dynamic structured pruning for efficient learning, yields stable, high-utility UAV edge networks in dynamic low-altitude scenarios.

title: Fast Forward: Accelerating LLM Prefill with Predictive FFN Sparsity
summary: The prefill stage of large language model (LLM) inference is a key computational bottleneck for long-context workloads. At short-to-moderate context lengths (1K--16K tokens), Feed-Forward Networks (FFNs) dominate this cost, accounting for most of the total FLOPs. Existing FFN sparsification methods, designed for autoregressive decoding, fail to exploit the prefill stage's parallelism and often degrade accuracy. To address this, we introduce FastForward, a predictive sparsity framework that accelerates LLM prefill through block-wise, context-aware FFN sparsity. FastForward combines (1) a lightweight expert predictor to select high-importance neurons per block, (2) an error compensation network to correct sparsity-induced errors, and (3) a layer-wise sparsity scheduler to allocate compute based on token-mixing importance. Across LLaMA and Qwen models up to 8B parameters, FastForward delivers up to 1.45$\times$ compute-bound speedup at 50% FFN sparsity with $<$ 6% accuracy loss compared to the dense baseline on LongBench, substantially reducing Time-to-First-Token (TTFT) for efficient, long-context LLM inference on constrained hardware.

AI.tldr: FastForward introduces block-wise, context-aware FFN sparsity to accelerate LLM prefill, combining a lightweight expert predictor, an error compensation net, and a layer-wise sparsity scheduler. It achieves up to 1.45x compute-bound speedup at 50% FFN sparsity with under 6% accuracy loss on 8B-scale models, reducing Time-to-First-Token on long-context workloads.
AI.motivation: In long-context LLM inference, the prefill stage is a major bottleneck, with FFNs dominating FLOPs at 1K–16K context lengths. Existing FFN sparsification targets autoregressive decoding and fails to leverage prefill parallelism, often harming accuracy.
AI.method: Block-wise, context-aware FFN sparsity via three components: (1) a lightweight expert predictor to select high-importance neurons per block, (2) an error compensation network to correct sparsity-induced inaccuracies, (3) a layer-wise sparsity scheduler that allocates compute based on token-mixing importance. Applied across LLaMA/Qwen models up to 8B, designed for prefill rather than autoregressive decoding.
AI.result: Empirical proof on LongBench shows up to 1.45× compute-bound speedup at 50% FFN sparsity with <6% accuracy loss compared to dense baseline, reducing Time-to-First-Token for long-context inference on constrained hardware.
AI.conclusion: FastForward demonstrates that predictive, block-wise FFN sparsity tailored to the prefill phase can deliver meaningful speedups with controlled accuracy loss on mid-size LLMs, enabling more efficient long-context inference; future work may extend to larger models and hardware platforms.

title: PersistBench: When Should Long-Term Memories Be Forgotten by LLMs?
summary: Conversational assistants are increasingly integrating long-term memory with large language models (LLMs). This persistence of memories, e.g., the user is vegetarian, can enhance personalization in future conversations. However, the same persistence can also introduce safety risks that have been largely overlooked. Hence, we introduce PersistBench to measure the extent of these safety risks. We identify two long-term memory-specific risks: cross-domain leakage, where LLMs inappropriately inject context from the long-term memories; and memory-induced sycophancy, where stored long-term memories insidiously reinforce user biases. We evaluate 18 frontier and open-source LLMs on our benchmark. Our results reveal a surprisingly high failure rate across these LLMs - a median failure rate of 53% on cross-domain samples and 97% on sycophancy samples. To address this, our benchmark encourages the development of more robust and safer long-term memory usage in frontier conversational systems.

AI.tldr: Introduces PersistBench to evaluate safety risks of long-term memory in LLMs, focusing on cross-domain leakage and memory-induced sycophancy; evaluates 18 models, finds high failure rates (53% and 97%), and advocates safer long-term memory usage in conversational AI.
AI.motivation: Long-term memory in LLM-powered assistants enables personalization but poses safety risks. A standardized benchmark is needed to quantify these risks and guide safer deployment.
AI.method: Define two memory-specific risks (cross-domain leakage and memory-induced sycophancy), construct evaluation samples, test 18 frontier/open-source LLMs, measure failure rates on both risk types, analyze results to motivate safer memory usage.
AI.result: High incidence of safety failures: median 53% for cross-domain leakage and 97% for memory-induced sycophancy across evaluated models, indicating substantial risk even in state-of-the-art systems.
AI.conclusion: PersistBench reveals critical safety vulnerabilities in long-term memory usage by LLMs and provides a benchmark to drive development of safer, more robust memory-enabled assistants; suggests future work to mitigate these risks.

title: MemoryLLM: Plug-n-Play Interpretable Feed-Forward Memory for Transformers
summary: Understanding how transformer components operate in LLMs is important, as it is at the core of recent technological advances in artificial intelligence. In this work, we revisit the challenges associated with interpretability of feed-forward modules (FFNs) and propose MemoryLLM, which aims to decouple FFNs from self-attention and enables us to study the decoupled FFNs as context-free token-wise neural retrieval memory. In detail, we investigate how input tokens access memory locations within FFN parameters and the importance of FFN memory across different downstream tasks. MemoryLLM achieves context-free FFNs by training them in isolation from self-attention directly using the token embeddings. This approach allows FFNs to be pre-computed as token-wise lookups (ToLs), enabling on-demand transfer between VRAM and storage, additionally enhancing inference efficiency. We also introduce Flex-MemoryLLM, positioning it between a conventional transformer design and MemoryLLM. This architecture bridges the performance gap caused by training FFNs with context-free token-wise embeddings.

AI.tldr: Proposes MemoryLLM to decouple FFNs from self-attention, treating FFN memory as context-free token-wise retrieval; introduces ToLs (token-wise lookups) and Flex-MemoryLLM to balance performance and efficiency.
AI.motivation: Improve interpretability of transformer FFNs and enhance inference efficiency by decoupling FFNs from attention and studying FFN memory across downstream tasks.
AI.method: Train FFNs in isolation from self-attention using token embeddings to create context-free FFN memory; pre-compute FFNs as token-wise lookups (ToLs) enabling on-demand VRAM/storage transfer; propose Flex-MemoryLLM as a bridge between conventional transformers and MemoryLLM.
AI.result: Provides a methodological framework for interpretable, memory-based FFN components; demonstrates token-wise memory lookups and on-demand memory transfer as efficiency gains; Flex-MemoryLLM aims to close the performance gap with decoupled FFN designs across tasks.
AI.conclusion: Decoupling FFNs from self-attention is feasible and beneficial for interpretability and efficiency, with MemoryLLM and Flex-MemoryLLM offering a spectrum between standard transformers and memory-augmented designs.

title: Online Fine-Tuning of Pretrained Controllers for Autonomous Driving via Real-Time Recurrent RL
summary: Deploying pretrained policies in real-world applications presents substantial challenges that fundamentally limit the practical applicability of learning-based control systems. When autonomous systems encounter environmental changes in system dynamics, sensor drift, or task objectives, fixed policies rapidly degrade in performance. We show that employing Real-Time Recurrent Reinforcement Learning (RTRRL), a biologically plausible algorithm for online adaptation, can effectively fine-tune a pretrained policy to improve autonomous agents' performance on driving tasks. We further show that RTRRL synergizes with a recent biologically inspired recurrent network model, the Liquid-Resistance Liquid-Capacitance RNN. We demonstrate the effectiveness of this closed-loop approach in a simulated CarRacing environment and in a real-world line-following task with a RoboRacer car equipped with an event camera.

AI.tldr: RTRRL enables online fine-tuning of pretrained policies for driving tasks, using a biologically inspired recurrent model (LR-LC RNN) to adapt in real time; demonstrated in CarRacing and a RoboRacer with an event camera.
AI.motivation: Deployment of pretrained policies in real-world driving tasks suffers when dynamics drift, sensor quality changes, or task objectives shift; a method for online adaptation is needed to sustain performance.
AI.method: Combine Real-Time Recurrent Reinforcement Learning (RTRRL) for online policy adaptation with the Liquid-Resistance Liquid-Capacitance (LR-LC) RNN as the underlying recurrent architecture; evaluate via closed-loop experiments in a simulated CarRacing environment and a real-world RoboRacer line-following task using an event camera.
AI.result: Online adaptation via RTRRL improves performance of pretrained policies on driving-related tasks; the approach works in both simulation and a real-world setting, leveraging the LR-LC RNN for recurrent dynamics.
AI.conclusion: RTRRL with LR-LC RNN provides an effective framework for real-time policy adaptation in driving tasks, enabling pretrained policies to recover or improve performance under changing conditions in both simulated and real-world environments.

title: DROGO: Default Representation Objective via Graph Optimization in Reinforcement Learning
summary: In computational reinforcement learning, the default representation (DR) and its principal eigenvector have been shown to be effective for a wide variety of applications, including reward shaping, count-based exploration, option discovery, and transfer. However, in prior investigations, the eigenvectors of the DR were computed by first approximating the DR matrix, and then performing an eigendecomposition. This procedure is computationally expensive and does not scale to high-dimensional spaces. In this paper, we derive an objective for directly approximating the principal eigenvector of the DR with a neural network. We empirically demonstrate the effectiveness of the objective in a number of environments, and apply the learned eigenvectors for reward shaping.

AI.tldr: Neural objective to directly approximate the principal eigenvector of the default representation (DR) without explicit matrix decomposition, enabling scalable computation and practical use in reward shaping.
AI.motivation: DR eigenvectors are useful for reward shaping, exploration, option discovery, and transfer, but prior methods require constructing and eigendecomposing the DR matrix—computationally expensive and not scalable to high-dimensional spaces.
AI.method: Derive a differentiable objective that trains a neural network to approximate the principal eigenvector of the DR directly, avoiding explicit DR matrix construction; validate its effectiveness empirically across environments and apply the learned eigenvectors to reward shaping.
AI.result: The proposed objective yields effective neural approximation of the DR's principal eigenvector in multiple environments and demonstrates utility of the learned eigenvectors for reward shaping.
AI.conclusion: Direct neural approximation of the DR's principal eigenvector is viable, scalable, and beneficial for RL applications such as reward shaping.

title: Bridging the Sim-to-Real Gap with multipanda ros2: A Real-Time ROS2 Framework for Multimanual Systems
comment: This work has been submitted to the IEEE for possible publication
summary: We present $multipanda\_ros2$, a novel open-source ROS2 architecture for multi-robot control of Franka Robotics robots. Leveraging ros2 control, this framework provides native ROS2 interfaces for controlling any number of robots from a single process. Our core contributions address key challenges in real-time torque control, including interaction control and robot-environment modeling. A central focus of this work is sustaining a 1kHz control frequency, a necessity for real-time control and a minimum frequency required by safety standards. Moreover, we introduce a controllet-feature design pattern that enables controller-switching delays of $\le 2$ ms, facilitating reproducible benchmarking and complex multi-robot interaction scenarios. To bridge the simulation-to-reality (sim2real) gap, we integrate a high-fidelity MuJoCo simulation with quantitative metrics for both kinematic accuracy and dynamic consistency (torques, forces, and control errors). Furthermore, we demonstrate that real-world inertial parameter identification can significantly improve force and torque accuracy, providing a methodology for iterative physics refinement. Our work extends approaches from soft robotics to rigid dual-arm, contact-rich tasks, showcasing a promising method to reduce the sim2real gap and providing a robust, reproducible platform for advanced robotics research.

AI.tldr: A novel ros2 control-based, open-source framework for multi-robot control of Franka robots enabling 1kHz torque control, fast controller switching, sim2real via MuJoCo, and inertial parameter identification; extends to rigid dual-arm contact-rich tasks with reproducible benchmarking.
AI.motivation: Address the need for scalable, real-time, reproducible multi-robot control with accurate sim2real transfer, aligning with safety and performance requirements for torque-controlled manipulation.
AI.method: Leverages ros2 control within ROS2 to provide native interfaces for multiple robots from a single process. Focuses on real-time torque control with interaction modeling. Introduces a controllet-pattern to achieve controller-switching delays ≤2 ms. Integrates high-fidelity MuJoCo simulation with quantitative kinematic and dynamic metrics. Uses real-world inertial parameter identification to refine force/torque accuracy.
AI.result: Claims 1 kHz control frequency usability; controller-switching delays ≤2 ms enabling reproducible benchmarking and complex multi-robot interactions. Demonstrates sim2real with MuJoCo and provides metrics for kinematic accuracy, dynamic consistency (torques, forces, control errors). Shows inertial parameter identification improves force/torque accuracy.
AI.conclusion: Extends soft-robotics-inspired approaches to rigid dual-arm, contact-rich tasks; offers a robust, reproducible platform for advanced robotics research and a path to reduce the sim2real gap.

title: Multi-Agent Causal Reasoning System for Error Pattern Rule Automation in Vehicles
summary: Modern vehicles generate thousands of different discrete events known as Diagnostic Trouble Codes (DTCs). Automotive manufacturers use Boolean combinations of these codes, called error patterns (EPs), to characterize system faults and ensure vehicle safety. Yet, EP rules are still manually handcrafted by domain experts, a process that is expensive and prone to errors as vehicle complexity grows. This paper introduces CAREP (Causal Automated Reasoning for Error Patterns), a multi-agent system that automatizes the generation of EP rules from high-dimensional event sequences of DTCs. CAREP combines a causal discovery agent that identifies potential DTC-EP relations, a contextual information agent that integrates metadata and descriptions, and an orchestrator agent that synthesizes candidate boolean rules together with interpretable reasoning traces. Evaluation on a large-scale automotive dataset with over 29,100 unique DTCs and 474 error patterns demonstrates that CAREP can automatically and accurately discover the unknown EP rules, outperforming LLM-only baselines while providing transparent causal explanations. By uniting practical causal discovery and agent-based reasoning, CAREP represents a step toward fully automated fault diagnostics, enabling scalable, interpretable, and cost-efficient vehicle maintenance.

AI.tldr: CAREP is a multi-agent causal reasoning framework that automatically derives error-pattern (EP) rules as Boolean combinations of Diagnostic Trouble Codes (DTCs) from high-dimensional event sequences, enabling scalable, interpretable, and automated fault diagnostics.
AI.motivation: Manual crafting of EP rules by domain experts is expensive and error-prone as vehicle complexity grows; there is a need for automated, transparent fault-diagnosis reasoning.
AI.method: Three-agent architecture: a causal discovery agent to detect DTC-EP relations, a contextual information agent to incorporate metadata/descriptions, and an orchestrator agent to synthesize candidate Boolean EP rules with interpretable reasoning traces.
AI.result: On a large automotive dataset with >29,100 unique DTCs and 474 EPs, CAREP automatically discovers unknown EP rules, outperforms LLM-only baselines, and provides transparent causal explanations.
AI.conclusion: CAREP demonstrates a scalable, interpretable pathway toward fully automated fault diagnostics by integrating causal discovery and agent-based reasoning, reducing manual effort and potentially lowering maintenance costs.

title: Fed-Listing: Federated Label Distribution Inference in Graph Neural Networks
summary: Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) have been intensively studied for their expressive representation and learning performance on graph-structured data, enabling effective modeling of complex relational dependencies among nodes and edges in various domains. However, the standalone GNNs can unleash threat surfaces and privacy implications, as some sensitive graph-structured data is collected and processed in a centralized setting. To solve this issue, Federated Graph Neural Networks (FedGNNs) are proposed to facilitate collaborative learning over decentralized local graph data, aiming to preserve user privacy. Yet, emerging research indicates that even in these settings, shared model updates, particularly gradients, can unintentionally leak sensitive information of local users. Numerous privacy inference attacks have been explored in traditional federated learning and extended to graph settings, but the problem of label distribution inference in FedGNNs remains largely underexplored. In this work, we introduce Fed-Listing (Federated Label Distribution Inference in GNNs), a novel gradient-based attack designed to infer the private label statistics of target clients in FedGNNs without access to raw data or node features. Fed-Listing only leverages the final-layer gradients exchanged during training to uncover statistical patterns that reveal class proportions in a stealthy manner. An auxiliary shadow dataset is used to generate diverse label partitioning strategies, simulating various client distributions, on which the attack model is obtained. Extensive experiments on four benchmark datasets and three GNN architectures show that Fed-Listing significantly outperforms existing baselines, including random guessing and Decaf, even under challenging non-i.i.d. scenarios. Moreover, applying defense mechanisms can barely reduce our attack performance, unless the model's utility is severely degraded.

AI.tldr: Fed-Listing is a gradient-based attack on Federated GNNs that uses only final-layer gradients to infer target clients' label distributions, outperforming baselines and remaining effective under non-i.i.d. settings; defenses offer limited mitigation without harming utility.
AI.motivation: Federated GNNs pose privacy risks; label distribution inference is underexplored and real-world attacks can reveal sensitive class proportions from model updates.
AI.method: Train an attack model using final-layer gradients from FedGNN training. Use an auxiliary shadow dataset to simulate diverse label partitions and distributions across clients. The attack learns mappings from gradients to estimated class proportions. Evaluate across four benchmarks and three GNN architectures under non-i.i.d. conditions.
AI.result: Fed-Listing significantly outperforms baselines (random guessing, Decaf). It remains effective under challenging non-i.i.d. scenarios. Defenses barely reduce its performance unless model utility is severely degraded.
AI.conclusion: Label distribution leakage is a practical vulnerability in FedGNNs. Existing defenses may be insufficient, highlighting the need for stronger privacy-preserving mechanisms that protect label statistics without crippling model performance.

title: TTT-Parkour: Rapid Test-Time Training for Perceptive Robot Parkour
summary: Achieving highly dynamic humanoid parkour on unseen, complex terrains remains a challenge in robotics. Although general locomotion policies demonstrate capabilities across broad terrain distributions, they often struggle with arbitrary and highly challenging environments. To overcome this limitation, we propose a real-to-sim-to-real framework that leverages rapid test-time training (TTT) on novel terrains, significantly enhancing the robot's capability to traverse extremely difficult geometries. We adopt a two-stage end-to-end learning paradigm: a policy is first pre-trained on diverse procedurally generated terrains, followed by rapid fine-tuning on high-fidelity meshes reconstructed from real-world captures. Specifically, we develop a feed-forward, efficient, and high-fidelity geometry reconstruction pipeline using RGB-D inputs, ensuring both speed and quality during test-time training. We demonstrate that TTT-Parkour empowers humanoid robots to master complex obstacles, including wedges, stakes, boxes, trapezoids, and narrow beams. The whole pipeline of capturing, reconstructing, and test-time training requires less than 10 minutes on most tested terrains. Extensive experiments show that the policy after test-time training exhibits robust zero-shot sim-to-real transfer capability.

AI.tldr: A real-to-sim-to-real framework (TTT-Parkour) uses rapid test-time training to adapt a humanoid policy to unseen terrains, combining pretraining on procedural terrains with rapid fine-tuning on high-fidelity RGB-D meshes; achieves fast adaptation (<10 minutes) and robust zero-shot sim-to-real transfer.
AI.motivation: Current general locomotion policies struggle with highly arbitrary and challenging terrains; a rapid, terrain-specific adaptation mechanism is needed to achieve robust real-world performance on unseen geometries.
AI.method: Two-stage learning: (1) pre-train a policy on diverse procedurally generated terrains; (2) perform rapid fine-tuning at test time on high-fidelity meshes reconstructed from real-world RGB-D captures. A fast, feed-forward, high-fidelity geometry reconstruction pipeline from RGB-D is used to enable quick test-time training. The policy enables dynamic humanoid parkour across complex obstacles (wedges, stakes, boxes, trapezoids, narrow beams).
AI.result: The method enables robust zero-shot sim-to-real transfer after test-time training. The entire capture-reconstruct-train cycle takes under 10 minutes for most terrains, and the robot can handle extremely difficult geometries.
AI.conclusion: A real-to-sim-to-real TT T framework with rapid test-time training and efficient geometry reconstruction can significantly enhance dynamic humanoid locomotion on unseen terrains, enabling fast adaptation and robust sim-to-real transfer.

title: Do All Individual Layers Help? An Empirical Study of Task-Interfering Layers in Vision-Language Models
summary: Current VLMs have demonstrated capabilities across a wide range of multimodal tasks. Typically, in a pretrained VLM, all layers are engaged by default to make predictions on downstream tasks. We find that intervening on a single layer, such as by zeroing its parameters, can improve the performance on certain tasks, indicating that some layers hinder rather than help downstream tasks. We systematically investigate how individual layers influence different tasks via layer intervention. Specifically, we measure the change in performance relative to the base model after intervening on each layer and observe improvements when bypassing specific layers. This improvement can be generalizable across models and datasets, indicating the presence of Task-Interfering Layers that harm downstream tasks' performance. We introduce Task-Layer Interaction Vector, which quantifies the effect of intervening on each layer of a VLM given a task. These task-interfering layers exhibit task-specific sensitivity patterns: tasks requiring similar capabilities show consistent response trends under layer interventions, as evidenced by the high similarity in their task-layer interaction vectors. Inspired by these findings, we propose TaLo (Task-Adaptive Layer Knockout), a training-free, test-time adaptation method that dynamically identifies and bypasses the most interfering layer for a given task. Without parameter updates, TaLo improves performance across various models and datasets, including boosting Qwen-VL's accuracy on the Maps task in ScienceQA by up to 16.6%. Our work reveals an unexpected form of modularity in pretrained VLMs and provides a plug-and-play, training-free mechanism to unlock hidden capabilities at inference time. The source code will be publicly available.

AI.tldr: Layer-wise interventions reveal Task-Interfering Layers in pretrained vision-language models (VLMs). The paper introduces Task-Layer Interaction Vector and TaLo, a training-free, test-time method that bypasses the most interfering layer per task, achieving consistent gains across models/datasets (e.g., up to 16.6% on Maps task).
AI.motivation: Reveal hidden modularity in pretrained VLMs: some layers hinder downstream tasks. Quantify per-layer effects to enable task-adaptive, training-free inference-time improvements.
AI.method: Systematically intervene on individual layers (e.g., zero-out parameters) to measure performance change per task. Build Task-Layer Interaction Vector (TLIV) to represent layer effects for a given task. Identify and bypass interfering layers. Propose TaLo, a testing-time adaptation that selects the most interfering layer without parameter updates.
AI.result: Found task-interfering layers with task-specific sensitivity patterns. Bypassing these layers yields performance gains across models/datasets, including a notable 16.6% boost on a ScienceQA Maps task for Qwen-VL.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates a new form of modularity in pretrained VLMs. Provides a plug-and-play, training-free mechanism (TaLo) to unlock hidden capabilities at inference time. Potential for broad applicability, with future release of source code.

title: Mapping-Guided Task Discovery and Allocation for Robotic Inspection of Underwater Structures
comment: This paper will appear in the proceedings of the 2026 IEEE International Conference on Robotics and Automation (ICRA)
summary: Task generation for underwater multi-robot inspections without prior knowledge of existing geometry can be achieved and optimized through examination of simultaneous localization and mapping (SLAM) data. By considering hardware parameters and environmental conditions, a set of tasks is generated from SLAM meshes and optimized through expected keypoint scores and distance-based pruning. In-water tests are used to demonstrate the effectiveness of the algorithm and determine the appropriate parameters. These results are compared to simulated Voronoi partitions and boustrophedon patterns for inspection coverage on a model of the test environment. The key benefits of the presented task discovery method include adaptability to unexpected geometry and distributions that maintain coverage while focusing on areas more likely to present defects or damage.

AI.tldr: A SLAM-derived task discovery method for underwater multi-robot inspections that generates and optimizes inspection tasks from SLAM meshes, tuned by hardware/environment factors and keypoint-based pruning; validated in-water and compared to Voronoi and boustrophedon baselines, aiming for adaptable coverage that emphasizes defect-prone areas.
AI.motivation: Enable autonomous, robust inspection without prior knowledge of geometry, improving coverage efficiency and directing attention to regions likely to harbor defects.
AI.method: Generate tasks from SLAM meshes considering hardware and environmental constraints; optimize using expected keypoint scores and distance-based pruning; validate with in-water experiments to tune parameters; compare results with simulated Voronoi partitions and boustrophedon patterns on a test environment model.
AI.result: In-water tests demonstrate the effectiveness of the algorithm and help determine suitable parameter values; results are benchmarked against Voronoi partitions and boustrophedon patterns in a model environment to assess coverage and adaptability.
AI.conclusion: The task discovery approach adapts to unexpected geometry and distributions while maintaining coverage and concentrating effort on areas more likely to exhibit defects or damage.

title: ASP-Bench: From Natural Language to Logic Programs
summary:
Automating the translation of natural-language specifications into logic programs is a challenging task that affects neurosymbolic engineering. We present ASP-Bench, a benchmark comprising 128 natural language problem instances, 64 base problems with easy and hard variants. It evaluates systems that translate natural-language problems into Answer Set Programs (ASPs), a prominent form of logic programming. It provides systematic coverage of ASP features, including choice rules, aggregates, and optimization. Each problem includes reference validators that check whether solutions satisfy the problem specification.
  We characterize problems along seven largely independent reasoning aspects (optimization, temporal reasoning, default logic, resource allocation, recursion, spatial reasoning, and quantitative complexity), providing a multidimensional view of modeling difficulty.
  We test the benchmark using an agentic approach based on the ReAct (Reason and Act) framework, which achieves full saturation, demonstrating that feedback-driven iterative refinement with solver feedback provides a reliable and robust approach for modeling natural language in ASP. Our analysis across multiple agent runs enables us to gain insights into what determines a problem's modeling hardness.

AI.tldr: ASP-Bench provides a 128 NL-to-ASP benchmark to evaluate systems translating natural-language problems into Answer Set Programs, with 64 base problems (easy/hard variants), validators, and seven reasoning axes; evaluated via ReAct agent, revealing modeling hardness factors.
AI.motivation: To automate and benchmark NL-to-logic translation in neurosymbolic engineering and to understand which ASP features and problem families drive modeling difficulty.
AI.method: Constructed ASP-Bench with 128 NL problem instances, including 64 base problems with easy/hard variants; integrated reference validators; characterized problems along seven reasoning aspects (optimization, temporal reasoning, default logic, resource allocation, recursion, spatial reasoning, quantitative complexity); evaluated using a ReAct-based agentic framework with feedback-driven iterative refinement interacting with solvers.
AI.result: ReAct-based exploration achieved full saturation, demonstrating the approach's reliability; multi-run analysis yielded insights into determinants of modeling hardness; benchmark covers key ASP features (choice rules, aggregates, optimization) and provides a multidimensional difficulty space.
AI.conclusion: ASP-Bench is a comprehensive and robust benchmark for NL-to-ASP translation systems, enabling systematic evaluation across ASP features and reasoning dimensions; feedback-driven, solver-informed iteration is effective for modeling natural language in ASP and for diagnosing modeling hardness.

title: Robustness of AutoML on Dirty Categorical Data
summary: The goal of automated machine learning (AutoML) is to reduce trial and error when doing machine learning (ML). Although AutoML methods for classification are able to deal with data imperfections, such as outliers, multiple scales and missing data, their behavior is less known on dirty categorical datasets. These datasets often have several categorical features with high cardinality arising from issues such as lack of curation and automated collection. Recent research has shown that ML models can benefit from morphological encoders for dirty categorical data, leading to significantly superior predictive performance. However the effects of using such encoders in AutoML methods are not known at the moment. In this paper, we propose a pipeline that transforms categorical data into numerical data so that an AutoML can handle categorical data transformed by more advanced encoding schemes. We benchmark the current robustness of AutoML methods on a set of dirty datasets and compare it with the proposed pipeline. This allows us to get insight on differences in predictive performance. We also look at the ML pipelines built by AutoMLs in order to gain insight beyond the best model as typically returned by these methods.

AI.tldr: A pipeline converts high-cardinality dirty categorical features to numeric via advanced encoders to enable AutoML, with benchmarking showing differences in predictive performance versus standard AutoML on such data; analysis of full ML pipelines beyond the top model.
AI.motivation: AutoML struggles with dirty categorical data; morphological encoders have shown predictive gains; need to understand their impact when integrated with AutoML.
AI.method: Introduce a pipeline that encodes categorical features into numeric representations using morphological encoders; apply to dirty datasets; compare AutoML performance with and without encoding; examine the full pipelines discovered by AutoML rather than only the top model.
AI.result: Benchmark results reveal differences in predictive performance between baseline AutoML on dirty data and the proposed encoding pipeline; morphological encoders can affect performance; provide insights into pipeline behavior.
AI.conclusion: Using advanced encoders to transform categoricals before AutoML can improve robustness and performance; deeper analysis of pipelines yields insights beyond the single best model.

title: A State-Transition Framework for Efficient LLM Reasoning
summary: While Long Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning significantly improves Large Language Models (LLMs) performance on complex reasoning tasks, the substantial computational and memory costs of generating long CoT sequences limit their efficiency and practicality. Existing studies usually enhance the reasoning efficiency of LLMs by compressing CoT sequences. However, this approach conflicts with test-time scaling, limiting the reasoning capacity of LLMs. In this paper, we propose an efficient reasoning framework that models the reasoning process of LLMs as a state-transition process. Specifically, we first apply a linear attention mechanism to estimate the LLM's reasoning state, which records the historical reasoning information from previous reasoning steps. Then, based on the query prompt and the reasoning state, the LLM can efficiently perform the current reasoning step and update the state. With the linear attention, each token in the current reasoning step can directly retrieve relevant historical reasoning information from the reasoning state, without explicitly attending to tokens in previous reasoning steps. In this way, the computational complexity of attention is reduced from quadratic to linear, significantly improving the reasoning efficiency of LLMs. In addition, we propose a state-based reasoning strategy to mitigate the over-thinking issue caused by noisy reasoning steps. Extensive experiments across multiple datasets and model sizes demonstrate that our framework not only improves the reasoning efficiency of LLMs but also enhances their reasoning performance.

AI.tldr: Proposes an efficient state-transition reasoning framework for LLMs using linear attention to estimate and update a reasoning state, reducing attention complexity from quadratic to linear while maintaining or improving reasoning performance, with a state-based mechanism to mitigate over-thinking.
AI.motivation: Long Chain-of-Thought (CoT) improves reasoning but is computationally expensive; compressing CoT harms test-time scaling. Need methods that maintain reasoning capacity while reducing cost.
AI.method: Represent reasoning as a state-transition process; apply linear attention to encode historical reasoning into a state; current reasoning step is generated using the prompt and the state; tokens retrieve from the state rather than attending to all past steps; employ a state-based strategy to curb over-thinking due to noisy steps.
AI.result: Empirical results across multiple datasets and model sizes show improved efficiency and enhanced reasoning performance.
AI.conclusion: State-transition reasoning with linear attention is a viable, scalable approach that improves both efficiency and reasoning quality in LLMs.

title: Federated-inspired Single-cell Batch Integration in Latent Space
summary: Advances in single-cell RNA sequencing enable the rapid generation of massive, high-dimensional datasets, yet the accumulation of data across experiments introduces batch effects that obscure true biological signals. Existing batch correction approaches either insufficiently correct batch effects or require centralized retraining on the complete dataset, limiting their applicability in distributed and continually evolving single-cell data settings. We introduce scBatchProx, a post-hoc optimization method inspired by federated learning principles for refining cell-level embeddings produced by arbitrary upstream methods. Treating each batch as a client, scBatchProx learns batch-conditioned adapters under proximal regularization, correcting batch structure directly in latent space without requiring raw expression data or centralized optimization. The method is lightweight and deployable, optimizing batch-specific adapter parameters only. Extensive experiments show that scBatchProx consistently yields relative gains of approximately 3-8% in overall embedding quality, with batch correction and biological conservation improving in 90% and 85% of data-method pairs, respectively. We envision this work as a step toward the practical refinement of learned representations in dynamic single-cell data systems.

AI.tldr: scBatchProx is a post-hoc, federated-learning-inspired adapter method that refines latent embeddings to correct batch effects without raw data or centralized retraining; it yields consistent gains in embedding quality and biological conservation.
AI.motivation: Batch effects impair integration of single-cell RNA-seq data across experiments; centralized retraining is impractical for distributed and continually evolving datasets.
AI.method: Treat each batch as a client and learn batch-conditioned adapters under proximal regularization. The method corrects batch structure directly in latent space, without requiring raw expression data or centralized optimization. It is lightweight, optimizing only batch-specific adapter parameters.
AI.result: 3-8% relative gains in overall embedding quality; batch correction improvements in ~90% of data-method pairs; biological conservation improvements in ~85% of data-method pairs.
AI.conclusion: A practical step toward refining learned representations in dynamic single-cell data systems using a lightweight, post-hoc adapter-based approach.

title: SoMA: A Real-to-Sim Neural Simulator for Robotic Soft-body Manipulation
summary: Simulating deformable objects under rich interactions remains a fundamental challenge for real-to-sim robot manipulation, with dynamics jointly driven by environmental effects and robot actions. Existing simulators rely on predefined physics or data-driven dynamics without robot-conditioned control, limiting accuracy, stability, and generalization. This paper presents SoMA, a 3D Gaussian Splat simulator for soft-body manipulation. SoMA couples deformable dynamics, environmental forces, and robot joint actions in a unified latent neural space for end-to-end real-to-sim simulation. Modeling interactions over learned Gaussian splats enables controllable, stable long-horizon manipulation and generalization beyond observed trajectories without predefined physical models. SoMA improves resimulation accuracy and generalization on real-world robot manipulation by 20%, enabling stable simulation of complex tasks such as long-horizon cloth folding.

AI.tldr: SoMA introduces a 3D Gaussian Splat-based soft-body simulator that unifies deformable dynamics, environmental forces, and robot actions in a latent space for end-to-end real-to-sim manipulation, enabling stable long-horizon tasks and better generalization (20% resimulation accuracy gain) without predefined physics.
AI.motivation: Bridge the gap between real-world deformable manipulation and simulation by removing reliance on fixed physics models and purely data-driven but robot-agnostic dynamics, aiming for accuracy, stability, and generalization in real-to-sim pipelines.
AI.method: Model deformable objects using learned Gaussian splats and couple them with environment forces and robot joint actions in a unified latent neural representation. Train to reproduce observed real-world dynamics and enable controllable manipulation, with end-to-end simulation that supports long-horizon tasks without explicit physical equations.
AI.result: Reported 20% improvement in resimulation accuracy and better generalization on real-world robot manipulation tasks, demonstrated on complex tasks such as long-horizon cloth folding.
AI.conclusion: Gaussian splat-based latent dynamics offer a controllable, stable, and generalizable route for real-to-sim deformation manipulation without predefined physics, enabling long-horizon planning and robust robot interaction with soft bodies.

title: Workflow-R1: Group Sub-sequence Policy Optimization for Multi-turn Workflow Construction
summary: The rapid evolution of agentic workflows has demonstrated strong performance of LLM-based agents in addressing complex reasoning tasks. However, existing workflow optimization methods typically formulate workflow synthesis as a static, one-shot code-centric generation problem. This paradigm imposes excessive constraints on the model's coding capabilities and restricts the flexibility required for dynamic problem-solving. In this paper, we present Workflow-R1, a framework that reformulates workflow construction as a multi-turn, natural language-based sequential decision-making process. To resolve the optimization granularity mismatch inherent in such multi-turn interactions, we introduce Group Sub-sequence Policy Optimization (GSsPO). While explicitly tailored to align with the interleaved Think-Action dynamics of agentic reasoning, GSsPO fundamentally functions as a structure-aware RL algorithm generalizable to a broad class of multi-turn agentic sequential decision-making tasks. By recalibrating the optimization unit to the composite sub-sequence, specifically the atomic Think-Action cycle, it aligns gradient updates with the semantic boundaries of these interactions, ensuring robust learning in complex multi-turn reasoning tasks. Through extensive experiments on multiple QA benchmarks, Workflow-R1 outperforms competitive baselines, validating GSsPO as a generalized solution for sequential reasoning and establishing Workflow-R1 as a promising new paradigm for automated workflow optimization.

AI.tldr: NL-driven multi-turn workflow construction via GSsPO improves agentic reasoning; outperforms baselines on QA benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Address limitations of static, one-shot, code-centric workflow synthesis; enable dynamic, natural-language interactions and alignment with Think-Action cycles.
AI.method: Reframe workflow construction as a multi-turn NL decision process; propose Group Sub-sequence Policy Optimization (GSsPO) as a structure-aware RL algorithm; adjust optimization unit to Think-Action sub-sequences.
AI.result: Workflow-R1 outperforms competitive baselines on multiple QA benchmarks; demonstrates GSsPO's generality for sequential reasoning; positions Workflow-R1 as a new paradigm for automated workflow optimization.
AI.conclusion: GSsPO enables robust learning for complex multi-turn reasoning; Workflow-R1 offers a promising, general framework for agentic sequential decision-making tasks.

title: Open Materials Generation with Inference-Time Reinforcement Learning
summary: Continuous-time generative models for crystalline materials enable inverse materials design by learning to predict stable crystal structures, but incorporating explicit target properties into the generative process remains challenging. Policy-gradient reinforcement learning (RL) provides a principled mechanism for aligning generative models with downstream objectives but typically requires access to the score, which has prevented its application to flow-based models that learn only velocity fields. We introduce Open Materials Generation with Inference-time Reinforcement Learning (OMatG-IRL), a policy-gradient RL framework that operates directly on the learned velocity fields and eliminates the need for the explicit computation of the score. OMatG-IRL leverages stochastic perturbations of the underlying generation dynamics preserving the baseline performance of the pretrained generative model while enabling exploration and policy-gradient estimation at inference time. Using OMatG-IRL, we present the first application of RL to crystal structure prediction (CSP). Our method enables effective reinforcement of an energy-based objective while preserving diversity through composition conditioning, and it achieves performance competitive with score-based RL approaches. Finally, we show that OMatG-IRL can learn time-dependent velocity-annealing schedules, enabling accurate CSP with order-of-magnitude improvements in sampling efficiency and, correspondingly, reduction in generation time.

AI.tldr: OMatG-IRL introduces policy-gradient RL on velocity fields of continuous-time generative models for crystal structure prediction (CSP), enabling inference-time RL without score computation. It uses stochastic perturbations to allow exploration and learns velocity-annealing schedules, achieving order-of-magnitude speedups, maintaining diversity, and matching score-based RL performance.
AI.motivation: To align flow-based, continuous-time generative models with downstream energy-based objectives for CSP at inference time, without access to the score, addressing the challenge of incorporating explicit target properties into generation.
AI.method: Develop OMatG-IRL that operates directly on learned velocity fields of a continuous-time model; incorporate stochastic perturbations to enable exploration while preserving baseline performance; condition on composition to maintain diversity; optimize an energy-based objective via policy-gradient at inference time; learn time-dependent velocity-annealing schedules to accelerate sampling.
AI.result: First RL application to crystal structure prediction; achieves effective reinforcement of energy-based objectives and preserves diversity; performance competitive with score-based RL; yields order-of-magnitude improvements in sampling efficiency and reduced generation time.
AI.conclusion: OMatG-IRL demonstrates that inference-time policy-gradient on velocity fields is viable for CSP, enabling score-free RL while maintaining diversity, and suggests broader applicability to flow-based generative models in materials design.

title: Multi-Agent Monte Carlo Tree Search for Makespan-Efficient Object Rearrangement in Cluttered Spaces
summary: Object rearrangement planning in complex, cluttered environments is a common challenge in warehouses, households, and rescue sites. Prior studies largely address monotone instances, whereas real-world tasks are often non-monotone-objects block one another and must be temporarily relocated to intermediate positions before reaching their final goals. In such settings, effective multi-agent collaboration can substantially reduce the time required to complete tasks. This paper introduces Centralized, Asynchronous, Multi-agent Monte Carlo Tree Search (CAM-MCTS), a novel framework for general-purpose makespan-efficient object rearrangement planning in challenging environments. CAM-MCTS combines centralized task assignment-where agents remain aware of each other's intended actions to facilitate globally optimized planning-with an asynchronous task execution strategy that enables agents to take on new tasks at appropriate time steps, rather than waiting for others, guided by a one-step look-ahead cost estimate. This design minimizes idle time, prevents unnecessary synchronization delays, and enhances overall system efficiency. We evaluate CAM-MCTS across a diverse set of monotone and non-monotone tasks in cluttered environments, demonstrating consistent reductions in makespan compared to strong baselines. Finally, we validate our approach on a real-world multi-agent system under different configurations, further confirming its effectiveness and robustness.

AI.tldr: CAM-MCTS: a centralized, asynchronous multi-agent Monte Carlo Tree Search framework for efficient non-monotone object rearrangement, achieving makespan reductions in simulation and real-world tests.
AI.motivation: Real-world object rearrangement is often non-monotone and cluttered; monotone-only methods fail to handle temporary relocations. Centralized task awareness and asynchronous execution can minimize idle time and synchronization delays, enabling faster multi-agent planning in diverse environments.
