AI.method: Integrates centralized task assignment with awareness of other agents' intended actions, and an asynchronous execution strategy where agents pick new tasks at appropriate time steps guided by a one-step look-ahead cost estimate. The planning backbone uses Monte Carlo Tree Search to navigate the multi-agent action space and produce makespan-efficient plans for both monotone and non-monotone tasks.
AI.result: CAM-MCTS achieved consistent makespan reductions over strong baselines across a range of monotone and non-monotone tasks in cluttered environments, and was validated on a real-world multi-agent system under different configurations, demonstrating robustness and practical effectiveness.
AI.conclusion: A centralized, asynchronous MCTS framework can effectively coordinate multi-agent object rearrangement in challenging environments, offering general-purpose, makespan-efficient planning that handles both monotone and non-monotone tasks.

title: LLMs as High-Dimensional Nonlinear Autoregressive Models with Attention: Training, Alignment and Inference
comment: 27 pages, 12 figures. Mathematical survey framing LLMs as high-dimensional nonlinear autoregressive models with attention, covering training, alignment, and inference, with nanoGPT/nanochat-style code examples. Feedback welcome
summary: Large language models (LLMs) based on transformer architectures are typically described through collections of architectural components and training procedures, obscuring their underlying computational structure. This review article provides a concise mathematical reference for researchers seeking an explicit, equation-level description of LLM training, alignment, and generation. We formulate LLMs as high-dimensional nonlinear autoregressive models with attention-based dependencies. The framework encompasses pretraining via next-token prediction, alignment methods such as reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF), direct preference optimization (DPO), rejection sampling fine-tuning (RSFT), and reinforcement learning from verifiable rewards (RLVR), as well as autoregressive generation during inference. Self-attention emerges naturally as a repeated bilinear--softmax--linear composition, yielding highly expressive sequence models. This formulation enables principled analysis of alignment-induced behaviors (including sycophancy), inference-time phenomena (such as hallucination, in-context learning, chain-of-thought prompting, and retrieval-augmented generation), and extensions like continual learning, while serving as a concise reference for interpretation and further theoretical development.

AI.tldr: A concise equation-level framework that unifies LLM training, alignment, and autoregressive generation as high-dimensional nonlinear autoregressive models with attention, enabling principled analysis of alignment and inference-time phenomena.
AI.motivation: To address the lack of a compact, equation-level description of LLMs that clarifies the computational structure underlying training, alignment, and generation and supports theoretical analysis.
AI.method: Formulate LLMs as high-dimensional nonlinear autoregressive models with attention-based dependencies. Represent self-attention as a repeated bilinear–softmax–linear composition. Include pretrained next-token prediction for pretraining, and model alignment methods (RLHF, DPO, RSFT, RLVR) and autoregressive generation during inference within the same formalism. Consider extensions like continual learning.
AI.result: A unified mathematical framework enabling principled analysis of alignment-induced behaviors (e.g., sycophancy), inference-time phenomena (hallucination, in-context learning, chain-of-thought, retrieval-augmented generation), and extensions such as continual learning; serves as a concise reference for interpretation and further theoretical development.
AI.conclusion: Self-attention arises naturally within the proposed formulation, and the framework provides a compact, equation-level reference for training, alignment, and generation, facilitating interpretation and theoretical advancement.

title: 3D Foundation Model-Based Loop Closing for Decentralized Collaborative SLAM
summary: Decentralized Collaborative Simultaneous Localization And Mapping (C-SLAM) techniques often struggle to identify map overlaps due to significant viewpoint variations among robots. Motivated by recent advancements in 3D foundation models, which can register images despite large viewpoint differences, we propose a robust loop closing approach that leverages these models to establish inter-robot measurements. In contrast to resource-intensive methods requiring full 3D reconstruction within a centralized map, our approach integrates foundation models into existing SLAM pipelines, yielding scalable and robust multi-robot mapping. Our contributions include: (1) integrating 3D foundation models to reliably estimate relative poses from monocular image pairs within decentralized C-SLAM; (2) introducing robust outlier mitigation techniques critical to the use of these relative poses; and (3) developing specialized pose graph optimization formulations that efficiently resolve scale ambiguities. We evaluate our method against state-of-the-art approaches, demonstrating improvements in localization and mapping accuracy, alongside significant gains in computational and memory efficiency. These results highlight the potential of our approach for deployment in large-scale multi-robot scenarios.

AI.tldr: A decentralized C-SLAM framework leveraging 3D foundation models to estimate inter-robot relative poses from monocular images, with robust outlier mitigation and pose-graph optimization to resolve scale ambiguities, achieving better localization/map accuracy with improved efficiency.
AI.motivation: Address the challenge of identifying map overlaps under significant viewpoint variation in multi-robot SLAM, while avoiding resource-intensive centralized full 3D reconstructions.
AI.method: Integrate 3D foundation models into decentralized SLAM to estimate relative poses from monocular image pairs; apply robust outlier mitigation; develop pose-graph optimization formulations to handle scale ambiguities; integrate with existing SLAM pipelines.
AI.result: Improved localization and mapping accuracy; substantial reductions in computational and memory requirements; scalable to large-scale multi-robot deployments.
AI.conclusion: Foundation-model-assisted decentralized SLAM provides robust cross-robot loop closures and efficient deployment potential for large teams, enabling scalable multi-robot mapping.

title: Not All Preferences Are Created Equal: Stability-Aware and Gradient-Efficient Alignment for Reasoning Models
summary: Preference-based alignment is pivotal for training large reasoning models; however, standard methods like Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) typically treat all preference pairs uniformly, overlooking the evolving utility of training instances. This static approach often leads to inefficient or unstable optimization, as it wastes computation on trivial pairs with negligible gradients and suffers from noise induced by samples near uncertain decision boundaries. Facing these challenges, we propose SAGE (Stability-Aware Gradient Efficiency), a dynamic framework designed to enhance alignment reliability by maximizing the Signal-to-Noise Ratio of policy updates. Concretely, SAGE integrates a coarse-grained curriculum mechanism that refreshes candidate pools based on model competence with a fine-grained, stability-aware scoring function that prioritizes informative, confident errors while filtering out unstable samples. Experiments on multiple mathematical reasoning benchmarks demonstrate that SAGE significantly accelerates convergence and outperforms static baselines, highlighting the critical role of policy-aware, stability-conscious data selection in reasoning alignment.

AI.tldr: SAGE dynamically selects training data for preference-based alignment by maximizing gradient signal-to-noise, using a coarse curriculum and a stability-aware scoring to accelerate convergence on math reasoning tasks.
AI.motivation: To address inefficiency and instability in standard DPO by focusing learning on informative, confident errors and by refreshing candidate pools as the model’s competence evolves.
AI.method: A two-part approach: (1) a coarse-grained curriculum that refreshes candidate pools based on measured model competence; (2) a fine-grained, stability-aware scoring function that prioritizes informative, confidently incorrect samples while filtering unstable ones, with training objective oriented toward maximizing the policy-update signal-to-noise ratio.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on multiple mathematical reasoning benchmarks shows faster convergence and superior performance compared to static baselines, highlighting the importance of policy-aware, stability-conscious data selection in reasoning alignment.
AI.conclusion: Dynamic, stability-aware data selection improves efficiency and reliability of preference-based alignment for reasoning tasks and offers a practical path to more robust alignment.

title: Towards Building Non-Fine-Tunable Foundation Models
summary: Open-sourcing foundation models (FMs) enables broad reuse but also exposes model trainers to economic and safety risks from unrestricted downstream fine-tuning. We address this problem by building non-fine-tunable foundation models: models that remain broadly usable in their released form while yielding limited adaptation gains under task-agnostic unauthorized fine-tuning. We propose Private Mask Pre-Training (PMP), a pre-training framework that concentrates representation learning into a sparse subnetwork identified early in training. The binary mask defining this subnetwork is kept private, and only the final dense weights are released. This forces unauthorized fine-tuning without access to the mask to update parameters misaligned with pretraining subspace, inducing an intrinsic mismatch between the fine-tuning objective and the pre-training geometry. We provide theoretical analysis showing that this mismatch destabilizes gradient-based adaptation and bounds fine-tuning gains. Empirical results on large language models demonstrating that PMP preserves base model performance while consistently degrading unauthorized fine-tuning across a wide range of downstream tasks, with the strength of non-fine-tunability controlled by the mask ratio.

AI.tldr: Proposes Private Mask Pre-Training (PMP) to create non-fine-tunable foundation models by hiding a sparse subnetwork mask; releases only dense weights; yields strong resistance to unauthorized fine-tuning while maintaining base performance.
AI.motivation: Open-sourced foundation models risk unrestricted downstream adaptation; need a mechanism to preserve usability while limiting adaptation gains from unauthorized fine-tuning, reducing economic/safety risks.
AI.method: PMP trains a sparse subnetwork early in training; the mask identifying this subnetwork is kept private; only final dense weights are released. The adaptation is constrained by subspace alignment, creating a mismatch with downstream fine-tuning objectives; theoretical analysis supports instability of gradient-based adaptation; empirical validation on large language models shows preserved base performance and degraded unauthorized fine-tuning; mask ratio controls strength.
AI.result: Theoretical: the mismatch between pretraining geometry and fine-tuning objective destabilizes gradient-based updates and bounds fine-tuning gains. Empirical: PMP maintains base model performance and consistently reduces unauthorized fine-tuning across diverse downstream tasks; effectiveness scales with mask ratio.
AI.conclusion: PMP provides a practical way to produce non-fine-tunable FMs, enabling broad use while constraining misuse through private masking, with tunable strength via the mask ratio and solid theoretical backing.

title: World-Gymnast: Training Robots with Reinforcement Learning in a World Model
summary: Robot learning from interacting with the physical world is fundamentally bottlenecked by the cost of physical interaction. The two alternatives, supervised finetuning (SFT) from expert demonstrations and reinforcement learning (RL) in a software-based simulator, are limited by the amount of expert data available and the sim-to-real gap for manipulation. With the recent emergence of world models learned from real-world video-action data, we ask the question of whether training a policy in a world model can be more effective than supervised learning or software simulation in achieving better real-robot performance. We propose World-Gymnast, which performs RL finetuning of a vision-language-action (VLA) policy by rolling out the policy in an action-conditioned video world model and rewarding the rollouts with a vision-language model (VLM). On the Bridge robot setup, World-Gymnast outperforms SFT by as much as 18x and outperforms software simulator by as much as 2x. More importantly, World-Gymnast demonstrates intriguing capabilities of RL with a world model, including training on diverse language instructions and novel scenes from the world model, test-time training in a novel scene, and online iterative world model and policy improvement. Our results suggest learning a world model and training robot policies in the cloud could be the key to bridging the gap between robots that work in demonstrations and robots that can work in anyone's household.

AI.tldr: World-Gymnast uses reinforcement learning in a real-world video-based world model to finetune a vision-language-action policy, achieving large gains over supervised fine-tuning and simulation in a real-robot setting and enabling diverse language instruction and novel-scene generalization.
AI.motivation: To overcome the high cost of physical robot interaction and the limitations of supervised fine-tuning with limited expert data and sim-to-real gaps; leverage world models learned from real-world video-action data to improve real-robot performance and generalization.
AI.method: Train a vision-language-action policy and finetune it with RL by rolling out in an action-conditioned video world model; reward the rollouts using a vision-language model; evaluate on the Bridge robot, comparing to SFT and software simulation.
AI.result: World-Gymnast outperforms supervised fine-tuning by up to 18x and software simulation by up to 2x. It demonstrates RL with a world model enabling training on diverse language instructions, generalization to novel scenes, test-time adaptation in new scenes, and online iterative improvement of the world model and policy.
AI.conclusion: Learning a world model and training policies in the cloud may bridge the gap between demonstration-driven robots and household-scale robots, enabling more robust, flexible, and language-capable robot behavior.

title: FutureMind: Equipping Small Language Models with Strategic Thinking-Pattern Priors via Adaptive Knowledge Distillation
summary: Small Language Models (SLMs) are attractive for cost-sensitive and resource-limited settings due to their efficient, low-latency inference. However, they often struggle with complex, knowledge-intensive tasks that require structured reasoning and effective retrieval. To address these limitations, we propose FutureMind, a modular reasoning framework that equips SLMs with strategic thinking-pattern priors via adaptive knowledge distillation from large language models (LLMs). FutureMind introduces a dynamic reasoning pipeline composed of four key modules: Problem Analysis, Logical Reasoning, Strategy Planning, and Retrieval Guidance. This pipeline is augmented by three distinct retrieval paradigms that decompose complex queries into tractable subproblems, ensuring efficient and accurate retrieval execution. Extensive experiments on multi-hop QA benchmarks, including 2WikiMultihopQA, MuSiQue, Bamboogle, and Frames, demonstrate the superiority of FutureMind. It consistently outperforms strong baselines such as Search-o1, achieving state-of-the-art results under free training conditions across diverse SLM architectures and scales. Beyond empirical gains, our analysis reveals that the process of thinking-pattern distillation is restricted by the cognitive bias bottleneck between the teacher (LLMs) and student (SLMs) models. This provides new perspectives on the transferability of reasoning skills, paving the way for the development of SLMs that combine efficiency with genuine cognitive capability.

AI.tldr: FutureMind introduces a modular four-stage reasoning framework for small language models with adaptive distillation from LLMs, plus three retrieval paradigms, achieving state-of-the-art results on multi-hop QA benchmarks and highlighting a cognitive-bias bottleneck in transferring reasoning skills.
AI.motivation: Enable efficient yet capable SLMs to tackle complex, knowledge-intensive tasks that require structured reasoning and retrieval, by injecting thinking-pattern priors learned from LLMs.
AI.method: A dynamic four-module pipeline (Problem Analysis, Logical Reasoning, Strategy Planning, Retrieval Guidance) combined with three retrieval paradigms that break complex queries into tractable subproblems; adaptive knowledge distillation from LLMs to instill reasoning patterns; evaluation on multi-hop QA benchmarks across diverse SLMs.
AI.result: Outperforms strong baselines (e.g., Search-o1) and achieves state-of-the-art results under free training conditions across multiple SLM architectures on 2WikiMultihopQA, MuSiQue, Bamboogle, Frames.
AI.conclusion: The transfer of thinking-patterns is limited by a cognitive-bias bottleneck between teacher (LLMs) and student (SLMs); this insight informs future work on designing SLMs that combine efficiency with genuine cognitive capabilities.

title: Stabilizing Decentralized Federated Fine-Tuning via Topology-Aware Alternating LoRA
summary: Decentralized federated learning (DFL), a serverless variant of federated learning, poses unique challenges for parameter-efficient fine-tuning due to the factorized structure of low-rank adaptation (LoRA). Unlike linear parameters, decentralized aggregation of LoRA updates introduces topology-dependent cross terms that can destabilize training under dynamic communication graphs. We propose \texttt{TAD-LoRA}, a Topology-Aware Decentralized Low-Rank Adaptation framework that coordinates the updates and mixing of LoRA factors to control inter-client misalignment. We theoretically prove the convergence of \texttt{TAD-LoRA} under non-convex objectives, explicitly characterizing the trade-off between topology-induced cross-term error and block-coordinate representation bias governed by the switching interval of alternative training. Experiments under various communication conditions validate our analysis, showing that \texttt{TAD-LoRA} achieves robust performance across different communication scenarios, remaining competitive in strongly connected topologies and delivering clear gains under moderately and weakly connected topologies, with particularly strong results on the MNLI dataset.

AI.tldr: Proposes TAD-LoRA, a topology-aware decentralized low-rank adaptation for federated learning that coordinates LoRA updates to mitigate cross-term misalignment in dynamic graphs. Proves convergence for non-convex objectives and validates robustness across communication topologies, with strong gains on MNLI.
AI.motivation: DFL with LoRA suffers topology-dependent cross terms that destabilize training; need a method to coordinate factor updates and mixing to maintain alignment across clients.
AI.method: Introduce TAD-LoRA that coordinates updates and mixing of LoRA factors; provide theoretical convergence analysis under non-convex objectives; characterize the trade-off between topology-induced cross-term error and block-coordinate representation bias via switching interval; validate with experiments across various communication graphs.
AI.result: Proved convergence for non-convex objectives; empirical results show robust performance across communication conditions; competitive in strongly connected graphs and superior under moderate/weak connectivity; notable gains on MNLI dataset.
AI.conclusion: Topology-aware coordination of LoRA in decentralized FL stabilizes training by controlling cross-term misalignment and bias; effective across graph topologies, especially when connectivity is limited; the switching interval is a key design parameter balancing error and representation bias.

title: Relationship-Aware Hierarchical 3D Scene Graph for Task Reasoning
summary: Representing and understanding 3D environments in a structured manner is crucial for autonomous agents to navigate and reason about their surroundings. While traditional Simultaneous Localization and Mapping (SLAM) methods generate metric reconstructions and can be extended to metric-semantic mapping, they lack a higher level of abstraction and relational reasoning. To address this gap, 3D scene graphs have emerged as a powerful representation for capturing hierarchical structures and object relationships. In this work, we propose an enhanced hierarchical 3D scene graph that integrates open-vocabulary features across multiple abstraction levels and supports object-relational reasoning. Our approach leverages a Vision Language Model (VLM) to infer semantic relationships. Notably, we introduce a task reasoning module that combines Large Language Models (LLM) and a VLM to interpret the scene graph's semantic and relational information, enabling agents to reason about tasks and interact with their environment more intelligently. We validate our method by deploying it on a quadruped robot in multiple environments and tasks, highlighting its ability to reason about them.

AI.tldr: An enhanced hierarchical 3D scene graph integrates open-vocabulary features across multiple abstraction levels, enabling object-relational reasoning with a VLM, plus a task reasoning module that combines an LLM and VLM to interpret semantic and relational information for autonomous agents. Validated on a quadruped robot across multiple environments/tasks to demonstrate improved reasoning about tasks.
AI.motivation: Traditional SLAM-based 3D representations provide metric reconstructions but lack high-level abstraction and relational reasoning. There is a need for open-vocabulary, multi-level semantic understanding and task-level reasoning to enable autonomous agents to plan and interact more intelligently.
AI.method: Develop an enhanced hierarchical 3D scene graph that incorporates open-vocabulary features at multiple abstraction levels. Use a Vision-Language Model (VLM) to infer semantic relationships within the scene graph. Introduce a task reasoning module that combines a Large Language Model (LLM) and the VLM to interpret scene graph semantics and relations for task-oriented reasoning. Validate by deploying on a quadruped robot across multiple environments and tasks.
AI.result: Qualitative demonstration on a quadruped robot across diverse environments and tasks, showing the system’s ability to reason about tasks and interact with objects and surroundings more intelligently.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework enables open-vocabulary, multi-level relational understanding of 3D scenes and enhances task-oriented reasoning for autonomous agents, potentially improving navigation, interaction, and decision-making in complex environments.

title: FedMOA: Federated GRPO for Personalized Reasoning LLMs under Heterogeneous Rewards
summary: Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) has recently emerged as an effective approach for improving the reasoning capabilities of large language models through online multi-objective reinforcement learning. While personalization on private data is increasingly vital, traditional Reinforcement Learning (RL) alignment is often memory-prohibitive for on-device federated learning due to the overhead of maintaining a separate critic network. GRPO's critic-free architecture enables feasible on-device training, yet transitioning to a federated setting introduces systemic challenges: heterogeneous reward definitions, imbalanced multi-objective optimization, and high training costs. We propose FedMOA, a federated GRPO framework for multi-objective alignment under heterogeneous rewards. FedMOA stabilizes local training through an online adaptive weighting mechanism via hypergradient descent, which prioritizes primary reasoning as auxiliary objectives saturate. On the server side, it utilizes a task- and accuracy-aware aggregation strategy to prioritize high-quality updates. Experiments on mathematical reasoning and code generation benchmarks demonstrate that FedMOA consistently outperforms federated averaging, achieving accuracy gains of up to 2.2% while improving global performance, personalization, and multi-objective balance.

AI.tldr: FedMOA: a federated GRPO framework enabling multi-objective alignment on heterogeneous rewards with adaptive local weighting and quality-aware server aggregation, achieving up to 2.2% accuracy gains over FedAvg.
AI.motivation: Enable on-device federated learning for Group Relative Policy Optimization without the memory overhead of critics, while addressing heterogeneous rewards and multi-objective trade-offs.
AI.method: Local training employs online adaptive weighting via hypergradient descent to emphasize primary reasoning as auxiliary objectives saturate; server-side aggregation is task- and accuracy-aware to prioritize high-quality updates.
AI.result: Empirical results on mathematical reasoning and code generation benchmarks show FedMOA consistently outperforms FedAvg, with accuracy gains up to 2.2% and improvements in global performance and personalization.
AI.conclusion: FedMOA stabilizes federated GRPO training under heterogeneous rewards and improves multi-objective balance and overall performance.

title: TIC-VLA: A Think-in-Control Vision-Language-Action Model for Robot Navigation in Dynamic Environments
summary: Robots in dynamic, human-centric environments must follow language instructions while maintaining real-time reactive control. Vision-language-action (VLA) models offer a promising framework, but they assume temporally aligned reasoning and control, despite semantic inference being inherently delayed relative to real-time action. We introduce Think-in-Control (TIC)-VLA, a latency-aware framework that explicitly models delayed semantic reasoning during action generation. TIC-VLA defines a delayed semantic-control interface that conditions action generation on delayed vision-language semantic states and explicit latency metadata, in addition to current observations, enabling policies to compensate for asynchronous reasoning. We further propose a latency-consistent training pipeline that injects reasoning inference delays during imitation learning and online reinforcement learning, aligning training with asynchronous deployment. To support realistic evaluation, we present DynaNav, a physics-accurate, photo-realistic simulation suite for language-guided navigation in dynamic environments. Extensive experiments in simulation and on a real robot show that TIC-VLA consistently outperforms prior VLA models while maintaining robust real-time control under multi-second reasoning latency. Project website: https://ucla-mobility.github.io/TIC-VLA/

AI.tldr: Latency-aware vision-language-action (VLA) model TIC-VLA enables asynchronous, delayed semantic reasoning in action generation, with a latency-consistent training pipeline and a realism-rich simulation suite DynaNav; results show improved real-time control under multi-second delays.
AI.motivation: VLA models assume temporally aligned reasoning and control, but semantic inference is inherently delayed in real deployments. A framework that explicitly models and compensates for such latency is needed to maintain robust, real-time robot control in dynamic, human-centric environments.
AI.method: Introduce a delayed semantic-control interface that conditions action generation on delayed vision-language semantic states and explicit latency metadata, in addition to current observations. Propose a latency-consistent training pipeline that injects reasoning inference delays during imitation learning and online reinforcement learning. Provide DynaNav, a physics-accurate, photo-realistic simulation suite for language-guided navigation in dynamic environments, and validate on real robots in addition to simulation.
AI.result: TIC-VLA consistently outperforms prior VLA models and maintains robust real-time control under multi-second reasoning latency, demonstrated through extensive simulation studies and real-robot experiments.
AI.conclusion: Modeling and training for delayed semantic reasoning is effective for latency-prone VLA systems. TIC-VLA offers a practical pathway to deploy language-guided robotics in dynamic, real-time settings, with DynaNav enabling realistic benchmarking.

title: LLM-Driven Ontology Construction for Enterprise Knowledge Graphs
summary: Enterprise Knowledge Graphs have become essential for unifying heterogeneous data and enforcing semantic governance. However, the construction of their underlying ontologies remains a resource-intensive, manual process that relies heavily on domain expertise. This paper introduces OntoEKG, a LLM-driven pipeline designed to accelerate the generation of domain-specific ontologies from unstructured enterprise data. Our approach decomposes the modelling task into two distinct phases: an extraction module that identifies core classes and properties, and an entailment module that logically structures these elements into a hierarchy before serialising them into standard RDF. Addressing the significant lack of comprehensive benchmarks for end-to-end ontology construction, we adopt a new evaluation dataset derived from documents across the Data, Finance, and Logistics sectors. Experimental results highlight both the potential and the challenges of this approach, achieving a fuzzy-match F1-score of 0.724 in the Data domain while revealing limitations in scope definition and hierarchical reasoning.

AI.tldr: Two-phase LLM-assisted ontology generation (extraction of classes/properties, then entailment-driven hierarchy), with RDF serialization; evaluated on Data/Finance/Logistics dataset; Data domain F1=0.724; promising but limited by scope definition and hierarchical reasoning.
AI.motivation: Enterprise Knowledge Graphs demand domain-specific ontologies but their construction is resource-intensive and manual; there is a lack of end-to-end benchmarks for ontology construction from unstructured data.
AI.method: Two-stage pipeline: an extraction module identifies core classes and properties from documents; an entailment module logically structures these elements into a hierarchy and serializes them into RDF. Evaluation uses a dataset derived from documents in Data, Finance, and Logistics sectors.
AI.result: Demonstrates potential and challenges of the approach; Data-domain fuzzy-match F1=0.724; identifies limitations in scope definition and hierarchical reasoning.
AI.conclusion: LLM-driven ontology construction is promising but requires improvements in scope specification and hierarchical reasoning; contributions include a new benchmark dataset and a two-phase pipeline."

title: LatentTrack: Sequential Weight Generation via Latent Filtering
summary:
We introduce LatentTrack (LT), a sequential neural architecture for online probabilistic prediction under nonstationary dynamics. LT performs causal Bayesian filtering in a low-dimensional latent space and uses a lightweight hypernetwork to generate predictive model parameters at each time step, enabling constant-time online adaptation without per-step gradient updates.
  At each time step, a learned latent model predicts the next latent distribution, which is updated via amortized inference using new observations, yielding a predict--generate--update filtering framework in function space. The formulation supports both structured (Markovian) and unstructured latent dynamics within a unified objective, while Monte Carlo inference over latent trajectories produces calibrated predictive mixtures with fixed per-step cost. Evaluated on long-horizon online regression using the Jena Climate benchmark, LT consistently achieves lower negative log-likelihood and mean squared error than stateful sequential and static uncertainty-aware baselines, with competitive calibration, demonstrating that latent-conditioned function evolution is an effective alternative to traditional latent-state modeling under distribution shift.

AI.tldr: LT is a lightweight online probabilistic predictor that uses latent-conditioned function evolution with an amortized, gradient-free adaptation loop to achieve constant-time online updates under nonstationary dynamics, outperforming baselines on Jena Climate.
AI.motivation: Nonstationary data streams require online probabilistic prediction with fast adaptation and calibrated uncertainty; traditional latent-state models may be slow or brittle; latent-conditioned function evolution offers robust performance with fixed per-step cost.
AI.method: Causal Bayesian filtering in a low-dimensional latent space; a hypernetwork generates predictive model parameters at each time step; latent distribution prediction and amortized update via new observations; Monte Carlo inference over latent trajectories yields predictive mixtures; supports structured/unstructured latent dynamics; fixed per-step cost.
AI.result: On the Jena Climate benchmark, LT yields lower negative log-likelihood and RMSE than stateful sequential and static uncertainty-aware baselines; competitive calibration; demonstrates effective adaptation under distribution shift.
AI.conclusion: Latent-conditioned function evolution provides an effective alternative to latent-state models for online nonstationary prediction, enabling constant-time adaptation with calibrated uncertainty.

title: HumanX: Toward Agile and Generalizable Humanoid Interaction Skills from Human Videos
summary: Enabling humanoid robots to perform agile and adaptive interactive tasks has long been a core challenge in robotics. Current approaches are bottlenecked by either the scarcity of realistic interaction data or the need for meticulous, task-specific reward engineering, which limits their scalability. To narrow this gap, we present HumanX, a full-stack framework that compiles human video into generalizable, real-world interaction skills for humanoids, without task-specific rewards. HumanX integrates two co-designed components: XGen, a data generation pipeline that synthesizes diverse and physically plausible robot interaction data from video while supporting scalable data augmentation; and XMimic, a unified imitation learning framework that learns generalizable interaction skills. Evaluated across five distinct domains--basketball, football, badminton, cargo pickup, and reactive fighting--HumanX successfully acquires 10 different skills and transfers them zero-shot to a physical Unitree G1 humanoid. The learned capabilities include complex maneuvers such as pump-fake turnaround fadeaway jumpshots without any external perception, as well as interactive tasks like sustained human-robot passing sequences over 10 consecutive cycles--learned from a single video demonstration. Our experiments show that HumanX achieves over 8 times higher generalization success than prior methods, demonstrating a scalable and task-agnostic pathway for learning versatile, real-world robot interactive skills.

AI.tldr: A data-efficient, task-agnostic framework (HumanX) that converts human video into generalizable humanoid interaction skills via XGen data generation and XMimic imitation learning, enabling zero-shot transfer and broad real-world applicability without task-specific rewards.
AI.motivation: Address the scarcity of realistic interaction data and the need for task-specific reward engineering that hinder scalable learning of humanoid interactive skills.
AI.method: Two co-designed components: XGen, a video-derived data generation pipeline that synthesizes diverse, physically plausible robot interaction data with scalable augmentation; and XMimic, a unified imitation learning framework that extracts generalizable interaction skills from that data.
AI.result: Validated across basketball, football, badminton, cargo pickup, and reactive fighting: 10 skills learned; zero-shot transfer to Unitree G1 humanoid; includes complex maneuvers and sustained human-robot interaction; >8x improvement in generalization over prior methods.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates a scalable, task-agnostic pathway for learning versatile real-world interactive skills for humanoids from human video without task-specific reward engineering.

title: RE-MCDF: Closed-Loop Multi-Expert LLM Reasoning for Knowledge-Grounded Clinical Diagnosis
summary: Electronic medical records (EMRs), particularly in neurology, are inherently heterogeneous, sparse, and noisy, which poses significant challenges for large language models (LLMs) in clinical diagnosis. In such settings, single-agent systems are vulnerable to self-reinforcing errors, as their predictions lack independent validation and can drift toward spurious conclusions. Although recent multi-agent frameworks attempt to mitigate this issue through collaborative reasoning, their interactions are often shallow and loosely structured, failing to reflect the rigorous, evidence-driven processes used by clinical experts. More fundamentally, existing approaches largely ignore the rich logical dependencies among diseases, such as mutual exclusivity, pathological compatibility, and diagnostic confusion. This limitation prevents them from ruling out clinically implausible hypotheses, even when sufficient evidence is available. To overcome these, we propose RE-MCDF, a relation-enhanced multi-expert clinical diagnosis framework. RE-MCDF introduces a generation--verification--revision closed-loop architecture that integrates three complementary components: (i) a primary expert that generates candidate diagnoses and supporting evidence, (ii) a laboratory expert that dynamically prioritizes heterogeneous clinical indicators, and (iii) a multi-relation awareness and evaluation expert group that explicitly enforces inter-disease logical constraints. Guided by a medical knowledge graph (MKG), the first two experts adaptively reweight EMR evidence, while the expert group validates and corrects candidate diagnoses to ensure logical consistency. Extensive experiments on the neurology subset of CMEMR (NEEMRs) and on our curated dataset (XMEMRs) demonstrate that RE-MCDF consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines in complex diagnostic scenarios.

AI.tldr: RE-MCDF is a three-expert closed-loop framework for clinical diagnosis on EMRs that uses generation, verification, and revision stages guided by a medical knowledge graph to enforce inter-disease logical constraints, achieving superior performance on NEEMRs and XMEMRs.
AI.motivation: EMR heterogeneity, sparsity, and noise hinder LLM-based diagnosis; single-agent predictions risk self-reinforcing errors; existing multi-agent approaches are shallow and ignore disease-level logical dependencies, limiting plausible hypotheses.
AI.method: A generation–verification–revision architecture with: (i) a primary expert producing candidate diagnoses and evidence, (ii) a laboratory expert prioritizing heterogeneous clinical indicators, and (iii) a multi-relation awareness and evaluation expert group enforcing inter-disease logical constraints via a medical knowledge graph to reweight evidence and validate diagnoses.
AI.result: Experiments on the neurology subset of CMEMR (NEEMRs) and the curated XMEMRs dataset show RE-MCDF consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines in complex diagnostic scenarios.
AI.conclusion: Integrating generation, verification, and revision with explicit disease-relations constraints and MKG guidance improves diagnostic accuracy and logical consistency; results motivate broader validation and potential clinical adoption, with attention to knowledge graph quality and scalability.

title: Search Inspired Exploration in Reinforcement Learning
summary: Exploration in environments with sparse rewards remains a fundamental challenge in reinforcement learning (RL). Existing approaches such as curriculum learning and Go-Explore often rely on hand-crafted heuristics, while curiosity-driven methods risk converging to suboptimal policies. We propose Search-Inspired Exploration in Reinforcement Learning (SIERL), a novel method that actively guides exploration by setting sub-goals based on the agent's learning progress. At the beginning of each episode, SIERL chooses a sub-goal from the \textit{frontier} (the boundary of the agent's known state space), before the agent continues exploring toward the main task objective. The key contribution of our method is the sub-goal selection mechanism, which provides state-action pairs that are neither overly familiar nor completely novel. Thus, it assures that the frontier is expanded systematically and that the agent is capable of reaching any state within it. Inspired by search, sub-goals are prioritized from the frontier based on estimates of cost-to-come and cost-to-go, effectively steering exploration towards the most informative regions. In experiments on challenging sparse-reward environments, SIERL outperforms dominant baselines in both achieving the main task goal and generalizing to reach arbitrary states in the environment.

AI.tldr: SIERL introduces frontier-based sub-goal selection to guide exploration in sparse-reward RL, using cost-to-come and cost-to-go estimates to prioritize frontier states; achieves better task success and generalization.
AI.motivation: Sparse rewards impede efficient exploration in RL. Existing curriculum and curiosity-based methods rely on heuristics or risk suboptimal convergence. A principled, scalable exploration strategy that expands the known state space is needed.
AI.method: At the start of each episode, SIERL selects a sub-goal from the frontier (boundary of the agent's known state space) and then explores toward the main task objective. The sub-goal is chosen to be neither too familiar nor too novel, ensuring systematic frontier expansion. Sub-goals are prioritized using search-inspired estimates of cost-to-come and cost-to-go to steer exploration toward informative regions.
AI.result: Across challenging sparse-reward environments, SIERL outperforms dominant baselines in both achieving the main task goal and generalizing to reach arbitrary states within the environment, demonstrating improved exploration efficiency and state-space coverage.
AI.conclusion: Sub-goal guided exploration based on frontier dynamics and cost estimates provides a principled, efficient alternative to handcrafted curricula or pure curiosity, improving both success and generalization in sparse-reward RL.

title: Flow Policy Gradients for Robot Control
summary: Likelihood-based policy gradient methods are the dominant approach for training robot control policies from rewards. These methods rely on differentiable action likelihoods, which constrain policy outputs to simple distributions like Gaussians. In this work, we show how flow matching policy gradients -- a recent framework that bypasses likelihood computation -- can be made effective for training and fine-tuning more expressive policies in challenging robot control settings. We introduce an improved objective that enables success in legged locomotion, humanoid motion tracking, and manipulation tasks, as well as robust sim-to-real transfer on two humanoid robots. We then present ablations and analysis on training dynamics. Results show how policies can exploit the flow representation for exploration when training from scratch, as well as improved fine-tuning robustness over baselines.

AI.tldr: Flow matching policy gradients extend policy-gradient robotics by bypassing action likelihoods, enabling expressive policies (e.g., normalizing flows) to achieve robust learning and sim-to-real transfer in challenging tasks.
AI.motivation: Likelihood-based policies constrain outputs to simple distributions (Gaussians), limiting expressiveness and exploration; there is a need for methods that support expressive policies and robust sim-to-real performance.
AI.method: Adopts flow matching policy gradients with an improved objective to train expressive policies without explicit likelihood computations; demonstrates on legged locomotion, humanoid motion tracking, manipulation, and sim-to-real transfer across two humanoid robots; includes ablations and training-dynamics analysis.
AI.result: Improved success across tasks: legged locomotion, humanoid tracking, and manipulation; robust sim-to-real transfer on two humanoid robots; flow representation enhances exploration during from-scratch training and improves fine-tuning robustness vs baselines.
AI.conclusion: Flow matching policy gradients enable effective training of expressive policies for complex robotics, yielding practical gains in performance and transfer, with insights into dynamics and exploration behavior.

title: Model Specific Task Similarity for Vision Language Model Selection via Layer Conductance
summary: While open sourced Vision-Language Models (VLMs) have proliferated, selecting the optimal pretrained model for a specific downstream task remains challenging. Exhaustive evaluation is often infeasible due to computational constraints and data limitations in few shot scenarios. Existing selection methods fail to fully address this: they either rely on data-intensive proxies or use symmetric textual descriptors that neglect the inherently directional and model-specific nature of transferability. To address this problem, we propose a framework that grounds model selection in the internal functional dynamics of the visual encoder. Our approach represents each task via layer wise conductance and derives a target-conditioned block importance distribution through entropy regularized alignment. Building on this, we introduce Directional Conductance Divergence (DCD), an asymmetric metric that quantifies how effectively a source task covers the target's salient functional blocks. This allows for predicting target model rankings by aggregating source task ranks without direct inference. Experimental results on 48 VLMs across 21 datasets demonstrate that our method outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, achieving a 14.7% improvement in NDCG@5 over SWAB.

AI.tldr: Proposes a directional, architecture-aware model selection framework for vision-language models (VLMs) that uses layer-wise conductance to encode task representations and a target-conditioned, entropy-regularized block importance distribution. Introduces Directional Conductance Divergence (DCD), an asymmetric metric that predicts target model rankings by aggregating source task rankings without direct inference. Demonstrates improved model selection performance (14.7% gain in NDCG@5 over SWAB) across 48 VLMs and 21 datasets.
AI.motivation: Selecting pretrained VLMs for a given downstream task is challenging due to computational constraints and limited data in few-shot settings. Existing methods rely on data-heavy proxies or symmetric textual descriptors that ignore directional transferability. A method that captures the intrinsic, task- and model-specific transfer dynamics could enable accurate, efficient model selection.
AI.method: Represent each task via layer-wise conductance of the visual encoder; derive a target-conditioned block importance distribution through entropy-regularized alignment. Define Directional Conductance Divergence (DCD) as an asymmetric metric measuring how well a source task covers the target's salient functional blocks. Predict target model rankings by aggregating source task rankings, obviating direct inference on the target.
AI.result: Empirically validated on 48 VLMs across 21 datasets, the approach outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, achieving a 14.7% improvement in NDCG@5 over SWAB.
AI.conclusion: Grounding model selection in the internal functional dynamics and directional transferability of VLMs yields more accurate target model rankings and scalable model selection across a large model and dataset suite; the directional conductance framework effectively leverages source-task information to predict target performance without requiring costly target evaluations.

title: PAIR-Former: Budgeted Relational MIL for miRNA Target Prediction
comment: Preprint. Under review. During the preprint stage, inquiries and feedback can be directed to Jiaqi Yin (yjqhit@gmail.com)
summary: Functional miRNA--mRNA targeting is a large-bag prediction problem: each transcript yields a heavy-tailed pool of candidate target sites (CTSs), yet only a pair-level label is observed. We formalize this regime as \emph{Budgeted Relational Multi-Instance Learning (BR-MIL)}, where at most $K$ instances per bag may receive expensive encoding and relational processing under a hard compute budget. We propose \textbf{PAIR-Former} (Pool-Aware Instance-Relational Transformer), a BR-MIL pipeline that performs a cheap full-pool scan, selects up to $K$ diverse CTSs on CPU, and applies a permutation-invariant Set Transformer aggregator on the selected tokens. On miRAW, PAIR-Former outperforms strong pooling baselines at a practical operating budget ($K^\star{=}64$) while providing a controllable accuracy--compute trade-off as $K$ varies. We further provide theory linking budgeted selection to (i) approximation error decreasing with $K$ and (ii) generalization terms governed by $K$ in the expensive relational component.

AI.tldr: Budgeted Relational MIL for functional miRNA–mRNA targeting; PAIR-Former uses a cheap full-pool scan, selects up to K diverse CTSs on CPU, and applies a permutation-invariant Set Transformer; achieves better performance over pooling baselines at budget K, with a theoretical link between K, approximation error, and generalization.
AI.motivation: Predicting miRNA–mRNA interactions is a large-scale, multi-instance learning problem where each transcript yields many candidate target sites (CTSs) but only a bag-level label is observed. Under a fixed compute budget, only up to K CTSs can be encoded/relationally processed, motivating a budgeted MIL approach.
AI.method: BR-MIL framework with PAIR-Former: perform a cheap full-pool scan to enumerate CTSs, select up to K diverse CTSs on CPU, and use a permutation-invariant Set Transformer to aggregate information over the selected tokens.
AI.result: PAIR-Former outperforms strong pooling baselines on miRAW under a practical budget (K* = 64) and offers a tunable accuracy–compute trade-off as K varies.
AI.conclusion: Budgeted relational MIL with PAIR-Former provides an effective, scalable solution for miRNA–mRNA targeting prediction with theoretical backing for how budget K controls approximation error and generalization.

title: Efficient UAV trajectory prediction: A multi-modal deep diffusion framework
summary: To meet the requirements for managing unauthorized UAVs in the low-altitude economy, a multi-modal UAV trajectory prediction method based on the fusion of LiDAR and millimeter-wave radar information is proposed. A deep fusion network for multi-modal UAV trajectory prediction, termed the Multi-Modal Deep Fusion Framework, is designed. The overall architecture consists of two modality-specific feature extraction networks and a bidirectional cross-attention fusion module, aiming to fully exploit the complementary information of LiDAR and radar point clouds in spatial geometric structure and dynamic reflection characteristics. In the feature extraction stage, the model employs independent but structurally identical feature encoders for LiDAR and radar. After feature extraction, the model enters the Bidirectional Cross-Attention Mechanism stage to achieve information complementarity and semantic alignment between the two modalities. To verify the effectiveness of the proposed model, the MMAUD dataset used in the CVPR 2024 UG2+ UAV Tracking and Pose-Estimation Challenge is adopted as the training and testing dataset. Experimental results show that the proposed multi-modal fusion model significantly improves trajectory prediction accuracy, achieving a 40% improvement compared to the baseline model. In addition, ablation experiments are conducted to demonstrate the effectiveness of different loss functions and post-processing strategies in improving model performance. The proposed model can effectively utilize multi-modal data and provides an efficient solution for unauthorized UAV trajectory prediction in the low-altitude economy.

AI.tldr: Multi-Modal Deep Fusion Framework for UAV trajectory prediction using LiDAR and mmWave radar with bidirectional cross-attention; evaluated on MMAUD dataset, achieving 40% improvement over baseline; includes ablations; aims to enhance unauthorized UAV tracking in low-altitude environments.
AI.motivation: Leverage complementary cues from LiDAR (geometry) and radar (dynamic reflections) to improve accuracy and robustness of UAV trajectory prediction in low-altitude settings, where single-modality methods underperform and security concerns require reliable tracking.
AI.method: Two modality-specific feature encoders for LiDAR and radar feed into a Bidirectional Cross-Attention Fusion Module within a Multi-Modal Deep Fusion Framework; independent yet structurally identical encoders; cross-attention enables semantic alignment and information exchange; training/testing on MMAUD (CVPR 2024 UG2+ UAV Challenge) with ablation studies on loss functions and post-processing.
AI.result: Significant improvement with a 40% gain over the baseline model; ablation experiments validate the contributions of specific loss functions and post-processing strategies.
AI.conclusion: The framework demonstrates effective fusion of LiDAR and radar modalities for UAV trajectory prediction in low-altitude contexts, offering an efficient solution for unauthorized UAV monitoring. Future work could explore real-time deployment, generalization to other modalities, and robustness to sensor failures.

title: Aggregation Queries over Unstructured Text: Benchmark and Agentic Method
summary: Aggregation query over free text is a long-standing yet underexplored problem. Unlike ordinary question answering, aggregate queries require exhaustive evidence collection and systems are required to "find all," not merely "find one." Existing paradigms such as Text-to-SQL and Retrieval-Augmented Generation fail to achieve this completeness. In this work, we formalize entity-level aggregation querying over text in a corpus-bounded setting with strict completeness requirement. To enable principled evaluation, we introduce AGGBench, a benchmark designed to evaluate completeness-oriented aggregation under realistic large-scale corpus. To accompany the benchmark, we propose DFA (Disambiguation--Filtering--Aggregation), a modular agentic baseline that decomposes aggregation querying into interpretable stages and exposes key failure modes related to ambiguity, filtering, and aggregation. Empirical results show that DFA consistently improves aggregation evidence coverage over strong RAG and agentic baselines. The data and code are available in \href{https://anonymous.4open.science/r/DFA-A4C1}.

AI.tldr: Formalizes complete entity-level aggregation over free text, introducing AGGBench for completeness-oriented evaluation and DFA, a modular baseline; results show DFA improves evidence coverage over strong RAG baselines.
AI.motivation: Aggregation over free text requires exhaustive evidence collection (find-all) rather than single-answer retrieval; existing paradigms (Text-to-SQL, RAG) do not guarantee completeness, necessitating principled benchmarks and baselines.
AI.method: Formalize entity-level aggregation querying in a corpus-bounded setting; introduce AGGBench as a benchmark for completeness-oriented aggregation on large-scale corpora; propose DFA (Disambiguation--Filtering--Aggregation) as a modular agentic baseline decomposing querying into Disambiguation, Filtering, and Aggregation stages, with analysis of ambiguity, filtering, and aggregation failure modes.
AI.result: Empirical results show DFA consistently improves aggregation evidence coverage over strong RAG and agentic baselines on AGGBench.
AI.conclusion: Presents a principled evaluation framework and a practical baseline for completeness-oriented aggregation in text; data and code are released to support further research.

title: Parallel Stochastic Gradient-Based Planning for World Models
summary: World models simulate environment dynamics from raw sensory inputs like video. However, using them for planning can be challenging due to the vast and unstructured search space. We propose a robust and highly parallelizable planner that leverages the differentiability of the learned world model for efficient optimization, solving long-horizon control tasks from visual input. Our method treats states as optimization variables ("virtual states") with soft dynamics constraints, enabling parallel computation and easier optimization. To facilitate exploration and avoid local optima, we introduce stochasticity into the states. To mitigate sensitive gradients through high-dimensional vision-based world models, we modify the gradient structure to descend towards valid plans while only requiring action-input gradients. Our planner, which we call GRASP (Gradient RelAxed Stochastic Planner), can be viewed as a stochastic version of a non-condensed or collocation-based optimal controller. We provide theoretical justification and experiments on video-based world models, where our resulting planner outperforms existing planning algorithms like the cross-entropy method (CEM) and vanilla gradient-based optimization (GD) on long-horizon experiments, both in success rate and time to convergence.

AI.tldr: GRASP is a differentiable, stochastic, gradient-based planner for video-based world models that treats states as virtual optimization variables with soft dynamics, enabling parallel, long-horizon planning; it adds stochasticity to promote exploration and modifies gradients to rely only on action-input signals, achieving superior success rate and faster convergence than CEM or vanilla GD.
AI.motivation: Planning in learned world models is hampered by huge, unstructured search spaces when planning from raw visual inputs; a robust, parallelizable, and differentiable planner is needed for long-horizon tasks.
AI.method: Propose GRASP: optimize over virtual states with soft dynamics constraints; introduce stochasticity in states to explore; re-structure gradients so that only action-input gradients are required; interpret as a stochastic variant of a non-condensed/collocation-based optimal controller; provide theoretical justification; apply to video-based world models.
AI.result: Empirically superior to CEM and vanilla gradient-based optimization on long-horizon video-world-model tasks, achieving higher success rates and faster convergence.
AI.conclusion: GRASP offers a robust, scalable planning framework for differentiable world models, combining stochastic exploration with gradient-based optimization to effectively plan long-horizon actions from visual input.

title: PovNet+: A Deep Learning Architecture for Socially Assistive Robots to Learn and Assist with Multiple Activities of Daily Living
summary: A significant barrier to the long-term deployment of autonomous socially assistive robots is their inability to both perceive and assist with multiple activities of daily living (ADLs). In this paper, we present the first multimodal deep learning architecture, POVNet+, for multi-activity recognition for socially assistive robots to proactively initiate assistive behaviors. Our novel architecture introduces the use of both ADL and motion embedding spaces to uniquely distinguish between a known ADL being performed, a new unseen ADL, or a known ADL being performed atypically in order to assist people in real scenarios. Furthermore, we apply a novel user state estimation method to the motion embedding space to recognize new ADLs while monitoring user performance. This ADL perception information is used to proactively initiate robot assistive interactions. Comparison experiments with state-of-the-art human activity recognition methods show our POVNet+ method has higher ADL classification accuracy. Human-robot interaction experiments in a cluttered living environment with multiple users and the socially assistive robot Leia using POVNet+ demonstrate the ability of our multi-modal ADL architecture in successfully identifying different seen and unseen ADLs, and ADLs being performed atypically, while initiating appropriate assistive human-robot interactions.

AI.tldr: Introduces POVNet+ for multimodal ADL recognition in socially assistive robots; distinguishes seen, unseen, and atypical ADLs using ADL and motion embeddings; includes user state estimation; enables proactive assistance; shows improved accuracy; validated in real-world-like HRI experiments with a Leia robot.
AI.motivation: Long-term deployment of autonomous socially assistive robots is hindered by their limited ability to perceive and assist with multiple activities of daily living (ADLs). A robust, proactive recognition system is needed to trigger appropriate assists in real time.
AI.method: Proposes POVNet+, a multimodal deep learning architecture that uses ADL embedding space and motion embedding space to discriminate known ADLs, new unseen ADLs, and atypical performances. Introduces a novel user state estimation method in the motion embedding space to recognize new ADLs while monitoring user performance. Evaluated against state-of-the-art HAR methods and via human-robot interaction experiments with the Leia robot in cluttered, multi-user environments.
AI.result: POVNet+ achieves higher ADL classification accuracy than state-of-the-art methods. In HRI tests, it successfully identifies seen, unseen, and atypical ADLs and initiates appropriate assistive interactions.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates the viability of a multi-modal ADL perception framework for proactive robot assistance and supports potential expansion to broader ADL sets and more complex real-world deployments.

title: Building Better Deception Probes Using Targeted Instruction Pairs
summary: Linear probes are a promising approach for monitoring AI systems for deceptive behaviour. Previous work has shown that a linear classifier trained on a contrastive instruction pair and a simple dataset can achieve good performance. However, these probes exhibit notable failures even in straightforward scenarios, including spurious correlations and false positives on non-deceptive responses. In this paper, we identify the importance of the instruction pair used during training. Furthermore, we show that targeting specific deceptive behaviors through a human-interpretable taxonomy of deception leads to improved results on evaluation datasets. Our findings reveal that instruction pairs capture deceptive intent rather than content-specific patterns, explaining why prompt choice dominates probe performance (70.6% of variance). Given the heterogeneity of deception types across datasets, we conclude that organizations should design specialized probes targeting their specific threat models rather than seeking a universal deception detector.

AI.tldr: Linear probes for deception detection in AI prompts are highly influenced by the instruction pair used during training; employing a human‑interpretable taxonomy of deception improves probe performance, suggesting specialized probes per threat model rather than a universal detector.
AI.motivation: To understand why linear probes fail or succeed in detecting deceptive behavior and to determine what the probes actually capture (intent vs content-specific patterns).
AI.method: Train a linear classifier on a contrastive instruction pair and simple dataset to detect deception; analyze the impact of different instruction pairs; develop a taxonomy of deception types; evaluate across datasets to assess variance explained by prompt choice.
AI.result: Prompt choice explains about 70.6% of the variance in probe performance; instruction pairs tend to capture deception intent rather than content, and specialized probes per deception type outperform generic probes; heterogeneity of deception types across datasets necessitates threat-model–specific probes.
AI.conclusion: Organizations should design specialized probes aligned with their specific threat model rather than pursue a universal deception detector, due to the diverse and dataset-dependent nature of deceptive behaviors.

title: Diffusion LMs Can Approximate Optimal Infilling Lengths Implicitly
summary: Diffusion language models (DLMs) provide a bidirectional generation framework naturally suited for infilling, yet their performance is constrained by the pre-specified infilling length. In this paper, we reveal that DLMs possess an inherent ability to discover the correct infilling length. We identify two key statistical phenomena in the first-step denoising confidence: a local \textit{Oracle Peak} that emerges near the ground-truth length and a systematic \textit{Length Bias} that often obscures this signal. By leveraging this signal and calibrating the bias, our training-free method \textbf{CAL} (\textbf{C}alibrated \textbf{A}daptive \textbf{L}ength) enables DLMs to approximate the optimal length through an efficient search before formal decoding. Empirical evaluations demonstrate that CAL improves Pass@1 by up to 47.7\% over fixed-length baselines and 40.5\% over chat-based adaptive methods in code infilling, while boosting BLEU-2 and ROUGE-L by up to 8.5\% and 9.9\% in text infilling. These results demonstrate that CAL paves the way for robust DLM infilling without requiring any specialized training. Code is available at https://github.com/NiuHechang/Calibrated_Adaptive_Length.

AI.tldr: A training-free method CAL enables diffusion language models to infer optimal infilling length by exploiting first-step denoising confidence, mitigating Length Bias, yielding significant improvements in code and text infilling without extra training.
AI.motivation: Overcome fixed pre-specified infilling length in diffusion language models and unlock robust, adaptive length selection to improve infilling quality during decoding.
AI.method: Identify two statistical phenomena in initial denoising: Oracle Peak near ground-truth length and Length Bias obscuring it. Calibrate this bias and perform an efficient, training-free search to approximate optimal length prior to decoding.
AI.result: CAL improves Pass@1 by up to 47.7% over fixed-length baselines and 40.5% over chat-based adaptive methods in code infilling; BLEU-2 and ROUGE-L improve by up to 8.5% and 9.9% in text infilling; demonstrates robustness and training-free practicality; code available.
AI.conclusion: CAL enables robust, training-free adaptive length estimation for DLM infilling, bridging the gap between fixed-length baselines and fully trained adaptive methods.

title: SDCM: Simulated Densifying and Compensatory Modeling Fusion for Radar-Vision 3-D Object Detection in Internet of Vehicles
summary: 3-D object detection based on 4-D radar-vision is an important part in Internet of Vehicles (IoV). However, there are two challenges which need to be faced. First, the 4-D radar point clouds are sparse, leading to poor 3-D representation. Second, vision datas exhibit representation degradation under low-light, long distance detection and dense occlusion scenes, which provides unreliable texture information during fusion stage. To address these issues, a framework named SDCM is proposed, which contains Simulated Densifying and Compensatory Modeling Fusion for radar-vision 3-D object detection in IoV. Firstly, considering point generation based on Gaussian simulation of key points obtained from 3-D Kernel Density Estimation (3-D KDE), and outline generation based on curvature simulation, Simulated Densifying (SimDen) module is designed to generate dense radar point clouds. Secondly, considering that radar data could provide more real time information than vision data, due to the all-weather property of 4-D radar. Radar Compensatory Mapping (RCM) module is designed to reduce the affects of vision datas' representation degradation. Thirdly, considering that feature tensor difference values contain the effective information of every modality, which could be extracted and modeled for heterogeneity reduction and modalities interaction, Mamba Modeling Interactive Fusion (MMIF) module is designed for reducing heterogeneous and achieving interactive Fusion. Experiment results on the VoD, TJ4DRadSet and Astyx HiRes 2019 dataset show that SDCM achieves best performance with lower parameter quantity and faster inference speed. Our code will be available.

AI.tldr: SDCM fuses sparse 4-D radar with degraded vision using SimDen, RCM, and MMIF to produce robust 3D object detection with state-of-the-art accuracy and efficiency on multiple IoV datasets.
AI.motivation: Addresses two main challenges in radar-vision fusion for 3D detection: (1) sparsity of 4-D radar point clouds leading to weak 3D representations, and (2) vision data degradation under adverse conditions (low light, long range, occlusion) reducing texture reliability during fusion.
AI.method: SimDen densifies radar using Gaussian simulation of keypoints from 3-D KDE and curvature-based outlines. RCM leverages the real-time, all-weather radar to compensate degraded vision information. MMIF models the feature tensors to reduce modality heterogeneity and enable interactive fusion.
AI.result: On VoD, TJ4DRadSet, and Astyx HiRes 2019, SDCM achieves state-of-the-art performance with fewer parameters and faster inference. The authors also indicate that code will be released, suggesting reproducibility and openness.
AI.conclusion: SDCM demonstrates a robust, efficient framework for radar-vision fusion in IoV, providing improved 3D object detection under challenging conditions. Potential limitations and future work likely include validation on more diverse scenarios and deeper ablation studies to isolate each component's contribution.

title: SimGym: Traffic-Grounded Browser Agents for Offline A/B Testing in E-Commerce
summary: A/B testing remains the gold standard for evaluating e-commerce UI changes, yet it diverts traffic, takes weeks to reach significance, and risks harming user experience. We introduce SimGym, a scalable system for rapid offline A/B testing using traffic-grounded synthetic buyers powered by Large Language Model agents operating in a live browser. SimGym extracts per-shop buyer profiles and intents from production interaction data, identifies distinct behavioral archetypes, and simulates cohort-weighted sessions across control and treatment storefronts. We validate SimGym against real human outcomes from real UI changes on a major e-commerce platform under confounder control. Even without alignment post training, SimGym agents achieve state of the art alignment with observed outcome shifts and reduces experiment cycles from weeks to under an hour , enabling rapid experimentation without exposure to real buyers.

AI.tldr: SimGym is a scalable offline A/B testing system using LLM-driven synthetic buyers in live browsers to quickly predict UI-change outcomes, reducing test cycles from weeks to under an hour.
AI.motivation: Classic A/B testing diverts traffic, takes weeks to reach significance, and risks degrading user experience; a fast, safe offline alternative is needed.
AI.method: Build per-shop buyer profiles and intents from production data, identify behavioral archetypes, and run cohort-weighted simulations with LLM agents in a live browser across control and treatment storefronts.
AI.result: Validated against real human outcomes on a major e-commerce platform with confounder control; agents align with observed outcome shifts and reduce experiment cycles to under an hour, even without post-training alignment.
AI.conclusion: SimGym enables rapid, scalable offline A/B testing without exposing real buyers, mitigating UX risk while accelerating experimentation.

title: Quality-Diversity Optimization as Multi-Objective Optimization
summary: The Quality-Diversity (QD) optimization aims to discover a collection of high-performing solutions that simultaneously exhibit diverse behaviors within a user-defined behavior space. This paradigm has stimulated significant research interest and demonstrated practical utility in domains including robot control, creative design, and adversarial sample generation. A variety of QD algorithms with distinct design principles have been proposed in recent years. Instead of proposing a new QD algorithm, this work introduces a novel reformulation by casting the QD optimization as a multi-objective optimization (MOO) problem with a huge number of optimization objectives. By establishing this connection, we enable the direct adoption of well-established MOO methods, particularly set-based scalarization techniques, to solve QD problems through a collaborative search process. We further provide a theoretical analysis demonstrating that our approach inherits theoretical guarantees from MOO while providing desirable properties for the QD optimization. Experimental studies across several QD applications confirm that our method achieves performance competitive with state-of-the-art QD algorithms.

AI.tldr: Reframes Quality-Diversity search as a many-objective optimization with a large number of objectives, enabling the use of set-based scalarization from multi-objective optimization (MOO).
AI.motivation: QD aims to discover a diverse set of high-performing solutions; current methods are varied and specialized. Reformulating QD as MOO with many objectives leverages established MOO theory and algorithms to unify and potentially improve QD performance.
AI.method: Model QD as a MOO problem with a huge (potentially infinite) set of objectives, each corresponding to a behavior-performance criterion. Apply set-based scalarization techniques to combine objectives and guide a collaborative search. Provide theoretical guarantees inherited from MOO and analyze properties favorable to QD optimization.
AI.result: Empirical studies across multiple QD tasks show performance competitive with state-of-the-art QD algorithms, validating the practical viability of the reformulation.
AI.conclusion: A novel bridge between QD and MOO that retains theoretical guarantees while enabling the use of mature MOO methods (notably set-based scalarization). This approach offers a scalable, principled alternative for achieving diverse, high-performing solutions.

title: DISK: Dynamic Inference SKipping for World Models
summary: We present DISK, a training-free adaptive inference method for autoregressive world models. DISK coordinates two coupled diffusion transformers for video and ego-trajectory via dual-branch controllers with cross-modal skip decisions, preserving motion-appearance consistency without retraining. We extend higher-order latent-difference skip testing to the autoregressive chain-of-forward regime and propagate controller statistics through rollout loops for long-horizon stability. When integrated into closed-loop driving rollouts on 1500 NuPlan and NuScenes samples using an NVIDIA L40S GPU, DISK achieves 2x speedup on trajectory diffusion and 1.6x speedup on video diffusion while maintaining L2 planning error, visual quality (FID/FVD), and NAVSIM PDMS scores, demonstrating practical long-horizon video-and-trajectory prediction at substantially reduced cost.

AI.tldr: DISK is a training-free adaptive-inference framework that speeds diffusion-based autoregressive video and ego-trajectory prediction via dual-branch diffusion transformers and cross-modal skip decisions, preserving accuracy.
AI.motivation: Autonomous driving requires long-horizon video-and-trajectory prediction but diffusion models are computationally expensive; a training-free method to accelerate inference without retraining is highly desirable.
AI.method: DISK employs two coupled diffusion transformers for video and ego-trajectory, with dual-branch controllers and cross-modal skip decisions. It extends higher-order latent-difference skip testing to the autoregressive chain-of-forward regime and propagates controller statistics through rollout loops to stabilize long-horizon predictions.
AI.result: On 1500 NuPlan and NuScenes samples using an NVIDIA L40S GPU, DISK achieves ~2x speedup for trajectory diffusion and ~1.6x speedup for video diffusion, while preserving L2 planning error, visual quality metrics (FID/FVD), and NAVSIM PDMS scores.
AI.conclusion: DISK demonstrates practical, long-horizon video-and-trajectory prediction at substantially reduced computational cost without retraining.

title: Agyn: A Multi-Agent System for Team-Based Autonomous Software Engineering
summary: Large language models have demonstrated strong capabilities in individual software engineering tasks, yet most autonomous systems still treat issue resolution as a monolithic or pipeline-based process. In contrast, real-world software development is organized as a collaborative activity carried out by teams following shared methodologies, with clear role separation, communication, and review. In this work, we present a fully automated multi-agent system that explicitly models software engineering as an organizational process, replicating the structure of an engineering team. Built on top of agyn, an open-source platform for configuring agent teams, our system assigns specialized agents to roles such as coordination, research, implementation, and review, provides them with isolated sandboxes for experimentation, and enables structured communication. The system follows a defined development methodology for working on issues, including analysis, task specification, pull request creation, and iterative review, and operates without any human intervention. Importantly, the system was designed for real production use and was not tuned for SWE-bench. When evaluated post hoc on SWE-bench 500, it resolves 72.4% of tasks, outperforming single-agent baselines using comparable language models. Our results suggest that replicating team structure, methodology, and communication is a powerful paradigm for autonomous software engineering, and that future progress may depend as much on organizational design and agent infrastructure as on model improvements.

AI.tldr: A fully automated multi-agent system replicates software engineering team structure to tackle issue resolution, achieving 72.4% task resolution on SWE-bench 500 and outperforming single-agent baselines, underscoring organizational design and agent infrastructure as key drivers.
AI.motivation: To address limitations of monolithic or pipeline-based autonomous software engineering by incorporating organizational context, roles, and collaboration patterns to improve task resolution and reliability in production-like settings.
