AI.method: Build on the agyn framework to configure agent teams with specialized roles (coordination, research, implementation, review), provide isolated sandboxes, enable structured communication, and follow a defined development workflow (analysis, task specification, PR creation, iterative review) culminating in end-to-end issue resolution without human intervention; evaluation on SWE-bench 500 (post hoc).
AI.result: Achieves 72.4% resolution of tasks on SWE-bench 500, outperforming single-agent baselines using comparable LMs; designed for production use rather than SWE-bench tuning.
AI.conclusion: Replicating team structure, methodology, and communication is a powerful paradigm for autonomous software engineering; progress may hinge as much on organizational design and agent infrastructure as on advances in language models.

title: AREAL-DTA: Dynamic Tree Attention for Efficient Reinforcement Learning of Large Language Models
summary: Reinforcement learning (RL) based post-training for large language models (LLMs) is computationally expensive, as it generates many rollout sequences that could frequently share long token prefixes. Existing RL frameworks usually process these sequences independently, repeatedly recomputing identical prefixes during forward and backward passes during policy model training, leading to substantial inefficiencies in computation and memory usage. Although prefix sharing naturally induces a tree structure over rollouts, prior tree-attention-based solutions rely on fully materialized attention masks and scale poorly in RL settings. In this paper, we introduce AREAL-DTA to efficiently exploit prefix sharing in RL training. AREAL-DTA employs a depth-first-search (DFS)-based execution strategy that dynamically traverses the rollout prefix tree during both forward and backward computation, materializing only a single root-to-leaf path at a time. To further improve scalability, AREAL-DTA incorporates a load-balanced distributed batching mechanism that dynamically constructs and processes prefix trees across multiple GPUs. Across the popular RL post-training workload, AREAL-DTA achieves up to $8.31\times$ in $τ^2$-bench higher training throughput.

AI.tldr: Proposes AREAL-DTA, a DFS-based, prefix-tree-aware execution for RL post-training of LLMs that avoids recomputing shared prefixes and scales with distributed batching; achieves up to 8.31x throughput on τ^2-bench.
AI.motivation: RL-based post-training of LLMs suffers from massive recomputation and memory usage due to many rollout sequences sharing long prefixes. Existing methods either process sequences independently or rely on full attention masks, limiting efficiency in RL contexts.
AI.method: Introduce AREAL-DTA which uses a DFS-based execution strategy to traverse the rollout prefix tree during both forward and backward passes, materializing a single root-to-leaf path at a time; implement load-balanced distributed batching to construct/process trees across multiple GPUs.
AI.result: Significant throughput gains on RL post-training workloads, achieving up to 8.31× higher training throughput on τ^2-bench compared to baselines.
AI.conclusion: AREAL-DTA effectively exploits shared prefixes inherent in rollout trees, reducing redundant computation and memory usage while scaling with distributed GPU resources, enabling more efficient RL post-training for LLMs.

title: Legal Infrastructure for Transformative AI Governance
summary: Most of our AI governance efforts focus on substance: what rules do we want in place? What limits or checks do we want to impose on AI development and deployment? But a key role for law is not only to establish substantive rules but also to establish legal and regulatory infrastructure to generate and implement rules. The transformative nature of AI calls especially for attention to building legal and regulatory frameworks. In this PNAS Perspective piece I review three examples I have proposed: the creation of registration regimes for frontier models; the creation of registration and identification regimes for autonomous agents; and the design of regulatory markets to facilitate a role for private companies to innovate and deliver AI regulatory services.

AI.tldr: A PNAS Perspective argues that AI governance should build legal/regulatory infrastructure in addition to substantive rules, proposing three concrete designs: registration regimes for frontier models, registration/identification for autonomous agents, and regulatory markets to mobilize private firms in delivering AI regulatory services.
AI.motivation: AI’s transformative impact requires governance mechanisms that enable rulemaking, compliance, and enforcement through scalable, interoperable legal frameworks rather than relying solely on substantive bans or limitations.
AI.method: Conceptual analysis and synthesis of three regulatory design proposals; normative argument drawing on governance and regulatory theory; no empirical evaluation within this abstract.
AI.result: The piece outlines three concrete regulatory design proposals and discusses their potential to enhance oversight, accountability, and innovation in AI governance; these proposals are conceptual and not empirically tested.
AI.conclusion: Regulatory infrastructure is essential to accompany substantive rules; the author calls for exploration, experimentation, and refinement of these designs to operationalize effective AI governance.

title: OD-DEAL: Dynamic Expert-Guided Adversarial Learning with Online Decomposition for Scalable Capacitated Vehicle Routing
summary: Solving large-scale capacitated vehicle routing problems (CVRP) is hindered by the high complexity of heuristics and the limited generalization of neural solvers on massive graphs. We propose OD-DEAL, an adversarial learning framework that tightly integrates hybrid genetic search (HGS) and online barycenter clustering (BCC) decomposition, and leverages high-fidelity knowledge distillation to transfer expert heuristic behavior. OD-DEAL trains a graph attention network (GAT)-based generative policy through a minimax game, in which divide-and-conquer strategies from a hybrid expert are distilled into dense surrogate rewards. This enables high-quality, clustering-free inference on large-scale instances. Empirical results demonstrate that OD-DEAL achieves state-of-the-art (SOTA) real-time CVRP performance, solving 10000-node instances with near-constant neural scaling. This uniquely enables the sub-second, heuristic-quality inference required for dynamic large-scale deployment.

AI.tldr: OD-DEAL presents an adversarial learning framework merging hybrid genetic search and online barycenter clustering with knowledge distillation to train a GAT-based policy for large-scale CVRP, achieving near-constant neural scaling and sub-second inference on 10000-node instances, i.e., state-of-the-art real-time performance.
AI.motivation: Address the challenge of solving very large CVRPs where standard heuristics are slow and neural solvers poorly generalize to massive graphs; enable real-time inference for dynamic, large-scale deployments.
AI.method: Integrate a hybrid expert's divide-and-conquer strategies into a minimax-trained GAT-based generative policy. Use high-fidelity knowledge distillation to transfer expert heuristic behavior into dense surrogate rewards, enabling clustering-free, online inference with HGS/BCC components.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art real-time CVRP performance on large-scale instances, solving 10,000-node problems with near-constant neural scaling and sub-second inference suitable for dynamic deployment.
AI.conclusion: The framework effectively combines hybrid heuristics with neural policies via adversarial distillation to enable scalable, fast CVRP solving; potential for generalization to other VRP variants and real-time routing scenarios, with questions remaining about robustness and training stability.

title: Learning to Guide Local Search for MPE Inference in Probabilistic Graphical Models
summary: Most Probable Explanation (MPE) inference in Probabilistic Graphical Models (PGMs) is a fundamental yet computationally challenging problem arising in domains such as diagnosis, planning, and structured prediction. In many practical settings, the graphical model remains fixed while inference must be performed repeatedly for varying evidence patterns. Stochastic Local Search (SLS) algorithms scale to large models but rely on myopic best-improvement rule that prioritizes immediate likelihood gains and often stagnate in poor local optima. Heuristics such as Guided Local Search (GLS+) partially alleviate this limitation by modifying the search landscape, but their guidance cannot be reused effectively across multiple inference queries on the same model. We propose a neural amortization framework for improving local search in this repeated-query regime. Exploiting the fixed graph structure, we train an attention-based network to score local moves by predicting their ability to reduce Hamming distance to a near-optimal solution. Our approach integrates seamlessly with existing local search procedures, using this signal to balance short-term likelihood gains with long-term promise during neighbor selection. We provide theoretical intuition linking distance-reducing move selection to improved convergence behavior, and empirically demonstrate consistent improvements over SLS and GLS+ on challenging high-treewidth benchmarks in the amortized inference setting.

AI.tldr: A neural amortization framework guides local search for MPE inference in fixed PGMs by predicting distance-reducing moves with an attention network, enabling effective reuse across multiple queries and improving convergence on high-treewidth graphs.
AI.motivation: MPE inference in fixed graphs with repeated query patterns is computationally expensive. SLS can scale but tends to get stuck; GLS+ helps but its guidance isn’t reusable across queries. An amortized approach can reuse the fixed structure across queries to improve performance.
AI.method: Train an attention-based network to score local moves by predicting their ability to reduce Hamming distance to a near-optimal solution. Integrate this signal into existing local search to balance short-term likelihood gains with long-term potential. Leverage the fixed graph structure to enable cross-query amortization. Provide theoretical intuition linking distance-reducing moves to faster convergence.
AI.result: Empirically, the method yields consistent improvements over standard SLS and GLS+ on challenging high-treewidth benchmarks in the amortized inference setting.
AI.conclusion: A neural amortization framework effectively guides local search for MPE inference in repeated-query scenarios on fixed PGMs, improving convergence and performance on high-treewidth models by reusing the graph structure across queries.

title: Partition of Unity Neural Networks for Interpretable Classification with Explicit Class Regions
summary:
Despite their empirical success, neural network classifiers remain difficult to interpret. In softmax-based models, class regions are defined implicitly as solutions to systems of inequalities among logits, making them difficult to extract and visualize. We introduce Partition of Unity Neural Networks (PUNN), an architecture in which class probabilities arise directly from a learned partition of unity, without requiring a softmax layer.
  PUNN constructs $k$ nonnegative functions $h_1, \ldots, h_k$ satisfying $\sum_i h_i(x) = 1$, where each $h_i(x)$ directly represents $P(\text{class } i \mid x)$. Unlike softmax, where class regions are defined implicitly through coupled inequalities among logits, each PUNN partition function $h_i$ directly defines the probability of class $i$ as a standalone function of $x$.
  We prove that PUNN is dense in the space of continuous probability maps on compact domains. The gate functions $g_i$ that define the partition can use various activation functions (sigmoid, Gaussian, bump) and parameterizations ranging from flexible MLPs to parameter-efficient shape-informed designs (spherical shells, ellipsoids, spherical harmonics).
  Experiments on synthetic data, UCI benchmarks, and MNIST show that PUNN with MLP-based gates achieves accuracy within 0.3--0.6\% of standard multilayer perceptrons. When geometric priors match the data structure, shape-informed gates achieve comparable accuracy with up to 300$\times$ fewer parameters. These results demonstrate that interpretable-by-design architectures can be competitive with black-box models while providing transparent class probability assignments.

AI.tldr: PUNN replaces softmax with a partition-of-unity representation, making class probabilities directly interpretable via nonnegative functions h_i(x) that sum to 1; density in probability maps; competitive accuracy with fewer parameters when using shape priors.
AI.motivation: Interpretable class regions in neural classifiers are hard to extract with softmax, since regions are defined indirectly by inequalities among logits. PUNN aims to achieve interpretable, transparent class probabilities by constructing a partition of unity over the input space.
AI.method: Construct k nonnegative gating functions h_i(x) with sum_i h_i(x) = 1, where each h_i(x) directly equals P(class i | x). Gate functions g_i define h_i using activations (sigmoid, Gaussian, bump) and flexible or shape-informed parameterizations (e.g., MLPs, spherical shells, ellipsoids, spherical harmonics). Prove density in space of continuous probability maps on compact domains. Train on synthetic data, UCI, MNIST.
AI.result: PUNN with MLP-based gates achieves accuracy within 0.3–0.6 percentage points of standard MLPs. When geometric priors align with data, shape-informed gates reach comparable accuracy with up to 300× fewer parameters.
AI.conclusion: PUNN demonstrates that an interpretable-by-design architecture can be competitive with black-box models while yielding transparent class probability assignments, and can leverage geometric priors to drastically reduce parameter counts without sacrificing performance.

title: Any3D-VLA: Enhancing VLA Robustness via Diverse Point Clouds
summary: Existing Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models typically take 2D images as visual input, which limits their spatial understanding in complex scenes. How can we incorporate 3D information to enhance VLA capabilities? We conduct a pilot study across different observation spaces and visual representations. The results show that explicitly lifting visual input into point clouds yields representations that better complement their corresponding 2D representations. To address the challenges of (1) scarce 3D data and (2) the domain gap induced by cross-environment differences and depth-scale biases, we propose Any3D-VLA. It unifies the simulator, sensor, and model-estimated point clouds within a training pipeline, constructs diverse inputs, and learns domain-agnostic 3D representations that are fused with the corresponding 2D representations. Simulation and real-world experiments demonstrate Any3D-VLA's advantages in improving performance and mitigating the domain gap. Our project homepage is available at https://xianzhefan.github.io/Any3D-VLA.github.io.

AI.tldr: Proposes Any3D-VLA to integrate 3D point cloud inputs with 2D VLA, addressing data scarcity and domain gaps by unifying simulator, sensor, and model-estimated point clouds in training to learn domain-agnostic 3D representations that fuse with 2D representations, improving performance in both simulation and real-world.
AI.motivation: 3D information is underutilized in Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models; reliance on 2D inputs limits spatial understanding in complex scenes; scarcity of 3D data and cross-environment/domain shifts (depth-scale biases) hinder generalization.
AI.method: Unifies simulator, sensor, and model-estimated point clouds within a training pipeline; constructs diverse inputs; learns domain-agnostic 3D representations; fuses these with corresponding 2D representations for VLA tasks; includes a pilot study across observation spaces with 3D lifting to point clouds.
AI.result: Simulation and real-world experiments show that explicitly lifting visual input to point clouds provides representations that better complement 2D features, improving performance and mitigating the domain gap; Any3D-VLA demonstrates advantages across simulation and real-world settings.
AI.conclusion: Integrating 3D information via unified, cross-source point-cloud representations enhances VLA models by addressing data scarcity and domain shift, enabling more robust 3D-augmented perception in both synthetic and real environments.

title: Qrita: High-performance Top-k and Top-p Algorithm for GPUs using Pivot-based Truncation and Selection
summary: Top-k and Top-p are the dominant truncation operators in the sampling of large language models. Despite their widespread use, implementing them efficiently over large vocabularies remains a significant challenge. Existing approaches often rely on sorting, which incur significant computation and memory overhead on GPUs, or stochastic approaches, which alter the algorithm output. In this work, we propose Qrita, an efficient Top-k and Top-p algorithm based on a pivot-based selection strategy. Based on RTop-k, which uses a pivot-based search for node selection in graph neural networks, Qrita extends the concept of pivot-based search to both Top-k and Top-p with two key techniques: 1. Gaussian-based sigma-truncation, which greatly reduces the search space of the target elements, and 2. Quaternary pivot search with duplication handling, which halves the pivot search iteration and guarantees deterministic output. We provide the full implementation of Qrita using Triton, a popular GPU programming language. Our evaluation of Qrita against the Top-k and Top-p kernels of high performance LLM execution engines such as vLLM, SGLang, and Flashinfer show that Qrita achieves up to 2 times throughput and half memory use while providing the same output to the the sorting-based algorithms.

AI.tldr: Qrita is an efficient Top-k and Top-p algorithm for large vocabularies that uses a pivot-based selection strategy with Gaussian sigma truncation and a quaternary pivot search to achieve deterministic output. Implemented in Triton, it doubles throughput and halves memory usage compared to sorting-based kernels while preserving outputs.
AI.motivation: Efficiently performing Top-k/Top-p sampling for large vocabularies in LLMs is computationally expensive with sorting-based methods; stochastic alternatives may change outputs. A fast, deterministic, GPU-friendly method is needed.
AI.method: Extends RTop-k's pivot-based search to Top-k and Top-p with two innovations: (1) Gaussian-based sigma truncation to shrink the search space, and (2) a quaternary pivot search with duplication handling to reduce iterations and ensure determinism. Implemented in Triton; evaluated against high-performance kernels from vLLM, SGLang, and Flashinfer.
AI.result: Achieves up to 2x throughput and about 50% memory usage compared with sorting-based Top-k/Top-p kernels, while producing identical outputs to those methods.
AI.conclusion: Qrita provides a deterministic, GPU-efficient solution for Top-k and Top-p, offering substantial speed and memory benefits without sacrificing correctness, suitable for real-time LLM inference.

title: Minerva: Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards for Cyber Threat Intelligence LLMs
summary: Cyber threat intelligence (CTI) analysts routinely convert noisy, unstructured security artifacts into standardized, automation-ready representations. Although large language models (LLMs) show promise for this task, existing approaches remain brittle when producing structured CTI outputs and have largely relied on supervised fine-tuning (SFT). In contrast, CTI standards and community-maintained resources define canonical identifiers and schemas that enable deterministic verification of model outputs. We leverage this structure to study reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) for CTI tasks. We introduce \textit{Minerva}, a unified dataset and training pipeline spanning multiple CTI subtasks, each paired with task-specific verifiers that score structured outputs and identifier predictions. To address reward sparsity during rollout, we propose a lightweight self-training mechanism that generates additional verified trajectories and distills them back into the model. Experiments across LLM backbones show consistent improvements in accuracy and robustness over SFT across multiple benchmarks.

AI.tldr: Introduces Minerva and RLVR for CTI: a unified dataset/pipeline with verifiable rewards and targeted verifiers, plus a lightweight self-training loop to counter reward sparsity; yields improved accuracy/robustness over SFT across CTI benchmarks.
AI.motivation: CTI tasks require structured, verifiable outputs; current LLM approaches underperform due to brittleness and reliance on supervised fine-tuning. Canonical identifiers/schemas in CTI standards enable deterministic verification, motivating a learning framework that leverages verifiable signals.
AI.method: Proposes reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) using task-specific verifiers to score structured outputs and identifier predictions. Introduces Minerva, a unified CTI dataset/pipeline covering multiple subtasks. Includes a lightweight self-training mechanism that generates additional verified trajectories and distills them back into the model to mitigate reward sparsity.
AI.result: Across multiple CTI backbones and benchmarks, RLVR with verifiable rewards yields consistent improvements in accuracy and robustness compared to supervised fine-tuning.
AI.conclusion: RLVR, aided by verifiable rewards and a self-training loop, improves CTI task performance and stability; Minerva provides a cohesive dataset/pipeline for multi-task CTI research.

title: VVLoc: Prior-free 3-DoF Vehicle Visual Localization
summary: Localization is a critical technology in autonomous driving, encompassing both topological localization, which identifies the most similar map keyframe to the current observation, and metric localization, which provides precise spatial coordinates. Conventional methods typically address these tasks independently, rely on single-camera setups, and often require additional 3D semantic or pose priors, while lacking mechanisms to quantify the confidence of localization results, making them less feasible for real industrial applications. In this paper, we propose VVLoc, a unified pipeline that employs a single neural network to concurrently achieve topological and metric vehicle localization using multi-camera system. VVLoc first evaluates the geo-proximity between visual observations, then estimates their relative metric poses using a matching strategy, while also providing a confidence measure. Additionally, the training process for VVLoc is highly efficient, requiring only pairs of visual data and corresponding ground-truth poses, eliminating the need for complex supplementary data. We evaluate VVLoc not only on the publicly available datasets, but also on a more challenging self-collected dataset, demonstrating its ability to deliver state-of-the-art localization accuracy across a wide range of localization tasks.

AI.tldr: VVLoc is a unified, multi-camera localization framework that jointly performs topological and metric localization with a single network, provides a localization confidence score, and trains efficiently from only paired images and ground-truth poses, achieving state-of-the-art accuracy on public and self-collected datasets.
AI.motivation: Traditional localization approaches treat topological (image-to-map matching) and metric (pose estimation) localization separately, rely on single-camera setups or extra priors, and lack a quantified confidence measure, hindering industrial deployment. A unified, data-efficient, confidence-enabled solution for multi-camera systems is highly desirable.
AI.method: A single neural network-based pipeline (VVLoc) that first assesses geo-proximity between observations, then estimates relative metric poses via a matching strategy; outputs a confidence score; training requires only pairs of visual data and corresponding ground-truth poses, without additional 3D semantics or pose priors; designed for multi-camera input and unified localization.
AI.result: Empirically achieves state-of-the-art localization accuracy across diverse tasks on public benchmarks and a challenging self-collected dataset; demonstrates robustness and efficiency.
AI.conclusion: Offers a compact, confident, and data-efficient localization framework suitable for industrial deployment, integrating topological and metric localization in a unified multi-camera system.

title: Contrastive Learning for Privacy Enhancements in Industrial Internet of Things
summary: The Industrial Internet of Things (IIoT) integrates intelligent sensing, communication, and analytics into industrial environments, including manufacturing, energy, and critical infrastructure. While IIoT enables predictive maintenance and cross-site optimization of modern industrial control systems, such as those in manufacturing and energy, it also introduces significant privacy and confidentiality risks due to the sensitivity of operational data. Contrastive learning, a self-supervised representation learning paradigm, has recently emerged as a promising approach for privacy-preserving analytics by reducing reliance on labeled data and raw data sharing. Although contrastive learning-based privacy-preserving techniques have been explored in the Internet of Things (IoT) domain, this paper offers a comprehensive review of these techniques specifically for privacy preservation in Industrial Internet of Things (IIoT) systems. It emphasizes the unique characteristics of industrial data, system architectures, and various application scenarios. Additionally, the paper discusses solutions and open challenges and outlines future research directions.

AI.tldr: A survey of contrastive-learning-based privacy-preserving techniques in IIoT, outlining industrial data characteristics, architectures, applications, and future research directions.
AI.motivation: Industrial systems generate sensitive operational data; privacy/confidentiality risks are high. Contrastive learning offers self-supervised, label-efficient, privacy-preserving analytics suitable for IIoT environments.
AI.method: Comprehensive literature review focusing on contrastive learning approaches for privacy preservation in IIoT across data types, system architectures, and application scenarios; taxonomy of solutions; discussion of open challenges.
AI.result: A consolidated taxonomy and critique of existing techniques, identification of industrial-specific challenges (data heterogeneity, real-time constraints, resource limits), and a set of open problems and directions for future work.
AI.conclusion: The paper provides a roadmap for advancing privacy-preserving analytics in IIoT via contrastive learning, highlighting gaps and potential avenues for impactful research.

title: Navigating Simply, Aligning Deeply: Winning Solutions for Mouse vs. AI 2025
comment: 15 pages, 8 tables. Technical Report for winning solutions (Track 1 & Track 2) at the NeurIPS 2025 Mouse vs. AI Challenge
summary: Visual robustness and neural alignment remain critical challenges in developing artificial agents that can match biological vision systems. We present the winning approaches from Team HCMUS_TheFangs for both tracks of the NeurIPS 2025 Mouse vs. AI: Robust Visual Foraging Competition. For Track 1 (Visual Robustness), we demonstrate that architectural simplicity combined with targeted components yields superior generalization, achieving 95.4% final score with a lightweight two-layer CNN enhanced by Gated Linear Units and observation normalization. For Track 2 (Neural Alignment), we develop a deep ResNet-like architecture with 16 convolutional layers and GLU-based gating that achieves top-1 neural prediction performance with 17.8 million parameters. Our systematic analysis of ten model checkpoints trained between 60K to 1.14M steps reveals that training duration exhibits a non-monotonic relationship with performance, with optimal results achieved around 200K steps. Through comprehensive ablation studies and failure case analysis, we provide insights into why simpler architectures excel at visual robustness while deeper models with increased capacity achieve better neural alignment. Our results challenge conventional assumptions about model complexity in visuomotor learning and offer practical guidance for developing robust, biologically-inspired visual agents.

AI.tldr: Two-track NeurIPS 2025 submission shows simple CNN-based robustness with GLU and normalization achieving 95.4% final score; deeper GLU-based ResNet-16 achieves best neural alignment with 17.8M parameters; training duration has a non-monotonic effect, optimal around 200k steps; findings suggest simplicity aids robustness while larger capacity aids neural alignment, challenging complexity-for-robustness assumptions.
AI.motivation: Investigate how model architecture and training dynamics influence visual robustness and neural alignment in visuomotor tasks, testing competing hypotheses about the role of architectural complexity.
AI.method: Track 1: lightweight two-layer CNN with Gated Linear Units (GLU) and observation normalization for robustness (final score 95.4%). Track 2: deep ResNet-like network with 16 conv layers and GLU-based gating for neural alignment (top-1 neural prediction with 17.8M parameters). Analyzed ten checkpoints from 60k to 1.14M steps; performed ablations and failure-case analyses.
AI.result: Track 1 achieved 95.4% final score with a simple CNN; Track 2 achieved top-1 neural prediction with 17.8M parameters. Training duration has a non-monotonic relationship with performance, with optimal results around 200k steps.
AI.conclusion: Simpler architectures excel for visual robustness, while deeper models with greater capacity yield better neural alignment. The findings challenge conventional views that greater complexity universally improves performance in visuomotor learning and offer guidance for building robust, biologically-inspired visual agents.

title: MAGIC: A Co-Evolving Attacker-Defender Adversarial Game for Robust LLM Safety
summary: Ensuring robust safety alignment is crucial for Large Language Models (LLMs), yet existing defenses often lag behind evolving adversarial attacks due to their \textbf{reliance on static, pre-collected data distributions}. In this paper, we introduce \textbf{MAGIC}, a novel multi-turn multi-agent reinforcement learning framework that formulates LLM safety alignment as an adversarial asymmetric game. Specifically, an attacker agent learns to iteratively rewrite original queries into deceptive prompts, while a defender agent simultaneously optimizes its policy to recognize and refuse such inputs. This dynamic process triggers a \textbf{co-evolution}, where the attacker's ever-changing strategies continuously uncover long-tail vulnerabilities, driving the defender to generalize to unseen attack patterns. Remarkably, we observe that the attacker, endowed with initial reasoning ability, evolves \textbf{novel, previously unseen combinatorial strategies} through iterative RL training, underscoring our method's substantial potential. Theoretically, we provide insights into a more robust game equilibrium and derive safety guarantees. Extensive experiments validate our framework's effectiveness, demonstrating superior defense success rates without compromising the helpfulness of the model. Our code is available at https://github.com/BattleWen/MAGIC.

AI.tldr: MAGIC introduces a co-evolving, multi-turn, multi-agent RL framework for LLM safety, where an attacker rewrites prompts and a defender learns to refuse them, driving robustness and uncovering unseen attack patterns.
AI.motivation: Current safety defenses rely on static, pre-collected data distributions and often fail to cover long-tail or evolving adversarial prompts; a dynamic, adversarial training setup can improve generalization and robustness.
AI.method: A two-agent adversarial setup: an attacker iteratively rewrites queries into deceptive prompts, while a defender learns a policy to detect/refuse such inputs; both agents co-evolve through iterative RL, enabling long-tail vulnerability discovery and policy generalization; theoretical analysis of game equilibrium and safety guarantees; experiments validate defense performance without harming helpfulness; code released.
AI.result: Demonstrates superior defense success rates and generalization to unseen attacks; attacker evolves novel combinatorial strategies; safety guarantees and insights into more robust equilibria.
AI.conclusion: Dynamic adversarial co-evolution via MAGIC is a promising direction for LLM safety alignment, offering improved robustness and theoretical grounding; practical implications include broader defense coverage and reproducibility via released code.

title: PolicyFlow: Policy Optimization with Continuous Normalizing Flow in Reinforcement Learning
summary: Among on-policy reinforcement learning algorithms, Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) demonstrates is widely favored for its simplicity, numerical stability, and strong empirical performance. Standard PPO relies on surrogate objectives defined via importance ratios, which require evaluating policy likelihood that is typically straightforward when the policy is modeled as a Gaussian distribution. However, extending PPO to more expressive, high-capacity policy models such as continuous normalizing flows (CNFs), also known as flow-matching models, is challenging because likelihood evaluation along the full flow trajectory is computationally expensive and often numerically unstable. To resolve this issue, we propose PolicyFlow, a novel on-policy CNF-based reinforcement learning algorithm that integrates expressive CNF policies with PPO-style objectives without requiring likelihood evaluation along the full flow path. PolicyFlow approximates importance ratios using velocity field variations along a simple interpolation path, reducing computational overhead without compromising training stability. To further prevent mode collapse and further encourage diverse behaviors, we propose the Brownian Regularizer, an implicit policy entropy regularizer inspired by Brownian motion, which is conceptually elegant and computationally lightweight. Experiments on diverse tasks across various environments including MultiGoal, PointMaze, IsaacLab and MuJoCo Playground show that PolicyFlow achieves competitive or superior performance compared to PPO using Gaussian policies and flow-based baselines including FPO and DPPO. Notably, results on MultiGoal highlight PolicyFlow's ability to capture richer multimodal action distributions.

AI.tldr: PPO with expressive CNF policies trained on-policy by approximating importance ratios via velocity-field interpolation and a Brownian-regulated entropy term, achieving competitive-to-superior results across diverse tasks and capturing multimodal actions.
AI.motivation: Extending PPO to high-capacity, expressive policies like continuous normalizing flows is hindered by costly and unstable likelihood evaluations over full flow trajectories; a computationally efficient, stable approach for CNFs in on-policy RL is needed.
AI.method: Introduce PolicyFlow: an on-policy CNF-based RL method that uses PPO-style objectives. Approximate importance ratios with velocity-field variations along a simple interpolation path between old and new policies. Add Brownian Regularizer, a lightweight implicit entropy regularizer inspired by Brownian motion, to promote diversity and mitigate mode collapse. Compare against PPO with Gaussian policies and flow-based baselines (FPO, DPPO) across multiple environments.
AI.result: PolicyFlow achieves competitive or superior performance versus PPO (Gaussian) and flow-based baselines on tasks such as MultiGoal, PointMaze, IsaacLab, and MuJoCo Playground. Notably, MultiGoal results indicate richer multimodal action distributions captured by PolicyFlow.
AI.conclusion: CNF-based on-policy RL with efficient ratio approximation and Brownian Regularizer is viable and effective, enabling richer policy expressivity without sacrificing stability and demonstrating potential for multimodal behaviors in complex tasks.

title: S1-NexusAgent: a Self-Evolving Agent Framework for Multidisciplinary Scientific Research
summary: Modern scientific research relies on large-scale data, complex workflows, and specialized tools, which existing LLMs and tool-based agents struggle to handle due to limitations in long-horizon planning, robust goal maintenance, and continual learning from execution. To address these issues, in this work, we propose S1-NexusAgent, a self-evolving agent framework designed for multidisciplinary scientific research. S1-NexusAgent adopts a hierarchical Plan-and-CodeAct execution paradigm, decoupling global scientific planning from subtask-level tool execution through a dual-loop architecture, thereby enabling stable modeling of complex research workflows. The system natively supports the Model Context Protocol (MCP), integrates up to thousands of cross-disciplinary scientific tools, and achieves efficient orchestration of heterogeneous research tools via intention-aware dynamic tool retrieval and hot-plug mechanisms. To address long-context and large-scale data challenges in scientific settings, S1-NexusAgent introduces object-reference-based sparse context management, which enables sub-task context isolation and intermediate result compression. Building on this, a Critic Agent automatically evaluates complete execution trajectories and distills high-quality research paths into reusable Scientific Skills, forming a closed loop for continuous self-evolution, which is valuable for sustainable and long-horizon scientific research. Experiments on authoritative scientific benchmarks involving long-horizon planning and complex specialized tool orchestration, including biomini-eval (biology), ChemBench (chemistry), and MatSciBench (material science), demonstrate that S1-NexusAgent achieves state-of-the-art performance, validating its effectiveness and generalization capability in complex scientific tasks.

AI.tldr: A self-evolving, hierarchical agent for multidisciplinary science that decouples high-level planning from subtask tool execution, enabling scalable tool use, long-horizon reasoning, and continual learning through a Critic Agent that extracts reusable Scientific Skills.
AI.motivation: LLMs and tool agents struggle with long-horizon scientific workflows, goal maintenance across diverse domains, and continual learning from execution; a unified, self-improving framework is needed for scalable multidisciplinary research.
AI.method: Introduce S1-NexusAgent with a hierarchical Plan-and-CodeAct paradigm and dual-loop architecture; Model Context Protocol; dynamic, intention-aware tool retrieval and hot-plug; object-reference-based sparse context management for task isolation and compression; a Critic Agent that evaluates trajectories and distills reusable Scientific Skills; experiments on biomini-eval, ChemBench, MatSciBench.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on long-horizon planning and complex tool orchestration benchmarks, demonstrating strong generalization across biology, chemistry, and materials science.
AI.conclusion: S1-NexusAgent provides stable, scalable planning and continual self-evolution for sustained, cross-disciplinary scientific research by integrating thousands of tools and learning from execution.

title: Physiology as Language: Translating Respiration to Sleep EEG
summary: This paper introduces a novel cross-physiology translation task: synthesizing sleep electroencephalography (EEG) from respiration signals. To address the significant complexity gap between the two modalities, we propose a waveform-conditional generative framework that preserves fine-grained respiratory dynamics while constraining the EEG target space through discrete tokenization. Trained on over 28,000 individuals, our model achieves a 7% Mean Absolute Error in EEG spectrogram reconstruction. Beyond reconstruction, the synthesized EEG supports downstream tasks with performance comparable to ground truth EEG on age estimation (MAE 5.0 vs. 5.1 years), sex detection (AUROC 0.81 vs. 0.82), and sleep staging (Accuracy 0.84 vs. 0.88), significantly outperforming baselines trained directly on breathing. Finally, we demonstrate that the framework generalizes to contactless sensing by synthesizing EEG from wireless radio-frequency reflections, highlighting the feasibility of remote, non-contact neurological assessment during sleep.

AI.tldr: Cross-physiology translation: synthesize sleep EEG from respiration using a waveform-conditioned generation with discrete tokenization; strong reconstruction and competitive downstream task performance; demonstrates non-contact EEG synthesis from RF signals.
AI.motivation: Bridge heterogeneous modalities ( respiration to EEG ) and enable scalable, non-invasive sleep assessment by constraining the EEG output space and preserving respiratory dynamics.
AI.method: A waveform-conditioned generative framework that uses discrete tokenization to constrain EEG target space; trained on >28,000 individuals; evaluates both reconstruction (EEG spectrogram MAE) and downstream tasks (age, sex, sleep staging); extends to contactless sensing via RF reflections.
AI.result: EEG spectrogram reconstruction achieves ~7% MAE; downstream tasks on synthesized EEG approach ground-truth performance (age MAE 5.0 vs 5.1; sex AUROC 0.81 vs 0.82; sleep staging accuracy 0.84 vs 0.88); baselines trained on breathing underperform; framework generalizes to RF-based EEG synthesis for non-contact sleep monitoring.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates feasible cross-physiology translation and remote sleep assessment potential; large-scale data supports generalization; invites further validation and robustness studies for clinical deployment.

title: Improving Robustness of Vision-Language-Action Models by Restoring Corrupted Visual Inputs
summary: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have emerged as a dominant paradigm for generalist robotic manipulation, unifying perception and control within a single end-to-end architecture. However, despite their success in controlled environments, reliable real-world deployment is severely hindered by their fragility to visual disturbances. While existing literature extensively addresses physical occlusions caused by scene geometry, a critical mode remains largely unexplored: image corruptions. These sensor-level artifacts, ranging from electronic noise and dead pixels to lens contaminants, directly compromise the integrity of the visual signal prior to interpretation. In this work, we quantify this vulnerability, demonstrating that state-of-the-art VLAs such as $π_{0.5}$ and SmolVLA, suffer catastrophic performance degradation, dropping from 90\% success rates to as low as 2\%, under common signal artifacts. To mitigate this, we introduce the Corruption Restoration Transformer (CRT), a plug-and-play and model-agnostic vision transformer designed to immunize VLA models against sensor disturbances. Leveraging an adversarial training objective, CRT restores clean observations from corrupted inputs without requiring computationally expensive fine-tuning of the underlying model. Extensive experiments across the LIBERO and Meta-World benchmarks demonstrate that CRT effectively recovers lost performance, enabling VLAs to maintain near-baseline success rates, even under severe visual corruption.

AI.tldr: CRT: a plug-and-play restoration transformer that immunizes Vision-Language-Action models against image corruptions by restoring clean observations from corrupted inputs using adversarial training, without fine-tuning the base model.
AI.motivation: Sensor-level image corruptions (noise, dead pixels, lens contamination) severely degrade VLA performance in real-world use; prior work focused on geometry-based occlusions; need robust perception to enable reliable deployment.
AI.method: Introduce Corruption Restoration Transformer (CRT), a vision transformer module integrated with VLA systems. Trained adversarially to reconstruct clean observations from corrupted inputs; model-agnostic and plug-and-play, avoiding fine-tuning of the underlying VLA. Benchmarked with LIBERO and Meta-World.
AI.result: CRT recovers lost performance, maintaining near-baseline success rates under severe visual corruption; mitigates the drop from ~90% to as low as 2% observed in state-of-the-art VLAs like π0.5 and SmolVLA.
AI.conclusion: CRT provides a practical, general solution to sensor-induced image corruptions in VLA pipelines, enabling robust real-world manipulation by restoring reliable perception without expensive retraining.

title: Autonomous Question Formation for Large Language Model-Driven AI Systems
summary: Large language model (LLM)-driven AI systems are increasingly important for autonomous decision-making in dynamic and open environments. However, most existing systems rely on predefined tasks and fixed prompts, limiting their ability to autonomously identify what problems should be solved when environmental conditions change. In this paper, we propose a human-simulation-based framework that enables AI systems to autonomously form questions and set tasks by reasoning over their internal states, environmental observations, and interactions with other AI systems. The proposed method treats question formation as a first-class decision process preceding task selection and execution, and integrates internal-driven, environment-aware, and inter-agent-aware prompting scopes to progressively expand cognitive coverage. In addition, the framework supports learning the question-formation process from experience, allowing the system to improve its adaptability and decision quality over time. xperimental results in a multi-agent simulation environment show that environment-aware prompting significantly reduces no-eat events compared with the internal-driven baseline, and inter-agent-aware prompting further reduces cumulative no-eat events by more than 60% over a 20-day simulation, with statistically significant improvements (p < 0.05).

AI.tldr: An autonomous AI framework that treats question formation as a first-class decision, enabling environment- and inter-agent-aware prompting and learning from experience to improve decision quality in dynamic multi-agent settings.
AI.motivation: Current LLM-based agents rely on predefined tasks and prompts, limiting their ability to identify and prioritize problems as environmental conditions evolve; a self-questioning mechanism could enhance adaptability.
AI.method: A human-simulation-based framework where AI systems autonomously form questions before task selection, using three prompting scopes—internal-driven, environment-aware, and inter-agent-aware—to progressively expand cognitive coverage; includes learning the question-formation process from experience.
AI.result: In multi-agent simulations, environment-aware prompting reduces no-eat events versus an internal-driven baseline; inter-agent-aware prompting further reduces cumulative no-eat events by over 60% across 20 days, with results achieving statistical significance (p < 0.05).
AI.conclusion: The framework enhances adaptability and decision quality by enabling autonomous, experience-driven question formation that guides task selection in open, dynamic environments.

title: Convergent World Representations and Divergent Tasks
summary: While neural representations are central to modern deep learning, the conditions governing their geometry and their roles in downstream adaptability remain poorly understood. We develop a framework clearly separating the underlying world, the data generation process and the resulting model representations to study these questions in a controlled setup. 5,075 city coordinates define the world and 7 geometric tasks generate the training data for autoregressive training. We find that different tasks give rise to qualitatively and quantitatively distinct world representation geometries. However, multi-task training drives convergence of world representations: models trained on non-overlapping tasks develop aligned geometric representations, providing controlled evidence for the Multitask Scaling Hypothesis of the Platonic Representation Hypothesis. To study adaptation, we pretrain models on all tasks, then test whether new entities (cities) can be consistently integrated into the representation space via fine-tuning. Surprisingly, we find that despite multi-task pretraining, some tasks, which we call divergent, actively harm the representational integration of new entities and harm generalization. Our results show that training on multiple relational tasks reliably produces convergent world representations, but lurking divergent tasks can catastrophically harm new entity integration via fine-tuning.

AI.tldr: Multi-task learning aligns world representations across tasks; divergent tasks can hinder future adaptation of new entities via fine-tuning.
AI.motivation: Understand geometry of neural representations and their adaptability by explicitly separating world, data generation, and model representations in a controlled setup using a city-world with multiple geometric tasks.
AI.method: Introduce a framework tagging world and data generation; autoregressive training on 5,075 city coordinates and 7 geometric tasks; analyze representation geometry; pretrain on all tasks; assess integration of new cities via fine-tuning; classify tasks as convergent or divergent and test the Multitask Scaling Hypothesis of the Platonic Representation Hypothesis.
AI.result: Different tasks yield qualitatively and quantitatively distinct world representation geometries; multi-task training causes convergence and alignment of representations across non-overlapping tasks; pretraining on all tasks performs well, but some divergent tasks continue to harm integration of new entities and generalization.
AI.conclusion: Training on multiple relational tasks reliably yields convergent world representations; however, the presence of divergent tasks can catastrophically impair incorporation of new entities during fine-tuning, underscoring the need for task-aware design in multi-task learning.

title: OASIS-DC: Generalizable Depth Completion via Output-level Alignment of Sparse-Integrated Monocular Pseudo Depth
summary: Recent monocular foundation models excel at zero-shot depth estimation, yet their outputs are inherently relative rather than metric, limiting direct use in robotics and autonomous driving. We leverage the fact that relative depth preserves global layout and boundaries: by calibrating it with sparse range measurements, we transform it into a pseudo metric depth prior. Building on this prior, we design a refinement network that follows the prior where reliable and deviates where necessary, enabling accurate metric predictions from very few labeled samples. The resulting system is particularly effective when curated validation data are unavailable, sustaining stable scale and sharp edges across few-shot regimes. These findings suggest that coupling foundation priors with sparse anchors is a practical route to robust, deployment-ready depth completion under real-world label scarcity.

AI.tldr: Leverage monocular foundation-model depth priors, calibrated with sparse range measurements, as a pseudo-metric depth prior; a refinement network then yields accurate metric depth from few labels, enabling robust, edge-preserving depth completion under label scarcity.
AI.motivation: Monocular foundation models produce relative depth maps that preserve layout but lack absolute scale, which limits robotic and autonomous driving use. Real-world deployment requires metric depth with stable scale, especially when labeled validation data is scarce. The approach seeks to combine strong priors with sparse measurements to enable metric depth with minimal supervision.
AI.method: 1) Extract relative depth from a monocular foundation model. 2) Calibrate with sparse range measurements to convert into a pseudo-metric depth prior. 3) Train a refinement network that follows the prior where reliable and deviates where needed, enabling metric predictions from very few labeled samples. 4) Emphasize deployment in scenarios with unavailable curated validation data, ensuring stable scale and sharp edges in few-shot regimes.
AI.result: The method yields metric-depth predictions from limited labels, maintaining global scale and sharp depth boundaries across few-shot settings, and providing robust depth completion under real-world label scarcity.
AI.conclusion: Coupling foundation priors with sparse anchors is a practical strategy for robust, deployment-ready depth completion in low-label regimes.

title: Reasoning with Autoregressive-Diffusion Collaborative Thoughts
summary: Autoregressive and diffusion models represent two complementary generative paradigms. Autoregressive models excel at sequential planning and constraint composition, yet struggle with tasks that require explicit spatial or physical grounding. Diffusion models, in contrast, capture rich spatial structure through high-dimensional generation, but lack the stepwise logical control needed to satisfy complex, multi-stage constraints or to reliably identify and correct errors. We introduce Collaborative Thoughts, a unified collaborative framework that enables autoregressive and diffusion models to reason and generate jointly through a closed-loop interaction. In Collaborative Thoughts, autoregressive models perform structured planning and constraint management, diffusion models instantiate these constraints as intermediate visual thoughts, and a vision-based critic module evaluates whether the visual thoughts satisfy the intended structural and physical requirements. This feedback is then used to iteratively refine subsequent planning and generation steps, mitigating error propagation across modalities. Importantly, Collaborative Thoughts uses the same collaborative loop regardless of whether the task is autoregressive question answering or diffusion-based visual generation. Through representative examples, we illustrate how Collaborative Thoughts can improve the reliability of spatial reasoning and the controllability of generation.

AI.tldr: A unified Collaborative Thoughts framework where autoregressive planning, diffusion-based generation, and a vision-based critic iteratively interact to improve spatial reasoning and controllability, applied across autoregressive QA and diffusion-driven visual tasks.
AI.motivation: To fuse the strengths of sequential, constraint-driven reasoning (AR models) with high-fidelity, spatially structured generation (diffusion models) while mitigating cross-modal error propagation through closed-loop feedback.
AI.method: AR models propose structured plans and constraints; diffusion models realize these constraints as intermediate visual thoughts; a vision-based critic evaluates alignment with structural/physical requirements; feedback loop refines subsequent planning and generation; loop is modality-agnostic.
AI.result: Conceptual/representative examples show improved reliability in spatial reasoning and controllability of generation via the collaborative loop.
AI.conclusion: A unified, modality-agnostic loop that improves multi-step constraint satisfaction and reduces error accumulation, applicable to both autoregressive QA and diffusion-based generation.

title: AdaptNC: Adaptive Nonconformity Scores for Uncertainty-Aware Autonomous Systems in Dynamic Environments
summary: Rigorous uncertainty quantification is essential for the safe deployment of autonomous systems in unconstrained environments. Conformal Prediction (CP) provides a distribution-free framework for this task, yet its standard formulations rely on exchangeability assumptions that are violated by the distribution shifts inherent in real-world robotics. Existing online CP methods maintain target coverage by adaptively scaling the conformal threshold, but typically employ a static nonconformity score function. We show that this fixed geometry leads to highly conservative, volume-inefficient prediction regions when environments undergo structural shifts. To address this, we propose \textbf{AdaptNC}, a framework for the joint online adaptation of both the nonconformity score parameters and the conformal threshold. AdaptNC leverages an adaptive reweighting scheme to optimize score functions, and introduces a replay buffer mechanism to mitigate the coverage instability that occurs during score transitions. We evaluate AdaptNC on diverse robotic benchmarks involving multi-agent policy changes, environmental changes and sensor degradation. Our results demonstrate that AdaptNC significantly reduces prediction region volume compared to state-of-the-art threshold-only baselines while maintaining target coverage levels.

AI.tldr: AdaptNC jointly tunes online nonconformity scores and CP threshold to handle distribution shifts, yielding smaller prediction regions without sacrificing coverage.
AI.motivation: Conformal Prediction (CP) provides distribution-free uncertainty quantification, but real-world robotics involve distribution shifts that violate exchangeability, making CP regions overly conservative when using fixed nonconformity scores; thus dynamic adaptation is needed.
AI.method: Adaptive reweighting of nonconformity score parameters coupled with online threshold adaptation, plus a replay buffer to stabilize coverage during score transitions.
AI.result: AdaptNC significantly reduces the volume of prediction regions compared to threshold-only baselines while maintaining target coverage across robotic benchmarks featuring multi-agent policy changes, environmental shifts, and sensor degradation.
AI.conclusion: Joint online adaptation of nonconformity scores and conformal threshold via AdaptNC yields more efficient CP regions under shifting conditions while preserving target coverage in robotic applications.

title: ToPT: Task-Oriented Prompt Tuning for Urban Region Representation Learning
comment: The paper has been accepted by ICASSP 2026
summary: Learning effective region embeddings from heterogeneous urban data underpins key urban computing tasks (e.g., crime prediction, resource allocation). However, prevailing two-stage methods yield task-agnostic representations, decoupling them from downstream objectives. Recent prompt-based approaches attempt to fix this but introduce two challenges: they often lack explicit spatial priors, causing spatially incoherent inter-region modeling, and they lack robust mechanisms for explicit task-semantic alignment. We propose ToPT, a two-stage framework that delivers spatially consistent fusion and explicit task alignment. ToPT consists of two modules: spatial-aware region embedding learning (SREL) and task-aware prompting for region embeddings (Prompt4RE). SREL employs a Graphormer-based fusion module that injects spatial priors-distance and regional centrality-as learnable attention biases to capture coherent, interpretable inter-region interactions. Prompt4RE performs task-oriented prompting: a frozen multimodal large language model (MLLM) processes task-specific templates to obtain semantic vectors, which are aligned with region embeddings via multi-head cross-attention for stable task conditioning. Experiments across multiple tasks and cities show state-of-the-art performance, with improvements of up to 64.2\%, validating the necessity and complementarity of spatial priors and prompt-region alignment. The code is available at https://github.com/townSeven/Prompt4RE.git.

AI.tldr: A two-stage framework, ToPT, yields spatially coherent region embeddings and task-aligned semantics via SREL (Graphormer-based spatial fusion with learnable priors) and Prompt4RE (MLLM-based task prompting with cross-attention alignment), achieving state-of-the-art results across tasks/cities; code available.
AI.motivation: To address two core gaps: (1) task-agnostic representations in two-stage pipelines, and (2) lack of explicit spatial priors and robust task-semantic alignment in prompt-based methods for region-level urban data.
AI.method: 1) SREL: Graphormer-based fusion module that injects spatial priors (distance, centrality) as learnable attention biases to produce coherent inter-region embeddings. 2) Prompt4RE: frozen multimodal LLM processes task-specific templates to derive semantic vectors; aligns these vectors with region embeddings via multi-head cross-attention for stable task conditioning.
AI.result: Experiments across multiple tasks and cities show state-of-the-art performance, with improvements reported up to 64.2%. Demonstrates the complementarity of spatial priors and prompt-region alignment.
AI.conclusion: Spatial priors and explicit task-region alignment are complementary; the two-stage ToPT framework yields robust, interpretable, task-conditioned region embeddings. Code is publicly available.

title: Invertible Memory Flow Networks
summary: Long sequence neural memory remains a challenging problem. RNNs and their variants suffer from vanishing gradients, and Transformers suffer from quadratic scaling. Furthermore, compressing long sequences into a finite fixed representation remains an intractable problem due to the difficult optimization landscape. Invertible Memory Flow Networks (IMFN) make long sequence compression tractable through factorization: instead of learning end-to-end compression, we decompose the problem into pairwise merges using a binary tree of "sweeper" modules. Rather than learning to compress long sequences, each sweeper learns a much simpler 2-to-1 compression task, achieving O(log N) depth with sublinear error accumulation in sequence length. For online inference, we distilled into a constant-cost recurrent student achieving O(1) sequential steps. Empirical results validate IMFN on long MNIST sequences and UCF-101 videos, demonstrating compression of high-dimensional data over long sequences.

AI.tldr: IMFN uses a binary-tree of 2-to-1 sweeper modules to factorize long-sequence compression, achieving O(log N) depth and sublinear error, with a constant-cost recurrent student for online inference; validated on long MNIST and UCF-101.
AI.motivation: Long sequences are difficult to model due to vanishing gradients in RNNs and quadratic scaling in Transformers; fixed representations are hard to learn because of difficult optimization landscapes.
AI.method: Decompose long-sequence compression into pairwise 2-to-1 merges arranged in a binary tree of sweeper modules. Each sweeper solves a simpler 2-to-1 task, enabling O(log N) depth and sublinear error accumulation. For online inference, distill the model into a constant-cost recurrent student.
AI.result: Empirical validation on long MNIST sequences and UCF-101 videos demonstrates effective compression of high-dimensional data over very long sequences.
AI.conclusion: Inverse Memory Flow Networks (IMFN) provide a tractable long-sequence compression approach with scalable depth and efficient online inference, achieving sublinear error growth and constant-time inference via distillation.

title: From Perception to Action: Spatial AI Agents and World Models
comment: 61 pages, 742 citations, 1 figure, 3 tables. Survey paper on spatial AI agents, embodied AI, graph neural networks, and world models
summary: While large language models have become the prevailing approach for agentic reasoning and planning, their success in symbolic domains does not readily translate to the physical world. Spatial intelligence, the ability to perceive 3D structure, reason about object relationships, and act under physical constraints, is an orthogonal capability that proves important for embodied agents. Existing surveys address either agentic architectures or spatial domains in isolation. None provide a unified framework connecting these complementary capabilities. This paper bridges that gap. Through a thorough review of over 2,000 papers, citing 742 works from top-tier venues, we introduce a unified three-axis taxonomy connecting agentic capabilities with spatial tasks across scales. Crucially, we distinguish spatial grounding (metric understanding of geometry and physics) from symbolic grounding (associating images with text), arguing that perception alone does not confer agency. Our analysis reveals three key findings mapped to these axes: (1) hierarchical memory systems (Capability axis) are important for long-horizon spatial tasks. (2) GNN-LLM integration (Task axis) is a promising approach for structured spatial reasoning. (3) World models (Scale axis) are essential for safe deployment across micro-to-macro spatial scales. We conclude by identifying six grand challenges and outlining directions for future research, including the need for unified evaluation frameworks to standardize cross-domain assessment. This taxonomy provides a foundation for unifying fragmented research efforts and enabling the next generation of spatially-aware autonomous systems in robotics, autonomous vehicles, and geospatial intelligence.

AI.tldr: Proposes a unified three-axis taxonomy that links agentic capabilities with spatial tasks for embodied AI, differentiates spatial grounding from symbolic grounding, and synthesizes insights from 2,000+ papers to reveal three core findings and six grand challenges, advocating a unified evaluation framework for cross-domain assessment.
AI.motivation: Existing surveys treat agentic architectures and spatial reasoning in isolation. A unified framework is needed to connect these capabilities for robust, spatially-aware autonomous systems across robotics, vehicles, and geospatial intelligence.
AI.method: Comprehensive literature review and synthesis of >2,000 papers, yielding a three-axis taxonomy (Capability, Task, Scale) that integrates agentic and spatial reasoning, with a distinction between spatial grounding and symbolic grounding. Derives key findings and practical implications, and outlines grand challenges and evaluation directions.
AI.result: Introduction of a unified three-axis taxonomy that couples agentic capabilities with spatial tasks; identification of three key findings (hierarchical memory for long-horizon spatial tasks, GNN-LLM integration for structured spatial reasoning, world models for safe multi-scale deployment); articulation of six grand challenges; proposal for unified evaluation frameworks to standardize cross-domain assessment.
AI.conclusion: The taxonomy provides a foundation for unifying fragmented research on spatially-aware autonomy, guiding future work toward integrated perception, reasoning, and action across micro-to-macro scales in robotics, autonomous vehicles, and geospatial intelligence.

title: ProjDevBench: Benchmarking AI Coding Agents on End-to-End Project Development
summary: Recent coding agents can generate complete codebases from simple prompts, yet existing evaluations focus on issue-level bug fixing and lag behind end-to-end development. We introduce ProjDevBench, an end-to-end benchmark that provides project requirements to coding agents and evaluates the resulting repositories. Combining Online Judge (OJ) testing with LLM-assisted code review, the benchmark evaluates agents on (1) system architecture design, (2) functional correctness, and (3) iterative solution refinement. We curate 20 programming problems across 8 categories, covering both concept-oriented tasks and real-world application scenarios, and evaluate six coding agents built on different LLM backends. Our evaluation reports an overall acceptance rate of 27.38%: agents handle basic functionality and data structures but struggle with complex system design, time complexity optimization, and resource management. Our benchmark is available at https://github.com/zsworld6/projdevbench.

AI.tldr: ProjDevBench is an end-to-end benchmark that assesses coding agents on project-level development tasks by combining Online Judge testing with LLM-assisted code review across 20 problems in 8 categories, evaluating six agents on system design, correctness, and iterative refinement, and reporting a 27.38% acceptance rate, indicating strengths in basic functionality but challenges in complex design and resource management.
AI.motivation: There is a gap in end-to-end evaluation of coding agents beyond issue-level bug fixing; current benchmarks underrepresent real-world project development complexities such as architecture decisions, time complexity, and resource constraints.
AI.method: Provide project requirements to agents, run end-to-end evaluations using OJ-style tests plus LLM-assisted code review, across three axes (system architecture, functional correctness, iterative refinement), with 20 problems spanning 8 categories and six agents from different LLM backends.
AI.result: Overall acceptance rate of 27.38%. Agents perform basic functionality and data-structure tasks but struggle with complex system design, time complexity optimization, and resource management.
AI.conclusion: The benchmark is publicly available and enables systematic, end-to-end evaluation of coding agents, highlighting current limitations and guiding future improvements in project-level AI-assisted software development.

title: OpenDDI: A Comprehensive Benchmark for DDI Prediction
summary: Drug-Drug Interactions (DDIs) significantly influence therapeutic efficacy and patient safety. As experimental discovery is resource-intensive and time-consuming, efficient computational methodologies have become essential. The predominant paradigm formulates DDI prediction as a drug graph-based link prediction task. However, further progress is hindered by two fundamental challenges: (1) lack of high-quality data: most studies rely on small-scale DDI datasets and single-modal drug representations; (2) lack of standardized evaluation: inconsistent scenarios, varied metrics, and diverse baselines. To address the above issues, we propose OpenDDI, a comprehensive benchmark for DDI prediction. Specifically, (1) from the data perspective, OpenDDI unifies 6 widely used DDI datasets and 2 existing forms of drug representation, while additionally contributing 3 new large-scale LLM-augmented datasets and a new multimodal drug representation covering 5 modalities. (2) From the evaluation perspective, OpenDDI unifies 20 SOTA model baselines across 3 downstream tasks, with standardized protocols for data quality, effectiveness, generalization, robustness, and efficiency. Based on OpenDDI, we conduct a comprehensive evaluation and derive 10 valuable insights for DDI prediction while exposing current limitations to provide critical guidance for this rapidly evolving field. Our code is available at https://github.com/xiaoriwuguang/OpenDDI

AI.tldr: OpenDDI is a unified benchmark for drug–drug interaction (DDI) prediction that consolidates six datasets and two existing drug representations, introduces three large-scale, LLM-augmented datasets plus a multimodal drug representation across five modalities, and standardizes evaluation with 20 state-of-the-art baselines across three tasks, providing protocols for data quality, generalization, robustness, and efficiency.
AI.motivation: Address two main bottlenecks in DDI prediction: (i) scarce high-quality data and reliance on single-modal representations, and (ii) lack of standardized evaluation protocols across diverse tasks, metrics, and baselines.
AI.method: Data unification (6 datasets) and dual representations (2 forms) plus 3 new LLM-augmented datasets and a multimodal representation (5 modalities). Evaluation harmonization with 20 SOTA models across 3 downstream tasks and standardized protocols covering data quality, effectiveness, generalization, robustness, and efficiency.
AI.result: Comprehensive benchmarking with OpenDDI, yielding 10 insights into DDI prediction and revealing current limitations; code released at the provided GitHub link.
AI.conclusion: OpenDDI establishes a comprehensive, standardized benchmark platform for DDI prediction that enables fair, scalable comparisons, accelerates progress, and guides future research by identifying limitations and best practices.

title: Real-Time Loop Closure Detection in Visual SLAM via NetVLAD and Faiss
summary: Loop closure detection (LCD) is a core component of simultaneous localization and mapping (SLAM): it identifies revisited places and enables pose-graph constraints that correct accumulated drift. Classic bag-of-words approaches such as DBoW are efficient but often degrade under appearance change and perceptual aliasing. In parallel, deep learning-based visual place recognition (VPR) descriptors (e.g., NetVLAD and Transformer-based models) offer stronger robustness, but their computational cost is often viewed as a barrier to real-time SLAM. In this paper, we empirically evaluate NetVLAD as an LCD module and compare it against DBoW on the KITTI dataset. We introduce a Fine-Grained Top-K precision-recall curve that better reflects LCD settings where a query may have zero or multiple valid matches. With Faiss-accelerated nearestneighbor search, NetVLAD achieves real-time query speed while improving accuracy and robustness over DBoW, making it a practical drop-in alternative for LCD in SLAM.

AI.tldr: NetVLAD, accelerated by Faiss, can replace DBoW for loop closure detection as a real-time, more robust LCD module, with Fine-Grained Top-K PR showing better match handling on KITTI.
AI.motivation: Address the robustness limits of traditional BoW LCD (DBoW) under appearance changes and perceptual aliasing, and the perceived computational barrier of deep-learning VPR descriptors for real-time SLAM.
AI.method: Empirical evaluation on KITTI comparing NetVLAD against DBoW for LCD. Introduces a Fine-Grained Top-K PR curve to reflect queries with zero or multiple valid matches. Uses Faiss-accelerated nearest-neighbor search to enable real-time query throughput.
AI.result: NetVLAD achieves real-time query speed and improves accuracy and robustness over DBoW; Faiss enables efficient ANN search; NetVLAD is a practical drop-in alternative for LCD in SLAM.
AI.conclusion: Deep-learning VPR descriptors like NetVLAD are viable, robust, and real-time capable LCD modules suitable to replace traditional BoW methods in SLAM when supported by efficient ANN search; this may improve loop-closure reliability in changing environments.

title: FlowSteer: Interactive Agentic Workflow Orchestration via End-to-End Reinforcement Learning
comment: 41 pages, 7 figures, 6 tables. Project page: http://flowsteer.org/
summary: In recent years, a variety of powerful agentic workflows have been applied to solve a wide range of human problems. However, existing workflow orchestration still faces key challenges, including high manual cost, reliance on specific operators/large language models (LLMs), and sparse reward signals. To address these challenges, we propose FlowSteer, an end-to-end reinforcement learning framework that takes a lightweight policy model as the agent and an executable canvas environment, automating workflow orchestration through multi-turn interaction. In this process, the policy model analyzes execution states and selects editing actions, while the canvas executes operators and returns feedback for iterative refinement. Moreover, FlowSteer provides a plug-and-play framework that supports diverse operator libraries and interchangeable LLM backends. To effectively train this interaction paradigm, we propose Canvas Workflow Relative Policy Optimization (CWRPO), which introduces diversity-constrained rewards with conditional release to stabilize learning and suppress shortcut behaviors. Experimental results on twelve datasets show that FlowSteer significantly outperforms baselines across various tasks.

AI.tldr: FlowSteer is an end-to-end RL framework for workflow orchestration that uses a lightweight policy and an executable canvas, enabling multi-turn interaction and plug‑and‑play operator/LLM support; it introduces CWRPO with diversity-constrained rewards to stabilize learning, showing superior performance across 12 datasets.
AI.motivation: Address high manual cost, reliance on specific operators/LLMs, and sparse reward signals in agentic workflow orchestration.
AI.method: A lightweight policy model acts as the agent within an executable canvas environment, performing multi-turn interaction by choosing editing actions and receiving feedback from the canvas after executing operators. FlowSteer is designed as a plug‑and‑play framework with interchangeable operator libraries and LLM backends. Training is guided by Canvas Workflow Relative Policy Optimization (CWRPO), which uses diversity-constrained rewards with conditional release to stabilize learning and reduce shortcut behaviors.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation across twelve datasets shows FlowSteer significantly outperforms baselines on diverse tasks.
AI.conclusion: FlowSteer demonstrates effective, generalizable end-to-end learning for automated workflow orchestration, with CWRPO providing stable, efficient training and broad compatibility with operator libraries and LLM backends.

title: One Loss to Rule Them All: Marked Time-to-Event for Structured EHR Foundation Models
summary: Clinical events captured in Electronic Health Records (EHR) are irregularly sampled and may consist of a mixture of discrete events and numerical measurements, such as laboratory values or treatment dosages. The sequential nature of EHR, analogous to natural language, has motivated the use of next-token prediction to train prior EHR Foundation Models (FMs) over events. However, this training fails to capture the full structure of EHR. We propose ORA, a marked time-to-event pretraining objective that jointly models event timing and associated measurements. Across multiple datasets, downstream tasks, and model architectures, this objective consistently yields more generalizable representations than next-token prediction and pretraining losses that ignore continuous measurements. Importantly, the proposed objective yields improvements beyond traditional classification evaluation, including better regression and time-to-event prediction. Beyond introducing a new family of FMs, our results suggest a broader takeaway: pretraining objectives that account for EHR structure are critical for expanding downstream capabilities and generalizability

AI.tldr: ORA: a marked time-to-event pretraining objective for EHRs that jointly models event timing and measurements, outperforming next-token and measurement-agnostic losses across tasks.
AI.motivation: EHR data are irregularly sampled, containing both discrete events and continuous measurements. Next-token pretraining neglects event timing and measurement structure, limiting downstream generalization.
AI.method: Introduce a joint marked time-to-event objective (ORA) that models the distribution over event times and associated measurements given the historical sequence. Train foundation models to maximize the joint likelihood of observed event times and marks, evaluated across diverse datasets, architectures, and tasks.
