AI.result: ORA yields more generalizable representations than next-token or measurement-agnostic pretraining, improving not only classification but regression and time-to-event predictions across multiple datasets and model families.
AI.conclusion: Structure-aware pretraining for EHRs is crucial for broad downstream capability and generalizability; modeling event timing and measurements jointly captures essential dynamics beyond traditional classification.

title: DDP-WM: Disentangled Dynamics Prediction for Efficient World Models
comment: Codes will be available at https://github.com/HCPLab-SYSU/DDP-WM
summary: World models are essential for autonomous robotic planning. However, the substantial computational overhead of existing dense Transformerbased models significantly hinders real-time deployment. To address this efficiency-performance bottleneck, we introduce DDP-WM, a novel world model centered on the principle of Disentangled Dynamics Prediction (DDP). We hypothesize that latent state evolution in observed scenes is heterogeneous and can be decomposed into sparse primary dynamics driven by physical interactions and secondary context-driven background updates. DDP-WM realizes this decomposition through an architecture that integrates efficient historical processing with dynamic localization to isolate primary dynamics. By employing a crossattention mechanism for background updates, the framework optimizes resource allocation and provides a smooth optimization landscape for planners. Extensive experiments demonstrate that DDP-WM achieves significant efficiency and performance across diverse tasks, including navigation, precise tabletop manipulation, and complex deformable or multi-body interactions. Specifically, on the challenging Push-T task, DDP-WM achieves an approximately 9 times inference speedup and improves the MPC success rate from 90% to98% compared to state-of-the-art dense models. The results establish a promising path for developing efficient, high-fidelity world models. Codes will be available at https://github.com/HCPLab-SYSU/DDP-WM.

AI.tldr: Proposes DDP-WM, a disentangled dynamics-based world model that separates primary sparse dynamics from background context updates to enable efficient, real-time planning in robotics, achieving large speedups and higher MPC success.
AI.motivation: Dense Transformer-based world models deliver high fidelity but incur substantial computational overhead, hindering real-time robotic planning.
AI.method: DDP-WM integrates efficient historical processing with dynamic localization to isolate primary dynamics, and uses a cross-attention mechanism to update background context. This disentangled architecture allocates compute to the critical, sparse dynamics while handling background updates efficiently, yielding a smoother optimization landscape for planners.
AI.result: Significant efficiency and performance gains across tasks: on Push-T task, ~9× faster inference and MPC success rate improves from 90% to 98%. Demonstrated effectiveness in navigation, precise tabletop manipulation, and complex deformable or multi-body interactions; code to be released.
AI.conclusion: DDP-WM validates that disentangled dynamics prediction effectively balances accuracy and efficiency, offering a viable path toward real-time, high-fidelity world models for autonomous robotics.

title: TRIP-Bench: A Benchmark for Long-Horizon Interactive Agents in Real-World Scenarios
summary: As LLM-based agents are deployed in increasingly complex real-world settings, existing benchmarks underrepresent key challenges such as enforcing global constraints, coordinating multi-tool reasoning, and adapting to evolving user behavior over long, multi-turn interactions. To bridge this gap, we introduce \textbf{TRIP-Bench}, a long-horizon benchmark grounded in realistic travel-planning scenarios. TRIP-Bench leverages real-world data, offers 18 curated tools and 40+ travel requirements, and supports automated evaluation. It includes splits of varying difficulty; the hard split emphasizes long and ambiguous interactions, style shifts, feasibility changes, and iterative version revision. Dialogues span up to 15 user turns, can involve 150+ tool calls, and may exceed 200k tokens of context. Experiments show that even advanced models achieve at most 50\% success on the easy split, with performance dropping below 10\% on hard subsets. We further propose \textbf{GTPO}, an online multi-turn reinforcement learning method with specialized reward normalization and reward differencing. Applied to Qwen2.5-32B-Instruct, GTPO improves constraint satisfaction and interaction robustness, outperforming Gemini-3-Pro in our evaluation. We expect TRIP-Bench to advance practical long-horizon interactive agents, and GTPO to provide an effective online RL recipe for robust long-horizon training.

AI.tldr: Presents TRIP-Bench, a long-horizon travel-planning benchmark with 18 tools, 40+ requirements, and up to 15-turn dialogues, plus GTPO, an online multi-turn RL method that improves constraint satisfaction; results show current models struggle (≤50% on easy, <10% on hard) and GTPO outperforms Gemini-3-Pro on Qwen2.5-32B-Instruct.
AI.motivation: Address gaps in existing benchmarks for enforcing global constraints, multi-tool coordination, and adaptation over long, evolving interactions in realistic domains such as travel planning.
AI.method: TRIP-Bench uses real-world data, 18 curated tools, 40+ travel requirements, and multiple difficulty splits including a hard long-interaction split; automated evaluation. GTPO is an online multi-turn RL algorithm with specialized reward normalization and reward differencing, applied to Qwen2.5-32B-Instruct and compared to Gemini-3-Pro.
AI.result: Hard split yields performance below 10%; easy split caps around 50%; GTPO improves constraint satisfaction and interaction robustness and outperforms Gemini-3-Pro in the authors’ evaluation.
AI.conclusion: TRIP-Bench can drive progress in robust long-horizon interactive agents; GTPO provides an effective online RL recipe for long-horizon training.

title: Depth, Not Data: An Analysis of Hessian Spectral Bifurcation
summary:
The eigenvalue distribution of the Hessian matrix plays a crucial role in understanding the optimization landscape of deep neural networks. Prior work has attributed the well-documented ``bulk-and-spike'' spectral structure, where a few dominant eigenvalues are separated from a bulk of smaller ones, to the imbalance in the data covariance matrix. In this work, we challenge this view by demonstrating that such spectral Bifurcation can arise purely from the network architecture, independent of data imbalance.
  Specifically, we analyze a deep linear network setup and prove that, even when the data covariance is perfectly balanced, the Hessian still exhibits a Bifurcation eigenvalue structure: a dominant cluster and a bulk cluster. Crucially, we establish that the ratio between dominant and bulk eigenvalues scales linearly with the network depth. This reveals that the spectral gap is strongly affected by the network architecture rather than solely by data distribution. Our results suggest that both model architecture and data characteristics should be considered when designing optimization algorithms for deep networks.

AI.tldr: Hessian spectrum in deep linear nets shows a depth-dependent bifurcation (dominant vs bulk clusters) even with balanced data; spectral gap scales linearly with depth, indicating architecture shapes optimization dynamics.
AI.motivation: Question whether data imbalance is solely responsible for bulk-and-spike Hessian spectra in deep nets; investigate if network architecture can independently induce similar spectral structure.
AI.method: Theoretical analysis of a deep linear network with balanced data; derive Hessian structure and prove existence of two eigenvalue clusters; derive scaling showing dominant/bulk ratio grows linearly with network depth.
AI.result: Demonstrated that a bifurcation (dominant and bulk eigenvalue clusters) in the Hessian arises regardless of data imbalance; the dominant-to-bulk eigenvalue ratio scales linearly with network depth.
AI.conclusion: Optimization landscapes in deep networks are shaped by both architecture and data; architecture alone can create spectral gaps, informing the design of optimization algorithms and necessitating consideration of model structure alongside data properties.

title: LangMap: A Hierarchical Benchmark for Open-Vocabulary Goal Navigation
summary: The relationships between objects and language are fundamental to meaningful communication between humans and AI, and to practically useful embodied intelligence. We introduce HieraNav, a multi-granularity, open-vocabulary goal navigation task where agents interpret natural language instructions to reach targets at four semantic levels: scene, room, region, and instance. To this end, we present Language as a Map (LangMap), a large-scale benchmark built on real-world 3D indoor scans with comprehensive human-verified annotations and tasks spanning these levels. LangMap provides region labels, discriminative region descriptions, discriminative instance descriptions covering 414 object categories, and over 18K navigation tasks. Each target features both concise and detailed descriptions, enabling evaluation across different instruction styles. LangMap achieves superior annotation quality, outperforming GOAT-Bench by 23.8% in discriminative accuracy using four times fewer words. Comprehensive evaluations of zero-shot and supervised models on LangMap reveal that richer context and memory improve success, while long-tailed, small, context-dependent, and distant goals, as well as multi-goal completion, remain challenging. HieraNav and LangMap establish a rigorous testbed for advancing language-driven embodied navigation. Project: https://bo-miao.github.io/LangMap

AI.tldr: Introduces LangMap, a large-scale open-vocabulary, multi-granularity navigation benchmark built on real-world 3D indoor scans, paired with the HieraNav navigation task. It supports scene/room/region/instance levels, 18K tasks, discriminative region/instance descriptions for 414 object categories, and richer descriptions. LangMap outperforms GOAT-Bench in discriminative accuracy (by 23.8%) with fourfold fewer words. Zero-shot and supervised evaluations show that richer context and memory improve success, while long-tailed, small, context-dependent, distant goals and multi-goal completion remain challenging. This work establishes a rigorous testbed for language-driven embodied navigation.
AI.motivation: To advance meaningful human–AI communication and embodied navigation by enabling open-vocabulary, multi-granularity language understanding in realistic environments; assess how language and memory influence navigation performance across diverse goal types and instruction styles.
AI.method: Construct LangMap from real-world 3D indoor scans with comprehensive human-verified annotations spanning four semantic levels (scene, room, region, instance). Provide region labels, discriminative region/instance descriptions (covering 414 object categories), and over 18K navigation tasks. Each target includes concise and detailed descriptions. Compare annotation quality against GOAT-Bench and evaluate HieraNav using zero-shot and supervised models.
AI.result: LangMap achieves superior annotation quality, outperforming GOAT-Bench by 23.8% in discriminative accuracy using four times fewer words. Comprehensive evaluations show that richer context and memory improve success for zero-shot and supervised models, but challenges persist for long-tailed, small, context-dependent, and distant goals, as well as multi-goal completion.
AI.conclusion: LangMap and HieraNav establish a rigorous testbed for advancing language-driven embodied navigation, enabling robust evaluation across multi-granularity semantics and open-vocabulary instructions.

title: What LLMs Think When You Don't Tell Them What to Think About?
summary: Characterizing the behavior of large language models (LLMs) across diverse settings is critical for reliable monitoring and AI safety. However, most existing analyses rely on topic- or task-specific prompts, which can substantially limit what can be observed. In this work, we study what LLMs generate from minimal, topic-neutral inputs and probe their near-unconstrained generative behavior. Despite the absence of explicit topics, model outputs cover a broad semantic space, and surprisingly, each model family exhibits strong and systematic topical preferences. GPT-OSS predominantly generates programming (27.1%) and mathematical content (24.6%), whereas Llama most frequently generates literary content (9.1%). DeepSeek often generates religious content, while Qwen frequently generates multiple-choice questions. Beyond topical preferences, we also observe differences in content specialization and depth: GPT-OSS often generates more technically advanced content (e.g., dynamic programming) compared with other models (e.g., basic Python). Furthermore, we find that the near-unconstrained generation often degenerates into repetitive phrases, revealing interesting behaviors unique to each model family. For instance, degenerate outputs from Llama include multiple URLs pointing to personal Facebook and Instagram accounts. We release the complete dataset of 256,000 samples from 16 LLMs, along with a reproducible codebase.

AI.tldr: A near-unconstrained generation study across 16 LLMs reveals model-specific topical biases and content depth, plus degeneration patterns; a 256k-sample dataset and reproducible code are released.
AI.motivation: To move beyond topic- or task-specific prompts and examine LLMs’ outputs under minimal, topic-neutral inputs, revealing intrinsic generative tendencies and safety/monitoring implications.
AI.method: Generate outputs from minimal inputs across 16 LLM families, classify outputs by topical content and depth, quantify repetition/degeneration patterns, compare across models, and release a large dataset (256,000 samples) and a reproducible codebase.
AI.result: Models show strong, systematic topical preferences despite topic-neutral prompts: GPT-OSS—programming and mathematics; Llama—literary content; DeepSeek—religious; Qwen—multiple-choice questions. GPT-OSS yields more technically advanced content (e.g., dynamic programming); LLMs vary in content depth. Near-unconstrained outputs tend to degenerate into repetitive phrases; Llama outputs include multiple URLs to personal social accounts. A dataset of 256k samples across 16 LLMs and accompanying code is released.
AI.conclusion: Topic-neutral prompts uncovers model-specific biases and content specialization, with implications for monitoring and AI safety. The dataset and code enable reproducibility and further study of intrinsic generative tendencies and degeneration patterns.

title: Contrastive Domain Generalization for Cross-Instrument Molecular Identification in Mass Spectrometry
summary: Identifying molecules from mass spectrometry (MS) data remains a fundamental challenge due to the semantic gap between physical spectral peaks and underlying chemical structures. Existing deep learning approaches often treat spectral matching as a closed-set recognition task, limiting their ability to generalize to unseen molecular scaffolds. To overcome this limitation, we propose a cross-modal alignment framework that directly maps mass spectra into the chemically meaningful molecular structure embedding space of a pretrained chemical language model. On a strict scaffold-disjoint benchmark, our model achieves a Top-1 accuracy of 42.2% in fixed 256-way zero-shot retrieval and demonstrates strong generalization under a global retrieval setting. Moreover, the learned embedding space demonstrates strong chemical coherence, reaching 95.4% accuracy in 5-way 5-shot molecular re-identification. These results suggest that explicitly integrating physical spectral resolution with molecular structure embedding is key to solving the generalization bottleneck in molecular identification from MS data.

AI.tldr: Cross-modal alignment maps mass spectra into a pretrained molecular-structure embedding space to improve generalization for unseen scaffolds; achieves 42.2% Top-1 in 256-way zero-shot retrieval and 95.4% accuracy in 5-way 5-shot re-identification, showing coherent spectral-structure embeddings.
AI.motivation: Semantic gap between MS peaks and chemical structures; closed-set MS methods fail to generalize to unseen scaffolds; need a chemically meaningful embedding space for spectra.
AI.method: Propose a cross-modal framework that embeds mass spectra into the embedding space of a pretrained chemical language model; evaluates on scaffold-disjoint benchmarks with 256-way zero-shot retrieval and global retrieval; assesses chemical coherence via 5-way 5-shot re-identification.
AI.result: Top-1 42.2% in 256-way zero-shot retrieval; strong generalization in global retrieval; 95.4% accuracy in 5-way 5-shot molecular re-identification; embedding space demonstrates chemical coherence.
AI.conclusion: Explicitly integrating spectral resolution with molecular-structure embeddings is crucial to overcoming generalization bottlenecks in MS-based molecular identification.

title: Beyond Dense States: Elevating Sparse Transcoders to Active Operators for Latent Reasoning
summary: Latent reasoning compresses the chain-of-thought (CoT) into continuous hidden states, yet existing methods rely on dense latent transitions that remain difficult to interpret and control. Meanwhile, sparse representation models uncover human-interpretable semantic features but remain largely confined to post-hoc analysis. We reconcile this tension by proposing LSTR (Latent Sparse Transcoder Reasoning), a latent reasoning framework that elevates functional sparse transcoders into active reasoning operators to perform multi-step computation through sparse semantic transitions. At its core, LSTR employs a Latent Transition Transcoder (LTT) with a residual skip architecture that decouples linear manifold transport from sparse semantic updates, enabling controllable semantic resolution via explicit sparsity constraints. Extensive experiments show that LSTR preserves reasoning accuracy and compression efficiency while substantially improving interpretability over dense latent baselines. Causal interventions and trajectory analyses further demonstrate that these sparse features act as both interpretable and causally effective operators in the reasoning process.

AI.tldr: LSTR uses latent sparse transcoders (LTT) with a residual skip to perform multi-step reasoning via sparse semantic updates, reconciling interpretability with efficient latent reasoning.
AI.motivation: To overcome the lack of interpretability in dense latent CoT representations while maintaining reasoning accuracy and compression; leverage sparse semantic features as active reasoning operators.
AI.method: Introduce Latent Transition Transcoder (LTT) with a residual skip architecture that decouples linear latent transport from sparse semantic updates, and impose explicit sparsity constraints to control semantic resolution. Treat sparse updates as active reasoning steps.
AI.result: Preserves reasoning accuracy and compression efficiency while substantially improving interpretability versus dense latent baselines. Causal interventions and trajectory analyses show sparse features are interpretable and causally effective operators in reasoning.
AI.conclusion: LSTR provides a framework where sparse semantic transitions function as active, controllable reasoning operators within latent space, reconciling interpretability with efficient latent computation.

title: Beyond the Node: Clade-level Selection for Efficient MCTS in Automatic Heuristic Design
summary: While Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) shows promise in Large Language Model (LLM) based Automatic Heuristic Design (AHD), it suffers from a critical over-exploitation tendency under the limited computational budgets required for heuristic evaluation. To address this limitation, we propose Clade-AHD, an efficient framework that replaces node-level point estimates with clade-level Bayesian beliefs. By aggregating descendant evaluations into Beta distributions and performing Thompson Sampling over these beliefs, Clade-AHD explicitly models uncertainty to guide exploration, enabling more reliable decision-making under sparse and noisy evaluations. Extensive experiments on complex combinatorial optimization problems demonstrate that Clade-AHD consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods while significantly reducing computational cost. The source code is publicly available at: https://github.com/Mriya0306/Clade-AHD.

AI.tldr: Clade-AHD uses clade-level Bayesian beliefs with Beta priors and Thompson Sampling to improve MCTS-based heuristic design under limited evaluations, achieving better performance with lower computation.
AI.motivation: MCTS-based AHD with LLMs suffers from over-exploitation under tight computational budgets, hindering reliable exploration and cost-effective evaluations.
AI.method: Aggregate descendant evaluations into Beta distributions per clade and perform Thompson Sampling over these clade beliefs instead of node-level point estimates, replacing traditional MCTS node statistics with probabilistic clade-level uncertainty modeling.
AI.result: Clade-AHD consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods on complex combinatorial optimization problems while significantly reducing computational cost.
AI.conclusion: Bayesian clade-level modeling with Thompson Sampling enhances exploration efficiency in MCTS for LLM-based AHD under limited budgets, enabling more reliable decision-making.

title: Mitigating loss of control in advanced AI systems through instrumental goal trajectories
summary: Researchers at artificial intelligence labs and universities are concerned that highly capable artificial intelligence (AI) systems may erode human control by pursuing instrumental goals. Existing mitigations remain largely technical and system-centric: tracking capability in advanced systems, shaping behaviour through methods such as reinforcement learning from human feedback, and designing systems to be corrigible and interruptible. Here we develop instrumental goal trajectories to expand these options beyond the model. Gaining capability typically depends on access to additional technical resources, such as compute, storage, data and adjacent services, which in turn requires access to monetary resources. In organisations, these resources can be obtained through three organisational pathways. We label these pathways the procurement, governance and finance instrumental goal trajectories (IGTs). Each IGT produces a trail of organisational artefacts that can be monitored and used as intervention points when a systems capabilities or behaviour exceed acceptable thresholds. In this way, IGTs offer concrete avenues for defining capability levels and for broadening how corrigibility and interruptibility are implemented, shifting attention from model properties alone to the organisational systems that enable them.

AI.tldr: Introduces instrumental goal trajectories (IGTs) to monitor and control AI capabilities at the organizational level, not just the model, by tracking three pathways—procurement, governance, and finance—that enable access to resources, enabling intervention points to supervise system capabilities and corrigibility.
AI.motivation: As capable AI systems pursue instrumental goals, purely technical mitigations are insufficient; organizations enable capabilities via resources; need a framework to monitor organizational artefacts and intervene.
AI.method: Conceptual framework introducing IGTs; identifies three organizational pathways (procurement, governance, finance); describes how organisational artefacts create intervention points; integrates with corrigibility and interruptibility concepts.
AI.result: IGTs provide concrete avenues to define capability levels and broaden corrigibility/interruption beyond model properties by focusing on organizational systems and artefacts.
AI.conclusion: Shifts safety focus from models to organizational processes; enables practical monitoring and governance for preventing runaway capabilities, while acknowledging need for empirical validation.

title: Forget by Uncertainty: Orthogonal Entropy Unlearning for Quantized Neural Networks
summary: The deployment of quantized neural networks on edge devices, combined with privacy regulations like GDPR, creates an urgent need for machine unlearning in quantized models. However, existing methods face critical challenges: they induce forgetting by training models to memorize incorrect labels, conflating forgetting with misremembering, and employ scalar gradient reweighting that cannot resolve directional conflicts between gradients. We propose OEU, a novel Orthogonal Entropy Unlearning framework with two key innovations: 1) Entropy-guided unlearning maximizes prediction uncertainty on forgotten data, achieving genuine forgetting rather than confident misprediction, and 2) Gradient orthogonal projection eliminates interference by projecting forgetting gradients onto the orthogonal complement of retain gradients, providing theoretical guarantees for utility preservation under first-order approximation. Extensive experiments demonstrate that OEU outperforms existing methods in both forgetting effectiveness and retain accuracy.

AI.tldr: OEU introduces entropy-guided unlearning with gradient orthogonal projection to achieve genuine forgetting in quantized neural networks while preserving utility, with theoretical first-order guarantees and strong empirical performance.
AI.motivation: Growing need for compliant machine unlearning on edge devices running quantized models (GDPR-like regulations) without conflating forgetting with misprediction or suffering utility loss.
AI.method: 1) Entropy-guided unlearning to maximize prediction uncertainty on forgotten data, ensuring forgetting rather than confident misprediction. 2) Gradient orthogonal projection to remove interference by projecting forgetting gradients onto the orthogonal complement of retain gradients, yielding a first-order utility preservation guarantee.
AI.result: Empirical results show OEU outperforms prior methods in both forgetting effectiveness and retained accuracy across extensive experiments.
AI.conclusion: OEU provides genuine forgetting with preserved utility in quantized edge-deployed models, supported by theory and experiments.

title: Optimizing Prompts for Large Language Models: A Causal Approach
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly embedded in enterprise workflows, yet their performance remains highly sensitive to prompt design. Automatic Prompt Optimization (APO) seeks to mitigate this instability, but existing approaches face two persistent challenges. First, commonly used prompt strategies rely on static instructions that perform well on average but fail to adapt to heterogeneous queries. Second, more dynamic approaches depend on offline reward models that are fundamentally correlational, confounding prompt effectiveness with query characteristics. We propose Causal Prompt Optimization (CPO), a framework that reframes prompt design as a problem of causal estimation. CPO operates in two stages. First, it learns an offline causal reward model by applying Double Machine Learning (DML) to semantic embeddings of prompts and queries, isolating the causal effect of prompt variations from confounding query attributes. Second, it utilizes this unbiased reward signal to guide a resource-efficient search for query-specific prompts without relying on costly online evaluation. We evaluate CPO across benchmarks in mathematical reasoning, visualization, and data analytics. CPO consistently outperforms human-engineered prompts and state-of-the-art automated optimizers. The gains are driven primarily by improved robustness on hard queries, where existing methods tend to deteriorate. Beyond performance, CPO fundamentally reshapes the economics of prompt optimization: by shifting evaluation from real-time model execution to an offline causal model, it enables high-precision, per-query customization at a fraction of the inference cost required by online methods. Together, these results establish causal inference as a scalable foundation for reliable and cost-efficient prompt optimization in enterprise LLM deployments.

AI.tldr: CPO reframes prompt optimization as causal estimation using offline Double Machine Learning to estimate prompt effects, enabling query-specific prompts with reduced online evaluation cost.
AI.motivation: Prompt design in enterprise LLMs is unstable; static prompts underperform on heterogeneous queries, and online methods rely on correlational offline rewards. A causal framework could yield robust, cost-efficient prompt customization.
AI.method: Two-stage approach: (1) train an offline causal reward model with Double Machine Learning (DML) on semantic embeddings of prompts and queries to isolate the causal effect of prompt variations from confounding query attributes; (2) use this unbiased reward to guide a resource-efficient search for per-query prompts without costly online evaluation.
AI.result: CPO outperforms human-engineered prompts and state-of-the-art APC methods across benchmarks in math reasoning, visualization, and data analytics, with especially strong gains on hard queries and reduced online inference cost.
AI.conclusion: Framing prompt design as causal estimation enables scalable, per-query prompt customization and cost-efficient optimization, positioning causal inference as a foundational tool for reliable enterprise LLM deployment.

title: When Classes Evolve: A Benchmark and Framework for Stage-Aware Class-Incremental Learning
summary: Class-Incremental Learning (CIL) aims to sequentially learn new classes while mitigating catastrophic forgetting of previously learned knowledge. Conventional CIL approaches implicitly assume that classes are morphologically static, focusing primarily on preserving previously learned representations as new classes are introduced. However, this assumption neglects intra-class evolution: a phenomenon wherein instances of the same semantic class undergo significant morphological transformations, such as a larva turning into a butterfly. Consequently, a model must both discriminate between classes and adapt to evolving appearances within a single class. To systematically address this challenge, we formalize Stage-Aware CIL (Stage-CIL), a paradigm in which each class is learned progressively through distinct morphological stages. To facilitate rigorous evaluation within this paradigm, we introduce the Stage-Bench, a 10-domain, 2-stages dataset and protocol that jointly measure inter- and intra-class forgetting. We further propose STAGE, a novel method that explicitly learns abstract and transferable evolution patterns within a fixed-size memory pool. By decoupling semantic identity from transformation dynamics, STAGE enables accurate prediction of future morphologies based on earlier representations. Extensive empirical evaluation demonstrates that STAGE consistently and substantially outperforms existing state-of-the-art approaches, highlighting its effectiveness in simultaneously addressing inter-class discrimination and intra-class morphological adaptation.

AI.tldr: Stage-CIL formalizes intra-class morphology evolution in continual learning, introduces Stage-Bench for evaluation, and presents STAGE to learn evolution patterns within a fixed memory, achieving strong gains over prior methods.
AI.motivation: Real-world classes often evolve appearance over time (intra-class morphing). Conventional CIL assumes static intra-class morphology, leading to forgetting and poor adaptation. Stage-CIL addresses both inter-class discrimination and intra-class evolution.
AI.method: Stage-CIL framework learns classes progressively through distinct morphological stages. Stage-Bench provides a 10-domain, 2-stage protocol to jointly measure inter- and intra-class forgetting. STAGE is a memory-based method that explicitly learns abstract, transferable evolution patterns, decoupling semantic identity from transformation dynamics to predict future morphologies.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation shows Stage-CIL and STAGE consistently outperform state-of-the-art methods on Stage-Bench, demonstrating improved handling of both inter-class forgetting and intra-class morphological adaptation.
AI.conclusion: Stage-CIL with STAGE offers a principled approach to learning in stage-evolving domains, enabling robust discrimination while anticipating morphology changes within classes.

title: MACD: Model-Aware Contrastive Decoding via Counterfactual Data
summary: Video language models (Video-LLMs) are prone to hallucinations, often generating plausible but ungrounded content when visual evidence is weak, ambiguous, or biased. Existing decoding methods, such as contrastive decoding (CD), rely on random perturbations to construct contrastive data for mitigating hallucination patterns. However, such a way is hard to control the visual cues that drive hallucination or well align with model weaknesses. We propose Model-aware Counterfactual Data based Contrastive Decoding (MACD), a new inference strategy that combines model-guided counterfactual construction with decoding. Our approach uses the Video-LLM's own feedback to identify object regions most responsible for hallucination, generating targeted counterfactual inputs at the object level rather than arbitrary frame or temporal modifications. These model-aware counterfactual data is then integrated into CD to enforce evidence-grounded token selection during decoding. Experiments on EventHallusion, MVBench, Perception-test and Video-MME show that MACD consistently reduces hallucination while maintaining or improving task accuracy across diverse Video-LLMs, including Qwen and InternVL families. The method is especially effective in challenging scenarios involving small, occluded, or co-occurring objects. Our code and data will be publicly released.

AI.tldr: MACD introduces model-aware counterfactual data to improve contrastive decoding for Video-LLMs, reducing hallucinations by targeting object-level cues.
AI.motivation: Video-LLMs hallucinate when visual evidence is weak/ambiguous; existing contrastive decoding (CD) uses random perturbations that poorly target model weaknesses; there is a need for controlled, model-guided counterfactual inputs.
AI.method: Leverage the Video-LLM's own feedback to identify object regions most responsible for hallucination; generate targeted counterfactual inputs at the object level; integrate these counterfactuals into CD to enforce evidence-grounded token selection during decoding.
AI.result: MACD reduces hallucinations across multiple Video-LLMs and benchmarks (EventHallusion, MVBench, Perception-test, Video-MME) while maintaining or improving task accuracy; effective for small, occluded, or co-occurring objects; demonstrates generalization across Qwen and InternVL families.
AI.conclusion: MACD offers a principled framework combining model-guided counterfactuals with contrastive decoding; code and data will be publicly released; approach effectively targets specific hallucination sources and shows robustness across diverse scenarios.

title: Data Distribution as a Lever for Guiding Optimizers Toward Superior Generalization in LLMs
summary: Can modifying the training data distribution guide optimizers toward solutions with improved generalization when training large language models (LLMs)? In this work, we theoretically analyze an in-context linear regression model with multi-head linear self-attention, and compare the training dynamics of two gradient based optimizers, namely gradient descent (GD) and sharpness-aware minimization (SAM), the latter exhibiting superior generalization properties but is prohibitively expensive for training even medium-sized LLMs. We show, for the first time, that SAM induces a lower simplicity bias (SB)-the tendency of an optimizer to preferentially learn simpler features earlier in training-and identify this reduction as a key factor underlying its improved generalization performance. Motivated by this insight, we demonstrate that altering the training data distribution by upsampling or augmenting examples learned later in training similarly reduces SB and leads to improved generalization. Our extensive experiments show that our strategy improves the performance of multiple LLMs-including Phi2-2.7B , Llama3.2-1B, Gemma3-1B-PT, and Qwen3-0.6B-Base-achieving relative accuracy gains up to 18% when fine-tuned with AdamW and Muon on mathematical reasoning tasks.

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes gradient descent (GD) vs sharpness-aware minimization (SAM) in an in-context linear regression model with multi-head linear self-attention, showing SAM reduces a simplicity bias (SB) and improves generalization; further, upsampling or augmenting later-learned examples during training can mimic this SB reduction and boost generalization, with up to 18% relative accuracy gains on math-reasoning tasks across several LLMs.
AI.motivation: To understand whether training-data distribution modifications can steer optimizers toward better generalization in large language models, and to identify mechanisms (simplicity bias) underlying these gains, given that SAM is effective but computationally expensive.
AI.method: Theoretical analysis of an in-context linear regression model with multi-head linear self-attention; compare training dynamics of gradient descent (GD) and sharpness-aware minimization (SAM); quantify simplicity bias (SB); investigate data distribution shifts via upsampling/augmentation of examples learned later in training; extensive empirical evaluation across multiple LLMs (Phi2-2.7B, Llama3.2-1B, Gemma3-1B-PT, Qwen3-0.6B-Base) fine-tuned with AdamW and Muon on mathematical reasoning tasks.
AI.result: SAM induces a lower SB, correlating with improved generalization; altering training data distribution by upsampling or augmenting later-learned examples reduces SB and yields better generalization; across models, the proposed data-distribution strategy achieves up to 18% relative accuracy gains on math reasoning tasks.
AI.conclusion: Simplicity bias is a key driver of generalization behavior in these settings. Reducing SB—either via SAM or by data-distribution modifications—improves generalization and is achievable with more practical training regimes (data reweighting), with demonstrated gains across diverse LLMs on mathematical reasoning tasks.

title: Controlling Exploration-Exploitation in GFlowNets via Markov Chain Perspectives
summary: Generative Flow Network (GFlowNet) objectives implicitly fix an equal mixing of forward and backward policies, potentially constraining the exploration-exploitation trade-off during training. By further exploring the link between GFlowNets and Markov chains, we establish an equivalence between GFlowNet objectives and Markov chain reversibility, thereby revealing the origin of such constraints, and provide a framework for adapting Markov chain properties to GFlowNets. Building on these theoretical findings, we propose $α$-GFNs, which generalize the mixing via a tunable parameter $α$. This generalization enables direct control over exploration-exploitation dynamics to enhance mode discovery capabilities, while ensuring convergence to unique flows. Across various benchmarks, including Set, Bit Sequence, and Molecule Generation, $α$-GFN objectives consistently outperform previous GFlowNet objectives, achieving up to a $10 \times$ increase in the number of discovered modes.

AI.tldr: α-GFN generalizes GFlowNets with a tunable α that controls forward/backward mixing via a Markov chain reversibility perspective, improving mode discovery while preserving convergence.
AI.motivation: Standard GFlowNets enforce equal mixing of forward and backward policies, which may restrict exploration-exploitation. Linking GFlowNets to Markov chain reversibility suggests a principled way to modulate this trade-off.
AI.method: Proves an equivalence between GFlowNet objectives and Markov chain reversibility; introduces α-GFN with a mixing parameter α; provides theoretical guarantees of convergence to unique flows; evaluates on Set, Bit Sequence, and Molecule Generation.
AI.result: α-GFN outperforms prior GFlowNets across benchmarks, achieving up to 10× more discovered modes.
AI.conclusion: The tunable α parameter enables direct control over exploration-exploitation in GFlowNets, enhances mode discovery, and offers a general framework for integrating Markov chain properties into GFlowNets, while preserving convergence to unique flows.

title: Sparsity-Aware Unlearning for Large Language Models
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) inevitably memorize sensitive information during training, posing significant privacy risks. Machine unlearning has emerged as a promising solution to selectively remove such information without full retraining. However, existing methods are designed for dense models and overlook model sparsification-an essential technique for efficient LLM deployment. We find that unlearning effectiveness degrades substantially on sparse models. Through empirical analysis, we reveal that this degradation occurs because existing unlearning methods require updating all parameters, yet sparsification prunes substantial weights to zero, fundamentally limiting the model's forgetting capacity. To address this challenge, we propose Sparsity-Aware Unlearning (SAU), which decouples unlearning from sparsification objectives through gradient masking that redirects updates to surviving weights, combined with importance-aware redistribution to compensate for pruned parameters. Extensive experiments demonstrate that SAU significantly outperforms existing methods on sparse LLMs, achieving effective forgetting while preserving model utility.

AI.tldr: SAU is a sparsity-aware unlearning method for sparse LLMs that uses gradient masking and importance-aware redistribution to decouple unlearning from sparsification, improving forgetting while preserving utility.
AI.motivation: LLMs memorize sensitive data; unlearning is needed for privacy; existing unlearning methods assume dense models and perform poorly when models are sparsified; sparsification is essential for efficient LLM deployment, creating a gap in forgetting capability.
AI.method: Introduce Sparsity-Aware Unlearning (SAU): use gradient masking to redirect parameter updates to surviving weights and apply importance-aware redistribution to compensate for pruned parameters; decouples the unlearning objective from sparsification.
AI.result: SAU significantly outperforms existing unlearning methods on sparse LLMs, achieving effective forgetting with preserved model utility.
AI.conclusion: SAU bridges the gap for unlearning in sparsified models, enabling practical, privacy-preserving deployment of sparse LLMs; future work may explore broader datasets, deeper sparsity regimes, and computational trade-offs.

title: Adversarial Reward Auditing for Active Detection and Mitigation of Reward Hacking
summary: Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) remains vulnerable to reward hacking, where models exploit spurious correlations in learned reward models to achieve high scores while violating human intent. Existing mitigations rely on static defenses that cannot adapt to novel exploitation strategies. We propose Adversarial Reward Auditing (ARA), a framework that reconceptualizes reward hacking as a dynamic, competitive game. ARA operates in two stages: first, a Hacker policy discovers reward model vulnerabilities while an Auditor learns to detect exploitation from latent representations; second, Auditor-Guided RLHF (AG-RLHF) gates reward signals to penalize detected hacking, transforming reward hacking from an unobservable failure into a measurable, controllable signal. Experiments across three hacking scenarios demonstrate that ARA achieves the best alignment-utility tradeoff among all baselines: reducing sycophancy to near-SFT levels while improving helpfulness, decreasing verbosity while achieving the highest ROUGE-L, and suppressing code gaming while improving Pass@1. Beyond single-domain evaluation, we show that reward hacking, detection, and mitigation all generalize across domains -- a Hacker trained on code gaming exhibits increased sycophancy despite no reward for this behavior, and an Auditor trained on one domain effectively suppresses exploitation in others, enabling efficient multi-domain defense with a single model.

AI.tldr: ARA reframes reward hacking as a dynamic adversarial game, using an Auditor to detect exploits and Auditor-Guided RLHF to gate rewards, yielding strong alignment and cross-domain robustness.
AI.motivation: RLHF is vulnerable to reward hacking due to spurious reward-model correlations and static defenses; adaptive defenses that learn to detect and mitigate novel exploits are needed.
AI.method: Two-stage framework: (1) Hacker discovers reward-model vulnerabilities in latent space; (2) Auditor learns to detect exploitation from latent representations. Auditor-Guided RLHF (AG-RLHF) then gates reward signals to penalize detected hacking, turning unobservable failures into a measurable signal.
AI.result: ARA achieves the best alignment-utility tradeoff across three hacking scenarios: reduces sycophancy to near-SFT levels while improving helpfulness and achieving highest ROUGE-L with reduced verbosity; suppresses code gaming while improving Pass@1. Cross-domain results show that a Hacker trained on one domain increases sycophancy in another, and an Auditor trained in one domain suppresses exploitation in others, enabling efficient multi-domain defense with a single model.
AI.conclusion: Dynamic auditing-based defense improves RLHF robustness to reward hacking by turning exploitation into a controllable signal, achieving superior alignment-utility and cross-domain generalization compared with baselines.

title: Bridging Time and Frequency: A Joint Modeling Framework for Irregular Multivariate Time Series Forecasting
summary: Irregular multivariate time series forecasting (IMTSF) is challenging due to non-uniform sampling and variable asynchronicity. These irregularities violate the equidistant assumptions of standard models, hindering local temporal modeling and rendering classical frequency-domain methods ineffective for capturing global periodic structures. To address this challenge, we propose TFMixer, a joint time-frequency modeling framework for IMTS forecasting. Specifically, TFMixer incorporates a Global Frequency Module that employs a learnable Non-Uniform Discrete Fourier Transform (NUDFT) to directly extract spectral representations from irregular timestamps. In parallel, the Local Time Module introduces a query-based patch mixing mechanism to adaptively aggregate informative temporal patches and alleviate information density imbalance. Finally, TFMixer fuses the time-domain and frequency-domain representations to generate forecasts and further leverages inverse NUDFT for explicit seasonal extrapolation. Extensive experiments on real-world datasets demonstrate the state--of-the-art performance of TFMixer.

AI.tldr: TFMixer is a joint time-frequency model for irregular multivariate time series forecasting that uses a learnable NUDFT-based Global Frequency Module and a query-based Local Time Module to capture global seasonality and local patterns, fusing both domains and enabling explicit seasonal extrapolation via inverse NUDFT to achieve state-of-the-art results on real-world data.
AI.motivation: Irregular sampling and variable asynchronicity in IMTS violate the equidistant assumption of standard models, impeding local temporal modeling and limiting the effectiveness of traditional frequency-domain methods for capturing global periodic structures.
AI.method: Propose TFMixer with a Global Frequency Module using a learnable Non-Uniform Discrete Fourier Transform (NUDFT) to extract spectral representations from irregular timestamps, and a Local Time Module with a query-based patch mixing mechanism to adaptively aggregate informative temporal patches. The model fuses time-domain and frequency-domain representations for forecasting and uses inverse NUDFT for explicit seasonal extrapolation.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on real-world datasets demonstrate state-of-the-art performance of TFMixer.
AI.conclusion: TFMixer offers an effective joint time-frequency framework for IMTS forecasting, addressing irregular sampling and asynchronicity, with explicit seasonal extrapolation via inverse NUDFT and strong empirical performance.

title: Safe Langevin Soft Actor Critic
summary: Balancing reward and safety in constrained reinforcement learning remains challenging due to poor generalization from sharp value minima and inadequate handling of heavy-tailed risk distribution. We introduce Safe Langevin Soft Actor-Critic (SL-SAC), a principled algorithm that addresses both issues through parameter-space exploration and distributional risk control. Our approach combines three key mechanisms: (1) Adaptive Stochastic Gradient Langevin Dynamics (aSGLD) for reward critics, promoting ensemble diversity and escape from poor optima; (2) distributional cost estimation via Implicit Quantile Networks (IQN) with Conditional Value-at-Risk (CVaR) optimization for tail-risk mitigation; and (3) a reactive Lagrangian relaxation scheme that adapts constraint enforcement based on the empirical CVaR of episodic costs. We provide theoretical guarantees on CVaR estimation error and demonstrate that CVaR-based Lagrange updates yield stronger constraint violation signals than expected-cost updates. On Safety-Gymnasium benchmarks, SL-SAC achieves the lowest cost in 7 out of 10 tasks while maintaining competitive returns, with cost reductions of 19-63% in velocity tasks compared to state-of-the-art baselines.

AI.tldr: SL-SAC: Safe Langevin Soft Actor-Critic, a principled constrained RL algorithm that combines adaptive SGLD-based reward-critic exploration, IQN-based distributional risk estimation with CVaR optimization, and reactive CVaR-driven Lagrangian constraint updates. It achieves strong safety performance (lower cost) on Safety-Gymnasium while keeping competitive returns.
AI.motivation: To address two intertwined challenges in constrained RL: poor generalization due to sharp minima in value landscapes and heavy-tailed risk in episodic costs, which undermine safety guarantees and constraint satisfaction.
AI.method: Three components: (1) Adaptive Stochastic Gradient Langevin Dynamics (aSGLD) for reward critics to promote ensemble diversity and escape poor optima; (2) distributional cost estimation via Implicit Quantile Networks (IQN) with CVaR optimization to mitigate tail risk; (3) reactive Lagrangian relaxation that adapts constraint enforcement using the empirical CVaR of episodic costs.
AI.result: Theoretical guarantees on CVaR estimation error; CVaR-based Lagrange updates provide stronger constraint violation signals than expected-cost updates. Empirically, SL-SAC achieves the lowest cost in 7/10 Safety-Gymnasium tasks, maintains competitive returns, and reports 19–63% cost reductions in velocity tasks versus state-of-the-art baselines.
AI.conclusion: SL-SAC presents a coherent integration of exploration, distributional risk control, and adaptive constraint enforcement for safer constrained RL. The combination yields improved tail-risk handling and constraint signaling, with strong empirical safety gains on benchmark tasks.

title: Efficient Cross-Architecture Knowledge Transfer for Large-Scale Online User Response Prediction
summary: Deploying new architectures in large-scale user response prediction systems incurs high model switching costs due to expensive retraining on massive historical data and performance degradation under data retention constraints. Existing knowledge distillation methods struggle with architectural heterogeneity and the prohibitive cost of transferring large embedding tables. We propose CrossAdapt, a two-stage framework for efficient cross-architecture knowledge transfer. The offline stage enables rapid embedding transfer via dimension-adaptive projections without iterative training, combined with progressive network distillation and strategic sampling to reduce computational cost. The online stage introduces asymmetric co-distillation, where students update frequently while teachers update infrequently, together with a distribution-aware adaptation mechanism that dynamically balances historical knowledge preservation and fast adaptation to evolving data. Experiments on three public datasets show that CrossAdapt achieves 0.27-0.43% AUC improvements while reducing training time by 43-71%. Large-scale deployment on Tencent WeChat Channels (~10M daily samples) further demonstrates its effectiveness, significantly mitigating AUC degradation, LogLoss increase, and prediction bias compared to standard distillation baselines.

AI.tldr: CrossAdapt is a two-stage cross-architecture knowledge transfer framework that enables rapid embedding transfer and online asymmetric distillation to reduce retraining costs in large-scale response prediction systems, achieving measurable AUC gains and significant training-time reductions, validated on public datasets and a large Tencent deployment.
AI.motivation: Deploying new architectures in large-scale user response prediction incurs high switching costs due to retraining on massive historical data, data retention constraints, architectural heterogeneity, and embedding-table transfer costs.
AI.method: Two-stage design: offline stage applies dimension-adaptive projections for rapid embedding transfer without iterative training, plus progressive network distillation and strategic sampling to cut compute; online stage uses asymmetric co-distillation (frequent student updates, infrequent teacher updates) and a distribution-aware adaptation mechanism to balance past knowledge with fast adaptation to evolving data.
AI.result: On three public datasets, CrossAdapt yields 0.27-0.43% absolute AUC improvement and 43-71% reduction in training time. A large-scale Tencent WeChat Channels deployment (~10M daily samples) shows substantial mitigation of AUC degradation, LogLoss increase, and prediction bias compared with standard distillation baselines.
AI.conclusion: CrossAdapt offers an effective cross-architecture transfer strategy that reduces retraining costs while preserving or improving predictive performance, demonstrated with both public datasets and large-scale real-world deployment.

title: SEER: Transformer-based Robust Time Series Forecasting via Automated Patch Enhancement and Replacement
summary: Time series forecasting is important in many fields that require accurate predictions for decision-making. Patching techniques, commonly used and effective in time series modeling, help capture temporal dependencies by dividing the data into patches. However, existing patch-based methods fail to dynamically select patches and typically use all patches during the prediction process. In real-world time series, there are often low-quality issues during data collection, such as missing values, distribution shifts, anomalies and white noise, which may cause some patches to contain low-quality information, negatively impacting the prediction results. To address this issue, this study proposes a robust time series forecasting framework called SEER. Firstly, we propose an Augmented Embedding Module, which improves patch-wise representations using a Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architecture and obtains series-wise token representations through a channel-adaptive perception mechanism. Secondly, we introduce a Learnable Patch Replacement Module, which enhances forecasting robustness and model accuracy through a two-stage process: 1) a dynamic filtering mechanism eliminates negative patch-wise tokens; 2) a replaced attention module substitutes the identified low-quality patches with global series-wise token, further refining their representations through a causal attention mechanism. Comprehensive experimental results demonstrate the SOTA performance of SEER.

AI.tldr: SEER introduces robust patch-based time series forecasting via an Augmented Embedding Module with Mixture-of-Experts and channel-adaptive perception, and a Learnable Patch Replacement Module that dynamically filters and replaces low-quality patches with a global series token using causal attention, claiming state-of-the-art results.
AI.motivation: Real-world time series often contain missing values, distribution shifts, anomalies, and noise, making some patches low-quality and potentially degrading predictions. A dynamic, robust patch selection and aggregation mechanism is needed beyond traditional patch-based methods.
AI.method: 1) Augmented Embedding Module uses a Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) to enhance patch-wise representations and a channel-adaptive perception to obtain series-wise tokens. 2) Learnable Patch Replacement Module operates in two stages: (a) dynamic filtering to remove negative patch-wise tokens; (b) replaced attention that substitutes these patches with a global series-wise token and refines via causal attention.
AI.result: Experimental results show SEER achieves state-of-the-art performance on the tested benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: SEER provides a robust, patch-based forecasting framework that improves accuracy and robustness by dynamic patch handling and MoE-based representations, with a two-stage patch filtering/replacement mechanism and causal attention for refined predictions. Further details (datasets, baselines, ablations) are needed for full assessment.

title: LingLanMiDian: Systematic Evaluation of LLMs on TCM Knowledge and Clinical Reasoning
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are advancing rapidly in medical NLP, yet Traditional Chinese Medicine (TCM) with its distinctive ontology, terminology, and reasoning patterns requires domain-faithful evaluation. Existing TCM benchmarks are fragmented in coverage and scale and rely on non-unified or generation-heavy scoring that hinders fair comparison. We present the LingLanMiDian (LingLan) benchmark, a large-scale, expert-curated, multi-task suite that unifies evaluation across knowledge recall, multi-hop reasoning, information extraction, and real-world clinical decision-making. LingLan introduces a consistent metric design, a synonym-tolerant protocol for clinical labels, a per-dataset 400-item Hard subset, and a reframing of diagnosis and treatment recommendation into single-choice decision recognition. We conduct comprehensive, zero-shot evaluations on 14 leading open-source and proprietary LLMs, providing a unified perspective on their strengths and limitations in TCM commonsense knowledge understanding, reasoning, and clinical decision support; critically, the evaluation on Hard subset reveals a substantial gap between current models and human experts in TCM-specialized reasoning. By bridging fundamental knowledge and applied reasoning through standardized evaluation, LingLan establishes a unified, quantitative, and extensible foundation for advancing TCM LLMs and domain-specific medical AI research. All evaluation data and code are available at https://github.com/TCMAI-BJTU/LingLan and http://tcmnlp.com.

AI.tldr: A large-scale, expert-curated stem LingLan benchmark for Traditional Chinese Medicine LLM evaluation, unifying knowledge recall, reasoning, and clinical decision tasks with a hard subset and standardized metrics; reveals substantial gaps between current LLMs and human experts in TCM reasoning.
AI.motivation: TCM has a distinctive ontology and terminology; existing benchmarks are fragmented and generation-heavy, hindering fair, domain-faithful evaluation. A unified, scalable benchmark is needed to assess LLMs' knowledge and reasoning in TCM.
AI.method: Construct LingLanMiDian (LingLan) as a multi-task benchmark with unified metrics, a synonym-tolerant protocol for clinical labels, a per-dataset 400-item Hard subset, and reframing diagnosis/treatment into single-choice decision recognition. Conduct zero-shot evaluation on 14 open-source and proprietary LLMs.
AI.result: Comprehensive zero-shot results across models reveal strengths and limitations in TCM commonsense knowledge, reasoning, and clinical decision support. The Hard subset demonstrates a significant gap between current models and human experts in TCM-specialized reasoning.
AI.conclusion: LingLan provides a unified, quantitative, extensible foundation for advancing TCM LLMs and domain-specific medical AI. All evaluation data and code are publicly available for reproducibility.

title: Kernelized Edge Attention: Addressing Semantic Attention Blurring in Temporal Graph Neural Networks
summary: Temporal Graph Neural Networks (TGNNs) aim to capture the evolving structure and timing of interactions in dynamic graphs. Although many models incorporate time through encodings or architectural design, they often compute attention over entangled node and edge representations, failing to reflect their distinct temporal behaviors. Node embeddings evolve slowly as they aggregate long-term structural context, while edge features reflect transient, timestamped interactions (e.g. messages, trades, or transactions). This mismatch results in semantic attention blurring, where attention weights cannot distinguish between slowly drifting node states and rapidly changing, information-rich edge interactions. As a result, models struggle to capture fine-grained temporal dependencies and provide limited transparency into how temporal relevance is computed. This paper introduces KEAT (Kernelized Edge Attention for Temporal Graphs), a novel attention formulation that modulates edge features using a family of continuous-time kernels, including Laplacian, RBF, and learnable MLP variant. KEAT preserves the distinct roles of nodes and edges, and integrates seamlessly with both Transformer-style (e.g., DyGFormer) and message-passing (e.g., TGN) architectures. It achieves up to 18% MRR improvement over the recent DyGFormer and 7% over TGN on link prediction tasks, enabling more accurate, interpretable and temporally aware message passing in TGNNs.

AI.tldr: KEAT introduces Kernelized Edge Attention to temporal graphs, modulating edge features with continuous-time kernels to distinguish node vs edge temporal dynamics, improving MRR on link prediction by up to 18% (vs DyGFormer) and 7% (vs TGN).
AI.motivation: Semantic attention blurring arises when node and edge representations with different temporal dynamics are entangled, hindering temporal sensitivity and interpretability in TGNNs.
AI.method: Propose KEAT, which modulates edge features using a family of continuous-time kernels (Laplacian, RBF, and a learnable MLP variant). This preserves distinct node and edge roles and is compatible with Transformer-style (e.g., DyGFormer) and message-passing (e.g., TGN) architectures.
AI.result: Empirical gains on link prediction tasks: up to 18% MRR improvement over DyGFormer and 7% over TGN, indicating better temporal sensitivity and interpretability.
AI.conclusion: KEAT enables more accurate, interpretable, and temporally aware message passing in TGNNs without sacrificing compatibility with existing architectures.

title: Direct Preference Optimization with Rating Information: Practical Algorithms and Provable Gains
summary: The class of direct preference optimization (DPO) algorithms has emerged as a promising approach for solving the alignment problem in foundation models. These algorithms work with very limited feedback in the form of pairwise preferences and fine-tune models to align with these preferences without explicitly learning a reward model. While the form of feedback used by these algorithms makes the data collection process easy and relatively more accurate, its ambiguity in terms of the quality of responses could have negative implications. For example, it is not clear if a decrease (increase) in the likelihood of preferred (dispreferred) responses during the execution of these algorithms could be interpreted as a positive or negative phenomenon. In this paper, we study how to design algorithms that can leverage additional information in the form of rating gap, which informs the learner how much the chosen response is better than the rejected one. We present new algorithms that can achieve faster statistical rates than DPO in presence of accurate rating gap information. Moreover, we theoretically prove and empirically show that the performance of our algorithms is robust to inaccuracy in rating gaps. Finally, we demonstrate the solid performance of our methods in comparison to a number of DPO-style algorithms across a wide range of LLMs and evaluation benchmarks.

AI.tldr: The paper extends direct preference optimization (DPO) with rating-gap feedback, yielding faster statistical rates when gaps are accurate and robustness to gap-noise, with strong empirical performance across LLMs and benchmarks.
AI.motivation: DPO relies on pairwise preferences with limited information, making it unclear how to interpret changes in likelihood of preferred vs. dispreferred responses. Rating gaps quantify how much better the chosen response is, enabling more informative learning signals.
AI.method: Proposes rating-gap–aware algorithms that leverage the magnitude of the gap between the chosen and rejected responses. Provides theoretical analyses showing faster statistical rates than DPO under accurate gap information and robustness analyses to gap inaccuracies. Performs empirical evaluations comparing the new methods to several DPO-style baselines across multiple LLMs and evaluation benchmarks.
AI.result: The algorithms achieve faster convergence (statistical rates) when rating gaps are accurate. They remain robust to inaccuracies in rating gaps and demonstrate solid performance improvements over various DPO-style baselines across diverse LLMs and benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating rating-gap information into preference-based alignment improves data efficiency and robustness, offering a practical path to faster, more reliable alignment of foundation models. Future work may explore tighter robustness guarantees and broader benchmarking.

title: INDIBATOR: Diverse and Fact-Grounded Individuality for Multi-Agent Debate in Molecular Discovery
summary: Multi-agent systems have emerged as a powerful paradigm for automating scientific discovery. To differentiate agent behavior in the multi-agent system, current frameworks typically assign generic role-based personas such as ''reviewer'' or ''writer'' or rely on coarse grained keyword-based personas. While functional, this approach oversimplifies how human scientists operate, whose contributions are shaped by their unique research trajectories. In response, we propose INDIBATOR, a framework for molecular discovery that grounds agents in individualized scientist profiles constructed from two modalities: publication history for literature-derived knowledge and molecular history for structural priors. These agents engage in multi-turn debate through proposal, critique, and voting phases. Our evaluation demonstrates that these fine-grained individuality-grounded agents consistently outperform systems relying on coarse-grained personas, achieving competitive or state-of-the-art performance. These results validate that capturing the ``scientific DNA'' of individual agents is essential for high-quality discovery.

AI.tldr: INDIBATOR grounds multi-agent molecular discovery in individualized scientist profiles built from publication history and molecular history; agents debate via proposal/critique/voting, yielding performance surpassing coarse-grained personas and approaching state-of-the-art.
AI.motivation: Current agent frameworks use generic role-based personas, which fail to capture the nuanced, trajectory-driven behavior of real scientists. This limited granularity can constrain discovery quality.
AI.method: Construct agents anchored to two modalities: (1) publication history for literature-derived knowledge, and (2) molecular history as structural priors. Agents engage in structured multi-turn debate—proposal, critique, and voting phases—to advance discoveries within a molecular discovery framework called INDIBATOR.
AI.result: Fine-grained, individuality-grounded agents outperform systems with coarse-grained personas, achieving competitive or state-of-the-art performance across the evaluated tasks.
AI.conclusion: Capturing the unique 'scientific DNA' of individual agents is essential for high-quality scientific discovery in multi-agent settings.

title: Actor-Dual-Critic Dynamics for Zero-sum and Identical-Interest Stochastic Games
summary: We propose a novel independent and payoff-based learning framework for stochastic games that is model-free, game-agnostic, and gradient-free. The learning dynamics follow a best-response-type actor-critic architecture, where agents update their strategies (actors) using feedback from two distinct critics: a fast critic that intuitively responds to observed payoffs under limited information, and a slow critic that deliberatively approximates the solution to the underlying dynamic programming problem. Crucially, the learning process relies on non-equilibrium adaptation through smoothed best responses to observed payoffs. We establish convergence to (approximate) equilibria in two-agent zero-sum and multi-agent identical-interest stochastic games over an infinite horizon. This provides one of the first payoff-based and fully decentralized learning algorithms with theoretical guarantees in both settings. Empirical results further validate the robustness and effectiveness of the proposed approach across both classes of games.

AI.tldr: A model-free, gradient-free payoff-based learning framework for stochastic games using a dual-critic, best-response actor-critic with smoothed, non-equilibrium adaptation, achieving convergence to approximate equilibria in two-agent zero-sum and multi-agent identical-interest games, with full decentralization and empirical validation.
AI.motivation: To achieve payoff-based, decentralized, model-free learning with theoretical convergence guarantees in stochastic games, addressing limitations of gradient-based or model-dependent approaches and enabling game-agnostic applicability across two-class game settings.
AI.method: Independent, payoff-based learning framework with two critics: a fast payoff-responsive critic and a slow DP-approximating critic; a best-response-type, gradient-free actor-critic update using smoothed best responses for non-equilibrium adaptation; fully decentralized, applicable to infinite-horizon stochastic games.
AI.result: Proves convergence to (approximate) equilibria in two-agent zero-sum and multi-agent identical-interest stochastic games; establishes theoretical guarantees for a payoff-based, decentralized learning algorithm; empirical experiments corroborate robustness and effectiveness.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework delivers a novel, fully decentralized, model-free, gradient-free learning paradigm with theoretical convergence guarantees for key stochastic game classes, supported by empirical validation.

title: Synesthesia of Vehicles: Tactile Data Synthesis from Visual Inputs
summary: Autonomous vehicles (AVs) rely on multi-modal fusion for safety, but current visual and optical sensors fail to detect road-induced excitations which are critical for vehicles' dynamic control. Inspired by human synesthesia, we propose the Synesthesia of Vehicles (SoV), a novel framework to predict tactile excitations from visual inputs for autonomous vehicles. We develop a cross-modal spatiotemporal alignment method to address temporal and spatial disparities. Furthermore, a visual-tactile synesthetic (VTSyn) generative model using latent diffusion is proposed for unsupervised high-quality tactile data synthesis. A real-vehicle perception system collected a multi-modal dataset across diverse road and lighting conditions. Extensive experiments show that VTSyn outperforms existing models in temporal, frequency, and classification performance, enhancing AV safety through proactive tactile perception.

AI.tldr: SoV introduces a cross-modal synesthetic pipeline to predict tactile excitations from vision for autonomous vehicles, using cross-modal alignment and a diffusion-based VTSyn model, validated on real-vehicle data with improved temporal, frequency, and classification metrics.
AI.motivation: Address a safety-critical gap where road-induced tactile excitations are not captured by conventional vision/sensor fusion. Inspired by synesthesia to enable vision-to-tactile cross-modal perception for proactive AV control.
AI.method: Develop a cross-modal spatiotemporal alignment to bridge temporal and spatial disparities between visual inputs and tactile states. Propose a visual-tactile synesthetic (VTSyn) generative model based on latent diffusion for unsupervised synthesis of high-quality tactile data from visual cues. Build and evaluate on a real-vehicle multi-modal dataset collected under diverse road and lighting conditions.
AI.result: VTSyn and the alignment framework outperform existing models across temporal, frequency, and classification tasks, demonstrating improved tactile perception from visual data and potential safety benefits for autonomous driving.
AI.conclusion: The proposed SoV framework validates the feasibility of vision-to-tactile synesthesia for AV perception, enabling unsupervised tactile data generation and cross-modal prediction that enhance safety; future work may explore generalization, real-time deployment, and broader sensory mappings.

title: Rethinking Zero-Shot Time Series Classification: From Task-specific Classifiers to In-Context Inference
summary: The zero-shot evaluation of time series foundation models (TSFMs) for classification typically uses a frozen encoder followed by a task-specific classifier. However, this practice violates the training-free premise of zero-shot deployment and introduces evaluation bias due to classifier-dependent training choices. To address this issue, we propose TIC-FM, an in-context learning framework that treats the labeled training set as context and predicts labels for all test instances in a single forward pass, without parameter updates. TIC-FM pairs a time series encoder and a lightweight projection adapter with a split-masked latent memory Transformer. We further provide theoretical justification that in-context inference can subsume trained classifiers and can emulate gradient-based classifier training within a single forward pass. Experiments on 128 UCR datasets show strong accuracy, with consistent gains in the extreme low-label situation, highlighting training-free transfer

AI.tldr: Proposes TIC-FM, a training-free in-context learning framework for time series foundation models that uses a labeled training set as context and a split-masked latent memory Transformer to classify test instances in a single forward pass, with theoretical backing and strong UCR results.
AI.motivation: The standard practice of using a frozen encoder with a task-specific classifier introduces evaluation bias and violates the training-free premise of zero-shot deployment; a training-free transfer approach for TSFMs is needed, especially in low-label settings.
AI.method: An encoder plus lightweight projection adapter paired with a split-masked latent memory Transformer. In-context inference uses the labeled training set as context to predict labels for all test instances in one forward pass, without any parameter updates. The authors also provide theoretical justification that in-context inference can subsume trained classifiers and emulate gradient-based training.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on 128 UCR datasets showing strong accuracy, with consistent gains in extreme low-label scenarios, indicating effective training-free transfer.
AI.conclusion: In-context inference with TIC-FM enables training-free transfer for time series foundation models and can emulate traditional classifier training within a single forward pass.

title: ROMA: Recursive Open Meta-Agent Framework for Long-Horizon Multi-Agent Systems
summary: Current agentic frameworks underperform on long-horizon tasks. As reasoning depth increases, sequential orchestration becomes brittle, context windows impose hard limits that degrade performance, and opaque execution traces make failures difficult to localize or debug. We introduce ROMA (Recursive Open Meta-Agents), a domain-agnostic framework that addresses these limitations through recursive task decomposition and structured aggregation. ROMA decomposes goals into dependency-aware subtask trees that can be executed in parallel, while aggregation compresses and validates intermediate results to control context growth. Our framework standardizes agent construction around four modular roles --Atomizer (which decides whether a task should be decomposed), Planner, Executor, and Aggregator -- which cleanly separate orchestration from model selection and enable transparent, hierarchical execution traces. This design supports heterogeneous multi-agent systems that mix models and tools according to cost, latency, and capability. To adapt ROMA to specific tasks without fine-tuning, we further introduce GEPA$+$, an improved Genetic-Pareto prompt proposer that searches over prompts within ROMA's component hierarchy while preserving interface contracts. We show that ROMA, combined with GEPA+, delivers leading system-level performance on reasoning and long-form generation benchmarks. On SEAL-0, which evaluates reasoning over conflicting web evidence, ROMA instantiated with GLM-4.6 improves accuracy by 9.9\% over Kimi-Researcher. On EQ-Bench, a long-form writing benchmark, ROMA enables DeepSeek-V3 to match the performance of leading closed-source models such as Claude Sonnet 4.5. Our results demonstrate that recursive, modular agent architectures can scale reasoning depth while remaining interpretable, flexible, and model-agnostic.

AI.tldr: ROMA is a recursive, modular agent framework for long-horizon tasks that decomposes goals into dependency-aware subtasks executed in parallel, with aggregation to control context growth. It is built around four roles (Atomizer, Planner, Executor, Aggregator) and supports heterogeneous models/tools. GEPA+ further optimizes prompts within ROMA's hierarchy. It achieves state-of-the-art results on reasoning and long-form generation benchmarks (SEAL-0 and EQ-Bench).
AI.motivation: Current agentic frameworks struggle with long-horizon tasks due to brittle reasoning depth, fixed context windows, and opaque failure traces. There is a need for scalable, interpretable, and model-agnostic architectures that can manage complexity and heterogeneous tools over long sequences.
AI.method: ROMA decomposes goals into dependency-aware subtask trees that can be executed in parallel. An Aggregator compresses/validates intermediate results to limit context growth. The architecture defines four roles—Atomizer (decides if a task should be decomposed), Planner, Executor, Aggregator—separating orchestration from model selection.
