ROMA supports heterogeneous models/tools and includes GEPA+, a Genetic-Pareto prompt proposer that searches prompts within ROMA’s hierarchy while preserving interface contracts.
AI.result: ROMA combined with GEPA+ yields leading system-level performance on reasoning and long-form generation benchmarks. On SEAL-0, ROMA with GLM-4.6 improves accuracy by 9.9 percentage points over Kimi-Researcher. On EQ-Bench, DeepSeek-V3 with ROMA matches the performance of Claude Sonnet 4.5. The approach demonstrates recursive, modular agent architectures can scale reasoning depth while remaining interpretable, flexible, and model-agnostic.
AI.conclusion: Recursive, modular, and hierarchical agent architectures like ROMA can scale reasoning depth for long-horizon tasks, provide transparency through execution traces, and remain compatible with diverse models and tools.

title: MoDEx: Mixture of Depth-specific Experts for Multivariate Long-term Time Series Forecasting
summary: Multivariate long-term time series forecasting (LTSF) supports critical applications such as traffic-flow management, solar-power scheduling, and electricity-transformer monitoring. The existing LTSF paradigms follow a three-stage pipeline of embedding, backbone refinement, and long-horizon prediction. However, the behaviors of individual backbone layers remain underexplored. We introduce layer sensitivity, a gradient-based metric inspired by GradCAM and effective receptive field theory, which quantifies both positive and negative contributions of each time point to a layer's latent features. Applying this metric to a three-layer MLP backbone reveals depth-specific specialization in modeling temporal dynamics in the input sequence. Motivated by these insights, we propose MoDEx, a lightweight Mixture of Depth-specific Experts, which replaces complex backbones with depth-specific MLP experts. MoDEx achieves state-of-the-art accuracy on seven real-world benchmarks, ranking first in 78 percent of cases, while using significantly fewer parameters and computational resources. It also integrates seamlessly into transformer variants, consistently boosting their performance and demonstrating robust generalizability as an efficient and high-performance LTSF framework.

AI.tldr: Introduces layer sensitivity, a gradient-based metric for analyzing how each time point contributes to a layer's latent features in multivariate LTSF, and leverages this insight to design MoDEx, a lightweight mixture of depth-specific MLP experts that replaces heavy backbones and improves accuracy and efficiency, including compatibility with transformer-based LTSF models.
AI.motivation: Current LTSF pipelines (embedding, backbone refinement, long-horizon prediction) lack systematic understanding of how different backbone layers contribute to forecasting; there is a need for interpretability-guided architecture design and more efficient models capable of achieving state-of-the-art performance on real-world benchmarks.
AI.method: Define layer sensitivity as a gradient-based metric inspired by GradCAM and effective receptive field theory to quantify positive and negative contributions of each time point to a layer's latent features. Apply this metric to a three-layer MLP backbone to uncover depth-specific specialization in temporal modeling. Based on these insights, propose MoDEx, a lightweight Mixture of Depth-specific Experts, which uses depth-specific MLP experts to replace complex backbones. Integrate MoDEx with transformer variants to enhance their LTSF performance.
AI.result: MoDEx achieves state-of-the-art accuracy on seven real-world LTSF benchmarks, ranking first in 78% of cases, while using significantly fewer parameters and computational resources. It can be integrated into transformer-based architectures and provides robust generalizability as an efficient, high-performance LTSF framework.
AI.conclusion: Layer-sensitivity-guided design enables depth-aware specialization in backbones and motivates a compact, mixture-of-experts architecture (MoDEx) that delivers superior accuracy with lower resource requirements. The approach not only improves standalone LTSF models but also enhances transformer-based LTSF systems, suggesting broad applicability for efficient, interpretable forecasting.

title: SOPRAG: Multi-view Graph Experts Retrieval for Industrial Standard Operating Procedures
summary: Standard Operating Procedures (SOPs) are essential for ensuring operational safety and consistency in industrial environments. However, retrieving and following these procedures presents unique challenges, such as rigid proprietary structures, condition-dependent relevance, and actionable execution requirement, which standard semantic-driven Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) paradigms fail to address. Inspired by the Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) paradigm, we propose SOPRAG, a novel framework specifically designed to address the above pain points in SOP retrieval. SOPRAG replaces flat chunking with specialized Entity, Causal, and Flow graph experts to resolve industrial structural and logical complexities. To optimize and coordinate these experts, we propose a Procedure Card layer that prunes the search space to eliminate computational noise, and an LLM-Guided gating mechanism that dynamically weights these experts to align retrieval with operator intent. To address the scarcity of domain-specific data, we also introduce an automated, multi-agent workflow for benchmark construction. Extensive experiments across four industrial domains demonstrate that SOPRAG significantly outperforms strong lexical, dense, and graph-based RAG baselines in both retrieval accuracy and response utility, achieving perfect execution scores in real-world critical tasks.

AI.tldr: SOPRAG is a Mixture-of-Experts–based RAG framework for SOP retrieval, deploying specialized Entity, Causal, and Flow graph experts, a Procedure Card pruning layer, and an LLM-guided gating mechanism. It includes an automated multi-agent workflow for benchmark construction and achieves superior retrieval accuracy and execution performance across four industrial domains, outperforming lexical, dense, and graph-based baselines.
AI.motivation: Conventional semantic RAG struggles with SOP retrieval due to rigid proprietary structures, condition-dependent relevance, and the need for actionable execution. The work targets these gaps, addressing structural/logical complexity and data scarcity via automated benchmarking.
AI.method: Introduce expert modules (Entity, Causal, Flow graph) instead of flat chunking; add a Procedure Card layer to prune the search space; implement an LLM-guided gating mechanism to dynamically weight experts according to operator intent; develop an automated, multi-agent workflow to construct benchmarks; evaluate against strong lexical, dense, and graph-based RAG baselines across four industrial domains.
AI.result: Significant improvements in retrieval accuracy and response utility; achieving perfect execution scores on real-world critical tasks.
AI.conclusion: SOPRAG demonstrates that structured, expert-coordinated RAG can better capture industrial SOP semantics, aligning retrieval with operator intent and reducing computational noise, with scalable benchmarking enabling broad industrial applicability.

title: From Associations to Activations: Comparing Behavioral and Hidden-State Semantic Geometry in LLMs
summary: We investigate the extent to which an LLM's hidden-state geometry can be recovered from its behavior in psycholinguistic experiments. Across eight instruction-tuned transformer models, we run two experimental paradigms -- similarity-based forced choice and free association -- over a shared 5,000-word vocabulary, collecting 17.5M+ trials to build behavior-based similarity matrices. Using representational similarity analysis, we compare behavioral geometries to layerwise hidden-state similarity and benchmark against FastText, BERT, and cross-model consensus. We find that forced-choice behavior aligns substantially more with hidden-state geometry than free association. In a held-out-words regression, behavioral similarity (especially forced choice) predicts unseen hidden-state similarities beyond lexical baselines and cross-model consensus, indicating that behavior-only measurements retain recoverable information about internal semantic geometry. Finally, we discuss implications for the ability of behavioral tasks to uncover hidden cognitive states.

AI.tldr: Behavioral tasks reveal latent semantic geometry; forced-choice aligns with hidden-state geometry and can predict unseen similarities, indicating recoverable internal semantics from behavior.
AI.motivation: Assess whether observable behavior in psycholinguistic experiments can recover the latent semantic geometry of hidden representations in instruction-tuned transformers.
AI.method: Eight instruction-tuned transformer models; two paradigms (similarity-based forced choice and free association) across a shared 5,000-word vocabulary; 17.5M+ trials; construct behavior-based similarity matrices; use representational similarity analysis to compare behavioral geometries to layerwise hidden-state similarity; benchmark against FastText, BERT, and cross-model consensus; include a held-out-words regression to test generalization.
AI.result: Forced-choice behavior aligns substantially more with hidden-state geometry than free association; behavioral similarity (especially forced choice) predicts unseen hidden-state similarities beyond lexical baselines and cross-model consensus; indicating behavior-only measurements retain recoverable information about internal semantic geometry.
AI.conclusion: Behavioral measurements can uncover hidden cognitive states; forced-choice paradigms are particularly informative for recovering internal semantic geometry in LLMs, with implications for interpretability and cognitive-science-inspired evaluation.

title: ProcMEM: Learning Reusable Procedural Memory from Experience via Non-Parametric PPO for LLM Agents
summary: LLM-driven agents demonstrate strong performance in sequential decision-making but often rely on on-the-fly reasoning, re-deriving solutions even in recurring scenarios. This insufficient experience reuse leads to computational redundancy and execution instability. To bridge this gap, we propose ProcMEM, a framework that enables agents to autonomously learn procedural memory from interaction experiences without parameter updates. By formalizing a Skill-MDP, ProcMEM transforms passive episodic narratives into executable Skills defined by activation, execution, and termination conditions to ensure executability. To achieve reliable reusability without capability degradation, we introduce Non-Parametric PPO, which leverages semantic gradients for high-quality candidate generation and a PPO Gate for robust Skill verification. Through score-based maintenance, ProcMEM sustains compact, high-quality procedural memory. Experimental results across in-domain, cross-task, and cross-agent scenarios demonstrate that ProcMEM achieves superior reuse rates and significant performance gains with extreme memory compression. Visualized evolutionary trajectories and Skill distributions further reveal how ProcMEM transparently accumulates, refines, and reuses procedural knowledge to facilitate long-term autonomy.

AI.tldr: ProcMEM enables autonomous learning of procedural memory for LLM-driven agents via a Skill-MDP and Non-Parametric PPO, achieving reusable, high-quality skills with compact memory through score-based maintenance; shows strong reuse and performance gains in in-domain, cross-task, and cross-agent settings.
AI.motivation: LLM agents often re-derive solutions in recurring scenarios, causing computational redundancy and unstable execution due to lack of experience reuse; a reusable procedural memory could improve efficiency and robustness.
AI.method: Formalize a Skill-MDP that converts episodic narratives into executable Skills with explicit activation, execution, and termination conditions. Propose Non-Parametric PPO that uses semantic gradients to generate high-quality skill candidates and employs a PPO Gate to verify skills, ensuring robustness. Use score-based maintenance to keep memory compact while preserving quality.
AI.result: ProcMEM achieves higher skill reuse rates and notable performance gains with extreme memory compression across in-domain, cross-task, and cross-agent scenarios; visualizations show progressive accumulation and refinement of skills, supporting long-term autonomy.
AI.conclusion: ProcMEM provides transparent accumulation, refinement, and reuse of procedural knowledge, enabling long-term autonomous operation with compact, high-quality procedural memory.

title: Equilibrium of Feasible Zone and Uncertain Model in Safe Exploration
summary: Ensuring the safety of environmental exploration is a critical problem in reinforcement learning (RL). While limiting exploration to a feasible zone has become widely accepted as a way to ensure safety, key questions remain unresolved: what is the maximum feasible zone achievable through exploration, and how can it be identified? This paper, for the first time, answers these questions by revealing that the goal of safe exploration is to find the equilibrium between the feasible zone and the environment model. This conclusion is based on the understanding that these two components are interdependent: a larger feasible zone leads to a more accurate environment model, and a more accurate model, in turn, enables exploring a larger zone. We propose the first equilibrium-oriented safe exploration framework called safe equilibrium exploration (SEE), which alternates between finding the maximum feasible zone and the least uncertain model. Using a graph formulation of the uncertain model, we prove that the uncertain model obtained by SEE is monotonically refined, the feasible zones monotonically expand, and both converge to the equilibrium of safe exploration. Experiments on classic control tasks show that our algorithm successfully expands the feasible zones with zero constraint violation, and achieves the equilibrium of safe exploration within a few iterations.

AI.tldr: Safe equilibrium exploration (SEE) defines safe exploration as an equilibrium between the feasible exploration zone and the environment model, and presents an iterative SEE framework that alternates maximizing the zone and minimizing model uncertainty, with theoretical guarantees of monotonic refinement and convergence, and empirical results showing zero constraint violation on classic control tasks.
AI.motivation: Safe exploration in reinforcement learning faces a core question: what is the maximum feasible exploration zone and how to identify it. The two key interdependent components are the feasible zone ( safety boundary ) and the environment model accuracy. A larger zone improves the model and a more accurate model enables exploring a larger zone; thus, an equilibrium-driven approach may be necessary.
AI.method: Propose safe equilibrium exploration (SEE): an equilibrium-oriented framework that alternates between finding the maximum feasible zone given the current model and updating the model to be least uncertain. The uncertain model is formulated as a graph; SEE provably yields monotonically refined models, monotonically expanding feasible zones, and convergence to safe equilibrium.
AI.result: The paper provides theoretical guarantees: monotone refinement of the uncertain model, monotone expansion of feasible zones, and convergence to the safe exploration equilibrium. Empirically, SEE expands feasible zones with zero constraint violation and reaches equilibrium within a few iterations on classic control tasks.
AI.conclusion: SEE offers a principled, equilibrium-driven approach to safe RL exploration, linking exploration capacity to model certainty. It demonstrates that safety and learning progress can be simultaneously and progressively improved until an equilibrium is reached; the framework has potential for broader applicability beyond the tested tasks.

title: Entropy-Guided Data-Efficient Training for Multimodal Reasoning Reward Models
summary: Multimodal reward models are crucial for aligning multimodal large language models with human preferences. Recent works have incorporated reasoning capabilities into these models, achieving promising results. However, training these models suffers from two critical challenges: (1) the inherent noise in preference datasets, which degrades model performance, and (2) the inefficiency of conventional training methods, which ignore the differences in sample difficulty. In this paper, we identify a strong correlation between response entropy and accuracy, indicating that entropy can serve as a reliable and unsupervised proxy for annotation noise and sample difficulty. Based on this insight, we propose a novel Entropy-Guided Training (EGT) approach for multimodal reasoning reward models, which combines two strategies: (1) entropy-guided data curation to mitigate the impact of unreliable samples, and (2) an entropy-guided training strategy that progressively introduces more complex examples. Extensive experiments across three benchmarks show that the EGT-trained model consistently outperforms state-of-the-art multimodal reward models.

AI.tldr: Entropy-Guided Training (EGT) improves multimodal reasoning reward models by using response entropy for data curation and curriculum learning, achieving state-of-the-art results on three benchmarks.
AI.motivation: To address two core challenges in multimodal reward modeling: (1) the noise in preference datasets that harms performance, and (2) training inefficiency due to ignoring sample difficulty. Response entropy correlates with both annotation noise and task difficulty, providing an unsupervised signal.
AI.method: Introduce two entropy-guided strategies: (a) data curation to filter or down-weight unreliable samples based on response entropy, and (b) a curriculum-style training regime that progressively incorporates more complex examples, both applied to multimodal reasoning reward models.
AI.result: The Entropy-Guided Training approach yields models that consistently outperform state-of-the-art multimodal reward models across three benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Entropy is a practical proxy for annotation noise and sample difficulty; leveraging it for data curation and curriculum design leads to more efficient and robust multimodal reward modeling.

title: Combinatorial Bandit Bayesian Optimization for Tensor Outputs
summary: Bayesian optimization (BO) has been widely used to optimize expensive and black-box functions across various domains. Existing BO methods have not addressed tensor-output functions. To fill this gap, we propose a novel tensor-output BO method. Specifically, we first introduce a tensor-output Gaussian process (TOGP) with two classes of tensor-output kernels as a surrogate model of the tensor-output function, which can effectively capture the structural dependencies within the tensor. Based on it, we develop an upper confidence bound (UCB) acquisition function to select the queried points. Furthermore, we introduce a more complex and practical problem setting, named combinatorial bandit Bayesian optimization (CBBO), where only a subset of the outputs can be selected to contribute to the objective function. To tackle this, we propose a tensor-output CBBO method, which extends TOGP to handle partially observed outputs, and accordingly design a novel combinatorial multi-arm bandit-UCB2 (CMAB-UCB2) criterion to sequentially select both the queried points and the optimal output subset. Theoretical regret bounds for the two methods are established, ensuring their sublinear performance. Extensive synthetic and real-world experiments demonstrate their superiority.

AI.tldr: Proposes tensor-output Gaussian processes for Bayesian optimization to handle tensor-valued outputs, introducing TOGP with two tensor-output kernels and a UCB acquisition; extends to combinatorial bandit BO (CBBO) with partial outputs, proposing CMAB-UCB2 for sequential data and demonstrating sublinear regret with experiments.
AI.motivation: Address the gap in Bayesian optimization for tensor-valued (multi-dimensional) outputs and practical scenarios where only subsets of outputs can contribute to the objective, which current BO methods cannot efficiently model.
AI.method: Introduce tensor-output Gaussian processes (TOGP) with two classes of tensor-output kernels to capture intra-tensor dependencies; develop an upper confidence bound (UCB) acquisition; extend TOGP to a combinatorial bandit BO (CBBO) framework for partial outputs; design CMAB-UCB2 to select both queried inputs and the subset of outputs; derive theoretical sublinear regret bounds; validate via synthetic and real-world experiments.
AI.result: The proposed TOGP with tensor-output kernels enables principled BO over tensor-valued functions; the CBBO extension with CMAB-UCB2 handles partial outputs; theoretical regret bounds are established (sublinear); extensive experiments show superior performance over baselines.
AI.conclusion: Tensor-output BO and its combinatorial variant effectively model structured tensor outputs and partial observations, achieving sublinear regret and strong empirical performance, suggesting wide applicability in expensive black-box optimization scenarios involving multi-dimensional outputs.

title: Geometric Analysis of Token Selection in Multi-Head Attention
summary: We present a geometric framework for analysing multi-head attention in large language models (LLMs). Without altering the mechanism, we view standard attention through a top-N selection lens and study its behaviour directly in value-state space. We define geometric metrics - Precision, Recall, and F-score - to quantify separability between selected and non-selected tokens, and derive non-asymptotic bounds with explicit dependence on dimension and margin under empirically motivated assumptions (stable value norms with a compressed sink token, exponential similarity decay, and piecewise attention weight profiles). The theory predicts a small-N operating regime of strongest non-trivial separability and clarifies how sequence length and sink similarity shape the metrics. Empirically, across LLaMA-2-7B, Gemma-7B, and Mistral-7B, measurements closely track the theoretical envelopes: top-N selection sharpens separability, sink similarity correlates with Recall. We also found that in LLaMA-2-7B heads specialize into three regimes - Retriever, Mixer, Reset - with distinct geometric signatures. Overall, attention behaves as a structured geometric classifier with measurable criteria for token selection, offering head level interpretability and informing geometry-aware sparsification and design of attention in LLMs.

AI.tldr: A geometric framework reframes multi-head attention as a top-N selection problem in value-space, with metrics (Precision, Recall, F-score) and non-asymptotic bounds; empirically validated across several LLMs, revealing head regimes and implications for interpretability and geometry-aware sparsification.
AI.motivation: To understand the geometry and interpretability of multi-head attention in LLMs, quantify token-selection separability, and guide sparsification and architecture design.
AI.method: Model attention as top-N selection in value-state space; define geometric metrics (Precision, Recall, F-score) to measure separability between selected and non-selected tokens; derive non-asymptotic bounds with explicit dependence on dimension and margin under assumptions (stable value norms with a compact sink token, exponential decay of similarity, piecewise attention weights); perform empirical evaluation on LLaMA-2-7B, Gemma-7B, and Mistral-7B; analyze head behavior and regime-specific geometric signatures.
AI.result: The theory identifies a small-N regime with strongest non-trivial separability; sequence length and sink similarity shape the metrics; empirical measurements align with theoretical envelopes, with top-N sharpening separability and sink similarity correlating with Recall; LLaMA-2-7B heads split into three regimes (Retriever, Mixer, Reset) exhibiting distinct geometric signatures.
AI.conclusion: Attention acts as a structured geometric classifier with measurable token-selection criteria, enabling head-level interpretability and informing geometry-aware sparsification and the design of attention mechanisms in LLMs.

title: CoRe-Fed: Bridging Collaborative and Representation Fairness via Federated Embedding Distillation
comment: 7 pages (main content), 2 pages (references), Accepted in AAAI 2026
summary: With the proliferation of distributed data sources, Federated Learning (FL) has emerged as a key approach to enable collaborative intelligence through decentralized model training while preserving data privacy. However, conventional FL algorithms often suffer from performance disparities across clients caused by heterogeneous data distributions and unequal participation, which leads to unfair outcomes. Specifically, we focus on two core fairness challenges, i.e., representation bias, arising from misaligned client representations, and collaborative bias, stemming from inequitable contribution during aggregation, both of which degrade model performance and generalizability. To mitigate these disparities, we propose CoRe-Fed, a unified optimization framework that bridges collaborative and representation fairness via embedding-level regularization and fairness-aware aggregation. Initially, an alignment-driven mechanism promotes semantic consistency between local and global embeddings to reduce representational divergence. Subsequently, a dynamic reward-penalty-based aggregation strategy adjusts each client's weight based on participation history and embedding alignment to ensure contribution-aware aggregation. Extensive experiments across diverse models and datasets demonstrate that CoRe-Fed improves both fairness and model performance over the state-of-the-art baseline algorithms.

AI.tldr: CoRe-Fed is a federated learning framework that tackles representation bias and collaborative bias via embedding-level regularization and fairness-aware aggregation, achieving improved fairness and accuracy across models/datasets.
AI.motivation: FL suffers from performance disparities due to data heterogeneity and unequal participation. Two fairness challenges—representation bias (misaligned client representations) and collaborative bias (unequal aggregation contributions)—degrade generalization.
AI.method: Introduce alignment-driven embedding regularization to align local and global embeddings; implement dynamic reward-penalty-based aggregation that adjusts client weights based on participation history and embedding alignment; unify representation and collaboration fairness.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on diverse models and datasets show CoRe-Fed improves fairness and model performance over state-of-the-art baselines.
AI.conclusion: CoRe-Fed provides a unified, effective approach to mitigate fairness-related disparities in FL by combining representation alignment with contribution-aware aggregation; demonstrates improved generalization; potential for broad applicability.

title: DomusFM: A Foundation Model for Smart-Home Sensor Data
summary: Smart-home sensor data holds significant potential for several applications, including healthcare monitoring and assistive technologies. Existing approaches, however, face critical limitations. Supervised models require impractical amounts of labeled data. Foundation models for activity recognition focus only on inertial sensors, failing to address the unique characteristics of smart-home binary sensor events: their sparse, discrete nature combined with rich semantic associations. LLM-based approaches, while tested in this domain, still raise several issues regarding the need for natural language descriptions or prompting, and reliance on either external services or expensive hardware, making them infeasible in real-life scenarios due to privacy and cost concerns. We introduce DomusFM, the first foundation model specifically designed and pretrained for smart-home sensor data. DomusFM employs a self-supervised dual contrastive learning paradigm to capture both token-level semantic attributes and sequence-level temporal dependencies. By integrating semantic embeddings from a lightweight language model and specialized encoders for temporal patterns and binary states, DomusFM learns generalizable representations that transfer across environments and tasks related to activity and event analysis. Through leave-one-dataset-out evaluation across seven public smart-home datasets, we demonstrate that DomusFM outperforms state-of-the-art baselines on different downstream tasks, achieving superior performance even with only 5% of labeled training data available for fine-tuning. Our approach addresses data scarcity while maintaining practical deployability for real-world smart-home systems.

AI.tldr: DomusFM is a new foundation model tailored to smart-home binary sensor data, trained with self-supervised dual-contrastive learning to capture token-level semantics and sequence-level temporal patterns, achieving cross-dataset generalization and strong performance with limited labeled data.
AI.motivation: The smart-home domain suffers from data scarcity and privacy concerns: supervised methods need large labeled datasets; existing inertial-focused foundation models ignore binary sensor semantics; LLM-based approaches raise issues of prompting, reliance on external services, and cost.
AI.method: A self-supervised dual-contrastive framework that combines semantic embeddings from a lightweight language model with encoders specialized for temporal patterns and binary states to learn token- and sequence-level representations, evaluated by leave-one-dataset-out on seven public datasets.
AI.result: DomusFM outperforms state-of-the-art baselines across downstream tasks and environments, with especially strong gains when fine-tuned with as little as 5% labeled data.
AI.conclusion: DomusFM provides a generalizable, privacy-preserving foundation model for smart-home sensor data, enabling effective activity/event analysis with limited labeled data and broad cross-environment transfer.

title: PHAT: Modeling Period Heterogeneity for Multivariate Time Series Forecasting
summary: While existing multivariate time series forecasting models have advanced significantly in modeling periodicity, they largely neglect the periodic heterogeneity common in real-world data, where variates exhibit distinct and dynamically changing periods. To effectively capture this periodic heterogeneity, we propose PHAT (Period Heterogeneity-Aware Transformer). Specifically, PHAT arranges multivariate inputs into a three-dimensional "periodic bucket" tensor, where the dimensions correspond to variate group characteristics with similar periodicity, time steps aligned by phase, and offsets within the period. By restricting interactions within buckets and masking cross-bucket connections, PHAT effectively avoids interference from inconsistent periods. We also propose a positive-negative attention mechanism, which captures periodic dependencies from two perspectives: periodic alignment and periodic deviation. Additionally, the periodic alignment attention scores are decomposed into positive and negative components, with a modulation term encoding periodic priors. This modulation constrains the attention mechanism to more faithfully reflect the underlying periodic trends. A mathematical explanation is provided to support this property. We evaluate PHAT comprehensively on 14 real-world datasets against 18 baselines, and the results show that it significantly outperforms existing methods, achieving highly competitive forecasting performance. Our sources is available at GitHub.

AI.tldr: PHAT introduces a period-heterogeneity aware transformer for multivariate time series by grouping variates into a 3D periodic bucket and using a restricted, masked attention with a positive-negative alignment mechanism and periodic priors; it yields strong performance on 14 datasets vs 18 baselines.
AI.motivation: Real-world multivariate time series exhibit heterogeneity in periodic patterns across variables; existing models capture periodicity but fail to handle dynamic, cross-variate differences, leading to interference and suboptimal forecast.
AI.method: Construct a 3D tensor: dimension 1 = variate groups by similar periods, dimension 2 = phase-aligned time steps, dimension 3 = offsets within period. Interactions restricted within buckets; cross-bucket connections masked. Introduce positive-negative attention capturing periodic alignment and deviation; decompose alignment scores into positive/negative components with modulation term encoding periodic priors; provide mathematical justification.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on 14 real-world datasets against 18 baselines shows significant improvement; competitive forecasting performance; GitHub source provided.
AI.conclusion: PHAT effectively handles periodic heterogeneity and improves forecasting; potential for broader applicability; theoretical grounding supports the mechanism.

title: Large Language Model and Formal Concept Analysis: a comparative study for Topic Modeling
summary: Topic modeling is a research field finding increasing applications: historically from document retrieving, to sentiment analysis and text summarization. Large Language Models (LLM) are currently a major trend in text processing, but few works study their usefulness for this task. Formal Concept Analysis (FCA) has recently been presented as a candidate for topic modeling, but no real applied case study has been conducted. In this work, we compare LLM and FCA to better understand their strengths and weakneses in the topic modeling field. FCA is evaluated through the CREA pipeline used in past experiments on topic modeling and visualization, whereas GPT-5 is used for the LLM. A strategy based on three prompts is applied with GPT-5 in a zero-shot setup: topic generation from document batches, merging of batch results into final topics, and topic labeling. A first experiment reuses the teaching materials previously used to evaluate CREA, while a second experiment analyzes 40 research articles in information systems to compare the extracted topics with the underling subfields.

AI.tldr: A study comparing LLM-based topic modeling (GPT-5) and Formal Concept Analysis (FCA) via the CREA pipeline, using zero-shot prompts, with two experiments (teaching materials and 40 IS articles) to assess strengths and weaknesses in topic modeling.
AI.motivation: Topic modeling is evolving with LLMs, but FCA has had limited applied evaluation. The work seeks an empirical, side-by-side assessment of LLM vs FCA for topic modeling and visualization in information systems contexts.
AI.method: FCA is evaluated using the CREA pipeline from prior topic modeling/visualization work. LLM-based topic modeling uses GPT-5 with a three-prompt zero-shot strategy: (1) topic generation from document batches, (2) merging batch topics into final topics, (3) topic labeling. Two experiments: (i) reuse of teaching materials previously used to evaluate CREA, (ii) analysis of 40 information systems research articles to compare extracted topics with underlying subfields.
AI.result: Not stated in the abstract. The abstract outlines the experimental setup but does not report outcomes or metrics.
AI.conclusion: Not stated in the abstract. The goal appears to be an empirical comparison of LLM vs FCA for topic modeling, but final conclusions are not provided in the abstract.

title: Riemannian Flow Matching for Disentangled Graph Domain Adaptation
summary: Graph Domain Adaptation (GDA) typically uses adversarial learning to align graph embeddings in Euclidean space. However, this paradigm suffers from two critical challenges: Structural Degeneration, where hierarchical and semantic representations are entangled, and Optimization Instability, which arises from oscillatory dynamics of minimax adversarial training. To tackle these issues, we propose DisRFM, a geometry-aware GDA framework that unifies Riemannian embedding and flow-based transport. First, to overcome structural degeneration, we embed graphs into a Riemannian manifold. By adopting polar coordinates, we explicitly disentangle structure (radius) from semantics (angle). Then, we enforce topology preservation through radial Wasserstein alignment and semantic discrimination via angular clustering, thereby preventing feature entanglement and collapse. Second, we address the instability of adversarial alignment by using Riemannian flow matching. This method learns a smooth vector field to guide source features toward the target along geodesic paths, guaranteeing stable convergence. The geometric constraints further guide the flow to maintain the disentangled structure during transport. Theoretically, we prove the asymptotic stability of the flow matching and derive a tighter bound for the target risk. Extensive experiments demonstrate that DisRFM consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods.

AI.tldr: DisRFM unifies Riemannian embedding with flow-based transport to address structural degeneration and instability in graph domain adaptation, yielding stable, geometry-preserving alignment and state-of-the-art performance.
AI.motivation: Adversarial GDA in Euclidean space entangles structure and semantics and suffers from training instability; a geometry-aware approach is needed to disentangle representations and stabilize transport.
AI.method: Embed graphs on a Riemannian manifold using polar coordinates to separate structure (radial) from semantics (angular); enforce topology preservation via radial Wasserstein alignment and semantic discrimination via angular clustering; replace adversarial alignment with Riemannian flow matching to transport source features along geodesics while preserving disentangled structure.
AI.result: Theoretical: proves asymptotic stability of the flow matching and derives a tighter bound on target risk. Empirically, the method consistently achieves state-of-the-art performance across benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: DisRFM provides a principled geometry-aware framework for graph domain adaptation that resolves structural degeneration and optimization instability, backed by theoretical guarantees and strong empirical results.

title: Small Generalizable Prompt Predictive Models Can Steer Efficient RL Post-Training of Large Reasoning Models
summary: Reinforcement learning enhances the reasoning capabilities of large language models but often involves high computational costs due to rollout-intensive optimization. Online prompt selection presents a plausible solution by prioritizing informative prompts to improve training efficiency. However, current methods either depend on costly, exact evaluations or construct prompt-specific predictive models lacking generalization across prompts. This study introduces Generalizable Predictive Prompt Selection (GPS), which performs Bayesian inference towards prompt difficulty using a lightweight generative model trained on the shared optimization history. Intermediate-difficulty prioritization and history-anchored diversity are incorporated into the batch acquisition principle to select informative prompt batches. The small predictive model also generalizes at test-time for efficient computational allocation. Experiments across varied reasoning benchmarks indicate GPS's substantial improvements in training efficiency, final performance, and test-time efficiency over superior baseline methods.

AI.tldr: GPS introduces a generalizable, Bayesian-driven prompt selection framework that uses a small generative model trained on shared optimization history to predict prompt difficulty, enabling efficient batch selection and improved RL training of LLMs.
AI.motivation: Reinforcement learning for large language models is computationally expensive due to rollout-intensive optimization; efficient, generalizable prompt selection is needed to reduce cost while maintaining or improving performance.
AI.method: Train a lightweight generative model on shared optimization history to perform Bayesian inference on prompt difficulty. Implement intermediate-difficulty prioritization and history-anchored diversity in batch acquisition to select informative prompt batches. The predictive model generalizes to test-time prompts for efficient resource allocation.
AI.result: GPS yields substantial improvements in training efficiency, final performance, and test-time efficiency across diverse reasoning benchmarks compared with strong baselines.
AI.conclusion: A generalizable, computation-efficient prompt selection method can accelerate RL-based LLM training without sacrificing performance by leveraging shared optimization history and principled batch acquisition.

title: Three-Way Emotion Classification of EEG-based Signals using Machine Learning
comment: 6 pages, 8 figures, and 3 tables. Submitted to a conference, under review
summary: Electroencephalography (EEG) is a widely used technique for measuring brain activity. EEG-based signals can reveal a persons emotional state, as they directly reflect activity in different brain regions. Emotion-aware systems and EEG-based emotion recognition are a growing research area. This paper presents how machine learning (ML) models categorize a limited dataset of EEG signals into three different classes, namely Negative, Neutral, or Positive. It also presents the complete workflow, including data preprocessing and comparison of ML models. To understand which ML classification model works best for this kind of problem, we train and test the following three commonly used models: logistic regression (LR), support vector machine (SVM), and random forest (RF). The performance of each is evaluated with respect to accuracy and F1-score. The results indicate that ML models can be effectively utilized for three-way emotion classification of EEG signals. Among the three ML models trained on the available dataset, the RF model gave the best results. Its higher accuracy and F1-score suggest that it is able to capture the emotional patterns more accurately and effectively than the other two models. The RF model also outperformed the existing state-of-the-art classification models in terms of the accuracy parameter.

AI.tldr: Three-class EEG emotion classification (Negative/Neutral/Positive) using ML; Random Forest outperforms Logistic Regression and SVM, and surpasses state-of-the-art on a limited dataset.
AI.motivation: Demonstrate feasibility of ML-based EEG emotion recognition and compare common classifiers on a constrained dataset; address the workflow from preprocessing to model evaluation.
AI.method: Preprocess EEG data, extract features (unspecified), train LR, SVM, RF; evaluate with accuracy and F1-score; three-way labels; dataset limited; RF best.
AI.result: RF achieved highest accuracy and F1-score; outperformed LR and SVM and existing state-of-the-art on accuracy.
AI.conclusion: RF is the preferred model for this three-class EEG emotion classification on the given dataset; ML can effectively model EEG-emotion patterns; potential for emotion-aware systems.

title: Evolving from Tool User to Creator via Training-Free Experience Reuse in Multimodal Reasoning
summary: Existing Tool-Integrated Reasoning (TIR) models have effectively extended the question-answering capabilities of LLMs by incorporating external tools. However, real-world scenarios present numerous open-ended problems where fixed tools often fail to meet task requirements. Furthermore, the lack of self-optimization mechanisms means that erroneous tool outputs can mislead the LLM's responses. Additionally, the construction of existing tools entails significant manual effort, which consequently constrains their applicability. Recognizing that the reasoning traces of LLMs encapsulate implicit problem-solving capabilities, we propose UCT, a novel training-free framework that transforms agents from tool users to tool creators. This approach harvests reasoning experiences and distills them into reusable assets. This method transforms the agent from a mere tool user into a tool creator, enabling adaptive tool creation and self-updating during the inference process. We also introduce a memory consolidation mechanism to maintain the tool library, ensuring high reusability of retained experiential memory for subsequent reasoning tasks. This novel automated tool construction paradigm continuously improves tool quality during reasoning, allowing the overall agent system to progress without additional training. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method serves as a novel paradigm for enhancing the capabilities of TIR models. In particular, the significant performance gains achieved +20.86%$\uparrow$ and +23.04%$\uparrow$ on benchmarks across multi-domain mathematical and scientific reasoning tasks validate the self-evolving capability of the agent.

AI.tldr: A training-free framework (UCT) that converts tool users into tool creators by distilling LLM reasoning traces into reusable tools, with memory consolidation to maintain a continually improving tool library, enabling self-updating tools during inference; shows substantial gains on multi-domain math and science tasks.
AI.motivation: Current Tool-Integrated Reasoning (TIR) relies on fixed tools and manual tool construction, which is brittle in open-ended tasks. LLM reasoning traces encode implicit problem-solving strategies, suggesting potential to generate adaptive tools and self-improve without retraining.
AI.method: UCT harvests reasoning experiences from LLMs during inference, distills them into reusable tool assets, and employs a memory consolidation mechanism to maintain and reuse this evolving tool library. Tools can be created and updated automatically during inference, eliminating the need for additional training.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation shows large performance gains (+20.86% and +23.04%) on benchmarks spanning multi-domain mathematical and scientific reasoning tasks, validating the self-evolving capability and utility of adaptive tool creation.
AI.conclusion: UCT proposes a new paradigm for enhancing Tool-Integrated Reasoning models by enabling autonomous tool construction and self-updating during inference, reducing manual tool engineering and training requirements while improving reasoning performance.

title: Strong Linear Baselines Strike Back: Closed-Form Linear Models as Gaussian Process Conditional Density Estimators for TSAD
summary: Research in time series anomaly detection (TSAD) has largely focused on developing increasingly sophisticated, hard-to-train, and expensive-to-infer neural architectures. We revisit this paradigm and show that a simple linear autoregressive anomaly score with the closed-form solution provided by ordinary least squares (OLS) regression consistently matches or outperforms state-of-the-art deep detectors. From a theoretical perspective, we show that linear models capture a broad class of anomaly types, estimating a finite-history Gaussian process conditional density. From a practical side, across extensive univariate and multivariate benchmarks, the proposed approach achieves superior accuracy while requiring orders of magnitude fewer computational resources. Thus, future research should consistently include strong linear baselines and, more importantly, develop new benchmarks with richer temporal structures pinpointing the advantages of deep learning models.

AI.tldr: A simple OLS-based linear autoregressive score for TSAD matches or beats deep detectors with far lower compute; theoretical justification and extensive benchmarks support linear baselines.
AI.motivation: Challenging the predominance of complex deep nets in time series anomaly detection by showing that simple linear models can capture a broad class of anomalies while being much more efficient.
AI.method: Use a closed-form ordinary least squares solution to fit a finite-history linear autoregressive model and derive an anomaly score from the residual/error; interpretation frames the problem as estimating a conditional Gaussian process density.
AI.result: Empirically, the linear AR-OLS approach matches or outperforms state-of-the-art deep detectors across extensive univariate and multivariate benchmarks, with orders of magnitude fewer computational resources required.
AI.conclusion: Strong linear baselines should be standard in TSAD research, and new benchmarks with richer temporal structures are needed to highlight any advantages of deep learning models.

title: Emergent Analogical Reasoning in Transformers
summary: Analogy is a central faculty of human intelligence, enabling abstract patterns discovered in one domain to be applied to another. Despite its central role in cognition, the mechanisms by which Transformers acquire and implement analogical reasoning remain poorly understood. In this work, inspired by the notion of functors in category theory, we formalize analogical reasoning as the inference of correspondences between entities across categories. Based on this formulation, we introduce synthetic tasks that evaluate the emergence of analogical reasoning under controlled settings. We find that the emergence of analogical reasoning is highly sensitive to data characteristics, optimization choices, and model scale. Through mechanistic analysis, we show that analogical reasoning in Transformers decomposes into two key components: (1) geometric alignment of relational structure in the embedding space, and (2) the application of a functor within the Transformer. These mechanisms enable models to transfer relational structure from one category to another, realizing analogy. Finally, we quantify these effects and find that the same trends are observed in pretrained LLMs. In doing so, we move analogy from an abstract cognitive notion to a concrete, mechanistically grounded phenomenon in modern neural networks.

AI.tldr: Formalizes analogical reasoning in Transformers via functor-like correspondences between categories, introduces synthetic tasks, and shows two mechanistic components—geometric alignment of relational structure and functor application—driving analogy; findings hold in pretrained LLMs.
AI.motivation: Address the lack of mechanistic understanding of how Transformers implement analogical reasoning and to operationalize analogy for controlled evaluation.
AI.method: Define analogy as cross-category correspondences inspired by functors; create synthetic tasks to test emergence; perform mechanistic analysIs to identify two components; verify trends in pretrained LLMs.
AI.result: Analogy emergence is highly sensitive to data, optimization, and model scale; two components (geometric alignment and functor application) underlie analogy; similar patterns observed in pretrained LLMs.
AI.conclusion: Brings analogy from abstract cognition to a mechanistically grounded phenomenon in neural networks, outlining how relational structure is transferred across categories via Transformer-wide functor-like operations.

title: Provably Protecting Fine-Tuned LLMs from Training Data Extraction
summary: Fine-tuning large language models (LLMs) on sensitive datasets raises privacy concerns, as training data extraction (TDE) attacks can expose highly confidential information. Existing defenses against such attacks either lack formal privacy guarantees or incur substantial utility degradation. We observe that fine-tuning induces widespread probability shifts, yet preserving only a small subset of influential token-level deviations is sufficient; the remaining shifts can be aggressively smoothed with minimal impact on utility. Motivated by this insight, we propose SCP-$Δ_r$, a Near Access Freeness (NAF)-based algorithm that operates on relative probabilities and explicitly smooths low-impact tokens using a base model. SCP-$Δ_r$ achieves orders-of-magnitude better theoretical bounds than existing NAF based methods and provides strong empirical protection against TDE attacks with minimal performance loss.

AI.tldr: SCP-Δ_r: a Near Access Freeness (NAF) based, relative-probability smoothing method for fine-tuning LLMs that protects against training data extraction with minimal utility loss and significantly stronger theoretical guarantees than prior NAF methods.
AI.motivation: Fine-tuning LLMs on sensitive data risks training data extraction attacks. Many defenses lack formal privacy guarantees or severely degrade utility. There is a need for methods with strong privacy guarantees and low impact on model performance.
AI.method: Introduce SCP-Δ_r, a NAF-based algorithm operating on relative probabilities. It preserves only a small subset of influential token-level deviations (important for utility) and aggressively smooths low-impact tokens using a base model. The approach targets probability shifts induced by fine-tuning and frames the defense within a relative-probability (Δ_r) context.
AI.result: Theoretical analysis yields orders-of-magnitude better bounds than existing NAF-based defenses. Empirically, SCP-Δ_r provides strong protection against training data extraction attacks with minimal degradation in model utility.
AI.conclusion: SCP-Δ_r achieves robust privacy protection during fine-tuning with negligible utility loss and substantially improved theoretical guarantees over prior NAF approaches.

title: Thinking Like a Doctor: Conversational Diagnosis through the Exploration of Diagnostic Knowledge Graphs
summary: Conversational diagnosis requires multi-turn history-taking, where an agent asks clarifying questions to refine differential diagnoses under incomplete information. Existing approaches often rely on the parametric knowledge of a model or assume that patients provide rich and concrete information, which is unrealistic. To address these limitations, we propose a conversational diagnosis system that explores a diagnostic knowledge graph to reason in two steps: (i) generating diagnostic hypotheses from the dialogue context, and (ii) verifying hypotheses through clarifying questions, which are repeated until a final diagnosis is reached. Since evaluating the system requires a realistic patient simulator that responds to the system's questions, we adopt a well-established simulator along with patient profiles from MIMIC-IV. We further adapt it to describe symptoms vaguely to reflect real-world patients during early clinical encounters. Experiments show improved diagnostic accuracy and efficiency over strong baselines, and evaluations by physicians support the realism of our simulator and the clinical utility of the generated questions. Our code will be released upon publication.

AI.tldr: A two-step, knowledge-graph–driven conversational diagnosis framework that generates and then verifies diagnostic hypotheses via clarifying questions, evaluated with a realistic patient simulator (adapted from MIMIC-IV) to reflect early clinical encounters; shows improved accuracy and efficiency; physician validation supports realism and utility; code will be released.
AI.motivation: Existing dialogue-based diagnosis methods rely on parametric model knowledge or assume rich, concrete patient information — unrealistic for early clinical encounters. A realistic evaluation environment and robust questioning strategy are needed.
AI.method: Employs a diagnostic knowledge graph to perform two-step reasoning: (1) generate diagnostic hypotheses from the dialogue context, and (2) verify hypotheses through targeted clarifying questions, iterating until a final diagnosis. Uses a well-established patient simulator with MIMIC-IV profiles, adapted to describe symptoms vaguely to reflect early encounters.
AI.result: Experiments show improved diagnostic accuracy and efficiency over strong baselines. Physician evaluations validate the realism of the simulator and the clinical usefulness of the generated questions.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates the viability of knowledge-graph–driven conversational diagnosis with realistic simulation; code to be released.

title: Topology and Geometry of the Learning Space of ReLU Networks: Connectivity and Singularities
summary: Understanding the properties of the parameter space in feed-forward ReLU networks is critical for effectively analyzing and guiding training dynamics. After initialization, training under gradient flow decisively restricts the parameter space to an algebraic variety that emerges from the homogeneous nature of the ReLU activation function. In this study, we examine two key challenges associated with feed-forward ReLU networks built on general directed acyclic graph (DAG) architectures: the (dis)connectedness of the parameter space and the existence of singularities within it. We extend previous results by providing a thorough characterization of connectedness, highlighting the roles of bottleneck nodes and balance conditions associated with specific subsets of the network. Our findings clearly demonstrate that singularities are intricately connected to the topology of the underlying DAG and its induced sub-networks. We discuss the reachability of these singularities and establish a principled connection with differentiable pruning. We validate our theory with simple numerical experiments.

AI.tldr: Characterizes the (dis)connectedness and singularities of the gradient-flow induced parameter space in DAG-structured feed-forward ReLU networks, linking bottleneck nodes and balance conditions to topology, and connecting singularities to differentiable pruning; supported by simple simulations.
AI.motivation: Understanding how initialization and gradient flow constrain the parameter space to an algebraic variety, and how DAG topology shapes connectivity and singular structures relevant for training and pruning.
AI.method: Theoretical analysis extending prior work on ReLU parameter spaces. Analyzes connectivity via bottlenecks and balance conditions on subnetworks; characterizes singularities through DAG topology and induced subgraphs; discusses reachability and relation to differentiable pruning; supports claims with simple numerical experiments.
AI.result: Thorough characterization of connectedness; identification of bottlenecks and subset balance as key factors; singularities tied to DAG topology; principled link established with differentiable pruning; numerical experiments corroborate theory.
AI.conclusion: DAG topology and induced sub-networks govern the geometry of the ReLU parameter space; findings illuminate training dynamics and pruning strategies in DAG-based networks.

title: Do I Really Know? Learning Factual Self-Verification for Hallucination Reduction
summary: Factual hallucination remains a central challenge for large language models (LLMs). Existing mitigation approaches primarily rely on either external post-hoc verification or mapping uncertainty directly to abstention during fine-tuning, often resulting in overly conservative behavior. We propose VeriFY, a training-time framework that teaches LLMs to reason about factual uncertainty through consistency-based self-verification. VeriFY augments training with structured verification traces that guide the model to produce an initial answer, generate and answer a probing verification query, issue a consistency judgment, and then decide whether to answer or abstain. To address the risk of reinforcing hallucinated content when training on augmented traces, we introduce a stage-level loss masking approach that excludes hallucinated answer stages from the training objective while preserving supervision over verification behavior. Across multiple model families and scales, VeriFY reduces factual hallucination rates by 9.7 to 53.3 percent, with only modest reductions in recall (0.4 to 5.7 percent), and generalizes across datasets when trained on a single source. The source code, training data, and trained model checkpoints will be released upon acceptance.

AI.tldr: VeriFY introduces a training-time, consistency-based verification framework to mitigate factual hallucinations in LLMs by guiding the model through initial answers, probing verifications, consistency judgments, and abstention decisions, with a stage-level loss masking to avoid training on hallucinated stages; it yields substantial hallucination reductions with modest recall costs and generalizes across datasets with single-source training.
AI.motivation: Factual hallucination in LLMs and the limitations of post-hoc verification or abstention during fine-tuning; need for training-time solutions that teach models to reason about uncertainty.
AI.method: Augment training with structured verification traces: generate initial answer, create and answer a probing verification query, produce a consistency judgment, and decide to answer or abstain. Use stage-level loss masking to exclude hallucinated answer stages from loss while supervising verification behavior. Evaluate across model families and scales and datasets.
AI.result: Factual hallucination rates reduced by 9.7–53.3%; recall drops 0.4–5.7%; generalizes across datasets when trained on a single source; code, data, and checkpoints to be released upon acceptance.
AI.conclusion: VeriFY demonstrates that training-time consistency-based verification can substantially reduce factual hallucination with manageable impact on recall and broad generalization; the approach offers a practical path toward more reliable LLMs and encourages release of resources for replication.

title: Forecasting Energy Availability in Local Energy Communities via LSTM Federated Learning
comment: Published as a book chapter in the MEDES 2024 proceedings (Springer LNCS)
summary: Local Energy Communities are emerging as crucial players in the landscape of sustainable development. A significant challenge for these communities is achieving self-sufficiency through effective management of the balance between energy production and consumption. To meet this challenge, it is essential to develop and implement forecasting models that deliver accurate predictions, which can then be utilized by optimization and planning algorithms. However, the application of forecasting solutions is often hindered by privacy constrains and regulations as the users participating in the Local Energy Community can be (rightfully) reluctant sharing their consumption patterns with others. In this context, the use of Federated Learning (FL) can be a viable solution as it allows to create a forecasting model without the need to share privacy sensitive information among the users. In this study, we demonstrate how FL and long short-term memory (LSTM) networks can be employed to achieve this objective, highlighting the trade-off between data sharing and forecasting accuracy.

AI.tldr: Federated Learning with LSTM enables privacy-preserving forecasting in Local Energy Communities, demonstrating feasibility and a trade-off between data sharing and accuracy.
AI.motivation: Privacy constraints in Local Energy Communities hinder sharing consumption data. Accurate forecasts are essential for balancing production and consumption. Federated Learning allows collaborative model training without exchanging sensitive data, and LSTM is well-suited for time-series energy data.
AI.method: Apply a Federated Learning framework where local LSTM models are trained on each participant’s data and aggregated centrally, enabling cross-user knowledge transfer without sharing raw data. Evaluate the privacy-utility trade-off and robustness to data heterogeneity.
AI.result: The study shows the approach is feasible for forecasting within Local Energy Communities and discusses how data sharing levels affect forecasting accuracy, indicating a privacy-preserving gain with some loss in accuracy compared to centralized training.
AI.conclusion: Federated Learning with LSTM is a viable privacy-preserving approach for forecasting in Local Energy Communities, enabling better planning and optimization while reducing data sharing; future work should address privacy guarantees, communication efficiency, and handling non-IID data in real deployments.

title: Light Alignment Improves LLM Safety via Model Self-Reflection with a Single Neuron
summary: The safety of large language models (LLMs) has increasingly emerged as a fundamental aspect of their development. Existing safety alignment for LLMs is predominantly achieved through post-training methods, which are computationally expensive and often fail to generalize well across different models. A small number of lightweight alignment approaches either rely heavily on prior-computed safety injections or depend excessively on the model's own capabilities, resulting in limited generalization and degraded efficiency and usability during generation. In this work, we propose a safety-aware decoding method that requires only low-cost training of an expert model and employs a single neuron as a gating mechanism. By effectively balancing the model's intrinsic capabilities with external guidance, our approach simultaneously preserves utility and enhances output safety. It demonstrates clear advantages in training overhead and generalization across model scales, offering a new perspective on lightweight alignment for the safe and practical deployment of large language models. Code: https://github.com/Beijing-AISI/NGSD.

AI.tldr: A lightweight safety-aware decoding approach for LLMs uses a low-cost trained expert model and a single-neuron gate to balance intrinsic model capabilities with external safety guidance, achieving better safety with reduced training overhead and better cross-model generalization while maintaining utility.
AI.motivation: Current safety alignment typically relies on expensive post-training methods that do not generalize well across different models. Lightweight approaches either depend on precomputed safety injections or on the model's own capacity, which limits generalization and efficiency.
AI.method: Train a small, low-cost expert model and use a single neuron as a gating mechanism to modulate outputs based on safety guidance, integrating external safety signals with the model's intrinsic capabilities during decoding.
AI.result: The approach preserves utility while enhancing output safety, reduces training overhead, and generalizes across different model scales more effectively than some existing methods.
AI.conclusion: Safety-aware decoding with minimal training and a gating neuron offers a practical, scalable path for lightweight alignment in safe, real-world deployment of large language models.

title: LocalV: Exploiting Information Locality for IP-level Verilog Generation
summary: The generation of Register-Transfer Level (RTL) code is a crucial yet labor-intensive step in digital hardware design, traditionally requiring engineers to manually translate complex specifications into thousands of lines of synthesizable Hardware Description Language (HDL) code. While Large Language Models (LLMs) have shown promise in automating this process, existing approaches-including fine-tuned domain-specific models and advanced agent-based systems-struggle to scale to industrial IP-level design tasks. We identify three key challenges: (1) handling long, highly detailed documents, where critical interface constraints become buried in unrelated submodule descriptions; (2) generating long RTL code, where both syntactic and semantic correctness degrade sharply with increasing output length; and (3) navigating the complex debugging cycles required for functional verification through simulation and waveform analysis. To overcome these challenges, we propose LocalV, a multi-agent framework that leverages information locality in modular hardware design. LocalV decomposes the long-document to long-code generation problem into a set of short-document, short-code tasks, enabling scalable generation and debugging. Specifically, LocalV integrates hierarchical document partitioning, task planning, localized code generation, interface-consistent merging, and AST-guided locality-aware debugging. Experiments on RealBench, an IP-level Verilog generation benchmark, demonstrate that LocalV substantially outperforms state-of-the-art (SOTA) LLMs and agents, achieving a pass rate of 45.0% compared to 21.6%.

AI.tldr: LocalV is a multi-agent framework for scalable RTL/HDL generation from long documents, using hierarchical partitioning and locality-aware debugging to tackle long-document, long-code, and debugging challenges; it achieves 45.0% pass rate on RealBench vs 21.6% for SOTA baselines.
AI.motivation: Industrial IP-level RTL design requires handling long, detailed specifications, generating long synthesizable code, and iterative debugging. Existing LLM approaches struggle with long-context, code length, and verification cycles, limiting scalability to industrial tasks.
AI.method: LocalV decomposes long-document RTL tasks into short-document, short-code subproblems using hierarchical document partitioning, task planning, localized code generation, interface-consistent merging, and AST-guided locality-aware debugging. It relies on a multi-agent system that exploits information locality in modular hardware designs to improve scalability and correctness.
AI.result: On RealBench, LocalV achieves a pass rate of 45.0% versus 21.6% for state-of-the-art LLMs/agents, indicating substantial performance gains in producing correct RTL code and managing interface constraints across modules.
AI.conclusion: LocalV demonstrates that exploiting locality and hierarchical task decomposition enables scalable RTL generation for industrial IP-level designs, with significant gains over previous LLM-based approaches; it offers a viable pathway toward automation of complex HDL design tasks.

title: Edit Knowledge, Not Just Facts via Multi-Step Reasoning over Background Stories
summary: Enabling artificial intelligence systems, particularly large language models, to integrate new knowledge and flexibly apply it during reasoning remains a central challenge. Existing knowledge editing approaches emphasize atomic facts, improving factual recall but often failing to integrate new information into a coherent framework usable across contexts. In this work, we argue that knowledge internalization is fundamentally a reasoning problem rather than a memorization problem. Consequently, a model should be trained in situations where the new information is instrumental to solving a task, combined with pre-existing knowledge, and exercised through multi-step reasoning. Based on this insight, we propose a training strategy based on three principles. First, new knowledge is introduced as a coherent background story that contextualizes novel facts and explains their relation to existing knowledge. Second, models are trained using self-generated multi-hop questions that require multi-step reasoning involving the new information. Third, training is done using knowledge distillation, forcing a student model to internalize the teacher's reasoning behavior without access to the novel information. Experiments show that models trained with this strategy effectively leverage newly acquired knowledge during reasoning and achieve remarkable performance on challenging questions that require combining multiple new facts.

AI.tldr: Reframes knowledge editing as a reasoning problem: integrate new facts by embedding them in a coherent background, training via self-generated multi-hop questions, and distillation to transfer reasoning behavior; leads to better cross-context use of new knowledge in LLMs.
AI.motivation: Knowledge editing in LLMs often improves recall of isolated facts but fails to integrate facts coherently for cross-context reasoning. The paper argues internalization of new knowledge requires reasoning that combines new and existing knowledge.
AI.method: 1) Introduce new knowledge as a coherent background story linking it to prior knowledge. 2) Train with self-generated multi-hop questions that require reasoning with the new information. 3) Use knowledge distillation to train a student to mimic the teacher's reasoning without direct access to the novel information.
AI.result: Models trained with this approach leverage newly acquired knowledge during reasoning and show strong performance on challenging questions that require combining multiple new facts.
AI.conclusion: Reasoning-centered training with contextualized knowledge and distillation can enhance internalization of new information in LLMs, enabling more flexible and coherent use across tasks.

title: Deep Time-series Forecasting Needs Kernelized Moment Balancing
summary: Deep time-series forecasting can be formulated as a distribution balancing problem aimed at aligning the distribution of the forecasts and ground truths. According to Imbens' criterion, true distribution balance requires matching the first moments with respect to any balancing function. We demonstrate that existing objectives fail to meet this criterion, as they enforce moment matching only for one or two predefined balancing functions, thus failing to achieve full distribution balance. To address this limitation, we propose direct forecasting with kernelized moment balancing (KMB-DF). Unlike existing objectives, KMB-DF adaptively selects the most informative balancing functions from a reproducing kernel hilbert space (RKHS) to enforce sufficient distribution balancing. We derive a tractable and differentiable objective that enables efficient estimation from empirical samples and seamless integration into gradient-based training pipelines. Extensive experiments across multiple models and datasets show that KMB-DF consistently improves forecasting accuracy and achieves state-of-the-art performance. Code is available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/KMB-DF-403C.

AI.tldr: Direct forecasting with kernelized moment balancing (KMB-DF) for deep time-series forecasting, leveraging RKHS to adaptively select informative balancing functions to enforce distribution balance beyond fixed moments, yielding improved accuracy and state-of-the-art performance.
AI.motivation: To satisfy Imbens' criterion that true distribution balance requires matching the first moments with respect to any balancing function. Existing objectives only enforce a few predefined moments, which can fail to achieve full distribution balance. A method that adaptively selects informative balancing functions aims to realize true distribution balance in forecasting.
AI.method: Introduce KMB-DF: kernelized moment balancing for direct forecasting. The method adaptively selects informative balancing functions from a reproducing kernel Hilbert space (RKHS) to enforce distribution balance. It derives a tractable, differentiable objective suitable for gradient-based training and efficient estimation from empirical samples, enabling seamless integration into existing forecasting pipelines.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation across multiple models and datasets shows that KMB-DF consistently improves forecasting accuracy and achieves state-of-the-art performance.
AI.conclusion: KMB-DF provides a scalable, effective approach to distribution-balanced deep time-series forecasting by moving beyond fixed moment matching and leveraging adaptive RKHS-based balancing function selection; it can be integrated into existing training frameworks and code is publicly available.

title: Canonical Intermediate Representation for LLM-based optimization problem formulation and code generation
summary: Automatically formulating optimization models from natural language descriptions is a growing focus in operations research, yet current LLM-based approaches struggle with the composite constraints and appropriate modeling paradigms required by complex operational rules. To address this, we introduce the Canonical Intermediate Representation (CIR): a schema that LLMs explicitly generate between problem descriptions and optimization models. CIR encodes the semantics of operational rules through constraint archetypes and candidate modeling paradigms, thereby decoupling rule logic from its mathematical instantiation. Upon a newly generated CIR knowledge base, we develop the rule-to-constraint (R2C) framework, a multi-agent pipeline that parses problem texts, synthesizes CIR implementations by retrieving domain knowledge, and instantiates optimization models. To systematically evaluate rule-to-constraint reasoning, we test R2C on our newly constructed benchmark featuring rich operational rules, and benchmarks from prior work. Extensive experiments show that R2C achieves state-of-the-art accuracy on the proposed benchmark (47.2% Accuracy Rate). On established benchmarks from the literature, R2C delivers highly competitive results, approaching the performance of proprietary models (e.g., GPT-5). Moreover, with a reflection mechanism, R2C achieves further gains and sets new best-reported results on some benchmarks.

AI.tldr: Proposes Canonical Intermediate Representation (CIR) and a rule-to-constraint (R2C) framework to systematically convert natural-language optimization problems into executable models, achieving state-of-the-art accuracy on a new benchmark and strong results on existing benchmarks, with reflection improving performance.
AI.motivation: Current LLM-based NL-to-model methods struggle with complex composite constraints and selecting appropriate modeling paradigms. A decoupled, rule-centric representation is needed to separate rule logic from mathematical instantiation and improve generalization.
AI.method: Introduce CIR as a schema encoding operational rules via constraint archetypes and candidate modeling paradigms. Develop R2C, a multi-agent pipeline that (1) parses problem texts, (2) retrieves domain knowledge to synthesize CIR implementations, and (3) instantiates optimization models. Construct a new benchmark of rich operational rules and evaluate against prior benchmarks. Incorporate a reflection mechanism to enhance reasoning.
AI.result: On the proposed benchmark, R2C achieves 47.2% accuracy, indicating state-of-the-art performance on that dataset. On established benchmarks from the literature, R2C is highly competitive and approaches the performance of proprietary models (e.g., GPT-5). The reflection mechanism yields further gains and sets new best-reported results on some benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: CIR plus the R2C pipeline provide a robust, decoupled pathway from natural-language problem descriptions to optimization models, improving accuracy and generalization. The reflection-enabled variant offers additional gains, underscoring the potential for near-parity with large proprietary models and advancing NL-to-optimization modeling.

title: Federated Learning at the Forefront of Fairness: A Multifaceted Perspective
comment: 7 pages (main content), 2 pages (references), Accepted and Published Proceedings of the 34th International Joint Conference on Artificial Intelligence (IJCAI). 2025
summary: Fairness in Federated Learning (FL) is emerging as a critical factor driven by heterogeneous clients' constraints and balanced model performance across various scenarios. In this survey, we delineate a comprehensive classification of the state-of-the-art fairness-aware approaches from a multifaceted perspective, i.e., model performance-oriented and capability-oriented.
