Moreover, we provide a framework to categorize and address various fairness concerns and associated technical aspects, examining their effectiveness in balancing equity and performance within FL frameworks. We further examine several significant evaluation metrics leveraged to measure fairness quantitatively. Finally, we explore exciting open research directions and propose prospective solutions that could drive future advancements in this important area, laying a solid foundation for researchers working toward fairness in FL.

AI.tldr: A survey on fairness in federated learning, offering a two-axis taxonomy (model performance-oriented and capability-oriented), a unifying framework to categorize fairness concerns, an evaluation of fairness metrics, and proposed future research directions.
AI.motivation: Address the growing need to ensure fair and equitable model performance across heterogeneous FL clients, where heterogeneity in data and resources can cause biased outcomes.
AI.method: Systematic literature review and synthesis to develop a comprehensive classification framework, evaluate fairness metrics, and outline open research directions and prospective solutions.
AI.result: Provides a comprehensive taxonomy of fairness-aware FL methods, a framework to categorize fairness concerns, an overview of quantitative fairness metrics, and proposed future research directions to advance fairness in FL.
AI.conclusion: The abstract articulates a solid foundation for researchers, summarizing current fairness approaches, introducing a structured framework, and outlining promising directions for advancing fairness in federated learning.

title: Constrained Process Maps for Multi-Agent Generative AI Workflows
summary: Large language model (LLM)-based agents are increasingly used to perform complex, multi-step workflows in regulated settings such as compliance and due diligence. However, many agentic architectures rely primarily on prompt engineering of a single agent, making it difficult to observe or compare how models handle uncertainty and coordination across interconnected decision stages and with human oversight. We introduce a multi-agent system formalized as a finite-horizon Markov Decision Process (MDP) with a directed acyclic structure. Each agent corresponds to a specific role or decision stage (e.g., content, business, or legal review in a compliance workflow), with predefined transitions representing task escalation or completion. Epistemic uncertainty is quantified at the agent level using Monte Carlo estimation, while system-level uncertainty is captured by the MDP's termination in either an automated labeled state or a human-review state. We illustrate the approach through a case study in AI safety evaluation for self-harm detection, implemented as a multi-agent compliance system. Results demonstrate improvements over a single-agent baseline, including up to a 19\% increase in accuracy, up to an 85x reduction in required human review, and, in some configurations, reduced processing time.

AI.tldr: A multi-agent, finite-horizon DAG-MDP approach for regulated workflows that quantifies agent-level epistemic uncertainty and system-level termination states, achieving higher accuracy and significant reductions in human review vs a single-agent baseline in an AI safety evaluation for self-harm detection.
AI.motivation: Address limitations of single-agent prompt-engineering pipelines and the need to observe/compare uncertainty and coordination across stages and human oversight in regulated contexts such as compliance and due diligence.
AI.method: Model the workflow as a finite-horizon directed acyclic graph Markov decision process (MDP). Each agent corresponds to a role or decision stage (content, business, legal) with predefined transitions representing escalation or completion. Epistemic uncertainty is estimated via Monte Carlo sampling at the agent level; system-level uncertainty is captured by termination into automated labeled states or human-review states. A case study on AI safety evaluation for self-harm detection demonstrates the approach.
AI.result: Compared to a single-agent baseline, the multi-agent DAG-MDP approach achieved up to 19% higher accuracy, up to 85x reduction in required human review, and, in some configurations, reduced processing time.
AI.conclusion: The multi-agent, DAG-based MDP framework improves performance and efficiency in regulated workflows by better handling uncertainty and human oversight, and is readily adaptable to other compliance and due-diligence processes.

title: Spectral Imbalance Causes Forgetting in Low-Rank Continual Adaptation
summary: Parameter-efficient continual learning aims to adapt pre-trained models to sequential tasks without forgetting previously acquired knowledge. Most existing approaches treat continual learning as avoiding interference with past updates, rather than considering what properties make the current task-specific update naturally preserve previously acquired knowledge. From a knowledge-decomposition perspective, we observe that low-rank adaptations exhibit highly imbalanced singular value spectra: a few dominant components absorb most of the adaptation energy, thereby (i) more likely to disrupt previously acquired knowledge and (ii) making the update more vulnerable to interference from subsequent tasks. To enable explicit balance among components, we decouple the magnitude of the task update from its directional structure and formulate it as a constrained optimization problem on a restricted Stiefel manifold. We address this problem using a projected first-order method compatible with standard deep-learning optimizers used in vision-language models. Our method mitigates both backward and forward forgetting, consistently outperforming continual learning baselines. The implementation code is available at https://github.com/haodotgu/EBLoRA.

AI.tldr: Proposes EBLoRA: decouples update magnitude from direction in low-rank adapters via constrained optimization on a restricted Stiefel manifold, solved with projected first-order method, to balance component contributions and reduce forgetting in parameter-efficient continual learning; outperforms baselines; code released.
AI.motivation: Low-rank adapters in parameter-efficient continual learning exhibit highly imbalanced singular value spectra, where a few components absorb most adaptation energy. This makes updates prone to disrupting past knowledge and vulnerable to interference from subsequent tasks. Existing work emphasizes avoiding past interference rather than balancing component updates.
AI.method: Formulate the update as a constrained optimization on a restricted Stiefel manifold to decouple update magnitude from directional structure. Solve with a projected first-order method compatible with standard deep-learning optimizers. The approach (EBLoRA) is designed for vision-language models and integrates with common PECL pipelines.
AI.result: Empirically mitigates both backward and forward forgetting and consistently outperforms continual learning baselines.
AI.conclusion: Balancing contributions among low-rank components via a manifold-constrained update improves the stability-plasticity trade-off in parameter-efficient continual learning and provides a practical optimization framework for large-scale models.

title: Hunt Instead of Wait: Evaluating Deep Data Research on Large Language Models
summary: The agency expected of Agentic Large Language Models goes beyond answering correctly, requiring autonomy to set goals and decide what to explore. We term this investigatory intelligence, distinguishing it from executional intelligence, which merely completes assigned tasks. Data Science provides a natural testbed, as real-world analysis starts from raw data rather than explicit queries, yet few benchmarks focus on it. To address this, we introduce Deep Data Research (DDR), an open-ended task where LLMs autonomously extract key insights from databases, and DDR-Bench, a large-scale, checklist-based benchmark that enables verifiable evaluation. Results show that while frontier models display emerging agency, long-horizon exploration remains challenging. Our analysis highlights that effective investigatory intelligence depends not only on agent scaffolding or merely scaling, but also on intrinsic strategies of agentic models.

AI.tldr: DDR introduces an open-ended, autonomous data science task and a large-scale benchmark to evaluate investigatory intelligence in LLMs. Frontier models show emerging agency, but long-horizon exploration remains challenging; intrinsic strategies of agentic models are crucial.
AI.motivation: To quantify and advance investigatory intelligence (autonomous data-driven inquiry) in LLMs by creating a benchmark that starts from raw data and allows open-ended insight extraction, addressing the lack of open-ended data science benchmarks.
AI.method: Propose Deep Data Research (DDR) as an open-ended task where LLMs autonomously extract key insights from databases, and DDR-Bench as a scalable, checklist-based benchmark for verifiable evaluation. Evaluate frontier models to assess emergent agency and analyze drivers beyond mere scaling or scaffolding.
AI.result: Frontier models exhibit emergent agentic behavior, indicating partial autonomy, but long-horizon, sustained exploration remains difficult. DDR-Bench enables verifiable evaluation and highlights the need for intrinsic strategies in agentic models beyond scaling or scaffolding.
AI.conclusion: Effective investigatory intelligence relies on intrinsic strategies of agentic models, not just architecture or scale. DDR-Bench provides a framework for evaluating and guiding the development of autonomous data-driven inquiry in LLMs.

title: Rethinking Hallucinations: Correctness, Consistency, and Prompt Multiplicity
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are known to "hallucinate" by generating false or misleading outputs. Hallucinations pose various harms, from erosion of trust to widespread misinformation. Existing hallucination evaluation, however, focuses only on correctness and often overlooks consistency, necessary to distinguish and address these harms. To bridge this gap, we introduce prompt multiplicity, a framework for quantifying consistency in LLM evaluations. Our analysis reveals significant multiplicity (over 50% inconsistency in benchmarks like Med-HALT), suggesting that hallucination-related harms have been severely misunderstood. Furthermore, we study the role of consistency in hallucination detection and mitigation. We find that: (a) detection techniques detect consistency, not correctness, and (b) mitigation techniques like RAG, while beneficial, can introduce additional inconsistencies. By integrating prompt multiplicity into hallucination evaluation, we provide an improved framework of potential harms and uncover critical limitations in current detection and mitigation strategies.

AI.tldr: Prompt multiplicity: a framework to quantify consistency in LLM evaluations; reveals substantial inconsistency in benchmarks (e.g., >50% in Med-HALT); detection focuses on consistency rather than correctness; mitigation (e.g., RAG) can introduce further inconsistencies; integrating multiplicity yields a more complete harms framework and highlights limitations in current methods.
AI.motivation: Existing hallucination evaluation prioritizes correctness and neglects consistency, which is essential to understanding harms from LLM hallucinations.
AI.method: Introduce and apply prompt multiplicity to quantify consistency in LLM evaluations; empirically assess inconsistency in the Med-HALT benchmark; examine how consistency affects hallucination detection and mitigation; evaluate how retrieval-augmented generation impacts consistency.
AI.result: Found significant multiplicity (over 50% inconsistency) in benchmarks like Med-HALT; detection methods tend to detect consistency rather than correctness; RAG and related mitigations can introduce additional inconsistencies; integrating prompt multiplicity clarifies harms and reveals limitations of current detection/mitigation strategies.
AI.conclusion: Integrating prompt multiplicity into hallucination evaluation yields a more comprehensive harms framework, uncovers critical limitations in current detection and mitigation approaches, and emphasizes the need to account for consistency in evaluating and mitigating LLM hallucinations.

title: Rethinking the Role of Entropy in Optimizing Tool-Use Behaviors for Large Language Model Agents
summary: Tool-using agents based on Large Language Models (LLMs) excel in tasks such as mathematical reasoning and multi-hop question answering. However, in long trajectories, agents often trigger excessive and low-quality tool calls, increasing latency and degrading inference performance, making managing tool-use behavior challenging. In this work, we conduct entropy-based pilot experiments and observe a strong positive correlation between entropy reduction and high-quality tool calls. Building on this finding, we propose using entropy reduction as a supervisory signal and design two reward strategies to address the differing needs of optimizing tool-use behavior. Sparse outcome rewards provide coarse, trajectory-level guidance to improve efficiency, while dense process rewards offer fine-grained supervision to enhance performance. Experiments across diverse domains show that both reward designs improve tool-use behavior: the former reduces tool calls by 72.07% compared to the average of baselines, while the latter improves performance by 22.27%. These results position entropy reduction as a key mechanism for enhancing tool-use behavior, enabling agents to be more adaptive in real-world applications.

AI.tldr: Entropy reduction as supervisory signal to optimize tool-use in LLM agents; two reward strategies (sparse and dense) improve efficiency and performance; broad-domain evidence.
AI.motivation: Long trajectories cause excessive low-quality tool calls; entropy reduction correlates with higher-quality calls; provides a principled mechanism to shape tool use.
AI.method: Pilot experiments measuring entropy reduction; design sparse outcome rewards for trajectory-level efficiency and dense process rewards for per-step performance; evaluate across diverse domains.
AI.result: Sparse rewards reduce tool calls by 72.07% on average; dense rewards improve performance by 22.27%.
AI.conclusion: Entropy reduction is an effective mechanism to guide tool-use in LLM agents, enabling more adaptive real-world behavior.

title: Pareto-Conditioned Diffusion Models for Offline Multi-Objective Optimization
comment: Accepted by ICLR 2026. Project page: https://sites.google.com/view/pcd-iclr26
summary: Multi-objective optimization (MOO) arises in many real-world applications where trade-offs between competing objectives must be carefully balanced. In the offline setting, where only a static dataset is available, the main challenge is generalizing beyond observed data. We introduce Pareto-Conditioned Diffusion (PCD), a novel framework that formulates offline MOO as a conditional sampling problem. By conditioning directly on desired trade-offs, PCD avoids the need for explicit surrogate models. To effectively explore the Pareto front, PCD employs a reweighting strategy that focuses on high-performing samples and a reference-direction mechanism to guide sampling towards novel, promising regions beyond the training data. Experiments on standard offline MOO benchmarks show that PCD achieves highly competitive performance and, importantly, demonstrates greater consistency across diverse tasks than existing offline MOO approaches.

AI.tldr: PCD reframes offline multi-objective optimization as conditional diffusion-based sampling conditioned on desired trade-offs, using reweighting and a reference-direction mechanism to explore the Pareto front beyond training data, achieving competitive and more consistent performance without surrogate models.
AI.motivation: Offline MOO suffers from distribution shift and limited generalization when only a static dataset is available. Existing surrogate-based methods can overfit and lack consistency across tasks. A direct, surrogate-free conditional sampling approach could improve exploration of the Pareto front and generalization to unseen trade-offs.
AI.method: Pareto-Conditioned Diffusion (PCD) trains a diffusion model that samples decision variables conditioned on target Pareto trade-offs. It employs a reweighting strategy to emphasize high-performing samples and a reference-direction mechanism to steer sampling toward novel regions beyond the training distribution, enabling effective Pareto-front exploration without explicit surrogate models.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on standard offline MOO benchmarks shows PCD achieves highly competitive performance and exhibits greater consistency across diverse tasks compared to existing offline MOO approaches.
AI.conclusion: PCD offers a simple, surrogate-free framework for offline MOO by conditioning diffusion-based samplers on Pareto targets, with reweighting and reference-direction guidance that yield robust performance and improved consistency.

title: SIDiffAgent: Self-Improving Diffusion Agent
summary: Text-to-image diffusion models have revolutionized generative AI, enabling high-quality and photorealistic image synthesis. However, their practical deployment remains hindered by several limitations: sensitivity to prompt phrasing, ambiguity in semantic interpretation (e.g., ``mouse" as animal vs. a computer peripheral), artifacts such as distorted anatomy, and the need for carefully engineered input prompts. Existing methods often require additional training and offer limited controllability, restricting their adaptability in real-world applications. We introduce Self-Improving Diffusion Agent (SIDiffAgent), a training-free agentic framework that leverages the Qwen family of models (Qwen-VL, Qwen-Image, Qwen-Edit, Qwen-Embedding) to address these challenges. SIDiffAgent autonomously manages prompt engineering, detects and corrects poor generations, and performs fine-grained artifact removal, yielding more reliable and consistent outputs. It further incorporates iterative self-improvement by storing a memory of previous experiences in a database. This database of past experiences is then used to inject prompt-based guidance at each stage of the agentic pipeline. \modelour achieved an average VQA score of 0.884 on GenAIBench, significantly outperforming open-source, proprietary models and agentic methods. We will publicly release our code upon acceptance.

AI.tldr: Training-free agentic diffusion framework SIDiffAgent leverages Qwen models to autonomously engineer prompts, correct poor generations, and remove artifacts, with a memory-based self-improvement loop; achieves high VQA performance on GenAIBench (0.884) and outperforms baselines; code to be released.
AI.motivation: Address limitations of diffusion models (prompt sensitivity, semantic ambiguity, artifacts, and controllability) without costly retraining; enable real-world, adaptive deployment through an agent that optimizes outputs via memory-informed guidance.
AI.method: A training-free pipeline that uses Qwen-VL, Qwen-Image, Qwen-Edit, and Qwen-Embedding to manage prompts, detect/correct failures, and perform artifact removal. It stores past experiences in a database and injects prompt-based cues at each pipeline stage for iterative self-improvement.
AI.result: SIDiffAgent achieves an average VQA score of 0.884 on GenAIBench, outperforming open-source and proprietary baselines as well as prior agentic methods.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates that a training-free, memory-augmented agentic diffusion framework can deliver reliable, higher-quality outputs with better controllability; future work includes broader evaluation, ablations, and code release.

title: GraphNNK -- Graph Classification and Interpretability
summary: Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) have become a standard approach for learning from graph-structured data. However, their reliance on parametric classifiers (most often linear softmax layers) limits interpretability and sometimes hinders generalization. Recent work on interpolation-based methods, particularly Non-Negative Kernel regression (NNK), has demonstrated that predictions can be expressed as convex combinations of similar training examples in the embedding space, yielding both theoretical results and interpretable explanations.

AI.tldr: NNK interpolation enables interpretable GNN predictions as convex combinations of similar training examples.
AI.motivation: Parametric classifiers in GNNs hinder interpretability and may affect generalization; interpolation-based methods promise theory-backed explanations.
AI.method: Non-Negative Kernel regression (NNK); predictions expressed as nonnegative weights forming a convex combination of training embeddings based on kernel similarities.
AI.result: The approach yields predictions that are convex combinations of training points and comes with theoretical results and interpretable explanations.
AI.conclusion: Interpolation-based methods like NNK offer a path to interpretable, potentially better-generalizing GNNs, offering an alternative to standard linear classifiers.

title: Understanding the Reversal Curse Mitigation in Masked Diffusion Models through Attention and Training Dynamics
summary: Autoregressive language models (ARMs) suffer from the reversal curse: after learning that "$A$ is $B$", they often fail on the reverse query "$B$ is $A$". Masked diffusion-based language models (MDMs) exhibit this failure in a much weaker form, but the underlying reason has remained unclear. A common explanation attributes this mitigation to the any-order training objective. However, observing "[MASK] is $B$" during training does not necessarily teach the model to handle the reverse prompt "$B$ is [MASK]". We show that the mitigation arises from architectural structure and its interaction with training. In a one-layer Transformer encoder, weight sharing couples the two directions by making forward and reverse attention scores positively correlated. In the same setting, we further show that the corresponding gradients are aligned, so minimizing the forward loss also reduces the reverse loss. Experiments on both controlled toy tasks and large-scale diffusion language models support these mechanisms, explaining why MDMs partially overcome a failure mode that persists in strong ARMs.

AI.tldr: Masked diffusion-based LMs mitigate reversal failures of autoregressive models due to architectural weight-sharing in a one-layer Transformer, which creates positive correlation between forward and reverse attention and aligned gradients; this coupling, not solely any-order training, explains partial mitigation.
AI.motivation: Understand why masked diffusion-based LMs reduce the reversal curse and critique the sole-attribution to any-order training objectives.
AI.method: Theoretical analysis in a one-layer Transformer encoder showing weight sharing ties forward and reverse attention; gradient alignment between forward and reverse losses; and empirical validation on controlled toy tasks and large-scale diffusion language models.
AI.result: Forward loss minimization also lowers the reverse loss; forward and reverse attention scores are positively correlated; gradients of forward and reverse objectives are aligned; empirical results support the proposed mechanism.
AI.conclusion: Architectural coupling via weight sharing explains partial mitigation of reversal failures in MDMS, clarifying limits of any-order training explanations and highlighting the interaction between architecture and training.

title: BLOCK-EM: Preventing Emergent Misalignment by Blocking Causal Features
summary: Emergent misalignment can arise when a language model is fine-tuned on a narrowly scoped supervised objective: the model learns the target behavior, yet also develops undesirable out-of-domain behaviors. We investigate a mechanistic approach to preventing emergent misalignment by identifying a small set of internal features that reliably control the misaligned behavior and then discouraging the model from strengthening these features during fine-tuning. Across six fine-tuning domains, blocking (i.e., constraining) a fixed set of features achieves up to 95\% relative reduction in emergent misalignment with no degradation in model quality or target-task performance. We strengthen validity with disjoint selection/evaluation splits, multiple independent judges, multiple random seeds for key settings, quality metrics, and extensive ablations demonstrating that the reduction in misalignment is specific to the identified mechanism. We also characterize a limiting regime in which misalignment re-emerges under prolonged fine-tuning, present evidence consistent with rerouting through alternative features or layers, and evaluate modifications that partially restore the misalignment-blocking effect. Overall, our results show that targeted training-time constraints on internal mechanisms can mitigate emergent misalignment without degrading target-task performance.

AI.tldr: Fix emergent misalignment by constraining a fixed set of internal features during fine-tuning, achieving up to 95% relative reduction in misalignment without hurting target-task performance across six domains; misalignment can re-emerge with prolonged fine-tuning via rerouting to other features or layers; some modifications can partially restore the blocking effect.
AI.motivation: Emergent misalignment arises when a narrowly supervised fine-tuning objective yields the target behavior but also enables undesirable out-of-domain behavior. A mechanistic, feature-level constraint approach aims for robust, verifiable mitigation without sacrificing task performance.
AI.method: Identify a small set of internal features that reliably control misalignment and enforce constraints to block them during fine-tuning. Apply across six fine-tuning domains with disjoint train/evaluation splits, multiple independent judges, multiple random seeds, and thorough ablations. Evaluate both misalignment reduction and target-task performance. Analyze limits where misalignment resurfaces under prolonged fine-tuning and explore rerouting through alternative features/layers, plus modifications that partially restore the effect.
AI.result: Blocking the identified feature set yields up to 95% relative reduction in emergent misalignment with no degradation in target-task performance across six domains. The evaluation framework is rigorous (disjoint splits, multiple judges/seeds, quality metrics, ablations). The reduction appears specific to the targeted mechanism. Under prolonged fine-tuning, misalignment can re-emerge, with evidence suggesting rerouting to alternative features or layers; certain modifications can partially restore the blocking effect.
AI.conclusion: Targeted training-time constraints on internal mechanisms can mitigate emergent misalignment without sacrificing target-task performance, indicating a viable mitigation path. However, durability under extended fine-tuning and potential rerouting present challenges requiring further study.

title: Mitigating Safety Tax via Distribution-Grounded Refinement in Large Reasoning Models
summary: Safety alignment incurs safety tax that perturbs a large reasoning model's (LRM) general reasoning ability. Existing datasets used for safety alignment for an LRM are usually constructed by distilling safety reasoning traces and answers from an external LRM or human labeler. However, such reasoning traces and answers exhibit a distributional gap with the target LRM that needs alignment, and we conjecture such distributional gap is the culprit leading to significant degradation of reasoning ability of the target LRM. Driven by this hypothesis, we propose a safety alignment dataset construction method, dubbed DGR. DGR transforms and refines an existing out-of-distributional safety reasoning dataset to be aligned with the target's LLM inner distribution. Experimental results demonstrate that i) DGR effectively mitigates the safety tax while maintaining safety performance across all baselines, i.e., achieving \textbf{+30.2\%} on DirectRefusal and \textbf{+21.2\%} on R1-ACT improvement in average reasoning accuracy compared to Vanilla SFT; ii) the degree of reasoning degradation correlates with the extent of distribution shift, suggesting that bridging this gap is central to preserving capabilities. Furthermore, we find that safety alignment in LRMs may primarily function as a mechanism to activate latent knowledge, as a mere \textbf{10} samples are sufficient for activating effective refusal behaviors. These findings not only emphasize the importance of distributional consistency but also provide insights into the activation mechanism of safety in reasoning models.

AI.tldr: DGR refines out-of-distribution safety data to align with the target LRM's inner distribution, reducing safety tax while preserving safety and improving reasoning accuracy; distribution shift drives degradation, and only a few samples can trigger effective refusal behavior.
AI.motivation: Current safety datasets are often distilled from external LMs or humans, causing distributional mismatch with the target LRM that harms reasoning capabilities; bridging this gap is hypothesized to preserve general reasoning while maintaining safety.
AI.method: Transform and refine an existing out-of-distribution safety reasoning dataset to match the target LRM's inner distribution (the 'DGR' method). The exact transformations are not detailed in the abstract but involve alignment of distributions and refinement steps.
AI.result: DGR reduces safety tax and sustains safety: +30.2% DirectRefusal and +21.2% R1-ACT in average reasoning accuracy vs Vanilla SFT; degradation correlates with distribution shift; only ~10 samples suffice to activate refusal behaviors.
AI.conclusion: Distributional consistency between safety data and target LRM is crucial for preserving capabilities during safety alignment; safety mechanisms may leverage latent knowledge activation, requiring only minimal examples.

title: Provable Model Provenance Set for Large Language Models
summary: The growing prevalence of unauthorized model usage and misattribution has increased the need for reliable model provenance analysis. However, existing methods largely rely on heuristic fingerprint-matching rules that lack provable error control and often overlook the existence of multiple sources, leaving the reliability of their provenance claims unverified. In this work, we first formalize the model provenance problem with provable guarantees, requiring rigorous coverage of all true provenances at a prescribed confidence level. Then, we propose the Model Provenance Set (MPS), which employs a sequential test-and-exclusion procedure to adaptively construct a small set satisfying the guarantee. The key idea of MPS is to test the significance of provenance existence within a candidate pool, thereby establishing a provable asymptotic guarantee at a user-specific confidence level. Extensive experiments demonstrate that MPS effectively achieves target provenance coverage while strictly limiting the inclusion of unrelated models, and further reveal its potential for practical provenance analysis in attribution and auditing tasks.

AI.tldr: Formalizes the model provenance problem with provable guarantees and introduces the Model Provenance Set (MPS), a sequential test-and-exclusion method that adaptively builds a small provenance set achieving user-specified coverage.
AI.motivation: Current provenance methods rely on heuristic fingerprinting without provable error control and often assume a single source, risking missed true provenances and over-inclusive results. There is a need for rigorous, provable guarantees that all true provenances are covered at a chosen confidence level and that multiple sources are handled.
AI.method: Propose Model Provenance Set (MPS) with a sequential test-and-exclusion procedure. It iteratively tests provenance existence within a candidate pool and expands or narrows the pool to construct a small set that satisfies the desired coverage guarantee, achieving provable asymptotic guarantees at a user-specified confidence level.
AI.result: Experimental evaluation shows MPS achieves target provenance coverage while strictly limiting inclusion of unrelated models, demonstrating practical effectiveness in attribution and auditing tasks.
AI.conclusion: MPS provides a principled, provable framework for model provenance with practical applicability to attribution and auditing, addressing limitations of heuristic fingerprinting and multi-source scenarios.

title: Traffic-Aware Navigation in Road Networks
summary: This project compares three graph search approaches for the task of traffic-aware navigation in Kingston's road network. These approaches include a single-run multi-query preprocessing algorithm (Floyd-Warshall-Ingerman), continuous single-query real-time search (Dijkstra's and A*), and an algorithm combining both approaches to balance between their trade-offs by first finding the top K shortest paths then iterating over them in real time (Yen's). Dijkstra's and A* resulted in the most traffic-aware optimal solutions with minimal preprocessing required. Floyd-Warshall-Ingerman was the fastest in real time but provided distance based paths with no traffic awareness. Yen's algorithm required significant preprocessing but balanced between the other two approaches in terms of runtime speed and optimality. Each approach presents advantages and disadvantages that need to be weighed depending on the circumstances of specific deployment contexts to select the best custom solution. *This project was completed as part of ELEC 844 (Search and Planning Algorithms for Robotics) in the Fall 2025 term.

AI.tldr: Comparative study of three graph-search strategies for traffic-aware navigation on Kingston’s road network, examining preprocessing, real-time performance, and traffic-awareness trade-offs.
AI.motivation: Assess the trade-offs between preprocessing-heavy all-pairs methods, real-time single-query methods, and a hybrid approach for traffic-aware routing in road networks.
AI.method: Evaluate Floyd-Warshall-Ingerman (precompute all-pairs distances with traffic considerations), Dijkstra/A* (real-time single-query with traffic data), and Yen’s algorithm (top-K paths precomputed then refined in real time) on Kingston’s road network; compare accuracy, preprocessing cost, and runtime performance.
AI.result: Dijkstra’s and A* yielded the most traffic-aware, optimal routes with minimal preprocessing; Floyd-Warshall-Ingerman was fastest in real time but produced distance-based (no traffic awareness) routes; Yen’s algorithm required substantial preprocessing but offered a balanced trade-off between speed and optimality.
AI.conclusion: The choice of algorithm should be guided by deployment constraints: use Dijkstra/A* for traffic-aware routing with low preprocessing; Floyd-Warshall-Ingerman for fastest real-time results without traffic awareness; Yen’s approach for a compromise given available preprocessing budget and performance requirements.

title: A novel VAE-DML fusion framework for casual analysis of greenwashing in the mining industry
summary: Against the backdrop of the global green transition and "dual carbon" goals, mining industry chain enterprises are pivotal entities in terms of resource consumption and environmental impact. Their environmental performance directly affects regional ecological security and is closely tied to national resource strategies and green transformation outcomes. Ensuring the authenticity and reliability of their environmental disclosure is thus a core and urgent issue for sustainable development and national strategic objectives.From a corporate governance perspective, this study examines equity balance as a fundamental governance mechanism, investigating its inhibitory effect on greenwashing behavior among these enterprises and the underlying pathways involved. Methodologically, the paper innovatively employs a Variational Autoencoder (VAE) and a Double Machine Learning (DML) model to construct counterfactual scenarios, mitigating endogeneity concerns and precisely identifying the causal relationship between equity balance and greenwashing. The findings indicate, first, a significant negative causal relationship between equity balance and corporate greenwashing, confirming its substantive governance effect. Second, this inhibitory effect exhibits notable heterogeneity, manifesting more strongly in western regions, upstream segments of the industrial chain, and industries with high environmental sensitivity. Third, the governance effect demonstrates clear temporal dynamics, with the strongest impact occurring in the current period, followed by a diminishing yet statistically significant lagged effect, and ultimately a stable long-term cumulative influence. Finally, mechanism analysis reveals that equity balance operates through three distinct channels to curb greenwashing: alleviating management performance pressure, enhancing the stability of the executive team, and intensifying media scrutiny.

AI.tldr: Equity balance governance reduces corporate greenwashing in mining industry chain firms; effects are strongest in the short term, with heterogeneous and multi-channel mechanisms identified using VAE and DML to estimate causal impact.
AI.motivation: To curb greenwashing in environmental disclosures amid the global green transition; investigates equity balance as a fundamental governance mechanism and its causal effect, addressing endogeneity.
AI.method: Variational Autoencoder (VAE) and Double Machine Learning (DML) to construct counterfactual scenarios, mitigating endogeneity and enabling causal inference on the relationship between equity balance and greenwashing.
AI.result: Significant negative causal relationship: higher equity balance reduces greenwashing. Heterogeneous effects: stronger in western regions, upstream supply chain segments, and environmentally sensitive industries. Temporal dynamics: strongest in the current period, with lagged persistence and a stable long-term cumulative effect. Mechanisms identified: (i) reducing management performance pressure, (ii) stabilizing the executive team, (iii) intensifying media scrutiny.
AI.conclusion: Equity balance acts as an effective governance tool to curb greenwashing in mining industry chain firms. Findings inform corporate governance and policy aimed at improving environmental disclosure integrity, with attention to regional, segmental, and industry-specific heterogeneity and clear guidance on the timing of governance effects.

title: Reasoning in a Combinatorial and Constrained World: Benchmarking LLMs on Natural-Language Combinatorial Optimization
summary: While large language models (LLMs) have shown strong performance in math and logic reasoning, their ability to handle combinatorial optimization (CO) -- searching high-dimensional solution spaces under hard constraints -- remains underexplored. To bridge the gap, we introduce NLCO, a \textbf{N}atural \textbf{L}anguage \textbf{C}ombinatorial \textbf{O}ptimization benchmark that evaluates LLMs on end-to-end CO reasoning: given a language-described decision-making scenario, the model must output a discrete solution without writing code or calling external solvers. NLCO covers 43 CO problems and is organized using a four-layer taxonomy of variable types, constraint families, global patterns, and objective classes, enabling fine-grained evaluation. We provide solver-annotated solutions and comprehensively evaluate LLMs by feasibility, solution optimality, and reasoning efficiency. Experiments across a wide range of modern LLMs show that high-performing models achieve strong feasibility and solution quality on small instances, but both degrade as instance size grows, even if more tokens are used for reasoning. We also observe systematic effects across the taxonomy: set-based tasks are relatively easy, whereas graph-structured problems and bottleneck objectives lead to more frequent failures.

AI.tldr: NLCO is a new end-to-end language-based combinatorial optimization benchmark with 43 problems, a four-layer taxonomy, and solver-annotated solutions; results show LLMs perform well on small instances but degrade as size grows, with set-based tasks easier and graph/bottleneck problems harder.
AI.motivation: Combinatorial optimization (CO) remains underexplored for LLMs that excel at math and logic. A benchmark that evaluates end-to-end CO reasoning in natural language without coding or external solvers is needed to diagnose capabilities and guide improvement.
AI.method: Construct NLCO with 43 CO problems organized by a four-layer taxonomy (variable types, constraint families, global patterns, objective classes). Provide solver-annotated solutions and evaluate LLMs on feasibility, solution optimality, and reasoning efficiency across models and instance sizes.
AI.result: High-performing models achieve strong feasibility and solution quality on small instances, but both degrade as instance size grows, and increased reasoning tokens do not substantially help. Taxonomy reveals systematic effects: set-based tasks are easier; graph-structured and bottleneck objectives induce more failures.
AI.conclusion: NLCO enables fine-grained, end-to-end evaluation of CO reasoning in LLMs and highlights current limitations in scaling LLMs to larger combinatorial problems. The taxonomy supports diagnostic analysis and points to directions such as improved prompting, architecture-aligned reasoning or solver integration to improve performance.

title: Stable Time Series Prediction of Enterprise Carbon Emissions Based on Causal Inference
summary: Against the backdrop of ongoing carbon peaking and carbon neutrality goals, accurate prediction of enterprise carbon emission trends constitutes an essential foundation for energy structure optimization and low-carbon transformation decision-making. Nevertheless, significant heterogeneity persists across regions, industries and individual enterprises regarding energy structure, production scale, policy intensity and governance efficacy, resulting in pronounced distribution shifts and non-stationarity in carbon emission data across both temporal and spatial dimensions. Such cross-regional and cross-enterprise data drift not only compromises the accuracy of carbon emission reporting but substantially undermines the guidance value of predictive models for production planning and carbon quota trading decisions. To address this critical challenge, we integrate causal inference perspectives with stable learning methodologies and time-series modelling, proposing a stable temporal prediction mechanism tailored to distribution shift environments. This mechanism incorporates enterprise-level energy inputs, capital investment, labour deployment, carbon pricing, governmental interventions and policy implementation intensity, constructing a risk consistency-constrained stable learning framework that extracts causal stable features (robust against external perturbations yet demonstrating long-term stable effects on carbon dioxide emissions) from multi-environment samples across diverse policies, regions and industrial sectors. Furthermore, through adaptive normalization and sample reweighting strategies, the approach dynamically rectifies temporal non-stationarity induced by economic fluctuations and policy transitions, ultimately enhancing model generalization capability and explainability in complex environments.

AI.tldr: A stable, causally informed time-series framework for predicting enterprise carbon emissions under distribution shifts across regions, industries, and policies, using adaptive normalization and sample reweighting to improve generalization and explainability.
AI.motivation: Significant heterogeneity in energy structure, production scale, policy intensity, and governance causes non-stationarity and data drift in carbon-emission data across time and space, degrading predictive accuracy and decision usefulness for production planning and carbon markets.
AI.method: A stable temporal prediction mechanism that (1) extracts causal stable features from multi-environment samples (regions, industries, policies) using enterprise-level inputs (energy inputs, capital, labor), carbon pricing, governmental interventions, and policy intensity; (2) imposes risk-consistency-constrained stable learning to ensure robustness to external perturbations and long-term stable effects; (3) applies adaptive normalization and sample reweighting to rectify temporal non-stationarity due to economic fluctuations and policy transitions, enhancing generalization and explainability.
AI.result: The framework aims to extract robust, causally stable features and rectify non-stationarity, leading to improved generalization and explainability in cross-environment carbon-emission predictions (across policies, regions, and sectors).
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates that integrating causal inference with stable learning and time-series modeling yields a robust, explainable predictor for enterprise carbon emissions under distribution shifts, supporting energy-structure optimization and low-carbon decision-making such as production planning and carbon quota trading.

title: TIDE: Trajectory-based Diagnostic Evaluation of Test-Time Improvement in LLM Agents
summary: Recent advances in autonomous LLM agents demonstrate their ability to improve performance through iterative interaction with the environment. We define this paradigm as Test-Time Improvement (TTI). However, the mechanisms under how and why TTI succeed or fail remain poorly understood, and existing evaluation metrics fail to capture their task optimization efficiency, behavior adaptation after erroneous actions, and the specific utility of working memory for task completion. To address these gaps, we propose Test-time Improvement Diagnostic Evaluation (TIDE), an agent-agnostic and environment-agnostic framework that decomposes TTI into three comprehensive and interconnected dimensions. The framework measures (1) the overall temporal dynamics of task completion and (2) identifies whether performance is primarily constrained by recursive looping behaviors or (3) by burdensome accumulated memory. Through extensive experiments across diverse agents and environments, TIDE highlights that improving agent performance requires more than scaling internal reasoning, calling for explicitly optimizing the interaction dynamics between the agent and the environment.

AI.tldr: A diagnostic framework (TIDE) for Test-Time Improvement (TTI) in autonomous LLM agents, decomposing performance into task dynamics, looping vs memory bottlenecks, and agent-environment interaction, with results showing gains require improved interaction dynamics, not just scaling reasoning.
AI.motivation: Existing evaluation metrics fail to capture task optimization efficiency, adaptation after errors, and the role of working memory in TTI; there is insufficient understanding of why TTI succeeds or fails.
AI.method: Introduce TIDE, an agent-agnostic and environment-agnostic diagnostic framework that decomposes TTI into three dimensions: (1) overall temporal dynamics of task completion, (2) whether performance is constrained by recursive looping, and (3) whether burdensome accumulated memory is the bottleneck. Conduct extensive experiments across diverse agents and environments to analyze interaction dynamics.
AI.result: TIDE reveals that improvements require more than scaling internal reasoning; success hinges on optimizing the interaction dynamics between the agent and the environment, as opposed to solely increasing reasoning or memory.
AI.conclusion: TIDE provides a holistic diagnostic tool for evaluating TTI, encouraging focus on interaction dynamics with environments for performance gains rather than only increasing reasoning capacity or memory.

title: Fast Non-Episodic Finite-Horizon RL with K-Step Lookahead Thresholding
summary: Online reinforcement learning in non-episodic, finite-horizon MDPs remains underexplored and is challenged by the need to estimate returns to a fixed terminal time. Existing infinite-horizon methods, which often rely on discounted contraction, do not naturally account for this fixed-horizon structure. We introduce a modified Q-function: rather than targeting the full-horizon, we learn a K-step lookahead Q-function that truncates planning to the next K steps. To further improve sample efficiency, we introduce a thresholding mechanism: actions are selected only when their estimated K-step lookahead value exceeds a time-varying threshold. We provide an efficient tabular learning algorithm for this novel objective, proving it achieves fast finite-sample convergence: it achieves minimax optimal constant regret for $K=1$ and $\mathcal{O}(\max((K-1),C_{K-1})\sqrt{SAT\log(T)})$ regret for any $K \geq 2$. We numerically evaluate the performance of our algorithm under the objective of maximizing reward. Our implementation adaptively increases K over time, balancing lookahead depth against estimation variance. Empirical results demonstrate superior cumulative rewards over state-of-the-art tabular RL methods across synthetic MDPs and RL environments: JumpRiverswim, FrozenLake and AnyTrading.

AI.tldr: Introduces a finite-horizon online RL method using a K-step lookahead Q-function with a thresholding mechanism, plus an adaptive-K strategy. Proves finite-sample regret guarantees: minimax optimal for K=1 and a sqrt(SAT log T) bound for K>=2; empirically outperforms state-of-the-art tabular methods on synthetic and standard RL environments.
AI.motivation: Finite-horizon MDPs require accurate estimation of returns up to a fixed terminal time. Infinite-horizon, discounted methods ignore the horizon structure, leading to suboptimal planning and sample efficiency in finite-horizon tasks.
AI.method: Define a K-step lookahead Q-function that truncates planning to the next K steps. Use a time-varying threshold to select actions only when the estimated K-step value exceeds the threshold. Provide an efficient tabular learning algorithm with finite-sample analysis and adapt K over time to balance lookahead depth against estimation variance.
AI.result: The algorithm achieves minimax optimal constant regret for K=1 and Regret = O(max((K-1), C_{K-1}) sqrt(S A T log T)) for any K≥2. Empirical results show superior cumulative rewards compared to state-of-the-art tabular RL methods on JumpRiverswim, FrozenLake, and AnyTrading.
AI.conclusion: A practical, provably efficient approach for online finite-horizon RL in tabular settings. Adaptive K exploration improves performance by balancing deeper lookahead with estimation noise, yielding better empirical rewards across diverse tasks.

title: More Than a Quick Glance: Overcoming the Greedy Bias in KV-Cache Compression
summary: While Large Language Models (LLMs) can theoretically support extensive context windows, their actual deployment is constrained by the linear growth of Key-Value (KV) cache memory. Prevailing compression strategies mitigate this through various pruning mechanisms, yet trade-off semantic recall for memory efficiency. In this work, we present LASER-KV (Layer Accumulated Selection with Exact-LSH Recall), a framework designed to test the limits of KV compression under a strict accumulative budgeting policy. We deviate from the standard fixed summary size approach by implementing a block-wise accumulation strategy governed by a protection divisor (n). This allows us to isolate the effects of compression from sliding window artifacts. Our experiments on the Babilong benchmark reveal performance degradation in previous compression methods by 15-30% on various long context tasks. LASER-KV maintains stable performance, achieving superior accuracies by a margin of upto 10% at 128k. These findings challenge the prevailing assumption that attention scores alone are a sufficient proxy for token utility.

AI.tldr: LASER-KV introduces Layer Accumulated Selection with Exact-LSH Recall to stress-test KV memory compression under a constrained accumulation budget, outperforming prior compression methods on long-context tasks and challenging the reliance on attention scores as the sole proxy for token utility.
AI.motivation: To push the limits of KV compression under accumulative budgeting, decouple compression effects from sliding-window artifacts, and reassess whether attention-based scoring is a sufficient measure of token utility in long-context LLM deployment.
AI.method: Proposes a block-wise accumulation strategy controlled by a protection divisor n within the LASER-KV framework (Layer Accumulated Selection with Exact-LSH Recall). Uses Exact-LSH recall for token selection and evaluates on Babilong long-context benchmarks, comparing against prior pruning/compression schemes under a fixed memory budget.
AI.result: Prior compression methods exhibit 15–30% degradation on long-context tasks. LASER-KV maintains stable performance and achieves up to ~10% higher accuracy at context lengths of 128k, indicating robust long-context effectiveness.
AI.conclusion: The results challenge the notion that attention scores alone adequately proxy token utility; an accumulation-aware approach with Exact-LSH recall can yield better robustness and performance in long-context settings.

title: Multi-Objective Multi-Fidelity Bayesian Optimization with Causal Priors
summary: Multi-fidelity Bayesian optimization (MFBO) accelerates the search for the global optimum of black-box functions by integrating inexpensive, low-fidelity approximations. The central task of an MFBO policy is to balance the cost-efficiency of low-fidelity proxies against their reduced accuracy to ensure effective progression toward the high-fidelity optimum. Existing MFBO methods primarily capture associational dependencies between inputs, fidelities, and objectives, rather than causal mechanisms, and can perform poorly when lower-fidelity proxies are poorly aligned with the target fidelity. We propose RESCUE (REducing Sampling cost with Causal Understanding and Estimation), a multi-objective MFBO method that incorporates causal calculus to systematically address this challenge. RESCUE learns a structural causal model capturing causal relationships between inputs, fidelities, and objectives, and uses it to construct a probabilistic multi-fidelity (MF) surrogate that encodes intervention effects. Exploiting the causal structure, we introduce a causal hypervolume knowledge-gradient acquisition strategy to select input-fidelity pairs that balance expected multi-objective improvement and cost. We show that RESCUE improves sample efficiency over state-of-the-art MF optimization methods on synthetic and real-world problems in robotics, machine learning (AutoML), and healthcare.

AI.tldr: RESCUE introduces a causal multi-fidelity Bayesian optimization framework that learns a structural causal model over inputs, fidelities, and objectives, and uses a causal intervention-based surrogate with a causal hypervolume knowledge-gradient to balance improvement and cost, improving sample efficiency over existing MFBO methods.
AI.motivation: Standard MFBO methods rely on associative dependencies and can fail when low-fidelity proxies are misaligned with the high-fidelity objective. Incorporating causal structure aims to correctly account for how interventions on fidelities affect outcomes and costs, improving efficiency and robustness.
AI.method: Learn a structural causal model capturing relationships among inputs, fidelities, and objectives. Build a probabilistic multi-fidelity surrogate that encodes intervention effects. Develop a causal hypervolume knowledge-gradient acquisition to select input–fidelity pairs by optimizing a multi-objective improvement against sampling cost.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation shows RESCUE yields improved sample efficiency over state-of-the-art MFBO methods on synthetic and real-world problems in robotics, AutoML, and healthcare.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating causal structure into MFBO with interventions and a causal acquisition strategy can substantially improve sample efficiency and robustness when fids align imperfectly with the high-fidelity objective.

title: Position: Explaining Behavioral Shifts in Large Language Models Requires a Comparative Approach
summary: Large-scale foundation models exhibit behavioral shifts: intervention-induced behavioral changes that appear after scaling, fine-tuning, reinforcement learning or in-context learning. While investigating these phenomena have recently received attention, explaining their appearance is still overlooked. Classic explainable AI (XAI) methods can surface failures at a single checkpoint of a model, but they are structurally ill-suited to justify what changed internally across different checkpoints and which explanatory claims are warranted about that change. We take the position that behavioral shifts should be explained comparatively: the core target should be the intervention-induced shift between a reference model and an intervened model, rather than any single model in isolation. To this aim we formulate a Comparative XAI ($Δ$-XAI) framework with a set of desiderata to be taken into account when designing proper explaining methods. To highlight how $Δ$-XAI methods work, we introduce a set of possible pipelines, relate them to the desiderata, and provide a concrete $Δ$-XAI experiment.

AI.tldr: Δ-XAI: a framework to explain how interventions cause shifts in foundation models by comparing reference vs intervened models; defines desiderata, outlines pipelines, and provides a demonstration.
AI.motivation: Traditional XAI explains a single model snapshot, but scale-up and interventions (scaling, fine-tuning, RL, in-context learning) induce cross-checkpoint behavioral shifts. Explaining these shifts requires comparing models rather than analyzing isolated instances.
AI.method: Formulate a Comparative XAI (Δ-XAI) framework with explicit desiderata; propose a set of explanatory pipelines that operate on model pairs; relate pipelines to desiderata; present a concrete Δ-XAI experiment to illustrate the approach.
AI.result: Conceptual framework plus a worked demonstration, showing how to structure explanations around intervention-induced deltas between a reference and an intervened model.
AI.conclusion: Comparative explanations are better suited for intervention-induced behavioral shifts in large models; Δ-XAI provides a principled starting point and concrete pipelines, with room for defining assessment protocols and empirical validation.

title: Sporadic Gradient Tracking over Directed Graphs: A Theoretical Perspective on Decentralized Federated Learning
summary: Decentralized Federated Learning (DFL) enables clients with local data to collaborate in a peer-to-peer manner to train a generalized model. In this paper, we unify two branches of work that have separately solved important challenges in DFL: (i) gradient tracking techniques for mitigating data heterogeneity and (ii) accounting for diverse availability of resources across clients. We propose $\textit{Sporadic Gradient Tracking}$ ($\texttt{Spod-GT}$), the first DFL algorithm that incorporates these factors over general directed graphs by allowing (i) client-specific gradient computation frequencies and (ii) heterogeneous and asymmetric communication frequencies. We conduct a rigorous convergence analysis of our methodology with relaxed assumptions on gradient estimation variance and gradient diversity of clients, providing consensus and optimality guarantees for GT over directed graphs despite intermittent client participation. Through numerical experiments on image classification datasets, we demonstrate the efficacy of $\texttt{Spod-GT}$ compared to well-known GT baselines.

AI.tldr: Spod-GT is a decentralized federated learning algorithm that uses sporadic gradient tracking on directed graphs, enabling client-specific update frequencies and heterogeneous/asymmetric communications; it provides convergence guarantees under relaxed gradient variance/diversity assumptions and demonstrates empirical gains on image datasets.
AI.motivation: To address two core challenges in decentralized federated learning: data heterogeneity across clients and variability in client participation and communication resources, especially on directed graphs.
AI.method: Introduce Sporadic Gradient Tracking (Spod-GT), a GT-based DFL algorithm that supports (i) client-specific gradient computation frequencies and (ii) heterogeneous and asymmetric communication frequencies on general directed graphs; provide a rigorous convergence analysis with relaxed assumptions on gradient estimation variance and gradient diversity, yielding consensus and optimality guarantees under intermittent participation.
AI.result: Theoretical results establish consensus and optimality guarantees for gradient tracking over directed graphs with intermittent client participation; empirical experiments on image classification datasets show Spod-GT outperforming established gradient-tracking baselines.
AI.conclusion: Spod-GT extends gradient-tracking-based DFL to realistic settings with sporadic participation and asymmetric communications, delivering robust convergence guarantees and practical performance gains; opens avenues for further variance analyses and broader application to heterogeneous network conditions.

title: Interpreting and Controlling LLM Reasoning through Integrated Policy Gradient
summary: Large language models (LLMs) demonstrate strong reasoning abilities in solving complex real-world problems. Yet, the internal mechanisms driving these complex reasoning behaviors remain opaque. Existing interpretability approaches targeting reasoning either identify components (e.g., neurons) correlated with special textual patterns, or rely on human-annotated contrastive pairs to derive control vectors. Consequently, current methods struggle to precisely localize complex reasoning mechanisms or capture sequential influence from model internal workings to the reasoning outputs. In this paper, built on outcome-oriented and sequential-influence-aware principles, we focus on identifying components that have sequential contribution to reasoning behavior where outcomes are cumulated by long-range effects. We propose Integrated Policy Gradient (IPG), a novel framework that attributes reasoning behaviors to model's inner components by propagating compound outcome-based signals such as post reasoning accuracy backward through model inference trajectories. Empirical evaluations demonstrate that our approach achieves more precise localization and enables reliable modulation of reasoning behaviors (e.g., reasoning capability, reasoning strength) across diverse reasoning models.

AI.tldr: IPG identifies internal components that causally influence reasoning by backpropagating outcome-based signals through model trajectories, enabling precise localization and controllable reasoning in LLMs.
AI.motivation: Current interpretability approaches either link components to surface patterns or rely on annotated contrastive data, but fail to capture sequential, long-range influence on reasoning outcomes.
AI.method: Integrated Policy Gradient (IPG) propagates compound outcomes (e.g., post-reasoning accuracy) backward through inference trajectories to attribute sequential contributions of inner components to reasoning behavior.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation shows improved localization precision and reliable modulation of reasoning capabilities across diverse reasoning models.
AI.conclusion: IPG enhances both localization accuracy and controllability of reasoning mechanisms in LLMs, offering a practical tool for analysis and control of complex reasoning.

title: Latent Shadows: The Gaussian-Discrete Duality in Masked Diffusion
summary: Masked discrete diffusion is a dominant paradigm for high-quality language modeling where tokens are iteratively corrupted to a mask state, yet its inference efficiency is bottlenecked by the lack of deterministic sampling tools. While diffusion duality enables deterministic distillation for uniform models, these approaches generally underperform masked models and rely on complex integral operators. Conversely, in the masked domain, prior methods typically assume the absence of deterministic trajectories, forcing a reliance on stochastic distillation. To bridge this gap, we establish explicit Masked Diffusion Duality, proving that the masked process arises as the projection of a continuous Gaussian process via a novel maximum-value index preservation mechanism. Furthermore, we introduce Masked Consistency Distillation (MCD), a principled framework that leverages this duality to analytically construct the deterministic coupled trajectories required for consistency distillation, bypassing numerical ODE solvers. This result strictly improves upon prior stochastic distillation methods, achieving a 16$\times$ inference speedup without compromising generation quality. Our findings not only provide a solid theoretical foundation connecting masked and continuous diffusion, but also unlock the full potential of consistency distillation for high-performance discrete generation. Our code is available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/MCD-70FD.

AI.tldr: Establishes Masked Diffusion Duality, showing masked diffusion is a projection of a continuous Gaussian process via a maximum-value index preservation mechanism, and introduces Masked Consistency Distillation (MCD) to construct deterministic trajectories for consistency distillation, achieving 16x inference speedup while maintaining quality.
AI.motivation: Bridge the gap between discrete masked diffusion and deterministic sampling by enabling efficient, deterministic distillation—overcoming reliance on stochastic distillation and avoiding complex operators/ODE solvers.
AI.method: Proves explicit duality between masked diffusion and a continuous Gaussian process using a novel maximum-value index preservation mechanism. Proposes Masked Consistency Distillation (MCD) to analytically construct deterministic coupled trajectories for consistency distillation, bypassing numerical ODE solvers.
AI.result: The approach strictly improves upon prior stochastic distillation, delivering about a 16× inference speedup without sacrificing generation quality; provides a solid theoretical link between masked and continuous diffusion.
AI.conclusion: Establishes a theoretical connection between masked and continuous diffusion and enables practical, high-performance deterministic distillation for discrete generation, with open-source code available.

title: Context Learning for Multi-Agent Discussion
summary: Multi-Agent Discussion (MAD) has garnered increasing attention very recently, where multiple LLM instances collaboratively solve problems via structured discussion. However, we find that current MAD methods easily suffer from discussion inconsistency, LLMs fail to reach a coherent solution, due to the misalignment between their individual contexts.In this paper, we introduce a multi-LLM context learning method (M2CL) that learns a context generator for each agent, capable of dynamically generating context instructions per discussion round via automatic information organization and refinement. Specifically, inspired by our theoretical insights on the context instruction, M2CL train the generators to control context coherence and output discrepancies via a carefully crafted self-adaptive mechanism.It enables LLMs to avoid premature convergence on majority noise and progressively reach the correct consensus. We evaluate M2CL on challenging tasks, including academic reasoning, embodied tasks, and mobile control. The results show that the performance of M2CL significantly surpasses existing methods by 20%--50%, while enjoying favorable transferability and computational efficiency.

AI.tldr: M2CL introduces per-agent context generators in a multi-LLM discussion framework to dynamically craft context instructions per round, using a self-adaptive mechanism to maintain coherence and prevent premature consensus on noise, achieving significant performance gains over prior MAD methods.
AI.motivation: MAD methods suffer from discussion inconsistency due to misalignment of individual contexts, leading to incoherent solutions. There is a need for dynamic, coherent context generation for each agent to guide discussion towards correct consensus.
AI.method: Propose M2CL: train per-agent context generators that produce context instructions per discussion round through automatic information organization and refinement. The generators are guided by a self-adaptive mechanism to control context coherence and output discrepancies, mitigating majority-noise convergence and promoting progressive consensus.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on tasks spanning academic reasoning, embodied tasks, and mobile control shows that M2CL significantly outperforms existing methods by 20%–50%, with favorable transferability and computational efficiency.
AI.conclusion: Dynamic, per-agent context generation via learnable context generators, coupled with a self-adaptive coherence mechanism, effectively addresses MAD inconsistencies and yields robust, scalable improvements across diverse tasks.

title: JTok: On Token Embedding as another Axis of Scaling Law via Joint Token Self-modulation
summary: LLMs have traditionally scaled along dense dimensions, where performance is coupled with near-linear increases in computational cost. While MoE decouples capacity from compute, it introduces large memory overhead and hardware efficiency challenges. To overcome these, we propose token-indexed parameters as a novel, orthogonal scaling axis that decouple model capacity from FLOPs. Specifically, we introduce Joint-Token (JTok) and Mixture of Joint-Token (JTok-M), which augment Transformer layers with modulation vectors retrieved from auxiliary embedding tables. These vectors modulate the backbone via lightweight, element-wise operations, incurring negligible FLOPs overhead. Extensive experiments on both dense and MoE backbones, spanning from 650M (190M + 460M embedding) to 61B (17B + 44B embedding) total parameters, demonstrate that our approach consistently reduces validation loss and significantly improves downstream task performance (e.g., +4.1 on MMLU, +8.3 on ARC, +8.9 on CEval). Rigorous isoFLOPs analysis further confirms that JTok-M fundamentally shifts the quality-compute Pareto frontier, achieving comparable model quality with 35% less compute relative to vanilla MoE architectures, and we validate that token-indexed parameters exhibit a predictable power-law scaling behavior. Moreover, our efficient implementation ensures that the overhead introduced by JTok and JTok-M remains marginal.

AI.tldr: Introduces token-indexed parameters (JTok, JTok-M) as an orthogonal scaling axis to decouple model capacity from FLOPs in dense and MoE Transformers, achieving better performance with reduced compute and minimal overhead.
AI.motivation: To overcome the entangled scaling of performance with compute in dense models and the memory/efficiency drawbacks of MoE by decoupling capacity from FLOPs via a new parameterization.
AI.method: Augment Transformer layers with modulation vectors retrieved from auxiliary embedding tables. JTok and JTok-M modulate activations element-wise with lightweight operations, incurring negligible FLOPs. Evaluated across dense and MoE backbones from 650M to 61B parameters; includes isoFLOPs analysis and demonstration of power-law scaling.
AI.result: Consistent reductions in validation loss and improved downstream task performance (e.g., +4.1 on MMLU, +8.3 on ARC, +8.9 on CEval). IsoFLOPs analysis shows JTok-M achieves comparable quality with ~35% less compute than vanilla MoE. Token-indexed parameters exhibit predictable power-law scaling. Overhead remains marginal in efficient implementations.
AI.conclusion: Token-indexed parameters provide an orthogonal scaling axis that decouples capacity from compute, enabling improved performance with modest overhead and shifting the quality–compute Pareto frontier for both dense and MoE transformers.

title: Live-Evo: Online Evolution of Agentic Memory from Continuous Feedback
summary: Large language model (LLM) agents are increasingly equipped with memory, which are stored experience and reusable guidance that can improve task-solving performance. Recent \emph{self-evolving} systems update memory based on interaction outcomes, but most existing evolution pipelines are developed for static train/test splits and only approximate online learning by folding static benchmarks, making them brittle under true distribution shift and continuous feedback. We introduce \textsc{Live-Evo}, an online self-evolving memory system that learns from a stream of incoming data over time. \textsc{Live-Evo} decouples \emph{what happened} from \emph{how to use it} via an Experience Bank and a Meta-Guideline Bank, compiling task-adaptive guidelines from retrieved experiences for each task. To manage memory online, \textsc{Live-Evo} maintains experience weights and updates them from feedback: experiences that consistently help are reinforced and retrieved more often, while misleading or stale experiences are down-weighted and gradually forgotten, analogous to reinforcement and decay in human memory. On the live \textit{Prophet Arena} benchmark over a 10-week horizon, \textsc{Live-Evo} improves Brier score by 20.8\% and increases market returns by 12.9\%, while also transferring to deep-research benchmarks with consistent gains over strong baselines. Our code is available at https://github.com/ag2ai/Live-Evo.

AI.tldr: Live-Evo is an online self-evolving memory system for LLM agents using an Experience Bank and Meta-Guideline Bank to learn from streaming data. It reinforces helpful experiences and forgets stale/misleading ones, yielding strong performance gains and transferability.
AI.motivation: Address brittleness and poor adaptation of static, benchmark-driven self-evolution pipelines by enabling true online learning from a data stream and continuous feedback.
AI.method: Decouples what happened from how to use it via two banks: Experience Bank (stores experiences with adaptive weights) and Meta-Guideline Bank (task-adaptive guidelines). Online memory management updates experience weights based on feedback, reinforcing beneficial experiences and decaying or forgetting misleading ones.
AI.result: On the Prophet Arena benchmark over 10 weeks, achieves a 20.8% improvement in Brier score and a 12.9% increase in market returns; transfers to deep-research benchmarks with consistent gains over strong baselines.
AI.conclusion: Live-Evo demonstrates effective online, self-evolving memory for LLM agents with transferable performance gains and a scalable memory management strategy.

title: Mobile Exergames: Activity Recognition Based on Smartphone Sensors
summary: Smartphone sensors can be extremely useful in providing information on the activities and behaviors of persons. Human activity recognition is increasingly used for games, medical, or surveillance. In this paper, we propose a proof-of-concept 2D endless game called Duck Catch & Fit, which implements a detailed activity recognition system that uses a smartphone accelerometer, gyroscope, and magnetometer sensors. The system applies feature extraction and learning mechanism to detect human activities like staying, side movements, and fake side movements. In addition, a voice recognition system is combined to recognize the word "fire" and raise the game's complexity. The results show that it is possible to use machine learning techniques to recognize human activity with high recognition levels. Also, the combination of movement-based and voice-based integrations contributes to a more immersive gameplay.

AI.tldr: Proof-of-concept mobile game Duck Catch & Fit using multimodal sensing (accelerometer, gyroscope, magnetometer) and voice input to perform activity recognition; shows high accuracy and immersive gameplay.
AI.motivation: Leverage ubiquitous smartphone sensors to recognize user activities for gaming and potential biomedical/surveillance applications; improve engagement via multimodal inputs.
AI.method: Developed a 2D endless game; implemented activity recognition through feature extraction and ML classifier on sensor data; integrated voice recognition for the word 'fire' to add complexity.
AI.result: Demonstrated high activity recognition accuracy; movement and voice modalities together improve gameplay immersion.
AI.conclusion: Movement-voice multimodal integration is effective for immersive, sensor-based gaming; the approach is a proof-of-concept with potential broader applications.

title: Trust by Design: Skill Profiles for Transparent, Cost-Aware LLM Routing
summary: How should Large Language Model (LLM) practitioners select the right model for a task without wasting money? We introduce BELLA (Budget-Efficient LLM Selection via Automated skill-profiling), a framework that recommends optimal LLM selection for tasks through interpretable skill-based model selection. Standard benchmarks report aggregate metrics that obscure which specific capabilities a task requires and whether a cheaper model could suffice. BELLA addresses this gap through three stages: (1) decomposing LLM outputs and extract the granular skills required by using critic-based profiling, (2) clustering skills into structured capability matrices, and (3) multi-objective optimization to select the right models to maximize performance while respecting budget constraints. BELLA provides natural-language rationale for recommendations, providing transparency that current black-box routing systems lack. We describe the framework architecture, situate it within the landscape of LLM routing and evaluation, and discuss its application to financial reasoning as a representative domain exhibiting diverse skill requirements and cost-variation across models. Our framework enables practitioners to make principled and cost-performance trade-offs for deploying LLMs.

AI.tldr: BELLA proposes budget-aware LLM selection via skill profiling, clustering of capabilities, and multi-objective optimization to maximize task performance under budget, with interpretable rationale.
AI.motivation: Standard benchmarks report aggregate metrics that hide task-specific skill requirements and cost trade-offs; practitioners need transparent, budget-conscious routing decisions.
AI.method: A three-stage framework: (1) critic-based profiling to decompose outputs and extract granular required skills; (2) clustering skills into structured capability matrices; (3) multi-objective optimization to select models that maximize performance within budget, plus natural-language rationales for recommendations.
AI.result: Proposes a practical, interpretable framework for cost-performance trade-offs in LLM deployment, situating it within LLM routing/evaluation and illustrating application to financial reasoning as a representative domain.
AI.conclusion: BELLA enables principled, transparent, cost-aware LLM deployment, addressing opacity in current black-box routing systems.

title: Over-Alignment vs Over-Fitting: The Role of Feature Learning Strength in Generalization
