summary: Feature learning strength (FLS), i.e., the inverse of the effective output scaling of a model, plays a critical role in shaping the optimization dynamics of neural nets. While its impact has been extensively studied under the asymptotic regimes -- both in training time and FLS -- existing theory offers limited insight into how FLS affects generalization in practical settings, such as when training is stopped upon reaching a target training risk. In this work, we investigate the impact of FLS on generalization in deep networks under such practical conditions. Through empirical studies, we first uncover the emergence of an $\textit{optimal FLS}$ -- neither too small nor too large -- that yields substantial generalization gains. This finding runs counter to the prevailing intuition that stronger feature learning universally improves generalization. To explain this phenomenon, we develop a theoretical analysis of gradient flow dynamics in two-layer ReLU nets trained with logistic loss, where FLS is controlled via initialization scale. Our main theoretical result establishes the existence of an optimal FLS arising from a trade-off between two competing effects: An excessively large FLS induces an $\textit{over-alignment}$ phenomenon that degrades generalization, while an overly small FLS leads to $\textit{over-fitting}$.

AI.tldr: Existence of an optimal Feature Learning Strength (FLS) that improves generalization under early stopping, due to a trade-off: too large FLS causes over-alignment and harms generalization, too small FLS causes over-fitting; demonstrated via empirical studies and gradient-flow analysis of two-layer ReLU networks with logistic loss, where FLS is controlled by initialization scale.
AI.motivation: Address practical generalization under early stopping and understand how FLS shapes optimization dynamics beyond asymptotic regimes.
AI.method: Combine empirical experiments with a theoretical gradient-flow analysis of two-layer ReLU networks trained with logistic loss. Control FLS by adjusting initialization scale and analyze generalization when training stops at a target training risk.
AI.result: Identification of an optimal FLS that yields substantial generalization gains, arising from a trade-off between over-alignment at large FLS and over-fitting at small FLS.
AI.conclusion: There is a sweet spot for FLS in practical training regimes; appropriate initialization that tunes FLS can improve generalization under early stopping, and the theory clarifies the non-monotonic influence of FLS on generalization.

title: Structure Enables Effective Self-Localization of Errors in LLMs
summary: Self-correction in language models remains elusive. In this work, we explore whether language models can explicitly localize errors in incorrect reasoning, as a path toward building AI systems that can effectively correct themselves. We introduce a prompting method that structures reasoning as discrete, semantically coherent thought steps, and show that models are able to reliably localize errors within this structure, while failing to do so in conventional, unstructured chain-of-thought reasoning. Motivated by how the human brain monitors errors at discrete decision points and resamples alternatives, we introduce Iterative Correction Sampling of Thoughts (Thought-ICS), a self-correction framework. Thought-ICS iteratively prompts the model to generate reasoning one discrete and complete thought at a time--where each thought represents a deliberate decision by the model--creating natural boundaries for precise error localization. Upon verification, the model localizes the first erroneous step, and the system backtracks to generate alternative reasoning from the last correct point. When asked to correct reasoning verified as incorrect by an oracle, Thought-ICS achieves 20-40% self-correction lift. In a completely autonomous setting without external verification, it outperforms contemporary self-correction baselines.

AI.tldr: A prompting framework (Thought-ICS) that structures reasoning into discrete, complete thoughts to enable error localization and iterative self-correction in language models, achieving 20-40% self-correction lift with verification and strong autonomous performance against baselines.
AI.motivation: Address the challenge of self-correction in LMs by enabling reliable localization of errors at decision points, inspired by human error monitoring, to build more reliable AI systems.
AI.method: Introduce Thought-ICS: generate reasoning one discrete, complete thought at a time; each thought is a deliberate decision. Use verification to identify the first erroneous step, backtrack to last correct point, and replan. In oracle-verified settings, measure self-correction lift; in autonomous settings, compare to baselines.
AI.result: Model localizes the first erroneous step reliably within the discrete-thought structure; achieves 20-40% self-correction lift when corrected by an oracle; in autonomous settings, outperforms contemporary self-correction baselines.
AI.conclusion: Discretizing reasoning into standalone thoughts with iterative correction bounds enables precise error localization and effective self-correction, advancing robust chain-of-thought reasoning and self-editing capabilities in LMs.

title: Don't Forget Its Variance! The Minimum Path Variance Principle for Accurate and Stable Score-Based Density Ratio Estimation
summary: Score-based methods have emerged as a powerful framework for density ratio estimation (DRE), but they face an important paradox in that, while theoretically path-independent, their practical performance depends critically on the chosen path schedule. We resolve this issue by proving that tractable training objectives differ from the ideal, ground-truth objective by a crucial, overlooked term: the path variance of the time score. To address this, we propose MinPV (\textbf{Min}imum \textbf{P}ath \textbf{V}ariance) Principle, which introduces a principled heuristic to minimize the overlooked path variance. Our key contribution is the derivation of a closed-form expression for the variance, turning an intractable problem into a tractable optimization. By parameterizing the path with a flexible Kumaraswamy Mixture Model, our method learns a data-adaptive, low-variance path without heuristic selection. This principled optimization of the complete objective yields more accurate and stable estimators, establishing new state-of-the-art results on challenging benchmarks.

AI.tldr: MinPV Principle for score-based density ratio estimation; derive closed-form path-variance, optimize a data-adaptive low-variance path via a Kumaraswamy mixture, achieving state-of-the-art DRE performance.
AI.motivation: Despite theoretical path-independence, practical score-based DRE suffers from path schedule sensitivity. The missing term is the path variance of the time score, causing bias/instability.
AI.method: Derive a closed-form variance expression; introduce MinPV objective to minimize path variance; parameterize the path with a flexible Kumaraswamy mixture model to learn data-adaptive, low-variance paths; optimize the complete objective.
AI.result: More accurate and stable density ratio estimators; new state-of-the-art on challenging benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Principled optimization of the full objective, including variance, yields improved DRE; path learning via Kumaraswamy mixtures enables adaptive, low-variance paths.

title: SafeGround: Know When to Trust GUI Grounding Models via Uncertainty Calibration
summary: Graphical User Interface (GUI) grounding aims to translate natural language instructions into executable screen coordinates, enabling automated GUI interaction. Nevertheless, incorrect grounding can result in costly, hard-to-reverse actions (e.g., erroneous payment approvals), raising concerns about model reliability. In this paper, we introduce SafeGround, an uncertainty-aware framework for GUI grounding models that enables risk-aware predictions through calibrations before testing. SafeGround leverages a distribution-aware uncertainty quantification method to capture the spatial dispersion of stochastic samples from outputs of any given model. Then, through the calibration process, SafeGround derives a test-time decision threshold with statistically guaranteed false discovery rate (FDR) control. We apply SafeGround on multiple GUI grounding models for the challenging ScreenSpot-Pro benchmark. Experimental results show that our uncertainty measure consistently outperforms existing baselines in distinguishing correct from incorrect predictions, while the calibrated threshold reliably enables rigorous risk control and potentials of substantial system-level accuracy improvements. Across multiple GUI grounding models, SafeGround improves system-level accuracy by up to 5.38% percentage points over Gemini-only inference.

AI.tldr: SafeGround: an uncertainty-aware GUI grounding framework that calibrates a test-time decision threshold with FDR control to improve reliability and system-level accuracy on ScreenSpot-Pro.
AI.motivation: GUI grounding is high-risk: incorrect spatial grounding can trigger costly, hard-to-reverse actions; need reliable uncertainty estimates and risk management.
AI.method: A distribution-aware uncertainty quantification (UQ) approach that captures spatial dispersion of stochastic outputs from any base GUI grounding model, followed by calibration to derive a test-time threshold with guaranteed false discovery rate control.
AI.result: Uncertainty measures outperform baselines in separating correct vs. incorrect predictions across GUI grounding models. The calibrated threshold enables rigorous risk control and yields system-level accuracy gains; up to 5.38 percentage-point improvement over Gemini-only inference on ScreenSpot-Pro.
AI.conclusion: SafeGround provides risk-aware grounding by integrating distribution-based UQ with FDR-calibrated decision thresholds, enabling improved reliability and system-level performance across GUI grounding models.

title: RMFlow: Refined Mean Flow by a Noise-Injection Step for Multimodal Generation
summary: Mean flow (MeanFlow) enables efficient, high-fidelity image generation, yet its single-function evaluation (1-NFE) generation often cannot yield compelling results. We address this issue by introducing RMFlow, an efficient multimodal generative model that integrates a coarse 1-NFE MeanFlow transport with a subsequent tailored noise-injection refinement step. RMFlow approximates the average velocity of the flow path using a neural network trained with a new loss function that balances minimizing the Wasserstein distance between probability paths and maximizing sample likelihood. RMFlow achieves near state-of-the-art results on text-to-image, context-to-molecule, and time-series generation using only 1-NFE, at a computational cost comparable to the baseline MeanFlows.

AI.tldr: RMFlow combines a coarse 1-NFE MeanFlow transport with a tailored noise-injection refinement, using a neural network to approximate average transport velocity via a loss that trades off Wasserstein-path distance and sample likelihood, delivering near-SOTA multimodal results at 1-NFE cost.
AI.motivation: Overcome the limitations of single-function 1-NFE MeanFlow generation, which may yield subpar results, by adding an efficient refinement step to achieve high-quality generation with low compute.
AI.method: A two-stage pipeline: (1) coarse 1-NFE MeanFlow transport, (2) noise-injection refinement guided by a neural network that estimates the average velocity along the flow. The network is trained with a novel loss balancing Wasserstein distance between probability paths and sample likelihood.
AI.result: RMFlow attains near state-of-the-art performance on text-to-image, context-to-molecule, and time-series generation using only 1-NFE, with computational cost similar to baseline MeanFlows.
AI.conclusion: RMFlow provides an efficient, high-quality multimodal generation framework under 1-NFE constraints, by coupling a coarse transport with a refined, probability-path-aware perturbation.

title: Thinking with Comics: Enhancing Multimodal Reasoning through Structured Visual Storytelling
summary: Chain-of-Thought reasoning has driven large language models to extend from thinking with text to thinking with images and videos. However, different modalities still have clear limitations: static images struggle to represent temporal structure, while videos introduce substantial redundancy and computational cost. In this work, we propose Thinking with Comics, a visual reasoning paradigm that uses comics as a high information-density medium positioned between images and videos. Comics preserve temporal structure, embedded text, and narrative coherence while requiring significantly lower reasoning cost. We systematically study two reasoning paths based on comics and evaluate them on a range of reasoning tasks and long-context understanding tasks. Experimental results show that Thinking with Comics outperforms Thinking with Images on multi-step temporal and causal reasoning tasks, while remaining substantially more efficient than Thinking with Video. Further analysis indicates that different comic narrative structures and styles consistently affect performance across tasks, suggesting that comics serve as an effective intermediate visual representation for improving multimodal reasoning.

AI.tldr: Comics provide a high-information-density, temporally coherent medium that sits between static images and videos, achieving better temporal/causal reasoning than images while being far cheaper than videos.
AI.motivation: To bridge the gap between static images and videos for chain-of-thought multimodal reasoning, reducing computational cost while preserving temporal structure and narrative coherence.
AI.method: Introduce Thinking with Comics as a reasoning paradigm, study two comics-based reasoning paths, evaluate on diverse reasoning and long-context understanding tasks, and analyze the effect of different comic narrative structures/styles.
AI.result: Comics outperform image-based reasoning on multi-step temporal and causal tasks and are substantially more efficient than video-based reasoning; the impact of comic narrative structures is consistent across tasks.
AI.conclusion: Comics are an effective intermediate visual representation for multimodal reasoning; narrative structure acts as a controllable factor that influences performance, offering a pathway to efficient, temporally-aware reasoning.

title: Investigating the Robustness of Subtask Distillation under Spurious Correlation
summary: Subtask distillation is an emerging paradigm in which compact, specialized models are extracted from large, general-purpose 'foundation models' for deployment in environments with limited resources or in standalone computer systems. Although distillation uses a teacher model, it still relies on a dataset that is often limited in size and may lack representativeness or exhibit spurious correlations. In this paper, we evaluate established distillation methods, as well as the recent SubDistill method, when using data with spurious correlations for distillation. As the strength of the correlations increases, we observe a widening gap between advanced methods, such as SubDistill, which remain fairly robust, and some baseline methods, which degrade to near-random performance. Overall, our study underscores the challenges of knowledge distillation when applied to imperfect, real-world datasets, particularly those with spurious correlations.

AI.tldr: Subtask distillation shows robustness to spurious correlations; SubDistill outperforms baselines as correlation strength grows, underscoring the impact of dataset quality on distillation.
AI.motivation: Evaluate how distillation methods perform when training data exhibit spurious correlations and assess whether SubDistill maintains robustness under imperfect datasets.
AI.method: Empirical evaluation across established distillation methods and SubDistill on data with varying strength of spurious correlations, comparing performance to baseline methods.
AI.result: As spurious correlations strengthen, advanced methods like SubDistill remain robust while several baselines degrade toward near-random performance; the performance gap widens with correlation strength.
AI.conclusion: Distillation in real-world, imperfect datasets with spurious correlations remains challenging; specialized methods (e.g., SubDistill) can offer resilience, but broader data quality issues in distillation require further attention.

title: Drift-Bench: Diagnosing Cooperative Breakdowns in LLM Agents under Input Faults via Multi-Turn Interaction
summary: As Large Language Models transition to autonomous agents, user inputs frequently violate cooperative assumptions (e.g., implicit intent, missing parameters, false presuppositions, or ambiguous expressions), creating execution risks that text-only evaluations do not capture. Existing benchmarks typically assume well-specified instructions or restrict evaluation to text-only, single-turn clarification, and thus do not measure multi-turn disambiguation under grounded execution risk. We introduce \textbf{Drift-Bench}, the first diagnostic benchmark that evaluates agentic pragmatics under input faults through multi-turn clarification across state-oriented and service-oriented execution environments. Grounded in classical theories of communication, \textbf{Drift-Bench} provides a unified taxonomy of cooperative breakdowns and employs a persona-driven user simulator with the \textbf{Rise} evaluation protocol. Experiments show substantial performance drops under these faults, with clarification effectiveness varying across user personas and fault types. \MethodName bridges clarification research and agent safety evaluation, enabling systematic diagnosis of failures that can lead to unsafe executions.

AI.tldr: Drift-Bench is the first diagnostic benchmark for evaluating agentic pragmatics under input faults via multi-turn clarification in grounded execution settings. Using a persona-driven user simulator and the Rise evaluation protocol, it reveals substantial performance drops and variable clarification effectiveness across personas and fault types, enabling systematic diagnosis of potential unsafe executions.
AI.motivation: Address the gap in evaluation for autonomous agents facing ill-specified, missing, or ambiguous user inputs beyond text-only, single-turn clarification, especially under grounded execution risks.
AI.method: Introduce a unified taxonomy of cooperative breakdowns; deploy a persona-driven user simulator; apply the Rise evaluation protocol; evaluate across state-oriented and service-oriented execution environments; assess multi-turn clarification under input faults.
AI.result: Experiments show substantial performance degradation when inputs contain faults; effectiveness of clarification varies by user persona and fault type; Drift-Bench bridges clarification research and agent safety evaluation.
AI.conclusion: Drift-Bench provides a systematic framework to diagnose failures that can lead to unsafe executions, guiding the development of safer, more robust agent behavior and multi-turn clarification strategies.

title: Towards Multiscale Graph-based Protein Learning with Geometric Secondary Structural Motifs
summary: Graph neural networks (GNNs) have emerged as powerful tools for learning protein structures by capturing spatial relationships at the residue level. However, existing GNN-based methods often face challenges in learning multiscale representations and modeling long-range dependencies efficiently. In this work, we propose an efficient multiscale graph-based learning framework tailored to proteins. Our proposed framework contains two crucial components: (1) It constructs a hierarchical graph representation comprising a collection of fine-grained subgraphs, each corresponding to a secondary structure motif (e.g., $α$-helices, $β$-strands, loops), and a single coarse-grained graph that connects these motifs based on their spatial arrangement and relative orientation. (2) It employs two GNNs for feature learning: the first operates within individual secondary motifs to capture local interactions, and the second models higher-level structural relationships across motifs. Our modular framework allows a flexible choice of GNN in each stage. Theoretically, we show that our hierarchical framework preserves the desired maximal expressiveness, ensuring no loss of critical structural information. Empirically, we demonstrate that integrating baseline GNNs into our multiscale framework remarkably improves prediction accuracy and reduces computational cost across various benchmarks.

AI.tldr: A modular multiscale GNN framework for proteins that builds fine-grained motif subgraphs and a coarse-grained global graph, using two GNNs (within motifs and across motifs) to achieve efficient, expressive multiscale protein representations.
AI.motivation: Address limitations of existing GNNs in capturing multiscale structure and long-range dependencies in proteins, while reducing computational cost.
AI.method: Create hierarchical graph; fine-grained subgraphs for secondary structure motifs (alpha helices, beta strands, loops) plus a coarse graph linking motifs by spatial arrangement and orientation. Use two GNNs: one within motifs for local interactions, another across motifs for higher-level relationships. Framework is modular, allowing different GNN choices at each stage. Theoretical analysis claims preservation of maximal expressiveness.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation shows improved prediction accuracy and reduced computational cost when integrating baseline GNNs into the multiscale framework across multiple benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: The proposed hierarchical, modular framework enables efficient multiscale protein representation without loss of critical information, and can enhance performance while lowering compute requirements; it also supports flexible GNN selections per stage.

title: MentisOculi: Revealing the Limits of Reasoning with Mental Imagery
summary: Frontier models are transitioning from multimodal large language models (MLLMs) that merely ingest visual information to unified multimodal models (UMMs) capable of native interleaved generation. This shift has sparked interest in using intermediate visualizations as a reasoning aid, akin to human mental imagery. Central to this idea is the ability to form, maintain, and manipulate visual representations in a goal-oriented manner. To evaluate and probe this capability, we develop MentisOculi, a procedural, stratified suite of multi-step reasoning problems amenable to visual solution, tuned to challenge frontier models. Evaluating visual strategies ranging from latent tokens to explicit generated imagery, we find they generally fail to improve performance. Analysis of UMMs specifically exposes a critical limitation: While they possess the textual reasoning capacity to solve a task and can sometimes generate correct visuals, they suffer from compounding generation errors and fail to leverage even ground-truth visualizations. Our findings suggest that despite their inherent appeal, visual thoughts do not yet benefit model reasoning. MentisOculi establishes the necessary foundation to analyze and close this gap across diverse model families.

AI.tldr: MentisOculi shows that visual thoughts do not yet improve reasoning in unified multimodal models, and provides a benchmark to analyze this gap.
AI.motivation: To evaluate whether intermediate visualizations can aid goal-directed reasoning in frontier/unified multimodal models, given the appeal of visual thinking as mental imagery.
AI.method: Create MentisOculi, a procedural, stratified suite of multi-step reasoning tasks solvable visually; assess visual strategies ranging from latent tokens to explicit imagery across frontier models.
AI.result: Visual strategies rarely improve performance; models exhibit compounding generation errors and cannot leverage even ground-truth visuals; textual reasoning alone often suffices but cannot be complemented by visuals.
AI.conclusion: Visual thinking, as currently realized in these models, does not enhance reasoning; MentisOculi provides a foundation to analyze and close this gap across model families.

title: Improving Flow Matching by Aligning Flow Divergence
summary: Conditional flow matching (CFM) stands out as an efficient, simulation-free approach for training flow-based generative models, achieving remarkable performance for data generation. However, CFM is insufficient to ensure accuracy in learning probability paths. In this paper, we introduce a new partial differential equation characterization for the error between the learned and exact probability paths, along with its solution. We show that the total variation gap between the two probability paths is bounded above by a combination of the CFM loss and an associated divergence loss. This theoretical insight leads to the design of a new objective function that simultaneously matches the flow and its divergence. Our new approach improves the performance of the flow-based generative model by a noticeable margin without sacrificing generation efficiency. We showcase the advantages of this enhanced training approach over CFM on several important benchmark tasks, including generative modeling for dynamical systems, DNA sequences, and videos. Code is available at \href{https://github.com/Utah-Math-Data-Science/Flow_Div_Matching}{Utah-Math-Data-Science}.

AI.tldr: Proposes a PDE-based characterization of the error between learned and exact probability paths in conditional flow matching and introduces a joint flow-divergence objective to bound the total variation gap, improving accuracy without sacrificing efficiency; validated on dynamical systems, DNA sequences, and videos; code released.
AI.motivation: CFM is efficient for training flow-based models but does not guarantee accurate probability paths. There is a need for a theory and method that tightens path-level accuracy while preserving efficiency.
AI.method: Derives a partial differential equation describing the probability-path error, proves a total variation bound in terms of the CFM loss and a divergence loss, and designs a new objective that jointly minimizes flow accuracy and divergence.
AI.result: Empirical gains of a noticeable margin on benchmark tasks with maintained generation speed; code is publicly available.
AI.conclusion: A theoretically grounded, practical objective improves path accuracy in flow-based models and generalizes across domains; encourages joint optimization of flow and divergence.

title: Avenir-Web: Human-Experience-Imitating Multimodal Web Agents with Mixture of Grounding Experts
summary: Despite advances in multimodal large language models, autonomous web agents still struggle to reliably execute long-horizon tasks on complex and dynamic web interfaces. Existing agents often suffer from inaccurate element grounding, the absence of site-specific procedural knowledge, and unstable long-term task tracking and memory, particularly when operating over complex Document Object Model structures. To address these limitations, we introduce Avenir-Web, a web agent that achieves a new open-source state of the art on the Online-Mind2Web benchmark in real-world deployment. Avenir-Web leverages a Mixture of Grounding Experts, Experience-Imitation Planning for incorporating procedural priors, and a task-tracking checklist combined with adaptive memory to enable robust and seamless interaction across diverse user interface paradigms. We evaluate Avenir-Web on Online-Mind2Web, a rigorous benchmark of live and user-centered web tasks. Our results demonstrate that Avenir-Web significantly surpasses prior open-source agents and attains performance parity with top-tier proprietary models, thereby establishing a new open-source state of the art for reliable web agents on live websites.

AI.tldr: Avenir-Web proposes a new open-source web agent that achieves state-of-the-art performance on Online-Mind2Web by combining a Mixture of Grounding Experts, Experience-Imitation Planning for procedural priors, and a task-tracking checklist with adaptive memory, enabling robust long-horizon web tasks across diverse UIs.
AI.motivation: Address core limitations of existing multimodal LLM web agents: inaccurate element grounding, lack of site-specific procedural knowledge, and unstable long-term task tracking/memory on complex DOMs, hindering reliable real-world deployment.
AI.method: Introduce Avenir-Web architecture with a Mixture of Grounding Experts for robust element grounding; Experience-Imitation Planning to inject procedural priors; a task-tracking checklist plus adaptive memory to maintain long-horizon consistency; evaluate on Online-Mind2Web benchmark with live, user-centered tasks.
AI.result: Avenir-Web significantly surpasses prior open-source agents and achieves parity with top-tier proprietary models on Online-Mind2Web, establishing a new open-source state of the art for reliable web agents on live websites.
AI.conclusion: The framework demonstrates robust, generalizable automation across diverse web interfaces and strengthens open-source capabilities for real-world web interaction, potentially accelerating development of reliable autonomous web agents.

title: Learning Heat-based Equations in Self-similar variables
summary: We study solution learning for heat-based equations in self-similar variables (SSV). We develop an SSV training framework compatible with standard neural-operator training. We instantiate this framework on the two-dimensional incompressible Navier-Stokes equations and the one-dimensional viscous Burgers equation, and perform controlled comparisons between models trained in physical coordinates and in the corresponding self-similar coordinates using two simple fully connected architectures (standard multilayer perceptrons and a factorized fully connected network). Across both systems and both architectures, SSV-trained networks consistently deliver substantially more accurate and stable extrapolation beyond the training window and better capture qualitative long-time trends. These results suggest that self-similar coordinates provide a mathematically motivated inductive bias for learning the long-time dynamics of heat-based equations.

AI.tldr: Self-similar variables improve long-time generalization in neural-operator learning for heat-like PDEs; demonstrated on 2D Navier–Stokes and 1D Burgers, with MLP and factorized FC nets, outperforming physical-coordinate training in extrapolation.
AI.motivation: Long-time dynamics of heat-based PDEs are challenging for ML models. Exploiting self-similarity provides a principled inductive bias to enhance extrapolation and stability.
AI.method: Introduce a self-similar variables (SSV) training framework compatible with standard neural-operator training. Apply it to 2D incompressible Navier–Stokes and 1D viscous Burgers equations. Compare models trained in physical coordinates vs self-similar coordinates using two simple FC architectures (MLP and a factorized FC). Evaluate extrapolation beyond the training window and long-time trend capture.
AI.result: SSV-trained networks achieve substantially more accurate and stable extrapolation beyond the training window and better capture qualitative long-time trends, consistently across both systems and both architectures.
AI.conclusion: Self-similar coordinates provide a mathematically motivated inductive bias for learning the long-time dynamics of heat-based equations, as evidenced by improved extrapolation and stability across tested PDEs and architectures.

title: Breaking the Reversal Curse in Autoregressive Language Models via Identity Bridge
summary: Autoregressive large language models (LLMs) have achieved remarkable success in many complex tasks, yet they can still fail in very simple logical reasoning such as the "reversal curse" -- when trained on forward knowledge data of the form "$A \rightarrow B$" (e.g., Alice's husband is Bob), the model is unable to deduce the reversal knowledge "$B \leftarrow A$" (e.g., Bob's wife is Alice) during test. Extensive prior research suggests that this failure is an inherent, fundamental limit of autoregressive causal LLMs, indicating that these models tend to memorize factual-level knowledge rather than capture higher-level rules. In this paper, we challenge this view by showing that this seemingly fundamental limit can be mitigated by slightly tweaking the training data with a simple regularization data recipe called the Identity Bridge of the form "$A \to A$" (e.g., The name of Alice is Alice). Theoretically, we prove that under this recipe, even a one-layer transformer can break the reversal curse by analyzing the implicit bias of gradient descent. Empirically, we show that a 1B pretrained language model finetuned with the proposed data recipe achieves a 40% success rate on reversal tasks, in stark contrast to a near-zero success rate when trained solely on forward-knowledge data. Our work provides a novel theoretical foundation for the reversal curse and offers a principled, low-cost path to encouraging LLMs to learn higher-level rules from data.

AI.tldr: Identity Bridge data (A -> A) regularization mitigates the reversal curse in autoregressive LLMs; theoretical analysis shows gradient-descent bias enables reversal-rule learning with a simple one-layer transformer; empirically yields ~40% reversal success on a 1B model, vs near-zero with only forward data.
AI.motivation: Challenging the view that the reversal curse is an inherent limitation of autoregressive LLMs by exploring data-level regularization that could promote learning of higher-level rules.
AI.method: Introduce an Identity Bridge data recipe (A -> A). Theoretically analyze the implicit bias of gradient descent enabling a one-layer transformer to acquire reverse inferences. Empirically finetune a 1B pretrained LLM with the recipe and evaluate reversal task performance.
AI.result: The finetuned 1B model achieves about 40% success on reversal tasks, substantially higher than near-zero when trained only on forward-knowledge data.
AI.conclusion: The reversal curse can be mitigated through simple data regularization, providing a theoretical and practical pathway to induce higher-level rule learning in LLMs with low additional cost.

title: Dynamic Expert Sharing: Decoupling Memory from Parallelism in Mixture-of-Experts Diffusion LLMs
summary: Among parallel decoding paradigms, diffusion large language models (dLLMs) have emerged as a promising candidate that balances generation quality and throughput. However, their integration with Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architectures is constrained by an expert explosion: as the number of tokens generated in parallel increases, the number of distinct experts activated grows nearly linearly. This results in substantial memory traffic that pushes inference into a memory-bound regime, negating the efficiency gains of both MoE and parallel decoding. To address this challenge, we propose Dynamic Expert Sharing (DES), a novel technique that shifts MoE optimization from token-centric pruning and conventional expert skipping methods to sequence-level coreset selection. To maximize expert reuse, DES identifies a compact, high-utility set of experts to satisfy the requirements of an entire parallel decoding block. We introduce two innovative selection strategies: (1) Intra-Sequence Sharing (DES-Seq), which adapts optimal allocation to the sequence level, and (2) Saliency-Aware Voting (DES-Vote), a novel mechanism that allows tokens to collectively elect a coreset based on aggregated router weights. Extensive experiments on MoE dLLMs demonstrate that DES reduces unique expert activations by over 55% and latency by up to 38%, while retaining 99% of vanilla accuracy, effectively decoupling memory overhead from the degree of parallelism.

AI.tldr: DES (Dynamic Expert Sharing) reduces unique MoE activations and latency in diffusion LLMs with parallel decoding by sharing a compact, high-utility set of experts across a decoding block via sequence-level coreset selection.
AI.motivation: MoE-based LLMs suffer an expert explosion under parallel decoding: as more tokens are generated in parallel, more distinct experts are activated, causing memory traffic and memory-bound inference that undermines MoE and parallelism gains.
AI.method: Introduce Dynamic Expert Sharing with two selection strategies: (1) DES-Seq for intra-sequence, sequence-level optimal allocation to maximize reuse, and (2) DES-Vote for saliency-aware voting where tokens collectively elect a coreset based on aggregated router weights, forming a compact expert set for a parallel decoding block.
AI.result: DES reduces the number of unique activated experts by over 55% and lowers latency by up to 38%, while preserving about 99% of vanilla accuracy, effectively decoupling memory overhead from the degree of parallelism.
AI.conclusion: DES enables efficient MoE diffusion LLMs by enabling substantial expert reuse across parallel decoding blocks, mitigating memory traffic and restoring gains from MoE and parallel decoding.

title: AgentRx: Diagnosing AI Agent Failures from Execution Trajectories
summary: AI agents often fail in ways that are difficult to localize because executions are probabilistic, long-horizon, multi-agent, and mediated by noisy tool outputs. We address this gap by manually annotating failed agent runs and release a novel benchmark of 115 failed trajectories spanning structured API workflows, incident management, and open-ended web/file tasks. Each trajectory is annotated with a critical failure step and a category from a grounded-theory derived, cross-domain failure taxonomy. To mitigate the human cost of failure attribution, we present AGENTRX, an automated domain-agnostic diagnostic framework that pinpoints the critical failure step in a failed agent trajectory. It synthesizes constraints, evaluates them step-by-step, and produces an auditable validation log of constraint violations with associated evidence; an LLM-based judge uses this log to localize the critical step and category. Our framework improves step localization and failure attribution over existing baselines across three domains.

AI.tldr: A new benchmark of 115 failed AI agent trajectories and an automated diagnostic framework (AGENTRX) to localize the critical failure step using constraints and LLM-based judgment, improving fault localization across multiple domains.
AI.motivation: Failures in AI agents are hard to localize due to probabilistic, long-horizon, multi-agent dynamics and noisy tool outputs; a domain-agnostic, auditable diagnostic tool is needed to pinpoint where and why failures occur while reducing human annotation burden.
AI.method: Manual annotation of failed agent runs to create a cross-domain benchmark; development of AGENTRX that (1) synthesizes constraints, (2) evaluates them step-by-step, (3) generates an auditable log of constraint violations with evidence; (4) uses an LLM-based judge to localize the critical failure step and its category.
AI.result: AGENTRX achieves improved step localization and failure attribution over existing baselines across three domains.
AI.conclusion: The work provides a domain-agnostic diagnostic framework for localizing critical failure steps and generating auditable evidence, validated on a benchmark of 115 failed trajectories across structured API workflows, incident management, and open-ended tasks.

title: Test-time Generalization for Physics through Neural Operator Splitting
summary: Neural operators have shown promise in learning solution maps of partial differential equations (PDEs), but they often struggle to generalize when test inputs lie outside the training distribution, such as novel initial conditions, unseen PDE coefficients or unseen physics. Prior works address this limitation with large-scale multiple physics pretraining followed by fine-tuning, but this still requires examples from the new dynamics, falling short of true zero-shot generalization. In this work, we propose a method to enhance generalization at test time, i.e., without modifying pretrained weights. Building on DISCO, which provides a dictionary of neural operators trained across different dynamics, we introduce a neural operator splitting strategy that, at test time, searches over compositions of training operators to approximate unseen dynamics. On challenging out-of-distribution tasks including parameter extrapolation and novel combinations of physics phenomena, our approach achieves state-of-the-art zero-shot generalization results, while being able to recover the underlying PDE parameters. These results underscore test-time computation as a key avenue for building flexible, compositional, and generalizable neural operators.

AI.tldr: A method to improve zero-shot generalization of neural operators by test-time composition search over a DISCO-like dictionary of trained operators, without changing pretrained weights.
AI.motivation: Neural operators often fail to generalize to out-of-distribution inputs (novel initial conditions, coefficients, or physics). Large pretraining with fine-tuning is not true zero-shot generalization and still requires data from new dynamics.
AI.method: At test time, perform a search over compositions of training neural operators from a DISCO-style dictionary to approximate unseen dynamics (neural operator splitting). No weight updates; leverages test-time computation to assemble operators that simulate new PDE dynamics.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art zero-shot generalization on challenging OOD tasks such as parameter extrapolation and novel physics combinations; can recover underlying PDE parameters.
AI.conclusion: Test-time computation with operator composition offers a flexible, compositional approach to generalization for neural operators, enabling zero-shot adaptation without retraining.

title: Privacy in Practice: Private COVID-19 Detection in X-Ray Images (Extended Version)
comment: Extended version of the paper accepted at the 20th International Conference on Security and Cryptography SECRYPT 2023. This version is more detailed and includes additional content: a longer results chapter and an appendix containing a proof
summary: Machine learning (ML) can help fight pandemics like COVID-19 by enabling rapid screening of large volumes of images. To perform data analysis while maintaining patient privacy, we create ML models that satisfy Differential Privacy (DP). Previous works exploring private COVID-19 models are in part based on small datasets, provide weaker or unclear privacy guarantees, and do not investigate practical privacy. We suggest improvements to address these open gaps. We account for inherent class imbalances and evaluate the utility-privacy trade-off more extensively and over stricter privacy budgets. Our evaluation is supported by empirically estimating practical privacy through black-box Membership Inference Attacks (MIAs). The introduced DP should help limit leakage threats posed by MIAs, and our practical analysis is the first to test this hypothesis on the COVID-19 classification task. Our results indicate that needed privacy levels might differ based on the task-dependent practical threat from MIAs. The results further suggest that with increasing DP guarantees, empirical privacy leakage only improves marginally, and DP therefore appears to have a limited impact on practical MIA defense. Our findings identify possibilities for better utility-privacy trade-offs, and we believe that empirical attack-specific privacy estimation can play a vital role in tuning for practical privacy.

AI.tldr: Differentially private COVID-19 image classifiers evaluated against practical privacy via black-box membership inference attacks; DP provides limited improvement against MIAs and privacy needs vary by task; advocates attack-aware tuning to improve utility-privacy trade-offs.
AI.motivation: Address privacy risks in ML-based COVID-19 screening. Existing private COVID-19 models rely on small datasets and weak or unclear practical privacy guarantees; the work aims to strengthen practical privacy assessments and improve utility under DP, accounting for class imbalance and stronger privacy budgets.
AI.method: Train image classifiers on COVID-19 data with differential privacy mechanisms (e.g., DP-SGD) considering class imbalance; evaluate utility across privacy budgets; estimate practical privacy using black-box membership inference attacks; compare to non-private baselines.
AI.result: Differential privacy helps mitigate some MIA leakage but gains saturate with higher privacy budgets; practical leakage reductions are marginal, indicating DP alone may be insufficient for strong practical privacy; results vary with the attack threat level and task.
AI.conclusion: Attack-aware, empirical privacy estimation is valuable for tuning DP parameters to practical privacy needs; DP should be used in conjunction with threat-model-specific evaluation to achieve better utility-privacy trade-offs; identifies avenues for refining private COVID-19 image classifiers.

title: Reliability-Aware Determinantal Point Processes for Robust Informative Data Selection in Large Language Models
summary: Informative data selection is a key requirement for large language models (LLMs) to minimize the amount of data required for fine-tuning, network distillation, and token pruning, enabling fast and efficient deployment, especially under computational and communication constraints. Traditional subset selection methods, including those based on Determinantal Point Processes (DPP), focus on maximizing diversity but assume that selected data batches are always available error-free. This presumption prohibits their use under partial storage outage, imperfect communication, and stochastic access failures. Furthermore, we show that the original formulation collapses under such conditions. To address this gap, we introduce ProbDPP, a novel reliability-aware implementation of k-DPP that accounts for probabilistic data access by recasting the objective function with a regularization term that remains well-posed and decomposes into a geometric diversity term and unreliability cost. The resulting objective facilitates robust selection of diverse data batches under uncertainty. Furthermore, we frame this reliability-aware diversity maximization as a combinatorial semi-bandit problem and propose a UCB-style algorithm to efficiently learn the unknown reliability online. Theoretical analysis provides regret bounds for the proposed approach, ensuring performance guarantees.

AI.tldr: ProbDPP: a reliability-aware k-DPP for robust data subset selection under probabilistic access, with a UCB-style semi-bandit algorithm and regret guarantees.
AI.motivation: Need for informative data selection for LLMs under data outages, imperfect communication, or stochastic access failures. Traditional DPP assumes error-free data availability and collapses under uncertainty; there is a gap for reliability-aware subset selection.
AI.method: Introduce ProbDPP by regularizing the k-DPP objective with an unreliability term. The objective splits into a geometric diversity component and an unreliability cost, making it well-posed under data access uncertainty. Reformulate as a combinatorial semi-bandit problem and develop a UCB-style algorithm to learn data reliability online.
AI.result: The approach enables robust, diverse data batch selection under uncertainty; provides theoretical regret bounds for the learning algorithm, ensuring performance guarantees.
AI.conclusion: Proposes a reliability-aware framework for diversity maximization in data selection, suitable for LLM training under practical data-access constraints, with provable guarantees.

title: Generating Synthetic Health Sensor Data for Privacy-Preserving Wearable Stress Detection
comment: Published in the MDPI Sensors Journal
summary: Smartwatch health sensor data are increasingly utilized in smart health applications and patient monitoring, including stress detection. However, such medical data often comprise sensitive personal information and are resource-intensive to acquire for research purposes. In response to this challenge, we introduce the privacy-aware synthetization of multi-sensor smartwatch health readings related to moments of stress, employing Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs) and Differential Privacy (DP) safeguards. Our method not only protects patient information but also enhances data availability for research. To ensure its usefulness, we test synthetic data from multiple GANs and employ different data enhancement strategies on an actual stress detection task. Our GAN-based augmentation methods demonstrate significant improvements in model performance, with private DP training scenarios observing an 11.90-15.48% increase in F1-score, while non-private training scenarios still see a 0.45% boost. These results underline the potential of differentially private synthetic data in optimizing utility-privacy trade-offs, especially with the limited availability of real training samples. Through rigorous quality assessments, we confirm the integrity and plausibility of our synthetic data, which, however, are significantly impacted when increasing privacy requirements.

AI.tldr: Generates privacy-aware synthetic smartwatch sensor data for stress detection using GANs with differential privacy; augmentation improves model performance, especially under DP, showing ~12-15% F1-score gains, while non-private gains are smaller (~0.5%); data quality declines as privacy constraints tighten.
AI.motivation: Address the tension between data utility and privacy in smartwatch health data; enable research with sensitive personal information and limited real data.
AI.method: Synthesize multi-sensor smartwatch readings related to stress using GANs; apply differential privacy safeguards; evaluate multiple GAN variants and data-augmentation strategies on a real stress-detection task; compare private DP vs non-private training; assess data quality.
AI.result: DP-trained synthetic data improve F1-score by about 11.9–15.48% on stress detection; non-private augmentation yields ~0.45% gain; synthetic data quality is validated but degrades with stronger privacy constraints.
AI.conclusion: Differentially private synthetic data from GANs can meaningfully boost model performance in stress detection under privacy constraints, highlighting a useful utility-privacy trade-off; still, stronger DP reduces data realism and usefulness, underscoring the need for careful privacy budget selection and robust quality assessments.

title: GAPNet: Plug-in Jointly Learning Task-Specific Graph for Dynamic Stock Relation
summary: The advent of the web has led to a paradigm shift in the financial relations, with the real-time dissemination of news, social discourse, and financial filings contributing significantly to the reshaping of financial forecasting. The existing methods rely on establishing relations a priori, i.e. predefining graphs to capture inter-stock relationships. However, the stock-related web signals are characterised by high levels of noise, asynchrony, and challenging to obtain, resulting in poor generalisability and non-alignment between the predefined graphs and the downstream tasks. To address this, we propose GAPNet, a Graph Adaptation Plug-in Network that jointly learns task-specific topology and representations in an end-to-end manner. GAPNet attaches to existing pairwise graph or hypergraph backbone models, enabling the dynamic adaptation and rewiring of edge topologies via two complementary components: a Spatial Perception Layer that captures short-term co-movements across assets, and a Temporal Perception Layer that maintains long-term dependency under distribution shift. Across two real-world stock datasets, GAPNet has been shown to consistently enhance the profitability and stability in comparision to the state-of-the-art models, yielding annualised cumulative returns of up to 0.47 for RT-GCN and 0.63 for CI-STHPAN, with peak Sharpe Ratio of 2.20 and 2.12 respectively. The plug-and-play design of GAPNet ensures its broad applicability to diverse GNN-based architectures. Our results underscore that jointly learning graph structures and representations is essential for task-specific relational modeling.

AI.tldr: GAPNet is a plug-in Graph Adaptation Network that jointly learns task-specific topology and representations for stock forecasting, improving performance and stability over baselines by dynamically rewiring edges via Spatial and Temporal Perception layers.
AI.motivation: Stock-related web signals are noisy, asynchronous, and difficult to obtain. Predefined graph structures fail to generalize to downstream tasks and shift distributions, necessitating a method that learns topology and representations jointly.
AI.method: GAPNet attaches to existing pairwise graph or hypergraph backbones and introduces two components: a Spatial Perception Layer for short-term co-movements and a Temporal Perception Layer for long-term dependencies under distribution shift, enabling dynamic edge rewiring in an end-to-end manner.
AI.result: On two real-world stock datasets, GAPNet consistently outperforms state-of-the-art models in profitability and stability, achieving annualized cumulative returns up to 0.47 (RT-GCN) and 0.63 (CI-STHPAN) with peak Sharpe ratios of 2.20 and 2.12; the design is plug-and-play across GNN architectures.
AI.conclusion: Jointly learning graph structures and representations is essential for task-specific relational modeling; GAPNet’s plug-in approach offers broad applicability and improved robustness across datasets and backbones.

title: Assessing the Impact of Image Dataset Features on Privacy-Preserving Machine Learning
comment: Accepted at 21st Conference on Database Systems for Business, Technology and Web (BTW 2025)
summary: Machine Learning (ML) is crucial in many sectors, including computer vision. However, ML models trained on sensitive data face security challenges, as they can be attacked and leak information. Privacy-Preserving Machine Learning (PPML) addresses this by using Differential Privacy (DP) to balance utility and privacy. This study identifies image dataset characteristics that affect the utility and vulnerability of private and non-private Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) models. Through analyzing multiple datasets and privacy budgets, we find that imbalanced datasets increase vulnerability in minority classes, but DP mitigates this issue. Datasets with fewer classes improve both model utility and privacy, while high entropy or low Fisher Discriminant Ratio (FDR) datasets deteriorate the utility-privacy trade-off. These insights offer valuable guidance for practitioners and researchers in estimating and optimizing the utility-privacy trade-off in image datasets, helping to inform data and privacy modifications for better outcomes based on dataset characteristics.

AI.tldr: Dataset characteristics greatly influence the utility-privacy trade-off in privacy-preserving CNNs; imbalanced minority classes are more vulnerable but DP mitigates this; fewer classes improve both utility and privacy; high entropy or low Fisher Discriminant Ratio (FDR) worsen the trade-off.
AI.motivation: To understand how image dataset properties affect the utility and vulnerability of DP-CNN models and to provide guidelines for optimizing privacy-utility trade-offs.
AI.method: Empirical analysis over multiple image datasets and privacy budgets (epsilon) to evaluate utility (accuracy) and privacy leakage for private vs non-private CNNs; examine effects of class imbalance, number of classes, dataset entropy, and Fisher Discriminant Ratio.
AI.result: Key findings: (1) class imbalance increases vulnerability in minority classes, which DP mitigates; (2) datasets with fewer classes improve both model utility and privacy; (3) high entropy or low FDR datasets degrade the utility-privacy trade-off.
AI.conclusion: Dataset-aware strategies are essential for PPML deployment: use dataset characteristics to estimate the utility-privacy frontier and guide data collection, preprocessing, and privacy budget choices to achieve better outcomes.

title: Domain-Adaptive and Scalable Dense Retrieval for Content-Based Recommendation
comment: 13 pages, 4 figures. Semantic dense retrieval for content-based recommendation on Amazon Reviews 2023 (Category - Fashion). Dataset statistics: 2.0M users; 825.9K items; 2.5M ratings; 94.9M review tokens; 510.5M metadata tokens. Timespan: May 1996 to September 2023. Metadata includes: user reviews (ratings, text, helpfulness votes, etc.); item metadata (descriptions, price, raw images, etc.)
summary:
E-commerce recommendation and search commonly rely on sparse keyword matching (e.g., BM25), which breaks down under vocabulary mismatch when user intent has limited lexical overlap with product metadata. We cast content-based recommendation as recommendation-as-retrieval: given a natural-language intent signal (a query or review), retrieve the top-K most relevant items from a large catalog via semantic similarity.
  We present a scalable dense retrieval system based on a two-tower bi-encoder, fine-tuned on the Amazon Reviews 2023 (Fashion) subset using supervised contrastive learning with Multiple Negatives Ranking Loss. We construct training pairs from review text (as a query proxy) and item metadata (as the positive document) and fine-tune on 50,000 sampled interactions with a maximum sequence length of 500 tokens.
  For efficient serving, we combine FAISS HNSW indexing with an ONNX Runtime inference pipeline using INT8 dynamic quantization. On a review-to-title benchmark over 826,402 catalog items, our approach improves Recall@10 from 0.26 (BM25) to 0.66, while meeting practical latency and model-size constraints: 6.1 ms median CPU inference latency (batch size 1) and a 4x reduction in model size.
  Overall, we provide an end-to-end, reproducible blueprint for taking domain-adapted dense retrieval from offline training to CPU-efficient serving at catalog scale.

AI.tldr: A scalable dense retrieval pipeline for e-commerce using a two-tower bi-encoder trained with supervised contrastive learning; achieves strong recall gains over BM25 on a review-to-title task; deploys with FAISS HNSW and ONNX Runtime INT8 for CPU-efficient serving; demonstrates end-to-end reproducibility for domain-adapted dense retrieval at catalog scale.
AI.motivation: Overcome vocabulary mismatch and sparse keyword matching in e-commerce search by treating content-based recommendation as retrieval using semantic similarity between natural-language intents (queries or reviews) and product metadata.
AI.method: Two-tower bi-encoder trained on Amazon Reviews 2023 (Fashion) subset with supervised contrastive learning using Multiple Negatives Ranking Loss. Training pairs consist of review text as query proxy and item metadata as positive document; fine-tuned on 50,000 interactions with max sequence length 500. For serving, use FAISS HNSW indexing with an ONNX Runtime inference pipeline employing INT8 dynamic quantization.
AI.result: On a review-to-title benchmark of 826,402 catalog items, Recall@10 improves from 0.26 (BM25) to 0.66. Latency is 6.1 ms median CPU inference (batch size 1) with about 4x model-size reduction.
AI.conclusion: Provides an end-to-end, reproducible blueprint for domain-adapted dense retrieval from offline training to CPU-efficient serving at catalog scale.

title: Federated Learning With Individualized Privacy Through Client Sampling
comment: Accepted at 10th International Conference on Machine Learning Technologies (ICMLT 2025)
summary: With growing concerns about user data collection, individualized privacy has emerged as a promising solution to balance protection and utility by accounting for diverse user privacy preferences. Instead of enforcing a uniform level of anonymization for all users, this approach allows individuals to choose privacy settings that align with their comfort levels. Building on this idea, we propose an adapted method for enabling Individualized Differential Privacy (IDP) in Federated Learning (FL) by handling clients according to their personal privacy preferences. By extending the SAMPLE algorithm from centralized settings to FL, we calculate client-specific sampling rates based on their heterogeneous privacy budgets and integrate them into a modified IDP-FedAvg algorithm. We test this method under realistic privacy distributions and multiple datasets. The experimental results demonstrate that our approach achieves clear improvements over uniform DP baselines, reducing the trade-off between privacy and utility. Compared to the alternative SCALE method in related work, which assigns differing noise scales to clients, our method performs notably better. However, challenges remain for complex tasks with non-i.i.d. data, primarily stemming from the constraints of the decentralized setting.

AI.tldr: Extends individualized differential privacy to federated learning by using client-specific sampling rates derived from privacy budgets via an adapted SAMPLE algorithm within a modified IDP-FedAvg, yielding better privacy-utility trade-offs than uniform DP and SCALE, with remaining non-iid data challenges.
AI.motivation: Balances diverse user privacy preferences with model utility in Federated Learning. Uniform DP imposes a single privacy level; SCALE varies noise scales without adapting sampling. This work introduces per-client sampling rates (via an adapted SAMPLE) to reflect heterogeneous privacy budgets in a decentralized FL setting.
AI.method: Extend the centralized SAMPLE algorithm to FL by computing client-specific sampling rates from heterogeneous privacy budgets and integrating these rates into a modified IDP-FedAvg training procedure. Evaluate under realistic privacy distributions across multiple datasets.
AI.result: Shows clear improvements over uniform DP baselines, reducing the privacy-utility trade-off. Outperforms the SCALE method by assigning different sampling rates rather than different noise scales. However, challenges persist for complex tasks with non-iid data due to decentralized constraints.
AI.conclusion: Per-client privacy-aware sampling in FL (IDP-FedAvg) is a practical approach to improve privacy-utility trade-offs under user heterogeneity, but non-iid data and decentralized constraints remain open challenges requiring further research.

title: Hallucination is a Consequence of Space-Optimality: A Rate-Distortion Theorem for Membership Testing
summary: Large language models often hallucinate with high confidence on "random facts" that lack inferable patterns. We formalize the memorization of such facts as a membership testing problem, unifying the discrete error metrics of Bloom filters with the continuous log-loss of LLMs. By analyzing this problem in the regime where facts are sparse in the universe of plausible claims, we establish a rate-distortion theorem: the optimal memory efficiency is characterized by the minimum KL divergence between score distributions on facts and non-facts. This theoretical framework provides a distinctive explanation for hallucination: even with optimal training, perfect data, and a simplified "closed world" setting, the information-theoretically optimal strategy under limited capacity is not to abstain or forget, but to assign high confidence to some non-facts, resulting in hallucination. We validate this theory empirically on synthetic data, showing that hallucinations persist as a natural consequence of lossy compression.

AI.tldr: Rate–distortion view of LLM memorization shows under limited capacity, optimal strategy can hedge by confidently asserting non-facts, causing hallucinations; validated on synthetic data.
AI.motivation: Explain persistent hallucinations in LLMs by a principled information-theoretic framework that ties memorization to a rate-distortion trade-off and KL-divergence between fact and non-fact score distributions.
AI.method: Model memorized facts as a membership test; unify Bloom-filter-like discrete errors with LLM log-loss; analyze sparse-facts regime; derive a rate-distortion theorem; identify optimal memory strategy via minimizing KL divergence between score distributions; empirical validation on synthetic data.
AI.result: The optimal memory efficiency equals the minimum KL divergence between fact and non-fact score distributions; hallucination emerges as the optimal strategy under capacity limits; experiments show hallucinations persist due to lossy compression.
AI.conclusion: Offers a fundamental explanation for hallucination as a consequence of information-theoretic limits; even perfect data and closed-world assumptions do not prevent high-confidence non-fact outputs; highlights a compression-driven trade-off in LLM behavior.

title: Reinforcement Learning via Conservative Agent for Environments with Random Delays
summary: Real-world reinforcement learning applications are often hindered by delayed feedback from environments, which violates the Markov assumption and introduces significant challenges. Although numerous delay-compensating methods have been proposed for environments with constant delays, environments with random delays remain largely unexplored due to their inherent variability and unpredictability. In this study, we propose a simple yet robust agent for decision-making under random delays, termed the conservative agent, which reformulates the random-delay environment into its constant-delay equivalent. This transformation enables any state-of-the-art constant-delay method to be directly extended to the random-delay environments without modifying the algorithmic structure or sacrificing performance. We evaluate the conservative agent-based algorithm on continuous control tasks, and empirical results demonstrate that it significantly outperforms existing baseline algorithms in terms of asymptotic performance and sample efficiency.

AI.tldr: A conservative agent converts random-delay RL environments into a constant-delay equivalent, enabling direct use of constant-delay methods and achieving superior performance and sample efficiency on continuous control tasks.
AI.motivation: Delayed feedback in real-world RL violates the Markov assumption; random delays introduce variability/unpredictability; existing delay-compensation methods mainly address fixed delays; a simple, robust approach is needed to handle random delays without changing core algorithms.
AI.method: Introduce a Conservative Agent that reformulates the random-delay environment into its constant-delay equivalent, effectively transforming the problem so that constant-delay algorithms can be applied unchanged. This preserves algorithmic structure while achieving robustness to varying delays.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on continuous control benchmarks shows the approach significantly outperforms baselines in asymptotic performance and sample efficiency.
AI.conclusion: The Conservative Agent offers a simple, robust, and generalizable approach to handling random delays in real-world RL, enabling direct extension of state-of-the-art constant-delay methods with improved performance and data efficiency.

title: PyGALAX: An Open-Source Python Toolkit for Advanced Explainable Geospatial Machine Learning
summary: PyGALAX is a Python package for geospatial analysis that integrates automated machine learning (AutoML) and explainable artificial intelligence (XAI) techniques to analyze spatial heterogeneity in both regression and classification tasks. It automatically selects and optimizes machine learning models for different geographic locations and contexts while maintaining interpretability through SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations) analysis. PyGALAX builds upon and improves the GALAX framework (Geospatial Analysis Leveraging AutoML and eXplainable AI), which has proven to outperform traditional geographically weighted regression (GWR) methods. Critical enhancements in PyGALAX from the original GALAX framework include automatic bandwidth selection and flexible kernel function selection, providing greater flexibility and robustness for spatial modeling across diverse datasets and research questions. PyGALAX not only inherits all the functionalities of the original GALAX framework but also packages them into an accessible, reproducible, and easily deployable Python toolkit while providing additional options for spatial modeling. It effectively addresses spatial non-stationarity and generates transparent insights into complex spatial relationships at both global and local scales, making advanced geospatial machine learning methods accessible to researchers and practitioners in geography, urban planning, environmental science, and related fields.

AI.tldr: PyGALAX automates GeoML with AutoML and SHAP explanations, offering location-specific models and automatic bandwidth/kernel selection, improving GALAX and GWR.
AI.motivation: Address spatial non-stationarity and heterogeneity while keeping models interpretable and accessible to researchers across geography-related fields.
AI.method: Extend GALAX with AutoML-driven model selection per location, automatic bandwidth selection, flexible kernel functions, and SHAP-based global/local explanations; package into a Python toolkit.
AI.result: Claims that GALAX outperforms traditional GWR; PyGALAX inherits GALAX functionality, adds automation, bandwidth/kernel flexibility, and deliverable Python toolkit for reproducible analyses.
AI.conclusion: PyGALAX provides a robust, flexible, and interpretable geospatial ML framework for regression and classification, enabling transparent insights into spatial relationships across global and local scales.

title: Efficient Deep Learning for Medical Imaging: Bridging the Gap Between High-Performance AI and Clinical Deployment
summary: Deep learning has revolutionized medical image analysis, playing a vital role in modern clinical applications. However, the deployment of large-scale models in real-world clinical settings remains challenging due to high computational costs, latency constraints, and patient data privacy concerns associated with cloud-based processing. To address these bottlenecks, this review provides a comprehensive synthesis of efficient and lightweight deep learning architectures specifically tailored for the medical domain. We categorize the landscape of modern efficient models into three primary streams: Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs), Lightweight Transformers, and emerging Linear Complexity Models. Furthermore, we examine key model compression strategies (including pruning, quantization, knowledge distillation, and low-rank factorization) and evaluate their efficacy in maintaining diagnostic performance while reducing hardware requirements. By identifying current limitations and discussing the transition toward on-device intelligence, this review serves as a roadmap for researchers and practitioners aiming to bridge the gap between high-performance AI and resource-constrained clinical environments.

AI.tldr: A comprehensive survey of efficient, on-device deep learning models for medical imaging, focusing on CNNs, lightweight transformers, and linear-complexity models, and on model compression techniques to reduce compute and memory while preserving diagnostic accuracy.
AI.motivation: Clinical deployment is hindered by high computational costs, latency, and patient privacy concerns with cloud processing; there is a need for efficient, on-device AI tailored to medical image analysis.
AI.method: Literature synthesis categorizing modern efficient models into three streams (CNNs, Lightweight Transformers, Linear Complexity Models), and evaluating model compression strategies (pruning, quantization, knowledge distillation, low-rank factorization) for preserving diagnostic performance with reduced hardware requirements; discusses limitations and a roadmap toward on-device intelligence.
AI.result: Provides a structured landscape of efficient medical imaging models across three model streams, assesses the effectiveness of compression methods in maintaining diagnostic accuracy, identifies current limitations, and outlines directions toward on-device AI in clinical settings.
AI.conclusion: A roadmap that bridges high-performance AI with resource-constrained clinical environments, emphasizing continued development of efficient architectures and privacy-preserving on-device deployments.

title: Early Classification of Time Series in Non-Stationary Cost Regimes
summary: Early Classification of Time Series (ECTS) addresses decision-making problems in which predictions must be made as early as possible while maintaining high accuracy. Most existing ECTS methods assume that the time-dependent decision costs governing the learning objective are known, fixed, and correctly specified. In practice, however, these costs are often uncertain and may change over time, leading to mismatches between training-time and deployment-time objectives. In this paper, we study ECTS under two practically relevant forms of cost non-stationarity: drift in the balance between misclassification and decision delay costs, and stochastic realizations of decision costs that deviate from the nominal training-time model. To address these challenges, we revisit representative ECTS approaches and adapt them to an online learning setting. Focusing on separable methods, we update only the triggering model during deployment, while keeping the classifier fixed. We propose several online adaptations and baselines, including bandit-based and RL-based approaches, and conduct controlled experiments on synthetic data to systematically evaluate robustness under cost non-stationarity. Our results demonstrate that online learning can effectively improve the robustness of ECTS methods to cost drift, with RL-based strategies exhibiting strong and stable performance across varying cost regimes.

AI.tldr: Online adaptations of separable Early Classification of Time Series (ECTS) under cost non-stationarity, using bandit and RL-based methods to update triggering rules online while keeping the classifier fixed; results on synthetic data show RL-based methods offer robust performance across drift regimes.
AI.motivation: ECTS relies on a fixed, known cost model for decision delay vs misclassification. In practice, costs drift or are stochastic, causing training-time objectives to misalign with deployment-time. Robust online methods are needed.
AI.method: Adaptation of representative separable ECTS approaches to online learning. During deployment, only the triggering model is updated; the classifier remains fixed. Proposed online baselines include bandit-based and RL-based strategies, plus standard baselines. Evaluations conducted on synthetic data to assess robustness to cost non-stationarity.
AI.result: Online learning improves robustness to cost drift; RL-based strategies show strong and stable performance across varying cost regimes.
AI.conclusion: Online RL-based adaptations can enhance ECTS robustness to non-stationary costs. Future work could extend validation to real datasets, provide theoretical guarantees, and explore more online objective formulations and computational trade-offs.

title: Beyond What Seems Necessary: Hidden Gains from Scaling Training-Time Reasoning Length under Outcome Supervision
summary: Training LLMs to think and reason for longer has become a key ingredient in building state-of-the-art models that can solve complex problems previously out of reach. Recent efforts pursue this in different ways, such as RL fine-tuning to elicit long CoT or scaling latent reasoning through architectural recurrence. This makes reasoning length an important scaling knob. In this work, we identify a novel phenomenon (both theoretically and experimentally): under outcome-only supervision, out-of-distribution (OOD) performance can continue improving as training-time reasoning length (e.g., the token budget in RL, or the loop count in looped Transformers) increases, even after in-distribution (ID) performance has saturated. This suggests that robustness may require a larger budget than ID validation alone would indicate. We provide theoretical explanations via two mechanisms: (i) self-iteration can induce a stronger inductive bias in the hypothesis class, reshaping ID-optimal solutions in ways that improve OOD generalization; and (ii) when shortcut solutions that work for ID samples but not for OOD samples persist in the hypothesis class, regularization can reduce the learned solution's reliance on these shortcuts as the number of self-iterations increases. We complement the theory with empirical evidence from two realizations of scaling training-time reasoning length: increasing the number of loops in looped Transformers on a synthetic task, and increasing token budgets during RL fine-tuning of LLMs on mathematical reasoning.

AI.tldr: Under outcome-only supervision, increasing training-time reasoning length can boost OOD generalization even after in-distribution saturation, owing to self-iteration shaping inductive bias and regulation reducing shortcuts. Demonstrated via looped-Transformer synthetic tasks and RL-finetuning with larger token budgets for mathematical reasoning.
AI.motivation: To understand whether longer training-time reasoning acts as a robustness knob and how it affects ID vs OOD generalization under outcome-only supervision.
AI.method: Theoretically analyzes two mechanisms: (i) self-iteration induces a stronger inductive bias in the hypothesis class, reshaping ID-optimal solutions to improve OOD generalization; (ii) when shortcut solutions persist, regularization reduces reliance on these shortcuts as self-iterations increase. Empirically validates with two setups: increasing loops in looped Transformers on a synthetic task; increasing token budgets during RL fine-tuning of LLMs on mathematical reasoning.
AI.result: OOD performance continues to improve as reasoning length increases even after ID performance saturates; two mechanisms explain this improvement; experimental evidence from looped Transformer synthetic task and RL-based math reasoning supports the theory.
AI.conclusion: Robustness to distribution shifts may require larger training-time reasoning budgets than ID validation alone suggests; scaling reasoning length is a viable knob for improving OOD generalization; implications include design of regularization to mitigate shortcuts and broader evaluation beyond ID metrics.

title: Continuous-Utility Direct Preference Optimization
summary: Large language model reasoning is often treated as a monolithic capability, relying on binary preference supervision that fails to capture partial progress or fine-grained reasoning quality. We introduce Continuous Utility Direct Preference Optimization (CU-DPO), a framework that aligns models to a portfolio of prompt-based cognitive strategies by replacing binary labels with continuous scores that capture fine-grained reasoning quality. We prove that learning with K strategies yields a Theta(K log K) improvement in sample complexity over binary preferences, and that DPO converges to the entropy-regularized utility-maximizing policy.
