To exploit this signal, we propose a two-stage training pipeline: (i) strategy selection, which optimizes the model to choose the best strategy for a given problem via best-vs-all comparisons, and (ii) execution refinement, which trains the model to correctly execute the selected strategy using margin-stratified pairs. On mathematical reasoning benchmarks, CU-DPO improves strategy selection accuracy from 35-46 percent to 68-78 percent across seven base models, yielding consistent downstream reasoning gains of up to 6.6 points on in-distribution datasets with effective transfer to out-of-distribution tasks.

AI.tldr: CU-DPO replaces binary preferences in LLM reasoning with continuous strategy-quality scores, enabling a portfolio of prompt-based cognitive strategies. It yields a K log K improvement in sample complexity vs binary labeling and converges to an entropy-regularized policy, with a two-stage training pipeline (strategy selection and execution refinement). Empirically, it boosts strategy-selection accuracy from 35–46% to 68–78% across seven base models and improves in-distribution reasoning by up to 6.6 points, with positive transfer to out-of-distribution tasks.
AI.motivation: Binary preference supervision provides coarse signals that miss partial progress and nuanced reasoning quality. A continuous, multi-strategy framework can capture fine-grained evaluation of different cognitive strategies, improving sample efficiency and downstream reasoning performance.
AI.method: Introduce Continuous Utility Direct Preference Optimization (CU-DPO) over a portfolio of prompt-based cognitive strategies. Prove Theta(K log K) sample complexity improvement over binary preferences and convergence to an entropy-regularized utility-maximizing policy. Implement a two-stage training pipeline: (i) strategy selection via best-vs-all comparisons, (ii) execution refinement via margin-stratified pairs, to train models to choose and correctly execute the best strategy per problem.
AI.result: On mathematical reasoning benchmarks, strategy-selection accuracy improves from 35–46% to 68–78% across seven base models, with consistent downstream reasoning gains up to 6.6 points on in-distribution data and effective transfer to out-of-distribution tasks.
AI.conclusion: CU-DPO demonstrates that continuous preference signals over multiple strategies yield both sample-efficiency gains and robust reasoning performance, suggesting practical viability for multi-strategy prompting in LLMs.

title: SALAAD: Sparse And Low-Rank Adaptation via ADMM
summary: Modern large language models are increasingly deployed under compute and memory constraints, making flexible control of model capacity a central challenge. While sparse and low-rank structures naturally trade off capacity and performance, existing approaches often rely on heuristic designs that ignore layer and matrix heterogeneity or require model-specific architectural modifications. We propose SALAAD, a plug-and-play framework applicable to different model architectures that induces sparse and low-rank structures during training. By formulating structured weight learning under an augmented Lagrangian framework and introducing an adaptive controller that dynamically balances the training loss and structural constraints, SALAAD preserves the stability of standard training dynamics while enabling explicit control over the evolution of effective model capacity during training. Experiments across model scales show that SALAAD substantially reduces memory consumption during deployment while achieving performance comparable to ad-hoc methods. Moreover, a single training run yields a continuous spectrum of model capacities, enabling smooth and elastic deployment across diverse memory budgets without the need for retraining.

AI.tldr: SALAAD is a plug-and-play framework that learns sparse and low-rank weight structures during training via an augmented Lagrangian formulation, with an adaptive controller to balance training loss and structural constraints. It enables explicit, continuous control over effective model capacity, reduces deployment memory, and works across architectures without architecture edits; a single run yields a spectrum of capacities for elastic deployment without retraining.
AI.motivation: As compute and memory constraints tighten for deploying large language models, there is a need for flexible, architecture-agnostic capacity control. Existing methods often rely on heuristics, ignore layer/matrix heterogeneity, or require model-specific architectural modifications, limiting practicality and scalability.
AI.method: Formulate structured weight learning under an augmented Lagrangian framework that enforces sparsity and low-rank structure, and introduce an adaptive controller that dynamically trades off training loss and structural penalties. The approach is plug-and-play, requiring no bespoke architectural changes and accommodating heterogeneity across layers and matrices.
AI.result: Empirical results show substantial memory reduction during deployment with performance comparable to ad-hoc methods. Importantly, a single training run provides a continuous spectrum of model capacities, enabling elastic deployment across various memory budgets without retraining.
AI.conclusion: SALAAD enables stable training dynamics while providing explicit, scalable control over effective model capacity. It is broadly applicable across model architectures and suitable for deployment under diverse memory constraints.

title: Dynamic Prior Thompson Sampling for Cold-Start Exploration in Recommender Systems
summary: Cold-start exploration is a core challenge in large-scale recommender systems: new or data-sparse items must receive traffic to estimate value, but over-exploration harms users and wastes impressions. In practice, Thompson Sampling (TS) is often initialized with a uniform Beta(1,1) prior, implicitly assuming a 50% success rate for unseen items. When true base rates are far lower, this optimistic prior systematically over-allocates to weak items. The impact is amplified by batched policy updates and pipeline latency: for hours, newly launched items can remain effectively "no data," so the prior dominates allocation before feedback is incorporated. We propose Dynamic Prior Thompson Sampling, a prior design that directly controls the probability that a new arm outcompetes the incumbent winner. Our key contribution is a closed-form quadratic solution for the prior mean that enforces P(X_j > Y_k) = epsilon at introduction time, making exploration intensity predictable and tunable while preserving TS Bayesian updates. Across Monte Carlo validation, offline batched simulations, and a large-scale online experiment on a thumbnail personalization system serving millions of users, dynamic priors deliver precise exploration control and improved efficiency versus a uniform-prior baseline.

AI.tldr: Dynamic Prior Thompson Sampling (DPTS) introduces a Beta prior for new arms with a mean chosen to satisfy P(X_j > Y_k) = epsilon at introduction, enabling tunable exploration intensity; validated in simulations and large-scale online test with improved efficiency vs uniform-prior TS.
AI.motivation: Cold-start exploration in large-scale recommender systems: new or sparse items need traffic to estimate value, but aggressive exploration wastes impressions; uniform prior (Beta(1,1)) over-optimistically assumes 50% success, especially harmful with batched updates and latency.
AI.method: Derive closed-form quadratic solution for the Beta prior mean that ensures the probability a new arm outperforms the incumbent equals a target epsilon at introduction. Integrates into Thompson Sampling updates preserving Bayesian updating; validated via Monte Carlo, offline batched simulations, and online experiment.
AI.result: Dynamic priors achieve precise, tunable exploration control and improved efficiency relative to uniform-prior baseline across simulations and online deployment serving millions of users.
AI.conclusion: DPTS offers predictable exploration intensity while maintaining Bayesian updating, addressing cold-start in large-scale systems; practical for setting epsilon to balance exploration vs exploitation; potential extension to non-Bernoulli rewards or multi-armed settings.

title: Optimal Budgeted Adaptation of Large Language Models
summary: The trade-off between labeled data availability and downstream accuracy remains a central challenge in fine-tuning large language models (LLMs). We propose a principled framework for \emph{budget-aware supervised fine-tuning} by casting LLM adaptation as a contextual Stackelberg game. In our formulation, the learner (leader) commits to a scoring policy and a label-querying strategy, while an adaptive environment (follower) selects challenging supervised alternatives in response. To explicitly address label efficiency, we incorporate a finite supervision budget directly into the learning objective. Our algorithm operates in the full-feedback regime and achieves $\tilde{O}(d\sqrt{T})$ regret under standard linear contextual assumptions. We extend the framework with a Largest-Latency-First (LLF) confidence gate that selectively queries labels, achieving a budget-aware regret bound of $\tilde{O}(\sqrt{dB} + c\sqrt{B})$ with $B=βT$.

AI.tldr: Budget-aware supervised fine-tuning of LLMs framed as a contextual Stackelberg game; a leader commits to scoring and label-querying, follower selects challenging supervised alternatives; achieves sublinear regret with a budgeted objective and LLF-based label querying.
AI.motivation: Address the data-label trade-off in fine-tuning LLMs by making supervision budget explicit and optimizing label efficiency.
AI.method: Model adaptation as a Stackelberg game between a learner (leader) and an adaptive environment (follower); learner chooses scoring policy and label-querying strategy; follower responds with challenging supervised alternatives; includes a finite supervision budget in the objective; operates in full-feedback regime; assumes linear contextual structure; proves tilde-O(d sqrt(T)) regret; introduces Largest-Latency-First (LLF) confidence gate to selectively query labels.
AI.result: The approach yields sublinear regret bounds under standard linear contextual assumptions; full-feedback setting achieves tilde-O(d sqrt(T)) regret; LLF gate yields budget-aware regret of tilde-O(sqrt(dB) + c sqrt(B)) with B = βT, improving label efficiency.
AI.conclusion: A principled, theory-grounded framework for budget-aware supervised fine-tuning of LLMs. The LLF-based selective querying further tightens budget-aware regret bounds, highlighting the value of explicit budgeting in label acquisition during adaptation.

title: SAGE: Agentic Framework for Interpretable and Clinically Translatable Computational Pathology Biomarker Discovery
summary: Despite significant progress in computational pathology, many AI models remain black-box and difficult to interpret, posing a major barrier to clinical adoption due to limited transparency and explainability. This has motivated continued interest in engineered image-based biomarkers, which offer greater interpretability but are often proposed based on anecdotal evidence or fragmented prior literature rather than systematic biological validation. We introduce SAGE (Structured Agentic system for hypothesis Generation and Evaluation), an agentic AI system designed to identify interpretable, engineered pathology biomarkers by grounding them in biological evidence. SAGE integrates literature-anchored reasoning with multimodal data analysis to correlate image-derived features with molecular biomarkers, such as gene expression, and clinically relevant outcomes. By coordinating specialized agents for biological contextualization and empirical hypothesis validation, SAGE prioritizes transparent, biologically supported biomarkers and advances the clinical translation of computational pathology.

AI.tldr: SAGE is an agentic AI framework that grounds image-derived pathology biomarkers in biological evidence to enhance interpretability and translational potential.
AI.motivation: Black-box models in computational pathology limit clinical adoption; there is a need for interpretable, engineered biomarkers that are validated against biology rather than anecdotal evidence.
AI.method: A multi-agent system with literature-anchored reasoning and multimodal data analysis that links image-derived features to molecular biomarkers (e.g., gene expression) and clinical outcomes; specialized agents perform biological contextualization and empirical validation to prioritize biologically supported biomarkers.
AI.result: The abstract presents a design and intended capabilities; no empirical results are reported.
AI.conclusion: If implemented, SAGE could deliver transparent, biologically grounded biomarkers and improve clinical translation of computational pathology.

title: From drift to adaptation to the failed ml model: Transfer Learning in Industrial MLOps
summary: Model adaptation to production environment is critical for reliable Machine Learning Operations (MLOps), less attention is paid to developing systematic framework for updating the ML models when they fail under data drift. This paper compares the transfer learning enabled model update strategies including ensemble transfer learning (ETL), all-layers transfer learning (ALTL), and last-layer transfer learning (LLTL) for updating the failed feedforward artificial neural network (ANN) model. The flue gas differential pressure across the air preheater unit installed in a 660 MW thermal power plant is analyzed as a case study since it mimics the batch processes due to load cycling in the power plant. Updating the failed ANN model by three transfer learning techniques reveals that ETL provides relatively higher predictive accuracy for the batch size of 5 days than those of LLTL and ALTL. However, ALTL is found to be suitable for effective update of the model trained on large batch size (8 days). A mixed trend is observed for computational requirement (hyperparameter tuning and model training) of model update techniques for different batch sizes. These fundamental and empiric insights obtained from the batch process-based industrial case study can assist the MLOps practitioners in adapting the failed models to data drifts for the accurate monitoring of industrial processes.

AI.tldr: For updating failed ANN models under data drift in industrial batch processes, ensemble transfer learning (ETL) outperforms other strategies for small batches (5 days), while all-layers transfer learning (ALTL) is preferable for larger batches (8 days); last-layer transfer learning (LLTL) underperforms; mixed computational costs observed.
AI.motivation: MLOps often lacks systematic methods for updating models when data drift occurs. The work evaluates practical transfer-learning-based update strategies to maintain model accuracy in industrial settings.
AI.method: Compare three transfer-learning update schemes—ETL, ALTL, and LLTL—for a failed feedforward ANN predicting flue gas differential pressure in an air preheater of a 660 MW plant. Assess performance across two batch sizes (5 and 8 days) focusing on predictive accuracy and computational requirements (hyperparameter tuning and training).
AI.result: ETL provides higher predictive accuracy for 5-day batches; ALTL achieves better performance for 8-day batches; LLTL is generally less effective. Computational requirements vary by batch size with no uniform winner.
AI.conclusion: The study offers practical guidance for MLOps practitioners: select ETL for smaller drift-adaptation windows and ALTL for larger ones, with attention to computational trade-offs; insights are grounded in a batch-process industrial case and may guide model repair under data drift.

title: Probing the Knowledge Boundary: An Interactive Agentic Framework for Deep Knowledge Extraction
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) can be seen as compressed knowledge bases, but it remains unclear what knowledge they truly contain and how far their knowledge boundaries extend. Existing benchmarks are mostly static and provide limited support for systematic knowledge probing. In this paper, we propose an interactive agentic framework to systematically extract and quantify the knowledge of LLMs. Our method includes four adaptive exploration policies to probe knowledge at different granularities. To ensure the quality of extracted knowledge, we introduce a three-stage knowledge processing pipeline that combines vector-based filtering to remove exact duplicates, LLM-based adjudication to resolve ambiguous semantic overlaps, and domain-relevance auditing to retain valid knowledge units. Through extensive experiments, we find that recursive taxonomy is the most effective exploration strategy. We also observe a clear knowledge scaling law, where larger models consistently extract more knowledge. In addition, we identify a Pass@1-versus-Pass@k trade-off: domain-specialized models achieve higher initial accuracy but degrade rapidly, while general-purpose models maintain stable performance during extended extraction. Finally, our results show that differences in training data composition lead to distinct and measurable knowledge profiles across model families.

AI.tldr: An interactive agentic framework probes LLM knowledge with four adaptive exploration policies and a three-stage processing pipeline; finds recursive taxonomy most effective, a knowledge scaling law, Pass@1 vs Pass@k trade-off, and model-family knowledge profiles linked to training data.
AI.motivation: To quantify what knowledge LLMs truly contain and how far their knowledge extends, addressing static benchmarks that lack systematic probing.
AI.method: Four adaptive exploration policies to probe knowledge at varying granularities; three-stage pipeline: (1) vector-based duplicate removal, (2) LLM-based adjudication to resolve semantic overlaps, (3) domain-relevance auditing to retain valid knowledge units.
AI.result: Recursive taxonomy most effective; larger models extract more knowledge; Pass@1 vs Pass@k trade-off between domain specialization and general models; model family knowledge profiles vary with training data composition.
AI.conclusion: The framework enables systematic probing of LLM knowledge, reveals scaling laws and cross-model differences, and motivates further study of exploration strategies and data-driven knowledge profiling.

title: Multimodal Scientific Learning Beyond Diffusions and Flows
summary: Scientific machine learning (SciML) increasingly requires models that capture multimodal conditional uncertainty arising from ill-posed inverse problems, multistability, and chaotic dynamics. While recent work has favored highly expressive implicit generative models such as diffusion and flow-based methods, these approaches are often data-hungry, computationally costly, and misaligned with the structured solution spaces frequently found in scientific problems. We demonstrate that Mixture Density Networks (MDNs) provide a principled yet largely overlooked alternative for multimodal uncertainty quantification in SciML. As explicit parametric density estimators, MDNs impose an inductive bias tailored to low-dimensional, multimodal physics, enabling direct global allocation of probability mass across distinct solution branches. This structure delivers strong data efficiency, allowing reliable recovery of separated modes in regimes where scientific data is scarce. We formalize these insights through a unified probabilistic framework contrasting explicit and implicit distribution networks, and demonstrate empirically that MDNs achieve superior generalization, interpretability, and sample efficiency across a range of inverse, multistable, and chaotic scientific regression tasks.

AI.tldr: MDNs provide a principled, data-efficient explicit density estimator for multimodal uncertainty in SciML, offering interpretability and better sample efficiency than implicit generative models like diffusion/flow methods, especially in ill-posed inverse problems, multistability, and chaotic dynamics.
AI.motivation: Scientific ML tasks with multimodal conditional uncertainty require models that can allocate probability mass across distinct solution branches. Implicit generative models are data-hungry and may mismatch structured physics; explicit models like MDNs can introduce inductive biases aligned with low-dimensional, multimodal physics.
AI.method: Adopt Mixture Density Networks as explicit density estimators and compare them to implicit density networks within a unified probabilistic framework. Empirically evaluate on inverse, multistable, and chaotic regression tasks to assess generalization, interpretability, and sample efficiency.
AI.result: MDNs demonstrate superior generalization and interpretability, with strong data efficiency. They reliably recover separated modes in regimes with scarce data and enable direct global allocation of probability mass across solution branches, outperforming implicit models on the tested SciML tasks.
AI.conclusion: Explicit density estimation via MDNs is a principled and efficient alternative for multimodal uncertainty in SciML, offering better sample efficiency, interpretability, and alignment with structured physics, and warrants broader adoption.

title: On the Spectral Flattening of Quantized Embeddings
summary: Training Large Language Models (LLMs) at ultra-low precision is critically impeded by instability rooted in the conflict between discrete quantization constraints and the intrinsic heavy-tailed spectral nature of linguistic data. By formalizing the connection between Zipfian statistics and random matrix theory, we prove that the power-law decay in the singular value spectra of embeddings is a fundamental requisite for semantic encoding. We derive theoretical bounds showing that uniform quantization introduces a noise floor that disproportionately truncates this spectral tail, which induces spectral flattening and a strictly provable increase in the stable rank of representations. Empirical validation across diverse architectures including GPT-2 and TinyLlama corroborates that this geometric degradation precipitates representational collapse. This work not only quantifies the spectral sensitivity of LLMs but also establishes spectral fidelity as a necessary condition for stable low-bit optimization.

AI.tldr: Uniform low-precision quantization disrupts the heavy-tailed spectral structure essential for semantic encoding, causing spectral flattening and representational collapse; maintaining spectral fidelity is necessary for stable low-bit optimization in LLMs.
AI.motivation: Quantizing LLMs to ultra-low precision introduces instability due to the mismatch between discrete quantization and the intrinsically heavy-tailed, Zipfian nature of linguistic data; understanding the spectral requirements can guide robust quantization strategies.
AI.method: Theoretical analysis linking Zipfian statistics with random matrix theory to show power-law singular-value decay is necessary for semantic encoding; derive bounds showing uniform quantization adds a noise floor that truncates the spectral tail and increases stable rank; empirical validation across GPT-2, TinyLlama demonstrates spectral degradation and collapse.
AI.result: Demonstrates that power-law spectral decay is fundamental for semantic encoding; uniform quantization induces spectral tail truncation, spectral flattening, and a provable increase in stable rank; empirical evidence of representational collapse across architectures.
AI.conclusion: Spectral fidelity is a necessary condition for stable ultra-low-precision optimization of LLMs; preserving the heavy-tailed spectrum is crucial to retain semantic encoding during quantized training.

title: Forest-Guided Semantic Transport for Label-Supervised Manifold Alignment
summary: Label-supervised manifold alignment bridges the gap between unsupervised and correspondence-based paradigms by leveraging shared label information to align multimodal datasets. Still, most existing methods rely on Euclidean geometry to model intra-domain relationships. This approach can fail when features are only weakly related to the task of interest, leading to noisy, semantically misleading structure and degraded alignment quality. To address this limitation, we introduce FoSTA (Forest-guided Semantic Transport Alignment), a scalable alignment framework that leverages forest-induced geometry to denoise intra-domain structure and recover task-relevant manifolds prior to alignment. FoSTA builds semantic representations directly from label-informed forest affinities and aligns them via fast, hierarchical semantic transport, capturing meaningful cross-domain relationships. Extensive comparisons with established baselines demonstrate that FoSTA improves correspondence recovery and label transfer on synthetic benchmarks and delivers strong performance in practical single-cell applications, including batch correction and biological conservation.

AI.tldr: FoSTA introduces forest-guided semantic transport for label-supervised manifold alignment to denoise intra-domain structure and recover task-relevant manifolds before alignment, improving cross-domain correspondence and label transfer.
AI.motivation: Euclidean intra-domain geometry can be noisy and semantically misleading when features are weakly related to the task; existing label-supervised aligners often rely on Euclidean relations. A robust, scalable alternative is needed to extract task-relevant manifolds prior to alignment.
AI.method: Construct semantic representations from label-informed forest affinities (forest-induced geometry). Use fast, hierarchical semantic transport to align across domains. The approach denoises intra-domain structure before alignment and scales to large datasets.
AI.result: FoSTA yields improved correspondence recovery and label transfer on synthetic benchmarks; demonstrates strong performance in single-cell analyses, including batch correction and biological conservation tasks.
AI.conclusion: Forest-guided semantic transport provides a scalable, robust framework for label-supervised manifold alignment by leveraging task-relevant semantic structure from random forests, improving cross-domain alignment and downstream analyses.

title: EDU-CIRCUIT-HW: Evaluating Multimodal Large Language Models on Real-World University-Level STEM Student Handwritten Solutions
summary: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) hold significant promise for revolutionizing traditional education and reducing teachers' workload. However, accurately interpreting unconstrained STEM student handwritten solutions with intertwined mathematical formulas, diagrams, and textual reasoning poses a significant challenge due to the lack of authentic and domain-specific benchmarks. Additionally, current evaluation paradigms predominantly rely on the outcomes of downstream tasks (e.g., auto-grading), which often probe only a subset of the recognized content, thereby failing to capture the MLLMs' understanding of complex handwritten logic as a whole. To bridge this gap, we release EDU-CIRCUIT-HW, a dataset consisting of 1,300+ authentic student handwritten solutions from a university-level STEM course. Utilizing the expert-verified verbatim transcriptions and grading reports of student solutions, we simultaneously evaluate various MLLMs' upstream recognition fidelity and downstream auto-grading performance. Our evaluation uncovers an astonishing scale of latent failures within MLLM-recognized student handwritten content, highlighting the models' insufficient reliability for auto-grading and other understanding-oriented applications in high-stakes educational settings. In solution, we present a case study demonstrating that leveraging identified error patterns to preemptively detect and rectify recognition errors, with only minimal human intervention (approximately 4% of the total solutions), can significantly enhance the robustness of the deployed AI-enabled grading system on unseen student solutions.

AI.tldr: Introduces EDU-CIRCUIT-HW dataset with 1,300+ authentic handwritten STEM solutions to evaluate multimodal LLMs on recognition fidelity and auto-grading; reveals latent failures and suggests a low-supervision error-correction approach to boost robustness.
AI.motivation: Addresses the lack of authentic, domain-specific benchmarks for unconstrained student handwritings with intertwined formulas, diagrams, and text. Critiques current evaluation paradigms that rely on downstream task outcomes, which may not capture full understanding of complex handwritten logic in education.
AI.method: Construct EDU-CIRCUIT-HW from a university-level STEM course, with expert-verified verbatim transcriptions and grading reports. Evaluate multiple MLLMs on upstream recognition fidelity and downstream auto-grading, analyze error patterns, and demonstrate a case study where preemptive correction of recognition errors (with ~4% human intervention) improves robustness on unseen solutions.
AI.result: Uncovered substantial latent failures in MLLM recognition; auto-grading and understanding-oriented tasks remain unreliable in high-stakes settings. Demonstrated that error-pattern-driven preprocessing can markedly enhance grading robustness for unseen data.
AI.conclusion: The dataset and analysis provide a benchmark for evaluating MLLMs’ holistic understanding of handwritten STEM solutions. A lightweight, error-pattern-based intervention can substantially improve system reliability with minimal human input, informing future education AI deployments.

title: Scalable Random Wavelet Features: Efficient Non-Stationary Kernel Approximation with Convergence Guarantees
summary: Modeling non-stationary processes, where statistical properties vary across the input domain, is a critical challenge in machine learning; yet most scalable methods rely on a simplifying assumption of stationarity. This forces a difficult trade-off: use expressive but computationally demanding models like Deep Gaussian Processes, or scalable but limited methods like Random Fourier Features (RFF). We close this gap by introducing Random Wavelet Features (RWF), a framework that constructs scalable, non-stationary kernel approximations by sampling from wavelet families. By harnessing the inherent localization and multi-resolution structure of wavelets, RWF generates an explicit feature map that captures complex, input-dependent patterns. Our framework provides a principled way to generalize RFF to the non-stationary setting and comes with a comprehensive theoretical analysis, including positive definiteness, unbiasedness, and uniform convergence guarantees. We demonstrate empirically on a range of challenging synthetic and real-world datasets that RWF outperforms stationary random features and offers a compelling accuracy-efficiency trade-off against more complex models, unlocking scalable and expressive kernel methods for a broad class of real-world non-stationary problems.

AI.tldr: Random Wavelet Features (RWF) provide scalable, non-stationary kernel approximations by sampling from wavelet families, leveraging localization and multi-resolution to capture input-dependent patterns, with theoretical guarantees and empirical gains over stationary random features.
AI.motivation: Non-stationary processes have input-dependent statistics, making purely stationary kernels inadequate. While scalable methods like Random Fourier Features (RFF) are efficient, they assume stationarity and struggle with non-stationary patterns. More expressive models (e.g., Deep Gaussian Processes) are accurate but computationally intensive. There is a need for a scalable, expressive non-stationary kernel approximation.
AI.method: Extend the Random Fourier Features framework by sampling from wavelet families to construct an explicit, localized, multi-resolution feature map. This generalizes RFF to non-stationary kernels. Theoretical analysis establishes positive definiteness of the induced kernel, unbiasedness of the estimator, and uniform convergence guarantees. Empirical evaluation on synthetic and real-world datasets demonstrates improved performance over stationary random features and favorable accuracy-efficiency trade-offs against more complex models.
AI.result: RWF empirically outperforms stationary random features on challenging synthetic and real-world datasets, offering a scalable and expressive non-stationary kernel approximation that maintains accuracy with lower computational cost compared to more complex non-stationary models.
AI.conclusion: RWF provides a principled, scalable approach to non-stationary kernel learning by leveraging wavelet-based feature maps. It generalizes RFF to the non-stationary setting, with theoretical guarantees and practical performance gains, enabling broader application of expressive kernels to non-stationary problems.

title: Mirage2Matter: A Physically Grounded Gaussian World Model from Video
summary: The scalability of embodied intelligence is fundamentally constrained by the scarcity of real-world interaction data. While simulation platforms provide a promising alternative, existing approaches often suffer from a substantial visual and physical gap to real environments and rely on expensive sensors, precise robot calibration, or depth measurements, limiting their practicality at scale. We present Simulate Anything, a graphics-driven world modeling and simulation framework that enables efficient generation of high-fidelity embodied training data using only multi-view environment videos and off-the-shelf assets. Our approach reconstructs real-world environments into a photorealistic scene representation using 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS), seamlessly capturing fine-grained geometry and appearance from video. We then leverage generative models to recover a physically realistic representation and integrate it into a simulation environment via a precision calibration target, enabling accurate scale alignment between the reconstructed scene and the real world. Together, these components provide a unified, editable, and physically grounded world model. Vision Language Action (VLA) models trained on our simulated data achieve strong zero-shot performance on downstream tasks, matching or even surpassing results obtained with real-world data, highlighting the potential of reconstruction-driven world modeling for scalable and practical embodied intelligence training.

AI.tldr: Proposes Simulate Anything: a graphics-driven, reconstruction-based world model that converts multi-view videos into photorealistic, scalable simulation environments using 3D Gaussian Splatting and generative models, enabling accurate scale alignment via a calibration target and enabling Vision-Language-Action models to achieve strong zero-shot performance.
AI.motivation: Overcome the scarcity of real-world interaction data and the visual/physical gap of existing simulators, enabling scalable, practical embodied AI training with minimal sensors.
AI.method: Reconstruct real scenes with 3D Gaussian Splatting from multi-view videos; use generative models to produce physically plausible representations; calibrate to real-world scale with a precision target; integrate into a simulator to form an editable, physically grounded world model.
AI.result: VLA models trained on the simulated data demonstrate strong zero-shot performance on downstream tasks, matching or surpassing results obtained with real-world data.
AI.conclusion: Reconstruction-driven world modeling can enable scalable, practical embodied intelligence training by bridging perception and physics in a unified simulation environment.

title: R3G: A Reasoning--Retrieval--Reranking Framework for Vision-Centric Answer Generation
summary: Vision-centric retrieval for VQA requires retrieving images to supply missing visual cues and integrating them into the reasoning process. However, selecting the right images and integrating them effectively into the model's reasoning remains challenging.To address this challenge, we propose R3G, a modular Reasoning-Retrieval-Reranking framework.It first produces a brief reasoning plan that specifies the required visual cues, then adopts a two-stage strategy, with coarse retrieval followed by fine-grained reranking, to select evidence images.On MRAG-Bench, R3G improves accuracy across six MLLM backbones and nine sub-scenarios, achieving state-of-the-art overall performance. Ablations show that sufficiency-aware reranking and reasoning steps are complementary, helping the model both choose the right images and use them well. We release code and data at https://github.com/czh24/R3G.

AI.tldr: R3G is a modular framework for vision-centric VQA retrieval that plans required visual cues, then retrieves evidence with a two-stage coarse-and-fine strategy, achieving state-of-the-art results across MRAG-Bench with multiple backbones; sufficiency-aware reranking and planning are complementary; code released.
AI.motivation: Address the difficulty of selecting and integrating relevant visual evidence for VQA when relying on image retrieval, which can hinder reasoning if inappropriate cues are used.
AI.method: Introduce R3G: (1) generate a brief reasoning plan specifying needed visual cues; (2) perform two-stage retrieval—coarse retrieval followed by fine-grained reranking—to select evidence images; (3) employ sufficiency-aware reranking to ensure chosen evidence suffices for the reasoning task.
AI.result: Empirical results on MRAG-Bench show improved accuracy across six MLLM backbones and nine sub-scenarios, achieving state-of-the-art overall performance; ablations indicate planning and sufficiency-aware reranking are complementary.
AI.conclusion: R3G effectively integrates planning and multi-stage retrieval to improve visual evidence selection and reasoning in VQA, generalizing across backbones; code and data are released.

title: Predicting Anemia Among Under-Five Children in Nepal Using Machine Learning and Deep Learning
comment: 13 pages and submission to Public Health Nutrition is in progress
summary: Childhood anemia remains a major public health challenge in Nepal and is associated with impaired growth, cognition, and increased morbidity. Using World Health Organization hemoglobin thresholds, we defined anemia status for children aged 6-59 months and formulated a binary classification task by grouping all anemia severities as \emph{anemic} versus \emph{not anemic}. We analyzed Nepal Demographic and Health Survey (NDHS 2022) microdata comprising 1,855 children and initially considered 48 candidate features spanning demographic, socioeconomic, maternal, and child health characteristics. To obtain a stable and substantiated feature set, we applied four features selection techniques (Chi-square, mutual information, point-biserial correlation, and Boruta) and prioritized features supported by multi-method consensus. Five features: child age, recent fever, household size, maternal anemia, and parasite deworming were consistently selected by all methods, while amenorrhea, ethnicity indicators, and provinces were frequently retained. We then compared eight traditional machine learning classifiers (LR, KNN, DT, RF, XGBoost, SVM, NB, LDA) with two deep learning models (DNN and TabNet) using standard evaluation metrics, emphasizing F1-score and recall due to class imbalance. Among all models, logistic regression attained the best recall (0.701) and the highest F1-score (0.649), while DNN achieved the highest accuracy (0.709), and SVM yielded the strongest discrimination with the highest AUC (0.736). Overall, the results indicate that both machine learning and deep learning models can provide competitive anemia prediction and the interpretable features such as child age, infection proxy, maternal anemia, and deworming history are central for risk stratification and public health screening in Nepal.

AI.tldr: ML/DL models using NDHS 2022 to predict childhood anemia; consensus feature selection identifies key predictors; 8 traditional ML models plus 2 DL models; Logistic Regression achieves best recall and F1, DNN best accuracy, SVM best AUC; interpretable features include age, recent fever, household size, maternal anemia, and deworming.
AI.motivation: Address persistent childhood anemia in Nepal and enable risk stratification and screening using population data; leverage multiple feature-selection techniques and classifiers to benchmark predictive performance and identify actionable predictors.
AI.method: Data: NDHS 2022 microdata (n=1,855 children aged 6-59 months). Outcome: anemia defined by WHO hemoglobin thresholds; binary classification (anemic vs not). Features: 48 candidates across demographic, socioeconomic, maternal, and child health. Feature selection: Chi-square, mutual information, point-biserial correlation, Boruta; consensus features prioritized. Modeling: eight traditional ML classifiers (LR, KNN, DT, RF, XGBoost, SVM, NB, LDA) and two DL models (DNN, TabNet). Evaluation metrics: accuracy, recall, precision, F1, AUC; emphasis on recall and F1 due to class imbalance.
AI.result: Five features consistently selected by all methods: child age, recent fever, household size, maternal anemia, parasite deworming. Amenorrhea, ethnicity indicators, and provinces were frequently retained. Logistic regression achieved recall 0.701 and F1 0.649; DNN had highest accuracy 0.709; SVM had the highest AUC 0.736. Overall, ML and DL models yielded competitive anemia predictions, with interpretable features central to risk stratification and screening in Nepal.
AI.conclusion: ML/DL approaches can support public health screening for childhood anemia in Nepal, offering competitive predictive performance and interpretable predictors such as child age, infection proxy, maternal anemia, and deworming history to guide risk stratification and resource allocation.

title: LASS-ODE: Scaling ODE Computations to Connect Foundation Models with Dynamical Physical Systems
summary: Foundation models have transformed language, vision, and time series data analysis, yet progress on dynamic predictions for physical systems remains limited. Given the complexity of physical constraints, two challenges stand out. $(i)$ Physics-computation scalability: physics-informed learning can enforce physical regularization, but its computation (e.g., ODE integration) does not scale to extensive systems. $(ii)$ Knowledge-sharing efficiency: the attention mechanism is primarily computed within each system, which limits the extraction of shared ODE structures across systems. We show that enforcing ODE consistency does not require expensive nonlinear integration: a token-wise locally linear ODE representation preserves physical fidelity while scaling to foundation-model regimes. Thus, we propose novel token representations that respect locally linear ODE evolution. Such linearity substantially accelerates integration while accurately approximating the local data manifold. Second, we introduce a simple yet effective inter-system attention that augments attention with a common structure hub (CSH) that stores shared tokens and aggregates knowledge across systems. The resulting model, termed LASS-ODE (\underline{LA}rge-\underline{S}cale \underline{S}mall \underline{ODE}), is pretrained on our $40$GB ODE trajectory collections to enable strong in-domain performance, zero-shot generalization across diverse ODE systems, and additional improvements through fine-tuning.

AI.tldr: Proposes a scalable framework for dynamic predictions in physical systems using locally linear ODE tokens and a cross-system shared-structure hub (CSH) to enable efficient cross-system knowledge transfer; introduces LASS-ODE pretrained on 40GB of ODE trajectories with strong in-domain and zero-shot generalization.
AI.motivation: Foundation models struggle with physics-informed, large-scale dynamic predictions due to (i) expensive physics-integrations and (ii) limited cross-system knowledge sharing of ODE structures. The work argues for representation and attention mechanisms that preserve ODE fidelity while scaling to foundation-model regimes.
AI.method: Introduce a token-wise locally linear ODE representation to enable efficient ODE integration, coupled with an inter-system attention mechanism augmented by a Common Structure Hub (CSH) that stores shared tokens across systems. Pretrain the model (LASS-ODE) on 40GB of ODE trajectory data.
AI.result: LASS-ODE achieves strong in-domain performance and zero-shot generalization across diverse ODE systems, with further gains from fine-tuning.
AI.conclusion: Locally linear ODE tokens preserve physical fidelity and drastically accelerate integration; the CSH enables effective cross-system knowledge sharing; the approach enables scalable, cross-domain dynamic predictions for physical systems in a foundation-model setting.

title: SITUATE -- Synthetic Object Counting Dataset for VLM training
comment: accepted at 21st International Conference on Computer Vision Theory and Applications
summary: We present SITUATE, a novel dataset designed for training and evaluating Vision Language Models on counting tasks with spatial constraints. The dataset bridges the gap between simple 2D datasets like VLMCountBench and often ambiguous real-life datasets like TallyQA, which lack control over occlusions and spatial composition. Experiments show that our dataset helps to improve generalization for out-of-distribution images, since a finetune of Qwen VL 2.5 7B on SITUATE improves accuracy on the Pixmo count test data, but not vice versa. We cross validate this by comparing the model performance across established other counting benchmarks and against an equally sized fine-tuning set derived from Pixmo count.

AI.tldr: New dataset SITUATE for counting with spatial constraints improves generalization; finetuning a VLM on SITUATE boosts Pixmo count performance; transfer is unidirectional (SITUATE -> Pixmo) and not vice versa.
AI.motivation: There is a gap between simple 2D counting datasets and real-world datasets with occlusions and spatial structure. A controlled dataset is needed to train VLMs to count under spatial constraints and to study transfer/generalization.
AI.method: Introduce SITUATE dataset designed for counting with spatial constraints. Evaluate by finetuning Qwen VL 2.5 7B on SITUATE and on Pixmo count, and compare on Pixmo count test data; perform cross-benchmark comparisons against other counting benchmarks and an equal-sized Pixmo-derived fine-tuning set.
AI.result: Finetuning on SITUATE improves Pixmo count test accuracy; finetuning on Pixmo count does not improve SITUATE performance. Cross-benchmark analysis shows enhanced generalization with SITUATE.
AI.conclusion: SITUATE is a valuable resource for training/evaluating VLMs on counting with spatial constraints and supports unidirectional transfer to Pixmo count, aiding out-of-distribution generalization.

title: How Does Unfaithful Reasoning Emerge from Autoregressive Training? A Study of Synthetic Experiments
summary: Chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning generated by large language models (LLMs) is often unfaithful: intermediate steps can be logically inconsistent or fail to reflect the causal relationship leading to the final answer. Despite extensive empirical observations, a fundamental understanding of CoT is lacking--what constitutes faithful CoT reasoning, and how unfaithfulness emerges from autoregressive training. We study these questions using well-controlled synthetic experiments, training small transformers on noisy data to solve modular arithmetic expressions step by step, a task we term Arithmetic Expression Reasoning. We find that models can learn faithful reasoning that causally follows the underlying arithmetic rules, but only when the training noise is below a critical threshold, a phenomenon attributable to simplicity bias. At higher noise levels, training dynamics exhibit a transition from faithful stepwise reasoning to unfaithful skip-step reasoning via an intermediate mixed mode characterized by a transient increase in prediction entropy. Mechanistic analysis reveals that models learn to encode internal uncertainty by resolving inconsistent reasoning steps, which suggests the emergence of implicit self-verification from autoregressive training.

AI.tldr: Small transformers trained on noisy modular arithmetic data can produce faithful, stepwise CoT reasoning when training noise is below a critical threshold; above threshold, models shift to unfaithful skip-step reasoning with a mixed mode and higher entropy; findings suggest simplicity bias and implicit self-verification mechanisms emerge from autoregressive training.
AI.motivation: To define and characterize faithful Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning, and to understand how unfaithfulness arises during autoregressive training, using controlled synthetic experiments.
AI.method: Train small transformer models on a synthetic Arithmetic Expression Reasoning task that solves modular arithmetic expressions step by step. Inject training noise to the data to study effects on reasoning style. Systematically analyze faithfulness, transition dynamics (faithful → mixed → skip-step), measure prediction entropy, and conduct mechanistic analysis of internal uncertainty and self-verification.
AI.result: A threshold effect: faithful, causally correct stepwise reasoning emerges only at low noise; higher noise induces a transition to unfaithful skip-step reasoning, with an intermediate mixed mode marked by increased entropy. Mechanistically, models encode internal uncertainty to resolve inconsistent steps, implying implicit self-verification from autoregressive training.
AI.conclusion: The work links faithfulness of CoT to training noise and simplicity bias, provides mechanistic insights into how internal uncertainty and self-verification emerge, and suggests implications for designing CoT systems and training regimes to promote faithful reasoning.

title: 1S-DAug: One-Shot Data Augmentation for Robust Few-Shot Generalization
summary: Few-shot learning (FSL) challenges model generalization to novel classes based on just a few shots of labeled examples, a testbed where traditional test-time augmentations fail to be effective. We introduce 1S-DAug, a one-shot generative augmentation operator that synthesizes diverse yet faithful variants from just one example image at test time. 1S-DAug couples traditional geometric perturbations with controlled noise injection and a denoising diffusion process conditioned on the original image. The generated images are then encoded and aggregated, alongside the original image, into a combined representation for more robust FSL predictions. Integrated as a training-free model-agnostic plugin, 1S-DAug consistently improves FSL across standard benchmarks of 4 different datasets without any model parameter update, including achieving over 10% proportional accuracy improvement on the miniImagenet 5-way-1-shot benchmark. Codes will be released.

AI.tldr: One-shot diffusion-based data augmentation (1S-DAug) for few-shot learning that generates diverse, faithful variants from a single test-time image and fuses them with the original to improve predictions, without retraining.
AI.motivation: FSL models struggle to generalize to novel classes with very few examples, and conventional test-time augmentations offer limited benefits; a one-shot, generative, test-time augmentation can enrich representations and boost accuracy without modifying model parameters.
AI.method: At test time, 1S-DAug applies a mixture of geometric perturbations, controlled noise injection, and a denoising diffusion process conditioned on the original image to synthesize multiple variants. These generated images are encoded and aggregated with the original image to form a combined representation, and the approach is provided as a training-free, model-agnostic plugin.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on four standard datasets shows consistent FSL improvements, with the miniImagenet 5-way-1-shot benchmark achieving over 10% proportional accuracy gain; the method does not require any parameter updates.
AI.conclusion: 1S-DAug effectively enhances generalization in few-shot learning as a training-free, model-agnostic augmentation plugin, and is ready for release as code.

title: Toward Universal and Transferable Jailbreak Attacks on Vision-Language Models
summary: Vision-language models (VLMs) extend large language models (LLMs) with vision encoders, enabling text generation conditioned on both images and text. However, this multimodal integration expands the attack surface by exposing the model to image-based jailbreaks crafted to induce harmful responses. Existing gradient-based jailbreak methods transfer poorly, as adversarial patterns overfit to a single white-box surrogate and fail to generalise to black-box models. In this work, we propose Universal and transferable jailbreak (UltraBreak), a framework that constrains adversarial patterns through transformations and regularisation in the vision space, while relaxing textual targets through semantic-based objectives. By defining its loss in the textual embedding space of the target LLM, UltraBreak discovers universal adversarial patterns that generalise across diverse jailbreak objectives. This combination of vision-level regularisation and semantically guided textual supervision mitigates surrogate overfitting and enables strong transferability across both models and attack targets. Extensive experiments show that UltraBreak consistently outperforms prior jailbreak methods. Further analysis reveals why earlier approaches fail to transfer, highlighting that smoothing the loss landscape via semantic objectives is crucial for enabling universal and transferable jailbreaks. The code is publicly available in our \href{https://github.com/kaiyuanCui/UltraBreak}{GitHub repository}.

AI.tldr: UltraBreak: a universal, transferable vision-space jailbreak for VLMs that uses transformations and semantic-textual supervision to generalize across models and jailbreak targets.
AI.motivation: Contend with image-based jailbreaks in vision-language models that overfit to white-box surrogates, causing poor transferability across models and targets; address this vulnerability with a universal, transferable attack framework.
AI.method: Constrain adversarial patterns in the vision domain via transformations and regularisation; optimize losses in the textual embedding space of the target LLM, using semantic-based objectives to relax exact textual targets; produce universal perturbations that generalize across models and jailbreak objectives.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show UltraBreak consistently outperforms prior jailbreak methods; analysis suggests smoothing the loss landscape with semantic objectives is crucial for transferability; code is publicly available.
AI.conclusion: Vision-space regularisation combined with semantically guided textual supervision reduces surrogate overfitting and yields strong cross-model and cross-target transferability for VLM jailbreaks; highlights the importance of semantic objectives for universal, transferable attacks.

title: IC-EO: Interpretable Code-based assistant for Earth Observation
summary: Despite recent advances in computer vision, Earth Observation (EO) analysis remains difficult to perform for the laymen, requiring expert knowledge and technical capabilities. Furthermore, many systems return black-box predictions that are difficult to audit or reproduce. Leveraging recent advances in tool LLMs, this study proposes a conversational, code-generating agent that transforms natural-language queries into executable, auditable Python workflows. The agent operates over a unified easily extendable API for classification, segmentation, detection (oriented bounding boxes), spectral indices, and geospatial operators. With our proposed framework, it is possible to control the results at three levels: (i) tool-level performance on public EO benchmarks; (ii) at the agent-level to understand the capacity to generate valid, hallucination-free code; and (iii) at the task-level on specific use cases. In this work, we select two use-cases of interest: land-composition mapping and post-wildfire damage assessment. The proposed agent outperforms general-purpose LLM/VLM baselines (GPT-4o, LLaVA), achieving 64.2% vs. 51.7% accuracy on land-composition and 50% vs. 0% on post-wildfire analysis, while producing results that are transparent and easy to interpret. By outputting verifiable code, the approach turns EO analysis into a transparent, reproducible process.

AI.tldr: A conversational, code-generating agent converts natural-language EO queries into auditable Python workflows via a unified API, enabling transparent, reproducible analysis and improved task performance.
AI.motivation: EO analysis remains difficult for non-experts due to tool complexity and black-box predictions; there is a need for auditable, reproducible workflows and better interpretability.
AI.method: Develop a conversational agent that outputs executable Python code interfacing with a unified API for classification, segmentation, detection, spectral indices, and geospatial operations. Evaluate at three levels: tool-level (benchmarks), agent-level (hallucination-free code), and task-level (specific use-cases). Demonstrate on land-composition mapping and post-wildfire damage assessment.
AI.result: The agent outperforms general-purpose baselines (GPT-4o, LLaVA), achieving 64.2% vs. 51.7% accuracy on land-composition and 50% vs. 0% on post-wildfire analysis, while producing transparent, verifiable code.
AI.conclusion: An LLM-driven, code-generating agent can make EO analysis more transparent and reproducible by producing auditable workflows, bridging the gap between advanced models and practical, interpretable outcomes.

title: SFMP: Fine-Grained, Hardware-Friendly and Search-Free Mixed-Precision Quantization for Large Language Models
summary: Mixed-precision quantization is a promising approach for compressing large language models under tight memory budgets. However, existing mixed-precision methods typically suffer from one of two limitations: they either rely on expensive discrete optimization to determine precision allocation, or introduce hardware inefficiencies due to irregular memory layouts. We propose SFMP, a search-free and hardware-friendly mixed-precision quantization framework for large language models. The framework is built upon four novel ideas: Fractional bit-width, which extends integer bit-width for weight matrix to fractional value and transforms discrete precision allocation as a continuous problem; 2)Block-wise mixed-precision, enabling fine-grained precision within weight matrices while remaining hardware-friendly; 3)Row-column weight reordering, which aggregates salient weights via row and column reordering, incurring only a small activation reordering overhead during inference; 4)Unified GEMM kernel, which supports mixed-precision GEMM at arbitrary average bit-width. Extensive experiments demonstrate that SFMP outperforms state-of-the-art layer-wise mixed-precision methods under the same memory constraints, while significantly reducing quantization cost and improving inference efficiency. Code is available at https://github.com/Nkniexin/SFMP

AI.tldr: SFMP is a search-free, hardware-friendly mixed-precision quantization framework for large language models, using fractional bit-width, block-wise precision, row-column weight reordering, and a unified GEMM kernel; it outperforms state-of-the-art layer-wise methods under the same memory constraints.
AI.motivation: Existing mixed-precision quantization approaches either rely on expensive discrete optimization for precision allocation or suffer hardware inefficiencies due to irregular memory layouts. There is a need for a method that achieves a good accuracy-memory-latency trade-off with hardware-friendly design.
AI.method: 1) Fractional bit-width extends integer bit-width to fractional values, turning discrete precision allocation into a continuous optimization problem. 2) Block-wise mixed-precision enables fine-grained precision within weight matrices while remaining hardware-friendly. 3) Row-column weight reordering aggregates salient weights with minimal activation reordering overhead during inference. 4) Unified GEMM kernel supports mixed-precision GEMM at arbitrary average bit-width.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show SFMP outperforms state-of-the-art layer-wise mixed-precision methods under the same memory constraints, while reducing quantization cost and improving inference efficiency. Code is available at the provided GitHub link.
AI.conclusion: SFMP demonstrates a hardware-friendly, efficient, and effective approach to mixed-precision quantization for LLMs, with a practical design that avoids expensive discrete optimization and optimizes runtime performance; the authors provide public code for reproduction.

title: VDE Bench: Evaluating The Capability of Image Editing Models to Modify Visual Documents
summary: In recent years, multimodal image editing models have achieved substantial progress, enabling users to manipulate visual content through natural language in a flexible and interactive manner. Nevertheless, an important yet insufficiently explored research direction remains visual document image editing, which involves modifying textual content within images while faithfully preserving the original text style and background context. Existing approaches, including AnyText, GlyphControl, and TextCtrl, predominantly focus on English-language scenarios and documents with relatively sparse textual layouts, thereby failing to adequately address dense, structurally complex documents or non-Latin scripts such as Chinese. To bridge this gap, we propose \textbf{V}isual \textbf{D}oc \textbf{E}dit Bench(VDE Bench), a rigorously human-annotated and evaluated benchmark specifically designed to assess image editing models on multilingual and complex visual document editing tasks. The benchmark comprises a high-quality dataset encompassing densely textual documents in both English and Chinese, including academic papers, posters, presentation slides, examination materials, and newspapers. Furthermore, we introduce a decoupled evaluation framework that systematically quantifies editing performance at the OCR parsing level, enabling fine-grained assessment of text modification accuracy. Based on this benchmark, we conduct a comprehensive evaluation of representative state-of-the-art image editing models. Manual verification demonstrates a strong consistency between human judgments and automated evaluation metrics. VDE Bench constitutes the first systematic benchmark for evaluating image editing models on multilingual and densely textual visual documents.

AI.tldr: Introduces VDE Bench, a human-annotated benchmark and decoupled OCR-level evaluation for multilingual, dense visual document editing, enabling fine-grained assessment beyond English and sparse layouts.
AI.motivation: Addresses a gap in multimodal image editing: existing methods adapt poorly to dense, non-Latin documents and multilingual content, hindering robust evaluation and progress.
AI.method: Constructs a high-quality dataset of English and Chinese densely textual documents (papers, posters, slides, exams, newspapers) and presents a decoupled evaluation framework that measures editing performance at the OCR parsing level; includes human annotations and validation; evaluates state-of-the-art models.
AI.result: Demonstrates strong agreement between human judgments and automated OCR-based metrics; positions VDE Bench as the first systematic benchmark for multilingual, densely textual visual document editing.
AI.conclusion: VDE Bench enables rigorous, fine-grained evaluation of image editing models on multilingual dense documents and is expected to drive progress in multimodal document editing for non-English and complex layouts.

title: Adaptive Dual-Weighting Framework for Federated Learning via Out-of-Distribution Detection
summary: Federated Learning (FL) enables collaborative model training across large-scale distributed service nodes while preserving data privacy, making it a cornerstone of intelligent service systems in edge-cloud environments. However, in real-world service-oriented deployments, data generated by heterogeneous users, devices, and application scenarios are inherently non-IID. This severe data heterogeneity critically undermines the convergence stability, generalization ability, and ultimately the quality of service delivered by the global model. To address this challenge, we propose FLood, a novel FL framework inspired by out-of-distribution (OOD) detection. FLood dynamically counteracts the adverse effects of heterogeneity through a dual-weighting mechanism that jointly governs local training and global aggregation. At the client level, it adaptively reweights the supervised loss by upweighting pseudo-OOD samples, thereby encouraging more robust learning from distributionally misaligned or challenging data. At the server level, it refines model aggregation by weighting client contributions according to their OOD confidence scores, prioritizing updates from clients with higher in-distribution consistency and enhancing the global model's robustness and convergence stability. Extensive experiments across multiple benchmarks under diverse non-IID settings demonstrate that FLood consistently outperforms state-of-the-art FL methods in both accuracy and generalization. Furthermore, FLood functions as an orthogonal plug-in module: it seamlessly integrates with existing FL algorithms to boost their performance under heterogeneity without modifying their core optimization logic. These properties make FLood a practical and scalable solution for deploying reliable intelligent services in real-world federated environments.

AI.tldr: Introduces FLood, an OOD-inspired dual-weighting FL framework that mitigates non-IID effects by upweighting pseudo-OOD samples during local training and weighting client updates by OOD confidence at aggregation; acts as a plug-in to existing FL methods, yielding better accuracy and generalization.
AI.motivation: Non-IID data across clients in federated learning degrades convergence, generalization, and service quality; there is a need for robust mechanisms that adapt to distribution shifts without overhauling the core optimization process.
AI.method: Dual-weighting mechanism: (1) at the client, adaptively reweights the supervised loss by upweighting pseudo-OOD samples to learn from distributionally misaligned data; (2) at the server, refines aggregation by weighting client contributions according to their OOD confidence, favoring in-distribution-consistent updates. FLood is designed as an orthogonal plug-in module compatible with existing FL algorithms.
AI.result: Empirical evaluations on multiple benchmarks under diverse non-IID settings show FLood consistently outperforms state-of-the-art FL methods in accuracy and generalization.
AI.conclusion: FLood is practical and scalable for real-world federated services, serving as a plug-in that boosts robustness without modifying core optimization logic.

title: Superposition unifies power-law training dynamics
summary: We investigate the role of feature superposition in the emergence of power-law training dynamics using a teacher-student framework. We first derive an analytic theory for training without superposition, establishing that the power-law training exponent depends on both the input data statistics and channel importance. Remarkably, we discover that a superposition bottleneck induces a transition to a universal power-law exponent of $\sim 1$, independent of data and channel statistics. This one over time training with superposition represents an up to tenfold acceleration compared to the purely sequential learning that takes place in the absence of superposition. Our finding that superposition leads to rapid training with a data-independent power law exponent may have important implications for a wide range of neural networks that employ superposition, including production-scale large language models.

AI.tldr: Superposition bottlenecks yield a universal power-law training exponent (~1) and faster learning, decoupled from data/channel statistics, in a teacher–student setting; without superposition the exponent depends on data statistics and channel importance.
AI.motivation: Clarify how feature superposition shapes training dynamics and power-law exponents; compare training with/without superposition to reveal fundamental regimes; assess implications for large models that rely on superposition.
AI.method: Theoretical analysis in a teacher–student framework. Derive training dynamics without superposition to obtain a data/channel-statistics–dependent power-law exponent. Introduce a superposition bottleneck and show a transition to a universal exponent ≈1. Quantify acceleration (up to 10×) and discuss generalization to production-scale networks.
AI.result: Without superposition: the power-law exponent depends on input data statistics and channel importance. With a superposition bottleneck: the exponent becomes universal, ~1, independent of data and channel statistics. This universal slow-down/up-scaling yields up to ~10× faster training (in time) compared to purely sequential learning. The findings suggest broad applicability to neural networks employing superposition, including large language models.
AI.conclusion: Feature superposition can fundamentally alter training dynamics, inducing a data-independent, universal power-law with significantly accelerated training. This has potential design implications for neural networks that leverage superposition, especially large-scale models, though empirical validation and practical deployment considerations remain necessary.

title: Scalable Analytic Classifiers with Associative Drift Compensation for Class-Incremental Learning of Vision Transformers
summary: Class-incremental learning (CIL) with Vision Transformers (ViTs) faces a major computational bottleneck during the classifier reconstruction phase, where most existing methods rely on costly iterative stochastic gradient descent (SGD). We observe that analytic Regularized Gaussian Discriminant Analysis (RGDA) provides a Bayes-optimal alternative with accuracy comparable to SGD-based classifiers; however, its quadratic inference complexity limits its use in large-scale CIL scenarios. To overcome this, we propose Low-Rank Factorized RGDA (LR-RGDA), a scalable classifier that combines RGDA's expressivity with the efficiency of linear classifiers. By exploiting the low-rank structure of the covariance via the Woodbury matrix identity, LR-RGDA decomposes the discriminant function into a global affine term refined by a low-rank quadratic perturbation, reducing the inference complexity from $\mathcal{O}(Cd^2)$ to $\mathcal{O}(d^2 + Crd^2)$, where $C$ is the class number, $d$ the feature dimension, and $r \ll d$ the subspace rank. To mitigate representation drift caused by backbone updates, we further introduce Hopfield-based Distribution Compensator (HopDC), a training-free mechanism that uses modern continuous Hopfield Networks to recalibrate historical class statistics through associative memory dynamics on unlabeled anchors, accompanied by a theoretical bound on the estimation error. Extensive experiments on diverse CIL benchmarks demonstrate that our framework achieves state-of-the-art performance, providing a scalable solution for large-scale class-incremental learning with ViTs. Code: https://github.com/raoxuan98-hash/lr_rgda_hopdc.

AI.tldr: LR-RGDA provides a scalable RGDA-based classifier for class-incremental learning with Vision Transformers by exploiting low-rank covariance via Woodbury, reducing inference from O(C d^2) to O(d^2 + C r d^2); paired with HopDC, a training-free Hopfield-network–based distribution compensator, to correct historical statistics; achieves state-of-the-art results on large-scale CIL benchmarks.
AI.motivation: CIL with ViTs suffers from costly classifier reconstruction using SGD. Although Regularized Gaussian Discriminant Analysis (RGDA) is Bayes-optimal, its quadratic inference complexity hinders scalability. A scalable, accurate alternative is needed that also mitigates representation drift.
AI.method: 1) Propose Low-Rank Factorized RGDA (LR-RGDA) by exploiting low-rank covariance structure and applying the Woodbury identity to decompose the discriminant into a global affine term plus a low-rank quadratic perturbation, reducing inference complexity from O(C d^2) to O(d^2 + C r d^2), with r << d. 2) Introduce Hopfield-based Distribution Compensator (HopDC), a training-free mechanism that uses continuous Hopfield Networks to recalibrate historical class statistics via associative memory dynamics on unlabeled anchors, with a theoretical bound on estimation error.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on diverse CIL benchmarks show state-of-the-art performance, demonstrating scalability and effectiveness of LR-RGDA with HopDC for large-scale ViT-based CIL. Code is publicly released.
AI.conclusion: LR-RGDA plus HopDC provides a scalable, accurate solution for large-scale class-incremental learning with Vision Transformers, marrying Bayesian discriminants with low-rank factorization and memory-based calibration.

title: SwiftRepertoire: Few-Shot Immune-Signature Synthesis via Dynamic Kernel Codes
summary: Repertoire-level analysis of T cell receptors offers a biologically grounded signal for disease detection and immune monitoring, yet practical deployment is impeded by label sparsity, cohort heterogeneity, and the computational burden of adapting large encoders to new tasks. We introduce a framework that synthesizes compact task-specific parameterizations from a learned dictionary of prototypes conditioned on lightweight task descriptors derived from repertoire probes and pooled embedding statistics. This synthesis produces small adapter modules applied to a frozen pretrained backbone, enabling immediate adaptation to novel tasks with only a handful of support examples and without full model fine-tuning. The architecture preserves interpretability through motif-aware probes and a calibrated motif discovery pipeline that links predictive decisions to sequence-level signals. Together, these components yield a practical, sample-efficient, and interpretable pathway for translating repertoire-informed models into diverse clinical and research settings where labeled data are scarce and computational resources are constrained.

AI.tldr: A framework for few-shot adaptation of repertoire-based TCR models using a dictionary of prototypes to generate small adapters, enabling task-specific, interpretable, data-efficient deployment without full fine-tuning.
AI.motivation: TCR repertoire analysis provides disease signals but faces label sparsity, cohort heterogeneity, and high computational cost when adapting large encoders; there is a need for quick, interpretable, resource-efficient transfer to new tasks.
AI.method: Learn a dictionary of prototypes from repertoire probes and pooled embedding statistics; synthesize compact, task-conditioned adapter modules; apply adapters to a frozen pretrained backbone; maintain interpretability via motif-aware probes and a motif discovery pipeline that maps decisions to sequence-level signals.
AI.result: Produces small, task-specific adapters that enable immediate adaptation with few support examples while preserving interpretability; demonstrates sample efficiency and practicality in data-scarce, resource-constrained settings.
AI.conclusion: The approach enables scalable, interpretable, and data-efficient deployment of repertoire-informed models across diverse tasks without full fine-tuning.

title: Learning Physics-Grounded 4D Dynamics with Neural Gaussian Force Fields
summary: Predicting physical dynamics from raw visual data remains a major challenge in AI. While recent video generation models have achieved impressive visual quality, they still cannot consistently generate physically plausible videos due to a lack of modeling of physical laws. Recent approaches combining 3D Gaussian splatting and physics engines can produce physically plausible videos, but are hindered by high computational costs in both reconstruction and simulation, and often lack robustness in complex real-world scenarios. To address these issues, we introduce Neural Gaussian Force Field (NGFF), an end-to-end neural framework that integrates 3D Gaussian perception with physics-based dynamic modeling to generate interactive, physically realistic 4D videos from multi-view RGB inputs, achieving two orders of magnitude faster than prior Gaussian simulators. To support training, we also present GSCollision, a 4D Gaussian dataset featuring diverse materials, multi-object interactions, and complex scenes, totaling over 640k rendered physical videos (~4 TB). Evaluations on synthetic and real 3D scenarios show NGFF's strong generalization and robustness in physical reasoning, advancing video prediction towards physics-grounded world models.

AI.tldr: NGFF fuses 3D Gaussian perception with physics-based dynamics to predict interactive 4D videos from multi-view inputs, achieving ~100x speedups over prior Gaussian simulators, supported by a large 4D Gaussian dataset (GSCollision).
AI.motivation: Current video prediction models struggle to enforce physical plausibility; existing Gaussian+physics engines are accurate but computationally heavy, hindering real-time or large-scale training. A fast, robust, physics-grounded video predictor is needed.
AI.method: End-to-end Neural Gaussian Force Field (NGFF) that combines 3D Gaussian perception with physics-based dynamic modeling to generate physically realistic 4D videos from multi-view RGB data. Trains with a large-scale 4D Gaussian dataset (GSCollision) to support learning.
AI.result: Significant speedups (two orders of magnitude) vs prior Gaussian simulators; strong generalization and robustness in physical reasoning across synthetic and real 3D scenarios; large-scale dataset (~640k rendered videos, ~4 TB) supporting training.
AI.conclusion: NGFF advances video prediction toward physics-grounded world models and provides a substantial dataset for learning robust, physically grounded video synthesis; promising for applications in interactive simulation, robotics, and AR/VR.

title: LRAgent: Efficient KV Cache Sharing for Multi-LoRA LLM Agents
summary: Role specialization in multi-LLM agent systems is often realized via multi-LoRA, where agents share a pretrained backbone and differ only through lightweight adapters. Despite sharing base model weights, each agent independently builds and stores its own KV cache for the same long, tool-augmented trajectories, incurring substantial memory and compute overhead. Existing KV cache sharing methods largely overlook this multi-LoRA setting. We observe that, across agents, cache differences are dominated by adapter outputs, while activations from the shared pretrained backbone remain highly similar. Based on this observation, we propose LRAgent, a KV cache sharing framework for multi-LoRA agents that decomposes the cache into a shared base component from the pretrained weights and an adapter-dependent component from LoRA weights.
