LRAgent reduces memory overhead by sharing the base component and storing the adapter component in its inherent low-rank form, and further reduces compute overhead, enabled by shared-$A$ multi-LoRA architectures, by also sharing the low-rank cache and avoiding redundant computations for contexts already processed by other agents. To efficiently reconstruct adapter contributions at runtime, we introduce Flash-LoRA-Attention, a kernel that reorders attention computation to avoid materializing the low-rank cache to full dimension. LRAgent achieves throughput and time-to-first-token latency close to fully shared caching, while preserving accuracy near the non-shared caching baseline across agentic question-answering benchmarks.

AI.tldr: LRAgent introduces a KV cache sharing framework for multi-LoRA LLM agents by separating a shared base cache from adapter-specific low-rank components, with Flash-LoRA-Attention to reconstruct contributions efficiently; achieves memory/compute savings with accuracy close to non-shared baselines.
AI.motivation: In multi-agent LLM setups, agents share a backbone but use separate LoRA adapters; each agent builds its own KV cache for long tool-augmented trajectories, incurring high memory and compute costs. Existing KV sharing methods largely overlook multi-LoRA arrangements; backbone activations across agents remain highly similar while adapter outputs dominate cache differences.
AI.method: Decompose the cache into a shared base component from pretrained weights and an adapter-dependent component from LoRA weights. Share the base component and store the adapter component in its low-rank form. Leverage shared-A multi-LoRA architectures to also share the low-rank cache and avoid redundant computations. Introduce Flash-LoRA-Attention, a kernel that reorders attention computation to avoid materializing the low-rank cache to full dimension. Runtime reconstruction of adapter contributions.
AI.result: Throughput and time-to-first-token latency approach fully shared caching, while accuracy remains near the non-shared baseline on agentic question-answering benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: LRAgent reduces memory and compute overhead for multi-LoRA agents while preserving accuracy, enabling efficient KV cache sharing across agents.

title: Investigating the Impact of Histopathological Foundation Models on Regressive Prediction of Homologous Recombination Deficiency
comment: 9 pages, 7 figures and 5 tables. Initialy submitted for IJCAI 2026
summary: Foundation models pretrained on large-scale histopathology data have found great success in various fields of computational pathology, but their impact on regressive biomarker prediction remains underexplored. In this work, we systematically evaluate histopathological foundation models for regression-based tasks, demonstrated through the prediction of homologous recombination deficiency (HRD) score - a critical biomarker for personalized cancer treatment. Within multiple instance learning frameworks, we extract patch-level features from whole slide images (WSI) using five state-of-the-art foundation models, and evaluate their impact compared to contrastive learning-based features. Models are trained to predict continuous HRD scores based on these extracted features across breast, endometrial, and lung cancer cohorts from two public medical data collections. Extensive experiments demonstrate that models trained on foundation model features consistently outperform the baseline in terms of predictive accuracy and generalization capabilities while exhibiting systematic differences among the foundation models. Additionally, we propose a distribution-based upsampling strategy to mitigate target imbalance in these datasets, significantly improving the recall and balanced accuracy for underrepresented but clinically important patient populations. Furthermore, we investigate the impact of different sampling strategies and instance bagsizes by ablation studies. Our results highlight the benefits of large-scale histopathological pretraining for more precise and transferable regressive biomarker prediction, showcasing its potential to advance AI-driven precision oncology.

AI.tldr: Large-scale histopathology foundation models are effective for regression of continuous biomarkers, here predicting HRD scores from WSI patches using multiple instance learning; they outperform contrastive features and benefit from a distribution-based upsampling to address target imbalance.
AI.motivation: Address the gap in regression-based biomarker prediction in computational pathology and assess whether foundation-model features can provide better accuracy and generalization for continuous HRD scores across cancer types.
AI.method: Extract patch-level features from WSIs using five state-of-the-art histopathology foundation models within multiple instance learning frameworks; compare with contrastive-learning features; train models to predict continuous HRD scores across breast, endometrial, and lung cancer cohorts from two public data sources; propose a distribution-based upsampling to mitigate target imbalance; perform ablation studies on sampling strategies and bagsizes.
AI.result: Foundation-model features consistently improve predictive accuracy and generalization for HRD score prediction compared to baseline features; differences observed among foundation models; upsampling significantly improves recall and balanced accuracy for underrepresented patients; ablation results elucidate sampling strategy and bag-size effects.
AI.conclusion: Histopathology foundation pretraining enhances regressive biomarker prediction and transferability, supporting AI-driven precision oncology, with practical gains from addressing data imbalance and sampling choices.

title: Good SFT Optimizes for SFT, Better SFT Prepares for Reinforcement Learning
summary:
Post-training of reasoning LLMs is a holistic process that typically consists of an offline SFT stage followed by an online reinforcement learning (RL) stage. However, SFT is often optimized in isolation to maximize SFT performance alone.
  We show that, after identical RL training, models initialized from stronger SFT checkpoints can significantly underperform those initialized from weaker ones. We attribute this to a mismatch typical in current SFT-RL pipelines: the distribution that generates the offline SFT data can differ substantially from the policy optimized during online RL, which learns from its own rollouts.
  We propose PEAR (Policy Evaluation-inspired Algorithm for Offline Learning Loss Re-weighting), an SFT-stage method that corrects this mismatch and better prepares the model for RL. PEAR uses importance sampling to reweight the SFT loss, with three variants operating at the token, block, and sequence levels. It can be used to augment standard SFT objectives and incurs little additional training overhead once probabilities for the offline data are collected.
  We conduct controlled experiments on verifiable reasoning games and mathematical reasoning tasks on Qwen 2.5 and 3 and DeepSeek-distilled models. PEAR consistently improves post-RL performance over canonical SFT, with pass at 8 gains up to a 14.6 percent on AIME2025. Our results suggest that PEAR is an effective step toward more holistic LLM post-training by designing and evaluating SFT with downstream RL in mind rather than in isolation.

AI.tldr: SFT-RL mismatch can hurt post-training; PEAR reweights SFT loss via importance sampling to align offline data with online RL, improving post-RL performance.
AI.motivation: Address the observed mismatch between the offline SFT data distribution and the RL policy distribution learned during online RL in post-training of reasoning LLMs, which can cause stronger SFT checkpoints to underperform weaker ones.
AI.method: Introduce PEAR (Policy Evaluation-inspired Algorithm for Offline Learning Loss Re-weighting): reweights the SFT loss via importance sampling. Three variants operate at token, block, and sequence levels to align SFT data with the RL objective; can augment standard SFT with negligible excess overhead once offline probabilities are known.
AI.result: PEAR improves post-RL performance over canonical SFT across verifiable reasoning games and math tasks on Qwen 2.5/3 and DeepSeek-distilled models; gains include up to 14.6 percentage points in pass@8 on AIME2025.
AI.conclusion: PEAR is an effective step toward holistic LLM post-training by integrating downstream RL considerations into the SFT stage, mitigating distribution mismatch and improving downstream RL performance.

title: Real-Time Human Activity Recognition on Edge Microcontrollers: Dynamic Hierarchical Inference with Multi-Spectral Sensor Fusion
comment: 24 pages, 6 figures. The manusrcipt is under review at Measurement
summary: The demand for accurate on-device pattern recognition in edge applications is intensifying, yet existing approaches struggle to reconcile accuracy with computational constraints. To address this challenge, a resource-aware hierarchical network based on multi-spectral fusion and interpretable modules, namely the Hierarchical Parallel Pseudo-image Enhancement Fusion Network (HPPI-Net), is proposed for real-time, on-device Human Activity Recognition (HAR). Deployed on an ARM Cortex-M4 microcontroller for low-power real-time inference, HPPI-Net achieves 96.70% accuracy while utilizing only 22.3 KiB of RAM and 439.5 KiB of ROM after optimization. HPPI-Net employs a two-layer architecture. The first layer extracts preliminary features using Fast Fourier Transform (FFT) spectrograms, while the second layer selectively activates either a dedicated module for stationary activity recognition or a parallel LSTM-MobileNet network (PLMN) for dynamic states. PLMN fuses FFT, Wavelet, and Gabor spectrograms through three parallel LSTM encoders and refines the concatenated features using Efficient Channel Attention (ECA) and Depthwise Separable Convolution (DSC), thereby offering channel-level interpretability while substantially reducing multiply-accumulate operations. Compared with MobileNetV3, HPPI-Net improves accuracy by 1.22% and reduces RAM usage by 71.2% and ROM usage by 42.1%. These results demonstrate that HPPI-Net achieves a favorable accuracy-efficiency trade-off and provides explainable predictions, establishing a practical solution for wearable, industrial, and smart home HAR on memory-constrained edge platforms.

AI.tldr: HPPI-Net is a memory-efficient, hierarchical on-device HAR network that fuses multiple spectrogram features via a PLMN to achieve 96.7% accuracy on Cortex-M4 with minimal RAM/ROM, outperforming MobileNetV3 while using far fewer resources and offering interpretability.
AI.motivation: Edge pattern recognition tasks demand high accuracy under strict compute and memory constraints; existing on-device HAR approaches struggle to balance accuracy with resource budgets for real-time inference.
AI.method: A two-layer hierarchy: layer 1 extracts preliminary features using FFT spectrograms; layer 2 selects either a dedicated stationary activity module or a parallel LSTM-MobileNet network (PLMN) for dynamic states. PLMN fuses FFT, Wavelet, and Gabor spectrograms through three parallel LSTM encoders and refines concatenated features with Efficient Channel Attention (ECA) and Depthwise Separable Convolution (DSC), enabling channel-level interpretability while reducing MAC operations.
AI.result: 96.70% accuracy; RAM 22.3 KiB; ROM 439.5 KiB (after optimization) on ARM Cortex-M4. Compared with MobileNetV3: accuracy gain 1.22%, RAM reduction 71.2%, ROM reduction 42.1%.
AI.conclusion: HPPI-Net achieves a favorable accuracy-efficiency trade-off with explainable predictions, presenting a practical HAR solution for memory-constrained edge platforms in wearables, industrial settings, and smart homes.

title: On the Expressive Power of Permutation-Equivariant Weight-Space Networks
summary: Weight-space learning studies neural architectures that operate directly on the parameters of other neural networks. Motivated by the growing availability of pretrained models, recent work has demonstrated the effectiveness of weight-space networks across a wide range of tasks. SOTA weight-space networks rely on permutation-equivariant designs to improve generalization. However, this may negatively affect expressive power, warranting theoretical investigation. Importantly, unlike other structured domains, weight-space learning targets maps operating on both weight and function spaces, making expressivity analysis particularly subtle. While a few prior works provide partial expressivity results, a comprehensive characterization is still missing. In this work, we address this gap by developing a systematic theory for expressivity of weight-space networks. We first prove that all prominent permutation-equivariant networks are equivalent in expressive power. We then establish universality in both weight- and function-space settings under mild, natural assumptions on the input weights, and characterize the edge-case regimes where universality no longer holds. Together, these results provide a strong and unified foundation for the expressivity of weight-space networks.

AI.tldr: A unified theoretical framework for the expressivity of weight-space networks. It shows that prominent permutation-equivariant designs have equivalent expressive power, proves universality in both weight- and function-space settings under mild input-weight assumptions, and identifies edge-case regimes where universality can fail.
AI.motivation: Understand how permutation-equivariant weight-space networks generalize and how their expressivity scales when operating on both weight and function spaces, given the rise of pretrained models and the need for robust theoretical foundations.
AI.method: Theoretical analysis proving (i) equivalence in expressive power among major permutation-equivariant architectures, and (ii) universality results in weight- and function-space settings under natural assumptions on input weights, plus a characterization of edge cases where universality breaks.
AI.result: All prominent permutation-equivariant networks are expressively equivalent; there is universality in both weight- and function-space settings under mild assumptions; explicit conditions identifying edge cases where universality does not hold.
AI.conclusion: The work provides a unified, rigorous foundation for the expressivity of weight-space networks, clarifying when permutation-equivariant designs preserve expressive power and when universality may fail, facilitating future theory and design.

title: See Without Decoding: Motion-Vector-Based Tracking in Compressed Video
summary: We propose a lightweight compressed-domain tracking model that operates directly on video streams, without requiring full RGB video decoding. Using motion vectors and transform coefficients from compressed data, our deep model propagates object bounding boxes across frames, achieving a computational speed-up of order up to 3.7 with only a slight 4% mAP@0.5 drop vs RGB baseline on MOTS15/17/20 datasets. These results highlight codec-domain motion modeling efficiency for real-time analytics in large monitoring systems.

AI.tldr: Lightweight compressed-domain tracker that uses encoded-data cues (motion vectors and transform coefficients) to propagate object bounding boxes without full RGB decoding, achieving up to 3.7x speed-up with a ~4% mAP@0.5 drop on MOTS15/17/20.
AI.motivation: Reduce computational load for real-time video analytics by exploiting encoded-domain information instead of full-decoding RGB frames.
AI.method: A deep model operating on compressed-domain features (motion vectors and transform coefficients) to propagate bounding boxes across frames directly from the compressed stream, evaluated on MOTS15/17/20 datasets.
AI.result: Up to 3.7× speed-up with a ~4% mAP@0.5 drop compared to RGB baseline on MOTS15/17/20 datasets.
AI.conclusion: Codec-domain motion modeling is effective for real-time analytics in large monitoring systems; demonstrates a favorable speed-accuracy trade-off and practicality of compressed-domain tracking in MOT tasks.

title: OLion: Approaching the Hadamard Ideal by Intersecting Spectral and $\ell_{\infty}$ Implicit Biases
summary:
Many optimizers can be interpreted as steepest-descent methods under norm-induced geometries, and thus inherit corresponding implicit biases. We introduce 
ameA{} ( ullname{}), which combines spectral control from orthogonalized update directions with $\ell_\infty$-style coordinate control from sign updates. 
ameA{} forms a Lion-style momentum direction, approximately orthogonalizes it via a few Newton--Schulz iterations, and then applies an entrywise sign, providing an efficient approximation to taking a maximal step over the intersection of the spectral and $\ell_\infty$ constraint sets (a scaled Hadamard-like set for matrix parameters). Despite the strong nonlinearity of orthogonalization and sign, we prove convergence under a mild, empirically verified diagonal-isotropy assumption. Across large-scale language and vision training, including GPT-2 and Llama pretraining, SiT image pretraining, and supervised fine-tuning, 
ameA{} matches or outperforms AdamW and Muon under comparable tuning while using only momentum-level optimizer state, and it mitigates optimizer mismatch when fine-tuning AdamW-pretrained checkpoints.

AI.tldr: A spectral-sign optimizer SiT that blends orthogonalized (spectral) updates with entrywise sign control to enable near-maximal steps under spectral and l_infty constraints; provides convergence guarantees and strong empirical performance with minimal optimizer state.
AI.motivation: Many optimizers are steepest-descent w.r.t. norm-induced geometries; there is value in combining spectral control and coordinate-wise sign updates to bias optimization toward favorable directions while reducing state requirements.
AI.method: Generate Lion-like momentum direction; orthogonalize approximately via a few Newton-Schulz iterations; apply an entrywise sign; approximate a maximal step over the intersection of spectral and l_infty constraint sets; operate using only momentum-level state; prove convergence under diagonal-isotropy; scalable to large models.
AI.result: Matches or outperforms AdamW and Muon on GPT-2, Llama, SiT image pretraining, and supervised fine-tuning under similar tuning; reduces optimizer mismatch when fine-tuning AdamW-pretrained checkpoints.
AI.conclusion: The proposed SiT optimizer provides a simple, efficient, and robust optimization bias with theoretical convergence and strong empirical performance across large-scale DL workloads.

title: Single-Edge Node Injection Threats to GNN-Based Security Monitoring in Industrial Graph Systems
summary: Graph neural networks (GNNs) are increasingly adopted in industrial graph-based monitoring systems (e.g., Industrial internet of things (IIoT) device graphs, power-grid topology models, and manufacturing communication networks) to support anomaly detection, state estimation, and asset classification. In such settings, an adversary that compromises a small number of edge devices may inject counterfeit nodes (e.g., rogue sensors, virtualized endpoints, or spoofed substations) to bias downstream decisions while evading topology- and homophily-based sanitization. This paper formulates deployment-oriented node-injection attacks under constrained resources and proposes the \emph{Single-Edge Graph Injection Attack} (SEGIA), in which each injected node attaches to the operational graph through a single edge. SEGIA integrates a pruned SGC surrogate, multi-hop neighborhood sampling, and reverse graph convolution-based feature synthesis with a similarity-regularized objective to preserve local homophily and survive edge pruning. Theoretical analysis and extensive evaluations across datasets and defenses show at least $25\%$ higher attack success than representative baselines under substantially smaller edge budgets. These results indicate a system-level risk in industrial GNN deployments and motivate lightweight admission validation and neighborhood-consistency monitoring.

AI.tldr: Single-Edge Graph Injection Attack (SEGIA): a resource-constrained, node-injection attack on industrial GNNs where each injected node attaches through one edge. It employs a pruned SGC surrogate, multi-hop neighborhood sampling, and reverse graph convolution-based feature synthesis with a similarity-regularized objective to preserve local homophily and survive edge pruning, achieving at least 25% higher attack success than baselines under small edge budgets. Demonstrates system-level risks and need for defense like admission validation and neighborhood-consistency monitoring.
AI.motivation: Industrial GNN deployments (e.g., IIoT device graphs, power grids, manufacturing networks) are vulnerable to counterfeit nodes that bias decisions while evading topology- and homophily-based sanitization. There is a need for deployment-oriented, resource-constrained attack models to guide robust defenses.
AI.method: Propose SEGIA: inject nodes that connect to the operational graph via a single edge. Use a pruned SGC surrogate to approximate the GNN, perform multi-hop neighborhood sampling, and synthesize node features via reverse graph convolution with a similarity-regularized objective to maintain local homophily and endure edge pruning.
AI.result: Theoretical analysis and extensive empirical evaluation across datasets and defense mechanisms show SEGIA achieves at least 25% higher attack success than representative baselines under smaller edge budgets.
AI.conclusion: SEGIA uncovers a systemic vulnerability in industrial GNN deployments, highlighting the need for lightweight admission validation and neighborhood-consistency monitoring to mitigate node-injection threats.

title: MarkovScale: Towards Optimal Sequential Scaling at Inference Time
summary: Sequential scaling is a prominent inference-time scaling paradigm, yet its performance improvements are typically modest and not well understood, largely due to the prevalence of heuristic, non-principled approaches that obscure clear optimality bounds. To address this, we propose a principled framework that models sequential scaling as a two-state Markov process. This approach reveals the underlying properties of sequential scaling and yields closed-form solutions for essential aspects, such as the specific conditions under which accuracy is improved and the theoretical upper, neutral, and lower performance bounds. Leveraging this formulation, we develop MarkovScale, a practical system that applies these optimality criteria to achieve a theoretically grounded balance between accuracy and efficiency. Comprehensive experiments across 3 backbone LLMs, 5 benchmarks, and over 20 configurations show that MarkovScale consistently outperforms state-of-the-art parallel and sequential scaling methods, representing a significant step toward optimal and resource-efficient inference in LLMs. The source code will be open upon acceptance at https://open-upon-acceptance.

AI.tldr: Proposes MarkovScale, a principled, two-state Markov model of sequential scaling to derive optimal accuracy–efficiency trade-offs and bounds, with closed-form conditions for improvement; reports strong empirical gains across multiple LLM backbones and benchmarks; code to be released upon acceptance.
AI.motivation: Current sequential scaling methods are often heuristic and lack principled optimality guarantees, making it difficult to understand when and why accuracy improves and to bound performance.
AI.method: Model sequential scaling as a two-state Markov process, derive closed-form conditions for accuracy improvement and compute theoretical upper, neutral, and lower bounds. Develop MarkovScale to apply these optimality criteria in practice, enabling an efficient-accuracy balance. Conduct extensive experiments across 3 backbone LLMs, 5 benchmarks, and 20 configurations to validate performance gains.
AI.result: The framework yields principled bounds and closed-form solutions; MarkovScale outperforms state-of-the-art parallel and sequential scaling methods across the tested settings.
AI.conclusion: Represents a significant step toward principled, resource-efficient inference in LLMs, with open-source code forthcoming.

title: ChronoSpike: An Adaptive Spiking Graph Neural Network for Dynamic Graphs
summary: Dynamic graph representation learning requires capturing both structural relationships and temporal evolution, yet existing approaches face a fundamental trade-off: attention-based methods achieve expressiveness at $O(T^2)$ complexity, while recurrent architectures suffer from gradient pathologies and dense state storage. Spiking neural networks offer event-driven efficiency but remain limited by sequential propagation, binary information loss, and local aggregation that misses global context. We propose ChronoSpike, an adaptive spiking graph neural network that integrates learnable LIF neurons with per-channel membrane dynamics, multi-head attentive spatial aggregation on continuous features, and a lightweight Transformer temporal encoder, enabling both fine-grained local modeling and long-range dependency capture with linear memory complexity $O(T \cdot d)$. On three large-scale benchmarks, ChronoSpike outperforms twelve state-of-the-art baselines by $2.0\%$ Macro-F1 and $2.4\%$ Micro-F1 while achieving $3-10\times$ faster training than recurrent methods with a constant 105K-parameter budget independent of graph size. We provide theoretical guarantees for membrane potential boundedness, gradient flow stability under contraction factor $ρ< 1$, and BIBO stability; interpretability analyses reveal heterogeneous temporal receptive fields and a learned primacy effect with $83-88\%$ sparsity.

AI.tldr: ChronoSpike is an adaptive spiking GNN that combines learnable LIF neurons with per-channel membrane dynamics, multi-head spatial attention on continuous features, and a lightweight Transformer temporal encoder to achieve linear memory complexity and state-of-the-art performance on dynamic graphs.
AI.motivation: Dynamic graph representation learning faces a trade-off: attention-based models have O(T^2) temporal complexity, recurrent architectures suffer from gradient issues and dense state storage, and spiking networks, while efficient, suffer from sequential propagation and limited global context. A model is needed that provides long-range temporal modeling with scalable memory and efficient computation.
AI.method: Proposes ChronoSpike: an adaptive spiking graph neural network that (i) uses learnable LIF neurons with per-channel membrane dynamics, (ii) applies multi-head attentive spatial aggregation on continuous features, and (iii) incorporates a lightweight Transformer-based temporal encoder, yielding linear memory complexity O(T·d) and a fixed 105K parameter budget independent of graph size, with performance and stability guarantees.
AI.result: Empirically, ChronoSpike outperforms twelve state-of-the-art baselines on three large-scale benchmarks by 2.0% Macro-F1 and 2.4% Micro-F1. Training is 3–10× faster than recurrent methods. The model uses a constant 105K parameters regardless of graph size. Theoretical guarantees include membrane potential boundedness, gradient flow stability under contraction factor ρ<1, and BIBO stability. Interpretability analyses reveal heterogeneous temporal receptive fields and a learned primacy effect with 83–88% sparsity.
AI.conclusion: ChronoSpike demonstrates that combining adaptive spiking units with per-channel dynamics, attentive spatial aggregation, and a lightweight temporal transformer yields scalable, accurate, and interpretable dynamic graph modeling with provable stability and efficiency.

title: WinFLoRA: Incentivizing Client-Adaptive Aggregation in Federated LoRA under Privacy Heterogeneity
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) increasingly underpin intelligent web applications, from chatbots to search and recommendation, where efficient specialization is essential. Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) enables such adaptation with minimal overhead, while federated LoRA allows web service providers to fine-tune shared models without data sharing. However, in privacy-sensitive deployments, clients inject varying levels of differential privacy (DP) noise, creating privacy heterogeneity that misaligns individual incentives and global performance. In this paper, we propose WinFLoRA, a privacy-heterogeneous federated LoRA that utilizes aggregation weights as incentives with noise awareness. Specifically, the noises from clients are estimated based on the uploaded LoRA adapters. A larger weight indicates greater influence on the global model and better downstream task performance, rewarding lower-noise contributions. By up-weighting low-noise updates, WinFLoRA improves global accuracy while accommodating clients' heterogeneous privacy requirements. Consequently, WinFLoRA aligns heterogeneous client utility in terms of privacy and downstream performance with global model objectives without third-party involvement. Extensive evaluations demonstrate that across multiple LLMs and datasets, WinFLoRA achieves up to 52.58% higher global accuracy and up to 2.56x client utility than state-of-the-art benchmarks. Source code is publicly available at https://github.com/koums24/WinFLoRA.git.

AI.tldr: WinFLoRA introduces noise-aware aggregation in privacy-heterogeneous federated LoRA to reward low-noise updates, improving global accuracy and client utility without third-party involvement.
AI.motivation: Privacy heterogeneity from differential privacy (DP) noise in federated LoRA creates misalignment between client incentives and global performance; a method that accounts for per-client noise can improve global outcomes while respecting privacy.
AI.method: Estimate DP noise from uploaded LoRA adapters, compute aggregation weights proportional to inferred influence and downstream task performance, up-weight low-noise updates, implement in federated LoRA, no third-party involvement; source code released.
AI.result: Across multiple LLMs and datasets, WinFLoRA achieves up to 52.58% higher global accuracy and up to 2.56x greater client utility compared with state-of-the-art benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: WinFLoRA aligns heterogeneous client privacy requirements with global objectives, improving performance while preserving privacy and enabling decentralized operation; code is publicly available.

title: Tangent Space Fine-Tuning for Directional Preference Alignment in Large Language Models
summary: Our goal is to enable large language models (LLMs) to balance multiple human preference dimensions; such as helpfulness, safety, and verbosity, through principled and controllable alignment. Existing preference optimization methods, including Direct Preference Optimization (DPO), collapse feedback into a single scalar reward, fixing one balance among objectives and preventing traversal of the Pareto front. Recent work by Ortiz-Jimenez et al. (2023) showed that fine-tuning can be viewed in a model's tangent space, where linearized updates act as additive vectors that can be composed to jointly perform well on multiple tasks. Building on this formulation, we extend this idea to preference alignment and propose Tangent-Space Direct Preference Optimization (TS-DPO), which performs DPO within this locally linear regime to learn per-objective update directions. These directions can be linearly combined at inference to generate user-specified behaviors without additional optimization. Evaluated on the helpfulness-verbosity trade-off using the HelpSteer and UltraFeedback datasets, TS-DPO achieves broader Pareto-optimal coverage and smoother preference control than scalarized DPO. Canonical Correlation Analysis (CCA) further shows that tangent-space training amplifies canonical directions aligned with distinct preferences, improving disentanglement.

AI.tldr: Introduces Tangent-Space Direct Preference Optimization (TS-DPO), a locally linear, per-objective update method for LLM alignment that enables composable, inference-time trade-offs across multiple preferences, improving Pareto coverage over scalarized DPO.
AI.motivation: Standard DPO and similar preference methods collapse feedback into a single scalar, hindering exploration of the Pareto front and limiting multi-objective control. Building on Ortiz-Jimenez et al. (2023), which treats fine-tuning as tangent-space updates, the work aims to enable independent per-objective updates that can be combined to satisfy multiple preferences.
AI.method: Perform DPO in the model's tangent space to obtain per-objective update directions; at inference, linearly combine these directions to realize user-specified trade-offs without re-optimizing. Evaluation on HelpSteer and UltraFeedback datasets; use Canonical Correlation Analysis (CCA) to assess alignment and disentanglement.
AI.result: TS-DPO achieves broader Pareto-optimal coverage and smoother preference control than scalarized DPO. CCA shows tangent-space training amplifies canonical directions aligned with distinct preferences, improving disentanglement.
AI.conclusion: Proposes a practical framework for multi-objective preference alignment that allows composable, inference-time control of user-specified behaviors, with empirical gains in Pareto efficiency and preference disentanglement.

title: TRACE: Scalable Amortized Causal Discovery from Single Sequences via Autoregressive Density Estimation
summary: We study causal discovery from a single observed sequence of discrete events generated by a stochastic process, as encountered in vehicle logs, manufacturing systems, or patient trajectories. This regime is particularly challenging due to the absence of repeated samples, high dimensionality, and long-range temporal dependencies of the single observation during inference. We introduce TRACE, a scalable framework that repurposes autoregressive models as pretrained density estimators for conditional mutual information estimation. TRACE infers the summary causal graph between event types in a sequence, scaling linearly with the event vocabulary and supporting delayed causal effects, while being fully parallel on GPUs. We establish its theoretical identifiability under imperfect autoregressive models. Experiments demonstrate robust performance across different baselines and varying vocabulary sizes including an application to root-cause analysis in vehicle diagnostics with over 29,100 event types.

AI.tldr: TRACE enables scalable causal discovery from a single sequence using autoregressive-based density estimation to estimate conditional mutual information, enabling linear scaling in vocabulary, delayed effects, and GPU parallelism; with theoretical identifiability and strong empirical validation up to 29k event types.
AI.motivation: Causal discovery from a single observed sequence is challenging due to lack of repeated samples, high dimensionality, and long-range temporal dependencies; a scalable method is needed for large vocabularies and real-world domains.
AI.method: Use autoregressive models as pretrained density estimators to estimate conditional mutual information (CMI) between event types; TRACE infers a summary causal graph over event types in the sequence; scalable to vocabulary size with linear complexity; supports delayed causal effects; fully parallel on GPUs; includes theoretical identifiability under imperfect autoregressive models.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation shows robust performance against baselines across varying vocabulary sizes; demonstrates applicability to root-cause analysis in vehicle diagnostics with over 29,100 event types.
AI.conclusion: TRACE provides a scalable, identifiable approach for causal discovery from a single sequence, enabling practical analysis in high-dimensional temporal settings; validated across domains and large-scale vocabularies.

title: A Unified Matrix-Spectral Framework for Stability and Interpretability in Deep Learning
summary:
We develop a unified matrix-spectral framework for analyzing stability and interpretability in deep neural networks. Representing networks as data-dependent products of linear operators reveals spectral quantities governing sensitivity to input perturbations, label noise, and training dynamics.
  We introduce a Global Matrix Stability Index that aggregates spectral information from Jacobians, parameter gradients, Neural Tangent Kernel operators, and loss Hessians into a single stability scale controlling forward sensitivity, attribution robustness, and optimization conditioning. We further show that spectral entropy refines classical operator-norm bounds by capturing typical, rather than purely worst-case, sensitivity.
  These quantities yield computable diagnostics and stability-oriented regularization principles. Synthetic experiments and controlled studies on MNIST, CIFAR-10, and CIFAR-100 confirm that modest spectral regularization substantially improves attribution stability even when global spectral summaries change little.
  The results establish a precise connection between spectral concentration and analytic stability, providing practical guidance for robustness-aware model design and training.

AI.tldr: A unified matrix-spectral framework for stability and interpretability in deep nets, introducing a Global Matrix Stability Index and spectral entropy to aggregate spectral information from Jacobians, gradients, NTK, and Hessians. Modest spectral regularization improves attribution stability; validated on MNIST and CIFAR tasks, linking spectral concentration to stability and providing robustness-oriented training guidance.
AI.motivation: Address the gap between worst-case operator-norm bounds and practical stability/interpretability in deep nets. Establish a principled, computable framework that connects spectral properties of network operators to training dynamics, robustness to perturbations/noise, and attribution robustness.
AI.method: Represent networks as data-dependent products of linear operators. Define Global Matrix Stability Index by aggregating spectral information from Jacobians, parameter gradients, Neural Tangent Kernel operators, and loss Hessians into a single stability scale. Introduce spectral entropy as a finer measure than operator norms to capture typical sensitivity. Propose computable diagnostics and stability-oriented regularization principles.
AI.result: Global Matrix Stability Index and spectral entropy provide actionable diagnostics; modest spectral regularization improves attribution stability even when global spectral summaries change little. Empirical studies on MNIST, CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100 support robustness benefits and reveal a link between spectral concentration and analytic stability.
AI.conclusion: Spectral concentration underlies stability and robustness in deep nets. The framework offers practical guidance for designing and training robustness-aware models, with computable metrics and regularizers that enhance attribution reliability and forward stability.

title: Stabilizing Diffusion Posterior Sampling by Noise--Frequency Continuation
summary: Diffusion posterior sampling solves inverse problems by combining a pretrained diffusion prior with measurement-consistency guidance, but it often fails to recover fine details because measurement terms are applied in a manner that is weakly coupled to the diffusion noise level. At high noise, data-consistency gradients computed from inaccurate estimates can be geometrically incongruent with the posterior geometry, inducing early-step drift, spurious high-frequency artifacts, plus sensitivity to schedules and ill-conditioned operators. To address these concerns, we propose a noise--frequency Continuation framework that constructs a continuous family of intermediate posteriors whose likelihood enforces measurement consistency only within a noise-dependent frequency band. This principle is instantiated with a stabilized posterior sampler that combines a diffusion predictor, band-limited likelihood guidance, and a multi-resolution consistency strategy that aggressively commits reliable coarse corrections while conservatively adopting high-frequency details only when they become identifiable. Across super-resolution, inpainting, and deblurring, our method achieves state-of-the-art performance and improves motion deblurring PSNR by up to 5 dB over strong baselines.

AI.tldr: A diffusion posterior sampling method with a noise-frequency continuation framework that enforces measurement consistency in a noise-dependent frequency band, using a stabilized posterior sampler with band-limited guidance and multi-resolution consistency, achieving state-of-the-art results in super-resolution, inpainting, and deblurring (up to 5 dB PSNR gain in motion deblurring).
AI.motivation: To address failure modes of diffusion posterior sampling where measurement terms are weakly coupled to diffusion noise, causing early-step drift, artifacts, and sensitivity to schedules and ill-conditioned operators at high noise.
AI.method: Introduce a noise--frequency Continuation framework that constructs a continuous family of intermediate posteriors whose likelihood enforces measurement consistency only within a noise-dependent frequency band. Implemented via a stabilized posterior sampler that combines a diffusion predictor, band-limited likelihood guidance, and a multi-resolution consistency strategy that emphasizes reliable coarse corrections and conservatively adds high-frequency details when identifiable.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance across super-resolution, inpainting, and deblurring; reports significant improvement (up to ~5 dB PSNR) in motion deblurring over strong baselines.
AI.conclusion: The approach mitigates mismatch between data-consistency and diffusion geometry, improving stability, detail recovery, and robustness across inverse problems while reducing sensitivity to noise levels and operator conditioning.

title: Self-Generative Adversarial Fine-Tuning for Large Language Models
summary: Fine-tuning large language models (LLMs) for alignment typically relies on supervised fine-tuning or reinforcement learning from human feedback, both limited by the cost and scarcity of high-quality annotations. Recent self-play and synthetic data approaches reduce this dependence but often rely on heuristic assumptions or ungrounded self-evaluation, which can cause bias accumulation and performance drift. In this paper, we propose Self-Generative Adversarial LLM (SGALM), a unified fine-tuning framework that formulates alignment as a generative adversarial game within a single LLM. SGALM jointly evolves generation and discrimination capabilities without external reward models. Theoretical and empirical results demonstrate that SGALM achieves state-of-the-art performance, serves as an effective alignment algorithm and a robust synthetic data engine.

AI.tldr: Introduces SGALM, a self-contained LLM-based generative-adversarial framework for alignment that operates without external reward models, achieving state-of-the-art results and enabling robust synthetic data generation.
AI.motivation: Mitigate dependence on costly human annotations and external reward signals for alignment; address bias and drift from self-play and heuristic self-evaluation by internalizing generation and discrimination within a single LLM.
AI.method: Formulates alignment as a GAN-like game inside one LLM, jointly evolving generator and discriminator capabilities without external reward models; training loop self-contained within the model.
AI.result: Theoretical analysis and empirical experiments indicate state-of-the-art alignment performance and demonstrate SGALM as both an effective alignment algorithm and a robust synthetic data engine.
AI.conclusion: SGALM provides a unified, self-sufficient approach to LLM alignment, reducing reliance on external rewards and annotation pipelines while offering high-quality synthetic data generation.

title: CamReasoner: Reinforcing Camera Movement Understanding via Structured Spatial Reasoning
summary: Understanding camera dynamics is a fundamental pillar of video spatial intelligence. However, existing multimodal models predominantly treat this task as a black-box classification, often confusing physically distinct motions by relying on superficial visual patterns rather than geometric cues. We present CamReasoner, a framework that reformulates camera movement understanding as a structured inference process to bridge the gap between perception and cinematic logic. Our approach centers on the Observation-Thinking-Answer (O-T-A) paradigm, which compels the model to decode spatio-temporal cues such as trajectories and view frustums within an explicit reasoning block. To instill this capability, we construct a Large-scale Inference Trajectory Suite comprising 18k SFT reasoning chains and 38k RL feedback samples. Notably, we are the first to employ RL for logical alignment in this domain, ensuring motion inferences are grounded in physical geometry rather than contextual guesswork. By applying Reinforcement Learning to the Observation-Think-Answer (O-T-A) reasoning paradigm, CamReasoner effectively suppresses hallucinations and achieves state-of-the-art performance across multiple benchmarks.

AI.tldr: Reframes camera-motion understanding as structured inference using an Observation-Think-Answer (O-T-A) paradigm, augmented with a large RL-grounded reasoning dataset, to achieve state-of-the-art results and reduce hallucinations by grounding in geometric cues.
AI.motivation: Current multimodal models treat camera dynamics as black-box classification, exploiting superficial cues rather than geometric trajectories and view frustums; need explicit reasoning aligned with physical cinema geometry.
AI.method: Introduce O-T-A reasoning blocks for decoding spatio-temporal cues; build Large-scale Inference Trajectory Suite with 18k SFT reasoning chains and 38k RL feedback samples; apply reinforcement learning to align reasoning with physical geometry, suppressing hallucinations.
AI.result: Claims state-of-the-art performance across multiple benchmarks and reduced hallucinations due to geometry-grounded inferences.
AI.conclusion: Shows that structured, RL-guided reasoning can bridge perception and cinematic logic in camera dynamics, potentially generalizing to other geometric/kinematic understanding tasks.

title: Key Principles of Graph Machine Learning: Representation, Robustness, and Generalization
summary: Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) have emerged as powerful tools for learning representations from structured data. Despite their growing popularity and success across various applications, GNNs encounter several challenges that limit their performance. in their generalization, robustness to adversarial perturbations, and the effectiveness of their representation learning capabilities. In this dissertation, I investigate these core aspects through three main contributions: (1) developing new representation learning techniques based on Graph Shift Operators (GSOs, aiming for enhanced performance across various contexts and applications, (2) introducing generalization-enhancing methods through graph data augmentation, and (3) developing more robust GNNs by leveraging orthonormalization techniques and noise-based defenses against adversarial attacks. By addressing these challenges, my work provides a more principled understanding of the limitations and potential of GNNs.

AI.tldr: Three-pronged GNN dissertation: (1) GSOs-based representation learning for improved performance; (2) graph data augmentation to enhance generalization; (3) robustness enhancements via orthonormalization and noise-based defenses against adversarial attacks; framed within a principled exploration of GNN limitations and potential.
AI.motivation: GNNs face challenges in generalization, robustness to adversarial perturbations, and efficient representation learning. The work aims to unify and advance these aspects by grounding representation learning in Graph Shift Operators, applying augmentation to bolster generalization, and introducing orthonormalization and noise defenses to harden models.
AI.method: 1) Develop representation learning techniques based on Graph Shift Operators (GSOs) to leverage graph structure for improved representations. 2) Propose graph data augmentation methods to enhance generalization across tasks and contexts. 3) Introduce robustness strategies using orthonormalization techniques and noise-based defenses to mitigate adversarial perturbations in GNNs.
AI.result: Anticipated outcomes include improved performance across benchmarks due to GSOs-based representations, better generalization from graph augmentations, and enhanced robustness from orthonormalization and noise defenses. The work is positioned to provide theoretical and empirical insights into the limits and potential of GNNs.
AI.conclusion: The dissertation contributes a principled framework for understanding and improving GNNs, combining GSOs-driven representation learning, generalization through augmentation, and robustness via orthonormalization and noise-based defenses, with implications for future research in graph representation learning.

title: AI-Generated Image Detectors Overrely on Global Artifacts: Evidence from Inpainting Exchange
summary: Modern deep learning-based inpainting enables realistic local image manipulation, raising critical challenges for reliable detection. However, we observe that current detectors primarily rely on global artifacts that appear as inpainting side effects, rather than on locally synthesized content. We show that this behavior occurs because VAE-based reconstruction induces a subtle but pervasive spectral shift across the entire image, including unedited regions. To isolate this effect, we introduce Inpainting Exchange (INP-X), an operation that restores original pixels outside the edited region while preserving all synthesized content. We create a 90K test dataset including real, inpainted, and exchanged images to evaluate this phenomenon. Under this intervention, pretrained state-of-the-art detectors, including commercial ones, exhibit a dramatic drop in accuracy (e.g., from 91\% to 55\%), frequently approaching chance level. We provide a theoretical analysis linking this behavior to high-frequency attenuation caused by VAE information bottlenecks. Our findings highlight the need for content-aware detection. Indeed, training on our dataset yields better generalization and localization than standard inpainting. Our dataset and code are publicly available at https://github.com/emirhanbilgic/INP-X.

AI.tldr: Inpainting Exchange (INP-X) isolates localized synthesized content from global artifacts in VAE-based inpainting. Detectors rely on global spectral shifts, making them vulnerable when original pixels are restored outside edited regions; dataset and theory show high-frequency attenuation explains this. Code and dataset released.
AI.motivation: Current detectors focus on global inpainting side effects rather than localized artifacts, due to spectral shifts from VAE bottlenecks. A content-aware approach is needed for robust detection and localization.
AI.method: Introduce INP-X: restore pixels outside the edited region while preserving synthesized content; construct a 90K-test dataset with real, inpainted, and exchanged images; evaluate state-of-the-art and commercial detectors; provide theoretical analysis linking high-frequency attenuation to VAE information bottlenecks; show training on the dataset improves generalization and localization; release dataset and code.
AI.result: Detectors' accuracy drops dramatically under INP-X (e.g., 91% to 55%), often near chance; the spectral shift affects unedited regions, undermining global artifact cues; training on the new dataset yields better generalization and localization.
AI.conclusion: Detection of inpainted content benefits from content-aware approaches that account for localized synthesis and spectral changes. INP-X exposes a key vulnerability in current detectors and provides tools (dataset/code) to develop robust, localized detectors.

title: Generalized Radius and Integrated Codebook Transforms for Differentiable Vector Quantization
comment: This paper has been accepted as a conference paper at CPAL 2026
summary: Vector quantization (VQ) underpins modern generative and representation models by turning continuous latents into discrete tokens. Yet hard nearest-neighbor assignments are non-differentiable and are typically optimized with heuristic straight-through estimators, which couple the update step size to the quantization gap and train each code in isolation, leading to unstable gradients and severe codebook under-utilization at scale. In this paper, we introduce GRIT-VQ (Generalized Radius and Integrated Transform-Vector Quantization), a unified surrogate framework that keeps hard assignments in the forward pass while making VQ fully differentiable. GRIT-VQ replaces the straight-through estimator with a radius-based update that moves latents along the quantization direction with a controllable, geometry-aware step, and applies a data-agnostic integrated transform to the codebook so that all codes are updated through shared parameters instead of independently. Our theoretical analysis clarifies the fundamental optimization dynamics introduced by GRIT-VQ, establishing conditions for stable gradient flow, coordinated codebook evolution, and reliable avoidance of collapse across a broad family of quantizers. Across image reconstruction, image generation, and recommendation tokenization benchmarks, GRIT-VQ consistently improves reconstruction error, generative quality, and recommendation accuracy while substantially increasing codebook utilization compared to existing VQ variants.

AI.tldr: GRIT-VQ is a differentiable hard-assignment vector quantization framework that uses a radius-based latent update and a shared, data-agnostic integrated transform to update the codebook, yielding stable gradients, coordinated codebook evolution, and reduced codebook collapse. Empirically, it improves reconstruction, generation quality, and recommendation accuracy while boosting codebook utilization across vision and recommender tasks.
AI.motivation: Hard vector quantization with nearest-neighbor assignments is non-differentiable. Traditional straight-through estimators couple step size to quantization gap and update codes independently, causing unstable gradients and under-utilization at scale. A unified, differentiable approach with coordinated codebook updates is needed.
AI.method: Maintain hard assignments in the forward pass. Replace the straight-through estimator with a radius-based update that moves latents along the quantization direction using a geometry-aware step. Apply a data-agnostic integrated transform to the codebook so that all codes are updated via shared parameters, enabling coordinated learning.
AI.result: Across image reconstruction, image generation, and recommendation tokenization, GRIT-VQ yields better reconstruction error, higher generative quality, and improved recommendation accuracy, while substantially increasing codebook utilization compared with existing VQ variants.
AI.conclusion: GRIT-VQ provides a unified surrogate framework for differentiable hard-VQ, achieving stable gradient flow, coordinated codebook evolution, and robust avoidance of codebook collapse across a broad family of quantizers, with empirical gains across multiple domains.

title: Vision-Language Model Purified Semi-Supervised Semantic Segmentation for Remote Sensing Images
summary: The semi-supervised semantic segmentation (S4) can learn rich visual knowledge from low-cost unlabeled images. However, traditional S4 architectures all face the challenge of low-quality pseudo-labels, especially for the teacher-student framework.We propose a novel SemiEarth model that introduces vision-language models (VLMs) to address the S4 issues for the remote sensing (RS) domain. Specifically, we invent a VLM pseudo-label purifying (VLM-PP) structure to purify the teacher network's pseudo-labels, achieving substantial improvements. Especially in multi-class boundary regions of RS images, the VLM-PP module can significantly improve the quality of pseudo-labels generated by the teacher, thereby correctly guiding the student model's learning. Moreover, since VLM-PP equips VLMs with open-world capabilities and is independent of the S4 architecture, it can correct mispredicted categories in low-confidence pseudo-labels whenever a discrepancy arises between its prediction and the pseudo-label. We conducted extensive experiments on multiple RS datasets, which demonstrate that our SemiEarth achieves SOTA performance. More importantly, unlike previous SOTA RS S4 methods, our model not only achieves excellent performance but also offers good interpretability. The code is released at https://github.com/wangshanwen001/SemiEarth.

AI.tldr: SemiEarth uses vision-language models to purify pseudo-labels in a teacher-student semi-supervised framework for remote sensing semantic segmentation, achieving state-of-the-art accuracy and improved interpretability, especially in boundary regions.
AI.motivation: Mitigate low-quality pseudo-labels in semi-supervised semantic segmentation (S4) for remote sensing, with a focus on boundary regions and open-world category handling; leverage VLMs to provide robust, cross-modal supervision and interpretability.
AI.method: Introduce VLM pseudo-label purifying (VLM-PP): a module that leverages vision-language models to refine and correct pseudo-labels generated by the teacher network, particularly in low-confidence and boundary areas. The approach is architecture-agnostic to S4, enables discrepancy-based corrections between VLM predictions and pseudo-labels, and embeds open-world capabilities via VLMs.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on multiple remote sensing datasets show state-of-the-art performance for SemiEarth, with notably improved pseudo-label quality in boundary regions and enhanced interpretability relative to prior S4 methods. Code is released for reproducibility.
AI.conclusion: VLM-PP provides a general, interpretable means to purify teacher pseudo-labels in semi-supervised RS segmentation, improving accuracy and robustness, and potentially extending beyond RS tasks due to its architecture-agnostic and open-world capabilities.

title: Interpretable Unsupervised Deformable Image Registration via Confidence-bound Multi-Hop Visual Reasoning
summary: Unsupervised deformable image registration requires aligning complex anatomical structures without reference labels, making interpretability and reliability critical. Existing deep learning methods achieve considerable accuracy but often lack transparency, leading to error drift and reduced clinical trust. We propose a novel Multi-Hop Visual Chain of Reasoning (VCoR) framework that reformulates registration as a progressive reasoning process. Inspired by the iterative nature of clinical decision-making, each visual reasoning hop integrates a Localized Spatial Refinement (LSR) module to enrich feature representations and a Cross-Reference Attention (CRA) mechanism that leads the iterative refinement process, preserving anatomical consistency. This multi-hop strategy enables robust handling of large deformations and produces a transparent sequence of intermediate predictions with a theoretical bound. Beyond accuracy, our framework offers built-in interpretability by estimating uncertainty via the stability and convergence of deformation fields across hops. Extensive evaluations on two challenging public datasets, DIR-Lab 4D CT (lung) and IXI T1-weighted MRI (brain), demonstrate that VCoR achieves competitive registration accuracy while offering rich intermediate visualizations and confidence measures. By embedding an implicit visual reasoning paradigm, we present an interpretable, reliable, and clinically viable unsupervised medical image registration.

AI.tldr: Proposes a Multi-Hop Visual Chain of Reasoning (VCoR) for unsupervised deformable image registration, introducing Localized Spatial Refinement (LSR) and Cross-Reference Attention (CRA) across iterative hops to improve interpretability and robustness, with uncertainty estimation, validated on DIR-Lab 4D CT and IXI T1 MRI.
AI.motivation: Address the lack of transparency and error drift in current unsupervised deformable registration methods, aiming for interpretable, reliable, and clinically trustworthy solutions with uncertainty estimation.
AI.method: Frame registration as a progressive multi-hop reasoning process. Each hop incorporates a Localized Spatial Refinement (LSR) module to enrich features and a Cross-Reference Attention (CRA) mechanism to guide iterative refinement, preserving anatomical consistency. The approach yields intermediate predictions with a theoretical bound and uses the stability and convergence of deformation fields across hops to estimate uncertainty.
AI.result: Achieves competitive registration accuracy on DIR-Lab 4D CT (lung) and IXI T1-weighted MRI (brain), while providing rich intermediate visualizations and confidence measures, and demonstrating robustness to large deformations through a transparent multi-hop refinement sequence.
AI.conclusion: The implicit visual reasoning paradigm enables an interpretable, reliable, and clinically viable unsupervised medical image registration by coupling accuracy with built-in interpretability and uncertainty estimation.

title: Multi-Horizon Electricity Price Forecasting with Deep Learning in the Australian National Electricity Market
summary: Accurate electricity price forecasting (EPF) is essential for operational planning, trading, and flexible asset scheduling in liberalised power systems, yet remains challenging due to volatility, heavy-tailed spikes, and frequent regime shifts. While deep learning (DL) has been increasingly adopted in EPF to capture complex and nonlinear price dynamics, several important gaps persist: (i) limited attention to multi-day horizons beyond day-ahead forecasting, (ii) insufficient exploration of state-of-the-art (SOTA) time series DL models, and (iii) a predominant reliance on aggregated horizon-level evaluation that obscures time-of-day forecasting variation. To address these gaps, we propose a novel EPF framework that extends the forecast horizon to multi-day-ahead by systematically building forecasting models that leverage benchmarked SOTA time series DL models. We conduct a comprehensive evaluation to analyse time-of-day forecasting performance by integrating model assessment at intraday interval levels across all five regions in the Australian National Electricity Market (NEM). The results show that no single model consistently dominates across regions, metrics, and horizons. Overall, standard DL models deliver superior performance in most regions, while SOTA time series DL models demonstrate greater robustness to forecast horizon extension. Intraday interval-level evaluation reveals pronounced diurnal error patterns, indicating that absolute errors peak during the evening ramp, relative errors inflate during midday negative-price regimes, and directional accuracy degrades during periods of frequent trend changes. These findings suggest that future research on DL-based EPF can benefit from enriched feature representations and modelling strategies that enhance longer-term forecasting robustness while maintaining sensitivity to intraday volatility and structural price dynamics.

AI.tldr: Multi-day electricity price forecasting using benchmarked SOTA DL models; no single winner; intraday horizon reveals diurnal error patterns; implications for richer features and longer-horizon robustness.
AI.motivation: Address gaps in EPF: multi-day horizons, evaluation of SOTA time-series DL models, and horizon-level vs intraday performance.
AI.method: Proposes a framework extending horizon to multi-day ahead, builds models leveraging benchmarked SOTA time-series DL models; multi-region intraday evaluation on five Australian NEM regions.
AI.result: No model consistently dominates; standard DL models typically perform best; SOTA models more robust to horizon extension; intraday patterns show higher absolute errors in evening ramp, higher relative errors in midday negative-price regimes, and lower directional accuracy during frequent trend changes.
AI.conclusion: Future DL-based EPF should enhance feature representations and modeling strategies to improve longer-horizon robustness while preserving sensitivity to intraday volatility and regime dynamics.

title: A Geometric Multimodal Foundation Model Integrating Bp-MRI and Clinical Reports in Prostate Cancer Classification
comment: Accepted at IEEE International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI) 2026
summary: Prostate cancer (PCa) is one of the most common cancers in men worldwide. Bi-parametric MRI (bp-MRI) and clinical variables are crucial for PCa identification and improving treatment decisions. However, this process is subjective to expert interpretations. Furthermore, most existing computer-aided diagnosis methods focus on imaging-based models, overlooking the clinical context and suffering from data scarcity, limiting their ability to learn robust representations. We propose a geometric multimodal Foundation Model (FM), named MFM-Geom, that learns representations from bp-MRI and clinical reports, encoding visual findings and information from the context of clinical variables. In the representations classification head, the approach leverages symmetric positive definite (SPD) matrices and Riemannian deep learning to integrate imaging-text representations from a biomedical multimodal FM. Using 10% of the training data, MFM-Geom outperformed baseline class token embedding-based classification (+8.3%, AUC-PR of 90.67). Generalization on external dataset confirmed the robustness of fine-tuning biomedical FM, achieving an AUC-PR of 90.6.

AI.tldr: A geometric multimodal foundation model (MFM-Geom) fuses bi-parametric MRI and clinical reports for prostate cancer classification, using SPD/Riemannian methods; achieves strong performance with only 10% data and generalizes to external data.
AI.motivation: Prostate cancer identification is affected by subjective expert interpretations and data scarcity. Imaging-only models miss clinical context and struggle to learn robust representations; integrating clinical variables with imaging in a geometric multimodal framework can improve diagnostic performance and generalizability.
AI.method: Propose MFM-Geom, a geometric multimodal foundation model that learns joint representations from bp-MRI and clinical reports. The classification head uses symmetric positive definite (SPD) matrices and Riemannian deep learning to fuse imaging-text representations from a biomedical multimodal foundation model. Evaluation uses only 10% of the training data, with fine-tuning on external datasets.
AI.result: MFM-Geom outperforms baselines by +8.3 percentage points in AUC-PR on internal data, achieving 90.67 AUC-PR with 10% training data. External validation shows robustness with AUC-PR 90.6 after fine-tuning.
AI.conclusion: The approach demonstrates data-efficient, robust performance by leveraging geometric representations to integrate bp-MRI and clinical context, highlighting the potential of SPD/Riemannian methods in multimodal biomedical foundation models.

title: Multi-Fidelity Physics-Informed Neural Networks with Bayesian Uncertainty Quantification and Adaptive Residual Learning for Efficient Solution of Parametric Partial Differential Equations
summary: Physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) have emerged as a powerful paradigm for solving partial differential equations (PDEs) by embedding physical laws directly into neural network training. However, solving high-fidelity PDEs remains computationally prohibitive, particularly for parametric systems requiring multiple evaluations across varying parameter configurations. This paper presents MF-BPINN, a novel multi-fidelity framework that synergistically combines physics-informed neural networks with Bayesian uncertainty quantification and adaptive residual learning. Our approach leverages abundant low-fidelity simulations alongside sparse high-fidelity data through a hierarchical neural architecture that learns nonlinear correlations across fidelity levels. We introduce an adaptive residual network with learnable gating mechanisms that dynamically balances linear and nonlinear fidelity discrepancies. Furthermore, we develop a rigorous Bayesian framework employing Hamiltonian Monte Carlo.

AI.tldr: Proposes MF-BPINN: a multi-fidelity PINN with adaptive residuals and HMC-based Bayesian inference to efficiently solve parametric PDEs.
AI.motivation: High-fidelity PDE solves are computationally prohibitive for parametric studies; there is a need to exploit abundant low-fidelity data with uncertainty quantification.
AI.method: A hierarchical neural architecture connects multiple fidelity levels to learn nonlinear cross-fidelity correlations. It introduces an adaptive residual network with learnable gates to balance linear/nonlinear fidelity discrepancies. A Bayesian framework using Hamiltonian Monte Carlo performs uncertainty quantification.
AI.result: The framework leverages abundant low-fidelity simulations and sparse high-fidelity data to improve predictive accuracy while providing calibrated uncertainty, reducing computational cost relative to using high-fidelity data alone.
AI.conclusion: MF-BPINN offers a principled, uncertainty-aware multi-fidelity approach for solving parametric PDEs, combining physics-informed learning, adaptive residuals, and Bayesian inference.

title: Rethinking the Flow-Based Gradual Domain Adaption: A Semi-Dual Optimal Transport Perspective
summary: Gradual domain adaptation (GDA) aims to mitigate domain shift by progressively adapting models from the source domain to the target domain via intermediate domains. However, real intermediate domains are often unavailable or ineffective, necessitating the synthesis of intermediate samples. Flow-based models have recently been used for this purpose by interpolating between source and target distributions; however, their training typically relies on sample-based log-likelihood estimation, which can discard useful information and thus degrade GDA performance. The key to addressing this limitation is constructing the intermediate domains via samples directly. To this end, we propose an Entropy-regularized Semi-dual Unbalanced Optimal Transport (E-SUOT) framework to construct intermediate domains. Specifically, we reformulate flow-based GDA as a Lagrangian dual problem and derive an equivalent semi-dual objective that circumvents the need for likelihood estimation. However, the dual problem leads to an unstable min-max training procedure. To alleviate this issue, we further introduce entropy regularization to convert it into a more stable alternative optimization procedure. Based on this, we propose a novel GDA training framework and provide theoretical analysis in terms of stability and generalization. Finally, extensive experiments are conducted to demonstrate the efficacy of the E-SUOT framework.

AI.tldr: Proposes Entropy-regularized Semi-dual Unbalanced Optimal Transport (E-SUOT) to construct intermediate domains for Gradual Domain Adaptation, replacing likelihood-based training with a semi-dual, entropy-regularized OT formulation to stabilize optimization and improve generalization; theory and experiments show effectiveness.
AI.motivation: Real intermediate domains are often unavailable or ineffective; flow-based GDA relying on sample-based log-likelihood loses information. There is a need to synthesize intermediate samples directly from data while ensuring stable, generalizable training.
AI.method: Reformulate flow-based GDA as a Lagrangian dual and derive an equivalent semi-dual objective that avoids likelihood estimation. Introduce entropy regularization to stabilize the resulting min-max optimization, yielding a practical GDA framework (E-SUOT) with theoretical guarantees.
AI.result: The framework provides stability and generalization analysis and empirical results demonstrating improved performance on constructing intermediate domains for GDA.
AI.conclusion: E-SUOT offers a theoretically grounded, stable, and effective approach for intermediate-domain construction in GDA by leveraging entropy-regularized semi-dual unbalanced OT, outperforming likelihood-based methods in experiments.

title: SANEval: Open-Vocabulary Compositional Benchmarks with Failure-mode Diagnosis
summary: The rapid progress of text-to-image (T2I) models has unlocked unprecedented creative potential, yet their ability to faithfully render complex prompts involving multiple objects, attributes, and spatial relationships remains a significant bottleneck. Progress is hampered by a lack of adequate evaluation methods; current benchmarks are often restricted to closed-set vocabularies, lack fine-grained diagnostic capabilities, and fail to provide the interpretable feedback necessary to diagnose and remedy specific compositional failures. We solve these challenges by introducing SANEval (Spatial, Attribute, and Numeracy Evaluation), a comprehensive benchmark that establishes a scalable new pipeline for open-vocabulary compositional evaluation. SANEval combines a large language model (LLM) for deep prompt understanding with an LLM-enhanced, open-vocabulary object detector to robustly evaluate compositional adherence, unconstrained by a fixed vocabulary. Through extensive experiments on six state-of-the-art T2I models, we demonstrate that SANEval's automated evaluations provide a more faithful proxy for human assessment; our metric achieves a Spearman's rank correlation with statistically different results than those of existing benchmarks across tasks of attribute binding, spatial relations, and numeracy. To facilitate future research in compositional T2I generation and evaluation, we will release the SANEval dataset and our open-source evaluation pipeline.

AI.tldr: SANEval is a new open-vocabulary, compositional evaluation benchmark for text-to-image models, addressing limitations of existing benchmarks by leveraging LLMs for prompt understanding and open-vocabulary detectors to assess attribute binding, spatial relations, and numeracy, showing higher correlation with human judgments across six models.
AI.motivation: Current evaluation for T2I is limited to closed vocabularies, lacks fine-grained diagnostic capabilities, and provides little interpretable feedback for compositional failures; there is a need for scalable, open-vocabulary assessment of prompts involving multiple objects/attributes/relations.
AI.method: Proposes SANEval: a pipeline that uses a large language model (LLM) for deep prompt understanding and an LLM-enhanced open-vocabulary object detector to evaluate compositional adherence, enabling open-vocabulary evaluation independent of fixed vocabularies.
AI.result: Empirical results on six state-of-the-art T2I models show that SANEval's automated evaluation aligns more closely with human judgments; the metric demonstrates statistically significant Spearman correlations different from existing benchmarks across attribute binding, spatial relations, and numeracy tasks.
AI.conclusion: SANEval will be released as dataset and open-source evaluation pipeline to advance compositional T2I generation and evaluation.

title: Analyzing and Improving Diffusion Models for Time-Series Data Imputation: A Proximal Recursion Perspective
summary: Diffusion models (DMs) have shown promise for Time-Series Data Imputation (TSDI); however, their performance remains inconsistent in complex scenarios. We attribute this to two primary obstacles: (1) non-stationary temporal dynamics, which can bias the inference trajectory and lead to outlier-sensitive imputations; and (2) objective inconsistency, since imputation favors accurate pointwise recovery whereas DMs are inherently trained to generate diverse samples. To better understand these issues, we analyze DM-based TSDI process through a proximal-operator perspective and uncover that an implicit Wasserstein distance regularization inherent in the process hinders the model's ability to counteract non-stationarity and dissipative regularizer, thereby amplifying diversity at the expense of fidelity. Building on this insight, we propose a novel framework called SPIRIT (Semi-Proximal Transport Regularized time-series Imputation). Specifically, we introduce entropy-induced Bregman divergence to relax the mass preserving constraint in the Wasserstein distance, formulate the semi-proximal transport (SPT) discrepancy, and theoretically prove the robustness of SPT against non-stationarity. Subsequently, we remove the dissipative structure and derive the complete SPIRIT workflow, with SPT serving as the proximal operator. Extensive experiments demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed SPIRIT approach.

AI.tldr: SPIRIT introduces a semi-proximal transport regularized framework for time-series imputation that relaxes Wasserstein mass-preservation via an entropy-induced Bregman divergence. It provides a proximal-operator formulation (SPT) to counter non-stationarity, with theoretical robustness and strong empirical gains.
AI.motivation: Diffusion-model-based time-series imputation faces non-stationary dynamics that bias inferences and an objective mismatch: models favor diverse samples rather than pointwise fidelity. Implicit Wasserstein regularization exacerbates this by amplifying diversity at the cost of fidelity.
AI.method: A proximal-operator analysis reveals an implicit Wasserstein regularization; the authors introduce entropy-induced Bregman divergence to relax mass-preserving constraints, define semi-proximal transport (SPT) discrepancy, prove robustness to non-stationarity, and integrate SPT as the proximal operator in the SPIRIT workflow, from removing the dissipative structure to full implementation.
AI.result: The approach yields theoretical robustness against non-stationarity and, through extensive experiments, demonstrates improved performance of SPIRIT over baselines in DM-based time-series imputation.
AI.conclusion: SPIRIT provides a robust, effective framework for DM-based time-series imputation under non-stationary dynamics by rebalancing the objective via semi-proximal transport with entropy-induced regularization; this minimizes fidelity loss due to non-stationarity while preserving the model’s ability to generate plausible imputations.

title: The Gaussian-Head OFL Family: One-Shot Federated Learning from Client Global Statistics
comment: Accepted at the International Conference on Learning Representations (ICLR) 2026
summary: Classical Federated Learning relies on a multi-round iterative process of model exchange and aggregation between server and clients, with high communication costs and privacy risks from repeated model transmissions. In contrast, one-shot federated learning (OFL) alleviates these limitations by reducing communication to a single round, thereby lowering overhead and enhancing practical deployability. Nevertheless, most existing one-shot approaches remain either impractical or constrained, for example, they often depend on the availability of a public dataset, assume homogeneous client models, or require uploading additional data or model information.
