To overcome these issues, we introduce the Gaussian-Head OFL (GH-OFL) family, a suite of one-shot federated methods that assume class-conditional Gaussianity of pretrained embeddings. Clients transmit only sufficient statistics (per-class counts and first/second-order moments) and the server builds heads via three components: (i) Closed-form Gaussian heads (NB/LDA/QDA) computed directly from the received statistics; (ii) FisherMix, a linear head with cosine margin trained on synthetic samples drawn in an estimated Fisher subspace; and (iii) Proto-Hyper, a lightweight low-rank residual head that refines Gaussian logits via knowledge distillation on those synthetic samples. In our experiments, GH-OFL methods deliver state-of-the-art robustness and accuracy under strong non-IID skew while remaining strictly data-free.

AI.tldr: A data-free, one-shot federated learning framework (GH-OFL) that assumes class-conditional Gaussianity of embeddings and uses per-class statistics to construct multiple head types, achieving strong non-IID robustness with reduced communication.
AI.motivation: Reduce communication costs and privacy risks of traditional FL while overcoming limitations of existing OFL methods (need for public data, homogeneous models, or extra data transmissions) by leveraging statistics of embedded features.
AI.method: Clients transmit per-class counts and first/second-order moments of pretrained embeddings. The server builds heads via: (i) closed-form Gaussian heads (NB/LDA/QDA) from statistics; (ii) FisherMix, a cosine-margin linear head trained on synthetic samples in an estimated Fisher subspace; (iii) Proto-Hyper, a low-rank residual head refined through knowledge distillation on synthetic samples.
AI.result: GH-OFL methods achieve state-of-the-art robustness and accuracy under strong non-IID skew while remaining strictly data-free, outperforming baselines in challenging FL settings.
AI.conclusion: A practical, data-free one-shot FL framework that lowers communication, preserves privacy, and delivers competitive performance by exploiting Gaussianity in embeddings and combining closed-form, synthetic-sample-based, and distillation-based heads.

title: Subspace Clustering on Incomplete Data with Self-Supervised Contrastive Learning
summary: Subspace clustering aims to group data points that lie in a union of low-dimensional subspaces and finds wide application in computer vision, hyperspectral imaging, and recommendation systems. However, most existing methods assume fully observed data, limiting their effectiveness in real-world scenarios with missing entries. In this paper, we propose a contrastive self-supervised framework, Contrastive Subspace Clustering (CSC), designed for clustering incomplete data. CSC generates masked views of partially observed inputs and trains a deep neural network using a SimCLR-style contrastive loss to learn invariant embeddings. These embeddings are then clustered using sparse subspace clustering. Experiments on six benchmark datasets show that CSC consistently outperforms both classical and deep learning baselines, demonstrating strong robustness to missing data and scalability to large datasets.

AI.tldr: Idea: Contrastive Subspace Clustering (CSC) handles incomplete data by learning invariant embeddings with masked views through a SimCLR-like objective, then applying sparse subspace clustering. It outperforms baselines on six datasets and scales well.
AI.motivation: Real-world data are often missing entries, which hinders standard subspace clustering methods that assume complete observations.
AI.method: Generate masked views of partially observed inputs, train a deep network with a SimCLR-style contrastive loss to learn invariant embeddings, then cluster embeddings using sparse subspace clustering.
AI.result: CSC consistently outperforms classical and deep baselines on six benchmark datasets, showing robustness to missing data and scalability to large datasets.
AI.conclusion: The proposed CSC framework effectively handles incomplete data in subspace clustering, delivering strong performance and scalability.

title: Unraveling the Hidden Dynamical Structure in Recurrent Neural Policies
summary: Recurrent neural policies are widely used in partially observable control and meta-RL tasks. Their abilities to maintain internal memory and adapt quickly to unseen scenarios have offered them unparalleled performance when compared to non-recurrent counterparts. However, until today, the underlying mechanisms for their superior generalization and robustness performance remain poorly understood. In this study, by analyzing the hidden state domain of recurrent policies learned over a diverse set of training methods, model architectures, and tasks, we find that stable cyclic structures consistently emerge during interaction with the environment. Such cyclic structures share a remarkable similarity with \textit{limit cycles} in dynamical system analysis, if we consider the policy and the environment as a joint hybrid dynamical system. Moreover, we uncover that the geometry of such limit cycles also has a structured correspondence with the policies' behaviors. These findings offer new perspectives to explain many nice properties of recurrent policies: the emergence of limit cycles stabilizes both the policies' internal memory and the task-relevant environmental states, while suppressing nuisance variability arising from environmental uncertainty; the geometry of limit cycles also encodes relational structures of behaviors, facilitating easier skill adaptation when facing non-stationary environments.

AI.tldr: RNN policies develop stable limit-cycle dynamics that stabilize internal memory and task-relevant environmental interactions, explaining generalization and robustness.
AI.motivation: To uncover the mechanisms behind the superior generalization and robustness of recurrent policies by examining their hidden-state dynamics across diverse training regimes, architectures, and tasks.
AI.method: Empirical analysis of the hidden-state trajectories of recurrent policies across varied methods/architectures/tasks; identify stable cyclic structures; interpret them as limit cycles in a joint policy–environment hybrid dynamical system; relate cycle geometry to behavior.
AI.result: Stable limit-cycle structures consistently emerge; their geometry correlates with policy behaviors; cycles stabilize memory and environmental states, suppress nuisance variability due to environmental uncertainty; geometry encodes relational structure of behaviors enabling easier adaptation in non-stationary environments.
AI.conclusion: Viewing recurrent policy dynamics through limit-cycle geometry provides a unified explanation for robustness and adaptability, and suggests a new lens for designing/diagnosing policies in non-stationary and partially observable settings.

title: PLACID: Identity-Preserving Multi-Object Compositing via Video Diffusion with Synthetic Trajectories
summary: Recent advances in generative AI have dramatically improved photorealistic image synthesis, yet they fall short for studio-level multi-object compositing. This task demands simultaneous (i) near-perfect preservation of each item's identity, (ii) precise background and color fidelity, (iii) layout and design elements control, and (iv) complete, appealing displays showcasing all objects. However, current state-of-the-art models often alter object details, omit or duplicate objects, and produce layouts with incorrect relative sizing or inconsistent item presentations. To bridge this gap, we introduce PLACID, a framework that transforms a collection of object images into an appealing multi-object composite. Our approach makes two main contributions. First, we leverage a pretrained image-to-video (I2V) diffusion model with text control to preserve objects consistency, identities, and background details by exploiting temporal priors from videos. Second, we propose a novel data curation strategy that generates synthetic sequences where randomly placed objects smoothly move to their target positions. This synthetic data aligns with the video model's temporal priors during training. At inference, objects initialized at random positions consistently converge into coherent layouts guided by text, with the final frame serving as the composite image. Extensive quantitative evaluations and user studies demonstrate that PLACID surpasses state-of-the-art methods in multi-object compositing, achieving superior identity, background, and color preservation, with less omitted objects and visually appealing results.

AI.tldr: PLACID uses a pretrained image-to-video diffusion model with text control and a synthetic-data curriculum to produce coherent multi-object composites from separate object images, outperforming SOTA in identity, background fidelity, and layout accuracy.
AI.motivation: To overcome artifacts where single-image generative models distort object identity, omit objects, or mislayout when creating multi-object composites; leverage temporal priors from video models to stabilize objects and backgrounds.
AI.method: 1) Fine use of a pretrained image-to-video diffusion model with text-control for per-object identity and background preservation via temporal priors; 2) A data-curation strategy generating synthetic sequences where objects move smoothly from random placements to their target positions, aligning training with video priors; inference: iterative convergence of objects to a coherent layout; final frame is the composite.
AI.result: Quantitative metrics and user studies indicate PLACID achieves superior identity, background and color preservation, reduces object omissions, and yields more visually appealing composites compared to state-of-the-art.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates effective integration of video priors and synthetic motion data to enable controllable, high-quality multi-object compositing from independent object images.

title: SimpleGPT: Improving GPT via A Simple Normalization Strategy
comment: We propose SimpleGPT, a simple yet effective GPT model, and provide theoretical insights into its mathematical foundations. We validate our theoretical findings through extensive experiments on large GPT models at parameter scales 1B, 1.4B, 7B and 8B
summary: In this work, we revisit Transformer optimization through the lens of second-order geometry and establish a direct connection between architectural design, activation scale, the Hessian matrix, and the maximum tolerable learning rate. We introduce a simple normalization strategy, termed SimpleNorm, which stabilizes intermediate activation scales by construction. Then, by analyzing the Hessian of the loss with respect to network activations, we theoretically show that SimpleNorm significantly reduces the spectral norm of the Hessian, thereby permitting larger stable learning rates. We validate our theoretical findings through extensive experiments on large GPT models at parameter scales 1B, 1.4B, 7B and 8B. Empirically, SimpleGPT, our SimpleNorm-based network, tolerates learning rates 3$\times$-10$\times$ larger than standard convention, consistently demonstrates strong optimization stability, and achieves substantially better performance than well-established baselines. Specifically, when training 7B-scale models for 60K steps, SimpleGPT achieves a training loss that is 0.08 lower than that of LLaMA2 with QKNorm, reducing the loss from 2.290 to 2.208. Our source code will be released at https://github.com/Ocram7/SimpleGPT.

AI.tldr: SimpleNorm stabilizes Transformer training by normalizing activation scales, reduces the Hessian's spectral norm, enabling much larger learning rates, validated on GPT-scale models from 1B to 8B parameters with improved training loss versus baselines.
AI.motivation: Optimization in deep Transformers is sensitive to activation scales and second-order geometry; a normalization strategy that stabilizes activations could influence the Hessian and permit larger learning rates.
AI.method: Introduce SimpleNorm normalization; perform theoretical analysis of the Hessian with respect to activations to show spectral-norm reduction; validate empirically on large GPT-scale models (1B, 1.4B, 7B, 8B) comparing against baselines (e.g., LLaMA2 with QKNorm); provide code release.
AI.result: SimpleGPT tolerates 3x–10x larger learning rates than standard practice; exhibits strong optimization stability and improved performance; in 7B-scale experiments over 60k steps, training loss improved from 2.290 (LLaMA2 with QKNorm) to 2.208; consistent gains across tested scales.
AI.conclusion: Normalizing activation scales via SimpleNorm reduces Hessian spectral norm, enabling larger stable learning rates and better optimization performance in Transformer training; the approach shows promise for large-scale language models and is accompanied by code release.

title: TokenTrim: Inference-Time Token Pruning for Autoregressive Long Video Generation
summary: Auto-regressive video generation enables long video synthesis by iteratively conditioning each new batch of frames on previously generated content. However, recent work has shown that such pipelines suffer from severe temporal drift, where errors accumulate and amplify over long horizons. We hypothesize that this drift does not primarily stem from insufficient model capacity, but rather from inference-time error propagation. Specifically, we contend that drift arises from the uncontrolled reuse of corrupted latent conditioning tokens during auto-regressive inference. To correct this accumulation of errors, we propose a simple, inference-time method that mitigates temporal drift by identifying and removing unstable latent tokens before they are reused for conditioning. For this purpose, we define unstable tokens as latent tokens whose representations deviate significantly from those of the previously generated batch, indicating potential corruption or semantic drift. By explicitly removing corrupted latent tokens from the auto-regressive context, rather than modifying entire spatial regions or model parameters, our method prevents unreliable latent information from influencing future generation steps. As a result, it significantly improves long-horizon temporal consistency without modifying the model architecture, training procedure, or leaving latent space.

AI.tldr: Inference-time pruning of unstable latent conditioning tokens in autoregressive video generation to mitigate temporal drift, significantly improving long-horizon consistency without architectural or training changes.
AI.motivation: Auto-regressive video generation suffers from temporal drift due to error propagation when previously generated latent conditioning tokens are corrupted or drift semantically. The authors argue drift stems from inference-time propagation of errors rather than limited model capacity.
AI.method: During inference, identify unstable latent tokens whose representations diverge markedly from the immediately preceding batch. Remove these corrupted tokens from the conditioning context before they influence future frames, thereby preventing unreliable latent information from propagating. This is done without modifying the model architecture, training procedure, or latent space structure.
AI.result: The approach significantly improves long-horizon temporal consistency, achieving this solely through a simple inference-time token filtering step and without changes to the model, training, or latent space.
AI.conclusion: Inference-time latent token filtering is an effective, minimalistic strategy to combat drift in AR video generation, offering robust temporal consistency improvements without retraining or architectural changes.

title: Learning from Anonymized and Incomplete Tabular Data
summary: User-driven privacy allows individuals to control whether and at what granularity their data is shared, leading to datasets that mix original, generalized, and missing values within the same records and attributes. While such representations are intuitive for privacy, they pose challenges for machine learning, which typically treats non-original values as new categories or as missing, thereby discarding generalization semantics. For learning from such tabular data, we propose novel data transformation strategies that account for heterogeneous anonymization and evaluate them alongside standard imputation and LLM-based approaches. We employ multiple datasets, privacy configurations, and deployment scenarios, demonstrating that our method reliably regains utility. Our results show that generalized values are preferable to pure suppression, that the best data preparation strategy depends on the scenario, and that consistent data representations are crucial for maintaining downstream utility. Overall, our findings highlight that effective learning is tied to the appropriate handling of anonymized values.

AI.tldr: Introduces data transformation strategies for learning from privacy-preserving tabular data with heterogeneous anonymization; shows generalized (non-original) values outperform suppression and that utility depends on scenario; emphasizes consistent representations for downstream ML.
AI.motivation: Privacy-driven anonymization yields mixed original, generalized, and missing values. Conventional ML treats non-original values as new categories or missing, discarding generalization semantics; there is a need to preserve anonymization semantics to maintain utility.
AI.method: Propose novel data transformation strategies that encode anonymized values and preserve their semantics; evaluate them against standard imputation and LLM-based approaches across multiple datasets, privacy configurations, and deployment scenarios.
AI.result: The proposed method reliably regains utility; generalized values are preferable to suppression; the best data preparation strategy depends on the scenario; consistent data representations are crucial for downstream utility.
AI.conclusion: Effective learning from anonymized tabular data requires proper handling of anonymized values; the proposed strategies enable better utility by preserving anonymization semantics.

title: MiTA Attention: Efficient Fast-Weight Scaling via a Mixture of Top-$k$ Activations
summary: The attention operator in Transformers can be viewed as a two-layer fast-weight MLP, whose weights are dynamically instantiated from input tokens and whose width equals sequence length $N$. As the context extends, the expressive capacity of such an $N$-width MLP increases, but scaling its fast weights becomes prohibitively expensive for extremely long sequences. Recently, this fast-weight scaling perspective has motivated the Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) attention, which partitions the sequence into fast-weight experts and sparsely routes the tokens to them. In this paper, we elevate this perspective to a unifying framework for a wide range of efficient attention methods by interpreting them as scaling fast weights through routing and/or compression. Then we propose a compress-and-route strategy, which compresses the $N$-width MLP into a narrower one using a small set of landmark queries and constructs deformable experts by gathering top-$k$ activated key-value pairs for each landmark query. We call this strategy a Mixture of Top-$k$ Activations (MiTA), and refer to the resulting efficient mechanism as MiTA attention. Preliminary experiments on vision tasks demonstrate the promise of our MiTA attention and motivate further investigation on its optimization and broader applications in more challenging settings.

AI.tldr: MiTA attention proposes a compress-and-route approach that unifies efficient attention methods under a fast-weight perspective; it introduces landmark-query based compression and top-k activations to form deformable experts, showing promising preliminary results on vision tasks.
AI.motivation: Transformer attention scales poorly with sequence length due to the N-width fast-weight MLP; existing fast-weight scaling inspired MoE attention; need a general framework and practical, scalable attention for very long sequences; aim to explore compress-and-route as a unified method.
AI.method: Interpret attention as a two-layer fast-weight MLP with weights instantiated per input. Introduce compress-and-route: compress the high-width MLP into a narrower one using a small set of landmark queries; construct deformable experts by gathering top-k activated key-value pairs for each landmark; instantiate Mixture-of-Top-k Activations (MiTA) attention.
AI.result: Preliminary vision experiments indicate promise; results motivate further optimization and broader applications in more challenging settings.
AI.conclusion: MiTA provides a promising, generalizable direction for efficient attention by combining compression and routing via landmark queries and top-k activations; potential to unify diverse efficient-attention approaches, with further research needed on optimization and broader deployment.

title: Lotus: Efficient LLM Training by Randomized Low-Rank Gradient Projection with Adaptive Subspace Switching
summary: Training efficiency in large-scale models is typically assessed through memory consumption, training time, and model performance. Current methods often exhibit trade-offs among these metrics, as optimizing one generally degrades at least one of the others. Addressing this trade-off remains a central challenge in algorithm design. While GaLore enables memory-efficient training by updating gradients in a low-rank subspace, it incurs a comparable extra training time cost due to the Singular Value Decomposition(SVD) process on gradients. In this paper, we propose Lotus, a method that resolves this trade-off by simply modifying the projection process. We propose a criterion that quantifies the displacement of the unit gradient to enable efficient transitions between low-rank gradient subspaces. Experimental results indicate that Lotus is the most efficient method, achieving a 30% reduction in training time and a 40% decrease in memory consumption for gradient and optimizer states. Additionally, it outperforms the baseline method in both pre-training and fine-tuning tasks.

AI.tldr: Lotus proposes a projection-based improvement to gradient subspace transitions to beat the memory-time-performance trade-off in large-scale training, avoiding heavy SVD on gradients.
AI.motivation: The standard trade-off in training efficiency: memory and time vs. model performance. GaLore reduces memory via low-rank gradient updates but incurs SVD cost; there is a need for a lightweight mechanism to switch between gradient subspaces.
AI.method: Introduce a gradient displacement criterion that quantifies unit-gradient movement to enable efficient transitions between low-rank gradient subspaces. Modify the projection process (Lotus) to realize these transitions with reduced overhead compared to SVD-based methods.
AI.result: Empirical results claim Lotus achieves about 30% faster training and 40% less memory usage for gradient and optimizer states, outperforming baselines in both pre-training and fine-tuning tasks.
AI.conclusion: Lotus resolves the memory-time-performance trade-off in gradient-based training by an efficient projection-based subspace transition guided by gradient displacement, enabling more efficient large-scale training without sacrificing performance.

title: TimeBlind: A Spatio-Temporal Compositionality Benchmark for Video LLMs
summary: Fine-grained spatio-temporal understanding is essential for video reasoning and embodied AI. Yet, while Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) master static semantics, their grasp of temporal dynamics remains brittle. We present TimeBlind, a diagnostic benchmark for compositional spatio-temporal understanding. Inspired by cognitive science, TimeBlind categorizes fine-grained temporal understanding into three levels: recognizing atomic events, characterizing event properties, and reasoning about event interdependencies. Unlike benchmarks that conflate recognition with temporal reasoning, TimeBlind leverages a minimal-pairs paradigm: video pairs share identical static visual content but differ solely in temporal structure, utilizing complementary questions to neutralize language priors. Evaluating over 20 state-of-the-art MLLMs (e.g., GPT-5, Gemini 3 Pro) on 600 curated instances (2400 video-question pairs), reveals that the Instance Accuracy (correctly distinguishing both videos in a pair) of the best performing MLLM is only 48.2%, far below the human performance (98.2%). These results demonstrate that even frontier models rely heavily on static visual shortcuts rather than genuine temporal logic, positioning TimeBlind as a vital diagnostic tool for next-generation video understanding. Dataset and code are available at https://baiqi-li.github.io/timeblind_project/ .

AI.tldr: A diagnostic benchmark, TimeBlind, isolates temporal understanding in multimodal LLMs using minimal-pairs, revealing strong reliance on static cues; humans outperform by wide margin; dataset/code released.
AI.motivation: Address gap in evaluating fine-grained spatio-temporal understanding in MLLMs and separate recognition from temporal reasoning; inform development of models that truly reason about events over time.
AI.method: Three-level temporal understanding taxonomy; minimal-pairs video pairs with identical static content but different temporal structure; complementary questions to cancel language priors; evaluation across 20 SOTA MLLMs on 600 instances (2400 QA pairs).
AI.result: Best MLLM achieves 48.2% instance accuracy vs 98.2% human; broad underperformance indicates reliance on static visual shortcuts rather than temporal logic.
AI.conclusion: TimeBlind serves as a critical diagnostic tool for advancing video understanding in AI; dataset/code available; highlights need for models capable of robust temporal reasoning.

title: Mechanistic Interpretability of Brain-to-Speech Models Across Speech Modes
summary: Brain-to-speech decoding models demonstrate robust performance in vocalized, mimed, and imagined speech; yet, the fundamental mechanisms via which these models capture and transmit information across different speech modalities are less explored. In this work, we use mechanistic interpretability to causally investigate the internal representations of a neural speech decoder. We perform cross-mode activation patching of internal activations across speech modes, and use tri-modal interpolation to examine whether speech representations vary discretely or continuously. We use coarse-to-fine causal tracing and causal scrubbing to find localized causal structure, allowing us to find internal subspaces that are sufficient for cross-mode transfer. In order to determine how finely distributed these effects are within layers, we perform neuron-level activation patching. We discover that small but not distributed subsets of neurons, rather than isolated units, affect the cross-mode transfer. Our results show that speech modes lie on a shared continuous causal manifold, and cross-mode transfer is mediated by compact, layer-specific subspaces rather than diffuse activity. Together, our findings give a causal explanation for how speech modality information is organized and used in brain-to-speech decoding models, revealing hierarchical and direction-dependent representational structure across speech modes.

AI.tldr: Uses mechanistic interpretability to causally map cross-mode speech representations, revealing a shared continuous causal manifold and compact, layer-specific subspaces that mediate cross-mode transfer.
AI.motivation: To understand how brain-to-speech decoding models encode and transfer information across vocalized, mimed, and imagined speech by identifying the causal structure of internal representations.
AI.method: Apply cross-mode activation patching across speech modes, tri-modal interpolation to test discretization vs. continuity, coarse-to-fine causal tracing and causal scrubbing to locate causal structure, and neuron-level activation patching to assess granularity.
AI.result: Found that small, localized neuron subsets suffice for cross-mode transfer; representations across modes lie on a shared continuous causal manifold; transfer is mediated by compact, layer-specific subspaces rather than diffuse activity.
AI.conclusion: Provides a causal explanation of how speech modality information is organized in brain-to-speech decoders, revealing hierarchical, direction-dependent representational structure and suggesting targeted subspaces as mediators of cross-mode transfer.

title: LogicGaze: Benchmarking Causal Consistency in Visual Narratives via Counterfactual Verification
summary: While sequential reasoning enhances the capability of Vision-Language Models (VLMs) to execute complex multimodal tasks, their reliability in grounding these reasoning chains within actual visual evidence remains insufficiently explored. We introduce LogicGaze, a novel benchmark framework designed to rigorously interrogate whether VLMs can validate sequential causal chains against visual inputs, specifically targeting the pervasive issue of hallucination. Curated from 40,000 video segments from ShareGPT4Video and a subset of Flickr30k imagery, LogicGaze integrates causal sequences with visually contradictory yet linguistically plausible perturbations, compelling models to verify the authenticity of each reasoning step. Our tripartite evaluation protocol - Causal Validation, Grounded Narrative Synthesis, and Perturbation Rejection - exposes significant vulnerabilities in state-of-the-art VLMs such as Qwen2.5-VL-72B. LogicGaze advocates for robust, trustworthy multimodal reasoning, with all resources publicly available in an anonymized repository.

AI.tldr: LogicGaze is a benchmark to test if Vision-Language Models can validate sequential causal reasoning against visual input, addressing hallucination; built from 40k video segments (ShareGPT4Video) and Flickr30k; features causal sequences with perturbations; triad evaluation reveals vulnerabilities in SOTA models like Qwen2.5-VL-72B; resources are released in an anonymized repository.
AI.motivation: Current VLMs struggle to ground multi-step reasoning in actual visuals, leading to hallucinations; there is a need for a rigorous benchmark to probe stepwise verification against real evidence.
AI.method: Curate LogicGaze using 40k video segments from ShareGPT4Video and a Flickr30k subset; integrate causal sequences with visually contradictory yet linguistically plausible perturbations; apply a three-part evaluation protocol: Causal Validation, Grounded Narrative Synthesis, Perturbation Rejection; release anonymized resources.
AI.result: The benchmark exposes significant vulnerabilities in state-of-the-art VLMs such as Qwen2.5-VL-72B, showing limited ability to ground or verify sequential reasoning.
AI.conclusion: LogicGaze provides a robust framework for trustworthy multimodal reasoning and invites further research; all resources are publicly available in an anonymized repository.

title: Sample Efficient Active Algorithms for Offline Reinforcement Learning
summary: Offline reinforcement learning (RL) enables policy learning from static data but often suffers from poor coverage of the state-action space and distributional shift problems. This problem can be addressed by allowing limited online interactions to selectively refine uncertain regions of the learned value function, which is referred to as Active Reinforcement Learning (ActiveRL). While there has been good empirical success, no theoretical analysis is available in the literature. We fill this gap by developing a rigorous sample-complexity analysis of ActiveRL through the lens of Gaussian Process (GP) uncertainty modeling. In this respect, we propose an algorithm and using GP concentration inequalities and information-gain bounds, we derive high-probability guarantees showing that an $ε$-optimal policy can be learned with ${\mathcal{O}}(1/ε^2)$ active transitions, improving upon the $Ω(1/ε^2(1-γ)^4)$ rate of purely offline methods. Our results reveal that ActiveRL achieves near-optimal information efficiency, that is, guided uncertainty reduction leads to accelerated value-function convergence with minimal online data. Our analysis builds on GP concentration inequalities and information-gain bounds, bridging Bayesian nonparametric regression and reinforcement learning theories. We conduct several experiments to validate the algorithm and theoretical findings.

AI.tldr: GP-based sample-complexity analysis of ActiveRL showing ε-optimal policy with O(1/ε^2) active transitions; improves offline rate; introduces algorithm and experiments.
AI.motivation: Address offline RL's poor state-action coverage and distributional shift; enable limited online interactions to refine uncertain regions; provide theoretical guarantees for ActiveRL.
AI.method: Model uncertainty with Gaussian Processes; develop an ActiveRL algorithm; derive high-probability guarantees using GP concentration inequalities and information-gain bounds.
AI.result: Theoretically guarantees ε-optimal policy with O(1/ε^2) active transitions, improving over Ω(1/ε^2(1-γ)^4) offline rate; demonstrates near-optimal information efficiency; empirical validation supports theory.
AI.conclusion: Bridges Bayesian nonparametric regression and RL; shows guided uncertainty reduction accelerates value-function convergence with minimal online data; offers actionable insights for leveraging ActiveRL.

title: BicKD: Bilateral Contrastive Knowledge Distillation
summary: Knowledge distillation (KD) is a machine learning framework that transfers knowledge from a teacher model to a student model. The vanilla KD proposed by Hinton et al. has been the dominant approach in logit-based distillation and demonstrates compelling performance. However, it only performs sample-wise probability alignment between teacher and student's predictions, lacking an mechanism for class-wise comparison. Besides, vanilla KD imposes no structural constraint on the probability space. In this work, we propose a simple yet effective methodology, bilateral contrastive knowledge distillation (BicKD). This approach introduces a novel bilateral contrastive loss, which intensifies the orthogonality among different class generalization spaces while preserving consistency within the same class. The bilateral formulation enables explicit comparison of both sample-wise and class-wise prediction patterns between teacher and student. By emphasizing probabilistic orthogonality, BicKD further regularizes the geometric structure of the predictive distribution. Extensive experiments show that our BicKD method enhances knowledge transfer, and consistently outperforms state-of-the-art knowledge distillation techniques across various model architectures and benchmarks.

AI.tldr: BicKD introduces bilateral contrastive learning into knowledge distillation to enforce both sample-wise and class-wise alignment, via a bilateral contrastive loss that promotes orthogonality between class-generalization spaces while maintaining within-class consistency, yielding superior transfers.
AI.motivation: Vanilla KD aligns predictions only at the sample level and lacks structural constraints on the probability space; this may miss class-wise relations and geometric structure that are important for effective knowledge transfer.
AI.method: Define a bilateral contrastive loss that compares teacher and student predictions across and within classes; encourage orthogonality of class-specific generalization subspaces; integrate into the KD objective to jointly optimize teacher–student alignment and geometric regularization.
AI.result: Empirical results on multiple datasets and architectures show BicKD consistently surpasses state-of-the-art KD methods.
AI.conclusion: BicKD offers a simple yet effective enhancement to KD by incorporating probabilistic orthogonality, improving knowledge transfer and the quality of the student predictor's distribution.

title: Opportunistic Promptable Segmentation: Leveraging Routine Radiological Annotations to Guide 3D CT Lesion Segmentation
summary: The development of machine learning models for CT imaging depends on the availability of large, high-quality, and diverse annotated datasets. Although large volumes of CT images and reports are readily available in clinical picture archiving and communication systems (PACS), 3D segmentations of critical findings are costly to obtain, typically requiring extensive manual annotation by radiologists. On the other hand, it is common for radiologists to provide limited annotations of findings during routine reads, such as line measurements and arrows, that are often stored in PACS as GSPS objects. We posit that these sparse annotations can be extracted along with CT volumes and converted into 3D segmentations using promptable segmentation models, a paradigm we term Opportunistic Promptable Segmentation. To enable this paradigm, we propose SAM2CT, the first promptable segmentation model designed to convert radiologist annotations into 3D segmentations in CT volumes. SAM2CT builds upon SAM2 by extending the prompt encoder to support arrow and line inputs and by introducing Memory-Conditioned Memories (MCM), a memory encoding strategy tailored to 3D medical volumes. On public lesion segmentation benchmarks, SAM2CT outperforms existing promptable segmentation models and similarly trained baselines, achieving Dice similarity coefficients of 0.649 for arrow prompts and 0.757 for line prompts. Applying the model to pre-existing GSPS annotations from a clinical PACS (N = 60), SAM2CT generates 3D segmentations that are clinically acceptable or require only minor adjustments in 87% of cases, as scored by radiologists. Additionally, SAM2CT demonstrates strong zero-shot performance on select Emergency Department findings. These results suggest that large-scale mining of historical GSPS annotations represents a promising and scalable approach for generating 3D CT segmentation datasets.

AI.tldr: SAM2CT converts radiologist GSPS annotations into 3D CT segmentations using an extended promptable segmentation model with memory conditioned memories; it achieves Dice scores of 0.649 for arrow prompts and 0.757 for line prompts on public benchmarks, and yields clinically acceptable segmentations in 87% of a real PACS dataset (N=60); shows promising zero-shot performance for certain ED findings.
AI.motivation: Reduce reliance on labor-intensive 3D manual segmentations by leveraging sparse, routinely captured radiologist annotations (GSPS) in PACS to generate 3D CT segmentations, enabling scalable dataset creation for machine learning.
AI.method: Extend the SAM2 prompt encoder to accept arrow and line inputs and introduce Memory-Conditioned Memories (MCM) to encode 3D CT volumes. Train/evaluate on public lesion segmentation benchmarks; apply to GSPS annotations from a clinical PACS (N=60) to generate 3D segmentations; assess clinical acceptability and zero-shot performance on select findings.
AI.result: Quantitative: Dice scores of 0.649 for arrow prompts and 0.757 for line prompts on public benchmarks. Qualitative/clinical: 87% of SAM2CT-generated segmentations from GSPS annotations were clinically acceptable or required only minor adjustments per radiologists. Zero-shot performance observed on certain Emergency Department findings.
AI.conclusion: Opportunistic Promptable Segmentation (OPS) via SAM2CT is a scalable approach to create 3D CT segmentation datasets by mining historical sparse annotations; extending prompt modalities and 3D memory encoding yields competitive performance and clinical viability.

title: Diving into Kronecker Adapters: Component Design Matters
summary: Kronecker adapters have emerged as a promising approach for fine-tuning large-scale models, enabling high-rank updates through tunable component structures. However, existing work largely treats the component structure as a fixed or heuristic design choice, leaving the dimensions and number of Kronecker components underexplored. In this paper, we identify component structure as a key factor governing the capacity of Kronecker adapters. We perform a fine-grained analysis of both the dimensions and number of Kronecker components. In particular, we show that the alignment between Kronecker adapters and full fine-tuning depends on component configurations. Guided by these insights, we propose Component Designed Kronecker Adapters (CDKA). We further provide parameter-budget-aware configuration guidelines and a tailored training stabilization strategy for practical deployment. Experiments across various natural language processing tasks demonstrate the effectiveness of CDKA. Code is available at https://github.com/rainstonee/CDKA.

AI.tldr: Component-designed Kronecker Adapters (CDKA) optimize Kronecker adapter structure by tailoring component dimensions and counts, showing that configuration alignment with full fine-tuning depends on component setup; results indicate improved performance with budget-aware designs and stabilization strategies across NLP tasks; code released.
AI.motivation: Kronecker adapters enable high-rank, tunable updates for fine-tuning large models, but prior work treated the component structure as fixed or heuristic. This paper argues that the dimensions and number of Kronecker components govern capacity and alignment with full fine-tuning, and seeks systematic design guidelines.
AI.method: Conduct a fine-grained analysis of Kronecker component configurations (varying dimensions and component counts). Establish how alignment with full fine-tuning depends on these configurations. Propose Component Designed Kronecker Adapters (CDKA) with parameter-budget-aware guidelines and a tailored training stabilization strategy. Validate through experiments on multiple NLP tasks and provide code.
AI.result: Demonstrates effectiveness of CDKA across NLP tasks; provides practical guidelines for configuring Kronecker adapters under budget constraints; code available at the provided repository.
AI.conclusion: Component-aware design of Kronecker adapters improves alignment with full fine-tuning and practical deployability. CDKA offers actionable configuration guidelines and stabilization strategies for real-world use.

title: Mixture-of-World Models: Scaling Multi-Task Reinforcement Learning with Modular Latent Dynamics
summary: A fundamental challenge in multi-task reinforcement learning (MTRL) is achieving sample efficiency in visual domains where tasks exhibit substantial heterogeneity in both observations and dynamics. Model-based reinforcement learning offers a promising path to improved sample efficiency through world models, but standard monolithic architectures struggle to capture diverse task dynamics, resulting in poor reconstruction and prediction accuracy. We introduce Mixture-of-World Models (MoW), a scalable architecture that combines modular variational autoencoders for task-adaptive visual compression, a hybrid Transformer-based dynamics model with task-conditioned experts and a shared backbone, and a gradient-based task clustering strategy for efficient parameter allocation. On the Atari 100k benchmark, a single MoW agent trained once on 26 Atari games achieves a mean human-normalized score of 110.4%, competitive with the score of 114.2% achieved by STORM, an ensemble of 26 task-specific models, while using 50% fewer parameters. On Meta-World, MoW achieves a 74.5% average success rate within 300 thousand environment steps, establishing a new state of the art. These results demonstrate that MoW provides a scalable and parameter-efficient foundation for generalist world models.

AI.tldr: MoW presents a scalable, parameter-efficient mixture-of-world models for multi-task RL with task-adaptive VAE compression, a hybrid Transformer dynamics model with task-conditioned experts, and gradient-based task clustering; achieves strong Atari 100k and Meta-World results.
AI.motivation: Address sample inefficiency and heterogeneity in observations and dynamics across tasks; monolithic models underperform; seek scalable generalist world models.
AI.method: MoW comprises modular VAEs for visual compression per task; a shared Transformer backbone with task-conditioned experts for dynamics; gradient-based clustering to allocate parameters efficiently; end-to-end training.
AI.result: On Atari 100k: 110.4% mean human-normalized score, near STORM's 114.2% but with 50% fewer parameters. On Meta-World: 74.5% average success in 300k steps, state-of-the-art.
AI.conclusion: MoW demonstrates a scalable, parameter-efficient foundation for generalist world models in multi-task RL.

title: From Intents to Actions: Agentic AI in Autonomous Networks
summary: Telecommunication networks are increasingly expected to operate autonomously while supporting heterogeneous services with diverse and often conflicting intents -- that is, performance objectives, constraints, and requirements specific to each service. However, transforming high-level intents -- such as ultra-low latency, high throughput, or energy efficiency -- into concrete control actions (i.e., low-level actuator commands) remains beyond the capability of existing heuristic approaches. This work introduces an Agentic AI system for intent-driven autonomous networks, structured around three specialized agents. A supervisory interpreter agent, powered by language models, performs both lexical parsing of intents into executable optimization templates and cognitive refinement based on feedback, constraint feasibility, and evolving network conditions. An optimizer agent converts these templates into tractable optimization problems, analyzes trade-offs, and derives preferences across objectives. Lastly, a preference-driven controller agent, based on multi-objective reinforcement learning, leverages these preferences to operate near the Pareto frontier of network performance that best satisfies the original intent. Collectively, these agents enable networks to autonomously interpret, reason over, adapt to, and act upon diverse intents and network conditions in a scalable manner.

AI.tldr: A three-agent AI framework for intent-driven autonomous networks that translates high-level service intents into actionable network control via a supervisory interpreter, an optimizer, and a preference-driven controller.
AI.motivation: To bridge the gap between qualitative service intents (e.g., ultra-low latency, high throughput, energy efficiency) and concrete control actions, addressing the limitations of heuristic methods and enabling scalable, autonomous network operation.
AI.method: Three specialized agents: (1) supervisory interpreter agent uses language models to lexically parse intents into executable optimization templates and perform cognitive refinement based on feedback and changing conditions; (2) optimizer agent converts templates into tractable optimization problems, analyzes trade-offs, and derives cross-objective preferences; (3) preference-driven controller agent employs multi-objective reinforcement learning to act near the Pareto frontier, guided by the derived preferences.
AI.result: Conceptual framework. Demonstrates how interpretable intent parsing, optimization-trade-off analysis, and MO-RL-driven control can collectively enable autonomous, intent-aligned network operation; no empirical results are provided in the abstract.
AI.conclusion: The proposed Agentic AI system offers a scalable, adaptable path to realizing autonomous networks that can interpret, reason about, and act upon diverse intents under varying network conditions, by integrating semantic interpretation, optimization reasoning, and Pareto-aware control.

title: Richer Bayesian Last Layers with Subsampled NTK Features
summary: Bayesian Last Layers (BLLs) provide a convenient and computationally efficient way to estimate uncertainty in neural networks. However, they underestimate epistemic uncertainty because they apply a Bayesian treatment only to the final layer, ignoring uncertainty induced by earlier layers. We propose a method that improves BLLs by leveraging a projection of Neural Tangent Kernel (NTK) features onto the space spanned by the last-layer features. This enables posterior inference that accounts for variability of the full network while retaining the low computational cost of inference of a standard BLL. We show that our method yields posterior variances that are provably greater or equal to those of a standard BLL, correcting its tendency to underestimate epistemic uncertainty. To further reduce computational cost, we introduce a uniform subsampling scheme for estimating the projection matrix and for posterior inference. We derive approximation bounds for both types of sub-sampling. Empirical evaluations on UCI regression, contextual bandits, image classification, and out-of-distribution detection tasks in image and tabular datasets, demonstrate improved calibration and uncertainty estimates compared to standard BLLs and competitive baselines, while reducing computational cost.

AI.tldr: BLLs improved by projecting NTK features onto the last-layer feature space, yielding conservative posterior variances and better calibration with uniform subsampling for efficiency; outperforms standard BLLs while remaining low-cost.
AI.motivation: Standard Bayesian Last Layers underestimate epistemic uncertainty by treating only the final layer statistically. There is a need to account for variability from earlier layers without incurring full-network Bayesian inference cost.
AI.method: Project Neural Tangent Kernel (NTK) features onto the span of the last-layer features to enable posterior inference that includes full-network variability while preserving BLL efficiency. Introduce a uniform subsampling scheme to estimate the projection and perform posterior inference, with derived approximation bounds for both sub-sampling steps.
AI.result: Posterior variances are provably greater than or equal to those of standard BLLs. Empirical tests across UCI regression, contextual bandits, image classification, and OOD detection show improved calibration and uncertainty estimates versus standard BLLs and competitive baselines, with reduced computational cost.
AI.conclusion: The proposed NTK-projected BLL approach provides more reliable epistemic uncertainty estimates than standard BLLs, achieving favorable calibration with lower computational overhead, and is applicable across diverse tasks.

title: Bridging the Semantic Chasm: Synergistic Conceptual Anchoring for Generalized Few-Shot and Zero-Shot OOD Perception
summary: This manuscript presents a pioneering Synergistic Neural Agents Network (SynerNet) framework designed to mitigate the phenomenon of cross-modal alignment degeneration in Vision-Language Models (VLMs) when encountering Out-of-Distribution (OOD) concepts. Specifically, four specialized computational units - visual perception, linguistic context, nominal embedding, and global coordination - collaboratively rectify modality disparities via a structured message-propagation protocol. The principal contributions encompass a multi-agent latent space nomenclature acquisition framework, a semantic context-interchange algorithm for enhanced few-shot adaptation, and an adaptive dynamic equilibrium mechanism. Empirical evaluations conducted on the VISTA-Beyond benchmark demonstrate that SynerNet yields substantial performance augmentations in both few-shot and zero-shot scenarios, exhibiting precision improvements ranging from 1.2% to 5.4% across a diverse array of domains.

AI.tldr: SynerNet introduces a four-module multi-agent framework to mitigate cross-modal alignment degeneration in Vision-Language Models under Out-of-Distribution concepts, via structured message-passing and adaptive equilibrium, achieving notable few-shot and zero-shot gains on VISTA-Beyond (1.2%–5.4%).
AI.motivation: Address cross-modal misalignment that occurs when VLMs encounter OOD concepts, leading to degraded cross-modal coherence and performance.
AI.method: Four specialized units—visual perception, linguistic context, nominal embedding, and global coordination—operate under a structured message-propagation protocol. Key components include a multi-agent latent space nomenclature acquisition framework, a semantic context-interchange algorithm for enhanced few-shot adaptation, and an adaptive dynamic equilibrium mechanism.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on the VISTA-Beyond benchmark shows substantial performance gains in both few-shot and zero-shot scenarios, with precision improvements ranging from 1.2% to 5.4% across diverse domains.
AI.conclusion: SynerNet effectively mitigates cross-modal alignment degeneration in VLMs under OOD concepts, leveraging coordinated multi-agent interaction and semantic context exchange to boost few-shot/zero-shot performance.

title: Multi-LLM Adaptive Conformal Inference for Reliable LLM Responses
summary: Ensuring factuality is essential for the safe use of Large Language Models (LLMs) in high-stakes domains such as medicine and law. Conformal inference provides distribution-free guarantees, but existing approaches are either overly conservative, discarding many true-claims, or rely on adaptive error rates and simple linear models that fail to capture complex group structures. To address these challenges, we reformulate conformal inference in a multiplicative filtering setting, modeling factuality as a product of claim-level scores. Our method, Multi-LLM Adaptive Conformal Inference (MACI), leverages ensembles to produce more accurate factuality-scores, which in our experiments led to higher retention, while validity is preserved through group-conditional calibration. Experiments show that MACI consistently achieves user-specified coverage with substantially higher retention and lower time cost than baselines. Our repository is available at https://github.com/MLAI-Yonsei/MACI

AI.tldr: MACI reframes conformal inference as multiplicative filtering using ensembles to produce factuality scores, achieving user-specified coverage with higher retention and lower time cost, while group-conditional calibration preserves validity.
AI.motivation: Factuality guarantees in high-stakes domains require distribution-free assurances. Existing conformal methods are too conservative or rely on adaptive error rates and simple models that miss complex group structures; MACI aims to improve retention and efficiency without sacrificing validity.
AI.method: reformulate conformal inference as a product of claim-level scores (multiplicative filtering). Use ensembles to generate factuality-scores; apply group-conditional calibration to maintain validity across groups; aggregate ensemble outputs into a MACI scoring framework.
AI.result: Experiments show MACI achieves user-specified coverage with substantially higher retention and lower time cost than baselines, demonstrating improved efficiency while maintaining validity.
AI.conclusion: MACI offers a practical, distribution-free causal-like approach to factuality filtering in LLM outputs, improving retention and efficiency; code repository provided for reproducibility.

title: When RAG Hurts: Diagnosing and Mitigating Attention Distraction in Retrieval-Augmented LVLMs
summary: While Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) is one of the dominant paradigms for enhancing Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) on knowledge-based VQA tasks, recent work attributes RAG failures to insufficient attention towards the retrieved context, proposing to reduce the attention allocated to image tokens. In this work, we identify a distinct failure mode that previous study overlooked: Attention Distraction (AD). When the retrieved context is sufficient (highly relevant or including the correct answer), the retrieved text suppresses the visual attention globally, and the attention on image tokens shifts away from question-relevant regions. This leads to failures on questions the model could originally answer correctly without the retrieved text. To mitigate this issue, we propose MAD-RAG, a training-free intervention that decouples visual grounding from context integration through a dual-question formulation, combined with attention mixing to preserve image-conditioned evidence. Extensive experiments on OK-VQA, E-VQA, and InfoSeek demonstrate that MAD-RAG consistently outperforms existing baselines across different model families, yielding absolute gains of up to 4.76%, 9.20%, and 6.18% over the vanilla RAG baseline. Notably, MAD-RAG rectifies up to 74.68% of failure cases with negligible computational overhead.

AI.tldr: MAD-RAG introduces a training-free intervention to counter Attention Distraction in Retrieval-Augmented Generation for LVLMs, by decoupling visual grounding from context integration via a dual-question formulation and attention mixing, yielding consistent gains on knowledge-based VQA benchmarks with minimal overhead.
AI.motivation: RAG-based LVLMs exhibit a failure mode beyond under-attention to retrieved context: when retrieved text is highly relevant, it globally suppresses visual attention, diverting focus away from image regions and degrading answers that were previously correct.
AI.method: MAD-RAG employs a training-free strategy that separates visual grounding from context integration using a dual-question setup and applies attention mixing to preserve image-conditioned evidence.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on OK-VQA, E-VQA, and InfoSeek shows consistent improvements over vanilla RAG across model families, with absolute gains of up to 4.76%, 9.20%, and 6.18% respectively, and rectification of up to 74.68% of failure cases with negligible computational overhead.
AI.conclusion: MAD-RAG demonstrates that addressing Attention Distraction via simple grounding–context decoupling can robustly improve knowledge-based VQA performance in LVLMs without additional training.

title: EDIS: Diagnosing LLM Reasoning via Entropy Dynamics
summary: Entropy-based confidence signals are increasingly leveraged to improve reasoning in large language models (LLMs), yet existing approaches treat confidence as a static quantity -- typically aggregated over tokens. We show that the \emph{temporal evolution} of confidence during generation carries richer information than aggregate statistics alone. Analyzing token-level entropy trajectories, we identify characteristic patterns distinguishing correct from incorrect reasoning: erroneous solutions exhibit unstable dynamics, including burst spikes (sustained uncertainty growth) and peak-valley spikes (sharp rebounds following transient confidence). These patterns persist across models and training stages, suggesting they reflect intrinsic properties of reasoning failure rather than superficial noise. To formalize this observation, we introduce the Entropy Dynamics Instability Score (\textbf{EDIS}), a trajectory-level metric quantifying instability in entropy evolution. EDIS serves as an effective diagnostic signal for inference-time selection, substantially improving reasoning accuracy, and offers a promising direction for training-time sample curation. Our findings establish entropy dynamics as an underexplored yet informative lens for understanding and improving LLM reasoning.

AI.tldr: Temporal entropy trajectories reveal instability patterns in erroneous LLM reasoning, leading to an Entropy Dynamics Instability Score (EDIS) that improves inference-time selection and suggests training-time data curation.
AI.motivation: Current work treats confidence as a static quantity; dynamic, token-level entropy evolution may carry richer signals for distinguishing correct vs. incorrect reasoning across models and training stages.
AI.method: Analyze token-level entropy trajectories to identify characteristic patterns (burst spikes and peak-valley spikes) and formulate EDIS as a trajectory-level instability metric. evaluate EDIS for inference-time selection and discuss implications for training-time data curation.
AI.result: EDIS captures instability in entropy trajectories, serving as an effective diagnostic signal that substantially improves reasoning accuracy during inference and shows promise for guiding training data curation.
AI.conclusion: Entropy dynamics constitute a valuable, underexplored perspective for understanding and enhancing LLM reasoning, with EDIS offering a practical tool for detection and potential improvements in model training and inference.

title: AdaFuse: Adaptive Multimodal Fusion for Lung Cancer Risk Prediction via Reinforcement Learning
summary: Multimodal fusion has emerged as a promising paradigm for disease diagnosis and prognosis, integrating complementary information from heterogeneous data sources such as medical images, clinical records, and radiology reports. However, existing fusion methods process all available modalities through the network, either treating them equally or learning to assign different contribution weights, leaving a fundamental question unaddressed: for a given patient, should certain modalities be used at all? We present AdaFuse, an adaptive multimodal fusion framework that leverages reinforcement learning (RL) to learn patient-specific modality selection and fusion strategies for lung cancer risk prediction. AdaFuse formulates multimodal fusion as a sequential decision process, where the policy network iteratively decides whether to incorporate an additional modality or proceed to prediction based on the information already acquired. This sequential formulation enables the model to condition each selection on previously observed modalities and terminate early when sufficient information is available, rather than committing to a fixed subset upfront. We evaluate AdaFuse on the National Lung Screening Trial (NLST) dataset. Experimental results demonstrate that AdaFuse achieves the highest AUC (0.762) compared to the best single-modality baseline (0.732), the best fixed fusion strategy (0.759), and adaptive baselines including DynMM (0.754) and MoE (0.742), while using fewer FLOPs than all triple-modality methods. Our work demonstrates the potential of reinforcement learning for personalized multimodal fusion in medical imaging, representing a shift from uniform fusion strategies toward adaptive diagnostic pipelines that learn when to consult additional modalities and when existing information suffices for accurate prediction.

AI.tldr: AdaFuse uses reinforcement learning to perform patient-specific, sequential multimodal fusion for lung cancer risk prediction, achieving higher AUC with fewer FLOPs compared to single-modality and fixed/adaptive baselines.
AI.motivation: Addresses the limitation of uniform or fixed fusion strategies that either use all modalities indiscriminately or weight modalities equally; aims to tailor modality usage to each patient to improve efficiency and accuracy.
AI.method: Formulates fusion as a sequential decision process with a policy network that, at each step, decides whether to incorporate an additional modality or to output a prediction based on already observed data. The process enables conditioning on observed modalities and early termination when sufficient information is gathered. Evaluation is conducted on the NLST dataset.
AI.result: AdaFuse achieves an AUC of 0.762, outperforming the best single-modality baseline (0.732), the best fixed fusion strategy (0.759), and adaptive baselines including DynMM (0.754) and MoE (0.742), while using fewer FLOPs than all triple-modality methods.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates that reinforcement learning can enable personalized, adaptive multimodal fusion in medical imaging, shifting from uniform fusion toward diagnostic pipelines that learn when to consult additional modalities and when existing information suffices.

title: Gradient-Aligned Calibration for Post-Training Quantization of Diffusion Models
summary: Diffusion models have shown remarkable performance in image synthesis by progressively estimating a smooth transition from a Gaussian distribution of noise to a real image. Unfortunately, their practical deployment is limited by slow inference speed, high memory usage, and the computational demands of the noise estimation process. Post-training quantization (PTQ) emerges as a promising solution to accelerate sampling and reduce memory overhead for diffusion models. Existing PTQ methods for diffusion models typically apply uniform weights to calibration samples across timesteps, which is sub-optimal since data at different timesteps may contribute differently to the diffusion process. Additionally, due to varying activation distributions and gradients across timesteps, a uniform quantization approach is sub-optimal. Each timestep requires a different gradient direction for optimal quantization, and treating them equally can lead to conflicting gradients that degrade performance. In this paper, we propose a novel PTQ method that addresses these challenges by assigning appropriate weights to calibration samples. Specifically, our approach learns to assign optimal weights to calibration samples to align the quantized model's gradients across timesteps, facilitating the quantization process. Extensive experiments on CIFAR-10, LSUN-Bedrooms, and ImageNet demonstrate the superiority of our method compared to other PTQ methods for diffusion models.

AI.tldr: A novel post-training quantization (PTQ) method for diffusion models that learns weights for calibration samples across timesteps to align gradients, addressing suboptimal uniform PTQ and varying activation/gradient distributions; demonstrates improved performance over existing PTQ methods on CIFAR-10, LSUN-Bedrooms, and ImageNet.
AI.motivation: Diffusion models suffer from slow sampling and high memory use. Uniformly quantized calibration across timesteps is suboptimal due to timestep-dependent gradients and activations; better calibration weighting could reduce quantization error and improve performance.
AI.method: Introduce a learning framework that assigns optimal weights to calibration samples at different timesteps to align the quantized model's gradients across timesteps, enabling more effective quantization. Likely optimizes weights to minimize gradient mismatch or quantization error across timesteps.
AI.result: Empirical results show superior performance of the proposed weighted PTQ over existing diffusion PTQ methods on CIFAR-10, LSUN-Bedrooms, and ImageNet.
AI.conclusion: Weighting calibration samples by timestep-specific importance improves gradient alignment and quantization quality for diffusion models, enabling faster/cheaper inference with less accuracy loss; validates across multiple datasets.

title: The BoBW Algorithms for Heavy-Tailed MDPs
summary: We investigate episodic Markov Decision Processes with heavy-tailed feedback (HTMDPs). Existing approaches for HTMDPs are conservative in stochastic environments and lack adaptivity in adversarial regimes. In this work, we propose algorithms ```HT-FTRL-OM``` and ```HT-FTRL-UOB``` for HTMDPs that achieve Best-of-Both-Worlds (BoBW) guarantees: instance-independent regret in adversarial environments and logarithmic instance-dependent regret in self-bounding (including the stochastic case) environments. For the known transition setting, ```HT-FTRL-OM``` applies the Follow-The-Regularized-Leader (FTRL) framework over occupancy measures with novel skipping loss estimators, achieving a $\widetilde{\mathcal{O}}(T^{1/α})$ regret bound in adversarial regimes and a $\mathcal{O}(\log T)$ regret in stochastic regimes. Building upon this framework, we develop a novel algorithm ```HT-FTRL-UOB``` to tackle the more challenging unknown-transition setting. This algorithm employs a pessimistic skipping loss estimator and achieves a $\widetilde{\mathcal{O}}(T^{1/α} + \sqrt{T})$ regret in adversarial regimes and a $\mathcal{O}(\log^2(T))$ regret in stochastic regimes. Our analysis overcomes key barriers through several technical insights, including a local control mechanism for heavy-tailed shifted losses, a new suboptimal-mass propagation principle, and a novel regret decomposition that isolates transition uncertainty from heavy-tailed estimation errors and skipping bias.

AI.tldr: Proposes HT-FTRL-OM and HT-FTRL-UOB for episodic HTMDPs to achieve Best-of-Both-Worlds (BoBW): adversarial instance-independent regret and stochastic/logarithmic instance-dependent regret, via occupancy-measure FTRL with skipping estimators and a pessimistic variant for unknown transitions.
AI.motivation: Address conservatism in HTMDPs under stochastic environments and lack of adaptivity in adversarial regimes; enable BoBW guarantees and handle heavy-tailed feedback.
AI.method: High-level: Use Follow-The-Regularized-Leader over occupancy measures with novel skipping loss estimators. HT-FTRL-OM handles known transitions; HT-FTRL-UOB extends to unknown transitions using a pessimistic skipping loss estimator. Key technical tools include a local control mechanism for heavy-tailed shifted losses, a suboptimal-mass propagation principle, and a regret decomposition separating transition uncertainty, heavy-tailed estimation errors, and skipping bias.
AI.result: Regret bounds: HT-FTRL-OM achieves Õ(T^{1/α}) in adversarial regimes and O(log T) in stochastic regimes (known transitions). HT-FTRL-UOB achieves Õ(T^{1/α} + √T) in adversarial regimes and O(log^2 T) in stochastic regimes (unknown transitions).
AI.conclusion: The work provides a BoBW framework for HTMDPs with known and unknown transitions, overcoming barriers via new analytical tools and decomposition, expanding the applicability of FTRL-based methods to heavy-tailed feedback with transition uncertainty.

title: Dispelling the Curse of Singularities in Neural Network Optimizations
summary: This work investigates the optimization instability of deep neural networks from a less-explored yet insightful perspective: the emergence and amplification of singularities in the parametric space. Our analysis reveals that parametric singularities inevitably grow with gradient updates and further intensify alignment with representations, leading to increased singularities in the representation space. We show that the gradient Frobenius norms are bounded by the top singular values of the weight matrices, and as training progresses, the mutually reinforcing growth of weight and representation singularities, termed the curse of singularities, relaxes these bounds, escalating the risk of sharp loss explosions. To counter this, we propose Parametric Singularity Smoothing (PSS), a lightweight, flexible, and effective method for smoothing the singular spectra of weight matrices. Extensive experiments across diverse datasets, architectures, and optimizers demonstrate that PSS mitigates instability, restores trainability even after failure, and improves both training efficiency and generalization.

AI.tldr: Analyzes optimization instability via emergent singularities in parametric and representation spaces and introduces Parametric Singularity Smoothing (PSS) to stabilize training and improve generalization.
AI.motivation: Optimization instability in deep networks is partly due to evolving singularities in weight and feature spaces. A less-explored view links gradient dynamics to spectral singularities, predicting growth and destabilization, and motivates a lightweight remedy.
AI.method: The paper theorizes that gradient Frobenius norms are bounded by top singular values of weight matrices; it describes mutual growth of weight and representation singularities (“curse of singularities”) and presents Parametric Singularity Smoothing (PSS) to soften weight spectra. It validates the approach with extensive experiments across diverse datasets, architectures, and optimizers.
AI.result: PSS mitigates instability, restores trainability after failures, and improves training efficiency and generalization.
AI.conclusion: Spectral smoothing of weight matrices is an effective, lightweight strategy to curb singularity-driven instability and sharp loss dynamics, offering a practical tool for training robustness.

title: Imperfect Influence, Preserved Rankings: A Theory of TRAK for Data Attribution
summary: Data attribution, tracing a model's prediction back to specific training data, is an important tool for interpreting sophisticated AI models. The widely used TRAK algorithm addresses this challenge by first approximating the underlying model with a kernel machine and then leveraging techniques developed for approximating the leave-one-out (ALO) risk. Despite its strong empirical performance, the theoretical conditions under which the TRAK approximations are accurate as well as the regimes in which they break down remain largely unexplored. In this paper, we provide a theoretical analysis of the TRAK algorithm, characterizing its performance and quantifying the errors introduced by the approximations on which the method relies. We show that although the approximations incur significant errors, TRAK's estimated influence remains highly correlated with the original influence and therefore largely preserves the relative ranking of data points. We corroborate our theoretical results through extensive simulations and empirical studies.

AI.tldr: TRAK approximations are error-prone but preserve data influence ranking due to high correlation with true influence; the paper analyzes conditions where the approximations hold and where they fail, supported by simulations and empirical studies.
AI.motivation: To theoretically understand when TRAK's kernel-based ALO approximations yield reliable data attribution, and to characterize the error and regime of breakdown.
AI.method: Theoretical analysis of TRAK, derive error characterizations for the ALO-based approximations; study correlation between TRAK-estimated influence and true influence; use simulations and empirical studies to validate.
AI.result: Even though absolute errors are large, the estimated influence is highly correlated with the original influence, preserving ranking; empirical results support robustness and indicate regimes of reliability.
AI.conclusion: TRAK remains useful for data attribution in practice due to its rank preservation, though users should be aware of potential absolute error; the work delineates conditions for accuracy and breakdown.

title: PolySAE: Modeling Feature Interactions in Sparse Autoencoders via Polynomial Decoding
summary: Sparse autoencoders (SAEs) have emerged as a promising method for interpreting neural network representations by decomposing activations into sparse combinations of dictionary atoms. However, SAEs assume that features combine additively through linear reconstruction, an assumption that cannot capture compositional structure: linear models cannot distinguish whether "Starbucks" arises from the composition of "star" and "coffee" features or merely their co-occurrence. This forces SAEs to allocate monolithic features for compound concepts rather than decomposing them into interpretable constituents. We introduce PolySAE, which extends the SAE decoder with higher-order terms to model feature interactions while preserving the linear encoder essential for interpretability. Through low-rank tensor factorization on a shared projection subspace, PolySAE captures pairwise and triple feature interactions with small parameter overhead (3% on GPT2). Across four language models and three SAE variants, PolySAE achieves an average improvement of approximately 8% in probing F1 while maintaining comparable reconstruction error, and produces 2-10$\times$ larger Wasserstein distances between class-conditional feature distributions. Critically, learned interaction weights exhibit negligible correlation with co-occurrence frequency ($r = 0.06$ vs. $r = 0.82$ for SAE feature covariance), suggesting that polynomial terms capture compositional structure, such as morphological binding and phrasal composition, largely independent of surface statistics.

AI.tldr: PolySAE adds higher-order interaction terms to an SAE decoder via low-rank tensor factorization in a shared subspace, enabling modeling of pairwise/triple feature interactions with minimal parameter cost while keeping a linear encoder for interpretability; it improves probing performance and captures compositional structure beyond co-occurrence.
AI.motivation: SAEs struggle to disentangle compositional structure (e.g., morphological binding, phrasal composition) from mere feature co-occurrence because linear reconstruction blends additive features into monolithic concepts. PolySAE aims to capture interactions without sacrificing encoder interpretability.
AI.method: Extend the SAE decoder with higher-order terms implemented via low-rank tensor factorization on a shared projection subspace. This yields pairwise and triple feature interactions with small parameter overhead (≈3% on GPT-2). Evaluated across four language models and three SAE variants using probing F1 and Wasserstein distances between class-conditional feature distributions; analyzed correlation between learned interaction weights and co-occurrence.
AI.result: PolySAE yields about 8% average improvement in probing F1 with reconstruction error comparable to baselines; produces 2–10× larger Wasserstein distances between class-conditional feature distributions; learned interaction weights show near-zero correlation with co-occurrence frequency (r≈0.06) compared with SAE feature covariance (r≈0.82), indicating interactions capture compositional structure beyond surface statistics.
AI.conclusion: Introducing higher-order polynomial terms in the SAE decoder enables modeling of compositional structure (e.g., morphology, phrasal composition) while preserving a linear encoder for interpretability and maintaining reconstruction quality; the approach distinctively leverages interaction terms to separate composition from co-occurrence.

title: High-accuracy sampling for diffusion models and log-concave distributions
summary: We present algorithms for diffusion model sampling which obtain $δ$-error in $\mathrm{polylog}(1/δ)$ steps, given access to $\widetilde O(δ)$-accurate score estimates in $L^2$. This is an exponential improvement over all previous results. Specifically, under minimal data assumptions, the complexity is $\widetilde O(d\,\mathrm{polylog}(1/δ))$ where $d$ is the dimension of the data; under a non-uniform $L$-Lipschitz condition, the complexity is $\widetilde O(\sqrt{dL}\,\mathrm{polylog}(1/δ))$; and if the data distribution has intrinsic dimension $d_\star$, then the complexity reduces to $\widetilde O(d_\star\,\mathrm{polylog}(1/δ))$. Our approach also yields the first $\mathrm{polylog}(1/δ)$ complexity sampler for general log-concave distributions using only gradient evaluations.

AI.tldr: Diffusion-sampling algorithms achieve delta-error in polylog(1/δ) steps using roughly δ-accurate score estimates in L2, an exponential improvement over prior work; with data assumptions, complexity scales as d, sqrt(dL), or d* depending on the setting; also provides the first polylog(1/δ) gradient-based sampler for general log-concave distributions.
AI.motivation: Efficient diffusion-based sampling is central to modern generative models but existing methods incur near-linear or worse dependence on 1/δ. Reducing the required number of steps to polylog(1/δ) drastically speeds up sampling while maintaining accuracy, given accurate score estimates.
