AI.method: Develop algorithms that operate with δ-accurate score estimates in L2 and analyze their convergence and sample complexity under multiple data assumptions: (i) minimal data assumptions (dimension d), (ii) non-uniform L-Lipschitz condition (dependence ~√dL), (iii) intrinsic data dimension d*, and (iv) extension to log-concave distributions using only gradient evaluations.
AI.result: Achieves δ-error in polylog(1/δ) steps with complexity ~ d polylog(1/δ) under minimal assumptions, ~ √(dL) polylog(1/δ) under non-uniform L-Lipschitz, and ~ d* polylog(1/δ) when intrinsic dimension d* is available; further provides the first polylog(1/δ) gradient-based sampler for general log-concave distributions.
AI.conclusion: Represents an exponential improvement over prior diffusion-sampling results and broadens applicability to high-dimensional and structured data, including log-concave distributions, by leveraging few, accurate score estimates and relying on intrinsic dimensionality where available.

title: Text is All You Need for Vision-Language Model Jailbreaking
summary: Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) are increasingly equipped with robust safety safeguards to prevent responses to harmful or disallowed prompts. However, these defenses often focus on analyzing explicit textual inputs or relevant visual scenes. In this work, we introduce Text-DJ, a novel jailbreak attack that bypasses these safeguards by exploiting the model's Optical Character Recognition (OCR) capability. Our methodology consists of three stages. First, we decompose a single harmful query into multiple and semantically related but more benign sub-queries. Second, we pick a set of distraction queries that are maximally irrelevant to the harmful query. Third, we present all decomposed sub-queries and distraction queries to the LVLM simultaneously as a grid of images, with the position of the sub-queries being middle within the grid. We demonstrate that this method successfully circumvents the safety alignment of state-of-the-art LVLMs. We argue this attack succeeds by (1) converting text-based prompts into images, bypassing standard text-based filters, and (2) inducing distractions, where the model's safety protocols fail to link the scattered sub-queries within a high number of irrelevant queries. Overall, our findings expose a critical vulnerability in LVLMs' OCR capabilities that are not robust to dispersed, multi-image adversarial inputs, highlighting the need for defenses for fragmented multimodal inputs.

AI.tldr: A three-stage jailbreak attack (Text-DJ) circumvents LVLM safety by exploiting OCR: (1) decompose a harmful query into semantically related, benign sub-queries, (2) add distraction queries irrelevant to the harmful prompt, (3) present all sub-queries and distractions as a grid of images with the sub-queries centered. This reveals a vulnerability in LVLMs’ OCR-based safety and calls for defenses against fragmented multimodal inputs.
AI.motivation: Current LVLM safety mechanisms largely target explicit textual prompts or relevant visual scenes. This leaves OCR-based and fragmented multi-image inputs vulnerable. Evaluating robustness against such multimodal adversarial strategies is necessary to ensure reliable safety behavior in real-world deployments.
AI.method: Three-stage approach: (1) decompose a harmful query into multiple semantically related but more benign sub-queries; (2) select distraction queries maximally irrelevant to the harmful query; (3) present all sub-queries and distraction queries simultaneously as a grid of images with the harmful sub-queries positioned centrally within the grid, leveraging OCR to interpret text in images.
AI.result: The method successfully bypasses safety alignment of state-of-the-art LVLMs, demonstrating that OCR-based, dispersed, multi-image inputs can defeat protective filters and cause unsafe outputs.
AI.conclusion: Highlights a critical vulnerability in LVLMs' OCR capabilities to fragmented multimodal inputs. Signals the need for defenses that robustly process dispersed text across multiple images and improves alignment across multimodal inputs.

title: Finding Differentially Private Second Order Stationary Points in Stochastic Minimax Optimization
summary: We provide the first study of the problem of finding differentially private (DP) second-order stationary points (SOSP) in stochastic (non-convex) minimax optimization. Existing literature either focuses only on first-order stationary points for minimax problems or on SOSP for classical stochastic minimization problems. This work provides, for the first time, a unified and detailed treatment of both empirical and population risks. Specifically, we propose a purely first-order method that combines a nested gradient descent--ascent scheme with SPIDER-style variance reduction and Gaussian perturbations to ensure privacy. A key technical device is a block-wise ($q$-period) analysis that controls the accumulation of stochastic variance and privacy noise without summing over the full iteration horizon, yielding a unified treatment of both empirical-risk and population formulations. Under standard smoothness, Hessian-Lipschitzness, and strong concavity assumptions, we establish high-probability guarantees for reaching an $(α,\sqrt{ρ_Φα})$-approximate second-order stationary point with $α= \mathcal{O}( (\frac{\sqrt{d}}{n\varepsilon})^{2/3})$ for empirical risk objectives and $\mathcal{O}(\frac{1}{n^{1/3}} + (\frac{\sqrt{d}}{n\varepsilon})^{1/2})$ for population objectives, matching the best known rates for private first-order stationarity.

AI.tldr: First study of differential privacy (DP) for second-order stationary points (SOSP) in stochastic non-convex minimax optimization. Proposes a purely first-order method—nested gradient descent/ascent with SPIDER variance reduction and Gaussian perturbations—to achieve DP SOSP. Introduces a block-wise (q-period) analysis to control stochastic variance and privacy noise, yielding high-probability guarantees for both empirical and population objectives. Achieves α-approximate SOSP with rates matching the best known private first-order stationarity results: α = O((√d/(nε))^{2/3}) for empirical risk and α = O(1/n^{1/3} + (√d/(nε))^{1/2}) for population risk.
AI.motivation: Addresses a gap in DP optimization: prior work handles either first-order stationarity in minimax or SOSP in standard stochastic optimization, but not DP SOSP for stochastic minimax. Seeks a unified treatment of empirical and population risks under standard smoothness and privacy assumptions.
AI.method: A purely first-order algorithm combining nested gradient descent–ascent with SPIDER-style variance reduction and Gaussian perturbations for privacy. Uses a block-wise (q-period) analysis to bound accumulation of stochastic variance and privacy noise without horizon-wide summation, enabling a unified analysis for empirical and population objectives.
AI.result: With standard assumptions (smoothness, Hessian-Lipschitzness, strong concavity), derives high-probability guarantees for reaching an (α,√(ρ_Φ α))-approximate SOSP. Achieves α rates as stated for empirical and population objectives, matching the best-known private first-order stationarity rates.
AI.conclusion: First to provide DP SOSP guarantees in stochastic minimax optimization; introduces a unified, block-wise analytical framework; results align with optimal private first-order rates, indicating effective privacy-preserving second-order guarantees in minimax settings.

title: Your Self-Play Algorithm is Secretly an Adversarial Imitator: Understanding LLM Self-Play through the Lens of Imitation Learning
summary: Self-play post-training methods has emerged as an effective approach for finetuning large language models and turn the weak language model into strong language model without preference data. However, the theoretical foundations for self-play finetuning remain underexplored. In this work, we tackle this by connecting self-play finetuning with adversarial imitation learning by formulating finetuning procedure as a min-max game between the model and a regularized implicit reward player parameterized by the model itself. This perspective unifies self-play imitation and general preference alignment within a common framework. Under this formulation, we present a game-theoretic analysis showing that the self-play finetuning will converge to it's equilibrium. Guided by this theoretical formulation, we propose a new self-play imitation finetuning algorithm based on the $χ^2$-divergence variational objective with bounded rewards and improved stability. Experiments on various of language model finetuning tasks demonstrate consistent improvements over existing self-play methods and validate our theoretical insights.

AI.tldr: Frames self-play finetuning as a min-max game against a model-parameterized implicit reward player, providing a theory-grounded convergence analysis and a new chi-square-divergence-based objective that improves stability and performance.
AI.motivation: Addresses the lack of theoretical foundations for self-play finetuning in language models and seeks a unified framework with adversarial imitation learning to explain and improve existing methods.
AI.method: Formulates finetuning as a two-player game between the model and a regularized implicit reward player parameterized by the model; derives a game-theoretic convergence analysis; proposes a new self-play objective based on the χ^2-divergence variational objective with bounded rewards.
AI.result: The approach yields convergence to equilibrium under the proposed framework; experiments across multiple LLM finetuning tasks show consistent performance gains and improved stability compared with existing self-play methods.
AI.conclusion: By unifying self-play imitation and preference alignment within a game-theoretic view, the work provides theoretical grounding for self-play finetuning and a practical, more stable objective that improves performance on diverse LLM finetuning tasks.

title: PaAno: Patch-Based Representation Learning for Time-Series Anomaly Detection
comment: Accepted by the 14th International Conference on Learning Representations (ICLR 2026)
summary: Although recent studies on time-series anomaly detection have increasingly adopted ever-larger neural network architectures such as transformers and foundation models, they incur high computational costs and memory usage, making them impractical for real-time and resource-constrained scenarios. Moreover, they often fail to demonstrate significant performance gains over simpler methods under rigorous evaluation protocols. In this study, we propose Patch-based representation learning for time-series Anomaly detection (PaAno), a lightweight yet effective method for fast and efficient time-series anomaly detection. PaAno extracts short temporal patches from time-series training data and uses a 1D convolutional neural network to embed each patch into a vector representation. The model is trained using a combination of triplet loss and pretext loss to ensure the embeddings capture informative temporal patterns from input patches. During inference, the anomaly score at each time step is computed by comparing the embeddings of its surrounding patches to those of normal patches extracted from the training time-series. Evaluated on the TSB-AD benchmark, PaAno achieved state-of-the-art performance, significantly outperforming existing methods, including those based on heavy architectures, on both univariate and multivariate time-series anomaly detection across various range-wise and point-wise performance measures.

AI.tldr: PaAno is a lightweight patch-based time-series anomaly detector that uses 1D CNN embeddings trained with triplet and pretext losses to detect anomalies efficiently; it achieves state-of-the-art on TSB-AD, outperforming heavy models on univariate and multivariate data.
AI.motivation: Addresses the computational burden and memory demands of large transformers/foundations models in time-series anomaly detection, while seeking rigorous evaluation to demonstrate gains over simple baselines.
AI.method: Extract short temporal patches from training data; embed patches via a 1D CNN; train embeddings with a combination of triplet loss and pretext loss; during inference, compute anomaly score by comparing embeddings of surrounding patches with normal patches from training.
AI.result: On the TSB-AD benchmark PaAno achieves state-of-the-art performance, significantly outperforming existing methods including heavy architectures, across univariate/multivariate time-series and various evaluation metrics.
AI.conclusion: Patch-based representation learning provides a fast, resource-efficient approach with competitive or superior anomaly-detection performance under realistic evaluation protocols.

title: LatentLens: Revealing Highly Interpretable Visual Tokens in LLMs
summary: Transforming a large language model (LLM) into a Vision-Language Model (VLM) can be achieved by mapping the visual tokens from a vision encoder into the embedding space of an LLM. Intriguingly, this mapping can be as simple as a shallow MLP transformation. To understand why LLMs can so readily process visual tokens, we need interpretability methods that reveal what is encoded in the visual token representations at every layer of LLM processing. In this work, we introduce LatentLens, a novel approach for mapping latent representations to descriptions in natural language. LatentLens works by encoding a large text corpus and storing contextualized token representations for each token in that corpus. Visual token representations are then compared to their contextualized textual representations, with the top-k nearest neighbor representations providing descriptions of the visual token. We evaluate this method on 10 different VLMs, showing that commonly used methods, such as LogitLens, substantially underestimate the interpretability of visual tokens. With LatentLens instead, the majority of visual tokens are interpretable across all studied models and all layers. Qualitatively, we show that the descriptions produced by LatentLens are semantically meaningful and provide more fine-grained interpretations for humans compared to individual tokens. More broadly, our findings contribute new evidence on the alignment between vision and language representations, opening up new directions for analyzing latent representations.

AI.tldr: LatentLens maps visual tokens to natural-language descriptions by matching their latent representations to contextualized token embeddings learned from a large text corpus, revealing that visual tokens are broadly interpretable across multiple VLMs and layers and outperforming LogitLens in interpretability studies.
AI.motivation: To understand why LLMs can readily process visual tokens and to quantify the interpretability of visual representations in LLM-based VLMs, addressing limitations of prior methods such as LogitLens.
AI.method: Encode a large text corpus to obtain contextualized token embeddings. For each visual token from a vision encoder, compute similarities to the corpus token representations and retrieve the top-k nearest neighbors to generate natural-language descriptions of the visual token. Evaluate across 10 different VLMs and all layers, comparing against LogitLens, and perform qualitative analysis of the produced descriptions.
AI.result: LatentLens reveals that the majority of visual tokens are interpretable across studied models and layers, with the descriptions being semantically meaningful and providing finer-grained interpretations than single tokens. In contrast, LogitLens substantially underestimates interpretability.
AI.conclusion: LatentLens provides a robust framework for interpreting latent visual representations in VLMs, offering new evidence on the alignment between vision and language representations and enabling more nuanced analyses of latent tokens across models and layers.

title: When Domains Interact: Asymmetric and Order-Sensitive Cross-Domain Effects in Reinforcement Learning for Reasoning
summary: Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) has become a key technique for improving reasoning abilities in large language models, yet its behavior under different domain sequencing strategies is poorly understood. In particular, the impact of sequential (one domain at a time) versus mixed-domain (multiple domain at a time) training in GRPO has not been systematically studied. We provide the first systematic analysis of training-order effects across math, science, logic, and puzzle reasoning tasks. We found (1) single-domain generalization is highly asymmetric: training on other domains improves math reasoning by approximately 25\% accuracy, while yielding negligible transfer to logic and puzzle; (2) cross-domain interactions are highly order-dependent: training in the order math$\rightarrow$science achieves 83\% / 41\% accuracy on math / science, while reversing the order to science$\rightarrow$math degrades performance to 77\% / 25\%; (3) no single strategy is universally optimal in multi-domain training: sequential training favors math (up to 84\%), mixed training favors science and logic, and poor ordering can incur large performance gaps (from 70\% to 56\%). Overall, our findings demonstrate that GRPO under multi-domain settings exhibits pronounced asymmetry, order sensitivity, and strategy dependence, highlighting the necessity of domain-aware and order-aware training design.

AI.tldr: Systematic study of training order in Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) reveals asymmetry in cross-domain transfer, strong order effects, and no universal multi-domain strategy.
AI.motivation: To understand how sequential vs mixed-domain training in GRPO affects reasoning across math, science, logic, and puzzles, enabling domain-aware curriculum design for LLMs.
AI.method: Evaluate single-domain generalization and cross-domain transfer under two training regimes (sequential vs mixed) across four domains; quantify performance on math, science, logic, puzzle tasks.
AI.result: Findings: (1) cross-domain transfer to math is asymmetric (+~25% accuracy) with little transfer to logic/puzzle; (2) order matters: math→science yields 83% math and 41% science, while science→math yields 77% math and 25% science; (3) no universally optimal strategy: sequential boosts math up to 84%, mixed training boosts science/logic, poor ordering yields 56–70% gaps.
AI.conclusion: GRPO in multi-domain settings is dominated by asymmetry and order sensitivity; effective training requires domain-aware and order-aware strategies.

title: Deep Variational Contrastive Learning for Joint Risk Stratification and Time-to-Event Estimation
summary: Survival analysis is essential for clinical decision-making, as it allows practitioners to estimate time-to-event outcomes, stratify patient risk profiles, and guide treatment planning. Deep learning has revolutionized this field with unprecedented predictive capabilities but faces a fundamental trade-off between performance and interpretability. While neural networks achieve high accuracy, their black-box nature limits clinical adoption. Conversely, deep clustering-based methods that stratify patients into interpretable risk groups typically sacrifice predictive power. We propose CONVERSE (CONtrastive Variational Ensemble for Risk Stratification and Estimation), a deep survival model that bridges this gap by unifying variational autoencoders with contrastive learning for interpretable risk stratification. CONVERSE combines variational embeddings with multiple intra- and inter-cluster contrastive losses. Self-paced learning progressively incorporates samples from easy to hard, improving training stability. The model supports cluster-specific survival heads, enabling accurate ensemble predictions. Comprehensive evaluation on four benchmark datasets demonstrates that CONVERSE achieves competitive or superior performance compared to existing deep survival methods, while maintaining meaningful patient stratification.

AI.tldr: A novel deep survival method CONVERSE unifies variational autoencoders with contrastive learning to deliver interpretable risk stratification and competitive predictive performance, using self-paced learning and cluster-specific survival heads.
AI.motivation: There is a trade-off between predictive performance and interpretability in deep survival models; neural networks are accurate but black-box; clustering-based methods offer interpretability but sacrifice accuracy. A method that provides both is needed.
AI.method: CONVERSE combines variational autoencoder embeddings with multiple intra- and inter-cluster contrastive losses, uses self-paced learning to progressively include easy-to-hard samples, and employs cluster-specific survival heads to enable ensemble predictions.
AI.result: Comprehensive evaluation on four benchmark datasets shows CONVERSE achieves competitive or superior performance compared to existing deep survival methods while maintaining meaningful patient stratification.
AI.conclusion: The approach bridges the gap between accuracy and interpretability in survival analysis, offering a scalable framework for risk stratification and ensemble predictions.

title: An Odd Estimator for Shapley Values
summary: The Shapley value is a ubiquitous framework for attribution in machine learning, encompassing feature importance, data valuation, and causal inference. However, its exact computation is generally intractable, necessitating efficient approximation methods. While the most effective and popular estimators leverage the paired sampling heuristic to reduce estimation error, the theoretical mechanism driving this improvement has remained opaque. In this work, we provide an elegant and fundamental justification for paired sampling: we prove that the Shapley value depends exclusively on the odd component of the set function, and that paired sampling orthogonalizes the regression objective to filter out the irrelevant even component. Leveraging this insight, we propose OddSHAP, a novel consistent estimator that performs polynomial regression solely on the odd subspace. By utilizing the Fourier basis to isolate this subspace and employing a proxy model to identify high-impact interactions, OddSHAP overcomes the combinatorial explosion of higher-order approximations. Through an extensive benchmark evaluation, we find that OddSHAP achieves state-of-the-art estimation accuracy.

AI.tldr: OddSHAP presents a consistent Shapley-value estimator by isolating the odd component of the set function via Fourier basis, enabling polynomial regression on a reduced subspace. It uses a proxy model to select high-impact interactions and claims state-of-the-art accuracy with reduced combinatorial complexity.
AI.motivation: To address the intractability of exact Shapley value computation and to demystify why paired sampling improves estimation, providing a principled, scalable estimator.
AI.method: The authors prove that Shapley values depend only on the odd component of the set function and that paired sampling orthogonalizes the regression objective to exclude the even component. They construct OddSHAP by performing polynomial regression restricted to the odd subspace using a Fourier basis to isolate this subspace, and use a proxy model to identify relevant higher-order interactions to mitigate combinatorial blow-up.
AI.result: Empirical benchmarks show that OddSHAP achieves state-of-the-art estimation accuracy, validating both the theoretical decomposition and the practical estimator.
AI.conclusion: OddSHAP offers a theoretically grounded and scalable approach to Shapley-value estimation by focusing on the odd subspace; it provides both conceptual insight into paired-sampling benefits and a practical, high-performance estimator.

title: Sparse Shortcuts: Facilitating Efficient Fusion in Multimodal Large Language Models
summary: With the remarkable success of large language models (LLMs) in natural language understanding and generation, multimodal large language models (MLLMs) have rapidly advanced in their ability to process data across multiple modalities. While most existing efforts focus on scaling up language models or constructing higher-quality training data, limited attention has been paid to effectively integrating cross-modal knowledge into the language space. In vision-language models, for instance, aligning modalities using only high-level visual features often discards the rich semantic information present in mid- and low-level features, limiting the model's ability of cross-modality understanding. To address this issue, we propose SparseCut, a general cross-modal fusion architecture for MLLMs, introducing sparse shortcut connections between the cross-modal encoder and the LLM. These shortcut connections enable the efficient and hierarchical integration of visual features at multiple levels, facilitating richer semantic fusion without increasing computational overhead. We further introduce an efficient multi-grained feature fusion module, which performs the fusion of visual features before routing them through the shortcuts. This preserves the original language context and does not increase the overall input length, thereby avoiding an increase in computational complexity for the LLM. Experiments demonstrate that SparseCut significantly enhances the performance of MLLMs across various multimodal benchmarks with generality and scalability for different base LLMs.

AI.tldr: SparseCut introduces sparse shortcut connections and a multi-grained feature fusion module to integrate cross-modal visual features into LLMs, improving multimodal understanding without increasing computation or input length.
AI.motivation: Current cross-modal fusion often relies on high-level visual features, which discards mid- and low-level semantic information. A scalable, efficient fusion mechanism that preserves language context and enables hierarchical integration is needed.
AI.method: Proposes SparseCut, a general cross-modal fusion architecture with sparse shortcut connections from the cross-modal encoder to the LLM, enabling hierarchical, efficient integration of visual features. Includes a multi-grained feature fusion module that fuses visual features before routing through shortcuts, preserving language context and avoiding extra input length.
AI.result: Empirical results show significant performance gains of SparseCut on multiple multimodal benchmarks across different base LLMs, indicating strong generality and scalability.
AI.conclusion: SparseCut offers a general, scalable cross-modal fusion approach that leverages multi-level visual information while maintaining language model efficiency, enabling richer cross-modal understanding in MLLMs.

title: SNIP: An Adaptive Mixed Precision Framework for Subbyte Large Language Model Training
summary: Training large language models (LLMs) efficiently while preserving model quality poses significant challenges, particularly with subbyte precision supported by state-of-the-art GPUs. Current mixed-precision training approaches either apply uniform precision to all GEMM operations or rely on heuristic-based methods that fail to generalize during training, leading to suboptimal convergence and instability. To address these challenges, this paper introduces SNIP, a fine-grained adaptive mixed-precision training framework for LLM pretraining that supports subbyte precision. SNIP periodically collects statistics on activations, gradients, and optimizer states to assess the precision loss impact on model quality. We define two key metrics: loss divergence in the forward pass, caused by quantization-induced increases in training loss, and weight divergence in the backward pass, which measures error propagation through gradients affecting model updates. These metrics guide an Integer Linear Programming (ILP) problem that systematically optimizes layerwise precision to minimize overall quality loss while meeting efficiency targets. Experiments on 1B, 3B, 7B and 70B Llama-like models demonstrate that SNIP consistently outperforms existing baselines, reducing FLOPs by up to 80% while preserving model quality across different model sizes and training phases with minimal computational overhead.

AI.tldr: SNIP is a fine-grained adaptive mixed-precision framework for LLM pretraining that uses subbyte precision guided by forward and backward loss-divergence metrics, solved via ILP to optimize layerwise precision under efficiency constraints, achieving up to 80% FLOPs reduction while preserving model quality across 1B–70B Llama-like models.
AI.motivation: Current mixed-precision strategies either apply uniform precision or rely on heuristic methods that fail to generalize during training, leading to suboptimal convergence and instability in LLM pretraining. A data-driven, granular approach is needed to balance efficiency and model quality.
AI.method: SNIP periodically collects statistics on activations, gradients, and optimizer states to compute two metrics: forward loss divergence (quantization-induced loss rise) and backward weight divergence (error propagation in gradients). These metrics feed an Integer Linear Programming (ILP) problem that selects layerwise precisions to minimize quality loss subject to efficiency targets, supporting subbyte precision.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on 1B, 3B, 7B, and 70B Llama-like models shows SNIP consistently outperforms baselines, achieving up to 80% FLOPs reduction with preserved quality and minimal overhead across model sizes and training phases.
AI.conclusion: Fine-grained adaptive mixed-precision with ILP-based optimization is effective for LLM pretraining, enabling substantial computational savings while maintaining model quality across diverse model scales and training stages.

title: Semi-supervised CAPP Transformer Learning via Pseudo-labeling
summary: High-level Computer-Aided Process Planning (CAPP) generates manufacturing process plans from part specifications. It suffers from limited dataset availability in industry, reducing model generalization. We propose a semi-supervised learning approach to improve transformer-based CAPP transformer models without manual labeling. An oracle, trained on available transformer behaviour data, filters correct predictions from unseen parts, which are then used for one-shot retraining. Experiments on small-scale datasets with simulated ground truth across the full data distribution show consistent accuracy gains over baselines, demonstrating the method's effectiveness in data-scarce manufacturing environments.

AI.tldr: Semi-supervised enhancement for transformer-based CAPP via an oracle-filtered one-shot retraining to address data scarcity.
AI.motivation: Industry CAPP models suffer from limited labeled data, hindering generalization across part specifications.
AI.method: Train an oracle on existing transformer behavior data to identify correct predictions on unseen parts; use these filtered predictions for one-shot retraining of the model.
AI.result: On small-scale, simulated-ground-truth datasets, the approach yields consistent accuracy gains over baselines across the full data distribution.
AI.conclusion: The proposed semi-supervised approach can improve CAPP performance in data-scarce manufacturing settings without manual labeling, enhancing generalization.

title: Improve the Trade-off Between Watermark Strength and Speculative Sampling Efficiency for Language Models
summary: Watermarking is a principled approach for tracing the provenance of large language model (LLM) outputs, but its deployment in practice is hindered by inference inefficiency. Speculative sampling accelerates inference, with efficiency improving as the acceptance rate between draft and target models increases. Yet recent work reveals a fundamental trade-off: higher watermark strength reduces acceptance, preventing their simultaneous achievement. We revisit this trade-off and show it is not absolute. We introduce a quantitative measure of watermark strength that governs statistical detectability and is maximized when tokens are deterministic functions of pseudorandom numbers. Using this measure, we fully characterize the trade-off as a constrained optimization problem and derive explicit Pareto curves for two existing watermarking schemes. Finally, we introduce a principled mechanism that injects pseudorandomness into draft-token acceptance, ensuring maximal watermark strength while maintaining speculative sampling efficiency. Experiments further show that this approach improves detectability without sacrificing efficiency. Our findings uncover a principle that unites speculative sampling and watermarking, paving the way for their efficient and practical deployment.

AI.tldr: Reframes the watermarking-inference trade-off: defines a quantifiable watermark strength tied to deterministic PRN-driven tokens, derives Pareto curves for two schemes, and proposes injecting pseudorandomness into draft-token acceptance to maximize watermark detectability without hurting speculative sampling efficiency; experiments validate improved detection with maintained efficiency.
AI.motivation: Watermarking enables provenance tracing for LLM outputs but practical deployment is hampered by inference efficiency. Prior work shows a trade-off where stronger watermarking reduces acceptance rate in speculative sampling. The paper aims to break this perceived trade-off and enable efficient, detectable watermarking.
AI.method: Introduce a quantitative watermark strength measure governed by statistical detectability, maximized when tokens are deterministic functions of pseudorandom numbers. Formulate the trade-off as a constrained optimization problem, derive explicit Pareto curves for two existing watermarking schemes, and propose a principled mechanism that injects pseudorandomness into draft-token acceptance to maximize watermark strength while preserving speculative sampling efficiency.
AI.result: Explicit Pareto curves for two watermarking schemes are derived; a mechanism that injects pseudorandomness into draft-token acceptance is proposed, achieving maximal watermark strength without sacrificing efficiency. Experiments show improved detectability with maintained efficiency.
AI.conclusion: A unifying principle connects speculative sampling and watermarking, enabling efficient and practical deployment of watermark-based provenance in LLM outputs.

title: DCD: Decomposition-based Causal Discovery from Autocorrelated and Non-Stationary Temporal Data
summary: Multivariate time series in domains such as finance, climate science, and healthcare often exhibit long-term trends, seasonal patterns, and short-term fluctuations, complicating causal inference under non-stationarity and autocorrelation. Existing causal discovery methods typically operate on raw observations, making them vulnerable to spurious edges and misattributed temporal dependencies. We introduce a decomposition-based causal discovery framework that separates each time series into trend, seasonal, and residual components and performs component-specific causal analysis. Trend components are assessed using stationarity tests, seasonal components using kernel-based dependence measures, and residual components using constraint-based causal discovery. The resulting component-level graphs are integrated into a unified multi-scale causal structure. This approach isolates long- and short-range causal effects, reduces spurious associations, and improves interpretability. Across extensive synthetic benchmarks and real-world climate data, our framework more accurately recovers ground-truth causal structure than state-of-the-art baselines, particularly under strong non-stationarity and temporal autocorrelation.

AI.tldr: Decomposition-based causal discovery separates trend, seasonal, and residual components to improve causal graph recovery in non-stationary multivariate time series, outperforming baselines on synthetic and climate data.
AI.motivation: Non-stationarity and autocorrelation in multivariate time series cause spurious edges; raw-observation methods struggle to disentangle long- vs short-range causal effects; need component-wise, multi-scale causal analysis for better accuracy and interpretability.
AI.method: Decompose each series into trend, seasonal, and residual; apply component-specific causal analysis: trend with stationarity tests; seasonal with kernel-based dependence measures; residual with constraint-based discovery; integrate into a unified multi-scale causal structure.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on extensive synthetic benchmarks and real climate data shows improved recovery of ground-truth causal structure versus state-of-the-art baselines, especially under strong non-stationarity and temporal autocorrelation.
AI.conclusion: The component-wise, multi-scale approach isolates long- and short-range causal effects, reduces spurious associations, enhances interpretability, and yields more accurate causal graphs.

title: Phase Transitions for Feature Learning in Neural Networks
summary:
According to a popular viewpoint, neural networks learn from data by first identifying low-dimensional representations, and subsequently fitting the best model in this space. Recent works provide a formalization of this phenomenon when learning multi-index models. In this setting, we are given $n$ i.i.d. pairs $({\boldsymbol x}_i,y_i)$, where the covariate vectors ${\boldsymbol x}_i\in\mathbb{R}^d$ are isotropic, and responses $y_i$ only depend on ${\boldsymbol x}_i$ through a $k$-dimensional projection ${\boldsymbol Θ}_*^{\sf T}{\boldsymbol x}_i$. Feature learning amounts to learning the latent space spanned by ${\boldsymbol Θ}_*$.
  In this context, we study the gradient descent dynamics of two-layer neural networks under the proportional asymptotics $n,d\to\infty$, $n/d\toδ$, while the dimension of the latent space $k$ and the number of hidden neurons $m$ are kept fixed. Earlier work establishes that feature learning via polynomial-time algorithms is possible if $δ> δ_{\text{alg}}$, for $δ_{\text{alg}}$ a threshold depending on the data distribution, and is impossible (within a certain class of algorithms) below $δ_{\text{alg}}$. Here we derive an analogous threshold $δ_{\text{NN}}$ for two-layer networks. Our characterization of $δ_{\text{NN}}$ opens the way to study the dependence of learning dynamics on the network architecture and training algorithm.
  The threshold $δ_{\text{NN}}$ is determined by the following scenario. Training first visits points for which the gradient of the empirical risk is large and learns the directions spanned by these gradients. Then the gradient becomes smaller and the dynamics becomes dominated by negative directions of the Hessian. The threshold $δ_{\text{NN}}$ corresponds to a phase transition in the spectrum of the Hessian in this second phase.

AI.tldr: Introduces a neural-network–specific learning threshold δ_NN for two-layer nets in a multi-index model; learning is feasible above the threshold and undergoes a Hessian-spectrum phase transition, extending prior algorithmic-threshold results.
AI.motivation: To understand when gradient-based learning of latent low-dimensional structure is feasible for two-layer networks under proportional asymptotics, and how network architecture and training affect learnability.
AI.method: Asymptotic analysis of gradient descent dynamics for two-layer networks with n,d → ∞, n/d → δ, while the latent dimension k and hidden units m are fixed; study the gradient and Hessian evolution and identify a phase transition in the Hessian spectrum that defines δ_NN.
AI.result: Derivation of δ_NN as a threshold separating learnable vs. non-learnable regimes for two-layer nets; learning proceeds in two phases—first aligning with gradient directions, then governed by negative Hessian directions; δ_NN corresponds to a spectral phase transition.
AI.conclusion: Provides a framework to link learning dynamics to network architecture and training algorithm, enabling analysis of how architectural choices influence the effective learnability threshold in latent-variable models.

title: Learning to Decode Against Compositional Hallucination in Video Multimodal Large Language Models
summary: Current research on video hallucination mitigation primarily focuses on isolated error types, leaving compositional hallucinations, arising from incorrect reasoning over multiple interacting spatial and temporal factors largely underexplored. We introduce OmniVCHall, a benchmark designed to systematically evaluate both isolated and compositional hallucinations in video multimodal large language models (VLLMs). OmniVCHall spans diverse video domains, introduces a novel camera-based hallucination type, and defines a fine-grained taxonomy, together with adversarial answer options (e.g., "All are correct" and "None of the above") to prevent shortcut reasoning. The evaluations of 39 representative VLLMs reveal that even advanced models (e.g., Qwen3-VL and GPT-5) exhibit substantial performance degradation. We propose TriCD, a contrastive decoding framework with a triple-pathway calibration mechanism. An adaptive perturbation controller dynamically selects distracting operations to construct negative video variants, while a saliency-guided enhancement module adaptively reinforces grounded token-wise visual evidences. These components are optimized via reinforcement learning to encourage precise decision-making under compositional hallucination settings. Experimental results show that TriCD consistently improves performance across two representative backbones, achieving an average accuracy improvement of over 10%. The data and code can be find at https://github.com/BMRETURN/OmniVCHall.

AI.tldr: OmniVCHall provides a fine-grained benchmark for isolated and compositional video hallucinations in VLLMs, and TriCD is a RL-optimized contrastive decoding approach that improves grounding with adaptive perturbations and saliency guidance.
AI.motivation: Isolated error-focused research in video hallucination mitigation underexplores compositional hallucinations that arise from interacting spatial-temporal factors in video multimodal LLMs.
AI.method: Introduce OmniVCHall (taxonomy, camera-based hallucination type, adversarial answer options) to evaluate 39 VLLMs across diverse video domains; propose TriCD with a triple-pathway calibration: (1) adaptive perturbation controller to generate negative video variants; (2) saliency-guided enhancement to reinforce token-wise visual evidence; (3) reinforcement learning to optimize for precise decision-making under compositional hallucinations.
AI.result: TriCD yields average accuracy gains >10% across two backbones; OmniVCHall reveals substantial degradation even in advanced models (e.g., Qwen3-VL, GPT-5); dataset and code released.
AI.conclusion: The benchmark and TriCD provide a systematic evaluation and practical mitigation for compositional hallucinations in video-language models, with implications for broader adoption and future research; data and code available at the linked repository.

title: Theoretical Analysis of Measure Consistency Regularization for Partially Observed Data
summary:
The problem of corrupted data, missing features, or missing modalities continues to plague the modern machine learning landscape. To address this issue, a class of regularization methods that enforce consistency between imputed and fully observed data has emerged as a promising approach for improving model generalization, particularly in partially observed settings. We refer to this class of methods as Measure Consistency Regularization (MCR). Despite its empirical success in various applications, such as image inpainting, data imputation and semi-supervised learning, a fundamental understanding of the theoretical underpinnings of MCR remains limited. This paper bridges this gap by offering theoretical insights into why, when, and how MCR enhances imputation quality under partial observability, viewed through the lens of neural network distance.
  Our theoretical analysis identifies the term responsible for MCR's generalization advantage and extends to the imperfect training regime, demonstrating that this advantage is not always guaranteed. Guided by these insights, we propose a novel training protocol that monitors the duality gap to determine an early stopping point that preserves the generalization benefit. We then provide detailed empirical evidence to support our theoretical claims and to show the effectiveness and accuracy of our proposed stopping condition. We further provide a set of real-world data simulations to show the versatility of MCR under different model architectures designed for different data sources.

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes Measure Consistency Regularization (MCR) for imputation under partial observability, identifies the regularization term that yields generalization gains, shows these gains may fail under imperfect training, and proposes a duality-gap-based early stopping rule, supported by theory and extensive experiments across architectures and data sources.
AI.motivation: Corrupted/missing data and modalities hamper ML models; there is a lack of theoretical understanding of why and when MCR improves imputation and generalization.
AI.method: The authors conduct a theoretical analysis using neural-network distance to identify the term in MCR responsible for generalization. They extend the analysis to imperfect training, and propose a practical stopping rule based on monitoring the duality gap to preserve the generalization benefits. They validate claims with detailed empirical experiments and real-world data simulations across different model architectures and data sources.
AI.result: Identification of the MCR term that drives generalization; demonstration that the generalization advantage is not guaranteed under imperfect training; a duality-gap-based early stopping protocol that preserves the benefit; empirical evidence supporting both the theory and the stopping rule; demonstration of MCR’s versatility across architectures and data sources.
AI.conclusion: Theoretical insights explain when MCR helps and when it does not, and provide a practical training protocol (duality-gap monitoring) to maintain its benefits. The work substantiates MCR’s generalization advantages and showcases its applicability across diverse data types and model families.

title: TQL: Scaling Q-Functions with Transformers by Preventing Attention Collapse
summary: Despite scale driving substantial recent advancements in machine learning, reinforcement learning (RL) methods still primarily use small value functions. Naively scaling value functions -- including with a transformer architecture, which is known to be highly scalable -- often results in learning instability and worse performance. In this work, we ask what prevents transformers from scaling effectively for value functions? Through empirical analysis, we identify the critical failure mode in this scaling: attention scores collapse as capacity increases. Our key insight is that we can effectively prevent this collapse and stabilize training by controlling the entropy of the attention scores, thereby enabling the use of larger models. To this end, we propose Transformer Q-Learning (TQL), a method that unlocks the scaling potential of transformers in learning value functions in RL. Our approach yields up to a 43% improvement in performance when scaling from the smallest to the largest network sizes, while prior methods suffer from performance degradation.

AI.tldr: Attention score collapse hinders scaling of transformers for RL value functions; controlling attention entropy via Transformer Q-Learning (TQL) stabilizes training and enables larger models, yielding substantial gains.
AI.motivation: To understand why transformer-based value-function approximators struggle to scale in reinforcement learning and to identify mechanisms that maintain stability and improve performance at larger capacities.
AI.method: Conduct empirical analysis of attention score behavior as model capacity increases; introduce entropy control over attention scores to prevent collapse; propose Transformer Q-Learning (TQL) to enable stable large-scale transformer value-function learning in RL.
AI.result: Scaling from small to large networks yields up to 43% performance improvement with TQL, whereas previous approaches degrade with scale.
AI.conclusion: Entropy-regularized attention enables scalable transformer-based value functions in RL; TQL unlocks the scalable potential of transformers for value estimation and evaluation in reinforcement learning.

title: MAUGen: A Unified Diffusion Approach for Multi-Identity Facial Expression and AU Label Generation
summary: The lack of large-scale, demographically diverse face images with precise Action Unit (AU) occurrence and intensity annotations has long been recognized as a fundamental bottleneck in developing generalizable AU recognition systems. In this paper, we propose MAUGen, a diffusion-based multi-modal framework that jointly generates a large collection of photorealistic facial expressions and anatomically consistent AU labels, including both occurrence and intensity, conditioned on a single descriptive text prompt. Our MAUGen involves two key modules: (1) a Multi-modal Representation Learning (MRL) module that captures the relationships among the paired textual description, facial identity, expression image, and AU activations within a unified latent space; and (2) a Diffusion-based Image label Generator (DIG) that decodes the joint representation into aligned facial image-label pairs across diverse identities. Under this framework, we introduce Multi-Identity Facial Action (MIFA), a large-scale multimodal synthetic dataset featuring comprehensive AU annotations and identity variations. Extensive experiments demonstrate that MAUGen outperforms existing methods in synthesizing photorealistic, demographically diverse facial images along with semantically aligned AU labels.

AI.tldr: Diffusion-based MAUGen jointly generates photorealistic facial expressions and AU labels with a new MIFA dataset.
AI.motivation: Address the shortage of large-scale, diverse, accurately annotated AU data for generalizable AU recognition by enabling synthetic data generation with aligned image-label pairs.
AI.method: Two-module framework: (1) Multi-modal Representation Learning (MRL) learns a unified latent space across text, identity, image, and AU activations; (2) Diffusion-based Image label Generator (DIG) decodes the joint representation into aligned image-label pairs conditioned on a text prompt, including AU occurrence and intensity.
AI.result: Outperforms existing methods in generating photorealistic, demographically diverse facial images with semantically aligned AU labels; introduces MIFA, a large synthetic multimodal dataset with comprehensive AU annotations and identity variation.
AI.conclusion: MAUGen provides a scalable pipeline to synthesize labeled facial data for AU recognition; MIFA offers a valuable benchmark for evaluating AU-sensitive generative and recognition tasks; the approach integrates cross-modal representation with diffusion-based generation to achieve aligned image-label synthesis.

title: The Gradient-Causal Gap: Why Gradient Importance Fails on Complex Tasks
comment: 8 pages, 4 figures. Submitted to the ICLR 2026 Workshop on Latent & Implicit Thinking (LIT). Code:https://anonymous.4open.science/r/ICLR_2026_LIT-workshop_CG-D42B
summary: Removing ''important'' high-gradient components from a neural network can improve generalization, while removing unimportant'' low-gradient components can destroy it. We demonstrate this paradox by formalizing the \textit{Gradient-Causal Gap} in Transformers trained on algorithmic tasks. While gradient magnitude and causal importance align on simple tasks ($ρ=0.73$ for reversal), this relationship collapses as task complexity increases ($ρ=0.32$ for sorting), sometimes becoming inverted ($ρ=-0.11$). Pruning experiments reveal that gradient magnitude is not merely inaccurate but \textit{unpredictably} so. Removing low-gradient ''Hidden Heroes'' consistently devastates OOD accuracy ($-32\%$). Removing high-gradient ''Gradient Bloats'' is a coin flip: harmless in most seeds (indicating optimization noise), catastrophic in others (indicating overfitting circuits). This unpredictability means gradient-based pruning cannot reliably preserve model capabilities.

AI.tldr: Gradient magnitude is not a stable proxy for component importance in transformer pruning: high-gradient components can be dispensable or harmful, and low-gradient components (Hidden Heroes) can be essential; the Gradient-Causal Gap worsens with task complexity, yielding unpredictable pruning outcomes and undermining generalization.
AI.motivation: To test whether gradient-based pruning aligns with causal importance in neural networks, especially transformers on algorithmic tasks, and to understand how this affects generalization and out-of-distribution (OOD) performance.
AI.method: Formalize and measure the Gradient-Causal Gap across tasks of varying complexity. Quantify alignment between gradient magnitude and causal importance (e.g., correlations like ρ on reversal vs. sorting). Conduct pruning experiments targeting low- and high-gradient components and assess impacts on in-distribution (ID) and OOD accuracy, revealing optimization noise vs. overfitting dynamics.
AI.result: On simple tasks, gradient magnitude aligns with causal importance (ρ=0.73 for reversal). As task complexity grows, alignment collapses (ρ=0.32 for sorting) and may invert (ρ=-0.11). Pruning low-gradient components (Hidden Heroes) consistently reduces OOD accuracy by about 32 percentage points. Pruning high-gradient components (Gradient Bloats) yields seed-dependent effects: often harmless (due to optimization noise) but occasionally catastrophic (overfitting circuits). Overall, gradient-based pruning is unreliable for preserving model capabilities.
AI.conclusion: Gradient-based pruning cannot reliably preserve model capabilities, especially on complex tasks. Gradient magnitude is an unstable proxy for importance; alternative criteria—such as causal attribution, perturbation-based importance, or task-aware pruning—are needed to ensure robust generalization and predictable pruning outcomes.

title: A Meta-Knowledge-Augmented LLM Framework for Hyperparameter Optimization in Time-Series Forecasting
summary: Hyperparameter optimization (HPO) plays a central role in the performance of deep learning models, yet remains computationally expensive and difficult to interpret, particularly for time-series forecasting. While Bayesian Optimization (BO) is a standard approach, it typically treats tuning tasks independently and provides limited insight into its decisions. Recent advances in large language models (LLMs) offer new opportunities to incorporate structured prior knowledge and reasoning into optimization pipelines. We introduce LLM-AutoOpt, a hybrid HPO framework that combines BO with LLM-based contextual reasoning. The framework encodes dataset meta-features, model descriptions, historical optimization outcomes, and target objectives as structured meta-knowledge within LLM prompts, using BO to initialize the search and mitigate cold-start effects. This design enables context-aware and stable hyperparameter refinement while exposing the reasoning behind optimization decisions. Experiments on a multivariate time series forecasting benchmark demonstrate that LLM-AutoOpt achieves improved predictive performance and more interpretable optimization behavior compared to BO and LLM baselines without meta-knowledge.

AI.tldr: Hybrid HPO framework LLM-AutoOpt combines Bayesian Optimization with LLM-driven contextual reasoning to leverage dataset/meta-features and past optimization outcomes for time-series forecasting, improving performance and interpretability.
AI.motivation: HPO for time-series forecasting is computationally expensive and hard to interpret; BO alone treats tasks independently and provides limited insight into decisions.
AI.method: Encode dataset meta-features, model descriptions, historical optimization outcomes, and targets as structured meta-knowledge in LLM prompts; use BO to initialize the search and mitigate cold-start; LLM-driven reasoning guides hyperparameter refinement.
AI.result: On a multivariate time-series forecasting benchmark, LLM-AutoOpt achieves better predictive performance and more interpretable optimization behavior than BO and LLM baselines that lack meta-knowledge.
AI.conclusion: Integrating BO with LLM-based contextual reasoning and meta-knowledge yields context-aware, stable hyperparameter tuning with enhanced interpretability for time-series forecasting.

title: Provable Cooperative Multi-Agent Exploration for Reward-Free MDPs
summary:
We study cooperative multi-agent reinforcement learning in the setting of reward-free exploration, where multiple agents jointly explore an unknown MDP in order to learn its dynamics (without observing rewards). We focus on a tabular finite-horizon MDP and adopt a phased learning framework. In each learning phase, multiple agents independently interact with the environment. More specifically, in each learning phase, each agent is assigned a policy, executes it, and observes the resulting trajectory. Our primary goal is to characterize the tradeoff between the number of learning phases and the number of agents, especially when the number of learning phases is small.
  Our results identify a sharp transition governed by the horizon $H$. When the number of learning phases equals $H$, we present a computationally efficient algorithm that uses only $\tilde{O}(S^6 H^6 A / ε^2)$ agents to obtain an $ε$ approximation of the dynamics (i.e., yields an $ε$-optimal policy for any reward function). We complement our algorithm with a lower bound showing that any algorithm restricted to $ρ< H$ phases requires at least $A^{H/ρ}$ agents to achieve constant accuracy. Thus, we show that it is essential to have an order of $H$ learning phases if we limit the number of agents to be polynomial.

AI.tldr: Identifies a phase/agent tradeoff in reward-free, cooperative multi-agent RL for tabular finite-horizon MDPs. Finds a sharp transition at the horizon H: with ρ = H phases, a poly-sized agent count suffices to learn dynamics (ε-approx); with ρ < H phases, any algorithm needs exponentially many agents (A^{H/ρ}).
AI.motivation: Clarify fundamental sample-efficiency limits in reward-free, cooperative multi-agent exploration and quantify how the number of learning phases and agents trade off, especially around the horizon.
AI.method: Phased learning with multiple agents per phase; each agent executes a fixed policy and collects trajectories; analysis yields upper bound for ρ = H with a polynomial-agent algorithm and a matching lower bound for ρ < H showing exponential agent requirements.
AI.result: Upper bound: with ρ = H, O~(S^6 H^6 A / ε^2) agents suffice to obtain ε-approximation of dynamics (hence ε-optimal policy for any reward). Lower bound: for ρ < H, any algorithm requires at least A^{H/ρ} agents for constant accuracy.
AI.conclusion: The number of learning phases must scale ~H to keep agent counts polynomial; the tradeoff is sharp and intrinsic, delineating regimes where efficient reward-free dynamics learning is possible versus infeasible without an exponential agent budget.

title: Modeling Topological Impact on Node Attribute Distributions in Attributed Graphs
summary:
We investigate how the topology of attributed graphs influences the distribution of node attributes. This work offers a novel perspective by treating topology and attributes as structurally distinct but interacting components. We introduce an algebraic approach that combines a graph's topology with the probability distribution of node attributes, resulting in topology-influenced distributions. First, we develop a categorical framework to formalize how a node perceives the graph's topology. We then quantify this point of view and integrate it with the distribution of node attributes to capture topological effects. We interpret these topology-conditioned distributions as approximations of the posteriors $P(\cdot \mid v)$ and $P(\cdot \mid \mathcal{G})$.
  We further establish a principled sufficiency condition by showing that, on complete graphs, where topology carries no informative structure, our construction recovers the original attribute distribution. To evaluate our approach, we introduce an intentionally simple testbed model, $\textbf{ID}$, and use unsupervised graph anomaly detection as a probing task.

AI.tldr: Algebraic-categorical framework to model topology-conditioned distributions of node attributes on graphs, linking topology and attribute distributions; introduces sufficiency result on complete graphs and evaluates with a simple testbed model for unsupervised anomaly detection.
AI.motivation: Understand how graph topology influences attribute distributions by treating topology and attributes as distinct but interacting components, and formalize their interplay.
AI.method: Develop a categorical framework to formalize how a node perceives the graph's topology, quantify this viewpoint, and integrate it with the attribute distribution to produce topology-influenced distributions; interpret these as approximations to posteriors P(·|v) and P(·|G); establish a sufficiency condition; propose a simple testbed model ID; apply unsupervised graph anomaly detection as evaluation.
AI.result: On complete graphs, topology carries no informative structure and the construction recovers the original attribute distribution; a principled sufficiency condition is established; a simple testbed model ID and unsupervised anomaly detection are used for evaluation.
AI.conclusion: The framework provides a principled way to quantify and leverage topology-conditioned attribute distributions, yielding a sufficiency criterion and a minimal testbed for evaluation, with relevance to anomaly detection.

title: Rectified LpJEPA: Joint-Embedding Predictive Architectures with Sparse and Maximum-Entropy Representations
summary: Joint-Embedding Predictive Architectures (JEPA) learn view-invariant representations and admit projection-based distribution matching for collapse prevention. Existing approaches regularize representations towards isotropic Gaussian distributions, but inherently favor dense representations and fail to capture the key property of sparsity observed in efficient representations. We introduce Rectified Distribution Matching Regularization (RDMReg), a sliced two-sample distribution-matching loss that aligns representations to a Rectified Generalized Gaussian (RGG) distribution. RGG enables explicit control over expected $\ell_0$ norm through rectification, while preserving maximum-entropy up to rescaling under expected $\ell_p$ norm constraints. Equipping JEPAs with RDMReg yields Rectified LpJEPA, which strictly generalizes prior Gaussian-based JEPAs. Empirically, Rectified LpJEPA learns sparse, non-negative representations with favorable sparsity-performance trade-offs and competitive downstream performance on image classification benchmarks, demonstrating that RDMReg effectively enforces sparsity while preserving task-relevant information.

AI.tldr: Proposes Rectified Distribution Matching Regularization (RDMReg) for Joint-Embedding Predictive Architectures (JEPA) to induce sparsity via a Rectified Generalized Gaussian (RGG); yields Rectified LpJEPA, which generalizes Gaussian-based JEPA and achieves sparse, non-negative representations with competitive performance.
AI.motivation: Standard JEPA approaches regularize to isotropic Gaussian priors, promoting dense representations and failing to capture observed sparsity in efficient representations. A controllable sparsity prior via a rectified distribution could improve efficiency without sacrificing task performance.
AI.method: Introduce RDMReg, a sliced two-sample distribution-matching loss that aligns representations to an RG Gaussian distribution. The Rectified Generalized Gaussian model enables explicit control of the expected L0 norm through rectification while maintaining maximum entropy under Lp-norm constraints. Combine with JEPA to form Rectified LpJEPA, a generalization of prior Gaussian-based JEPA methods.
AI.result: Empirically, Rectified LpJEPA learns sparse, non-negative representations with favorable sparsity-performance trade-offs and competitive downstream performance on image classification benchmarks, indicating that RDMReg effectively enforces sparsity while preserving task-relevant information.
AI.conclusion: Rectified LpJEPA generalizes Gaussian-based JEPA and demonstrates that RDMReg can enforce sparsity without harming discriminative power; RGG-based modeling provides explicit sparsity control within distribution-mMatching for self-supervised learning.

title: Non-Contrastive Vision-Language Learning with Predictive Embedding Alignment
summary: Vision-language models have transformed multimodal representation learning, yet dominant contrastive approaches like CLIP require large batch sizes, careful negative sampling, and extensive hyperparameter tuning. We introduce NOVA, a NOn-contrastive Vision-language Alignment framework based on joint embedding prediction with distributional regularization. NOVA aligns visual representations to a frozen, domain-specific text encoder by predicting text embeddings from augmented image views, while enforcing an isotropic Gaussian structure via Sketched Isotropic Gaussian Regularization (SIGReg). This eliminates the need for negative sampling, momentum encoders, or stop-gradients, reducing the training objective to a single hyperparameter. We evaluate NOVA on zeroshot chest X-ray classification using ClinicalBERT as the text encoder and Vision Transformers trained from scratch on MIMIC-CXR. On zero-shot classification across three benchmark datasets, NOVA outperforms multiple standard baselines while exhibiting substantially more consistent training runs. Our results demonstrate that non-contrastive vision-language pretraining offers a simpler, more stable, and more effective alternative to contrastive methods.

AI.tldr: NOVA is a non-contrastive vision-language pretraining method that aligns augmented image views to a frozen text encoder using a distributional regularizer, eliminating negatives and momentum encoders, and achieving strong zero-shot medical image classification with stable training.
AI.motivation: Overcome the drawbacks of contrastive methods (e.g., CLIP) that require large batch sizes, careful negative sampling, and extensive hyperparameter tuning, by offering a simpler, more stable alternative.
AI.method: NOVA predicts text embeddings from augmented image views while keeping a fixed domain-specific text encoder. It imposes an isotropic Gaussian structure on the visual representations via Sketched Isotropic Gaussian Regularization (SIGReg). This non-contrastive setup removes the need for negative samples, momentum encoders, or stop-gradients, reducing training to a single hyperparameter.
AI.result: Evaluated on zero-shot chest X-ray classification using ClinicalBERT as the text encoder and Vision Transformers trained from scratch on MIMIC-CXR. Across three benchmark datasets, NOVA outperforms standard baselines and exhibits substantially more consistent training runs.
AI.conclusion: Non-contrastive vision-language pretraining with distributional regularization offers a simpler, more stable, and effective alternative to contrastive methods, with strong performance in domain-specific zero-shot tasks.

title: A Statistical Theory of Gated Attention through the Lens of Hierarchical Mixture of Experts
comment: Viet Nguyen, Tuan Minh Pham, and Thinh Cao contributed equally to this work
summary: Self-attention has greatly contributed to the success of the widely used Transformer architecture by enabling learning from data with long-range dependencies. In an effort to improve performance, a gated attention model that leverages a gating mechanism within the multi-head self-attention has recently been proposed as a promising alternative. Gated attention has been empirically demonstrated to increase the expressiveness of low-rank mapping in standard attention and even to eliminate the attention sink phenomenon. Despite its efficacy, a clear theoretical understanding of gated attention's benefits remains lacking in the literature. To close this gap, we rigorously show that each entry in a gated attention matrix or a multi-head self-attention matrix can be written as a hierarchical mixture of experts. By recasting learning as an expert estimation problem, we demonstrate that gated attention is more sample-efficient than multi-head self-attention. In particular, while the former needs only a polynomial number of data points to estimate an expert, the latter requires exponentially many data points to achieve the same estimation error. Furthermore, our analysis also provides a theoretical justification for why gated attention yields higher performance when a gate is placed at the output of the scaled dot product attention or the value map rather than at other positions in the multi-head self-attention architecture.

AI.tldr: Gated attention entries are characterized as hierarchical mixtures of experts, yielding better sample efficiency than standard multi-head self-attention; analysis also explains why gating at certain points (output or value map) improves performance.
AI.motivation: To provide a theoretical understanding of why gated attention enhances expressiveness and data efficiency, addressing the lack of theory in prior empirical work.
AI.method: The authors decompose each attention entry into a hierarchical mixture of experts, recast learning as expert estimation, and compare sample complexities between gated attention and standard multi-head self-attention; they also analyze the impact of gate placement within the attention architecture.
AI.result: Gated attention entries can be expressed as hierarchical mixtures of experts. Gated attention is more sample-efficient: polynomial data suffices to accurately estimate an expert, whereas MHSA requires exponential data for the same error. Gate placement at the output of scaled dot-product attention or the value map is theoretically justified for improved performance.
AI.conclusion: The work provides a rigorous theoretical justification for gated attention’s data efficiency and informs architectural choices, particularly regarding where to place gating within multi-head self-attention.

title: Improving Neuropathological Reconstruction Fidelity via AI Slice Imputation
comment: 12 pages of main content, 5 pages of supplement
summary: Neuropathological analyses benefit from spatially precise volumetric reconstructions that enhance anatomical delineation and improve morphometric accuracy. Our prior work has shown the feasibility of reconstructing 3D brain volumes from 2D dissection photographs. However these outputs sometimes exhibit coarse, overly smooth reconstructions of structures, especially under high anisotropy (i.e., reconstructions from thick slabs). Here, we introduce a computationally efficient super-resolution step that imputes slices to generate anatomically consistent isotropic volumes from anisotropic 3D reconstructions of dissection photographs. By training on domain-randomized synthetic data, we ensure that our method generalizes across dissection protocols and remains robust to large slab thicknesses. The imputed volumes yield improved automated segmentations, achieving higher Dice scores, particularly in cortical and white matter regions. Validation on surface reconstruction and atlas registration tasks demonstrates more accurate cortical surfaces and MRI registration. By enhancing the resolution and anatomical fidelity of photograph-based reconstructions, our approach strengthens the bridge between neuropathology and neuroimaging. Our method is publicly available at https://surfer.nmr.mgh.harvard.edu/fswiki/mri_3d_photo_recon

AI.tldr: A domain-randomized super-resolution imputation method converts anisotropic, 2D-photo-based neuropathology reconstructions into isotropic 3D volumes, yielding improved segmentation and registration and enabling broader neuroimaging integration.
AI.motivation: Resolve coarse, overly smoothed reconstructions from thick slabs and anisotropic data to enable accurate morphometrics and alignment with MRI/atlas data.
AI.method: A computationally efficient super-resolution step imputes slices to generate anatomically consistent isotropic volumes from anisotropic 3D reconstructions derived from dissection photographs. The model is trained on domain-randomized synthetic data to ensure generalization across dissection protocols and robustness to large slab thicknesses.
AI.result: Imputed volumes yield higher Dice scores, particularly in cortical and white matter regions, and validation shows more accurate cortical surfaces and MRI registration. The approach improves resolution and anatomical fidelity of photograph-based reconstructions.
AI.conclusion: This method strengthens the bridge between neuropathology and neuroimaging by enabling isotropic, high-fidelity reconstructions from photo-based datasets; the tool is publicly available at the provided URL.

title: P-EAGLE: Parallel-Drafting EAGLE with Scalable Training
summary: Reasoning LLMs produce longer outputs, requiring speculative decoding drafters trained on extended sequences. Parallel drafting - predicting multiple tokens per forward pass - offers latency benefits over sequential generation, but training complexity scales quadratically with the product of sequence length and parallel positions, rendering long-context training impractical. We present P(arallel)-EAGLE, which transforms EAGLE from autoregressive to parallel multi-token prediction via a learnable shared hidden state. To scale training to long contexts, we develop a framework featuring attention mask pre-computation and sequence partitioning techniques, enabling gradient accumulation within individual sequences for parallel-prediction training. We implement P-EAGLE in vLLM and demonstrate speedups of 1.10-1.36x over autoregressive EAGLE-3 across GPT-OSS 120B, 20B, and Qwen3-Coder 30B.

AI.tldr: Parallel multi-token prediction via P-EAGLE converts EAGLE from autoregressive to a shared-hidden-state framework, enabling long-context training with attention-mask pre-computation and sequence partitioning; yields 1.10–1.36x speedups over autoregressive EAGLE-3 on GPT-OSS models in vLLM.
AI.motivation: Long-context LLM reasoning produces longer outputs; speculative decoding requires training on extended sequences. Training parallel-prediction scales quadratically with sequence length and parallel positions, making long-context training impractical. A scalable, latency-efficient method is needed for parallel drafting.
AI.method: Introduce P-EAGLE to transform autoregressive EAGLE into parallel multi-token prediction using a learnable shared hidden state. Develop a training framework with attention-mask pre-computation and sequence-partitioning, enabling gradient accumulation within single sequences for parallel-prediction training. Implement P-EAGLE in vLLM.
AI.result: Demonstrates speedups of 1.10–1.36x over autoregressive EAGLE-3 on GPT-OSS 120B, 20B, and Qwen3-Coder 30B.
AI.conclusion: P-EAGLE enables scalable long-context parallel-prediction training and inference, achieving notable latency gains and validating a viable approach for fast, long-context drafting in large models.

title: Rod Flow: A Continuous-Time Model for Gradient Descent at the Edge of Stability
summary: How can we understand gradient-based training over non-convex landscapes? The edge of stability phenomenon, introduced in Cohen et al. (2021), indicates that the answer is not so simple: namely, gradient descent (GD) with large step sizes often diverges away from the gradient flow. In this regime, the "Central Flow", recently proposed in Cohen et al. (2025), provides an accurate ODE approximation to the GD dynamics over many architectures. In this work, we propose Rod Flow, an alternative ODE approximation, which carries the following advantages: (1) it rests on a principled derivation stemming from a physical picture of GD iterates as an extended one-dimensional object -- a "rod"; (2) it better captures GD dynamics for simple toy examples and matches the accuracy of Central Flow for representative neural network architectures, and (3) is explicit and cheap to compute. Theoretically, we prove that Rod Flow correctly predicts the critical sharpness threshold and explains self-stabilization in quartic potentials. We validate our theory with a range of numerical experiments.

AI.tldr: Rod Flow offers a principled, explicit ODE approximation for gradient descent in non-convex landscapes, deriving from a rod-like picture of iterates; it matches Central Flow in accuracy, is cheaper to compute, and provides theoretical guarantees on sharpness thresholds and self-stabilization, backed by numerical validation.
AI.motivation: To understand gradient-based training beyond gradient flow under large step sizes, where gradient descent can diverge from the gradient flow due to edge-of-stability phenomena; to develop a tractable, interpretable ODE model with theoretical guarantees.
AI.method: Derive Rod Flow from modeling GD iterates as an extended one-dimensional object ('rod'); compare its dynamics to Central Flow across simple toy examples and representative neural network architectures; provide theoretical proofs for the critical sharpness threshold and self-stabilization in quartic potentials; conduct numerical experiments to validate predictions.
AI.result: Rod Flow captures GD dynamics with accuracy comparable to Central Flow on representative architectures; it is explicit and cheap to compute; it correctly predicts the critical sharpness threshold and explains self-stabilization phenomena in quartic potentials; numerical experiments corroborate the theory.
AI.conclusion: Rod Flow is a principled, efficient ODE approximation for large-step GD in non-convex landscapes, offering theoretical insights and empirical validation, and serving as a practical alternative to existing flow approximations like Central Flow.

title: Causal Preference Elicitation
summary: We propose causal preference elicitation, a Bayesian framework for expert-in-the-loop causal discovery that actively queries local edge relations to concentrate a posterior over directed acyclic graphs (DAGs). From any black-box observational posterior, we model noisy expert judgments with a three-way likelihood over edge existence and direction. Posterior inference uses a flexible particle approximation, and queries are selected by an efficient expected information gain criterion on the expert's categorical response. Experiments on synthetic graphs, protein signaling data, and a human gene perturbation benchmark show faster posterior concentration and improved recovery of directed effects under tight query budgets.

AI.tldr: A Bayesian, expert-in-the-loop framework for causal discovery that actively queries local edges to concentrate the posterior over DAGs, using a three-way edge likelihood and information-gain queries; demonstrates faster concentration and better directed-edge recovery under tight query budgets.
