VEQ combines two strategies: (1) Modality-expert-aware Quantization that prioritizes error minimization for influential experts based on activation frequency, and (2) Modality-affinity-aware Quantization that builds an enhanced Hessian by incorporating token-expert affinity and modality information to guide calibration. It achieves notable accuracy gains over SOTA PTQ baselines on Kimi-VL (2.04%) and Qwen3-VL (3.09%) under W3A16, demonstrating robustness across multimodal tasks; code to be released.
AI.motivation: Mixture-of-Experts Vision-Language Models (MoE VLMs) deliver high performance but incur large memory/computation costs. Post-Training Quantization (PTQ) is attractive since it is training-free, but existing quantization methods fail to account for two key heterogeneities: (i) the intrinsic discrepancy between vision and language tokens (cross-modal differences), and (ii) non-uniform contribution of different experts (expert heterogeneity). A method that jointly addresses these two aspects could enable more efficient deployment without retraining.
AI.method: VEQ introduces a dual-aware PTQ framework: (1) Modality-expert-aware Quantization uses expert activation frequency to prioritize error minimization for the most pivotal experts, effectively allocating quantization precision by expert importance; (2) Modality-affinity-aware Quantization builds an enhanced Hessian by integrating token-expert affinity with modality information, guiding calibration to preserve important cross-modal and expert interactions. The approach operates in a post-training, quantization setting without requiring fine-tuning.
AI.result: Extensive experiments across diverse multimodal benchmarks show VEQ consistently outperforms state-of-the-art PTQ baselines. Under the W3A16 configuration, VEQ achieves average accuracy gains of 2.04% on Kimi-VL and 3.09% on Qwen3-VL compared to prior SOTA quantization methods, indicating strong robustness across multimodal tasks.
AI.conclusion: VEQ effectively addresses both cross-modal token discrepancies and MoE expert heterogeneity in a training-free PTQ framework, enabling more accurate quantization of MoE VLMs. The results suggest strong generalization across multimodal tasks, with substantial gains over existing baselines. Code will be available at the provided repository.

title: Toward Enhancing Representation Learning in Federated Multi-Task Settings
comment: This paper has been accepted at ICLR 2026
summary: Federated multi-task learning (FMTL) seeks to collaboratively train customized models for users with different tasks while preserving data privacy. Most existing approaches assume model congruity (i.e., the use of fully or partially homogeneous models) across users, which limits their applicability in realistic settings. To overcome this limitation, we aim to learn a shared representation space across tasks rather than shared model parameters. To this end, we propose Muscle loss, a novel contrastive learning objective that simultaneously aligns representations from all participating models. Unlike existing multi-view or multi-model contrastive methods, which typically align models pairwise, Muscle loss can effectively capture dependencies across tasks because its minimization is equivalent to the maximization of mutual information among all the models' representations. Building on this principle, we develop FedMuscle, a practical and communication-efficient FMTL algorithm that naturally handles both model and task heterogeneity. Experiments on diverse image and language tasks demonstrate that FedMuscle consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, delivering substantial improvements and robust performance across heterogeneous settings.

AI.tldr: FedMuscle enables federated multi-task learning with a model-heterogeneous setup by aligning a shared representation space via Muscle loss, outperforming baselines on image and language tasks.
AI.motivation: Addresses the limitations of assuming model congruity across users in federated multi-task learning; aims to learn a unified representation across heterogeneous models and tasks to improve personalization and collaboration while preserving privacy.
AI.method: Proposes Muscle loss, a contrastive objective that aligns representations from all participating models, effectively maximizing mutual information across all models' representations. Builds FedMuscle, a practical, communication-efficient FMTL algorithm that handles both model and task heterogeneity.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on diverse image and language tasks shows FedMuscle consistently surpasses state-of-the-art baselines, achieving substantial improvements and robust performance under heterogeneity.
AI.conclusion: Learning a shared representation space via Muscle loss is effective for federated multi-task learning with heterogeneous models, enabling better transfer and collaboration across tasks while maintaining privacy and communication efficiency.

title: COMET: Codebook-based Online-adaptive Multi-scale Embedding for Time-series Anomaly Detection
summary: Time series anomaly detection is a critical task across various industrial domains. However, capturing temporal dependencies and multivariate correlations within patch-level representation learning remains underexplored, and reliance on single-scale patterns limits the detection of anomalies across different temporal ranges. Furthermore, focusing on normal data representations makes models vulnerable to distribution shifts at inference time. To address these limitations, we propose Codebook-based Online-adaptive Multi-scale Embedding for Time-series anomaly detection (COMET), which consists of three key components: (1) Multi-scale Patch Encoding captures temporal dependencies and inter-variable correlations across multiple patch scales. (2) Vector-Quantized Coreset learns representative normal patterns via codebook and detects anomalies with a dual-score combining quantization error and memory distance. (3) Online Codebook Adaptation generates pseudo-labels based on codebook entries and dynamically adapts the model at inference through contrastive learning. Experiments on five benchmark datasets demonstrate that COMET achieves the best performance in 36 out of 45 evaluation metrics, validating its effectiveness across diverse environments.

AI.tldr: COMET proposes a three-part framework for time-series anomaly detection: (i) multi-scale patch encoding to capture temporal and inter-variable dependencies across scales; (ii) a vector-quantized coreset with a codebook to model normal patterns and a dual-score (quantization error + memory distance) for anomaly scoring; (iii) online codebook adaptation using pseudo-labels and contrastive learning to adapt at inference under distribution shifts. It achieves best performance on 36 of 45 metrics across five benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Capture temporal dependencies and multivariate correlations across multiple patch scales; overcome single-scale limitations; mitigate vulnerability to distribution shifts by moving beyond normal-data representations.
AI.method: Three components: (1) Multi-scale Patch Encoding to model temporal and inter-variable relations across multiple patch sizes; (2) Vector-Quantized Coreset via a codebook that represents normal patterns and a dual-score combining quantization error and memory-distance for anomaly detection; (3) Online Codebook Adaptation that generates pseudo-labels from codebook entries and uses contrastive learning to adapt the model at inference.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on five benchmark datasets shows COMET achieves the best performance in 36 out of 45 evaluation metrics, indicating robustness across diverse environments.
AI.conclusion: COMET effectively addresses multi-scale temporal dependencies and distribution-shift challenges in time-series anomaly detection, delivering strong performance across varied datasets through a codebook-based, online-adaptive, multi-scale framework.

title: Residual Decoding: Mitigating Hallucinations in Large Vision-Language Models via History-Aware Residual Guidance
summary: Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) can reason effectively from image-text inputs and perform well in various multimodal tasks. Despite this success, they are affected by language priors and often produce hallucinations. Hallucinations denote generated content that is grammatically and syntactically coherent, yet bears no match or direct relevance to actual visual input. To address this problem, we propose Residual Decoding (ResDec). It is a novel training-free method that uses historical information to aid decoding. The method relies on the internal implicit reasoning mechanism and token logits evolution mechanism of LVLMs to correct biases. Extensive experiments demonstrate that ResDec effectively suppresses hallucinations induced by language priors, significantly improves visual grounding, and reduces object hallucinations. In addition to mitigating hallucinations, ResDec also performs exceptionally well on comprehensive LVLM benchmarks, highlighting its broad applicability.

AI.tldr: ResDec is a training-free residual decoding method for LVLMs that leverages the model's internal reasoning and token-logit dynamics to suppress language-prior hallucinations, improve visual grounding, and reduce object hallucinations, while maintaining strong performance on LVLM benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Language priors in large vision-language models cause hallucinations and misalignment with visual input, reducing reliability in multimodal tasks.
AI.method: ResDec uses historical information and the LVLMs' implicit reasoning and token logit evolution during decoding to correct biases without additional training. It acts as a decoding-time residual correction mechanism.
AI.result: ResDec significantly reduces hallucinations caused by language priors, improves visual grounding, and lowers object hallucinations, while also delivering strong performance on comprehensive LVLM benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: ResDec is effective, broadly applicable across LVLM benchmarks, and offers a training-free approach to mitigating hallucinations by exploiting internal decoding dynamics.

title: Chance-Constrained Inference for Hallucination Risk Control in Large Language Models
summary: Large language models generate outputs stochastically and may produce fluent but invalid responses, including factual hallucinations. Existing mitigation strategies reduce average error rates but do not provide explicit control over the \emph{frequency} of such failures under repeated use. We formulate inference as a deployment-time risk control problem and introduce \emph{chance-constrained inference}, which directly bounds the probability of hallucinations among accepted generations. Hallucinations are modeled as stochastic constraint violations, and we show that confidence-based selective prediction does not, in general, imply probabilistic risk guarantees. To enforce chance constraints efficiently, we propose a sequential, anytime-valid inference procedure that adaptively certifies feasibility or infeasibility using finite samples, avoiding conservative fixed-sample bounds. Experiments on questions inspired by NaturalQuestions and controlled multi-hop question answering demonstrate reliable risk control, early detection of intrinsically infeasible inputs, and safe composition under repeated use, while confidence-based baselines fail to provide consistent guarantees.

AI.tldr: Proposes chance-constrained inference to bound hallucination frequency in LLM outputs via a sequential, anytime-valid procedure that certifies feasibility with finite samples, enabling deployment-time risk control.
AI.motivation: LLMs are stochastic and can hallucinate; existing mitigation reduces average error but does not guarantee a target risk level under repeated use.
AI.method: Model hallucinations as stochastic constraint violations; show confidence-based selective prediction lacks general probabilistic guarantees; introduce a sequential, anytime-valid inference procedure that adaptively certifies feasibility/infeasibility for a chance constraint using finite samples.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on NaturalQuestions-inspired QA and controlled multi-hop QA shows reliable risk control, early detection of intrinsically infeasible inputs, and safe reuse under repeated interactions; baseline confidence-based methods fail to provide consistent guarantees.
AI.conclusion: Enables deployment-time control of hallucination risk with probabilistic guarantees, offering safer long-run use of LLMs and a framework that can be extended to other risk-aware generation settings.

title: The Effect of Mini-Batch Noise on the Implicit Bias of Adam
summary: With limited high-quality data and growing compute, multi-epoch training is gaining back its importance across sub-areas of deep learning. Adam(W), versions of which are go-to optimizers for many tasks such as next token prediction, has two momentum hyperparameters $(β_1, β_2)$ controlling memory and one very important hyperparameter, batch size, controlling (in particular) the amount mini-batch noise. We introduce a theoretical framework to understand how mini-batch noise influences the implicit bias of memory in Adam (depending on $β_1$, $β_2$) towards sharper or flatter regions of the loss landscape, which is commonly observed to correlate with the generalization gap in multi-epoch training. We find that in the case of large batch sizes, higher $β_2$ increases the magnitude of anti-regularization by memory (hurting generalization), but as the batch size becomes smaller, the dependence of (anti-)regulariation on $β_2$ is reversed. A similar monotonicity shift (in the opposite direction) happens in $β_1$. In particular, the commonly "default" pair $(β_1, β_2) = (0.9, 0.999)$ is a good choice if batches are small; for larger batches, in many settings moving $β_1$ closer to $β_2$ is much better in terms of validation accuracy in multi-epoch training. Moreover, our theoretical derivations connect the scale of the batch size at which the shift happens to the scale of the critical batch size. We illustrate this effect in experiments with small-scale data in the about-to-overfit regime.

AI.tldr: A theoretical framework links mini-batch noise, Adam's memory (β1, β2), and generalization in multi-epoch training, showing batch-size–dependent shifts in how β1 and β2 affect implicit regularization toward sharp vs flat minima and validating with small-scale experiments.
AI.motivation: Understand how mini-batch noise influences the implicit bias of Adam's memory with respect to sharp/flat minima and generalization in multi-epoch training.
AI.method: Develop a theoretical framework analyzing the interaction between mini-batch noise and Adam's momentum hyperparameters (β1, β2); derive monotonicity shifts for these hyperparameters as batch size varies; relate shift scale to a critical batch size; corroborate with experiments on small-scale data in the near-overfitting regime.
AI.result: Large batch sizes: increasing β2 amplifies anti-regularization by memory, hurting generalization; as batch size decreases, this dependence reverses. β1 shows a similar opposite shift. Default (β1, β2) = (0.9, 0.999) is favorable for small batches; for large batches, moving β1 closer to β2 improves validation accuracy in multi-epoch training. The shift scale aligns with the critical batch size.
AI.conclusion: The framework explains how batch size modulates the impact of Adam’s momentum on implicit memory regularization and offers practical guidelines for selecting β1, β2 depending on batch size; validated on small-scale data in an about-to-overfit setting.

title: De Novo Molecular Generation from Mass Spectra via Many-Body Enhanced Diffusion
summary: Molecular structure generation from mass spectrometry is fundamental for understanding cellular metabolism and discovering novel compounds. Although tandem mass spectrometry (MS/MS) enables the high-throughput acquisition of fragment fingerprints, these spectra often reflect higher-order interactions involving the concerted cleavage of multiple atoms and bonds-crucial for resolving complex isomers and non-local fragmentation mechanisms. However, most existing methods adopt atom-centric and pairwise interaction modeling, overlooking higher-order edge interactions and lacking the capacity to systematically capture essential many-body characteristics for structure generation. To overcome these limitations, we present MBGen, a Many-Body enhanced diffusion framework for de novo molecular structure Generation from mass spectra. By integrating a many-body attention mechanism and higher-order edge modeling, MBGen comprehensively leverages the rich structural information encoded in MS/MS spectra, enabling accurate de novo generation and isomer differentiation for novel molecules. Experimental results on the NPLIB1 and MassSpecGym benchmarks demonstrate that MBGen achieves superior performance, with improvements of up to 230% over state-of-the-art methods, highlighting the scientific value and practical utility of many-body modeling for mass spectrometry-based molecular generation. Further analysis and ablation studies show that our approach effectively captures higher-order interactions and exhibits enhanced sensitivity to complex isomeric and non-local fragmentation information.

AI.tldr: MBGen is a diffusion-based de novo molecular structure generator from MS/MS data that uses many-body attention and higher-order edge modeling to capture complex fragmentation and isomer information, achieving large gains on standard benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Current MS/MS-based structure generation largely relies on atom-centric, pairwise interactions and fails to capture higher-order, many-body effects encoded in spectra, hindering isomer differentiation and accurate generation.
AI.method: MBGen introduces a many-body enhanced diffusion framework with a many-body attention module and higher-order edge modeling to exploit the rich structural information in MS/MS spectra for de novo generation and isomer differentiation.
AI.result: On NPLIB1 and MassSpecGym benchmarks MBGen achieves superior performance, with improvements up to 230% over state-of-the-art methods; ablations show effective capture of higher-order interactions and improved sensitivity to complex isomeric and non-local fragmentation information.
AI.conclusion: Many-body modeling substantially improves MS/MS-guided molecular structure generation, offering practical utility and scientific value for structure elucidation and discovery in metabolomics and natural products.

title: On the Spatiotemporal Dynamics of Generalization in Neural Networks
summary: Why do neural networks fail to generalize addition from 16-digit to 32-digit numbers, while a child who learns the rule can apply it to arbitrarily long sequences? We argue that this failure is not an engineering problem but a violation of physical postulates. Drawing inspiration from physics, we identify three constraints that any generalizing system must satisfy: (1) Locality -- information propagates at finite speed; (2) Symmetry -- the laws of computation are invariant across space and time; (3) Stability -- the system converges to discrete attractors that resist noise accumulation. From these postulates, we derive -- rather than design -- the Spatiotemporal Evolution with Attractor Dynamics (SEAD) architecture: a neural cellular automaton where local convolutional rules are iterated until convergence. Experiments on three tasks validate our theory: (1) Parity -- demonstrating perfect length generalization via light-cone propagation; (2) Addition -- achieving scale-invariant inference from L=16 to L=1 million with 100% accuracy, exhibiting input-adaptive computation; (3) Rule 110 -- learning a Turing-complete cellular automaton without trajectory divergence. Our results suggest that the gap between statistical learning and logical reasoning can be bridged -- not by scaling parameters, but by respecting the physics of computation.

AI.tldr: A physics-inspired neural cellular automaton (SEAD) enforces locality, symmetry, and stability to achieve robust long-sequence generalization and Turing-complete behavior, demonstrated on parity, addition, and Rule 110 tasks.
AI.motivation: Explain persistent generalization failure of standard neural nets in arithmetic-like tasks and propose fundamental, physics-like constraints that any generalizing system must satisfy.
AI.method: Introduce SEAD: a neural cellular automaton with local convolutional rules; iterates updates until convergence to discrete attractors. Train/test on three tasks: parity (length-generalization via light-cone propagation), addition (scale-invariant inference up to 1e6 with 100% accuracy and input-adaptive computation), and Rule 110 (learning a Turing-complete CA without trajectory divergence).
AI.result: Parity shows perfect length generalization due to finite-speed information propagation (light-cone). Addition achieves scale-invariant performance across huge lengths with 100% accuracy. Rule 110 learned as a CA without divergent trajectories, implying Turing-completeness.
AI.conclusion: Assertions that the gap between statistical learning and logical reasoning can be bridged by enforcing physics-inspired constraints rather than merely scaling parameters; SEAD provides a principled framework for robust, local-rule computation with attractor dynamics.

title: Efficient Adversarial Attacks on High-dimensional Offline Bandits
summary: Bandit algorithms have recently emerged as a powerful tool for evaluating machine learning models, including generative image models and large language models, by efficiently identifying top-performing candidates without exhaustive comparisons. These methods typically rely on a reward model, often distributed with public weights on platforms such as Hugging Face, to provide feedback to the bandit. While online evaluation is expensive and requires repeated trials, offline evaluation with logged data has become an attractive alternative. However, the adversarial robustness of offline bandit evaluation remains largely unexplored, particularly when an attacker perturbs the reward model (rather than the training data) prior to bandit training. In this work, we fill this gap by investigating, both theoretically and empirically, the vulnerability of offline bandit training to adversarial manipulations of the reward model. We introduce a novel threat model in which an attacker exploits offline data in high-dimensional settings to hijack the bandit's behavior. Starting with linear reward functions and extending to nonlinear models such as ReLU neural networks, we study attacks on two Hugging Face evaluators used for generative model assessment: one measuring aesthetic quality and the other assessing compositional alignment. Our results show that even small, imperceptible perturbations to the reward model's weights can drastically alter the bandit's behavior. From a theoretical perspective, we prove a striking high-dimensional effect: as input dimensionality increases, the perturbation norm required for a successful attack decreases, making modern applications such as image evaluation especially vulnerable. Extensive experiments confirm that naive random perturbations are ineffective, whereas carefully targeted perturbations achieve near-perfect attack success rates ...

AI.tldr: Offline bandit evaluation is vulnerable: adversaries perturb reward model weights (not data) to hijack bandit training, with high-dimensional perturbations requiring smaller norm; targeted attacks succeed on Hugging Face evaluators for aesthetics and alignment, while random perturbations fail.
AI.motivation: Bandit-based offline evaluation is increasingly used to rank generative models and LMs. Relying on external reward models raises security concerns—attackers could manipulate the reward signal before training, altering outcomes without changing data. Understanding robustness is crucial, especially in high dimensions.
AI.method: Theoretical analysis begins with linear reward functions and extends to nonlinear ReLU networks. A novel threat model perturbs offline reward-model weights to influence the bandit’s decisions. Attacks are tested on two Hugging Face evaluators (aesthetic quality, compositional alignment). We compare naive random perturbations to carefully crafted, targeted perturbations and analyze high-dimensional effects.
AI.result: Small, imperceptible changes to reward-model weights can drastically change bandit behavior. A high-dimensional effect emerges: as input dimensionality grows, the perturbation norm needed for a successful attack decreases, making image evaluation especially vulnerable. Experiments show random perturbations are ineffective, while targeted perturbations achieve near-perfect attack success rates.
AI.conclusion: Offline bandit evaluation is susceptible to reward-model attacks, particularly in high dimensions. This calls for defenses (robust reward models, data auditing, anomaly detection) and further study of secure offline evaluation frameworks. Future work could explore detection, certified robustness, and defense strategies against reward-model perturbations.

title: Semantically Aware UAV Landing Site Assessment from Remote Sensing Imagery via Multimodal Large Language Models
summary: Safe UAV emergency landing requires more than just identifying flat terrain; it demands understanding complex semantic risks (e.g., crowds, temporary structures) invisible to traditional geometric sensors. In this paper, we propose a novel framework leveraging Remote Sensing (RS) imagery and Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) for global context-aware landing site assessment. Unlike local geometric methods, our approach employs a coarse-to-fine pipeline: first, a lightweight semantic segmentation module efficiently pre-screens candidate areas; second, a vision-language reasoning agent fuses visual features with Point-of-Interest (POI) data to detect subtle hazards. To validate this approach, we construct and release the Emergency Landing Site Selection (ELSS) benchmark. Experiments demonstrate that our framework significantly outperforms geometric baselines in risk identification accuracy. Furthermore, qualitative results confirm its ability to generate human-like, interpretable justifications, enhancing trust in automated decision-making. The benchmark dataset is publicly accessible at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/ELSS-dataset-43D7.

AI.tldr: A framework that combines remote-sensing imagery and multimodal LLMs for global-context emergency landing site assessment, using a coarse-to-fine pipeline (segmentation screen, then vision-language reasoning with POI data); introduces ELSS benchmark; outperforms geometric baselines in risk identification; provides interpretable justifications; dataset released.
AI.motivation: To address semantic risks in landing sites that are invisible to geometric sensors (e.g., crowds, temporary structures) and enable safer, explainable UAV emergency landings using RS imagery and MLLMs.
AI.method: Coarse-to-fine pipeline: (1) lightweight semantic segmentation to pre-screen candidate areas; (2) vision-language reasoning agent fuses visual features with POI data to detect hazards. Development of ELSS benchmark dataset.
AI.result: Significant improvement over geometric baselines in risk identification accuracy; qualitative results show human-like, interpretable justifications increasing trust.
AI.conclusion: Global context-aware, semantically rich landing site assessment is feasible with RS imagery and MLLMs; dataset is publicly available to support research.

title: Quantifying Epistemic Predictive Uncertainty in Conformal Prediction
summary: We study the problem of quantifying epistemic predictive uncertainty (EPU) -- that is, uncertainty faced at prediction time due to the existence of multiple plausible predictive models -- within the framework of conformal prediction (CP). To expose the implicit model multiplicity underlying CP, we build on recent results showing that, under a mild assumption, any full CP procedure induces a set of closed and convex predictive distributions, commonly referred to as a credal set. Importantly, the conformal prediction region (CPR) coincides exactly with the set of labels to which all distributions in the induced credal set assign probability at least $1-α$. As our first contribution, we prove that this characterisation also holds in split CP. Building on this connection, we then propose a computationally efficient and analytically tractable uncertainty measure, based on \emph{Maximum Mean Imprecision}, to quantify the EPU by measuring the degree of conflicting information within the induced credal set. Experiments on active learning and selective classification demonstrate that the quantified EPU provides substantially more informative and fine-grained uncertainty assessments than reliance on CPR size alone. More broadly, this work highlights the potential of CP serving as a principled basis for decision-making under epistemic uncertainty.

AI.tldr: Shows that conformal prediction (CP) induces a credal set of predictive distributions and that the CP region equals the intersection of high-probability labels across all distributions. Extends this to split CP, and introduces Maximum Mean Imprecision to quantify epistemic predictive uncertainty (EPU). Empirically, EPU outperforms CPR size as an uncertainty metric in active learning and selective classification.
AI.motivation: Epistemic uncertainty at prediction time arises from model multiplicity. CP provides a principled framework for uncertainty but the implicit multiplicity was not fully exploited. The work seeks to formalize the link between CP and credal sets and to quantify EPU in a scalable way.
AI.method: 1) Theoretical analysis showing that CP induces a closed convex credal set of predictive distributions, and that the CP region equals the set of labels with probability ≥ 1−α under all distributions in the credal set. 2) Extension of this characterization to split CP. 3) Introduction of Maximum Mean Imprecision (MMI) as an uncertainty measure derived from the credal set to quantify EPU. 4) Empirical evaluation in active learning and selective classification.
AI.result: The CPR coincides with the intersection of high-probability labels across the credal set generated by CP, and this holds for split CP. MMI provides a tractable, informative metric of EPU that captures conflicting information inside the credal set. Experiments show MMI yields more informative uncertainty assessments than CPR size alone in active learning and selective classification.
AI.conclusion: CP can serve as a principled basis for decision-making under epistemic uncertainty by enabling both a distributional view (credal set) and a practical uncertainty metric (MMI). The work strengthens the theoretical foundations of CP and offers a scalable tool for EPU quantification.

title: Finite and Corruption-Robust Regret Bounds in Online Inverse Linear Optimization under M-Convex Action Sets
summary: We study online inverse linear optimization, also known as contextual recommendation, where a learner sequentially infers an agent's hidden objective vector from observed optimal actions over feasible sets that change over time. The learner aims to recommend actions that perform well under the agent's true objective, and the performance is measured by the regret, defined as the cumulative gap between the agent's optimal values and those achieved by the learner's recommended actions. Prior work has established a regret bound of $O(d\log T)$, as well as a finite but exponentially large bound of $\exp(O(d\log d))$, where $d$ is the dimension of the optimization problem and $T$ is the time horizon, while a regret lower bound of $Ω(d)$ is known (Gollapudi et al. 2021; Sakaue et al. 2025). Whether a finite regret bound polynomial in $d$ is achievable or not has remained an open question. We partially resolve this by showing that when the feasible sets are M-convex -- a broad class that includes matroids -- a finite regret bound of $O(d\log d)$ is possible. We achieve this by combining a structural characterization of optimal solutions on M-convex sets with a geometric volume argument. Moreover, we extend our approach to adversarially corrupted feedback in up to $C$ rounds. We obtain a regret bound of $O((C+1)d\log d)$ without prior knowledge of $C$, by monitoring directed graphs induced by the observed feedback to detect corruptions adaptively.

AI.tldr: Show that for online inverse linear optimization (contextual recommendation), finite regret polynomial in d is achievable when feasible sets are M-convex (incl. matroids), with O(d log d); extend to adversarial feedback with up to C corruptions giving O((C+1)d log d) regret without knowing C.
AI.motivation: Resolve whether regret can be polynomial in problem dimension d in online inverse linear optimization; identify a broad feasible-set class (M-convex) where this holds and address robustness to corruptions.
AI.method: Leverage a structural characterization of optimal solutions on M-convex sets and a geometric volume argument to derive O(d log d) regret; for corruption, monitor directed graphs induced by observed feedback to detect and adapt to corruptions, yielding O((C+1)d log d) regret without prior C knowledge.
AI.result: Finite regret bound of O(d log d) when feasible sets are M-convex; for adversarially corrupted feedback up to C rounds, achievable regret O((C+1)d log d) without knowledge of C.
AI.conclusion: Shows polynomial-in-d regret in a broad class of feasible sets and robustness to a bounded number of corruptions; invites exploration beyond M-convex sets and tighter constants, as well as practical computational aspects.

title: Semantic-aware Wasserstein Policy Regularization for Large Language Model Alignment
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are commonly aligned with human preferences using reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF). In this method, LLM policies are generally optimized through reward maximization with Kullback-Leibler (KL) divergence regularization of the reference policy. However, KL and its $f$-divergence variants only compare token probabilities at identical indices, failing to capture semantic similarity. We propose Wasserstein Policy Regularization (WPR), a semantic-aware regularization for the RLHF framework based on the entropy-regularized Wasserstein distance, which incorporates the geometry of the token space. The dual formulation of the distance expresses the regularization as penalty terms applied to the reward via optimal dual variables, which yield a tractable objective compatible with standard RL algorithms. Empirically, our method outperforms KL- and $f$-divergence-based baselines, demonstrating the benefits of semantic-aware policy distances for alignment. Our code is available at https://github.com/aailab-kaist/WPR.

AI.tldr: Proposes Wasserstein Policy Regularization (WPR) for RLHF, using entropy-regularized Wasserstein distance to incorporate token-space geometry into policy regularization; yields better alignment than KL/f-divergence baselines; code available.
AI.motivation: KL and f-divergence regularizers compare token probabilities only at identical indices and miss semantic similarity between tokens; a geometry-aware, semantic-aware distance is needed for improved alignment.
AI.method: Define WPR via entropy-regularized Wasserstein distance between the current policy and a reference policy. Utilize its dual formulation to obtain penalty terms on the reward, producing a tractable objective compatible with standard RLHF algorithms.
AI.result: Empirically, WPR outperforms KL- and f-divergence-based baselines, demonstrating the benefit of incorporating semantic token-space geometry into alignment.
AI.conclusion: WPR provides a principled, tractable, semantic-aware regularization for RLHF that improves alignment over traditional KL/f-divergence approaches.

title: $\textbf{AGT$^{AO}$}$: Robust and Stabilized LLM Unlearning via Adversarial Gating Training with Adaptive Orthogonality
summary: While Large Language Models (LLMs) have achieved remarkable capabilities, they unintentionally memorize sensitive data, posing critical privacy and security risks. Machine unlearning is pivotal for mitigating these risks, yet existing paradigms face a fundamental dilemma: aggressive unlearning often induces catastrophic forgetting that degrades model utility, whereas conservative strategies risk superficial forgetting, leaving models vulnerable to adversarial recovery. To address this trade-off, we propose $\textbf{AGT$^{AO}$}$ (Adversarial Gating Training with Adaptive Orthogonality), a unified framework designed to reconcile robust erasure with utility preservation. Specifically, our approach introduces $\textbf{Adaptive Orthogonality (AO)}$ to dynamically mitigate geometric gradient conflicts between forgetting and retention objectives, thereby minimizing unintended knowledge degradation. Concurrently, $\textbf{Adversarial Gating Training (AGT)}$ formulates unlearning as a latent-space min-max game, employing a curriculum-based gating mechanism to simulate and counter internal recovery attempts. Extensive experiments demonstrate that $\textbf{AGT$^{AO}$}$ achieves a superior trade-off between unlearning efficacy (KUR $\approx$ 0.01) and model utility (MMLU 58.30). Code is available at https://github.com/TiezMind/AGT-unlearning.

AI.tldr: Proposes AGT^AO, a unified framework for robust unlearning in LLMs, combining Adaptive Orthogonality to mitigate gradient conflicts and Adversarial Gating Training as a latent-space min–max game, achieving a favorable balance between erasure efficacy and utility. Reported metrics include KUR ≈ 0.01 and MMLU 58.30; code is released.
AI.motivation: To address privacy and security risks from memorized sensitive data in large language models. Existing unlearning methods struggle with a trade-off: aggressive erasure causes catastrophic forgetting, while conservative approaches leave the model vulnerable to recovery.
AI.method: Adaptive Orthogonality dynamically mitigates gradient conflicts between forgetting and retention objectives. Adversarial Gating Training casts unlearning as a latent-space min–max game, using a curriculum-based gating mechanism to simulate and counter internal recovery attempts.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation shows improved trade-off between unlearning effectiveness and model utility, with KUR ≈ 0.01 and MMLU 58.30. Code available at the provided GitHub URL.
AI.conclusion: AGT^AO offers a unified solution that reconciles robust erasure with utility preservation in LLMs by combining gradient-conflict mitigation and adversarial gating; the framework demonstrates practical potential for privacy-preserving unlearning.

title: Beyond Mode Elicitation: Diversity-Preserving Reinforcement Learning via Latent Diffusion Reasoner
summary: Recent reinforcement learning (RL) methods improve LLM reasoning by optimizing discrete Chain-of-Thought (CoT) generation; however, exploration in token space often suffers from diversity collapse as policy entropy decreases due to mode elicitation behavior in discrete RL. To mitigate this issue, we propose Latent Diffusion Reasoning with Reinforcement Learning (LaDi-RL), a framework that conducts exploration directly in a continuous latent space, where latent variables encode semantic-level reasoning trajectories. By modeling exploration via guided diffusion, multi-step denoising distributes stochasticity and preserves multiple coexisting solution modes without mutual suppression. Furthermore, by decoupling latent-space exploration from text-space generation, we show that latent diffusion-based optimization is more effective than text-space policy optimization alone, while a complementary text policy provides additional gains when combined with latent exploration. Experiments on code generation and mathematical reasoning benchmarks demonstrate consistent improvements in both pass@1 and pass@k over discrete RL baselines, with absolute pass@1 gains of +9.4% on code generation and +5.7% on mathematical reasoning, highlighting diffusion-based latent RL as a principled alternative to discrete token-level RL for reasoning.

AI.tldr: LaDi-RL uses latent diffusion to explore reasoning trajectories in a continuous latent space, mitigating diversity collapse in discrete RL and enhancing CoT reasoning for code and math benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Discrete RL for Chain-of-Thought tends to induce mode elicitation and entropy collapse, reducing diverse reasoning paths. A latent-space exploration could preserve multiple reasoning modes.
AI.method: Model exploration via guided diffusion in a latent space that encodes semantic reasoning trajectories; multi-step denoising distributes stochasticity; decouples latent-space exploration from text generation; combines latent diffusion with a text policy for additional gains.
AI.result: Across code generation and mathematical reasoning benchmarks, LaDi-RL yields consistent improvements over discrete RL baselines, with absolute pass@1 gains of +9.4% (code) and +5.7% (math). Also improves pass@k.
AI.conclusion: Diffusion-based latent RL provides a principled alternative to token-level discrete RL for reasoning, effectively preserving diverse reasoning modes and improving performance.

title: Revisiting Generalization Measures Beyond IID: An Empirical Study under Distributional Shift
summary: Generalization remains a central yet unresolved challenge in deep learning, particularly the ability to predict a model's performance beyond its training distribution using quantities available prior to test-time evaluation. Building on the large-scale study of Jiang et al. (2020). and concerns by Dziugaite et al. (2020). about instability across training configurations, we benchmark the robustness of generalization measures beyond IID regime. We train small-to-medium models over 10,000 hyperparameter configurations and evaluate more than 40 measures computable from the trained model and the available training data alone. We significantly broaden the experimental scope along multiple axes: (i) extending the evaluation beyond the standard IID setting to include benchmarking for robustness across diverse distribution shifts, (ii) evaluating multiple architectures and training recipes, and (iii) newly incorporating calibration- and information-criteria-based measures to assess their alignment with both IID and OOD generalization. We find that distribution shifts can substantially alter the predictive performance of many generalization measures, while a smaller subset remains comparatively stable across settings.

AI.tldr: A large-scale benchmark analyzes how well various generalization measures predict model performance under distribution shifts, across multiple architectures and 10,000 hyperparameter configurations, finding that most measures' predictive power changes with distribution shift, while a smaller subset remains stable.
AI.motivation: To address concerns that generalization proxies trained under IID conditions do not reliably predict OOD performance, and that findings may be unstable across training configurations and architectures.
AI.method: Train small-to-medium models across 10,000 hyperparameter configurations; compute >40 measures from the trained model and training data; evaluate these measures under IID and multiple distribution shifts across diverse architectures and training recipes; incorporate calibration- and information-criteria-based measures to assess their alignment with IID and OOD generalization.
AI.result: Distribution shifts substantially alter the predictive performance of many generalization measures; a smaller subset of measures remains comparatively stable across IID and OOD settings.
AI.conclusion: Some generalization measures are robust to distribution shifts and across training configurations, but many are not; careful selection of measures is needed for reliable OOD generalization estimation, potentially favoring calibration-/information-criterion-based proxies that better align with both IID and OOD generalization.

title: MSign: An Optimizer Preventing Training Instability in Large Language Models via Stable Rank Restoration
summary: Training instability remains a critical challenge in large language model (LLM) pretraining, often manifesting as sudden gradient explosions that waste significant computational resources. We study training failures in a 5M-parameter NanoGPT model scaled via $μ$P, identifying two key phenomena preceding collapse: (1) rapid decline in weight matrix stable rank (ratio of squared Frobenius norm to squared spectral norm), and (2) increasing alignment between adjacent layer Jacobians. We prove theoretically that these two conditions jointly cause exponential gradient norm growth with network depth. To break this instability mechanism, we propose MSign, a new optimizer that periodically applies matrix sign operations to restore stable rank. Experiments on models from 5M to 3B parameters demonstrate that MSign effectively prevents training failures with a computational overhead of less than 7.0%.

AI.tldr: MSign uses periodic matrix sign operations to restore weight matrix stable rank, preventing gradient explosions during LLM pretraining with modest compute overhead.
AI.motivation: Training instability in LLM pretraining manifests as sudden gradient explosions. The abstract identifies two precursors—rapid decline in weight-stable-rank and increasing Jacobian alignment between adjacent layers—that theoretically drive exponential gradient growth with depth.
AI.method: Introduce MSign, an optimizer that periodically applies matrix sign operations to matrices to restore stable rank. Evaluation spans models from 5M to 3B parameters pretrained with μP, with training-overhead claims under 7%.
AI.result: MSign prevents training failures and stabilizes training across scales, with computational overhead under 7%. The work provides theoretical linkage between the precursors and gradient growth and empirically validates the mitigation.
AI.conclusion: MSign offers a practical approach to mitigating LLM pretraining instability by maintaining stable rank via matrix sign operations, effective across a wide scale with modest overhead; future work may explore broader applicability and interaction with other optimization strategies.

title: Position: The Inevitable End of One-Architecture-Fits-All-Domains in Time Series Forecasting
summary: Recent work has questioned the effectiveness and robustness of neural network architectures for time series forecasting tasks. We summarize these concerns and analyze groundly their inherent limitations: i.e. the irreconcilable conflict between single (or few similar) domains SOTA and generalizability over general domains for time series forecasting neural network architecture designs. Moreover, neural networks architectures for general domain time series forecasting are becoming more and more complicated and their performance has almost saturated in recent years. As a result, network architectures developed aiming at fitting general time series domains are almost not inspiring for real world practices for certain single (or few similar) domains such as Finance, Weather, Traffic, etc: each specific domain develops their own methods that rarely utilize advances in neural network architectures of time series community in recent 2-3 years. As a result, we call for the time series community to shift focus away from research on time series neural network architectures for general domains: these researches have become saturated and away from domain-specific SOTAs over time. We should either (1) focus on deep learning methods for certain specific domain(s), or (2) turn to the development of meta-learning methods for general domains.

AI.tldr: General-domain neural architectures for time series forecasting show limited cross-domain generalization and diminishing returns; domain-specific methods outperform, suggesting a shift toward domain-focused DL or meta-learning for generalization.
AI.motivation: Address concerns about the effectiveness and robustness of neural network architectures in time series forecasting; highlight a conflict between specialized SOTA in single/few domains and generalizability across domains; note increasing complexity and saturation of general architectures.
AI.method: Conceptual analysis and literature synthesis (no new empirical experiments) to identify core limitations and trends in time series forecasting architectures.
AI.result: Identified key limitations: (1) irreconcilable tension between domain-specific SOTA and cross-domain generalization; (2) growing architectural complexity with near-saturated performance; (3) domain-specific methods maintain practical superiority for real-world domains (e.g., Finance, Weather, Traffic); (4) recommendation to redirect focus toward domain-specific DL methods or meta-learning for general domains.
AI.conclusion: Propose shifting research focus from time series NN architectures for general domains to either domain-specific deep learning approaches or meta-learning strategies that improve generalization across domains.

title: Softmax Linear Attention: Reclaiming Global Competition
summary: While linear attention reduces the quadratic complexity of standard Transformers to linear time, it often lags behind in expressivity due to the removal of softmax normalization. This omission eliminates \emph{global competition}, a critical mechanism that enables models to sharply focus on relevant information amidst long-context noise. In this work, we propose \textbf{Softmax Linear Attention (SLA)}, a framework designed to restore this competitive selection without sacrificing efficiency. By lifting the softmax operation from the token level to the head level, SLA leverages attention heads as coarse semantic slots, applying a competitive gating mechanism to dynamically select the most relevant subspaces. This reintroduces the ``winner-take-all'' dynamics essential for precise retrieval and robust long-context understanding. Distinct from prior methods that focus on refining local kernel functions, SLA adopts a broader perspective by exploiting the higher-level multi-head aggregation structure. Extensive experiments demonstrate that SLA consistently enhances state-of-the-art linear baselines (RetNet, GLA, GDN) across language modeling and long-context benchmarks, particularly in challenging retrieval scenarios where it significantly boosts robustness against noise, validating its capability to restore precise focus while maintaining linear complexity.

AI.tldr: Softmax Linear Attention (SLA) reintroduces global competition in linear-time attention by lifting softmax to the head level, enabling winner-take-all subspace selection and improving long-context retrieval while preserving linear complexity.
AI.motivation: Linear attention reduces complexity but sacrifices expressivity due to removing softmax normalization; global competition among tokens helps focus on relevant information in long contexts.
AI.method: Lift the softmax operation from tokens to attention heads; treat heads as coarse semantic slots and apply a competitive gating mechanism among heads to select the most relevant subspaces; leverage multi-head aggregation to maintain efficiency and expressivity.
AI.result: Empirically, SLA improves state-of-the-art linear baselines (RetNet, GLA, GDN) on language modeling and long-context benchmarks, especially in retrieval tasks and noisy settings, demonstrating robustness and more precise focus without increasing asymptotic complexity.
AI.conclusion: SLA restores the competitive selection mechanism within linear attention, balancing efficiency with enhanced long-context retrieving capabilities by exploiting head-level competition rather than refining local kernels.

title: Probability-Entropy Calibration: An Elastic Indicator for Adaptive Fine-tuning
summary: Token-level reweighting is a simple yet effective mechanism for controlling supervised fine-tuning, but common indicators are largely one-dimensional: the ground-truth probability reflects downstream alignment, while token entropy reflects intrinsic uncertainty induced by the pre-training prior. Ignoring entropy can misidentify noisy or easily replaceable tokens as learning-critical, while ignoring probability fails to reflect target-specific alignment. RankTuner introduces a probability--entropy calibration signal, the Relative Rank Indicator, which compares the rank of the ground-truth token with its expected rank under the prediction distribution. The inverse indicator is used as a token-wise Relative Scale to reweight the fine-tuning objective, focusing updates on truly under-learned tokens without over-penalizing intrinsically uncertain positions. Experiments on multiple backbones show consistent improvements on mathematical reasoning benchmarks, transfer gains on out-of-distribution reasoning, and pre code generation performance over probability-only or entropy-only reweighting baselines.

AI.tldr: RankTuner introduces a probability–entropy calibrated token reweighting mechanism for supervised fine-tuning. It uses the Relative Rank Indicator to compare the ground-truth token's rank against its expected rank under the model's prediction distribution and applies the inverse of this indicator as a token-wise scale, focusing updates on under-learned tokens without over-penalizing intrinsically uncertain positions.
AI.motivation: Existing token reweighting often relies on a single signal (e.g., ground-truth probability or token entropy) and may misidentify informative vs. noisy tokens. A joint calibration signal is needed to reflect both downstream target alignment and intrinsic pre-training uncertainty.
AI.method: Define the Relative Rank Indicator as the comparison between the rank of the ground-truth token and its expected rank under the prediction distribution. Use the inverse of this indicator as a Relative Scale to reweight the supervised fine-tuning loss, thereby emphasizing under-learned tokens while not over-penalizing tokens with high entropy. Train and evaluate across multiple backbones.
AI.result: The approach yields consistent improvements on mathematical reasoning benchmarks, gains in transfer to out-of-distribution reasoning, and better pre-code generation performance compared with probability-only or entropy-only reweighting baselines.
AI.conclusion: Joint probability–entropy calibration via the Relative Rank Indicator and its inverse-based scale provides effective, token-level reweighting for supervised fine-tuning, improving reasoning and code-generation tasks across diverse models.

title: Rethinking LoRA for Data Heterogeneous Federated Learning: Subspace and State Alignment
summary: Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) is widely used for federated fine-tuning. Yet under non-IID settings, it can substantially underperform full-parameter fine-tuning. Through with-high-probability robustness analysis, we uncover that this gap can be attributed to two coupled mismatches: (i) update-space mismatch, where clients optimize in a low-rank subspace but aggregation occurs in the full space; and (ii) optimizer-state mismatch, where unsynchronized adaptive states amplify drift across rounds. We propose FedGaLore, which combines client-side GaLore-style gradient-subspace optimization with server-side drift-robust synchronization of projected second-moment states via spectral shared-signal extraction, to address this challenge. Across NLU, vision, and NLG benchmarks, FedGaLore improves robustness and accuracy over state-of-the-art federated LoRA baselines in non-IID settings.

AI.tldr: Identifies two mismatches causing low-rank LoRA to underperform in non-IID federated learning and proposes FedGaLore, combining gradient-subspace optimization with drift-robust server synchronization to improve robustness and accuracy across NLP/vision/NLG benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Federated fine-tuning with LoRA under non-IID distributions suffers performance gaps compared to full-parameter fine-tuning. The authors diagnose two coupled causes—update-space mismatch and optimizer-state mismatch—and seek a remedy that aligns client updates with the aggregation space and stabilizes optimizer states.
AI.method: FedGaLore integrates (1) client-side GaLore-style gradient-subspace optimization to constrain updates to meaningful subspaces, and (2) server-side drift-robust synchronization of projected second-moment states using spectral shared-signal extraction to mitigate cross-round drift.
AI.result: Empirical experiments on NLU, vision, and NLG benchmarks show FedGaLore achieves higher robustness and accuracy than state-of-the-art federated LoRA baselines in non-IID settings.
AI.conclusion: Addressing both update-space and optimizer-state mismatches yields more reliable federated fine-tuning with LoRA across modalities, suggesting broader applicability for robust federated adaptation.

title: PolyGen: Fully Synthetic Vision-Language Training via Multi-Generator Ensembles
summary: Synthetic data offers a scalable solution for vision-language pre-training, yet current state-of-the-art methods typically rely on scaling up a single generative backbone, which introduces generator-specific spectral biases and limits feature diversity. In this work, we introduce PolyGen, a framework that redefines synthetic data construction by prioritizing manifold coverage and compositional rigor over simple dataset size. PolyGen employs a Polylithic approach to train on the intersection of architecturally distinct generators, effectively marginalizing out model-specific artifacts. Additionally, we introduce a Programmatic Hard Negative curriculum that enforces fine-grained syntactic understanding. By structurally reallocating the same data budget from unique captions to multi-source variations, PolyGen achieves a more robust feature space, outperforming the leading single-source baseline (SynthCLIP) by +19.0% on aggregate multi-task benchmarks and on the SugarCrepe++ compositionality benchmark (+9.1%). These results demonstrate that structural diversity is a more data-efficient scaling law than simply increasing the volume of single-source samples.

AI.tldr: PolyGen introduces a Polylithic, multi-generator synthetic data framework with a Hard Negative curriculum to improve vision-language pretraining by prioritizing manifold coverage and compositionality, outperforming single-source baselines.
AI.motivation: Single-generator synthetic data introduces generator-specific artifacts and limited feature diversity; need methods that maximize manifold coverage and compositional understanding without excessive data volume.
AI.method: Train on the intersection of architecturally distinct generators (Polylithic approach) to marginalize generator artifacts; introduce a Programmatic Hard Negative curriculum to enforce syntactic understanding; reallocate the same data budget from unique captions to multi-source variations.
AI.result: Outperforms the leading single-source baseline SynthCLIP by +19.0% on aggregate multi-task benchmarks and +9.1% on SugarCrepe++ compositionality benchmark.
AI.conclusion: Structural diversity serves as a more data-efficient scaling law than simply increasing single-source data; multi-source synthetic data improves robustness and diversity of learned representations.

title: MGKAN: Predicting Asymmetric Drug-Drug Interactions via a Multimodal Graph Kolmogorov-Arnold Network
comment: This paper has been accepted by ICASSP 2026
summary: Predicting drug-drug interactions (DDIs) is essential for safe pharmacological treatments. Previous graph neural network (GNN) models leverage molecular structures and interaction networks but mostly rely on linear aggregation and symmetric assumptions, limiting their ability to capture nonlinear and heterogeneous patterns. We propose MGKAN, a Graph Kolmogorov-Arnold Network that introduces learnable basis functions into asymmetric DDI prediction. MGKAN replaces conventional MLP transformations with KAN-driven basis functions, enabling more expressive and nonlinear modeling of drug relationships. To capture pharmacological dependencies, MGKAN integrates three network views-an asymmetric DDI network, a co-interaction network, and a biochemical similarity network-with role-specific embeddings to preserve directional semantics. A fusion module combines linear attention and nonlinear transformation to enhance representational capacity. On two benchmark datasets, MGKAN outperforms seven state-of-the-art baselines. Ablation studies and case studies confirm its predictive accuracy and effectiveness in modeling directional drug effects.

AI.tldr: MGKAN: a learnable basis-function-based GNN for asymmetric drug-drug interaction prediction, using three network views and a fusion module to capture nonlinear and directional patterns; achieves state-of-the-art performance on two benchmarks with ablations validating components.
AI.motivation: Address limitations of linear aggregation and symmetry in traditional GNNs for DDIs, by capturing nonlinear patterns and directional semantics through learnable basis functions and multi-view integration.
AI.method: Introduce Graph Kolmogorov-Arnold Network (MGKAN) with KAN-driven basis functions replacing MLPs; integrate three views (asymmetric DDI network, co-interaction network, biochemical similarity network) with role-specific embeddings; use a fusion module combining linear attention and nonlinear transformation.
AI.result: Outperforms seven state-of-the-art baselines on two benchmark datasets; ablation studies and case studies confirm predictive accuracy and directional modeling capabilities.
AI.conclusion: MGKAN provides a more expressive, direction-aware framework for DDI prediction, showing benefits from basis-function-based nonlinearity and multi-view fusion.

title: A Provable Expressiveness Hierarchy in Hybrid Linear-Full Attention
summary: Transformers serve as the foundation of most modern large language models. To mitigate the quadratic complexity of standard full attention, various efficient attention mechanisms, such as linear and hybrid attention, have been developed. A fundamental gap remains: their expressive power relative to full attention lacks a rigorous theoretical characterization. In this work, we theoretically characterize the performance differences among these attention mechanisms. Our theory applies to all linear attention variants that can be formulated as a recurrence, including Mamba, DeltaNet, etc. Specifically, we establish an expressiveness hierarchy: for the sequential function composition-a multi-step reasoning task that must occur within a model's forward pass, an ($L+1$)-layer full attention network is sufficient, whereas any hybrid network interleaving $L-1$ layers of full attention with a substantially larger number ($2^{3L^2}$) of linear attention layers cannot solve it. This result demonstrates a clear separation in expressive power between the two types of attention. Our work provides the first provable separation between hybrid attention and standard full attention, offering a theoretical perspective for understanding the fundamental capabilities and limitations of different attention mechanisms.

AI.tldr: There is a provable expressiveness separation between full-attention transformers and linear/hybrid attention variants. For a sequential multi-step reasoning task, an (L+1)-layer full-attention network suffices, while any hybrid network that interleaves L-1 full-attention layers with an exponentially large number (2^{3L^2}) of linear-attention layers cannot solve it, establishing a formal separation in expressive power.
AI.motivation: Efficient attention mechanisms reduce quadratic complexity of full attention, but their expressive power relative to full attention has not been rigorously characterized. The work seeks a theoretical understanding of what can be expressed by linear/hybrid attention versus full attention.
AI.method: Develop a theoretical framework applicable to all linear attention variants representable as a recurrence (e.g., Mamba, DeltaNet). Prove an expressiveness hierarchy by constructing a sequential function composition task that requires multi-step reasoning: (i) an (L+1)-layer full-attention network suffices, (ii) any hybrid network with L-1 layers of full attention and 2^{3L^2} linear-attention layers cannot solve it.
AI.result: Established a formal expressiveness separation: full attention with (L+1) layers can solve the target sequential task, whereas hybrid architectures with vastly more linear-attention layers cannot, proving a separation in expressive power between the two attention types.
AI.conclusion: This work provides the first provable separation between hybrid attention and standard full attention, offering a theoretical basis for comparing attention mechanisms and informing design choices for attention-based models.

title: CoMeT: Collaborative Memory Transformer for Efficient Long Context Modeling
summary: The quadratic complexity and indefinitely growing key-value (KV) cache of standard Transformers pose a major barrier to long-context processing. To overcome this, we introduce the Collaborative Memory Transformer (CoMeT), a novel architecture that enables LLMs to handle arbitrarily long sequences with constant memory usage and linear time complexity. Designed as an efficient, plug-in module, CoMeT can be integrated into pre-trained models with only minimal fine-tuning. It operates on sequential data chunks, using a dual-memory system to manage context: a temporary memory on a FIFO queue for recent events, and a global memory with a gated update rule for long-range dependencies. These memories then act as a dynamic soft prompt for the next chunk. To enable efficient fine-tuning on extremely long contexts, we introduce a novel layer-level pipeline parallelism strategy. The effectiveness of our approach is remarkable: a model equipped with CoMeT and fine-tuned on 32k contexts can accurately retrieve a passkey from any position within a 1M token sequence. On the SCROLLS benchmark, CoMeT surpasses other efficient methods and achieves performance comparable to a full-attention baseline on summarization tasks. Its practical effectiveness is further validated on real-world agent and user behavior QA tasks. The code is available at: https://anonymous.4open.science/r/comet-B00B/

AI.tldr: CoMeT introduces a dual-memory augmentation for transformers enabling constant memory and linear-time long-context processing, with a plug-in design and efficiency gains validated on SCROLLS and QA tasks.
AI.motivation: Quadratic memory and time scaling of standard transformers restricts long-context processing; scalable alternatives often require heavy fine-tuning or architectural changes. There is a need for a plug-in, memory-based solution that supports extremely long sequences with minimal fine-tuning.
AI.method: Proposes a dual-memory system: a temporary FIFO-based memory for recent events and a global memory updated by a gated rule to capture long-range dependencies. The memories serve as a dynamic soft prompt for the next chunk. Introduces layer-level pipeline parallelism to enable fine-tuning on very long contexts. The module is designed as a plug-in integrated into pre-trained models.
AI.result: With CoMeT, a model fine-tuned on 32k-contexts can retrieve a passkey from any position in a 1M-token sequence. On SCROLLS, CoMeT outperforms other efficient methods and matches full-attention baselines on summarization. Real-world agent and user-behavior QA tasks validate practical effectiveness.
AI.conclusion: CoMeT achieves constant memory and linear-time long-context processing with minimal fine-tuning, demonstrating strong scalability and practical utility for extremely long sequences; code is released.

title: IRIS: Implicit Reward-Guided Internal Sifting for Mitigating Multimodal Hallucination
summary:
Hallucination remains a fundamental challenge for Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs). While Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) is a key alignment framework, existing approaches often rely heavily on costly external evaluators for scoring or rewriting, incurring off-policy learnability gaps and discretization loss. Due to the lack of access to internal states, such feedback overlooks the fine-grained conflicts between different modalities that lead to hallucinations during generation.
  To address this issue, we propose IRIS (Implicit Reward-Guided Internal Sifting), which leverages continuous implicit rewards in the native log-probability space to preserve full information density and capture internal modal competition. This on-policy paradigm eliminates learnability gaps by utilizing self-generated preference pairs. By sifting these pairs based on multimodal implicit rewards, IRIS ensures that optimization is driven by signals that directly resolve modal conflicts. Extensive experiments demonstrate that IRIS achieves highly competitive performance on key hallucination benchmarks using only 5.7k samples, without requiring any external feedback during preference alignment. These results confirm that IRIS provides an efficient and principled paradigm for mitigating MLLM hallucinations.

AI.tldr: IRIS uses implicit rewards in the native log-probability space to guide on-policy, self-generated preferences for multimodal LLMs, addressing modal conflicts and hallucination without external evaluators, and achieves competitive results with only 5.7k samples.
AI.motivation: Hallucinations in Multimodal LLMs are exacerbated by reliance on external evaluators (DPO) that introduce off-policy learnability gaps and ignore fine-grained cross-modal conflicts; access to internal states is needed to resolve modal competition.
AI.method: Introduce IRIS (Implicit Reward-Guided Internal Sifting). Leverage continuous implicit rewards in log-probability space to preserve information density. Generate self-derived preference pairs on-policy and sift them by multimodal implicit rewards to drive optimization that resolves modal conflicts.
AI.result: IRIS delivers highly competitive hallucination mitigation on key benchmarks using only 5.7k samples and without external feedback during preference alignment.
AI.conclusion: IRIS provides an efficient, principled on-policy paradigm for reducing MLLM hallucinations by leveraging internal, information-dense signals that directly address modal conflicts, reducing reliance on external evaluators.

title: Position: Beyond Model-Centric Prediction -- Agentic Time Series Forecasting
summary: Time series forecasting has traditionally been formulated as a model-centric, static, and single-pass prediction problem that maps historical observations to future values. While this paradigm has driven substantial progress, it proves insufficient in adaptive and multi-turn settings where forecasting requires informative feature extraction, reasoning-driven inference, iterative refinement, and continual adaptation over time. In this paper, we argue for agentic time series forecasting (ATSF), which reframes forecasting as an agentic process composed of perception, planning, action, reflection, and memory. Rather than focusing solely on predictive models, ATSF emphasizes organizing forecasting as an agentic workflow that can interact with tools, incorporate feedback from outcomes, and evolve through experience accumulation. We outline three representative implementation paradigms -- workflow-based design, agentic reinforcement learning, and a hybrid agentic workflow paradigm -- and discuss the opportunities and challenges that arise when shifting from model-centric prediction to agentic forecasting. Together, this position aims to establish agentic forecasting as a foundation for future research at the intersection of time series forecasting.

AI.tldr: Proposes agentic time series forecasting (ATSF): reframing forecasting as an agent-based, interactive workflow comprising perception, planning, action, reflection, and memory. Outlines three implementation paradigms (workflow-based design, agentic reinforcement learning, and hybrid workflows) and discusses opportunities and challenges of moving from model-centric prediction to agentic forecasting, aiming to establish ATSF as a foundation for future research in time series forecasting.
AI.motivation: Traditional time series forecasting is model-centric, static, and single-pass, which limits adaptability in dynamic, multi-turn settings. ATSF argues for interactive, feedback-driven systems that can evolve through experience and tool integration.
AI.method: Conceptual framework introducing agentic components (perception, planning, action, reflection, memory) and three representative paradigms: (1) workflow-based design, (2) agentic reinforcement learning, (3) hybrid agentic workflow, with a discussion of opportunities and challenges rather than empirical results.
AI.result: As a position/perspective paper, it outlines a new research direction and a taxonomy of implementation paradigms rather than presenting experimental findings.
AI.conclusion: Shifting to agentic forecasting could enable continual adaptation and interaction with tools, guiding future work at the intersection of time series forecasting and agent-based AI, though it raises questions about evaluation, reliability, and integration with domain knowledge.

title: Cross-Paradigm Evaluation of Gaze-Based Semantic Object Identification for Intelligent Vehicles
summary: Understanding where drivers direct their visual attention during driving, as characterized by gaze behavior, is critical for developing next-generation advanced driver-assistance systems and improving road safety. This paper tackles this challenge as a semantic identification task from the road scenes captured by a vehicle's front-view camera. Specifically, the collocation of gaze points with object semantics is investigated using three distinct vision-based approaches: direct object detection (YOLOv13), segmentation-assisted classification (SAM2 paired with EfficientNetV2 versus YOLOv13), and query-based Vision-Language Models, VLMs (Qwen2.5-VL-7b versus Qwen2.5-VL-32b). The results demonstrate that the direct object detection (YOLOv13) and Qwen2.5-VL-32b significantly outperform other approaches, achieving Macro F1-Scores over 0.84. The large VLM (Qwen2.5-VL-32b), in particular, exhibited superior robustness and performance for identifying small, safety-critical objects such as traffic lights, especially in adverse nighttime conditions. Conversely, the segmentation-assisted paradigm suffers from a "part-versus-whole" semantic gap that led to large failure in recall. The results reveal a fundamental trade-off between the real-time efficiency of traditional detectors and the richer contextual understanding and robustness offered by large VLMs. These findings provide critical insights and practical guidance for the design of future human-aware intelligent driver monitoring systems.

title: Stein-Rule Shrinkage for Stochastic Gradient Estimation in High Dimensions
summary: Stochastic gradient methods are central to large-scale learning, yet their analysis typically treats mini-batch gradients as unbiased estimators of the population gradient. In high-dimensional settings, however, classical results from statistical decision theory show that unbiased estimators are generally inadmissible under quadratic loss, suggesting that standard stochastic gradients may be suboptimal from a risk perspective. In this work, we formulate stochastic gradient computation as a high-dimensional estimation problem and introduce a decision-theoretic framework based on Stein-rule shrinkage. We construct a shrinkage gradient estimator that adaptively contracts noisy mini-batch gradients toward a stable restricted estimator derived from historical momentum. The shrinkage intensity is determined in a data-driven manner using an online estimate of gradient noise variance, leveraging second-moment statistics commonly maintained by adaptive optimization methods. Under a Gaussian noise model and for dimension p>=3, we show that the proposed estimator uniformly dominates the standard stochastic gradient under squared error loss and is minimax-optimal in the classical decision-theoretic sense. We further demonstrate how this estimator can be incorporated into the Adam optimizer, yielding a practical algorithm with negligible additional computational cost. Empirical evaluations on CIFAR10 and CIFAR100, across multiple levels of label noise, show consistent improvements over Adam in the large-batch regime. Ablation studies indicate that the gains arise primarily from selectively applying shrinkage to high-dimensional convolutional layers, while indiscriminate shrinkage across all parameters degrades performance. These results illustrate that classical shrinkage principles provide a principled and effective approach to improving stochastic gradient estimation in modern deep learning.

AI.tldr: A high-dimensional decision-theoretic shrinkage approach to stochastic gradient estimation that contracts noisy mini-batch gradients toward a history-based stable estimator, improving performance over Adam in large-batch regimes with label noise, and is minimax-optimal under Gaussian noise.
AI.motivation: In high dimensions, unbiased gradient estimators are often inadmissible under quadratic loss; standard SGD may be suboptimal from a risk perspective. Need a principled shrinkage mechanism to improve gradient estimation.
AI.method: Formulate gradient estimation as a high-dimensional estimation problem; apply Stein-rule shrinkage toward a momentum-derived estimator; estimate gradient noise variance online from second-moment statistics; integrate shrinkage into Adam with negligible cost.
AI.result: Under Gaussian noise with p>=3, shrinkage gradient uniformly dominates standard SGD under squared-error loss and is minimax-optimal; empirical gains on CIFAR10/100 with label noise in large-batch regime; gains mainly from shrinking high-dimensional conv layers; indiscriminate shrinkage harms performance.
AI.conclusion: Classical shrinkage principles provide a principled, effective enhancement to stochastic gradient estimation in deep learning; the approach can be practically deployed via Adam and yields robust improvements in appropriate regimes.

title: Understanding vision transformer robustness through the lens of out-of-distribution detection
summary: Vision transformers have shown remarkable performance in vision tasks, but enabling them for accessible and real-time use is still challenging. Quantization reduces memory and inference costs at the risk of performance loss. Strides have been made to mitigate low precision issues mainly by understanding in-distribution (ID) task behaviour, but the attention mechanism may provide insight on quantization attributes by exploring out-of-distribution (OOD) situations. We investigate the behaviour of quantized small-variant popular vision transformers (DeiT, DeiT3, and ViT) on common OOD datasets. ID analyses show the initial instabilities of 4-bit models, particularly of those trained on the larger ImageNet-22k, as the strongest FP32 model, DeiT3, sharply drop 17% from quantization error to be one of the weakest 4-bit models.
