While ViT shows reasonable quantization robustness for ID calibration, OOD detection reveals more: ViT and DeiT3 pretrained on ImageNet-22k respectively experienced a 15.0% and 19.2% average quantization delta in AUPR-out between full precision to 4-bit while their ImageNet-1k-only counterparts experienced a 9.5% and 12.0% delta. Overall, our results suggest pretraining on large scale datasets may hinder low-bit quantization robustness in OOD detection and that data augmentation may be a more beneficial option.

AI.tldr: Quantized small vision transformers show mixed OOD robustness, with large-scale pretraining (ImageNet-22k) reducing quantization performance in OOD; data augmentation may be more beneficial for robustness than simply increasing bit precision.
AI.motivation: Understand how 4-bit quantization affects ViT family (DeiT, DeiT3, ViT) under out-of-distribution conditions and how pretraining on large datasets influences robustness.
AI.method: Empirical study quantizing DeiT, DeiT3, and ViT variants to 4-bit; evaluate both in-distribution calibration and out-of-distribution detection (AUPR-out) on standard OOD datasets; compare models pretrained on ImageNet-22k vs ImageNet-1k; analyze impact of data augmentation.
AI.result: ID analysis shows instability in 4-bit models, especially for those pretrained on ImageNet-22k; DeiT3 (the strongest FP32) drops 17% due to quantization and becomes among the weakest 4-bit models. ViT attains reasonable ID robustness but OOD reveals larger gaps: ViT and DeiT3 pretrained on ImageNet-22k exhibit average AUPR-out deltas of 15.0% and 19.2% from full-precision to 4-bit, versus 9.5% and 12.0% for their ImageNet-1k-only counterparts. Overall, large-scale pretraining may hinder low-bit quantization robustness in OOD; data augmentation may be more beneficial for robustness.
AI.conclusion: Pretraining on large-scale datasets can degrade OOD robustness of very-low-precision ViTs; quantization improvements may require targeted strategies (e.g., data augmentation, quantization-aware training) rather than relying solely on increased pretraining scale.

title: Grad2Reward: From Sparse Judgment to Dense Rewards for Improving Open-Ended LLM Reasoning
summary: Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) has catalyzed significant breakthroughs in complex LLM reasoning within verifiable domains, such as mathematics and programming. Recent efforts have sought to extend this paradigm to open-ended tasks by employing LLMs-as-a-Judge to provide sequence-level rewards for policy optimization. However, these rewards are inherently sparse, failing to provide the fine-grained supervision necessary for generating complex, long-form trajectories. Furthermore, current work treats the Judge as a black-box oracle, discarding the rich intermediate feedback signals encoded in it. To address these limitations, we introduce Grad2Reward, a novel framework that extracts dense process rewards directly from the Judge's model inference process via a single backward pass. By leveraging gradient-based attribution, Grad2Reward enables precise token-level credit assignment, substantially enhancing training efficiency and reasoning quality. Additionally, Grad2Reward introduces a self-judging mechanism, allowing the policy to improve through its own evaluative signals without training specialized reward models or reliance on superior external Judges. The experiments demonstrate that policies optimized with Grad2Reward achieve outstanding performance across diverse open-ended tasks, affirming its effectiveness and broad generalizability.

AI.tldr: Grad2Reward extracts dense, token-level rewards from a Judge's inference process via a single backward pass, enabling fine-grained credit assignment and self-judging for RL with verifiable rewards in open-ended tasks.
AI.motivation: Current RLVR setups rely on sparse, sequence-level rewards and treat the Judge as a black-box oracle, limiting supervision and learning efficiency for long-form reasoning tasks.
AI.method: Leverages gradient-based attribution to derive dense process rewards from the Judge's model inference in a single backward pass, enabling token-level credit assignment. Introduces a self-judging mechanism so the policy can improve using evaluative signals without external reward models.
AI.result: Empirical results show policies trained with Grad2Reward achieve outstanding performance across diverse open-ended tasks, indicating strong effectiveness and broad generalizability.
AI.conclusion: Grad2Reward provides efficient, fine-grained credit assignment and self-evaluative capability, enhancing training efficiency and reasoning quality for open-ended RL with verifiable rewards.

title: Beyond Precision: Training-Inference Mismatch is an Optimization Problem and Simple LR Scheduling Fixes It
summary: Reinforcement Learning (RL) for training Large Language Models is notoriously unstable. While recent studies attribute this to "training inference mismatch stemming" from inconsistent hybrid engines, standard remedies, such as Importance Sampling, might fail during extended training runs. In this work, we analyze this instability through the lens of optimization, demonstrating that gradient noise and training-inference mismatch escalate in tandem as training progresses. Meanwhile, we find that the mismatch can be effectively suppressed by shrinking the update size. Taken together, we deduce that the mismatch is not merely a static numerical discrepancy, but a dynamic failure coupled with the model's optimization. Based on this insight, we propose a simple yet effective solution: a specialized Learning Rate (LR) scheduler. Instead of pre-defined decay schedule in traditional LR scheduler, our method dynamically triggers LR decay based on response length, which we identify as a reliable early-warning signal for impending instability. Empirical evidence suggests that by reducing the learning rate as gradient noise rises, we can consistently stabilize RL training and keep the training-inference mismatch at a safe level.

AI.tldr: Dynamic LR-based stabilization for RL training of LLMs: monitoring gradient noise and training-inference mismatch shows they escalate together; shrinking update size via a response-length–triggered LR decay stabilizes training and keeps mismatch at safe levels.
AI.motivation: RL for training large language models often becomes unstable due to a coupling between gradient noise and training-inference mismatch as training progresses. Traditional fixes like Importance Sampling can fail in long runs, motivating a deeper optimization-centric analysis and a simple stabilizing remedy.
AI.method: Analyze instability through optimization lens, establishing a tandem rise of gradient noise and training-inference mismatch. Argue that updating too aggressively worsens mismatch; propose a dynamic LR scheduler that decays based on response length (an early-warning signal) to reduce update size when instability nears. Implement and test this LR-triggered decay to suppress mismatch.
AI.result: Empirical results indicate that lowering learning rate as gradient noise increases stabilizes RL training and keeps training-inference mismatch at a safe level, enabling more reliable long-horizon training of LLMs with RL.
AI.conclusion: The mismatch is dynamic and tied to optimization dynamics, not a static numerical discrepancy. An adaptive LR strategy, triggered by response length, effectively suppresses mismatch and stabilizes RL training for LLMs without complex changes to the training loop.

title: Hyperbolic Graph Neural Networks Under the Microscope: The Role of Geometry-Task Alignment
summary: Many complex networks exhibit hyperbolic structural properties, making hyperbolic space a natural candidate for representing hierarchical and tree-like graphs with low distortion. Based on this observation, Hyperbolic Graph Neural Networks (HGNNs) have been widely adopted as a principled choice for representation learning on tree-like graphs. In this work, we question this paradigm by proposing an additional condition of geometry-task alignment, i.e., whether the metric structure of the target follows that of the input graph. We theoretically and empirically demonstrate the capability of HGNNs to recover low-distortion representations on two synthetic regression problems, and show that their geometric inductive bias becomes helpful when the problem requires preserving metric structure. Additionally, we evaluate HGNNs on the tasks of link prediction and node classification by jointly analyzing predictive performance and embedding distortion, revealing that only link prediction is geometry-aligned. Overall, our findings shift the focus from only asking "Is the graph hyperbolic?" to also questioning "Is the task aligned with hyperbolic geometry?", showing that HGNNs consistently outperform Euclidean models under such alignment, while their advantage vanishes otherwise.

AI.tldr: HGNNs are beneficial only when the task's metric structure aligns with hyperbolic geometry; otherwise, they offer no clear advantage over Euclidean GNNs.
AI.motivation: To move beyond the question 'Is the graph hyperbolic?' by introducing geometry-task alignment and evaluating when hyperbolic geometry is actually beneficial for a given task.
AI.method: Theoretical analysis plus experiments on two synthetic regression tasks showing low-distortion representations; empirical evaluation on link prediction and node classification, analyzing predictive performance and embedding distortion to assess geometry alignment.
AI.result: HGNNs can recover low-distortion representations on synthetic regression; geometry-aware bias helps when metric structure aligns; in link prediction and node classification, only link prediction demonstrates geometry alignment; overall HGNNs outperform Euclidean models under alignment, but lose advantage otherwise.
AI.conclusion: Propose shifting evaluation criterion to alignment between task and hyperbolic geometry; careful matching yields consistent HGNN advantages; otherwise, Euclidean models may suffice.

title: DOGMA: Weaving Structural Information into Data-centric Single-cell Transcriptomics Analysis
summary:
Recently, data-centric AI methodology has been a dominant paradigm in single-cell transcriptomics analysis, which treats data representation rather than model complexity as the fundamental bottleneck. In the review of current studies, earlier sequence methods treat cells as independent entities and adapt prevalent ML models to analyze their directly inherited sequence data. Despite their simplicity and intuition, these methods overlook the latent intercellular relationships driven by the functional mechanisms of biological systems and the inherent quality issues of the raw sequence data. Therefore, a series of structured methods has emerged. Although they employ various heuristic rules to capture intricate intercellular relationships and enhance the raw sequencing data, these methods often neglect biological prior knowledge. This omission incurs substantial overhead and yields suboptimal graph representations, thereby hindering the utility of ML models.
  To address them, we propose DOGMA, a holistic data-centric framework designed for the structural reshaping and semantic enhancement of raw data through multi-level biological prior knowledge. Transcending reliance on stochastic heuristics, DOGMA redefines graph construction by integrating Statistical Anchors with Cell Ontology and Phylogenetic Trees to enable deterministic structure discovery and robust cross-species alignment. Furthermore, Gene Ontology is utilized to bridge the feature-level semantic gap by incorporating functional priors. In complex multi-species and multi-organ benchmarks, DOGMA achieves SOTA performance, exhibiting superior zero-shot robustness and sample efficiency while operating with significantly lower computational cost.

AI.tldr: DOGMA is a data-centric framework for single-cell analysis that reshapes data structure and semantics by integrating rich biological priors, enabling deterministic graph construction and cross-species alignment for improved ML performance.
AI.motivation: Current single-cell methods either model cells as independent units or rely on heuristic, non-biologically-informed graph construction, leading to suboptimal representations and higher data/compute costs. A principled integration of biological priors is needed for robust, scalable analysis.
AI.method: DOGMA combines Statistical Anchors, Cell Ontology, and Phylogenetic Trees for deterministic graph construction; uses Gene Ontology to bridge feature-level semantics; enables cross-species alignment and semantic enhancement across multi-species/multi-organ datasets.
AI.result: On complex multi-species/multi-organ benchmarks, DOGMA achieves state-of-the-art performance, with superior zero-shot robustness and sample efficiency, while reducing computational cost compared to baselines.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating multi-level biological priors into a data-centric framework yields robust, efficient, cross-species single-cell analysis with improved graph representations and functional semantics, advancing practical ML utility in transcriptomics.

title: Prism: Efficient Test-Time Scaling via Hierarchical Search and Self-Verification for Discrete Diffusion Language Models
summary: Inference-time compute has re-emerged as a practical way to improve LLM reasoning. Most test-time scaling (TTS) algorithms rely on autoregressive decoding, which is ill-suited to discrete diffusion language models (dLLMs) due to their parallel decoding over the entire sequence. As a result, developing effective and efficient TTS methods to unlock dLLMs' full generative potential remains an underexplored challenge. To address this, we propose Prism (Pruning, Remasking, and Integrated Self-verification Method), an efficient TTS framework for dLLMs that (i) performs Hierarchical Trajectory Search (HTS) which dynamically prunes and reallocates compute in an early-to-mid denoising window, (ii) introduces Local branching with partial remasking to explore diverse implementations while preserving high-confidence tokens, and (iii) replaces external verifiers with Self-Verified Feedback (SVF) obtained via self-evaluation prompts on intermediate completions. Across four mathematical reasoning and code generation benchmarks on three dLLMs, including LLaDA 8B Instruct, Dream 7B Instruct, and LLaDA 2.0-mini, our Prism achieves a favorable performance-efficiency trade-off, matching best-of-N performance with substantially fewer function evaluations (NFE). The code is released at https://github.com/viiika/Prism.

AI.tldr: Prism is an efficient inference-time framework for discrete diffusion LLMs that uses hierarchical trajectory search, local branching with partial remasking, and self-verified feedback to achieve strong performance with far fewer function evaluations.
AI.motivation: Inference-time compute for discrete diffusion LLMs (dLLMs) is bottlenecked by parallel denoising and rich branching; existing test-time scaling (TTS) methods are ill-suited for dLLMs, necessitating new strategies to unlock their generative potential.
AI.method: Prism introduces: (i) Hierarchical Trajectory Search (HTS) that dynamically prunes and reallocates compute during early-to-mid denoising; (ii) Local branching with partial remasking to explore diverse completions while preserving high-confidence tokens; (iii) Self-Verified Feedback (SVF) via self-evaluation prompts on intermediate outputs to replace external verifiers.
AI.result: On four math reasoning and code-generation benchmarks across three dLLMs (LLaDA 8B Instruct, Dream 7B Instruct, LLaDA 2.0-mini), Prism achieves a favorable performance-efficiency trade-off, matching best-of-N with substantially fewer function evaluations; code released at https://github.com/viiika/Prism.
AI.conclusion: Prism provides an effective TTS framework for dLLMs, delivering competitive results with significantly reduced computation and enabling practical deployment of discrete diffusion-based language models.

title: No Generation without Representation: Efficient Causal Protein Language Models Enable Zero-Shot Fitness Estimation
summary: Protein language models (PLMs) face a fundamental divide: masked language models (MLMs) excel at fitness prediction while causal models enable generation, forcing practitioners to maintain separate architectures. We introduce \textbf{Proust}, a 309M-parameter causal PLM that bridges this gap through architectural innovations adapted from recent LLM research, including grouped-query attention with shared K/V projections, cross-layer value residuals, and depthwise causal convolutions. Trained on 33B tokens in 40 B200 GPU-hours, Proust achieves Spearman $ρ= 0.390$ on ProteinGym substitutions, competitive with MLMs requiring 50--200$\times$ the compute. On indels, Proust sets a new state-of-the-art, outperforming models up to 20$\times$ larger. On EVEREST viral fitness benchmarks, it approaches structure-aware methods using sequence alone. These powerful representations position Proust in a sweet spot as it also retains native generative capabilities that MLMs lack by design. Interpretability analysis reveals that per-position entropy variance predicts, to an extent, when retrieval augmentation helps and hurts. Such insights can grow in both quantity and quality at scale and inform capabilities such as test-time scaling. Code and weights are available at https://github.com/Furkan9015/proust-inference

AI.tldr: Proust: a 309M-parameter causal PLM that unifies generation and fitness prediction through architectural innovations, achieving strong substitutions and indels performance with far lower compute, and retaining generation capabilities.
AI.motivation: Addresses the fundamental divide in protein language models between masked models (fitness prediction) and causal models (generation) by introducing a unified, efficient causal architecture for proteins.
AI.method: Introduce Proust with grouped-query attention with shared K/V projections, cross-layer value residuals, and depthwise causal convolutions. Train on 33B tokens using 40B200 GPU-hours. Evaluate on ProteinGym substitutions and indels, EVEREST viral fitness benchmarks. Analyze interpretability via per-position entropy variance relating to retrieval augmentation.
AI.result: Spearman ρ = 0.390 on ProteinGym substitutions, competitive with MLMs that require 50–200× more compute. New state-of-the-art on indels, outperforming models up to 20× larger. On EVEREST, approaches structure-aware methods using sequence alone. Maintains native generative capabilities. Interpretability shows per-position entropy variance can predict when retrieval augmentation helps or hurts.
AI.conclusion: Proust occupies a sweet spot between generative and predictive capabilities in PLMs, scalable and adaptable at test time with meaningful interpretability signals; code and weights are publicly available.

title: FUPareto: Bridging the Forgetting-Utility Gap in Federated Unlearning via Pareto Augmented Optimization
summary: Federated Unlearning (FU) aims to efficiently remove the influence of specific client data from a federated model while preserving utility for the remaining clients. However, three key challenges remain: (1) existing unlearning objectives often compromise model utility or increase vulnerability to Membership Inference Attacks (MIA); (2) there is a persistent conflict between forgetting and utility, where further unlearning inevitably harms retained performance; and (3) support for concurrent multi-client unlearning is poor, as gradient conflicts among clients degrade the quality of forgetting. To address these issues, we propose FUPareto, an efficient unlearning framework via Pareto-augmented optimization. We first introduce the Minimum Boundary Shift (MBS) Loss, which enforces unlearning by suppressing the target class logit below the highest non-target class logit; this can improve the unlearning efficiency and mitigate MIA risks. During the unlearning process, FUPareto performs Pareto improvement steps to preserve model utility and executes Pareto expansion to guarantee forgetting. Specifically, during Pareto expansion, the framework integrates a Null-Space Projected Multiple Gradient Descent Algorithm (MGDA) to decouple gradient conflicts. This enables effective, fair, and concurrent unlearning for multiple clients while minimizing utility degradation. Extensive experiments across diverse scenarios demonstrate that FUPareto consistently outperforms state-of-the-art FU methods in both unlearning efficacy and retained utility.

AI.tldr: FUPareto introduces a Pareto-augmented optimization framework for federated unlearning, tackling utility loss, MIA vulnerability, and concurrent multi-client forgetting via a Minimum Boundary Shift loss and gradient-decoupling with Null-Space Projected MGDA.
AI.motivation: Federated learning models must remove the influence of specific clients' data efficiently without sacrificing overall utility, but current unlearning methods face (1) utility loss or increased MIA risk, (2) a fundamental forgetting-utility trade-off, and (3) poor support for concurrent multi-client unlearning due to gradient conflicts.
AI.method: 1) Minimum Boundary Shift (MBS) Loss to enforce forgetting by pushing the target class logit below the highest non-target logit. 2) Pareto improvement steps to preserve utility. 3) Pareto expansion to guarantee forgetting. 4) Null-Space Projected MGDA to decouple gradient conflicts across clients, enabling concurrent unlearning.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show FUPareto consistently outperforms state-of-the-art FU methods in both unlearning efficacy and retained utility across diverse scenarios.
AI.conclusion: A Pareto-augmented framework with MBS loss and gradient-decoupled optimization enables efficient, fair, and concurrent federated unlearning with improved utility preservation and forgetting guarantees.

title: Making Avatars Interact: Towards Text-Driven Human-Object Interaction for Controllable Talking Avatars
summary: Generating talking avatars is a fundamental task in video generation. Although existing methods can generate full-body talking avatars with simple human motion, extending this task to grounded human-object interaction (GHOI) remains an open challenge, requiring the avatar to perform text-aligned interactions with surrounding objects. This challenge stems from the need for environmental perception and the control-quality dilemma in GHOI generation. To address this, we propose a novel dual-stream framework, InteractAvatar, which decouples perception and planning from video synthesis for grounded human-object interaction. Leveraging detection to enhance environmental perception, we introduce a Perception and Interaction Module (PIM) to generate text-aligned interaction motions. Additionally, an Audio-Interaction Aware Generation Module (AIM) is proposed to synthesize vivid talking avatars performing object interactions. With a specially designed motion-to-video aligner, PIM and AIM share a similar network structure and enable parallel co-generation of motions and plausible videos, effectively mitigating the control-quality dilemma. Finally, we establish a benchmark, GroundedInter, for evaluating GHOI video generation. Extensive experiments and comparisons demonstrate the effectiveness of our method in generating grounded human-object interactions for talking avatars. Project page: https://interactavatar.github.io

AI.tldr: A dual-stream framework (InteractAvatar) decouples perception/planning from video synthesis to enable grounded human-object interaction for talking avatars, using PIM for perception and motion planning and AIM for audio-aware video synthesis; a motion-to-video aligner enables parallel co-generation and consistency, complemented by a new GroundedInter benchmark.
AI.motivation: To extend talking-avatar generation to grounded human-object interaction (GHOI), addressing environmental perception and the control-quality dilemma when coordinating object interactions with text-aligned speech.
AI.method: A dual-stream architecture: (1) Perception and Interaction Module (PIM) leveraging object/object-detection to perceive the environment and generate text-aligned interaction motions; (2) Audio-Interaction Aware Generation Module (AIM) to synthesize talking-avatar videos that exhibit object interactions; (3) a motion-to-video aligner to synchronize motion and video generation; modules share a common network structure to enable parallel co-generation; a new benchmark GroundedInter evaluates GHOI video generation.
AI.result: Extensive experiments and comparisons show the method effectively generates grounded human-object interactions for talking avatars and outperforms baselines on GroundedInter; a project page is provided for code/demos.
AI.conclusion: Decoupling perception/planning from video synthesis mitigates the control-quality dilemma in GHOI talking-avatar generation and enables parallel co-generation of motions and videos, with a dedicated benchmark validating effectiveness.

title: Designing Time Series Experiments in A/B Testing with Transformer Reinforcement Learning
summary: A/B testing has become a gold standard for modern technological companies to conduct policy evaluation. Yet, its application to time series experiments, where policies are sequentially assigned over time, remains challenging. Existing designs suffer from two limitations: (i) they do not fully leverage the entire history for treatment allocation; (ii) they rely on strong assumptions to approximate the objective function (e.g., the mean squared error of the estimated treatment effect) for optimizing the design. We first establish an impossibility theorem showing that failure to condition on the full history leads to suboptimal designs, due to the dynamic dependencies in time series experiments. To address both limitations simultaneously, we next propose a transformer reinforcement learning (RL) approach which leverages transformers to condition allocation on the entire history and employs RL to directly optimize the MSE without relying on restrictive assumptions. Empirical evaluations on synthetic data, a publicly available dispatch simulator, and a real-world ridesharing dataset demonstrate that our proposal consistently outperforms existing designs.

AI.tldr: Transformer RL approach for time-series A/B testing that uses full history to condition allocations and directly optimizes MSE of treatment effects, supported by an impossibility theorem and empirical validation showing improved performance over existing designs.
AI.motivation: Time-series experiments in policy evaluation are challenged by dynamic dependencies and underutilization of historical information; existing designs rely on restrictive surrogate objectives.
AI.method: Use a transformer-based reinforcement learning framework to condition treatment allocation on the entire history and directly optimize the mean-squared error of the estimated treatment effect without relying on approximate proxies.
AI.result: Empirical results on synthetic data, a public dispatch simulator, and a real ridesharing dataset show consistent performance gains over existing designs.
AI.conclusion: Conditioning on full history via transformer RL can address both key limitations in time-series A/B testing, yielding more efficient and accurate treatment allocations.

title: Toward Cognitive Supersensing in Multimodal Large Language Model
summary: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have achieved remarkable success in open-vocabulary perceptual tasks, yet their ability to solve complex cognitive problems remains limited, especially when visual details are abstract and require visual memory. Current approaches primarily scale Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning in the text space, even when language alone is insufficient for clear and structured reasoning, and largely neglect visual reasoning mechanisms analogous to the human visuospatial sketchpad and visual imagery. To mitigate this deficiency, we introduce Cognitive Supersensing, a novel training paradigm that endows MLLMs with human-like visual imagery capabilities by integrating a Latent Visual Imagery Prediction (LVIP) head that jointly learns sequences of visual cognitive latent embeddings and aligns them with the answer, thereby forming vision-based internal reasoning chains. We further introduce a reinforcement learning stage that optimizes text reasoning paths based on this grounded visual latent. To evaluate the cognitive capabilities of MLLMs, we present CogSense-Bench, a comprehensive visual question answering (VQA) benchmark assessing five cognitive dimensions. Extensive experiments demonstrate that MLLMs trained with Cognitive Supersensing significantly outperform state-of-the-art baselines on CogSense-Bench and exhibit superior generalization on out-of-domain mathematics and science VQA benchmarks, suggesting that internal visual imagery is potentially key to bridging the gap between perceptual recognition and cognitive understanding. We will open-source the CogSense-Bench and our model weights.

AI.tldr: Cognitive Supersensing: endows multimodal LLMs with grounded visual imagery via a latent visual imagery prediction head (LVIP) and RL to produce internal visual reasoning chains; evaluates on CogSense-Bench, showing strong out-of-domain generalization in math/science VQA; open-sourcing model and benchmark.
AI.motivation: MLLMs are strong at perceptual tasks but struggle with cognitive problems requiring visual memory and visuospatial reasoning; current text-centric CoT is insufficient when language alone cannot structure reasoning; need human-like visual imagery mechanisms.
AI.method: Introduce LVIP head that learns sequences of visual cognitive latent embeddings aligned with answers to form visual-based internal reasoning; train with a cognitively grounded objective; reinforcement learning stage optimizes text reasoning paths using the grounded visual latent; propose CogSense-Bench to assess five cognitive dimensions in VQA.
AI.result: MLLMs trained with Cognitive Supersensing outperform state-of-the-art baselines on CogSense-Bench and generalize better to out-of-domain math and science VQA benchmarks; demonstrates the utility of internal visual imagery for bridging perceptual recognition and cognitive understanding.
AI.conclusion: Open-source CogSense-Bench and model weights; internal visual imagery may be key to advancing cognitive capabilities in MLLMs.

title: Time2Vec Transformer for Robust Gesture Recognition from Low-Density sEMG
summary: Accurate and responsive myoelectric prosthesis control typically relies on complex, dense multi-sensor arrays, which limits consumer accessibility. This paper presents a novel, data-efficient deep learning framework designed to achieve precise and accurate control using minimal sensor hardware. Leveraging an external dataset of 8 subjects, our approach implements a hybrid Transformer optimized for sparse, two-channel surface electromyography (sEMG). Unlike standard architectures that use fixed positional encodings, we integrate Time2Vec learnable temporal embeddings to capture the stochastic temporal warping inherent in biological signals. Furthermore, we employ a normalized additive fusion strategy that aligns the latent distributions of spatial and temporal features, preventing the destructive interference common in standard implementations. A two-stage curriculum learning protocol is utilized to ensure robust feature extraction despite data scarcity. The proposed architecture achieves a state-of-the-art multi-subject F1-score of 95.7% $\pm$ 0.20% for a 10-class movement set, statistically outperforming both a standard Transformer with fixed encodings and a recurrent CNN-LSTM model. Architectural optimization reveals that a balanced allocation of model capacity between spatial and temporal dimensions yields the highest stability. Furthermore, while direct transfer to a new unseen subject led to poor accuracy due to domain shifts, a rapid calibration protocol utilizing only two trials per gesture recovered performance from 21.0% $\pm$ 2.98% to 96.9% $\pm$ 0.52%. By validating that high-fidelity temporal embeddings can compensate for low spatial resolution, this work challenges the necessity of high-density sensing. The proposed framework offers a robust, cost-effective blueprint for next-generation prosthetic interfaces capable of rapid personalization.

AI.tldr: Data-efficient 2-channel sEMG prosthesis control using a hybrid Transformer with Time2Vec temporal embeddings and normalized additive fusion; achieves state-of-the-art multi-subject 10-class F1 (95.7%); robust with curriculum learning and two-trial rapid calibration, suggesting temporal embeddings can compensate for low spatial resolution and reducing sensor density.
AI.motivation: Reduce reliance on dense sensor arrays for myoelectric prosthesis control by achieving high accuracy with minimal hardware through data-efficient learning.
AI.method: A hybrid Transformer optimized for sparse two-channel sEMG, incorporating Time2Vec learnable temporal embeddings, normalized additive fusion to align latent distributions of spatial and temporal features, and a two-stage curriculum learning protocol. Evaluated on an external dataset of 8 subjects.
AI.result: Achieves a multi-subject F1-score of 95.7% ± 0.20% for a 10-class movement set, outperforming a standard Transformer with fixed encodings and a CNN-LSTM. Balanced spatial/temporal model capacity yields maximal stability. Direct transfer to unseen subjects shows domain shifts; rapid calibration with two trials per gesture recovers performance from 21.0% ± 2.98% to 96.9% ± 0.52%.
AI.conclusion: High-fidelity temporal embeddings can compensate for low spatial resolution, challenging the need for high-density sensing. The framework presents a robust, cost-effective blueprint for rapid personalization in next-generation prosthetic interfaces.

title: Autocorrelated Optimize-via-Estimate: Predict-then-Optimize versus Finite-sample Optimal
summary: Models that directly optimize for out-of-sample performance in the finite-sample regime have emerged as a promising alternative to traditional estimate-then-optimize approaches in data-driven optimization. In this work, we compare their performance in the context of autocorrelated uncertainties, specifically, under a Vector Autoregressive Moving Average VARMA(p,q) process. We propose an autocorrelated Optimize-via-Estimate (A-OVE) model that obtains an out-of-sample optimal solution as a function of sufficient statistics, and propose a recursive form for computing its sufficient statistics. We evaluate these models on a portfolio optimization problem with trading costs. A-OVE achieves low regret relative to a perfect information oracle, outperforming predict-then-optimize machine learning benchmarks. Notably, machine learning models with higher accuracy can have poorer decision quality, echoing the growing literature in data-driven optimization. Performance is retained under small mis-specification.

AI.tldr: Autocorrelated, finite-sample optimization improves decision quality; A-OVE outperforms predict-then-optimize baselines and is robust to mild mis-specification in a VARMA-driven portfolio setting.
AI.motivation: To close the gap where data-driven decisions under finite samples with correlated uncertainty perform poorly; demonstrate that optimizing for out-of-sample performance yields better practical decisions than traditional estimate-then-optimize, even under model misspecification.
AI.method: Introduce autocorrelated Optimize-via-Estimate (A-OVE) that yields out-of-sample-optimal solutions as a function of sufficient statistics; derive a recursive form for updating those statistics; evaluate on a portfolio optimization problem with transaction costs and compare to predict-then-optimize ML baselines.
AI.result: A-OVE achieves low regret relative to a perfect-information oracle and consistently outperforms predict-then-optimize ML benchmarks; higher ML accuracy does not guarantee better decisions; performance persists under small mis-specification.
AI.conclusion: Directly optimizing for out-of-sample performance under autocorrelated uncertainty is beneficial for data-driven optimization; A-OVE offers a robust, computable approach and motivates further exploration of model misspecification and dynamic sufficient statistics in optimization with correlated disturbances.

title: Internal Flow Signatures for Self-Checking and Refinement in LLMs
summary: Large language models can generate fluent answers that are unfaithful to the provided context, while many safeguards rely on external verification or a separate judge after generation. We introduce \emph{internal flow signatures} that audit decision formation from depthwise dynamics at a fixed inter-block monitoring boundary. The method stabilizes token-wise motion via bias-centered monitoring, then summarizes trajectories in compact \emph{moving} readout-aligned subspaces constructed from the top token and its close competitors within each depth window. Neighboring window frames are aligned by an orthogonal transport, yielding depth-comparable transported step lengths, turning angles, and subspace drift summaries that are invariant to within-window basis choices. A lightweight GRU validator trained on these signatures performs self-checking without modifying the base model. Beyond detection, the validator localizes a culprit depth event and enables a targeted refinement: the model rolls back to the culprit token and clamps an abnormal transported step at the identified block while preserving the orthogonal residual. The resulting pipeline provides actionable localization and low-overhead self-checking from internal decision dynamics. \emph{Code is available at} \texttt{github.com/EavnJeong/Internal-Flow-Signatures-for-Self-Checking-and-Refinement-in-LLMs}.

AI.tldr: Introduces internal flow signatures to audit LLM decision dynamics for self-checking and targeted refinement without modifying the base model, via depthwise dynamics, moving subspaces, and a GRU validator.
AI.motivation: Address unfaithful outputs by auditing internal decision processes and enabling lightweight, self-contained verification and refinement without external judges or modifiers to the base model.
AI.method: Define internal flow signatures by monitoring bias-centered decision dynamics at fixed inter-block boundaries. Stabilize token-wise motion and construct moving readout-aligned subspaces from the top token and close competitors within each depth window. Align neighboring frames via orthogonal transport to obtain depth-comparable transported metrics (step lengths, turning angles, subspace drift). Train a lightweight GRU validator on these signatures to perform self-checking without altering the base model. If anomalies are detected, localize the culprit depth event and enable refinement by rolling back to the culprit token and clamping the abnormal transported step at that block while preserving the orthogonal residual.
AI.result: Stabilizes token-wise motion, enables self-checking without model modification, localizes culprit depth events, and supports targeted refinement with low overhead. Provides actionable localization from internal decision dynamics; code available at the provided repository.
AI.conclusion: The approach yields actionable localization and low-overhead self-checking derived from internal LLM decision dynamics, with a practical workflow for detection, localization, and refinement.

title: Multimodal UNcommonsense: From Odd to Ordinary and Ordinary to Odd
summary: Commonsense reasoning in multimodal contexts remains a foundational challenge in artificial intelligence. We introduce Multimodal UNcommonsense(MUN), a benchmark designed to evaluate models' ability to handle scenarios that deviate from typical visual or contextual expectations. MUN pairs visual scenes with surprising or unlikely outcomes described in natural language, prompting models to either rationalize seemingly odd images using everyday logic or uncover unexpected interpretations in ordinary scenes. To support this task, we propose a retrieval-based in-context learning (R-ICL) framework that transfers reasoning capabilities from larger models to smaller ones without additional training. Leveraging a novel Multimodal Ensemble Retriever (MER), our method identifies semantically relevant exemplars even when image and text pairs are deliberately discordant. Experiments show an average improvement of 8.3% over baseline ICL methods, highlighting the effectiveness of R-ICL in low-frequency, atypical settings. MUN opens new directions for evaluating and improving visual-language models' robustness and adaptability in real-world, culturally diverse, and non-prototypical scenarios.

AI.tldr: Introduce MUN benchmark for multimodal commonsense reasoning with surprising outcomes, and a retrieval-based in-context learning (R-ICL) framework using a Multimodal Ensemble Retriever (MER) to enable small models to leverage reasoning from larger models; achieves ~8.3% gains over baseline ICL in atypical settings.
AI.motivation: Address the gap in evaluating multimodal models on non-prototypical, surprising, and culturally diverse scenarios where typical visual or contextual cues fail, by providing a challenging benchmark and a method to transfer reasoning capabilities to smaller models.
AI.method: Propose Multimodal UNcommonsense (MUN) benchmark pairing visual scenes with unlikely outcomes described in natural language. Develop a retrieval-based in-context learning (R-ICL) framework that uses a Multimodal Ensemble Retriever (MER) to retrieve semantically relevant exemplars for in-context prompts, even when image-text pairs are discordant, enabling small models to reason with the help of large-model exemplars without additional training.
AI.result: Empirical results show an average 8.3% improvement over baseline in-context learning methods, demonstrating the effectiveness of R-ICL in handling low-frequency, atypical scenarios.
AI.conclusion: MUN advances evaluation and improvement of visual-language models by stressing robustness to non-prototypical, real-world contexts and supports efficient knowledge transfer from large to small models via retrieval-based prompting.

title: Observation-dependent Bayesian active learning via input-warped Gaussian processes
summary: Bayesian active learning relies on the precise quantification of predictive uncertainty to explore unknown function landscapes. While Gaussian process surrogates are the standard for such tasks, an underappreciated fact is that their posterior variance depends on the observed outputs only through the hyperparameters, rendering exploration largely insensitive to the actual measurements. We propose to inject observation-dependent feedback by warping the input space with a learned, monotone reparameterization. This mechanism allows the design policy to expand or compress regions of the input space in response to observed variability, thereby shaping the behavior of variance-based acquisition functions. We demonstrate that while such warps can be trained via marginal likelihood, a novel self-supervised objective yields substantially better performance. Our approach improves sample efficiency across a range of active learning benchmarks, particularly in regimes where non-stationarity challenges traditional methods.

AI.tldr: Introduce a monotone input warp for Gaussian process-based Bayesian active learning to inject observation-dependent feedback, enabling variance-based acquisition to respond to data; trainable via a self-supervised objective and marginal likelihood, with the self-supervised objective delivering notable performance gains in non-stationary settings.
AI.motivation: GP posterior variance, and thus exploration, is largely determined by hyperparameters rather than observed data. This limits adaptive sampling in non-stationary landscapes. A learned input transformation can reweight regions of input space to reflect observed variability, shaping acquisition behavior.
AI.method: Propose a learned, monotone input reparameterization (warp) of the input space, which the GP uses for training and acquisition. Warp parameters are learned to influence exploration—either via marginal likelihood optimization or a novel self-supervised objective that correlates the warp with predictive uncertainty.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation shows improved sample efficiency across active learning benchmarks, particularly under non-stationarity. The self-supervised training objective outperforms marginal likelihood alone.
AI.conclusion: Input-space warping provides observation-dependent control over GP uncertainty, enabling more efficient exploration in Bayesian active learning and better handling of non-stationarity.

title: Data- and Variance-dependent Regret Bounds for Online Tabular MDPs
summary: This work studies online episodic tabular Markov decision processes (MDPs) with known transitions and develops best-of-both-worlds algorithms that achieve refined data-dependent regret bounds in the adversarial regime and variance-dependent regret bounds in the stochastic regime. We quantify MDP complexity using a first-order quantity and several new data-dependent measures for the adversarial regime, including a second-order quantity and a path-length measure, as well as variance-based measures for the stochastic regime. To adapt to these measures, we develop algorithms based on global optimization and policy optimization, both built on optimistic follow-the-regularized-leader with log-barrier regularization. For global optimization, our algorithms achieve first-order, second-order, and path-length regret bounds in the adversarial regime, and in the stochastic regime, they achieve a variance-aware gap-independent bound and a variance-aware gap-dependent bound that is polylogarithmic in the number of episodes. For policy optimization, our algorithms achieve the same data- and variance-dependent adaptivity, up to a factor of the episode horizon, by exploiting a new optimistic $Q$-function estimator. Finally, we establish regret lower bounds in terms of data-dependent complexity measures for the adversarial regime and a variance measure for the stochastic regime, implying that the regret upper bounds achieved by the global-optimization approach are nearly optimal.

AI.tldr: Introduces data-dependent, best-of-both-worlds regret guarantees for online episodic tabular MDPs with known transitions via two algorithmic frameworks (global optimization and policy optimization) using optimistic log-barrier FTRL; achieves adversarial first-/second-order/path-length bounds and stochastic variance-aware bounds, with nearly optimal lower bounds.
AI.motivation: Unify regret guarantees across adversarial and stochastic MDP regimes by leveraging data-dependent complexity measures (first-/second-order, path-length, and variance-based quantities) to enable adaptive algorithms.
AI.method: Two algorithmic designs based on optimistic follow-the-regularized-leader (FTRL) with log-barrier regularization: (i) global-optimization approach yielding first-/second-order/path-length regret in adversarial MDPs and variance-aware bounds in stochastic MDPs; (ii) policy-optimization approach using a new optimistic Q-function estimator to achieve similar adaptivity (up to a horizon factor).
AI.result: Adversarial regime: first-order, second-order, and path-length regret bounds; stochastic regime: variance-aware gap-independent and variance-aware gap-dependent bounds polylog in the episode count; policy optimization attains the same adaptivity up to a factor of the horizon; lower bounds in terms of data-dependent complexity and variance, showing near-optimality of the upper bounds.
AI.conclusion: The work demonstrates nearly optimal, data-dependent, best-of-both-worlds regret for online episodic tabular MDPs with known transitions, advancing the understanding of how first-/second-order, path-length, and variance-based measures govern performance across regimes and supporting near-optimal lower bounds.

title: Towards Long-Horizon Interpretability: Efficient and Faithful Multi-Token Attribution for Reasoning LLMs
summary: Token attribution methods provide intuitive explanations for language model outputs by identifying causally important input tokens. However, as modern LLMs increasingly rely on extended reasoning chains, existing schemes face two critical challenges: (1) efficiency bottleneck, where attributing a target span of M tokens within a context of length N requires O(M*N) operations, making long-context attribution prohibitively slow; and (2) faithfulness drop, where intermediate reasoning tokens absorb attribution mass, preventing importance from propagating back to the original input. To address these, we introduce FlashTrace, an efficient multi-token attribution method that employs span-wise aggregation to compute attribution over multi-token targets in a single pass, while maintaining faithfulness. Moreover, we design a recursive attribution mechanism that traces importance through intermediate reasoning chains back to source inputs. Extensive experiments on long-context retrieval (RULER) and multi-step reasoning (MATH, MorehopQA) tasks demonstrate that FlashTrace achieves over 130x speedup over existing baselines while maintaining superior faithfulness. We further analyze the dynamics of recursive attribution, showing that even a single recursive hop improves faithfulness by tracing importance through the reasoning chain.

AI.tldr: FlashTrace combines span-wise aggregation for efficient multi-token attribution with a recursive mechanism to propagate importance through reasoning chains, achieving over 130x speedup and improved faithfulness on long-context and multi-step tasks.
AI.motivation: To overcome two core limitations of token attribution in modern LLMs: (1) high computational cost for attributing multi-token targets within long contexts (O(M*N)); and (2) faithfulness degradation where attribution mass concentrates on intermediate reasoning tokens, hindering propagation back to source inputs.
AI.method: Introduce span-wise aggregation to compute attribution for multi-token targets in a single pass, and implement a recursive attribution process that traces importance through intermediate reasoning steps back to the original inputs.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation shows FlashTrace delivers over 130x speedup over baselines on long-context retrieval (RULER) and multi-step reasoning tasks (MATH, MorehopQA) while maintaining superior faithfulness; analysis indicates that even a single recursive hop improves faithfulness by routing attribution through the reasoning chain.
AI.conclusion: FlashTrace offers an efficient and faithful solution for multi-token attribution in long-context and reasoning-based tasks, validating the effectiveness of span-wise aggregation and recursive attribution for robust explanations.

title: VLM-Guided Experience Replay
summary: Recent advances in Large Language Models (LLMs) and Vision-Language Models (VLMs) have enabled powerful semantic and multimodal reasoning capabilities, creating new opportunities to enhance sample efficiency, high-level planning, and interpretability in reinforcement learning (RL). While prior work has integrated LLMs and VLMs into various components of RL, the replay buffer, a core component for storing and reusing experiences, remains unexplored. We propose addressing this gap by leveraging VLMs to guide the prioritization of experiences in the replay buffer. Our key idea is to use a frozen, pre-trained VLM (requiring no fine-tuning) as an automated evaluator to identify and prioritize promising sub-trajectories from the agent's experiences. Across scenarios, including game-playing and robotics, spanning both discrete and continuous domains, agents trained with our proposed prioritization method achieve 11-52% higher average success rates and improve sample efficiency by 19-45% compared to previous approaches. https://esharony.me/projects/vlm-rb/

AI.tldr: A frozen, pre-trained Vision-Language Model (VLM) is used to automatically evaluate and prioritize promising sub-trajectories in an RL replay buffer, without fine-tuning. This prioritization improves sample efficiency and success rates across discrete and continuous domains (games and robotics) by 11–52% and 19–45%, respectively.
AI.motivation: To address the under-explored role of the replay buffer in RL, leveraging powerful multimodal models to guide experience prioritization can boost learning efficiency and planning interpretability without additional training of the evaluator.
AI.method: Employ a frozen pre-trained VLM as an automated evaluator that scores and selects high-potential sub-trajectories from agent experiences; no fine-tuning of the VLM; applicable to both discrete and continuous domains across game-playing and robotics.
AI.result: Prioritized replay guided by the VLM yields 11–52% higher average success rates and 19–45% improvements in sample efficiency over prior approaches.
AI.conclusion: VLM-driven, zero-shot evaluation of experiences is an effective, domain-general augmentation for RL replay buffers, offering gains in sample efficiency and performance without requiring model fine-tuning.

title: PIMPC-GNN: Physics-Informed Multi-Phase Consensus Learning for Enhancing Imbalanced Node Classification in Graph Neural Networks
summary: Graph neural networks (GNNs) often struggle in class-imbalanced settings, where minority classes are under-represented and predictions are biased toward majorities. We propose \textbf{PIMPC-GNN}, a physics-informed multi-phase consensus framework for imbalanced node classification. Our method integrates three complementary dynamics: (i) thermodynamic diffusion, which spreads minority labels to capture long-range dependencies, (ii) Kuramoto synchronisation, which aligns minority nodes through oscillatory consensus, and (iii) spectral embedding, which separates classes via structural regularisation. These perspectives are combined through class-adaptive ensemble weighting and trained with an imbalance-aware loss that couples balanced cross-entropy with physics-based constraints. Across five benchmark datasets and imbalance ratios from 5-100, PIMPC-GNN outperforms 16 state-of-the-art baselines, achieving notable gains in minority-class recall (up to +12.7\%) and balanced accuracy (up to +8.3\%). Beyond empirical improvements, the framework also provides interpretable insights into consensus dynamics in graph learning. The code is available at \texttt{https://github.com/afofanah/PIMPC-GNN}.

AI.tldr: PIMPC-GNN is a physics-informed, multi-phase consensus framework for imbalanced node classification in graphs, combining diffusion, synchronization, and spectral embedding with an imbalance-aware loss; it achieves state-of-the-art performance and provides interpretable consensus dynamics.
AI.motivation: GNNs underperform on minority classes due to class imbalance, requiring methods that capture long-range dependencies, consensus among minority nodes, and robust separation of classes while maintaining interpretability.
AI.method: Integrates three complementary dynamics: (i) thermodynamic diffusion to spread minority labels and capture long-range dependencies, (ii) Kuramoto synchronisation to align minority nodes through oscillatory consensus, and (iii) spectral embedding to separate classes via structural regularisation. These are fused via class-adaptive ensemble weighting and trained with an imbalance-aware loss that blends balanced cross-entropy with physics-informed constraints.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on five benchmark datasets with imbalance ratios from 5 to 100 shows PIMPC-GNN outperforms 16 state-of-the-art baselines, achieving up to +12.7 percentage points in minority-class recall and up to +8.3 points in balanced accuracy. The work also provides interpretable insights into consensus dynamics and supplies code for reproducibility.
AI.conclusion: The proposed physics-informed multi-phase framework yields substantial improvements for imbalanced graph learning, offering both strong empirical gains and interpretable dynamics, with practical reproducibility through available code.

title: Embedding Learning on Multiplex Networks for Link Prediction
summary: Over the past years, embedding learning on networks has shown tremendous results in link prediction tasks for complex systems, with a wide range of real-life applications. Learning a representation for each node in a knowledge graph allows us to capture topological and semantic information, which can be processed in downstream analyses later. In the link prediction task, high-dimensional network information is encoded into low-dimensional vectors, which are then fed to a predictor to infer new connections between nodes in the network. As the network complexity (that is, the numbers of connections and types of interactions) grows, embedding learning turns out increasingly challenging. This review covers published models on embedding learning on multiplex networks for link prediction. First, we propose refined taxonomies to classify and compare models, depending on the type of embeddings and embedding techniques. Second, we review and address the problem of reproducible and fair evaluation of embedding learning on multiplex networks for the link prediction task. Finally, we tackle evaluation on directed multiplex networks by proposing a novel and fair testing procedure. This review constitutes a crucial step towards the development of more performant and tractable embedding learning approaches for multiplex networks and their fair evaluation for the link prediction task. We also suggest guidelines on the evaluation of models, and provide an informed perspective on the challenges and tools currently available to address downstream analyses applied to multiplex networks.

AI.tldr: A review of embedding learning on multiplex networks for link prediction, proposing refined taxonomies, addressing reproducibility/fair evaluation, and introducing a fair testing procedure for directed multiplex networks, with guidelines and future perspectives.
AI.motivation: To tackle the growing complexity of embedding learning on multiplex networks for link prediction and to establish rigorous, fair, and reproducible evaluation frameworks.
AI.method: A literature review that (i) develops refined taxonomies of embedding types and techniques, (ii) analyzes reproducibility and fairness in evaluation, and (iii) proposes a novel testing procedure for directed multiplex networks, culminating in guidelines and practical recommendations.
AI.result: A structured framework for classifying models, a set of fairness/reproducibility considerations, and a new evaluation approach for directed multiplex networks, along with guidelines for downstream analyses.
AI.conclusion: The review lays groundwork for more effective and tractable multiplex-network embeddings and emphasizes standardized evaluation protocols and tools to support downstream link-prediction analyses.

title: Bayesian Integration of Nonlinear Incomplete Clinical Data
summary: Multimodal clinical data are characterized by high dimensionality, heterogeneous representations, and structured missingness, posing significant challenges for predictive modeling, data integration, and interpretability. We propose BIONIC (Bayesian Integration of Nonlinear Incomplete Clinical data), a unified probabilistic framework that integrates heterogeneous multimodal data under missingness through a joint generative-discriminative latent architecture. BIONIC uses pretrained embeddings for complex modalities such as medical images and clinical text, while incorporating structured clinical variables directly within a Bayesian multimodal formulation. The proposed framework enables robust learning in partially observed and semi-supervised settings by explicitly modeling modality-level and variable-level missingness, as well as missing labels. We evaluate BIONIC on three multimodal clinical and biomedical datasets, demonstrating strong and consistent discriminative performance compared to representative multimodal baselines, particularly under incomplete data scenarios. Beyond predictive accuracy, BIONIC provides intrinsic interpretability through its latent structure, enabling population-level analysis of modality relevance and supporting clinically meaningful insight.

AI.tldr: BIONIC is a Bayesian multimodal integration framework that handles incomplete clinical data with a joint generative-discriminative latent model, leveraging pretrained embeddings, and providing interpretable latent factors; it achieves strong predictive performance on three datasets under missing data.
AI.motivation: Clinical multimodal data are high-dimensional and heterogeneous with structured missingness, hindering predictive modeling, data integration, and interpretability; there's a need for a unified framework that can model missingness and support semi-supervised learning while preserving interpretability.
AI.method: A unified probabilistic model (BIONIC) with a joint generative-discriminative latent architecture; uses pretrained embeddings for modalities like images and text; integrates structured variables directly in a Bayesian multimodal formulation; models modality- and variable-level missingness and missing labels; supports semi-supervised learning.
AI.result: Evaluated on three multimodal clinical/biomedical datasets; outperforms representative multimodal baselines, especially when data are incomplete; robust discriminative performance across missingness scenarios.
AI.conclusion: BIONIC yields intrinsic interpretability through its latent structure, enabling population-level analysis of modality relevance and yielding clinically meaningful insights; the framework provides robust, interpretable integration for incomplete multimodal data.

title: COLT: Lightweight Multi-LLM Collaboration through Shared MCTS Reasoning for Model Compilation
summary: Model serving costs dominate AI systems, making compiler optimization essential for scalable deployment. Recent works show that a large language model (LLM) can guide compiler search by reasoning over program structure and optimization history. However, using a single large model throughout the search is expensive, while smaller models are less reliable when used alone. Thus, this paper seeks to answer whether multi-LLM collaborative reasoning relying primarily on small LLMs can match or exceed the performance of a single large model. As such, we propose a lightweight collaborative multi-LLM framework, dubbed COLT, for compiler optimization that enables coordinated reasoning across multiple models within a single Monte Carlo tree search (MCTS) process. A key contribution is the use of a single shared MCTS tree as the collaboration substrate across LLMs, enabling the reuse of transformation prefixes and cross-model value propagation. Hence, we circumvent both heavy internal reasoning mechanisms and conventional agentic machinery that relies on external planners, multiple concurrent LLMs, databases, external memory/versioning of intermediate results, and controllers by simply endogenizing model selection within the lightweight MCTS optimization loop. Every iteration, the acting LLM proposes a joint action: (compiler transformation, model to be queried next). We also introduce a model-aware tree policy that biases search toward smaller models while preserving exploration, and a course-alteration mechanism that escalates to the largest model when the search exhibits persistent regressions attributable to smaller models.

AI.tldr: COLT: a lightweight collaborative multi-LLM framework for compiler optimization that uses a shared MCTS tree to coordinate multiple small LLMs, with model-aware policies and escalation to larger models.
AI.motivation: Reduce costs and improve scalability of LLM-guided compiler optimization by relying primarily on small models; address reliability gaps of small models and avoid heavy external agents or planners.
AI.method: A single shared Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) tree serves as the collaboration substrate. Each iteration, the acting LLM proposes a joint action (compiler transformation, which model to query next). The framework includes a model-aware tree policy that biases toward smaller models while preserving exploration, and a course-alteration mechanism that escalates to the largest model if persistent regressions are attributable to smaller models. Cross-model value propagation and reuse of transformation prefixes occur within the shared tree to avoid external memory or planners.
AI.result: The abstract presents the COLT framework and its mechanisms but does not report empirical results. It claims potential efficiency gains from cross-model collaboration and reusing prefixes, with the ability to escalate to larger models to recover from regressions.
AI.conclusion: COLT offers a practical, cost-efficient approach to LLM-guided compiler optimization by coordinating multiple small LLMs through a single MCTS process, enabling adaptive model selection and cross-model learning without external agentic infrastructure.

title: PIMCST: Physics-Informed Multi-Phase Consensus and Spatio-Temporal Few-Shot Learning for Traffic Flow Forecasting
summary: Accurate traffic flow prediction remains a fundamental challenge in intelligent transportation systems, particularly in cross-domain, data-scarce scenarios where limited historical data hinders model training and generalisation. The complex spatio-temporal dependencies and nonlinear dynamics of urban mobility networks further complicate few-shot learning across different cities. This paper proposes MCPST, a novel Multi-phase Consensus Spatio-Temporal framework for few-shot traffic forecasting that reconceptualises traffic prediction as a multi-phase consensus learning problem. Our framework introduces three core innovations: (1) a multi-phase engine that models traffic dynamics through diffusion, synchronisation, and spectral embeddings for comprehensive dynamic characterisation; (2) an adaptive consensus mechanism that dynamically fuses phase-specific predictions while enforcing consistency; and (3) a structured meta-learning strategy for rapid adaptation to new cities with minimal data. We establish extensive theoretical guarantees, including representation theorems with bounded approximation errors and generalisation bounds for few-shot adaptation. Through experiments on four real-world datasets, MCPST outperforms fourteen state-of-the-art methods in spatio-temporal graph learning methods, dynamic graph transfer learning methods, prompt-based spatio-temporal prediction methods and cross-domain few-shot settings, improving prediction accuracy while reducing required training data and providing interpretable insights. The implementation code is available at https://github.com/afofanah/MCPST.

AI.tldr: MCPST is a multi-phase consensus spatio-temporal framework for few-shot cross-city traffic forecasting that combines diffusion, synchronization, and spectral embeddings, with adaptive consensus and structured meta-learning; backed by theoretical guarantees and strong empirical performance across four real datasets, outperforming 14 baselines; code available.
AI.motivation: Tackle data-scarce, cross-domain few-shot traffic forecasting by capturing complex spatio-temporal dependencies in urban mobility networks and enabling rapid adaptation to new cities with limited historical data.
AI.method: A three-phase engine (diffusion, synchronization, spectral embeddings) to characterize traffic dynamics; an adaptive consensus mechanism to fuse phase-specific predictions while enforcing consistency; a structured meta-learning strategy for rapid adaptation to new cities with minimal data; theoretical guarantees including representation theorems with bounded approximation errors and generalisation bounds.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on four real-world datasets shows MCPST outperforms fourteen state-of-the-art methods across spatio-temporal graph learning, dynamic graph transfer, prompt-based ST prediction, and cross-domain few-shot settings; achieves higher accuracy with reduced training data and provides interpretable insights.
AI.conclusion: MCPST provides a principled, theoretically backed solution for few-shot cross-city traffic forecasting, achieving strong performance and data efficiency, with available code for reproducibility.

title: T-LLM: Teaching Large Language Models to Forecast Time Series via Temporal Distillation
summary: Time series forecasting plays a critical role in decision-making across many real-world applications. Unlike data in vision and language domains, time series data is inherently tied to the evolution of underlying processes and can only accumulate as real-world time progresses, limiting the effectiveness of scale-driven pretraining alone. This time-bound constraint poses a challenge for enabling large language models (LLMs) to acquire forecasting capability, as existing approaches primarily rely on representation-level alignment or inference-time temporal modules rather than explicitly teaching forecasting behavior to the LLM. We propose T-LLM, a temporal distillation framework that equips general-purpose LLMs with time series forecasting capability by transferring predictive behavior from a lightweight temporal teacher during training. The teacher combines trend modeling and frequency-domain analysis to provide structured temporal supervision, and is removed entirely at inference, leaving the LLM as the sole forecasting model. Experiments on benchmark datasets and infectious disease forecasting tasks demonstrate that T-LLM consistently outperforms existing LLM-based forecasting methods under full-shot, few-shot, and zero-shot settings, while enabling a simple and efficient deployment pipeline.

AI.tldr: T-LLM introduces temporal distillation to teach LLMs time series forecasting by training with a lightweight temporal teacher that combines trend modeling and frequency-domain analysis; the teacher is removed at inference, leaving the LLM as the sole forecasting model. It outperforms existing LLM-based forecasting methods in full-shot, few-shot, and zero-shot settings and enables simple deployment.
AI.motivation: Time series data are inherently tied to evolving processes and grow with real time, limiting scale-driven pretraining. Current LLM-based forecasting relies on representation alignment or inference-time modules, not explicit forecasting behavior; thus LLMs struggle to learn forecasting from data with time constraints.
AI.method: Train an LLM with a temporal teacher that provides structured forecasting supervision. The teacher combines trend modeling and frequency-domain analysis to guide the LLM's forecasting behavior. The teacher is removed at inference time, so the LLM alone performs forecasting.
AI.result: Empirical evaluations on benchmark datasets and infectious disease forecasting tasks show that T-LLM consistently outperforms existing LLM-based forecasting methods under full-shot, few-shot, and zero-shot settings, while maintaining a simple and efficient deployment pipeline.
AI.conclusion: Temporal distillation is effective for instilling forecasting capabilities in general-purpose LLMs without increasing inference complexity, enabling practical deployment for time series forecasting.

title: Boundary-Constrained Diffusion Models for Floorplan Generation: Balancing Realism and Diversity
summary: Diffusion models have become widely popular for automated floorplan generation, producing highly realistic layouts conditioned on user-defined constraints. However, optimizing for perceptual metrics such as the Fréchet Inception Distance (FID) causes limited design diversity. To address this, we propose the Diversity Score (DS), a metric that quantifies layout diversity under fixed constraints. Moreover, to improve geometric consistency, we introduce a Boundary Cross-Attention (BCA) module that enables conditioning on building boundaries. Our experiments show that BCA significantly improves boundary adherence, while prolonged training drives diversity collapse undiagnosed by FID, revealing a critical trade-off between realism and diversity. Out-Of-Distribution evaluations further demonstrate the models' reliance on dataset priors, emphasizing the need for generative systems that explicitly balance fidelity, diversity, and generalization in architectural design tasks.

AI.tldr: Introduces Diversity Score to quantify layout diversity under fixed constraints and Boundary Cross-Attention to improve boundary-conditioned floorplan generation; reveals a realism-diversity trade-off and dataset-prior reliance in OOD settings.
AI.motivation: Address the predominance of perceptual realism (FID) in diffusion-based floorplan generation, which suppresses design diversity, and the need for metrics and mechanisms to balance fidelity, diversity, and generalization in architectural design tasks.
AI.method: Propose Diversity Score (DS) as a diversity metric under fixed constraints and a Boundary Cross-Attention (BCA) module for conditioning on building boundaries; evaluate on realism, boundary adherence, and diversity, including OOD analysis.
AI.result: BCA significantly improves boundary adherence; longer training leads to diversity collapse not detected by FID; DS provides a measure of diversity; OOD evaluations show models rely on dataset priors.
AI.conclusion: There is a critical trade-off between fidelity and diversity in architectural design generation; future work should balance fidelity, diversity, and generalization, possibly via explicit multi-objective frameworks and robust diversity metrics.

title: Deep Multivariate Models with Parametric Conditionals
summary: We consider deep multivariate models for heterogeneous collections of random variables. In the context of computer vision, such collections may e.g. consist of images, segmentations, image attributes, and latent variables. When developing such models, most existing works start from an application task and design the model components and their dependencies to meet the needs of the chosen task. This has the disadvantage of limiting the applicability of the resulting model for other downstream tasks. Here, instead, we propose to represent the joint probability distribution by means of conditional probability distributions for each group of variables conditioned on the rest. Such models can then be used for practically any possible downstream task. Their learning can be approached as training a parametrised Markov chain kernel by maximising the data likelihood of its limiting distribution. This has the additional advantage of allowing a wide range of semi-supervised learning scenarios.

AI.tldr: Proposes a modular joint model built from conditional distributions of variable groups conditioned on the rest; trained via maximizing the likelihood of a Markov chain's stationary distribution, enabling broad downstream tasks and semi-supervised learning.
AI.motivation: Current models are often designed for a specific application task, limiting generality. A joint distribution represented via conditionals offers task-agnostic flexibility and better support for downstream tasks and semi-supervised learning.
AI.method: Factor the joint distribution into conditional distributions for each group conditioned on the others; learn a parametrised Markov chain kernel by maximizing the data likelihood of its limiting (stationary) distribution.
AI.result: Provides a general, flexible probabilistic framework for heterogeneous variable groups that can accommodate a wide range of downstream tasks and semi-supervised scenarios.
AI.conclusion: This conditional-factorized, Markov-chain-based approach yields a broadly applicable probabilistic modeling paradigm that supports diverse downstream tasks and semi-supervised learning.

title: Contribution-aware Token Compression for Efficient Video Understanding via Reinforcement Learning
comment: This paper is accepted by AAAI2026
summary: Video large language models have demonstrated remarkable capabilities in video understanding tasks. However, the redundancy of video tokens introduces significant computational overhead during inference, limiting their practical deployment. Many compression algorithms are proposed to prioritize retaining features with the highest attention scores to minimize perturbations in attention computations. However, the correlation between attention scores and their actual contribution to correct answers remains ambiguous. To address the above limitation, we propose a novel \textbf{C}ontribution-\textbf{a}ware token \textbf{Co}mpression algorithm for \textbf{VID}eo understanding (\textbf{CaCoVID}) that explicitly optimizes the token selection policy based on the contribution of tokens to correct predictions. First, we introduce a reinforcement learning-based framework that optimizes a policy network to select video token combinations with the greatest contribution to correct predictions. This paradigm shifts the focus from passive token preservation to active discovery of optimal compressed token combinations. Secondly, we propose a combinatorial policy optimization algorithm with online combination space sampling, which dramatically reduces the exploration space for video token combinations and accelerates the convergence speed of policy optimization. Extensive experiments on diverse video understanding benchmarks demonstrate the effectiveness of CaCoVID. Codes will be released.

AI.tldr: Contribution-aware video token compression (CaCoVID) uses reinforcement learning and online combinatorial policy optimization to select token subsets that most contribute to correct predictions, reducing inference cost in video LLMs.
AI.motivation: Mitigate high computational overhead from redundant video tokens by aligning token selection with actual predictive contribution rather than relying on attention scores alone.
AI.method: A RL-based policy network optimizes token selection; an online combinatorial space sampling strategy reduces exploration and accelerates convergence; evaluated on diverse video understanding benchmarks.
AI.result: Empirical results show effectiveness in preserving accuracy with compressed tokens; claims of improved efficiency; code will be released.
AI.conclusion: CaCoVID demonstrates that contribution-aware token selection is a viable path to efficient video understanding in LLMs, with potential for practical deployment.

title: Grounding Generated Videos in Feasible Plans via World Models
summary: Large-scale video generative models have shown emerging capabilities as zero-shot visual planners, yet video-generated plans often violate temporal consistency and physical constraints, leading to failures when mapped to executable actions. To address this, we propose Grounding Video Plans with World Models (GVP-WM), a planning method that grounds video-generated plans into feasible action sequences using a learned action-conditioned world model. At test-time, GVP-WM first generates a video plan from initial and goal observations, then projects the video guidance onto the manifold of dynamically feasible latent trajectories via video-guided latent collocation.
