In particular, we formulate grounding as a goal-conditioned latent-space trajectory optimization problem that jointly optimizes latent states and actions under world-model dynamics, while preserving semantic alignment with the video-generated plan. Empirically, GVP-WM recovers feasible long-horizon plans from zero-shot image-to-video-generated and motion-blurred videos that violate physical constraints, across navigation and manipulation simulation tasks.

AI.tldr: Ground video-generated plans into executable action sequences via a world-model–based latent trajectory optimization, enabling feasible long-horizon control from zero-shot video plans.
AI.motivation: Video-generated plans frequently violate temporal consistency and physical constraints; a grounding mechanism is needed to map them to dynamically feasible actions.
AI.method: Grounding is achieved with Grounding Video Plans with World Models (GVP-WM): a two-stage approach where a video plan is first generated from start/goal observations, then projected onto the manifold of feasible latent trajectories through video-guided latent collocation. This solves a goal-conditioned latent-space trajectory optimization problem that jointly optimizes latent states and actions under world-model dynamics while preserving semantic alignment with the video plan.
AI.result: Empirically, GVP-WM recovers feasible long-horizon plans from zero-shot image-to-video-generated and motion-blurred videos that violate physical constraints, across navigation and manipulation simulations.
AI.conclusion: GVP-WM bridges video planning and actionable control by grounding plans in a learned world model, improving feasibility and robustness; performance depends on the accuracy of the world model and the alignment between video guidance and latent trajectories.

title: Self-Consolidation for Self-Evolving Agents
summary: While large language model (LLM) agents have demonstrated impressive problem-solving capabilities, they typically operate as static systems, lacking the ability to evolve through lifelong interaction. Existing attempts to bridge this gap primarily rely on retrieving successful past trajectories as demonstrations. However, this paradigm faces two critical limitations. First, by focusing solely on success, agents overlook the rich pedagogical value embedded in failed attempts, preventing them from identifying and avoiding recurrent pitfalls. Second, continually accumulating textual experiences not only increases the time consumption during retrieval but also inevitably introduces noise and exhausts the largest context window of current LLMs. To address these challenges, we propose a novel self-evolving framework for LLM agents that introduces a complementary evolution mechanism: First, a contrastive reflection strategy is introduced to explicitly summarize error-prone patterns and capture reusable insights. Second, we propose a self-consolidation mechanism that distills non-parametric textual experience into compact learnable parameters. This enables the agent to internalize extensive historical experience directly into its latent space. Extensive experiments demonstrate the advantages of our method in long-term agent evolution.

AI.tldr: Proposes a self-evolving framework for LLM agents that learns from failed attempts through contrastive reflection and compresses textual experience into learnable parameters, addressing retrieval noise and context-window limits for improved long-term agent evolution.
AI.motivation: Current lifelong-learning approaches rely on past successful trajectories, ignoring failed attempts and suffering from noise and context-window limitations; this limits long-term evolution of LLM agents.
AI.method: Introduce two mechanisms: (1) contrastive reflection to summarize error-prone patterns and extract reusable insights from failures; (2) self-consolidation to distill non-parametric textual history into compact learnable parameters, embedding historical experience into the model's latent space.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show the proposed framework improves long-term evolution of LLM agents compared to baselines.
AI.conclusion: The framework enables more robust, scalable lifelong evolution by leveraging failures and compressing experiences into internal representations, mitigating retrieval noise and context-window constraints.

title: IntraSlice: Towards High-Performance Structural Pruning with Block-Intra PCA for LLMs
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) achieve strong performance across diverse tasks but face deployment challenges due to their massive size. Structured pruning offers acceleration benefits but leads to significant performance degradation. Recent PCA-based pruning methods have alleviated this issue by retaining key activation components, but are only applied between modules in order to fuse the transformation matrix, which introduces extra parameters and severely disrupts activation distributions due to residual connections. To address these issues, we propose IntraSlice, a framework that applies block-wise module-intra PCA compression pruning. By leveraging the structural characteristics of Transformer modules, we design an approximate PCA method whose transformation matrices can be fully fused into the model without additional parameters. We also introduce a PCA-based global pruning ratio estimator that further considers the distribution of compressed activations, building on conventional module importance. We validate our method on Llama2, Llama3, and Phi series across various language benchmarks. Experimental results demonstrate that our approach achieves superior compression performance compared to recent baselines at the same compression ratio or inference speed.

AI.tldr: IntraSlice introduces block-wise intra-module PCA pruning for Transformer-based LLMs to achieve higher compression with minimal performance loss, by fusing PCA transformations into the model and using a global pruning ratio estimator that accounts for activation distributions.
AI.motivation: LLMs are large and costly; structured pruning helps speed but often degrades performance. PCA-based pruning improved retention of key activation components but was applied only between modules, causing extra parameters and disrupted activation distributions due to residual connections. There is a need for intra-module, parameter-free transformation fusion and better pruning ratio estimation.
AI.method: Propose IntraSlice: a block-wise, module-intra PCA pruning framework for Transformer modules. Develop an approximate PCA that yields transformation matrices that can be fully fused into the model without additional parameters. Introduce a PCA-based global pruning ratio estimator that accounts for the distribution of compressed activations while building on module importance. Validate on Llama2, Llama3, and Phi series across benchmarks.
AI.result: Achieves superior compression performance relative to recent baselines at the same compression ratio or inference speed across multiple LLM families (Llama2/3, Phi series).
AI.conclusion: IntraSlice effectively enables higher compression with preserved activation distributions by intra-module PCA and parameter-free fusion, supported by a global pruning ratio estimator, demonstrating strong generalization across major LLM families.

title: FlyPrompt: Brain-Inspired Random-Expanded Routing with Temporal-Ensemble Experts for General Continual Learning
summary: General continual learning (GCL) challenges intelligent systems to learn from single-pass, non-stationary data streams without clear task boundaries. While recent advances in continual parameter-efficient tuning (PET) of pretrained models show promise, they typically rely on multiple training epochs and explicit task cues, limiting their effectiveness in GCL scenarios. Moreover, existing methods often lack targeted design and fail to address two fundamental challenges in continual PET: how to allocate expert parameters to evolving data distributions, and how to improve their representational capacity under limited supervision. Inspired by the fruit fly's hierarchical memory system characterized by sparse expansion and modular ensembles, we propose FlyPrompt, a brain-inspired framework that decomposes GCL into two subproblems: expert routing and expert competence improvement. FlyPrompt introduces a randomly expanded analytic router for instance-level expert activation and a temporal ensemble of output heads to dynamically adapt decision boundaries over time. Extensive theoretical and empirical evaluations demonstrate FlyPrompt's superior performance, achieving up to 11.23%, 12.43%, and 7.62% gains over state-of-the-art baselines on CIFAR-100, ImageNet-R, and CUB-200, respectively. Our source code is available at https://github.com/AnAppleCore/FlyGCL.

AI.tldr: FlyPrompt is a brain-inspired GCL framework that uses a randomly expanded router to activate a modular set of experts per instance and a temporal ensemble of output heads to adapt decision boundaries, achieving noticeable gains on standard benchmarks.
AI.motivation: General continual learning (GCL) requires learning from single-pass, non-stationary data streams without clear task boundaries. Pet-based fine-tuning methods often rely on multiple epochs and task cues, and struggle with allocating expanding experts and boosting representation under limited supervision in GCL.
AI.method: Two subproblems are addressed: expert routing and expert competence improvement. FlyPrompt employs a randomly expanded analytic router to enable instance-level expert activation and a temporal ensemble of output heads to dynamically adjust decision boundaries over time. The approach is inspired by the fruit fly's sparse expansion and modular ensembles, forming a hierarchical memory-like structure to support continual PET.
AI.result: Empirical and theoretical evaluation shows FlyPrompt outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, with gains of up to 11.23% (CIFAR-100), 12.43% (ImageNet-R), and 7.62% (CUB-200). The authors provide analysis across multiple benchmarks and release source code at the given repository.
AI.conclusion: FlyPrompt demonstrates the effectiveness of brain-inspired, modular, and temporally adaptive routing and ensembling for GCL under limited supervision, offering a scalable pathway to improve continual PET without requiring task labels or extensive training epochs.

title: SAME: Stabilized Mixture-of-Experts for Multimodal Continual Instruction Tuning
summary: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) achieve strong performance through instruction tuning, but real-world deployment requires them to continually expand their capabilities, making Multimodal Continual Instruction Tuning (MCIT) essential. Recent methods leverage sparse expert routing to promote task specialization, but we find that the expert routing process suffers from drift as the data distribution evolves. For example, a grounding query that previously activated localization experts may instead be routed to irrelevant experts after learning OCR tasks. Meanwhile, the grounding-related experts can be overwritten by new tasks and lose their original functionality. Such failure reflects two problems: router drift, where expert selection becomes inconsistent over time, and expert drift, where shared experts are overwritten across tasks. Therefore, we propose StAbilized Mixture-of-Experts (SAME) for MCIT. To address router drift, SAME stabilizes expert selection by decomposing routing dynamics into orthogonal subspaces and updating only task-relevant directions. To mitigate expert drift, we regulate expert updates via curvature-aware scaling using historical input covariance in a rehearsal-free manner. SAME also introduces adaptive expert activation to freeze selected experts during training, reducing redundant computation and cross-task interference. Extensive experiments demonstrate its SOTA performance.

AI.tldr: SAME stabilizes mixture-of-experts for Multimodal Continual Instruction Tuning by preventing router drift and expert drift through subspace routing, curvature-aware updates, and adaptive activation, achieving state-of-the-art results with rehearsal-free training.
AI.motivation: MLLMs must continually expand capabilities; sparse expert routing is prone to router drift (inconsistent task routing) and expert drift (shared experts overwritten across tasks), degrading performance on new tasks and degrading previously learned skills.
AI.method: 1) Decompose routing dynamics into orthogonal subspaces and update only task-relevant directions to stabilize router choices. 2) Use curvature-aware scaling based on historical input covariance to regulate updates (rehearsal-free). 3) Introduce adaptive expert activation to freeze selected experts during training to reduce computation and cross-task interference.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show state-of-the-art performance on MCIT benchmarks, with improved stability and efficiency due to reduced redundant computation and preserved task-specific expertise.
AI.conclusion: SAME provides a practical, scalable approach to MCIT by addressing both router and expert drift, enabling robust continual multimodal instruction tuning without rehearsal and with adaptive computation.

title: FreshMem: Brain-Inspired Frequency-Space Hybrid Memory for Streaming Video Understanding
summary: Transitioning Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) from offline to online streaming video understanding is essential for continuous perception. However, existing methods lack flexible adaptivity, leading to irreversible detail loss and context fragmentation. To resolve this, we propose FreshMem, a Frequency-Space Hybrid Memory network inspired by the brain's logarithmic perception and memory consolidation. FreshMem reconciles short-term fidelity with long-term coherence through two synergistic modules: Multi-scale Frequency Memory (MFM), which projects overflowing frames into representative frequency coefficients, complemented by residual details to reconstruct a global historical "gist"; and Space Thumbnail Memory (STM), which discretizes the continuous stream into episodic clusters by employing an adaptive compression strategy to distill them into high-density space thumbnails. Extensive experiments show that FreshMem significantly boosts the Qwen2-VL baseline, yielding gains of 5.20%, 4.52%, and 2.34% on StreamingBench, OV-Bench, and OVO-Bench, respectively. As a training-free solution, FreshMem outperforms several fully fine-tuned methods, offering a highly efficient paradigm for long-horizon streaming video understanding.

AI.tldr: FreshMem is a training-free, frequency-space memory architecture for online streaming video understanding that improves the Qwen2-VL baseline on multiple benchmarks via two modules: Multi-scale Frequency Memory (MFM) and Space Thumbnail Memory (STM).
AI.motivation: To enable flexible, long-horizon streaming video understanding in Multimodal LLMs by preserving both short-term fidelity and long-term coherence, avoiding irreversible detail loss and context fragmentation.
AI.method: FreshMem uses two synergistic modules. (1) Multi-scale Frequency Memory (MFM): projects overflowing frames into representative frequency coefficients plus residuals to reconstruct a global historical 'gist'. (2) Space Thumbnail Memory (STM): discretizes the continuous stream into episodic clusters using adaptive compression to distill them into high-density space thumbnails, enabling efficient long-horizon memory.
AI.result: Empirical gains of 5.20%, 4.52%, and 2.34% on StreamingBench, OV-Bench, and OVO-Bench respectively compared to the Qwen2-VL baseline. It is presented as a training-free solution that outperforms several fully fine-tuned methods.
AI.conclusion: FreshMem provides a memory-augmented, brain-inspired approach for long-horizon streaming video understanding that is both efficient (training-free) and effective, offering a robust paradigm for continuous perception in MLLMs.

title: Optimizing Tensor Train Decomposition in DNNs for RISC-V Architectures Using Design Space Exploration and Compiler Optimizations
comment: 36 pages, 16 figures, this is the author-accepted version of the article published in ACM Transactions on Embedded Computing Systems (TECS), Vol. 24, No. 6
summary: Deep neural networks (DNNs) have become indispensable in many real-life applications like natural language processing, and autonomous systems. However, deploying DNNs on resource-constrained devices, e.g., in RISC-V platforms, remains challenging due to the high computational and memory demands of fully connected (FC) layers, which dominate resource consumption. Low-rank factorization (LRF) offers an effective approach to compressing FC layers, but the vast design space of LRF solutions involves complex trade-offs among FLOPs, memory size, inference time, and accuracy, making the LRF process complex and time-consuming. This paper introduces an end-to-end LRF design space exploration methodology and a specialized design tool for optimizing FC layers on RISC-V processors. Using Tensor Train Decomposition (TTD) offered by TensorFlow T3F library, the proposed work prunes the LRF design space by excluding first, inefficient decomposition shapes and second, solutions with poor inference performance on RISC-V architectures. Compiler optimizations are then applied to enhance custom T3F layer performance, minimizing inference time and boosting computational efficiency. On average, our TT-decomposed layers run 3x faster than IREE and 8x faster than Pluto on the same compressed model. This work provides an efficient solution for deploying DNNs on edge and embedded devices powered by RISC-V architectures.

AI.tldr: End-to-end factorization-based FC layer compression on RISC-V via Tensor Train Decomposition (TTD) with design space exploration and compiler optimizations; achieves substantial speedups (3x vs IREE, 8x vs Pluto) on the same compressed model.
AI.motivation: DNNs, especially FC layers, are heavy for resource-constrained devices; LRF reduces size but design space is large; need an efficient method and tooling.
AI.method: Proposes end-to-end LRF design space exploration; prunes design space by excluding inefficient shapes; filters by on-device inference performance on RISC-V; uses Tensor Train Decomposition via TensorFlow T3F; applies custom compiler optimizations to T3F layers; evaluates against IREE and Pluto.
AI.result: TT-decomposed layers run 3x faster than IREE and 8x faster than Pluto on the same compressed model; improved inference time and efficiency on RISC-V; architecture-specific optimizations applied.
AI.conclusion: Provides an efficient, end-to-end framework and tool for deploying DNN FC layers on edge/embedded RISC-V devices; demonstrates practical gains and reduces design-space exploration burden.

title: On the Limits of Layer Pruning for Generative Reasoning in LLMs
summary: Recent works have shown that layer pruning can compress large language models (LLMs) while retaining strong performance on classification benchmarks with little or no finetuning. However, existing pruning techniques often suffer severe degradation on generative reasoning tasks. Through a systematic study across multiple model families, we find that tasks requiring multi-step reasoning are particularly sensitive to depth reduction. Beyond surface-level text degeneration, we observe degradation of critical algorithmic capabilities, including arithmetic computation for mathematical reasoning and balanced parenthesis generation for code synthesis. Under realistic post-training constraints, without access to pretraining-scale data or compute, we evaluate a simple mitigation strategy based on supervised finetuning with Self-Generated Responses. This approach achieves strong recovery on classification tasks, retaining up to 90\% of baseline performance, and yields substantial gains of up to 20--30 percentage points on generative benchmarks compared to prior post-pruning techniques. Crucially, despite these gains, recovery for generative reasoning remains fundamentally limited relative to classification tasks and is viable primarily at lower pruning ratios. Overall, we characterize the practical limits of layer pruning for generative reasoning and provide guidance on when depth reduction can be applied effectively under constrained post-training regimes.

AI.tldr: Post-training layer pruning with Self-Generated Responses recovers classification performance substantially and provides notable gains on generative benchmarks, but generative reasoning remains limited under depth reduction, especially at higher pruning ratios.
AI.motivation: To characterize the practical limits of layer pruning for generative reasoning in LLMs under constrained post-training, without access to pretraining-scale data or compute.
AI.method: Systematic evaluation across multiple model families to assess depth pruning effects on multi-step reasoning tasks; analysis of algorithmic capabilities (e.g., arithmetic, balanced parentheses for code synthesis); introduction of supervised finetuning with Self-Generated Responses (SGR) as a mitigation; comparison against prior post-pruning techniques.
AI.result: Recovery of classification performance up to ~90% of baseline; generative benchmarks improved by 20–30 percentage points over prior post-pruning methods; generative reasoning recovery remains fundamentally limited and viable primarily at lower pruning ratios.
AI.conclusion: Layer pruning has practical limits for generative reasoning; SGR-based supervised finetuning provides substantial gains under constrained post-training but cannot fully close the gap with full models; provides guidelines on when depth reduction is feasible under post-training constraints.

title: Cross-Modal Alignment and Fusion for RGB-D Transmission-Line Defect Detection
summary: Transmission line defect detection remains challenging for automated UAV inspection due to the dominance of small-scale defects, complex backgrounds, and illumination variations. Existing RGB-based detectors, despite recent progress, struggle to distinguish geometrically subtle defects from visually similar background structures under limited chromatic contrast. This paper proposes CMAFNet, a Cross-Modal Alignment and Fusion Network that integrates RGB appearance and depth geometry through a principled purify-then-fuse paradigm. CMAFNet consists of a Semantic Recomposition Module that performs dictionary-based feature purification via a learned codebook to suppress modality-specific noise while preserving defect-discriminative information, and a Contextual Semantic Integration Framework that captures global spatial dependencies using partial-channel attention to enhance structural semantic reasoning. Position-wise normalization within the purification stage enforces explicit reconstruction-driven cross-modal alignment, ensuring statistical compatibility between heterogeneous features prior to fusion. Extensive experiments on the TLRGBD benchmark, where 94.5% of instances are small objects, demonstrate that CMAFNet achieves 32.2% mAP@50 and 12.5% APs, outperforming the strongest baseline by 9.8 and 4.0 percentage points, respectively. A lightweight variant reaches 24.8% mAP50 at 228 FPS with only 4.9M parameters, surpassing all YOLO-based detectors while matching transformer-based methods at substantially lower computational cost.

AI.tldr: CMAFNet advances RGB-D fusion for UAV-based line defect detection by purifying RGB/Depth features with a dictionary-based module and fusing them via cross-modal alignment and partial-channel attention, achieving state-of-the-art results with a lightweight variant that is real-time.
AI.motivation: RGB-based defect detectors struggle with small, geometrically subtle defects in cluttered, low-contrast backgrounds; integrating depth geometry can improve discrimination, but effective cross-modal alignment and noise suppression are needed.
AI.method: A Cross-Modal Alignment and Fusion Network (CMAFNet) with: (1) Semantic Recomposition Module using a learned codebook for dictionary-based feature purification to suppress modality-specific noise while preserving defect-discriminative information, and (2) Contextual Semantic Integration Framework using partial-channel attention to model global spatial dependencies; position-wise normalization enforces reconstruction-driven cross-modal alignment before fusion.
AI.result: On the TLRGBD benchmark with 94.5% small objects, CMAFNet achieves 32.2% mAP@50 and 12.5% APs, outperforming the strongest baseline by 9.8 and 4.0 percentage points respectively. A lightweight variant reaches 24.8% mAP50 at 228 FPS with 4.9M parameters, surpassing YOLO-based detectors and rivaling transformer-based methods at lower computational cost.
AI.conclusion: The purify-then-fuse approach with explicit cross-modal alignment yields substantial gains for small-object, cluttered-background defect detection, offering a favorable accuracy/efficiency trade-off and real-time potential for UAV inspection; the lightweight model demonstrates strong practical viability.

title: Preserve-Then-Quantize: Balancing Rank Budgets for Quantization Error Reconstruction in LLMs
summary: Quantization Error Reconstruction (QER) reduces accuracy loss in Post-Training Quantization (PTQ) by approximating weights as $\mathbf{W} \approx \mathbf{Q} + \mathbf{L}\mathbf{R}$, using a rank-$r$ correction to reconstruct quantization error. Prior methods devote the full rank budget to error reconstruction, which is suboptimal when $\mathbf{W}$ has intrinsic low-rank structure and quantization corrupts dominant directions. We propose Structured Residual Reconstruction (SRR), a rank-allocation framework that preserves the top-$k$ singular subspace of the activation-scaled weight before quantization, quantizes only the residual, and uses the remaining rank $r-k$ for error reconstruction. We derive a theory-guided criterion for selecting $k$ by balancing quantization-exposed energy and unrecoverable error under rank constraints. We further show that resulting $\mathbf{Q} + \mathbf{L}\mathbf{R}$ parameterization naturally supports Quantized Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (QPEFT), and stabilizes fine-tuning via gradient scaling along preserved directions. Experiments demonstrate consistent perplexity reductions across diverse models and quantization settings in PTQ, along with a 5.9 percentage-point average gain on GLUE under 2-bit QPEFT.

AI.tldr: Structured Residual Reconstruction (SRR) reallocates rank budget in quantized weight reconstruction by preserving the top-k singular directions of the activation-scaled weight, quantizing only the residual and using the remaining rank for error reconstruction. This yields improved PTQ accuracy and enables stable QPEFT with gradient scaling, achieving perplexity reductions across models and a 5.9-point average gain on GLUE under 2-bit QPEFT.
AI.motivation: PTQ can incur accuracy loss when quantization distorts dominant low-rank directions of weight matrices. Allocating the full rank budget to error correction is suboptimal if the weight has intrinsic low-rank structure and quantization corrupts the main subspace.
AI.method: Introduce Structured Residual Reconstruction (SRR), which preserves the top-k singular subspace of the activation-scaled weight before quantization, quantizes only the residual, and allocates the remaining rank r-k to reconstruct quantization error. Derive a theory-guided criterion to select k by balancing quantization-exposed energy and unrecoverable error under rank constraints. Show that the parameterization Q+LR supports Quantized Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (QPEFT) and stabilizes fine-tuning via gradient scaling along preserved directions.
AI.result: Empirical results show consistent perplexity reductions across diverse models and PTQ settings, and a 5.9 percentage-point average gain on GLUE with 2-bit QPEFT.
AI.conclusion: SRR provides an effective rank-allocation strategy for PTQ that leverages intrinsic low-rank structure, improves accuracy under quantization, and enables stable QPEFT with tangible NLP benchmarks.

title: Logic-Guided Vector Fields for Constrained Generative Modeling
summary: Neuro-symbolic systems aim to combine the expressive structure of symbolic logic with the flexibility of neural learning; yet, generative models typically lack mechanisms to enforce declarative constraints at generation time. We propose Logic-Guided Vector Fields (LGVF), a neuro-symbolic framework that injects symbolic knowledge, specified as differentiable relaxations of logical constraints, into flow matching generative models. LGVF couples two complementary mechanisms: (1) a training-time logic loss that penalizes constraint violations along continuous flow trajectories, with weights that emphasize correctness near the target distribution; and (2) an inference-time adjustment that steers sampling using constraint gradients, acting as a lightweight, logic-informed correction to the learned dynamics. We evaluate LGVF on three constrained generation case studies spanning linear, nonlinear, and multi-region feasibility constraints. Across all settings, LGVF reduces constraint violations by 59-82% compared to standard flow matching and achieves the lowest violation rates in each case. In the linear and ring settings, LGVF also improves distributional fidelity as measured by MMD, while in the multi-obstacle setting, we observe a satisfaction-fidelity trade-off, with improved feasibility but increased MMD. Beyond quantitative gains, LGVF yields constraint-aware vector fields exhibiting emergent obstacle-avoidance behavior, routing samples around forbidden regions without explicit path planning.

AI.tldr: Logic-Guided Vector Fields (LGVF) integrates differentiable logical constraints into flow-matching generative models via training-time constraint losses and inference-time constraint gradient corrections, achieving substantial constraint satisfaction improvements and emergent obstacle-avoidance with limited path-planning.
AI.motivation: To address the lack of declarative constraint enforcement in generative models and to leverage symbolic knowledge within neuro-symbolic frameworks for feasible, safe, and guided generation.
AI.method: Two-pronged approach: (1) training-time differentiable logic loss that penalizes constraint violations along flow trajectories with weighting that emphasizes the target distribution; (2) inference-time adjustment using constraint gradients to steer sampling as a lightweight correction to the learned dynamics. Evaluation on linear, nonlinear, and multi-region feasibility constraints; metrics include constraint violation rates and MMD; qualitative observation of obstacle-avoidance.
AI.result: Significant reductions in constraint violations (59-82%) compared with standard flow matching; LGVF achieves the lowest violation rates across all cases; distributional fidelity improvements in linear and ring settings (lower MMD) but a feasibility-fidelity trade-off in the multi-obstacle setting (reduced feasibility with higher MMD). Emergent obstacle-avoidance behavior observed without explicit path-planning.
AI.conclusion: LGVF demonstrates effective integration of differentiable symbolic constraints into flow-based generative models, offering improved feasibility and interpretable, constraint-aware dynamics, with potential for broader neuro-symbolic generation tasks; balancing constraint satisfaction with distribution fidelity may be needed in complex constraint landscapes.

title: Physics Informed Generative AI Enabling Labour Free Segmentation For Microscopy Analysis
summary: Semantic segmentation of microscopy images is a critical task for high-throughput materials characterisation, yet its automation is severely constrained by the prohibitive cost, subjectivity, and scarcity of expert-annotated data. While physics-based simulations offer a scalable alternative to manual labelling, models trained on such data historically fail to generalise due to a significant domain gap, lacking the complex textures, noise patterns, and imaging artefacts inherent to experimental data. This paper introduces a novel framework for labour-free segmentation that successfully bridges this simulation-to-reality gap. Our pipeline leverages phase-field simulations to generate an abundant source of microstructural morphologies with perfect, intrinsically-derived ground-truth masks. We then employ a Cycle-Consistent Generative Adversarial Network (CycleGAN) for unpaired image-to-image translation, transforming the clean simulations into a large-scale dataset of high-fidelity, realistic SEM images. A U-Net model, trained exclusively on this synthetic data, demonstrated remarkable generalisation when deployed on unseen experimental images, achieving a mean Boundary F1-Score of 0.90 and an Intersection over Union (IOU) of 0.88. Comprehensive validation using t-SNE feature-space projection and Shannon entropy analysis confirms that our synthetic images are statistically and featurally indistinguishable from the real data manifold. By completely decoupling model training from manual annotation, our generative framework transforms a data-scarce problem into one of data abundance, providing a robust and fully automated solution to accelerate materials discovery and analysis.

AI.tldr: CycleGAN-based domain adaptation bridges simulation-to-reality for semantic segmentation of microscopy images. Phase-field simulations provide ground-truth masks; CycleGAN translates simulated images into realistic SEM-like images; a U-Net trained solely on synthetic data generalizes to unseen real SEM images, achieving high mean Boundary F1-Score (0.90) and IOU (0.88). Validation suggests synthetic data are statistically/featurally indistinguishable from real data; training is fully automated without manual annotation.
AI.motivation: Manual annotation for microscopy segmentation is costly, subjective, and scarce. Physics-based simulations offer scalable labeling but exhibit a domain gap to real images due to textures, noise, and imaging artifacts. A labour-free pipeline that closes this gap can accelerate materials discovery.
AI.method: Generate microstructure morphologies and perfect ground-truth masks via phase-field simulations. Use CycleGAN for unpaired image-to-image translation to create a large set of realistic SEM-like images from synthetic data. Train a U-Net segmentation model exclusively on synthetic images. Evaluate on unseen experimental SEM images using standard segmentation metrics (Boundary F1-Score, IOU). Validate distribution similarity with t-SNE and Shannon entropy analyses.
AI.result: A U-Net trained on synthetic CycleGAN-translated images generalizes to real SEM images, achieving mean Boundary F1-Score ≈ 0.90 and IOU ≈ 0.88 on unseen data. t-SNE and entropy analyses indicate the synthetic data occupy the real data manifold, supporting a successful simulation-to-reality bridge.
AI.conclusion: The framework decouples model training from manual annotation, converting data-scarce segmentation into data-abundant training. It offers a robust, fully automated pathway to accelerate materials discovery and analysis by leveraging phase-field simulations and adversarial domain adaptation.

title: SNAP: A Self-Consistent Agreement Principle with Application to Robust Computation
summary: We introduce SNAP (Self-coNsistent Agreement Principle), a self-supervised framework for robust computation based on mutual agreement. Based on an Agreement-Reliability Hypothesis SNAP assigns weights that quantify agreement, emphasizing trustworthy items and downweighting outliers without supervision or prior knowledge. A key result is the Exponential Suppression of Outlier Weights, ensuring that outliers contribute negligibly to computations, even in high-dimensional settings. We study properties of SNAP weighting scheme and show its practical benefits on vector averaging and subspace estimation. Particularly, we demonstrate that non-iterative SNAP outperforms the iterative Weiszfeld algorithm and two variants of multivariate median of means. SNAP thus provides a flexible, easy-to-use, broadly applicable approach to robust computation.

AI.tldr: SNAP is a self-supervised robust computation framework that uses mutual agreement to weight data items, exponentially suppressing outliers and enabling non-iterative, robust vector averaging and subspace estimation.
AI.motivation: To perform robust computations without supervision or prior knowledge, by downweighting unreliable items and mitigating outliers in high-dimensional data.
AI.method: Introduce the Agreement-Reliability Hypothesis to assign weights based on agreement; rely on an exponential suppression mechanism for outlier weights; enable non-iterative computation that competes with or outperforms iterative robust estimators.
AI.result: Outlier weights decay exponentially, making outliers contribute negligibly even in high dimensions; SNAP improves vector averaging and subspace estimation; non-iterative SNAP outperforms Weiszfeld and two variants of multivariate median of means.
AI.conclusion: SNAP offers a flexible, easy-to-use, broadly applicable framework for robust computation with strong outlier suppression and competitive performance against standard robust estimators.

title: Robust Domain Generalization under Divergent Marginal and Conditional Distributions
summary: Domain generalization (DG) aims to learn predictive models that can generalize to unseen domains. Most existing DG approaches focus on learning domain-invariant representations under the assumption of conditional distribution shift (i.e., primarily addressing changes in $P(X\mid Y)$ while assuming $P(Y)$ remains stable). However, real-world scenarios with multiple domains often involve compound distribution shifts where both the marginal label distribution $P(Y)$ and the conditional distribution $P(X\mid Y)$ vary simultaneously. To address this, we propose a unified framework for robust domain generalization under divergent marginal and conditional distributions. We derive a novel risk bound for unseen domains by explicitly decomposing the joint distribution into marginal and conditional components and characterizing risk gaps arising from both sources of divergence. To operationalize this bound, we design a meta-learning procedure that minimizes and validates the proposed risk bound across seen domains, ensuring strong generalization to unseen ones. Empirical evaluations demonstrate that our method achieves state-of-the-art performance not only on conventional DG benchmarks but also in challenging multi-domain long-tailed recognition settings where both marginal and conditional shifts are pronounced.

AI.tldr: Unified domain generalization framework addressing simultaneous shifts in P(Y) and P(X|Y) via a decomposed joint-distribution risk bound and a meta-learning objective, achieving state-of-the-art results on standard DG benchmarks and challenging multi-domain long-tailed recognition.
AI.motivation: Conventional DG largely handles conditional distribution shifts P(X|Y) with stable P(Y). Real-world, multi-domain data often exhibit concurrent shifts in both the marginal label distribution P(Y) and the conditional distribution P(X|Y). This joint shift degrades generalization unless explicitly accounted for with principled guarantees and training strategies.
AI.method: Derive a risk bound by explicitly decomposing the joint distribution into marginal and conditional components and quantifying risk gaps from each source of divergence. Introduce a meta-learning procedure that optimizes and validates the bound across seen domains, guiding the learning process to generalize to unseen domains.
AI.result: Empirical evaluations show state-of-the-art generalization on standard DG benchmarks and in challenging multi-domain long-tailed recognition tasks where both P(Y) and P(X|Y) shifts are pronounced.
AI.conclusion: The proposed bound-guided meta-learning framework provides a principled approach to robust DG under joint marginal and conditional distribution shifts, yielding strong unseen-domain performance and highlighting directions for tighter bounds and more efficient estimation of divergences.

title: DASH: Faster Shampoo via Batched Block Preconditioning and Efficient Inverse-Root Solvers
summary: Shampoo is one of the leading approximate second-order optimizers: a variant of it has won the MLCommons AlgoPerf competition, and it has been shown to produce models with lower activation outliers that are easier to compress. Yet, applying Shampoo currently comes at the cost of significant computational slowdown, due to its expensive internal operations. In this paper, we take a significant step to address this shortcoming by proposing \method (for \textbf{D}istributed \textbf{A}ccelerated \textbf{SH}ampoo), a faster implementation of Distributed Shampoo based on two main new techniques: First, we show that preconditioner blocks can be stacked into 3D tensors to significantly improve GPU utilization; second, we introduce the Newton-DB iteration and the Chebyshev polynomial approximations as novel and faster approaches for computing the inverse matrix roots required by Shampoo. Along with these algorithmic contributions, we provide a first in-depth analysis of how matrix scaling critically affects Shampoo convergence. On the practical side, our GPU-aware implementation achieves up to $4.83\times$ faster optimizer steps compared to the well-optimized Distributed Shampoo, while Newton-DB attains the lowest validation perplexity per iteration among all tested methods. Our code is available at https://github.com/IST-DASLab/DASH.

AI.tldr: DASH: a faster Distributed Shampoo variant that stacks preconditioners into 3D tensors and uses Newton-DB and Chebyshev polynomial approximations for inverse roots, achieving up to 4.83x faster optimizer steps and better perplexity per iteration; includes matrix-scaling analysis; code released.
AI.motivation: Shampoo, a leading approximate second-order optimizer, is hampered by substantial computational slowdown. The paper aims to reduce this overhead to enable practical training and improve performance.
AI.method: Introduce DASH (Distributed Accelerated SHampoo): (1) stack preconditioner blocks into 3D tensors to boost GPU utilization; (2) apply Newton-DB iteration and Chebyshev polynomial approximations to compute inverse matrix roots more quickly; provide analysis on how matrix scaling affects convergence; provide a GPU-aware implementation.
AI.result: GPU-aware DASH achieves up to 4.83× faster optimizer steps than the well-optimized Distributed Shampoo; Newton-DB achieves the lowest validation perplexity per iteration among tested methods; code available at GitHub.
AI.conclusion: DASH substantially speeds up distributed Shampoo while maintaining or improving optimization performance; the study also provides insights into matrix scaling for convergence and releases its implementation for practical use.

title: On Stability and Robustness of Diffusion Posterior Sampling for Bayesian Inverse Problems
summary: Diffusion models have recently emerged as powerful learned priors for Bayesian inverse problems (BIPs). Diffusion-based solvers rely on a presumed likelihood for the observations in BIPs to guide the generation process. However, the link between likelihood and recovery quality for BIPs is unclear in previous works. We bridge this gap by characterizing the posterior approximation error and proving the \emph{stability} of the diffusion-based solvers. Meanwhile, an immediate result of our findings on stability demonstrates the lack of robustness in diffusion-based solvers, which remains unexplored. This can degrade performance when the presumed likelihood mismatches the unknown true data generation processes. To address this issue, we propose a simple yet effective solution, \emph{robust diffusion posterior sampling}, which is provably \emph{robust} and compatible with existing gradient-based posterior samplers. Empirical results on scientific inverse problems and natural image tasks validate the effectiveness and robustness of our method, showing consistent performance improvements under challenging likelihood misspecifications.

AI.tldr: Diffusion-model priors for Bayesian inverse problems are analyzed; stability under likelihood misspecification is established, revealing robustness issues; a robust diffusion posterior sampling (RDPS) is proposed, with provable robustness and compatibility with existing gradient-based samplers; empirical tests show improved performance under misspecified likelihoods on scientific and natural-image tasks.
AI.motivation: To understand how the assumed observation likelihood affects recovery quality in diffusion-based Bayesian inverse problems and to address potential robustness issues when the likelihood is mis-specified.
AI.method: The authors characterize posterior approximation error and prove stability of diffusion-based solvers; they identify a lack of robustness under likelihood misspecification; they introduce robust diffusion posterior sampling (RDPS), a method that yields provable robustness and works with standard gradient-based posterior samplers.
AI.result: The approach demonstrates robustness and improved performance under likelihood misspecification across scientific inverse problems and natural-image tasks.
AI.conclusion: RDPS provides provable robustness to likelihood misspecification, improves practical performance in diffusion-based BIPs, and highlights the importance of addressing likelihood misspecification in diffusion priors.

title: Dissecting Outlier Dynamics in LLM NVFP4 Pretraining
summary: Training large language models using 4-bit arithmetic enhances throughput and memory efficiency. Yet, the limited dynamic range of FP4 increases sensitivity to outliers. While NVFP4 mitigates quantization error via hierarchical microscaling, a persistent loss gap remains compared to BF16. This study conducts a longitudinal analysis of outlier dynamics across architecture during NVFP4 pretraining, focusing on where they localize, why they occur, and how they evolve temporally. We find that, compared with Softmax Attention (SA), Linear Attention (LA) reduces per-tensor heavy tails but still exhibits persistent block-level spikes under block quantization. Our analysis attributes outliers to specific architectural components: Softmax in SA, gating in LA, and SwiGLU in FFN, with "post-QK" operations exhibiting higher sensitivity to quantization. Notably, outliers evolve from transient spikes early in training to a small set of persistent hot channels (i.e., channels with persistently large magnitudes) in later stages. Based on these findings, we introduce Hot-Channel Patch (HCP), an online compensation mechanism that identifies hot channels and reinjects residuals using hardware-efficient kernels. We then develop CHON, an NVFP4 training recipe integrating HCP with post-QK operation protection. On GLA-1.3B model trained for 60B tokens, CHON reduces the loss gap to BF16 from 0.94% to 0.58% while maintaining downstream accuracy.

AI.tldr: NVFP4 training for LLMs faces outliers due to limited FP4 dynamic range. Through longitudinal analysis, outliers localize to specific components (Softmax, gating, SwiGLU) and evolve from transient spikes to persistent hot channels. Introduces Hot-Channel Patch (HCP) for online compensation and CHON recipe, which narrows the BF16 loss gap from 0.94% to 0.58% on a 1.3B model trained to 60B tokens, while preserving accuracy.
AI.motivation: Close the quantization-induced performance gap when training large language models with 4-bit arithmetic by understanding outlier dynamics and developing an efficient online correction mechanism.
AI.method: Longitudinal empirical analysis of outlier dynamics across SA, LA, and FFN components during NVFP4 pretraining; identify hot channels; develop HCP to reinject residuals through hardware-efficient kernels; integrate HCP into CHON NVFP4 training recipe and evaluate on a GLA-1.3B model trained to 60B tokens.
AI.result: Outliers in SA, LA, and FFN are tied to post-QK operations; LA reduces per-tensor heavy tails but block-level spikes persist; outliers move from transient spikes to a small set of persistent hot channels; HCP identifies hot channels and reinjects residuals; CHON reduces BF16 loss gap from 0.94% to 0.58% and preserves downstream accuracy.
AI.conclusion: Architectural-level outlier dynamics can be mitigated via online compensation (HCP) coupled with a targeted NVFP4 training recipe (CHON). Post-QK operations are particularly sensitive; hot channels emerge as a stable target for residual reinjection, enabling closer performance to BF16 with 4-bit arithmetic.

title: FORLER: Federated Offline Reinforcement Learning with Q-Ensemble and Actor Rectification
comment: accetped by IEEE International Conference on Communications (ICC 2026)
summary: In Internet-of-Things systems, federated learning has advanced online reinforcement learning (RL) by enabling parallel policy training without sharing raw data. However, interacting with real environments online can be risky and costly, motivating offline federated RL (FRL), where local devices learn from fixed datasets. Despite its promise, offline FRL may break down under low-quality, heterogeneous data. Offline RL tends to get stuck in local optima, and in FRL, one device's suboptimal policy can degrade the aggregated model, i.e., policy pollution. We present FORLER, combining Q-ensemble aggregation on the server with actor rectification on devices. The server robustly merges device Q-functions to curb policy pollution and shift heavy computation off resource-constrained hardware without compromising privacy. Locally, actor rectification enriches policy gradients via a zeroth-order search for high-Q actions plus a bespoke regularizer that nudges the policy toward them. A $δ$-periodic strategy further reduces local computation. We theoretically provide safe policy improvement performance guarantees. Extensive experiments show FORLER consistently outperforms strong baselines under varying data quality and heterogeneity.

AI.tldr: Offline federated RL with server Q-ensemble aggregation and device actor rectification; δ-periodic updates and theoretical safe policy improvement guarantees; outperforms baselines under data heterogeneity while preserving privacy.
AI.motivation: In IoT FRL, offline learning with heterogeneous, low-quality data risks suboptimal policies and policy pollution, especially when local data quality varies and sharing raw data is restricted.
AI.method: Server-side Q-ensemble aggregation to curb policy pollution; device-side actor rectification using zeroth-order search for high-Q actions plus a regularizer guiding policy toward them; δ-periodic local updates to reduce computation; formal safe policy improvement guarantees.
AI.result: FORLER consistently outperforms strong baselines across varying data quality and heterogeneity in extensive experiments.
AI.conclusion: FORLER effectively mitigates offline FRL challenges by combining robust server aggregation with local policy rectification, enabling privacy-preserving, computation-efficient learning with performance guarantees.

title: FiLoRA: Focus-and-Ignore LoRA for Controllable Feature Reliance
summary: Multimodal foundation models integrate heterogeneous signals across modalities, yet it remains poorly understood how their predictions depend on specific internal feature groups and whether such reliance can be deliberately controlled. Existing studies of shortcut and spurious behavior largely rely on post hoc analyses or feature removal, offering limited insight into whether reliance can be modulated without altering task semantics. We introduce FiLoRA (Focus-and-Ignore LoRA), an instruction-conditioned, parameter-efficient adaptation framework that enables explicit control over internal feature reliance while keeping the predictive objective fixed. FiLoRA decomposes adaptation into feature group-aligned LoRA modules and applies instruction-conditioned gating, allowing natural language instructions to act as computation-level control signals rather than task redefinitions. Across text--image and audio--visual benchmarks, we show that instruction-conditioned gating induces consistent and causal shifts in internal computation, selectively amplifying or suppressing core and spurious feature groups without modifying the label space or training objective. Further analyses demonstrate that FiLoRA yields improved robustness under spurious feature interventions, revealing a principled mechanism to regulate reliance beyond correlation-driven learning.

AI.tldr: FiLoRA is a parameter-efficient adaptation framework that uses feature-group aligned LoRA modules and instruction-conditioned gating to explicitly control internal feature reliance (core vs spurious) in multimodal foundation models, preserving the task objective while inducing causal shifts in computation and improving robustness to spurious features.
AI.motivation: To understand and regulate how multimodal foundation models rely on internal feature groups, addressing the limitation of post hoc analyses and feature removals that cannot modulate reliance without changing semantics.
AI.method: FiLoRA decomposes adaptation into feature-group specific LoRA modules and applies instruction-conditioned gating. Natural language instructions act as computation-level control signals rather than altering the labels or training objective, enabling selective amplification or suppression of core or spurious feature groups in both text–image and audio–visual models.
AI.result: Instruction-conditioned gating yields consistent, causal shifts in internal computation, allowing selective amplification or suppression of feature groups without changing the label space or objective. FiLoRA improves robustness under interventions targeting spurious features, demonstrating a principled mechanism to regulate reliance beyond correlation-driven learning.
AI.conclusion: FiLoRA provides a controllable, efficient mechanism to modulate internal feature reliance in multimodal models, enabling task-preserving reweighting of core versus spurious features through language-driven control signals and improving robustness to spurious correlations.

title: Learning to Route and Schedule LLMs from User Retrials via Contextual Queueing Bandits
summary: Explosive demands for LLMs often cause user queries to accumulate in server queues, requiring efficient routing (query-LLM matching) and scheduling (query prioritization) mechanisms. Several online algorithms are being deployed, but they overlook the following two key challenges inherent to conversational LLM services: (1) unsatisfied users may retry queries, increasing the server backlog, and (2) requests for ``explicit" feedback, such as ratings, degrade user experiences. In this paper, we develop a joint routing and scheduling algorithm that leverages ``implicit" feedback inferred from user retrial behaviors. The key idea is to propose and study the framework of contextual queueing bandits with multinomial logit feedback (CQB-MNL). CQB-MNL models query retrials, as well as context-based learning for user preferences over LLMs. Our algorithm, anytime CQB (ACQB), achieves efficient learning while maintaining queue stability by combining Thompson sampling with forced exploration at a decaying rate. We show that ACQB simultaneously achieves a cumulative regret of $\widetilde{\mathcal{O}}(\sqrt{t})$ for routing and a queue length regret of $\widetilde{\mathcal{O}}(t^{-1/4})$ for any large $t$. For experiments, we refine query embeddings via contrastive learning while adopting a disjoint parameter model to learn LLM-specific parameters. Experiments on SPROUT, EmbedLLM, and RouterBench datasets confirm that both algorithms consistently outperform baselines.

AI.tldr: Proposes CQB-MNL and its online algorithm ACQB for joint routing and scheduling of LLM queries using implicit retrial feedback; provides regret bounds and empirical validation showing improvement over baselines across multiple datasets.
AI.motivation: Addresses two core issues in conversational LLM services under high demand: user retrials that inflate backlog and explicit feedback (ratings) that harms user experience. Leverages implicit feedback from retrials to learn user preferences and improve routing/scheduling.
AI.method: Introduce contextual queueing bandits with multinomial logit feedback (CQB-MNL). Model query retrials and context-based preferences. Propose Anytime CQB (ACQB): Thompson sampling with decaying forced exploration to balance learning and queue stability. Use a disjoint parameter model with contrastive learning for embeddings and LLM-specific parameters. Theoretical guarantees: cumulative routing regret ~ O~(sqrt(t)) and queue-length regret ~ O~(t^{-1/4}) as t grows. Evaluation on SPROUT, EmbedLLM, RouterBench with baselines.
AI.result: ACQB achieves efficient learning with queue stability and empirically outperforms baselines on all three datasets. Embedding refinement via contrastive learning improves LLM-specific parameter estimation; results demonstrate robustness across different LLMs and routing scenarios.
AI.conclusion: Joint routing and scheduling under implicit retrial feedback is effective for large-scale LLM services. CQB-MNL provides a principled framework with solid theoretical guarantees and practical performance improvements; future work could extend to non-stationary user preferences and explore richer feedback models.

title: BAPS: A Fine-Grained Low-Precision Scheme for Softmax in Attention via Block-Aware Precision reScaling
summary: As the performance gains from accelerating quantized matrix multiplication plateau, the softmax operation becomes the critical bottleneck in Transformer inference. This bottleneck stems from two hardware limitations: (1) limited data bandwidth between matrix and vector compute cores, and (2) the significant area cost of high-precision (FP32/16) exponentiation units (EXP2). To address these issues, we introduce a novel low-precision workflow that employs a specific 8-bit floating-point format (HiF8) and block-aware precision rescaling for softmax. Crucially, our algorithmic innovations make low-precision softmax feasible without the significant model accuracy loss that hampers direct low-precision approaches. Specifically, our design (i) halves the required data movement bandwidth by enabling matrix multiplication outputs constrained to 8-bit, and (ii) substantially reduces the EXP2 unit area by computing exponentiations in low (8-bit) precision. Extensive evaluation on language models and multi-modal models confirms the validity of our method. By alleviating the vector computation bottleneck, our work paves the way for doubling end-to-end inference throughput without increasing chip area, and offers a concrete co-design path for future low-precision hardware and software.

AI.tldr: Low-precision HiF8 softmax reduces bandwidth and EXP2 area, enabling faster Transformer inference with minimal accuracy loss.
AI.motivation: Softmax is a bottleneck in Transformer inference due to limited data bandwidth between matrix and vector cores and the area cost of high-precision exponentiation units; improving softmax in low precision can unlock throughput.
AI.method: Propose HiF8 8-bit floating-point format with block-aware precision rescaling and 8-bit exponentiation; constrain matrix-multiplication outputs to 8-bit; hardware-software co-design; evaluate on language and multi-modal models.
AI.result: Demonstrates substantial bandwidth reduction and reduced EXP2 area; maintains accuracy compared to full-precision softmax; claims potential to double end-to-end inference throughput without increasing chip area.
AI.conclusion: Provides a concrete co-design path for future low-precision hardware/software to mitigate the softmax bottleneck in Transformer inference.

title: Fast Autoregressive Video Diffusion and World Models with Temporal Cache Compression and Sparse Attention
summary: Autoregressive video diffusion models enable streaming generation, opening the door to long-form synthesis, video world models, and interactive neural game engines. However, their core attention layers become a major bottleneck at inference time: as generation progresses, the KV cache grows, causing both increasing latency and escalating GPU memory, which in turn restricts usable temporal context and harms long-range consistency. In this work, we study redundancy in autoregressive video diffusion and identify three persistent sources: near-duplicate cached keys across frames, slowly evolving (largely semantic) queries/keys that make many attention computations redundant, and cross-attention over long prompts where only a small subset of tokens matters per frame. Building on these observations, we propose a unified, training-free attention framework for autoregressive diffusion: TempCache compresses the KV cache via temporal correspondence to bound cache growth; AnnCA accelerates cross-attention by selecting frame-relevant prompt tokens using fast approximate nearest neighbor (ANN) matching; and AnnSA sparsifies self-attention by restricting each query to semantically matched keys, also using a lightweight ANN. Together, these modules reduce attention, compute, and memory and are compatible with existing autoregressive diffusion backbones and world models. Experiments demonstrate up to x5--x10 end-to-end speedups while preserving near-identical visual quality and, crucially, maintaining stable throughput and nearly constant peak GPU memory usage over long rollouts, where prior methods progressively slow down and suffer from increasing memory usage.

AI.tldr: A training-free, unified attention framework for autoregressive video diffusion that reduces KV-cache growth and self-, cross-attention costs, yielding 5–10× speedups with stable memory for long rollouts.
AI.motivation: Autoregressive video diffusion's attention bottleneck grows with generation as KV caches accumulate, causing latency, memory growth, and degraded long-range consistency. Redundancies exist across frames and in cross- and self-attention, especially with long prompts.
AI.method: Introduce three components: TempCache (temporal KV compression via temporal correspondences), AnnCA (frame-relevant prompt token selection for cross-attention using approximate nearest neighbors), and AnnSA (sparse self-attention by semantically matching queries to a subset of keys using lightweight ANN). These are training-free and compatible with existing backbones.
AI.result: End-to-end speedups of 5–10× with near-identical visual quality. Maintains stable throughput and nearly constant peak GPU memory during long rollouts, overcoming prior methods’ slowing speed and growing memory usage.
AI.conclusion: A practical, training-free solution that tackles attention bottlenecks in autoregressive video diffusion, enabling longer-form synthesis and improved efficiency without sacrificing quality; compatible with current backbones and world models.

title: AICD Bench: A Challenging Benchmark for AI-Generated Code Detection
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly capable of generating functional source code, raising concerns about authorship, accountability, and security. While detecting AI-generated code is critical, existing datasets and benchmarks are narrow, typically limited to binary human-machine classification under in-distribution settings. To bridge this gap, we introduce $\emph{AICD Bench}$, the most comprehensive benchmark for AI-generated code detection. It spans $\emph{2M examples}$, $\emph{77 models}$ across $\emph{11 families}$, and $\emph{9 programming languages}$, including recent reasoning models. Beyond scale, AICD Bench introduces three realistic detection tasks: ($\emph{i}$)~$\emph{Robust Binary Classification}$ under distribution shifts in language and domain, ($\emph{ii}$)~$\emph{Model Family Attribution}$, grouping generators by architectural lineage, and ($\emph{iii}$)~$\emph{Fine-Grained Human-Machine Classification}$ across human, machine, hybrid, and adversarial code. Extensive evaluation on neural and classical detectors shows that performance remains far below practical usability, particularly under distribution shift and for hybrid or adversarial code. We release AICD Bench as a $\emph{unified, challenging evaluation suite}$ to drive the next generation of robust approaches for AI-generated code detection. The data and the code are available at https://huggingface.co/AICD-bench}.

AI.tldr: Proposes AICD Bench, a large-scale, multi-task benchmark for AI-generated code detection across 2M examples, 77 models, 11 families, and 9 languages; introduces robust binary classification under distribution shifts, model-family attribution, and fine-grained human-machine classification; detectors perform poorly, especially under shifts and for hybrid/adversarial code; released as a unified evaluation suite.
AI.motivation: Current datasets for AI-generated code detection are narrow, often limited to in-distribution binary classification. A comprehensive benchmark is needed to evaluate robustness, lineage-aware attribution, and fine-grained human-machine classification across languages and model families.
AI.method: Assemble a large-scale benchmark (AICD Bench) with 2M examples drawn from 77 models spanning 11 families and 9 programming languages, including recent reasoning models. Define three tasks: (i) Robust Binary Classification under distribution shifts in language and domain, (ii) Model Family Attribution grouping generators by architectural lineage, and (iii) Fine-Grained Human-Machine Classification across human, machine, hybrid, and adversarial code. Evaluate both neural and classical detectors on the suite.
AI.result: Detectors remain far from practical usability, with degraded performance under distribution shifts and for hybrid/adversarial code. The authors release AICD Bench as a unified, challenging evaluation suite, with data and code available on HuggingFace.
AI.conclusion: AICD Bench provides a comprehensive and challenging benchmark to drive the development of robust AI-generated code detection methods and to enable more realistic evaluation across distributions, model families, and code types.

title: Learning Half-Spaces from Perturbed Contrastive Examples
summary:
We study learning under a two-step contrastive example oracle, as introduced by Mansouri et. al. (2025), where each queried (or sampled) labeled example is paired with an additional contrastive example of opposite label. While Mansouri et al. assume an idealized setting, where the contrastive example is at minimum distance of the originally queried/sampled point, we introduce and analyze a mechanism, parameterized by a non-decreasing noise function $f$, under which this ideal contrastive example is perturbed. The amount of perturbation is controlled by $f(d)$, where $d$ is the distance of the queried/sampled point to the decision boundary. Intuitively, this results in higher-quality contrastive examples for points closer to the decision boundary. We study this model in two settings: (i) when the maximum perturbation magnitude is fixed, and (ii) when it is stochastic.
  For one-dimensional thresholds and for half-spaces under the uniform distribution on a bounded domain, we characterize active and passive contrastive sample complexity in dependence on the function $f$. We show that, under certain conditions on $f$, the presence of contrastive examples speeds up learning in terms of asymptotic query complexity and asymptotic expected query complexity.

AI.tldr: We analyze learning with a perturbed two-step contrastive oracle, where the contrastive pair’s distance is controlled by a non-decreasing function f of the distance to the decision boundary; for 1D thresholds and half-spaces under a uniform distribution on a bounded domain, we characterize active and passive contrastive sample complexity and show that, under certain conditions on f, the contrastive information speeds up learning in both asymptotic and expected query complexity.
AI.motivation: Generalize Mansouri et al.'s idealized contrastive oracle to a realistic perturbation model and quantify how the quality of contrastive hints, governed by f, impacts sample complexity.
AI.method: Model with perturbation magnitude f(d), where d is distance to the decision boundary; analyze two settings (fixed max perturbation and stochastic perturbation); study one-dimensional thresholds and half-spaces under a uniform distribution on a bounded domain; derive characterizations of active and passive contrastive sample complexity as a function of f.
AI.result: Under certain conditions on f, the presence of contrastive examples speeds up learning in terms of asymptotic query complexity and expected query complexity.
AI.conclusion: Perturbations that yield higher-quality contrastive examples near the decision boundary can improve learning efficiency; the results identify when and how contrastive information helps in the studied geometric settings and suggest directions for extending to broader models.

title: CloDS: Visual-Only Unsupervised Cloth Dynamics Learning in Unknown Conditions
summary: Deep learning has demonstrated remarkable capabilities in simulating complex dynamic systems. However, existing methods require known physical properties as supervision or inputs, limiting their applicability under unknown conditions. To explore this challenge, we introduce Cloth Dynamics Grounding (CDG), a novel scenario for unsupervised learning of cloth dynamics from multi-view visual observations. We further propose Cloth Dynamics Splatting (CloDS), an unsupervised dynamic learning framework designed for CDG. CloDS adopts a three-stage pipeline that first performs video-to-geometry grounding and then trains a dynamics model on the grounded meshes. To cope with large non-linear deformations and severe self-occlusions during grounding, we introduce a dual-position opacity modulation that supports bidirectional mapping between 2D observations and 3D geometry via mesh-based Gaussian splatting in video-to-geometry grounding stage. It jointly considers the absolute and relative position of Gaussian components. Comprehensive experimental evaluations demonstrate that CloDS effectively learns cloth dynamics from visual data while maintaining strong generalization capabilities for unseen configurations. Our code is available at https://github.com/whynot-zyl/CloDS. Visualization results are available at https://github.com/whynot-zyl/CloDS_video}.%\footnote{As in this example.

AI.tldr: Unsupervised learning of cloth dynamics from multi-view visuals via Cloth Dynamics Grounding (CDG) and Cloth Dynamics Splatting (CloDS); a three-stage pipeline: 1) video-to-geometry grounding with mesh-based Gaussian splatting and dual-position opacity modulation to handle large deformations and occlusions; 2) grounding meshes; 3) train a dynamics model on meshes; demonstrates strong generalization to unseen configurations; code released.
AI.motivation: Address the lack of supervision and unknown physical properties for cloth dynamics by enabling unsupervised learning from visual observations, without explicit priors or inputs.
AI.method: CloDS uses a three-stage pipeline: (1) video-to-geometry grounding using mesh-based Gaussian splatting with dual-position opacity modulation to enable bidirectional 2D-3D mapping under large non-linear deformations and severe self-occlusions; (2) obtain grounded cloth meshes; (3) train a dynamics model on the grounded meshes; the approach jointly considers absolute and relative positions of Gaussian components to enhance grounding.
AI.result: Experimental evaluations show that CloDS can learn cloth dynamics from visual data and generalize well to unseen configurations.
AI.conclusion: CDG and CloDS enable unsupervised cloth dynamics learning from multi-view visual data, robust to non-linear deformations and occlusions, with good generalization; code and visualization resources are provided.

title: Active learning from positive and unlabeled examples
summary: Learning from positive and unlabeled data (PU learning) is a weakly supervised variant of binary classification in which the learner receives labels only for (some) positively labeled instances, while all other examples remain unlabeled. Motivated by applications such as advertising and anomaly detection, we study an active PU learning setting where the learner can adaptively query instances from an unlabeled pool, but a queried label is revealed only when the instance is positive and an independent coin flip succeeds; otherwise the learner receives no information. In this paper, we provide the first theoretical analysis of the label complexity of active PU learning.

AI.tldr: First theoretical analysis of label complexity in active PU learning with stochastic feedback.
AI.motivation: PU learning is important for weak supervision in domains like advertising and anomaly detection. Active PU frameworks aim to reduce labeling costs, but the feedback mechanism is stochastic (a queried instance yields a label only if it is positive and a coin flip succeeds), making it crucial to understand the label complexity under this model.
AI.method: Formalize an active PU learning model with the probabilistic label revelation. Derive fundamental limits on the number of queried labels (label complexity) needed to achieve a target accuracy, providing upper and lower bounds.
AI.result: The paper provides the first theoretical bounds on the label complexity for active PU learning under the described stochastic feedback, establishing baseline limits and characterizing factors that influence query efficiency.
AI.conclusion: This work initiates the theoretical study of label complexity in active PU learning under stochastic feedback and suggests directions for improving query efficiency and developing algorithms robust to limited and noisy feedback.

title: Efficient Swap Regret Minimization in Combinatorial Bandits
summary: This paper addresses the problem of designing efficient no-swap regret algorithms for combinatorial bandits, where the number of actions $N$ is exponentially large in the dimensionality of the problem. In this setting, designing efficient no-swap regret translates to sublinear -- in horizon $T$ -- swap regret with polylogarithmic dependence on $N$. In contrast to the weaker notion of external regret minimization - a problem which is fairly well understood in the literature - achieving no-swap regret with a polylogarithmic dependence on $N$ has remained elusive in combinatorial bandits. Our paper resolves this challenge, by introducing a no-swap-regret learning algorithm with regret that scales polylogarithmically in $N$ and is tight for the class of combinatorial bandits. To ground our results, we also demonstrate how to implement the proposed algorithm efficiently -- that is, with a per-iteration complexity that also scales polylogarithmically in $N$ -- across a wide range of well-studied applications.

AI.tldr: A no-swap-regret algorithm for combinatorial bandits achieves sublinear swap regret in the horizon with polylogarithmic dependence on the action set size N, and runs with polylog(N) per-iteration complexity; the bounds are tight for this problem class and the method is efficiently implementable across applications.
AI.motivation: In combinatorial bandits, the action space is exponentially large in problem dimension, making swap-regret minimization challenging. While external regret is well-understood, achieving no-swap regret with polylog(N) growth has remained open and desirable for better long-term performance in high-dimensional, structured action spaces.
AI.method: Develop and analyze a no-swap-regret learning algorithm tailored to combinatorial bandits. The algorithm attains swap-regret sublinear in horizon T with polylogarithmic dependence on N and is designed for efficient implementation, achieving per-iteration complexity that is polylogarithmic in N.
AI.result: The proposed algorithm attains no-swap regret with polylogarithmic dependence on N and sublinear dependence on T; the bound is tight for the class of combinatorial bandits. The construction also supports efficient implementation with per-iteration complexity that scales polylogarithmically in N, and the approach applies across a broad set of well-studied applications.
AI.conclusion: This work resolves the challenge of achieving no-swap regret in combinatorial bandits with favorable (polylogarithmic) dependence on the action count, providing a theoretically tight and computationally efficient solution with broad applicability.

title: Learning Sparse Visual Representations via Spatial-Semantic Factorization
summary: Self-supervised learning (SSL) faces a fundamental conflict between semantic understanding and image reconstruction. High-level semantic SSL (e.g., DINO) relies on global tokens that are forced to be location-invariant for augmentation alignment, a process that inherently discards the spatial coordinates required for reconstruction. Conversely, generative SSL (e.g., MAE) preserves dense feature grids for reconstruction but fails to produce high-level abstractions. We introduce STELLAR, a framework that resolves this tension by factorizing visual features into a low-rank product of semantic concepts and their spatial distributions. This disentanglement allows us to perform DINO-style augmentation alignment on the semantic tokens while maintaining the precise spatial mapping in the localization matrix necessary for pixel-level reconstruction. We demonstrate that as few as 16 sparse tokens under this factorized form are sufficient to simultaneously support high-quality reconstruction (2.60 FID) and match the semantic performance of dense backbones (79.10% ImageNet accuracy). Our results highlight STELLAR as a versatile sparse representation that bridges the gap between discriminative and generative vision by strategically separating semantic identity from spatial geometry. Code available at https://aka.ms/stellar.

AI.tldr: STELLAR factorizes visual features into a low-rank product of semantic concepts and spatial distributions to reconcile high-level semantic SSL with reconstruction, enabling DINO-style augmentation on semantic tokens while preserving spatial maps for pixel-level reconstruction; with as few as 16 sparse tokens, it achieves 2.60 FID and 79.10% ImageNet accuracy.
AI.motivation: There is a fundamental conflict in SSL between semantic understanding (global, location-invariant tokens) and image reconstruction (dense, spatial structure). A method that cleanly separates semantic identity from spatial geometry could enable simultaneous high-level understanding and reconstruction.
AI.method: Factorize visual features into a low-rank product of semantic concepts and their spatial distributions (V ≈ L×S). Perform DINO-style augmentation alignment on semantic tokens while retaining a localization matrix for precise spatial mapping used in reconstruction. Use sparse tokens (as few as 16) to maintain reconstruction quality and semantic performance.
AI.result: The approach yields high reconstruction quality (2.60 FID) and matches the semantic performance of dense backbones (79.10% ImageNet accuracy) with only 16 sparse tokens, indicating efficient, discriminative-generative bridging.
