AI.motivation: Address real-time clustering of small, sparse event clusters from event cameras; traditional HAC is costly for high-rate, asynchronous streams and large pixel grids; need scalable online processing.
AI.method: An asynchronous, event-driven hierarchical agglomerative clustering approach that uses tempo-spatial distance; exploits the event camera's asynchronous data stream; employs a simple decision rule to merge events into clusters; claims linear-time complexity and independence from pixel grid size.
AI.result: Claims O(n) time with respect to the number of events; runtime is independent of image dimensions, enabling real-time operation.
AI.conclusion: Presents a scalable, efficient clustering method for event cameras focused on small clusters; potential for real-time robotics and computer vision applications; invites evaluation on robustness, parameter sensitivity, and comparison with other online HAC or density-based methods.

title: CAPA: Contribution-Aware Pruning and FFN Approximation for Efficient Large Vision-Language Models
summary: Efficient inference in Large Vision-Language Models is constrained by the high cost of processing thousands of visual tokens, yet it remains unclear which tokens and computations can be safely removed. While attention scores are commonly used to estimate visual token importance, they are an imperfect proxy for actual contribution. We show that Attention Contribution, which weights attention probabilities by value vector magnitude, provides a more accurate criterion for visual token selection. Our empirical analysis reveals that visual attention sinks are functionally heterogeneous, comprising Probability Dumps with low contribution that can be safely pruned, and Structural Anchors with high contribution essential for maintaining model performance. Further, we identify substantial redundancy in Feed-Forward Networks (FFNs) associated with visual tokens, particularly in intermediate layers where image tokens exhibit linear behavior. Based on our findings, we introduce CAPA (Contribution-Aware Pruning and FFN Approximation), a dual-strategy framework that prunes visual tokens using attention contribution at critical functional transitions and reduces FFN computation through efficient linear approximations. Experiments on various benchmarks across baselines show that CAPA achieves competent efficiency--performance trade-offs with improved robustness.

AI.tldr: Contribution-Aware Pruning and FFN Approximation (CAPA) for large vision-language models; prunes visual tokens based on a refined attention criterion and approximates FFN computation to improve efficiency with robust performance.
AI.motivation: Reduce inference cost in large vision-language models by identifying which visual tokens and FFN computations are essential. Attention scores alone are imperfect proxies for contribution; a more accurate criterion is needed to prune tokens without harming performance.
AI.method: Introduce Attention Contribution = attention probability × value-vector magnitude to assess token contribution. Classify visual attention sinks into: (1) Probability Dumps (low contribution, safe to prune) and (2) Structural Anchors (high contribution, essential). Observe FFN redundancy in intermediate layers where token behavior is linear. Propose CAPA: (a) prune tokens using attention contribution at critical functional transitions in the model, (b) approximate FFN computation with efficient linear methods.
AI.result: CAPA achieves competitive efficiency–performance trade-offs across benchmarks and baselines, with improved robustness. Visual token pruning reduces compute while preserving accuracy in critical layers; FFN approximation provides additional speedups without incurring substantial accuracy loss; results highlight heterogeneous token importance and the value of combining pruning with FFN approximation.
AI.conclusion: Attention-contribution-based pruning and FFN linear approximations enable more efficient LVM inference by distinguishing low-contribution versus high-contribution tokens (Probability Dumps vs Structural Anchors) and exploiting FFN redundancy, yielding robust, scalable performance improvements.

title: Modeling Image-Caption Rating from Comparative Judgments
summary: Rating the accuracy of captions in describing images is time-consuming and subjective for humans. In contrast, it is often easier for people to compare two captions and decide which one better matches a given image. In this work, we propose a machine learning framework that models such comparative judgments instead of direct ratings. The model can then be applied to rank unseen image-caption pairs in the same way as a regression model trained on direct ratings. Using the VICR dataset, we extract visual features with ResNet-50 and text features with MiniLM, then train both a regression model and a comparative learning model. While the regression model achieves better performance (Pearson's $ρ$: 0.7609 and Spearman's $r_s$: 0.7089), the comparative learning model steadily improves with more data and approaches the regression baseline. In addition, a small-scale human evaluation study comparing absolute rating, pairwise comparison, and same-image comparison shows that comparative annotation yields faster results and has greater agreement among human annotators. These results suggest that comparative learning can effectively model human preferences while significantly reducing the cost of human annotations.

AI.tldr: Comparative learning for image-caption evaluation offers faster annotations and higher agreement while approaching regression-based ranking performance, though direct regression still performs best on VICR.
AI.motivation: Caption evaluation is time-consuming and subjective when assigning absolute scores; pairwise comparisons are easier for humans. Modeling comparative judgments could reduce annotation cost while capturing human preferences.
AI.method: Use the VICR dataset with ResNet-50 visual features and MiniLM text features. Train a regression model to predict absolute quality scores and a comparative learning model to predict preferences between caption pairs for the same image. Evaluate on standard correlation metrics (Pearson ρ, Spearman rs) and conduct a small-scale human study comparing annotation schemes.
AI.result: Regression achieves higher correlations (Pearson ρ = 0.7609, Spearman rs = 0.7089). The comparative model improves with more data and converges toward the regression baseline. The human study shows comparative annotation is faster and yields greater annotator agreement.
AI.conclusion: Comparative learning effectively models human preferences with reduced annotation cost and, with sufficient data, can match regression-based ranking performance for image-caption evaluation.

title: Brazilian Portuguese Image Captioning with Transformers: A Study on Cross-Native-Translated Dataset
summary: Image captioning (IC) refers to the automatic generation of natural language descriptions for images, with applications ranging from social media content generation to assisting individuals with visual impairments. While most research has been focused on English-based models, low-resource languages such as Brazilian Portuguese face significant challenges due to the lack of specialized datasets and models. Several studies create datasets by automatically translating existing ones to mitigate resource scarcity. This work addresses this gap by proposing a cross-native-translated evaluation of Transformer-based vision and language models for Brazilian Portuguese IC. We use a version of Flickr30K comprised of captions manually created by native Brazilian Portuguese speakers and compare it to a version with captions automatically translated from English to Portuguese. The experiments include a cross-context approach, where models trained on one dataset are tested on the other to assess the translation impact. Additionally, we incorporate attention maps for model inference interpretation and use the CLIP-Score metric to evaluate the image-description alignment. Our findings show that Swin-DistilBERTimbau consistently outperforms other models, demonstrating strong generalization across datasets. ViTucano, a Brazilian Portuguese pre-trained VLM, surpasses larger multilingual models (GPT-4o, LLaMa 3.2 Vision) in traditional text-based evaluation metrics, while GPT-4 models achieve the highest CLIP-Score, highlighting improved image-text alignment. Attention analysis reveals systematic biases, including gender misclassification, object enumeration errors, and spatial inconsistencies. The datasets and the models generated and analyzed during the current study are available in: https://github.com/laicsiifes/transformer-caption-ptbr.

AI.tldr: Cross-native-translated evaluation for Brazilian Portuguese image captioning using Transformer-based vision-language models on native vs translated Flickr30K; Swin-DistilBERTimbau generalizes well; ViTucano excels on traditional metrics; GPT-4o/LLaMa 3.2 Vision top CLIP-Score; attention analyses reveal biases; datasets/models are released.
AI.motivation: Mitigate Portuguese resource scarcity for image captioning by studying how automated translations of captions affect model performance and generalization, and by interpreting model behavior via attention analyses.
AI.method: Two Flickr30K variants: captions written by native Brazilian Portuguese speakers and captions automatically translated from English. Train on one variant and test on the other (cross-context). Evaluate with attention maps for interpretability and CLIP-Score for image-description alignment. Compare Swin-DistilBERTimbau, ViTucano, GPT-4o, and LLaMa 3.2 Vision; release datasets/models.
AI.result: Swin-DistilBERTimbau shows strong generalization across datasets; ViTucano surpasses larger multilingual models on traditional text-based metrics; GPT-4o models achieve the highest CLIP-Score; attention analysis reveals systematic biases (gender misclassification, object enumeration errors, spatial inconsistencies). The work provides open access to datasets/models at the linked repository.
AI.conclusion: Cross-native-translated evaluation clarifies the impact of dataset translation on captioning performance and interpretability. Native PT-BR pre-trained models yield robust generalization, while very large models excel in alignment metrics but still exhibit biases. The study emphasizes careful dataset construction and bias mitigation, and provides resources for the community.

title: 3DGS$^2$-TR: Scalable Second-Order Trust-Region Method for 3D Gaussian Splatting
summary: We propose 3DGS$^2$-TR,a second-order optimizer for accelerating the scene training problem in 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS). Unlike existing second-order approaches that rely on explicit or dense curvature representations, such as 3DGS-LM (Höllein et al., 2025) or 3DGS2 (Lan et al., 2025), our method approximates curvature using only the diagonal of the Hessian matrix, efficiently via Hutchinson's method. Our approach is fully matrix-free and has the same complexity as ADAM (Kingma, 2024), $O(n)$ in both computation and memory costs. To ensure stable optimization in the presence of strong nonlinearity in the 3DGS rasterization process, we introduce a parameter-wise trust-region technique based on the squared Hellinger distance, regularizing updates to Gaussian parameters. Under identical parameter initialization and without densification, 3DGS$^2$-TR is able to achieve better reconstruction quality on standard datasets, using 50% fewer training iterations compared to ADAM, while incurring less than 1GB of peak GPU memory overhead (17% more than ADAM and 85% less than 3DGS-LM), enabling scalability to very large scenes and potentially to distributed training settings.

AI.tldr: Matrix-free second-order optimization for 3D Gaussian Splatting that uses a diagonal Hessian approximation via Hutchinson’s method, with a parameter-wise squared Hellinger trust-region, yielding faster convergence and low memory usage.
AI.motivation: To accelerate scene training in 3DGS while avoiding explicit or dense curvature representations and high memory costs, enabling scalable optimization for large scenes.
AI.method: Estimate diagonal Hessian entries using Hutchinson’s stochastic trace estimator to form a matrix-free second-order update. Introduce a parameter-wise trust-region based on squared Hellinger distance to stabilize updates during the nonlinear 3DGS rasterization, ensuring stable optimization without densification.
AI.result: Outperforms standard first-order ADAM in reconstruction quality with about 50% fewer training iterations, while maintaining <1 GB peak GPU memory overhead (about 17% more than ADAM and 85% less than 3DGS-LM). Comparable complexity to ADAM (O(n)) and scalable to very large scenes; potential for distributed training.
AI.conclusion: 3DGS2-TR provides an efficient, scalable second-order optimization for 3DGS that improves convergence and memory efficiency without dense curvature representations, facilitating larger-scale or distributed training setups.

title: Toward Autonomous Laboratory Safety Monitoring with Vision Language Models: Learning to See Hazards Through Scene Structure
summary: Laboratories are prone to severe injuries from minor unsafe actions, yet continuous safety monitoring -- beyond mandatory pre-lab safety training -- is limited by human availability. Vision language models (VLMs) offer promise for autonomous laboratory safety monitoring, but their effectiveness in realistic settings is unclear due to the lack of visual evaluation data, as most safety incidents are documented primarily as unstructured text. To address this gap, we first introduce a structured data generation pipeline that converts textual laboratory scenarios into aligned triples of (image, scene graph, ground truth), using large language models as scene graph architects and image generation models as renderers. Our experiments on the synthetic dataset of 1,207 samples across 362 unique scenarios and seven open- and closed-source models show that VLMs perform effectively given textual scene graph, but degrade substantially in visual-only settings indicating difficulty in extracting structured object relationships directly from pixels. To overcome this, we propose a post-training context-engineering approach, scene-graph-guided alignment, to bridge perceptual gaps in VLMs by translating visual inputs into structured scene graphs better aligned with VLM reasoning, improving hazard detection performance in visual only settings.

AI.tldr: A synthetic, graph-grounded evaluation dataset reveals VLMs excel with textual scene graphs but struggle with pure vision; a scene-graph-guided alignment method improves hazard detection in visual-only settings by bridging perceptual gaps between pixels and structured reasoning.
AI.motivation: Lack of visual evaluation data for laboratory safety monitoring using vision-language models; most incidents are documented as text, hindering robust, autonomous safety monitoring. Develop a pipeline to align visual data with structured scene graphs to enable better VLM reasoning.
AI.method: Generate a synthetic dataset of 1,207 samples across 362 scenarios by using LLMs to craft scene graphs and image generation models to render aligned (image, scene graph, ground truth) triples. Evaluate seven open- and closed-source VLMs on this data, comparing performance with text-only scene graphs vs. visual inputs. Propose a post-training approach—scene-graph-guided alignment—to translate visual inputs into structured scene graphs that align with VLM reasoning.
AI.result: VLMs perform well when provided a textual scene graph but degrade significantly in visual-only settings due to difficulty extracting structured object relationships from pixels. The proposed scene-graph-guided alignment improves hazard detection performance in visual-only scenarios by bridging perceptual gaps between raw images and the structured reasoning expected by VLMs.
AI.conclusion: Synthetic, graph-grounded datasets enable targeted evaluation of VLMs for laboratory safety monitoring. A post-training, scene-graph-guided alignment can substantially improve hazard detection from visual data by aligning perception with structured reasoning.

title: NPNet: A Non-Parametric Network with Adaptive Gaussian-Fourier Positional Encoding for 3D Classification and Segmentation
comment: Accepted to the 2026 IEEE Intelligent Vehicles Symposium (IV 2026)
summary: We present NPNet, a fully non-parametric approach for 3D point-cloud classification and part segmentation. NPNet contains no learned weights; instead, it builds point features using deterministic operators such as farthest point sampling, k-nearest neighbors, and pooling. Our key idea is an adaptive Gaussian-Fourier positional encoding whose bandwidth and Gaussian-cosine mixing are chosen from the input geometry, helping the method remain stable across different scales and sampling densities. For segmentation, we additionally incorporate fixed-frequency Fourier features to provide global context alongside the adaptive encoding. Across ModelNet40/ModelNet-R, ScanObjectNN, and ShapeNetPart, NPNet achieves strong performance among non-parametric baselines, and it is particularly effective in few-shot settings on ModelNet40. NPNet also offers favorable memory use and inference time compared to prior non-parametric methods

AI.tldr: NPNet is a fully non-parametric method for 3D point-cloud classification and part segmentation that builds features with deterministic operators and adaptive Gaussian-Fourier encodings, achieving competitive accuracy and efficiency among non-parametric baselines, especially in few-shot settings.
AI.motivation: To remove learned weights and improve robustness to varying scales and sampling densities in point-cloud tasks, while keeping memory and inference requirements low; address limitations of existing non-parametric methods.
AI.method: Compute point features using deterministic operators: farthest-point sampling, kNN, and pooling. Use adaptive Gaussian-Fourier positional encoding with bandwidth and Gaussian-cosine mixing selected from input geometry. For segmentation, add fixed-frequency Fourier features for global context. No learned weights; operations are deterministic and non-parametric.
AI.result: On ModelNet40/ModelNet-R, ScanObjectNN, and ShapeNetPart, NPNet ranks among strong non-parametric baselines, with particular strength in few-shot ModelNet40; favorable memory usage and inference time relative to prior non-parametric approaches.
AI.conclusion: NPNet demonstrates that a fully non-parametric framework with carefully designed adaptive encodings can achieve competitive accuracy and efficiency in 3D point-cloud classification and segmentation, offering a viable baseline for scale/density-robust tasks.

title: From Pixels to Facts (Pix2Fact): Benchmarking Multi-Hop Reasoning for Fine-Grained Visual Fact Checking
summary: Despite progress on general tasks, VLMs struggle with challenges demanding both detailed visual grounding and deliberate knowledge-based reasoning, a synergy not captured by existing benchmarks that evaluate these skills separately. To close this gap, we introduce Pix2Fact, a new visual question-answering benchmark designed to evaluate expert-level perception and knowledge-intensive multi-hop reasoning. Pix2Fact contains 1,000 high-resolution (4K+) images spanning 8 daily-life scenarios and situations, with questions and answers meticulously crafted by annotators holding PhDs from top global universities working in partnership with a professional data annotation firm. Each question requires detailed visual grounding, multi-hop reasoning, and the integration of external knowledge to answer. Our evaluation of 9 state-of-the-art VLMs, including proprietary models like Gemini-3-Pro and GPT-5, reveals the substantial challenge posed by Pix2Fact: the most advanced model achieves only 24.0% average accuracy, in stark contrast to human performance of 56%. This significant gap underscores the limitations of current models in replicating human-level visual comprehension. We believe Pix2Fact will serve as a critical benchmark to drive the development of next-generation multimodal agents that combine fine-grained perception with robust, knowledge-based reasoning.

AI.tldr: Pix2Fact introduces a visual QA benchmark that pairs expert-level visual grounding with knowledge-intensive, multi-hop reasoning. It uses 1,000 4K+ images across 8 scenarios; questions demand grounding, reasoning, and external knowledge. Leading models perform poorly (avg 24% accuracy) versus humans (56%).
AI.motivation: Current VLM benchmarks evaluate grounding and reasoning in isolation. There is a need for a benchmark that requires integrated, knowledge-based multi-hop reasoning over rich visual inputs to drive development of next-generation multimodal agents.
AI.method: Constructed Pix2Fact with 1,000 high-resolution images across eight daily-life scenarios. Questions and answers crafted by PhD annotators in collaboration with a professional data-annotation firm. Questions demand fine-grained visual grounding, multi-hop reasoning, and integration of external knowledge. Evaluated nine state-of-the-art VLMs, including Gemini-3-Pro and GPT-5.
AI.result: The strongest model attains only 24.0% average accuracy, far below human performance at 56%, highlighting a substantial gap in current models’ ability to perform combined perception and knowledge-based reasoning.
AI.conclusion: Pix2Fact provides a challenging, holistic benchmark to spur the development of multimodal agents capable of fine-grained perception and robust knowledge-based reasoning.

title: WorldVQA: Measuring Atomic World Knowledge in Multimodal Large Language Models
summary: We introduce WorldVQA, a benchmark designed to evaluate the atomic visual world knowledge of Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs). Unlike current evaluations, which often conflate visual knowledge retrieval with reasoning, WorldVQA decouples these capabilities to strictly measure "what the model memorizes." The benchmark assesses the atomic capability of grounding and naming visual entities across a stratified taxonomy, spanning from common head-class objects to long-tail rarities. We expect WorldVQA to serve as a rigorous test for visual factuality, thereby establishing a standard for assessing the encyclopedic breadth and hallucination rates of current and next-generation frontier models.

AI.tldr: WorldVQA is a benchmark to isolate and assess memorized visual world knowledge in Multimodal LLMs by grounding and naming visual entities across a head-to-tail taxonomy, decoupling knowledge retrieval from reasoning to measure memorization and reduce hallucinations.
AI.motivation: Current evaluations conflate visual knowledge retrieval with reasoning, making it hard to quantify what models actually memorize. A robust, encyclopedic benchmark is needed to quantify factuality and hallucination across a broad object distribution.
AI.method: Construct a stratified taxonomy of visual entities from common to long-tail; design tasks that require identifying and naming entities from images without requiring reasoning beyond factual grounding; decouple retrieval from reasoning to specifically test memorized knowledge; evaluate across head and tail categories to assess coverage and hallucination rates.
AI.result: Not specified in abstract (likely future work/papers).
AI.conclusion: WorldVQA promises a rigorous standard for evaluating visual factuality and encyclopedic breadth, enabling measurement of hallucination rates in current and frontier MLLMs.

title: AdaptMMBench: Benchmarking Adaptive Multimodal Reasoning for Mode Selection and Reasoning Process
summary: Adaptive multimodal reasoning has emerged as a promising frontier in Vision-Language Models (VLMs), aiming to dynamically modulate between tool-augmented visual reasoning and text reasoning to enhance both effectiveness and efficiency. However, existing evaluations rely on static difficulty labels and simplistic metrics, which fail to capture the dynamic nature of difficulty relative to varying model capacities. Consequently, they obscure the distinction between adaptive mode selection and general performance while neglecting fine-grained process analyses. In this paper, we propose AdaptMMBench, a comprehensive benchmark for adaptive multimodal reasoning across five domains: real-world, OCR, GUI, knowledge, and math, encompassing both direct perception and complex reasoning tasks. AdaptMMBench utilizes a Matthews Correlation Coefficient (MCC) metric to evaluate the selection rationality of different reasoning modes, isolating this meta-cognition ability by dynamically identifying task difficulties based on models' capability boundaries. Moreover, AdaptMMBench facilitates multi-dimensional process evaluation across key step coverage, tool effectiveness, and computational efficiency. Our evaluation reveals that while adaptive mode selection scales with model capacity, it notably decouples from final accuracy. Conversely, key step coverage aligns with performance, though tool effectiveness remains highly inconsistent across model architectures.

AI.tldr: AdaptMMBench: a benchmark for adaptive multimodal reasoning across five domains, using MCC to measure mode-selection meta-cognition and dynamic difficulty; multi-dimensional process evaluation; findings: mode selection scales with model capacity but decouples from accuracy; key step coverage aligns with performance; tool effectiveness inconsistent.
AI.motivation: Address insufficiencies of static difficulty labels and simplistic metrics in evaluating adaptive multimodal reasoning; need to isolate mode selection (meta-cognition) and analyze process dynamics across architectures.
AI.method: Five-domain benchmark (real-world, OCR, GUI, knowledge, math) including direct perception and complex reasoning; uses MCC to quantify mode-selection rationality; dynamically identifies task difficulty based on model capability boundaries; evaluates process metrics: step coverage, tool effectiveness, computational efficiency.
AI.result: Adaptive mode selection improves with model capacity but not necessarily final accuracy; step coverage correlates with performance; tool effectiveness varies across architectures; coverage alone may suffice for performance, but tool usage reliability is architecture-dependent.
AI.conclusion: Adaptive modulation offers benefits but is not guaranteed to improve end results; comprehensive process metrics are essential; future work should address tool integration consistency and cross-architecture robustness.

title: End-to-end reconstruction of OCT optical properties and speckle-reduced structural intensity via physics-based learning
summary: Inverse scattering in optical coherence tomography (OCT) seeks to recover both structural images and intrinsic tissue optical properties, including refractive index, scattering coefficient, and anisotropy. This inverse problem is challenging due to attenuation, speckle noise, and strong coupling among parameters. We propose a regularized end-to-end deep learning framework that jointly reconstructs optical parameter maps and speckle-reduced OCT structural intensity for layer visualization. Trained with Monte Carlo-simulated ground truth, our network incorporates a physics-based OCT forward model that generates predicted signals from the estimated parameters, providing physics-consistent supervision for parameter recovery and artifact suppression. Experiments on the synthetic corneal OCT dataset demonstrate robust optical map recovery under noise, improved resolution, and enhanced structural fidelity. This approach enables quantitative multi-parameter tissue characterization and highlights the benefit of combining physics-informed modeling with deep learning for computational OCT.

AI.tldr: Physics-informed end-to-end deep learning for OCT inverse scattering: jointly estimate optical property maps and speckle-reduced structural images using a physics-based forward model and Monte Carlo-ground-truth, showing robust optical map recovery on synthetic corneal data.
AI.motivation: Reconstruct both tissue optical properties (refractive index, scattering coefficient, anisotropy) and structural OCT images. Challenges include attenuation, speckle noise, and strong coupling between parameters, which hamper accurate, quantitative tissue characterization.
AI.method: A regularized, end-to-end deep learning framework that jointly reconstructs optical parameter maps and speckle-reduced structural OCT images. The network is trained with Monte Carlo-simulated ground truth and incorporates a physics-based OCT forward model to generate predicted signals from estimated parameters, providing physics-consistent supervision for parameter recovery and artifact suppression.
AI.result: On synthetic corneal OCT datasets, the method achieves robust optical map recovery under noise, improved resolution, and enhanced structural fidelity, enabling quantitative multi-parameter tissue characterization.
AI.conclusion: Integrating physics-informed modeling with deep learning improves quantitative OCT characterization by enforcing physical consistency, enabling artifact suppression, and potentially generalizing to multi-parameter tissue analysis beyond conventional intensity-based imaging.

title: SVD-ViT: Does SVD Make Vision Transformers Attend More to the Foreground?
summary: Vision Transformers (ViT) have been established as large-scale foundation models. However, because self-attention operates globally, they lack an explicit mechanism to distinguish foreground from background. As a result, ViT may learn unnecessary background features and artifacts, leading to degraded classification performance. To address this issue, we propose SVD-ViT, which leverages singular value decomposition (SVD) to prioritize the learning of foreground features. SVD-ViT consists of three components-\textbf{SPC module}, \textbf{SSVA}, and \textbf{ID-RSVD}-and suppresses task-irrelevant factors such as background noise and artifacts by extracting and aggregating singular vectors that capture object foreground information. Experimental results demonstrate that our method improves classification accuracy and effectively learns informative foreground representations while reducing the impact of background noise.

AI.tldr: SVD-ViT uses singular value decomposition to prioritize foreground features in Vision Transformers, via SPC module, SSVA, and ID-RSVD, improving accuracy and foreground representation while reducing background noise.
AI.motivation: ViTs rely on global self-attention which blends foreground and background, potentially learning irrelevant background artifacts that hurt classification performance.
AI.method: Introduces three components—SPC module, SSVA, and ID-RSVD—that extract and aggregate singular vectors associated with foreground information to suppress background noise within the ViT framework.
AI.result: Empirical results show higher classification accuracy and more informative foreground representations with reduced sensitivity to background artifacts.
AI.conclusion: SVD-based foreground prioritization in ViT effectively suppresses task-irrelevant factors and enhances foreground learning.

title: LmPT: Conditional Point Transformer for Anatomical Landmark Detection on 3D Point Clouds
comment: This paper has been accepted at International Symposium on Biomedical Imaging (ISBI) 2026
summary: Accurate identification of anatomical landmarks is crucial for various medical applications. Traditional manual landmarking is time-consuming and prone to inter-observer variability, while rule-based methods are often tailored to specific geometries or limited sets of landmarks. In recent years, anatomical surfaces have been effectively represented as point clouds, which are lightweight structures composed of spatial coordinates. Following this strategy and to overcome the limitations of existing landmarking techniques, we propose Landmark Point Transformer (LmPT), a method for automatic anatomical landmark detection on point clouds that can leverage homologous bones from different species for translational research. The LmPT model incorporates a conditioning mechanism that enables adaptability to different input types to conduct cross-species learning. We focus the evaluation of our approach on femoral landmarking using both human and newly annotated dog femurs, demonstrating its generalization and effectiveness across species. The code and dog femur dataset will be publicly available at: https://github.com/Pierreoo/LandmarkPointTransformer.

AI.tldr: LmPT: a cross-species landmark detection method on bone point clouds using a conditioning mechanism to adapt to different input types, demonstrated on human and dog femurs.
AI.motivation: Manual landmarking is time-consuming and variable; rule-based methods are limited to specific geometries; representing anatomical surfaces as point clouds enables lightweight processing; need cross-species learning for translational research.
AI.method: A Landmark Point Transformer with a conditioning mechanism that adapts to different input types, enabling cross-species learning from homologous bones. Evaluated on femoral landmarks in human and dog femurs with newly annotated data; code and dataset released.
AI.result: Demonstrates generalization and effectiveness across species in automatic femoral landmarking on point clouds.
AI.conclusion: LmPT enables automatic, cross-species landmark detection on bone surfaces and supports translational research; code and dog femur dataset will be publicly available.

title: Self-Supervised Uncalibrated Multi-View Video Anonymization in the Operating Room
summary: Privacy preservation is a prerequisite for using video data in Operating Room (OR) research. Effective anonymization relies on the exhaustive localization of every individual; even a single missed detection necessitates extensive manual correction. However, existing approaches face two critical scalability bottlenecks: (1) they usually require manual annotations of each new clinical site for high accuracy; (2) while multi-camera setups have been widely adopted to address single-view ambiguity, camera calibration is typically required whenever cameras are repositioned. To address these problems, we propose a novel self-supervised multi-view video anonymization framework consisting of whole-body person detection and whole-body pose estimation, without annotation or camera calibration. Our core strategy is to enhance the single-view detector by "retrieving" false negatives using temporal and multi-view context, and conducting self-supervised domain adaptation. We first run an off-the-shelf whole-body person detector in each view with a low-score threshold to gather candidate detections. Then, we retrieve the low-score false negatives that exhibit consistency with the high-score detections via tracking and self-supervised uncalibrated multi-view association. These recovered detections serve as pseudo labels to iteratively fine-tune the whole-body detector. Finally, we apply whole-body pose estimation on each detected person, and fine-tune the pose model using its own high-score predictions. Experiments on the 4D-OR dataset of simulated surgeries and our dataset of real surgeries show the effectiveness of our approach achieving over 97% recall. Moreover, we train a real-time whole-body detector using our pseudo labels, achieving comparable performance and highlighting our method's practical applicability. Code is available at https://github.com/CAMMA-public/OR_anonymization.

AI.tldr: A self-supervised, calibration-free, multi-view anonymization framework for OR video that bootstraps a detector with low-threshold detections, recovers missed detections via temporal and cross-view consistency to create pseudo-labels, and iteratively refines both person detection and pose estimation. It achieves high recall (>97%), supports real-time inference, and eliminates site-specific annotations and camera calibration.
AI.motivation: To enable scalable privacy-preserving anonymization of surgical video by removing the need for manual site-specific annotations and camera recalibration when camera setups change.
AI.method: 1) Run a low-threshold off-the-shelf whole-body detector per view. 2) Retrieve low-score false negatives consistent with high-score detections using tracking and uncalibrated multi-view association to form pseudo labels. 3) Iteratively fine-tune the detector with pseudo labels. 4) Apply whole-body pose estimation and fine-tune the pose model using high-score predictions. 5) Train a real-time detector with pseudo labels. All steps are self-supervised and do not require annotations or calibration.
AI.result: Experiments on the 4D-OR dataset (simulated surgeries) and a real-surgery dataset show recall >97%. A real-time detector trained with pseudo labels achieves comparable performance.
AI.conclusion: The proposed self-supervised, calibration-free multi-view framework effectively addresses both scalability bottlenecks for OR video anonymization, enabling accurate, real-time whole-body detection and pose estimation without manual annotations or camera calibration.

title: Hybrid Topological and Deep Feature Fusion for Accurate MRI-Based Alzheimer's Disease Severity Classification
summary:
Early and accurate diagnosis of Alzheimer's disease (AD) remains a critical challenge in neuroimaging-based clinical decision support systems. In this work, we propose a novel hybrid deep learning framework that integrates Topological Data Analysis (TDA) with a DenseNet121 backbone for four-class Alzheimer's disease classification using structural MRI data from the OASIS dataset. TDA is employed to capture complementary topological characteristics of brain structures that are often overlooked by conventional neural networks, while DenseNet121 efficiently learns hierarchical spatial features from MRI slices. The extracted deep and topological features are fused to enhance class separability across the four AD stages.
  Extensive experiments conducted on the OASIS-1 Kaggle MRI dataset demonstrate that the proposed TDA+DenseNet121 model significantly outperforms existing state-of-the-art approaches. The model achieves an accuracy of 99.93% and an AUC of 100%, surpassing recently published CNN-based, transfer learning, ensemble, and multi-scale architectures. These results confirm the effectiveness of incorporating topological insights into deep learning pipelines and highlight the potential of the proposed framework as a robust and highly accurate tool for automated Alzheimer's disease diagnosis.

AI.tldr: Hybrid Topological Data Analysis (TDA) with DenseNet121 for four-class Alzheimer's disease classification using structural MRI (OASIS), achieving 99.93% accuracy and 100% AUC on the OASIS-1 Kaggle dataset; claims to surpass CNN-based, transfer learning, ensemble, and multi-scale models.
AI.motivation: Address the need for early and accurate AD diagnosis by capturing brain topological features that conventional CNNs may miss, thereby improving diagnostic performance and robustness.
AI.method: Integrate Topological Data Analysis with a DenseNet121 backbone. Extract deep spatial features from MRI slices and topological features (e.g., persistence diagrams/ barcodes). Fuse deep and topological features for four-class AD classification on the OASIS MRI dataset (OASIS-1 Kaggle).
AI.result: The model reports an accuracy of 99.93% and an AUC of 100%, outperforming recent CNN-based, transfer learning, ensemble, and multi-scale approaches on the same dataset.
AI.conclusion: Combining TDA with deep learning can enhance feature representation and class separability, yielding a highly accurate framework for automated AD diagnosis with potential clinical utility.

title: ViThinker: Active Vision-Language Reasoning via Dynamic Perceptual Querying
summary: Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning excels in language models but struggles in vision-language models due to premature visual-to-text conversion that discards continuous information such as geometry and spatial layout. While recent methods enhance CoT through static enumeration or attention-based selection, they remain passive, i.e., processing pre-computed inputs rather than actively seeking task-relevant details. Inspired by human active perception, we introduce ViThinker, a framework that enables vision-language models to autonomously generate decision (query) tokens triggering the synthesis of expert-aligned visual features on demand. ViThinker internalizes vision-expert capabilities during training, performing generative mental simulation during inference without external tool calls. Through a two-stage curriculum: first distilling frozen experts into model parameters, then learning task-driven querying via sparsity penalties, i.e., ViThinker discovers minimal sufficient perception for each reasoning step. Evaluations across vision-centric benchmarks demonstrate consistent improvements, validating that active query generation outperforms passive approaches in both perceptual grounding and reasoning accuracy.

AI.tldr: Active query-driven perception (ViThinker) enables vision-language models to synthesize on-demand, expert-aligned visual features for each reasoning step, improving CoT performance over passive methods.
AI.motivation: CoT in vision-language models degrades due to loss of continuous geometric/spatial information during static visual-to-text conversion; human active perception inspires an active-perception-based approach.
AI.method: ViThinker introduces decision (query) tokens that trigger synthesis of expert-aligned features; two-stage curriculum: (1) distill frozen vision experts into model parameters, (2) train task-driven querying with sparsity penalties to learn minimal sufficient perception per reasoning step; no external tool calls; generative mental simulation during inference.
AI.result: Across vision-centric benchmarks, ViThinker yields consistent improvements; active query generation outperforms passive approaches in perceptual grounding and reasoning accuracy.
AI.conclusion: Active, query-driven perception with a two-stage curriculum enables vision-language models to perform more effective chain-of-thought by maintaining richer perceptual information and on-demand feature synthesis.

title: DoubleTake: Contrastive Reasoning for Faithful Decision-Making in Medical Imaging
summary: Accurate decision making in medical imaging requires reasoning over subtle visual differences between confusable conditions, yet most existing approaches rely on nearest neighbor retrieval that returns redundant evidence and reinforces a single hypothesis. We introduce a contrastive, document-aware reference selection framework that constructs compact evidence sets optimized for discrimination rather than similarity by explicitly balancing visual relevance, embedding diversity, and source-level provenance using ROCO embeddings and metadata. While ROCO provides large-scale image-caption pairs, it does not specify how references should be selected for contrastive reasoning, and naive retrieval frequently yields near-duplicate figures from the same document. To address this gap, we release a reproducible reference selection protocol and curated reference bank that enable a systematic study of contrastive retrieval in medical image reasoning. Building on these contrastive evidence sets, we propose Counterfactual-Contrastive Inference, a confidence-aware reasoning framework that performs structured pairwise visual comparisons and aggregates evidence using margin-based decision rules with faithful abstention. On the MediConfusion benchmark, our approach achieves state-of-the-art performance, improving set-level accuracy by nearly 15% relative to prior methods while reducing confusion and improving individual accuracy.

AI.tldr: A contrastive, document-aware reference selection framework for medical image reasoning that builds compact, diverse, provenance-aware evidence sets using ROCO embeddings, plus a Counterfactual-Contrastive Inference with abstention; achieves state-of-the-art on MediConfusion (~15% relative improvement).
AI.motivation: To overcome redundancy and bias in nearest-neighbor retrieval and to enable discrimination between confusable medical conditions by using compact, diverse, provenance-rich evidence for contrastive reasoning.
AI.method: 1) Construct compact evidence sets by balancing visual relevance, embedding diversity, and source provenance using ROCO embeddings and metadata. 2) Release a reproducible reference selection protocol and curated reference bank to study contrastive retrieval in medical image reasoning. 3) Propose Counterfactual-Contrastive Inference, performing structured pairwise visual comparisons and aggregating evidence via margin-based decision rules with abstention.
AI.result: On the MediConfusion benchmark, the approach achieves state-of-the-art performance, with set-level accuracy increased by ~15% relative to prior methods, and improved individual accuracy while reducing confusion.
AI.conclusion: The framework enhances medical imaging reasoning by producing diverse, provenance-aware contrastive evidence and by using a confidence-aware, abstaining decision rule, yielding substantial performance gains over prior approaches.

title: CortiNet: A Physics-Perception Hybrid Cortical-Inspired Dual-Stream Network for Gallbladder Disease Diagnosis from Ultrasound
summary: Ultrasound imaging is the primary diagnostic modality for detecting Gallbladder diseases due to its non-invasive nature, affordability, and wide accessibility. However, the low resolution and speckle noise inherent to ultrasound images hinder diagnostic reliability, prompting the use of large convolutional neural networks that are difficult to deploy in routine clinical settings. In this work, we propose CortiNet, a lightweight, cortical-inspired dual-stream neural architecture for gallbladder disease diagnosis that integrates physically interpretable multi-scale signal decomposition with perception-driven feature learning. Inspired by parallel processing pathways in the human visual cortex, CortiNet explicitly separates low-frequency structural information from high-frequency perceptual details and processes them through specialized encoding streams. By operating directly on structured, frequency-selective representations rather than raw pixel intensities, the architecture embeds strong physics-based inductive bias, enabling efficient feature learning with a significantly reduced parameter footprint. A late-stage cortical-style fusion mechanism integrates complementary structural and textural cues while preserving computational efficiency. Additionally, we propose a structure-aware explainability framework wherein gradient-weighted class activation mapping is only applied to the structural branch of the proposed CortiNet architecture. This choice allows the model to only focus on the structural features, making it robust against speckle noise. We evaluate CortiNet on 10,692 expert-annotated images spanning nine clinically relevant gallbladder disease categories. Experimental results demonstrate that CortiNet achieves high diagnostic accuracy (98.74%) with only a fraction of the parameters required by conventional deep convolutional models.

AI.tldr: CortiNet is a lightweight, dual-stream, physics-informed architecture for gallbladder ultrasound classification that separates low-frequency structure and high-frequency texture, fusing them later; it includes a structure-focused explainability module and achieves 98.74% accuracy on 10,692 images across nine diseases with far fewer parameters than typical CNNs.
AI.motivation: Ultrasound is widely used but limited by low resolution and speckle noise; large CNNs are impractical for routine clinical deployment. A compact model with physics-informed inductive biases could improve reliability and efficiency.
AI.method: A cortical-inspired dual-stream network that processes frequency-decomposed representations (low-frequency structural info and high-frequency perceptual details) in separate encoding streams, with late fusion and a structure-aware Grad-CAM applied only to the structural branch for explainability and robustness to speckle noise.
AI.result: Evaluation on 10,692 expert-annotated ultrasound images spanning nine gallbladder disease categories shows high diagnostic accuracy (98.74%) with a significantly reduced parameter count compared to conventional deep CNNs.
AI.conclusion: CortiNet demonstrates that frequency-aware, dual-stream architectures with physics-based inductive bias and structure-focused explainability can deliver high-accuracy gallbladder disease diagnosis with improved efficiency, suitable for clinical deployment.

title: FaceLinkGen: Rethinking Identity Leakage in Privacy-Preserving Face Recognition with Identity Extraction
summary: Transformation-based privacy-preserving face recognition (PPFR) aims to verify identities while hiding facial data from attackers and malicious service providers. Existing evaluations mostly treat privacy as resistance to pixel-level reconstruction, measured by PSNR and SSIM. We show that this reconstruction-centric view fails. We present FaceLinkGen, an identity extraction attack that performs linkage/matching and face regeneration directly from protected templates without recovering original pixels. On three recent PPFR systems, FaceLinkGen reaches over 98.5\% matching accuracy and above 96\% regeneration success, and still exceeds 92\% matching and 94\% regeneration in a near zero knowledge setting. These results expose a structural gap between pixel distortion metrics, which are widely used in PPFR evaluation, and real privacy. We show that visual obfuscation leaves identity information broadly exposed to both external intruders and untrusted service providers.

AI.tldr: FaceLinkGen shows that PPFR's pixel-based privacy metrics do not capture true privacy risk; an attack extracts identity from protected templates via linkage/matching and face regeneration, achieving very high accuracy and regeneration success across three systems, revealing a gap between PSNR/SSIM and real privacy.
AI.motivation: Pixel-distortion metrics (PSNR/SSIM) used in PPFR evaluations inadequately reflect identity privacy; need to assess whether protected templates truly conceal identities from both external and internal adversaries.
AI.method: Introduce FaceLinkGen, an identity extraction attack that operates on protected templates to perform linkage/matching and regenerate recognizable faces without recovering original pixels. Evaluate on three recent PPFR systems and in near zero-knowledge settings.
AI.result: FaceLinkGen achieves >98.5% matching accuracy and >96% regeneration success on three PPFR systems; in near zero-knowledge settings it remains >92% matching and >94% regeneration.
AI.conclusion: There is a fundamental gap between pixel-level distortion metrics and actual privacy; visual obfuscation in PPFR does not adequately protect identity from attackers who can exploit protected templates for linkage and regeneration; evaluation should incorporate identity-leakage metrics beyond PSNR/SSIM.

title: PDE-Constrained Optimization for Neural Image Segmentation with Physics Priors
summary: Segmentation of microscopy images constitutes an ill-posed inverse problem due to measurement noise, weak object boundaries, and limited labeled data. Although deep neural networks provide flexible nonparametric estimators, unconstrained empirical risk minimization often leads to unstable solutions and poor generalization. In this work, image segmentation is formulated as a PDE-constrained optimization problem that integrates physically motivated priors into deep learning models through variational regularization. The proposed framework minimizes a composite objective function consisting of a data fidelity term and penalty terms derived from reaction-diffusion equations and phase-field interface energies, all implemented as differentiable residual losses. Experiments are conducted on the LIVECell dataset, a high-quality, manually annotated collection of phase-contrast microscopy images. Training is performed on two cell types, while evaluation is carried out on a distinct, unseen cell type to assess generalization. A UNet architecture is used as the unconstrained baseline model. Experimental results demonstrate consistent improvements in segmentation accuracy and boundary fidelity compared to unconstrained deep learning baselines. Moreover, the PDE-regularized models exhibit enhanced stability and improved generalization in low-sample regimes, highlighting the advantages of incorporating structured priors. The proposed approach illustrates how PDE-constrained optimization can strengthen data-driven learning frameworks, providing a principled bridge between variational methods, statistical learning, and scientific machine learning.

AI.tldr: PDE-constrained variational priors improve deep-learning segmentation of microscopy images, yielding better boundary fidelity and generalization under limited data; demonstrated on LIVECell with a UNet baseline.
AI.motivation: Mitigate ill-posed segmentation due to noise, weak boundaries, and scarce labels by injecting physics-based priors into deep learning through differentiable PDE-inspired regularization.
AI.method: Formulate segmentation as PDE-constrained optimization with a composite objective: data fidelity plus penalties from reaction–diffusion dynamics and phase-field interface energies; implement penalties as differentiable residual losses; train a UNet baseline and compare against unconstrained models; evaluate generalization on unseen cell type in LIVECell dataset; assess stability in low-sample regimes.
AI.result: Consistent gains in segmentation accuracy and boundary fidelity over unconstrained baselines; PDE-regularized models show improved stability and generalization when data are scarce.
AI.conclusion: PDE-constrained optimization provides a principled framework to incorporate physically motivated priors into data-driven segmentation, strengthening robustness and generalization and linking variational methods with deep learning.

title: A Multi-scale Linear-time Encoder for Whole-Slide Image Analysis
comment: Accepted to ISBI 2026, 4 pages with 2 figures
summary: We introduce Multi-scale Adaptive Recurrent Biomedical Linear-time Encoder (MARBLE), the first \textit{purely Mamba-based} multi-state multiple instance learning (MIL) framework for whole-slide image (WSI) analysis. MARBLE processes multiple magnification levels in parallel and integrates coarse-to-fine reasoning within a linear-time state-space model, efficiently capturing cross-scale dependencies with minimal parameter overhead. WSI analysis remains challenging due to gigapixel resolutions and hierarchical magnifications, while existing MIL methods typically operate at a single scale and transformer-based approaches suffer from quadratic attention costs. By coupling parallel multi-scale processing with linear-time sequence modeling, MARBLE provides a scalable and modular alternative to attention-based architectures. Experiments on five public datasets show improvements of up to \textbf{6.9\%} in AUC, \textbf{20.3\%} in accuracy, and \textbf{2.3\%} in C-index, establishing MARBLE as an efficient and generalizable framework for multi-scale WSI analysis.

AI.tldr: MARBLE: a purely Mamba-based, multi-scale MIL framework for whole-slide image analysis that parallelizes multiple magnifications with a linear-time recurrent state-space model, enabling scalable cross-scale reasoning without attention. Improves AUC, accuracy, and C-index on five datasets; offers an efficient alternative to transformer-based approaches.
AI.motivation: WSI data are gigapixel-scale and hierarchically magnified, making single-scale MIL approaches and Transformer-based methods inefficient or costly. There is a need for a scalable, multi-scale MIL framework that can capture cross-scale dependencies with low parameter overhead.
AI.method: Develop MARBLE which processes multiple magnification levels in parallel and uses a linear-time state-space model (purely Mamba-based) to integrate coarse-to-fine reasoning across scales, achieving cross-scale dependency modeling with minimal parameter overhead.
AI.result: Empirical evaluations on five public datasets show improvements up to 6.9% in AUC, 20.3% in accuracy, and 2.3% in C-index, demonstrating MARBLE’s efficiency and generalizability for multi-scale WSI analysis.
AI.conclusion: MARBLE provides a scalable, modular alternative to attention-based architectures for multi-scale WSI analysis, combining parallel multi-scale processing with linear-time sequence modeling to address the computational bottlenecks of attention while maintaining or improving performance.

title: SRA-Seg: Synthetic to Real Alignment for Semi-Supervised Medical Image Segmentation
summary: Synthetic data, an appealing alternative to extensive expert-annotated data for medical image segmentation, consistently fails to improve segmentation performance despite its visual realism. The reason being that synthetic and real medical images exist in different semantic feature spaces, creating a domain gap that current semi-supervised learning methods cannot bridge. We propose SRA-Seg, a framework explicitly designed to align synthetic and real feature distributions for medical image segmentation. SRA-Seg introduces a similarity-alignment (SA) loss using frozen DINOv2 embeddings to pull synthetic representations toward their nearest real counterparts in semantic space. We employ soft edge blending to create smooth anatomical transitions and continuous labels, eliminating the hard boundaries from traditional copy-paste augmentation. The framework generates pseudo-labels for synthetic images via an EMA teacher model and applies soft-segmentation losses that respect uncertainty in mixed regions. Our experiments demonstrate strong results: using only 10% labeled real data and 90% synthetic unlabeled data, SRA-Seg achieves 89.34% Dice on ACDC and 84.42% on FIVES, significantly outperforming existing semi-supervised methods and matching the performance of methods using real unlabeled data.

AI.tldr: SRA-Seg aligns synthetic and real medical images in semantic space to improve segmentation with limited real labels, employing a DINOv2-based similarity loss, soft edge blending, and EMA pseudo-labeling; achieves strong results on two datasets.
AI.motivation: Domain gap between synthetic and real medical images prevents these data from boosting segmentation performance; leveraging abundant synthetic data with minimal real annotations is desirable.
AI.method: Similarity-alignment (SA) loss using frozen DINOv2 embeddings to pull synthetic representations toward their nearest real counterparts in semantic space. Soft edge blending creates smooth anatomical transitions and continuous labels. Pseudo-labels for synthetic images are generated via an EMA teacher model, with soft-segmentation losses that account for uncertainty in mixed regions.
AI.result: With 10% labeled real data and 90% synthetic unlabeled data, SRA-Seg achieves 89.34% Dice on ACDC and 84.42% on FIVES, outperforming existing semi-supervised methods and matching techniques that use real unlabeled data.
AI.conclusion: Semantic-space alignment plus soft augmentations effectively bridge the synthetic-real domain gap, enabling competitive medical segmentation performance using largely synthetic data with a small fraction of real labels.

title: Refining Context-Entangled Content Segmentation via Curriculum Selection and Anti-Curriculum Promotion
summary: Biological learning proceeds from easy to difficult tasks, gradually reinforcing perception and robustness. Inspired by this principle, we address Context-Entangled Content Segmentation (CECS), a challenging setting where objects share intrinsic visual patterns with their surroundings, as in camouflaged object detection. Conventional segmentation networks predominantly rely on architectural enhancements but often ignore the learning dynamics that govern robustness under entangled data distributions. We introduce CurriSeg, a dual-phase learning framework that unifies curriculum and anti-curriculum principles to improve representation reliability. In the Curriculum Selection phase, CurriSeg dynamically selects training data based on the temporal statistics of sample losses, distinguishing hard-but-informative samples from noisy or ambiguous ones, thus enabling stable capability enhancement. In the Anti-Curriculum Promotion phase, we design Spectral-Blindness Fine-Tuning, which suppresses high-frequency components to enforce dependence on low-frequency structural and contextual cues and thus strengthens generalization. Extensive experiments demonstrate that CurriSeg achieves consistent improvements across diverse CECS benchmarks without adding parameters or increasing total training time, offering a principled view of how progression and challenge interplay to foster robust and context-aware segmentation. Code will be released.

AI.tldr: CurriSeg is a dual-phase learning framework for Context-Entangled Content Segmentation (CECS) that combines Curriculum Selection (data-driven, loss-based sample curation) with Anti-Curriculum (Spectral-Blindness Fine-Tuning to suppress high-frequency cues) to improve robustness and generalization without adding parameters or training time.
AI.motivation: CECS arises when objects share visual patterns with their surroundings, making segmentation difficult. Traditional networks emphasize architecture rather than learning dynamics under entangled data; thus there is a need for robust, progression-aware training dynamics that exploit easy-to-hard growth.
AI.method: Phase 1 Curriculum Selection dynamically selects informative hard samples based on temporal loss statistics to stabilize capability growth. Phase 2 Anti-Curriculum Promotion uses Spectral-Blindness Fine-Tuning to suppress high-frequency components, forcing reliance on low-frequency structural/context cues during fine-tuning, thereby improving generalization. The framework claims no extra parameters or training-time cost.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show CurriSeg yields consistent improvements across diverse CECS benchmarks without increasing model size or training time. The authors also commit to releasing the code.
AI.conclusion: CurriSeg demonstrates that integrating progression (curriculum) and challenge (anti-curriculum) dynamics can foster robust, context-aware segmentation in CP/CECS tasks, offering a principled approach applicable to entangled data distributions.

title: Nüwa: Mending the Spatial Integrity Torn by VLM Token Pruning
summary: Vision token pruning has proven to be an effective acceleration technique for the efficient Vision Language Model (VLM). However, existing pruning methods demonstrate excellent performance preservation in visual question answering (VQA) and suffer substantial degradation on visual grounding (VG) tasks. Our analysis of the VLM's processing pipeline reveals that strategies utilizing global semantic similarity and attention scores lose the global spatial reference frame, which is derived from the interactions of tokens' positional information. Motivated by these findings, we propose $\text{Nüwa}$, a two-stage token pruning framework that enables efficient feature aggregation while maintaining spatial integrity. In the first stage, after the vision encoder, we apply three operations, namely separation, alignment, and aggregation, which are inspired by swarm intelligence algorithms to retain information-rich global spatial anchors. In the second stage, within the LLM, we perform text-guided pruning to retain task-relevant visual tokens. Extensive experiments demonstrate that $\text{Nüwa}$ achieves SOTA performance on multiple VQA benchmarks (from 94% to 95%) and yields substantial improvements on visual grounding tasks (from 7% to 47%).

AI.tldr: Two-stage token pruning method Nüwa preserves spatial information in vision-language models, improving VQA and visual grounding; uses swarm-inspired separation/alignment/aggregation after the vision encoder and text-guided pruning in the LLM, achieving SOTA VQA and large gains on VG.
AI.motivation: Existing token pruning methods preserve accuracy on VQA but degrade visual grounding because they lose the global spatial reference frame derived from token positional interactions; a method is needed to maintain spatial integrity during pruning.
AI.method: Stage 1 (after the vision encoder): separation, alignment, and aggregation inspired by swarm intelligence to retain information-rich global spatial anchors. Stage 2 (in the LLM): text-guided pruning to retain task-relevant visual tokens.
AI.result: Empirical results report state-of-the-art performance on multiple VQA benchmarks (approximately 94–95%) and substantial improvements on visual grounding tasks (about 7% to 47%).
AI.conclusion: Nüwa demonstrates that preserving spatial cues during token pruning is crucial for VG tasks while enabling efficient VLM operation, via a principled two-stage framework that balances global spatial awareness with task relevance.

title: TRACE: Temporal Radiology with Anatomical Change Explanation for Grounded X-ray Report Generation
summary: Temporal comparison of chest X-rays is fundamental to clinical radiology, enabling detection of disease progression, treatment response, and new findings. While vision-language models have advanced single-image report generation and visual grounding, no existing method combines these capabilities for temporal change detection. We introduce Temporal Radiology with Anatomical Change Explanation (TRACE), the first model that jointly performs temporal comparison, change classification, and spatial localization. Given a prior and current chest X-ray, TRACE generates natural language descriptions of interval changes (worsened, improved, stable) while grounding each finding with bounding box coordinates. TRACE demonstrates effective spatial localization with over 90% grounding accuracy, establishing a foundation for this challenging new task. Our ablation study uncovers an emergent capability: change detection arises only when temporal comparison and spatial grounding are jointly learned, as neither alone enables meaningful change detection. This finding suggests that grounding provides a spatial attention mechanism essential for temporal reasoning.

AI.tldr: First model TRACE jointly performs temporal chest X-ray comparison with localization, enabling interval-change descriptions and bounding-box grounding; emergent property: temporal change detection arises only with joint learning of temporal comparison and grounding.
AI.motivation: To enable reliable temporal radiology reasoning by detecting interval changes and explaining them with spatial localization, addressing the gap where existing methods handle single-image reports or grounding but not temporal changes.
AI.method: Introduce TRACE, a vision-language model that takes prior and current chest X-rays, performs temporal comparison, classifies interval change (worsened, improved, stable), and grounds findings with bounding boxes. The model integrates spatial grounding with temporal reasoning. Evaluation reports over 90% grounding accuracy and an ablation showing that change detection requires joint learning of temporal comparison and grounding.
AI.result: TRACE achieves strong spatial grounding accuracy (>90%), generates natural-language interval-change descriptions, and reveals an emergent property: change detection materializes only when temporal comparison and spatial grounding are learned jointly, indicating grounding provides a spatial attention mechanism for temporal reasoning.
AI.conclusion: Joint learning of temporal comparison and grounding is essential for effective temporal change detection in radiology; TRACE lays the groundwork for temporally aware, explainable radiology analysis and can enhance automated report generation and localization of interval changes.

title: PromptRL: Prompt Matters in RL for Flow-Based Image Generation
summary:
Flow matching models (FMs) have revolutionized text-to-image (T2I) generation, with reinforcement learning (RL) serving as a critical post-training strategy for alignment with reward objectives. In this research, we show that current RL pipelines for FMs suffer from two underappreciated yet important limitations: sample inefficiency due to insufficient generation diversity, and pronounced prompt overfitting, where models memorize specific training formulations and exhibit dramatic performance collapse when evaluated on semantically equivalent but stylistically varied prompts. We present PromptRL (Prompt Matters in RL for Flow-Based Image Generation), a framework that incorporates language models (LMs) as trainable prompt refinement agents directly within the flow-based RL optimization loop. This design yields two complementary benefits: rapid development of sophisticated prompt rewriting capabilities and, critically, a synergistic training regime that reshapes the optimization dynamics. PromptRL achieves state-of-the-art performance across multiple benchmarks, obtaining scores of 0.97 on GenEval, 0.98 on OCR accuracy, and 24.05 on PickScore.
  Furthermore, we validate the effectiveness of our RL approach on large-scale image editing models, improving the EditReward of FLUX.1-Kontext from 1.19 to 1.43 with only 0.06 million rollouts, surpassing Gemini 2.5 Flash Image (also known as Nano Banana), which scores 1.37, and achieving comparable performance with ReasonNet (1.44), which relied on fine-grained data annotations along with a complex multi-stage training. Our extensive experiments empirically demonstrate that PromptRL consistently achieves higher performance ceilings while requiring over 2$\times$ fewer rollouts compared to naive flow-only RL. Our code is available at https://github.com/G-U-N/UniRL.

AI.tldr: PromptRL integrates trainable LM-based prompt refinement into flow-based RL for text-to-image generation, addressing sample inefficiency and prompt overfitting. It achieves state-of-the-art results on multiple benchmarks and reduces required rollouts, with code released.
AI.motivation: RL pipelines for flow-based text-to-image generation exhibit sample inefficiency due to limited generation diversity and strong prompt overfitting, leading to performance collapse under semantically equivalent prompts. A method that improves alignment with reward objectives while being data-efficient is needed.
AI.method: Incorporate language models as trainable prompt refinement agents directly within the flow-based RL optimization loop. This enables rapid development of prompt rewriting and reshapes optimization dynamics through synergistic training between prompts and RL objectives. Evaluate on GenEval, OCR accuracy, and PickScore; also apply to large-scale image editing with EditReward, comparing to Gemini 2.5 Flash Image and ReasonNet. Code released.
AI.result: PromptRL achieves GenEval 0.97, OCR accuracy 0.98, and PickScore 24.05. In image editing, EditReward improves from 1.19 to 1.43 with only 0.06M rollouts, surpassing Gemini 2.5 (1.37) and rivaling ReasonNet (1.44) which uses more complex training. Overall, PromptRL yields higher performance ceilings with >2x fewer rollouts than flow-only RL; code at provided URL.
AI.conclusion: PromptRL demonstrates that integrating trainable LM prompts into flow-based RL substantially improves data efficiency and generalization, achieving SOTA results across text-to-image generation and editing benchmarks and reducing reliance on extensive labeled data.

title: Dynamic High-frequency Convolution for Infrared Small Target Detection
summary: Infrared small targets are typically tiny and locally salient, which belong to high-frequency components (HFCs) in images. Single-frame infrared small target (SIRST) detection is challenging, since there are many HFCs along with targets, such as bright corners, broken clouds, and other clutters. Current learning-based methods rely on the powerful capabilities of deep networks, but neglect explicit modeling and discriminative representation learning of various HFCs, which is important to distinguish targets from other HFCs. To address the aforementioned issues, we propose a dynamic high-frequency convolution (DHiF) to translate the discriminative modeling process into the generation of a dynamic local filter bank. Especially, DHiF is sensitive to HFCs, owing to the dynamic parameters of its generated filters being symmetrically adjusted within a zero-centered range according to Fourier transformation properties. Combining with standard convolution operations, DHiF can adaptively and dynamically process different HFC regions and capture their distinctive grayscale variation characteristics for discriminative representation learning. DHiF functions as a drop-in replacement for standard convolution and can be used in arbitrary SIRST detection networks without significant decrease in computational efficiency. To validate the effectiveness of our DHiF, we conducted extensive experiments across different SIRST detection networks on real-scene datasets. Compared to other state-of-the-art convolution operations, DHiF exhibits superior detection performance with promising improvement. Codes are available at https://github.com/TinaLRJ/DHiF.

AI.tldr: Introduces Dynamic High-Frequency Convolution (DHiF) to better discriminate infrared small targets by learning a dynamic local filter bank that targets high-frequency components (HFCs), improving SIRST detection with a drop-in convolution replacement.
AI.motivation: SIRST is challenged by numerous HFCs (bright corners, clouds, clutter) that resemble targets; existing deep models neglect explicit modeling/discriminative representation of HFCs, limiting detection performance.
AI.method: Proposes DHiF, a dynamic local filter bank generated by Fourier-informed parameters that are symmetrically adjusted within a zero-centered range. DHiF can be integrated with standard convolutions as a drop-in replacement, enabling adaptive processing of different HFC regions to capture distinctive grayscale variations. Implementation is network-agnostic and code is released.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on real-scene datasets across various SIRST networks show that DHiF yields superior detection performance compared with existing convolution operations, with promising efficiency. The method maintains computational efficiency while enhancing discrimination of HFCs.
AI.conclusion: DHiF effectively models and exploits HFCs for SIRST detection, providing a flexible, efficient enhancement that can be plugged into existing networks to improve robustness against cluttered high-frequency structures.

title: Where to Attend: A Principled Vision-Centric Position Encoding with Parabolas
summary: We propose Parabolic Position Encoding (PaPE), a parabola-based position encoding for vision modalities in attention-based architectures. Given a set of vision tokens-such as images, point clouds, videos, or event camera streams-our objective is to encode their positions while accounting for the characteristics of vision modalities. Prior works have largely extended position encodings from 1D-sequences in language to nD-structures in vision, but only with partial account of vision characteristics. We address this gap by designing PaPE from principles distilled from prior work: translation invariance, rotation invariance (PaPE-RI), distance decay, directionality, and context awareness. We evaluate PaPE on 8 datasets that span 4 modalities. We find that either PaPE or PaPE-RI achieves the top performance on 7 out of 8 datasets. Extrapolation experiments on ImageNet-1K show that PaPE extrapolates remarkably well, improving in absolute terms by up to 10.5% over the next-best position encoding. Code is available at https://github.com/DTU-PAS/parabolic-position-encoding.

AI.tldr: Parabolic Position Encoding (PaPE) introduces a parabola-based, invariant position encoding for vision tokens, addressing translation/rotation invariance, distance decay, directionality, and context awareness to improve attention; it achieves strong cross-modal performance and notable extrapolation, with public code.
AI.motivation: Vision data are multi-dimensional and non-sequential, requiring position encodings tailored to vision characteristics. Prior practice largely extends 1D encodings to higher dimensions but often neglects invariances and context-aware behavior. PaPE aims to explicitly encode translation invariance, rotation invariance (PaPE-RI), distance decay, directionality, and context awareness to improve generalization across modalities.
AI.method: Introduce PaPE, a parabola-based position encoding scheme for attention in vision models, with a variant PaPE-RI for rotation invariance. Applicable to diverse vision tokens (images, point clouds, videos, event streams). Encodes spatial (and possibly temporal) relationships via a parabola-shaped function to capture distance decay and directionality, integrated into the attention mechanism.
AI.result: Evaluated on 8 datasets spanning 4 modalities; PaPE or PaPE-RI achieves top performance on 7 of 8 datasets. Extrapolation experiments on ImageNet-1K show up to 10.5% absolute improvement over the next-best position encoding. Code is released at the cited GitHub repository.
AI.conclusion: PaPE provides a versatile, generalizable position encoding for vision transformers, delivering strong cross-modal performance and robust extrapolation. PaPE-RI extends rotation invariance. Further work could analyze ablations of invariance components, computational costs, and applicability to additional modalities and tasks.

title: Fisheye Stereo Vision: Depth and Range Error
summary: This study derives analytical expressions for the depth and range error of fisheye stereo vision systems as a function of object distance, specifically accounting for accuracy at large angles.

AI.tldr: Analytical depth and range error expressions for fisheye stereo as a function of distance and angle, addressing large-angle accuracy.
AI.motivation: Fisheye stereo cameras enable wide field of view but introduce non-linear distortion that causes angle-dependent depth errors; a closed-form error model is needed for design, calibration, and fusion tasks involving wide-angle depth estimation.
AI.method: Derive closed-form error propagation formulas for depth and range in a fisheye stereo geometry using a standard fisheye projection model and known stereo parameters (baseline, focal length, distortion characteristics) and image measurement noise; analyze dependence on object distance and viewing angle, and compare with or discuss limitations relative to pinhole models.
AI.result: Analytical expressions are obtained for depth error δZ and range error as functions of object distance Z and angle θ, with explicit terms showing how accuracy degrades at large viewing angles; the expressions enable prediction of depth uncertainty across the FOV.
AI.conclusion: The derived expressions provide a practical tool for predicting and mitigating depth unreliability in fisheye stereo systems at wide angles, informing calibration, sensor fusion, and application-specific depth requirements.
