Specifically, iCCDM introduces a novel matrix-form EDM formulation together with an adaptive vicinal training strategy. Extensive experiments on four benchmark datasets, spanning image resolutions from $64\times64$ to $256\times256$, demonstrate that iCCDM consistently outperforms existing methods, including state-of-the-art large-scale text-to-image diffusion models (e.g., Stable Diffusion 3, FLUX.1, and Qwen-Image), achieving higher generation quality while significantly reducing sampling cost.

AI.tldr: iCCDM strengthens Conditional Diffusion Models by adopting the Elucidated Diffusion Model (EDM) with a matrix-form formulation and adaptive vicinal training, yielding higher-quality image generation at lower sampling cost across 64×64 to 256×256 resolutions, outperforming prior diffusion methods and large-scale T2I models.
AI.motivation: Address CCDM's outdated diffusion framework and poor sampling efficiency, and counter competition from GAN-based CcGAN-AVAR by exploiting EDM's improvements and a more efficient training paradigm.
AI.method: Integrate a matrix-form EDM formulation into CCDM and apply adaptive vicinal training. Evaluate on four datasets from 64×64 to 256×256, comparing against state-of-the-art diffusion models (e.g., Stable Diffusion 3, FLUX.1, Qwen-Image).
AI.result: iCCDM consistently surpasses existing methods in generation quality while notably reducing sampling cost, matching or exceeding the performance of large-scale text-to-image diffusion models.
AI.conclusion: The proposed iCCDM demonstrates superior generation quality and sampling efficiency by leveraging EDM and adaptive vicinal training, enabling scalable high-quality conditional image synthesis across resolutions and datasets.

title: VIRAL: Visual In-Context Reasoning via Analogy in Diffusion Transformers
summary: Replicating In-Context Learning (ICL) in computer vision remains challenging due to task heterogeneity. We propose \textbf{VIRAL}, a framework that elicits visual reasoning from a pre-trained image editing model by formulating ICL as conditional generation via visual analogy ($x_s : x_t :: x_q : y_q$). We adapt a frozen Diffusion Transformer (DiT) using role-aware multi-image conditioning and introduce a Mixture-of-Experts LoRA to mitigate gradient interference across diverse tasks. Additionally, to bridge the gaps in current visual context datasets, we curate a large-scale dataset spanning perception, restoration, and editing. Experiments demonstrate that VIRAL outperforms existing methods, validating that a unified V-ICL paradigm can handle the majority of visual tasks, including open-domain editing. Our code is available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/VIRAL-744A

AI.tldr: A unified V-ICL framework (VIRAL) for visual in-context learning that uses visual analogy and a frozen diffusion transformer, with role-aware conditioning and Mixture-of-Experts LoRA; plus a large-scale dataset; shows superior performance including open-domain editing.
AI.motivation: Overcome task heterogeneity in CV ICL and lack of unified V-ICL approaches; bridge gaps in visual-context datasets.
AI.method: Formulate ICL as conditional generation via visual analogy; adapt frozen DiT with role-aware multi-image conditioning; introduce Mixture-of-Experts LoRA to mitigate gradient interference; curate large-scale perception/restoration/editing dataset.
AI.result: VIRAL outperforms existing methods across tasks; demonstrates viability of a unified V-ICL paradigm for diverse visual tasks including open-domain editing.
AI.conclusion: VIRAL provides a practical path toward unified V-ICL; the approach generalizes across CV tasks and emphasizes dataset expansion; code released.

title: ConsisDrive: Identity-Preserving Driving World Models for Video Generation by Instance Mask
summary: Autonomous driving relies on robust models trained on large-scale, high-quality multi-view driving videos. Although world models provide a cost-effective solution for generating realistic driving data, they often suffer from identity drift, where the same object changes its appearance or category across frames due to the absence of instance-level temporal constraints. We introduce ConsisDrive, an identity-preserving driving world model designed to enforce temporal consistency at the instance level. Our framework incorporates two key components: (1) Instance-Masked Attention, which applies instance identity masks and trajectory masks within attention blocks to ensure that visual tokens interact only with their corresponding instance features across spatial and temporal dimensions, thereby preserving object identity consistency; and (2) Instance-Masked Loss, which adaptively emphasizes foreground regions with probabilistic instance masking, reducing background noise while maintaining overall scene fidelity. By integrating these mechanisms, ConsisDrive achieves state-of-the-art driving video generation quality and demonstrates significant improvements in downstream autonomous driving tasks on the nuScenes dataset. Our project page is https://shanpoyang654.github.io/ConsisDrive/page.html.

AI.tldr: ConsisDrive introduces an identity-preserving driving world model with Instance-Masked Attention and Instance-Masked Loss to maintain object identity across frames, achieving state-of-the-art driving video generation and improved downstream tasks on nuScenes.
AI.motivation: World models for driving data often suffer from identity drift due to lack of instance-level temporal constraints, causing the same object to change appearance or category across frames.
AI.method: Two components: (1) Instance-Masked Attention that applies instance identity masks and trajectory masks within attention blocks so visual tokens interact only with the corresponding instance features across spatial and temporal dimensions; (2) Instance-Masked Loss that adaptively emphasizes foreground regions with probabilistic instance masking to reduce background noise while preserving scene fidelity.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art driving video generation quality and yields significant improvements on downstream autonomous driving tasks on the nuScenes dataset; project page provided.
AI.conclusion: Integrating instance-level masking in attention and loss produces a robust, identity-preserving driving world model that enhances both synthetic video quality and downstream driving performance.

title: FARTrack: Fast Autoregressive Visual Tracking with High Performance
summary: Inference speed and tracking performance are two critical evaluation metrics in the field of visual tracking. However, high-performance trackers often suffer from slow processing speeds, making them impractical for deployment on resource-constrained devices. To alleviate this issue, we propose FARTrack, a Fast Auto-Regressive Tracking framework. Since autoregression emphasizes the temporal nature of the trajectory sequence, it can maintain high performance while achieving efficient execution across various devices. FARTrack introduces Task-Specific Self-Distillation and Inter-frame Autoregressive Sparsification, designed from the perspectives of shallow-yet-accurate distillation and redundant-to-essential token optimization, respectively. Task-Specific Self-Distillation achieves model compression by distilling task-specific tokens layer by layer, enhancing the model's inference speed while avoiding suboptimal manual teacher-student layer pairs assignments. Meanwhile, Inter-frame Autoregressive Sparsification sequentially condenses multiple templates, avoiding additional runtime overhead while learning a temporally-global optimal sparsification strategy. FARTrack demonstrates outstanding speed and competitive performance. It delivers an AO of 70.6% on GOT-10k in real-time. Beyond, our fastest model achieves a speed of 343 FPS on the GPU and 121 FPS on the CPU.

AI.tldr: FARTrack presents a Fast Auto-Regressive Tracking framework that uses Task-Specific Self-Distillation and Inter-frame Autoregressive Sparsification to achieve real-time speed with competitive accuracy on GOT-10k, including 343 FPS on GPU and 121 FPS on CPU.
AI.motivation: The persistent speed–accuracy trade-off in visual tracking; high-performance trackers are often too slow for resource-limited devices, necessitating efficient architectures that preserve accuracy.
AI.method: Task-Specific Self-Distillation distills task-specific tokens layer-by-layer to compress the model without fixed teacher–student layer mappings. Inter-frame Autoregressive Sparsification sequentially condenses multiple templates, reducing runtime overhead while learning a temporally global sparsification strategy.
AI.result: Achieves an AO of 70.6% on GOT-10k in real-time, with the fastest variant reaching 343 FPS on GPU and 121 FPS on CPU, demonstrating strong speed with competitive performance.
AI.conclusion: FARTrack effectively leverages autoregressive temporal modeling and token-level compression to deliver real-time visual tracking on diverse devices, indicating a favorable speed–accuracy balance and practical deployment potential.

title: PokeFusion Attention: Enhancing Reference-Free Style-Conditioned Generation
comment: 7 pages, 5 figures. Under review at IJCNN 2026
summary: This paper studies reference-free style-conditioned character generation in text-to-image diffusion models, where high-quality synthesis requires both stable character structure and consistent, fine-grained style expression across diverse prompts. Existing approaches primarily rely on text-only prompting, which is often under-specified for visual style and tends to produce noticeable style drift and geometric inconsistency, or introduce reference-based adapters that depend on external images at inference time, increasing architectural complexity and limiting deployment flexibility.We propose PokeFusion Attention, a lightweight decoder-level cross-attention mechanism that fuses textual semantics with learned style embeddings directly inside the diffusion decoder. By decoupling text and style conditioning at the attention level, our method enables effective reference-free stylized generation while keeping the pretrained diffusion backbone fully frozen.PokeFusion Attention trains only decoder cross-attention layers together with a compact style projection module, resulting in a parameter-efficient and plug-and-play control component that can be easily integrated into existing diffusion pipelines and transferred across different backbones.Experiments on a stylized character generation benchmark (Pokemon-style) demonstrate that our method consistently improves style fidelity, semantic alignment, and character shape consistency compared with representative adapter-based baselines, while maintaining low parameter overhead and inference-time simplicity.

AI.tldr: Introduces PokeFusion Attention, a lightweight decoder-level cross-attention module that fuses text semantics with learned style embeddings for reference-free stylized diffusion generation, while keeping the backbone frozen and parameter-efficient.
AI.motivation: To overcome style drift and geometric inconsistency in text-prompted, style-conditioned diffusion, and to avoid heavy reference adapters that require external images and increase architectural complexity.
AI.method: Proposes PokeFusion Attention: a decoder cross-attention mechanism that decouples text and style conditioning by fusing textual semantics with style embeddings inside the diffusion decoder. Train only decoder cross-attention layers plus a compact style projection module; keep pretrained backbone frozen; plug-and-play with diffusion pipelines and transferable across backbones.
AI.result: On a Pokemon-style stylized character generation benchmark, the method improves style fidelity, semantic alignment, and character shape consistency relative to adapter-based baselines, with low parameter overhead and simple inference.
AI.conclusion: Decoder-level fusion of text and learned style enables effective, reference-free stylization in diffusion models, offering a lightweight, transferable, and deployable control component for stylized generation.

title: Spiral RoPE: Rotate Your Rotary Positional Embeddings in the 2D Plane
summary: Rotary Position Embedding (RoPE) is the de facto positional encoding in large language models due to its ability to encode relative positions and support length extrapolation. When adapted to vision transformers, the standard axial formulation decomposes two-dimensional spatial positions into horizontal and vertical components, implicitly restricting positional encoding to axis-aligned directions. We identify this directional constraint as a fundamental limitation of the standard axial 2D RoPE, which hinders the modeling of oblique spatial relationships that naturally exist in natural images. To overcome this limitation, we propose Spiral RoPE, a simple yet effective extension that enables multi-directional positional encoding by partitioning embedding channels into multiple groups associated with uniformly distributed directions. Each group is rotated according to the projection of the patch position onto its corresponding direction, allowing spatial relationships to be encoded beyond the horizontal and vertical axes. Across a wide range of vision tasks including classification, segmentation, and generation, Spiral RoPE consistently improves performance. Qualitative analysis of attention maps further show that Spiral RoPE exhibits more concentrated activations on semantically relevant objects and better respects local object boundaries, highlighting the importance of multi-directional positional encoding in vision transformers.

AI.tldr: Spiral RoPE extends 2D RoPE by dividing embedding channels into multiple directional groups and rotating each by the projection of a patch's position onto that direction, enabling multi-directional positional encoding beyond horizontal/vertical. This yields performance gains across vision tasks and sharper, more object-aligned attention.
AI.motivation: Standard axial 2D RoPE constrains positional encodings to axis-aligned directions, hindering the modeling of oblique spatial relationships common in natural images. A multi-directional encoding aims to capture oblique and curved spatial relations to improve vision transformer performance.
AI.method: Partition embedding channels into several groups, each linked to a uniformly distributed direction. For a patch position, project onto each direction and rotate the corresponding group accordingly, combining the results to form the final positional encoding (Spiral RoPE). Integrate into vision transformers and evaluate on classification, segmentation, and generation; include qualitative attention-map analyses.
AI.result: Consistent performance improvements across classification, segmentation, and generation tasks. Attention maps show more concentrated activations on semantically relevant objects and clearer adherence to object boundaries, indicating better modeling of multi-directional spatial relations.
AI.conclusion: Spiral RoPE demonstrates the value of multi-directional positional encoding for vision transformers, surpassing axis-aligned axial RoPE in capturing oblique spatial relationships and improving both quantitative metrics and qualitative interpretability; motivates further exploration of direction-aware encodings and their implications for model design and scaling.

title: EventFlash: Towards Efficient MLLMs for Event-Based Vision
summary: Event-based multimodal large language models (MLLMs) enable robust perception in high-speed and low-light scenarios, addressing key limitations of frame-based MLLMs. However, current event-based MLLMs often rely on dense image-like processing paradigms, overlooking the spatiotemporal sparsity of event streams and resulting in high computational cost. In this paper, we propose EventFlash, a novel and efficient MLLM to explore spatiotemporal token sparsification for reducing data redundancy and accelerating inference. Technically, we build EventMind, a large-scale and scene-diverse dataset with over 500k instruction sets, providing both short and long event stream sequences to support our curriculum training strategy. We then present an adaptive temporal window aggregation module for efficient temporal sampling, which adaptively compresses temporal tokens while retaining key temporal cues. Finally, a sparse density-guided attention module is designed to improve spatial token efficiency by selecting informative regions and suppressing empty or sparse areas. Experimental results show that EventFlash achieves a $12.4\times$ throughput improvement over the baseline (EventFlash-Zero) while maintaining comparable performance. It supports long-range event stream processing with up to 1,000 bins, significantly outperforming the 5-bin limit of EventGPT. We believe EventFlash serves as an efficient foundation model for event-based vision.

AI.tldr: EventFlash introduces spatiotemporal token sparsification for event-based MLLMs, combining an adaptive temporal window to compress temporal tokens and a sparse density-guided attention to focus on informative spatial regions. It is trained on EventMind, a large 500k+ instruction dataset, and achieves high throughput (12.4x) with comparable accuracy, supporting long-range event streams (up to 1000 bins) and outperforming the prior 5-bin limit of EventGPT.
AI.motivation: To overcome the computational inefficiency of dense image-like processing in event-based MLLMs by exploiting the inherent spatiotemporal sparsity of event streams, enabling efficient long-range processing in high-speed and low-light scenarios.
AI.method: 1) Build EventMind, a large-scale, diverse dataset with short and long event streams for curriculum training. 2) Propose adaptive temporal window aggregation to compress temporal tokens while preserving key temporal cues. 3) Introduce sparse density-guided attention to select informative spatial regions and suppress empty/sparse areas, reducing spatial token load.
AI.result: Significant throughput gains: ~12.4x improvement over EventFlash-Zero baseline, with comparable performance. Demonstrates support for long-range event streams up to 1000 bins, exceeding the 5-bin limit of prior approaches like EventGPT.
AI.conclusion: EventFlash provides an efficient, scalable foundation model for event-based vision by combining temporal sparsification and density-guided spatial attention, enabling robust perception in high-speed/low-light conditions while reducing computational cost.

title: InstaDrive: Instance-Aware Driving World Models for Realistic and Consistent Video Generation
summary: Autonomous driving relies on robust models trained on high-quality, large-scale multi-view driving videos. While world models offer a cost-effective solution for generating realistic driving videos, they struggle to maintain instance-level temporal consistency and spatial geometric fidelity. To address these challenges, we propose InstaDrive, a novel framework that enhances driving video realism through two key advancements: (1) Instance Flow Guider, which extracts and propagates instance features across frames to enforce temporal consistency, preserving instance identity over time. (2) Spatial Geometric Aligner, which improves spatial reasoning, ensures precise instance positioning, and explicitly models occlusion hierarchies. By incorporating these instance-aware mechanisms, InstaDrive achieves state-of-the-art video generation quality and enhances downstream autonomous driving tasks on the nuScenes dataset. Additionally, we utilize CARLA's autopilot to procedurally and stochastically simulate rare but safety-critical driving scenarios across diverse maps and regions, enabling rigorous safety evaluation for autonomous systems. Our project page is https://shanpoyang654.github.io/InstaDrive/page.html.

AI.tldr: InstaDrive introduces two modules—Instance Flow Guider and Spatial Geometric Aligner—to enforce instance-level temporal consistency and accurate spatial geometry in world-model-based driving video generation, achieving state-of-the-art realism and improved downstream autonomous driving performance on nuScenes; it also uses CARLA autopilot to generate diverse rare safety scenarios for rigorous evaluation (project page provided).
AI.motivation: Address the limitations of world-model video synthesis for autonomous driving: poor temporal consistency of instance identities across frames and weak spatial geometric fidelity, which hurt downstream tasks and safety assessments.
AI.method: Two key components: (1) Instance Flow Guider that extracts and propagates instance features across frames to maintain consistent identity over time, and (2) Spatial Geometric Aligner that enhances spatial reasoning, precise instance positioning, and explicit occlusion hierarchy modeling. Evaluation on nuScenes and leveraging CARLA autopilot to procedurally generate rare, safety-critical scenarios across maps region.”
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art driving video generation quality and improves downstream autonomous driving tasks on nuScenes; enables rigorous safety evaluation via CARLA-based scenario generation.
AI.conclusion: InstaDrive advances realistic, instance-aware driving video synthesis and safety analysis for autonomous systems, highlighting the benefit of explicit instance-level temporal and spatial modeling. Project page: https://shanpoyang654.github.io/InstaDrive/page.html

title: LaVPR: Benchmarking Language and Vision for Place Recognition
summary: Visual Place Recognition (VPR) often fails under extreme environmental changes and perceptual aliasing. Furthermore, standard systems cannot perform "blind" localization from verbal descriptions alone, a capability needed for applications such as emergency response. To address these challenges, we introduce LaVPR, a large-scale benchmark that extends existing VPR datasets with over 650,000 rich natural-language descriptions. Using LaVPR, we investigate two paradigms: Multi-Modal Fusion for enhanced robustness and Cross-Modal Retrieval for language-based localization. Our results show that language descriptions yield consistent gains in visually degraded conditions, with the most significant impact on smaller backbones. Notably, adding language allows compact models to rival the performance of much larger vision-only architectures. For cross-modal retrieval, we establish a baseline using Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) and Multi-Similarity loss, which substantially outperforms standard contrastive methods across vision-language models. Ultimately, LaVPR enables a new class of localization systems that are both resilient to real-world stochasticity and practical for resource-constrained deployment. Our dataset and code are available at https://github.com/oferidan1/LaVPR.

AI.tldr: LaVPR introduces a large-scale, language-annotated VPR benchmark enabling robust cross-modal localization; language descriptions improve robustness, especially for compact models, and a LoRA+Multi-Similarity baseline boosts cross-modal retrieval, suggesting language can compensate for vision-only limits and enable efficient models.
AI.motivation: Address visual place recognition under extreme environmental changes and perceptual aliasing; enable blind localization from natural-language descriptions for emergency response and other practical scenarios; create a dataset and benchmarks that fuse vision and language.
AI.method: Develop LaVPR, a large-scale VPR benchmark with over 650,000 natural-language descriptions. Investigate two paradigms—Multi-Modal Fusion for robustness and Cross-Modal Retrieval for language-based localization. Establish a baseline for cross-modal retrieval using Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) and Multi-Similarity loss, and evaluate across vision-language models to show gains, especially for compact backbones.
AI.result: Language descriptions consistently improve VPR performance in visually degraded conditions, with the largest gains for smaller backbones. Language enables compact models to approach or rival larger vision-only architectures. For cross-modal retrieval, the LoRA + Multi-Similarity baseline substantially outperforms standard contrastive methods across models.
AI.conclusion: LaVPR enables resilient, language-augmented localization suited for resource-constrained deployment. The dataset and code are publicly available, fostering further research in robust cross-modal localization and real-world applications.

title: HypCBC: Domain-Invariant Hyperbolic Cross-Branch Consistency for Generalizable Medical Image Analysis
comment: Accepted to Transactions on Machine Learning Research (TMLR)
summary: Robust generalization beyond training distributions remains a critical challenge for deep neural networks. This is especially pronounced in medical image analysis, where data is often scarce and covariate shifts arise from different hardware devices, imaging protocols, and heterogeneous patient populations. These factors collectively hinder reliable performance and slow down clinical adoption. Despite recent progress, existing learning paradigms primarily rely on the Euclidean manifold, whose flat geometry fails to capture the complex, hierarchical structures present in clinical data. In this work, we exploit the advantages of hyperbolic manifolds to model complex data characteristics. We present the first comprehensive validation of hyperbolic representation learning for medical image analysis and demonstrate statistically significant gains across eleven in-distribution datasets and three ViT models. We further propose an unsupervised, domain-invariant hyperbolic cross-branch consistency constraint. Extensive experiments confirm that our proposed method promotes domain-invariant features and outperforms state-of-the-art Euclidean methods by an average of $+2.1\%$ AUC on three domain generalization benchmarks: Fitzpatrick17k, Camelyon17-WILDS, and a cross-dataset setup for retinal imaging. These datasets span different imaging modalities, data sizes, and label granularities, confirming generalization capabilities across substantially different conditions. The code is available at https://github.com/francescodisalvo05/hyperbolic-cross-branch-consistency .

AI.tldr: Hyperbolic representation learning is validated for medical image analysis, achieving improved domain generalization across multiple datasets/models via a novel unsupervised hyperbolic cross-branch consistency constraint.
AI.motivation: Medical imaging data suffer from distribution shifts due to hardware, imaging protocols, and patient heterogeneity; Euclidean embedding space fails to capture hierarchical and complex data structure, hindering robust generalization and clinical deployment.
AI.method: The study validates hyperbolic representations in medical imaging, including 11 in-distribution datasets and 3 Vision Transformer models, and introduces an unsupervised, domain-invariant hyperbolic cross-branch consistency constraint to promote domain-invariant features.
AI.result: Significant performance gains over Euclidean methods, averaging +2.1% AUC across three domain generalization benchmarks (Fitzpatrick17k, Camelyon17-WILDS, retinal cross-dataset), indicating better generalization across modalities and data conditions.
AI.conclusion: Hyperbolic geometry effectively captures hierarchical clinical data structure and yields improved domain generalization; the proposed cross-branch consistency constraint further enforces domain-invariant representations; code is released for reproducibility.

title: NAB: Neural Adaptive Binning for Sparse-View CT reconstruction
summary: Computed Tomography (CT) plays a vital role in inspecting the internal structures of industrial objects. Furthermore, achieving high-quality CT reconstruction from sparse views is essential for reducing production costs. While classic implicit neural networks have shown promising results for sparse reconstruction, they are unable to leverage shape priors of objects. Motivated by the observation that numerous industrial objects exhibit rectangular structures, we propose a novel \textbf{N}eural \textbf{A}daptive \textbf{B}inning (\textbf{NAB}) method that effectively integrates rectangular priors into the reconstruction process. Specifically, our approach first maps coordinate space into a binned vector space. This mapping relies on an innovative binning mechanism based on differences between shifted hyperbolic tangent functions, with our extension enabling rotations around the input-plane normal vector. The resulting representations are then processed by a neural network to predict CT attenuation coefficients. This design enables end-to-end optimization of the encoding parameters -- including position, size, steepness, and rotation -- via gradient flow from the projection data, thus enhancing reconstruction accuracy. By adjusting the smoothness of the binning function, NAB can generalize to objects with more complex geometries. This research provides a new perspective on integrating shape priors into neural network-based reconstruction. Extensive experiments demonstrate that NAB achieves superior performance on two industrial datasets. It also maintains robust on medical datasets when the binning function is extended to more general expression. The code will be made available.

AI.tldr: Neural Adaptive Binning (NAB) integrates rectangular priors into sparse-view CT reconstruction via a differentiable, rotation-capable binning encoding that is end-to-end optimizable, yielding superior industrial results and generalizing to medical data.
AI.motivation: Reduce production costs and improve sparse-view CT by leveraging prevalent rectangular geometry in industrial objects; incorporate shape priors into neural implicit reconstructions to enhance accuracy.
AI.method: Coordinate space is mapped into a binned vector space using a binning mechanism based on differences between shifted hyperbolic tangent functions, extended to rotate around the input-plane normal. A neural network processes these representations to predict CT attenuation coefficients. Encoding parameters (position, size, steepness, rotation) are optimized end-to-end via gradient flow from projection data. The binning smoothness controls geometric complexity, enabling generalization to more complex shapes.
AI.result: The NAB framework achieves superior reconstruction performance on two industrial datasets and remains robust on medical datasets when the binning is extended to more general expressions; code will be released.
AI.conclusion: NAB provides a new mechanism to inject shape priors into neural network-based CT reconstruction, improving sparse-view performance and offering generalization; it broadens the integration of priors in neural implicit representations.

title: Global Geometry Is Not Enough for Vision Representations
summary: A common assumption in representation learning is that globally well-distributed embeddings support robust and generalizable representations. This focus has shaped both training objectives and evaluation protocols, implicitly treating global geometry as a proxy for representational competence. While global geometry effectively encodes which elements are present, it is often insensitive to how they are composed. We investigate this limitation by testing the ability of geometric metrics to predict compositional binding across 21 vision encoders. We find that standard geometry-based statistics exhibit near-zero correlation with compositional binding. In contrast, functional sensitivity, as measured by the input-output Jacobian, reliably tracks this capability. We further provide an analytic account showing that this disparity arises from objective design, as existing losses explicitly constrain embedding geometry but leave the local input-output mapping unconstrained. These results suggest that global embedding geometry captures only a partial view of representational competence and establish functional sensitivity as a critical complementary axis for modeling composite structure.

AI.tldr: Global embedding geometry poorly predicts compositional binding, while functional sensitivity via the input-output Jacobian reliably tracks compositional capabilities across vision encoders; geometry is constrained by losses, leaving local mappings unconstrained, suggesting a need for Jacobian-based measures as a complementary axis.
AI.motivation: To challenge the reliance on global geometry as a proxy for representational competence and to identify metrics that capture compositional binding, which geometry alone fails to predict.
AI.method: Empirically evaluate geometry-based statistics versus functional sensitivity (input-output Jacobian) across 21 vision encoders to predict compositional binding; provide an analytic account linking objective design to observed disparity.
AI.result: Geometry-based statistics show near-zero correlation with compositional binding; input-output Jacobian correlates reliably with compositional binding; losses explicitly constrain embedding geometry but leave local input-output mappings unconstrained.
AI.conclusion: Global embedding geometry offers only a partial view of representational competence; functional sensitivity measured by the Jacobian is a crucial complementary axis for modeling and evaluating composite structure.

title: A3-TTA: Adaptive Anchor Alignment Test-Time Adaptation for Image Segmentation
comment: Accepted by IEEE Transactions on Image Processing
summary: Test-Time Adaptation (TTA) offers a practical solution for deploying image segmentation models under domain shift without accessing source data or retraining. Among existing TTA strategies, pseudo-label-based methods have shown promising performance. However, they often rely on perturbation-ensemble heuristics (e.g., dropout sampling, test-time augmentation, Gaussian noise), which lack distributional grounding and yield unstable training signals. This can trigger error accumulation and catastrophic forgetting during adaptation. To address this, we propose \textbf{A3-TTA}, a TTA framework that constructs reliable pseudo-labels through anchor-guided supervision. Specifically, we identify well-predicted target domain images using a class compact density metric, under the assumption that confident predictions imply distributional proximity to the source domain. These anchors serve as stable references to guide pseudo-label generation, which is further regularized via semantic consistency and boundary-aware entropy minimization. Additionally, we introduce a self-adaptive exponential moving average strategy to mitigate label noise and stabilize model update during adaptation. Evaluated on both multi-domain medical images (heart structure and prostate segmentation) and natural images, A3-TTA significantly improves average Dice scores by 10.40 to 17.68 percentage points compared to the source model, outperforming several state-of-the-art TTA methods under different segmentation model architectures. A3-TTA also excels in continual TTA, maintaining high performance across sequential target domains with strong anti-forgetting ability. The code will be made publicly available at https://github.com/HiLab-git/A3-TTA.

AI.tldr: A3-TTA proposes anchor-guided pseudo-labeling for test-time adaptation in image segmentation, using a class-compact density metric to select well-predicted target samples as anchors to supervise pseudo-labels via semantic consistency and boundary-aware entropy minimization, augmented with a self-adaptive EMA for noise-robust updates. It achieves substantial Dice gains (≈10.4–17.7 points) over the source model across medical and natural images and maintains strong continual TTA performance, outperforming SOTA methods and enabling cross-architecture robustness; code is released.
AI.motivation: Tackle instability and error accumulation in pseudo-label-based TTA caused by perturbation-ensemble heuristics, by grounding pseudo-labels in distributional proximity to the source through anchor-based supervision.
AI.method: 1) Compute a class-compact density metric to identify well-predicted target-domain images (anchors). 2) Use these anchors to generate/regularize pseudo-labels. 3) Enforce semantic consistency and boundary-aware entropy minimization on predictions. 4) Apply a self-adaptive exponential moving average to stabilize updates and mitigate label noise during adaptation.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on multi-domain medical segmentation (heart structure and prostate) and natural images shows average Dice gains of 10.40–17.68 percentage points over the source model. Outperforms several state-of-the-art TTA methods across multiple architectures and preserves high performance in continual TTA, indicating strong anti-forgetting capabilities.
AI.conclusion: Anchor-guided supervision provides stable, distribution-aligned pseudo-labels for TTA, yielding robust adaptation across domains and architectures while mitigating forgetting; the approach is practical and gains are substantial enough to favor deployment, with code available.

title: LEVIO: Lightweight Embedded Visual Inertial Odometry for Resource-Constrained Devices
comment: This article has been accepted for publication in the IEEE Sensors Journal (JSEN)
summary: Accurate, infrastructure-less sensor systems for motion tracking are essential for mobile robotics and augmented reality (AR) applications. The most popular state-of-the-art visual-inertial odometry (VIO) systems, however, are too computationally demanding for resource-constrained hardware, such as micro-drones and smart glasses. This work presents LEVIO, a fully featured VIO pipeline optimized for ultra-low-power compute platforms, allowing six-degrees-of-freedom (DoF) real-time sensing. LEVIO incorporates established VIO components such as Oriented FAST and Rotated BRIEF (ORB) feature tracking and bundle adjustment, while emphasizing a computationally efficient architecture with parallelization and low memory usage to suit embedded microcontrollers and low-power systems-on-chip (SoCs). The paper proposes and details the algorithmic design choices and the hardware-software co-optimization approach, and presents real-time performance on resource-constrained hardware. LEVIO is validated on a parallel-processing ultra-low-power RISC-V SoC, achieving 20 FPS while consuming less than 100 mW, and benchmarked against public VIO datasets, offering a compelling balance between efficiency and accuracy. To facilitate reproducibility and adoption, the complete implementation is released as open-source.

AI.tldr: LEVIO is a low-power visual-inertial odometry (VIO) pipeline optimized for ultra-low-power hardware, delivering 6-DoF real-time tracking with ORB features and bundle adjustment on resource-constrained platforms (e.g., micro-drones, smart glasses) at ~20 FPS and <100 mW, with open-source release.
AI.motivation: To enable accurate, infrastructure-less motion tracking for mobile robotics and AR on devices with strict energy, computation, and memory limits; existing VIO systems are too demanding for microcontrollers and low-power SoCs.
AI.method: Algorithmic design and hardware-software co-optimization focusing on parallelization and low memory usage; integrates established VIO components (ORB feature tracking, bundle adjustment) in a computationally efficient architecture; implemented on a parallel-processing ultra-low-power RISC-V SoC with real-time performance; open-source release to support reproducibility.
AI.result: Real-time 6-DoF VIO at ~20 FPS consuming <100 mW on a RISC-V SoC; validated on public VIO datasets; performance and efficiency balance demonstrated; implementation available as open-source.
AI.conclusion: LEVIO demonstrates that a carefully optimized VIO pipeline can run on ultra-low-power hardware with competitive accuracy, enabling deployment in energy-constrained mobile robotics and AR devices, with potential for broader adoption due to open-source availability.

title: Personalized Image Generation via Human-in-the-loop Bayesian Optimization
summary: Imagine Alice has a specific image $x^\ast$ in her mind, say, the view of the street in which she grew up during her childhood. To generate that exact image, she guides a generative model with multiple rounds of prompting and arrives at an image $x^{p*}$. Although $x^{p*}$ is reasonably close to $x^\ast$, Alice finds it difficult to close that gap using language prompts. This paper aims to narrow this gap by observing that even after language has reached its limits, humans can still tell when a new image $x^+$ is closer to $x^\ast$ than $x^{p*}$. Leveraging this observation, we develop MultiBO (Multi-Choice Preferential Bayesian Optimization) that carefully generates $K$ new images as a function of $x^{p*}$, gets preferential feedback from the user, uses the feedback to guide the diffusion model, and ultimately generates a new set of $K$ images. We show that within $B$ rounds of user feedback, it is possible to arrive much closer to $x^\ast$, even though the generative model has no information about $x^\ast$. Qualitative scores from $30$ users, combined with quantitative metrics compared across $5$ baselines, show promising results, suggesting that multi-choice feedback from humans can be effectively harnessed for personalized image generation.

AI.tldr: Proposes MultiBO, a preferential Bayesian optimization framework to refine generative images with human comparative feedback, improving closeness to a target image beyond language prompts alone.
AI.motivation: Human preference signals can surpass linguistic prompts in steering diffusion models toward a target image; enabling more personalized image generation with limited rounds of feedback.
AI.method: Iteratively generate K candidate images from the current prompt, collect preferential user feedback comparing candidates to the target prompt or to each other (x^{p*} vs new candidates), and use the feedback to update a Bayesian surrogate and guide the diffusion model to produce a new set of K images. Repeat for B rounds.
AI.result: In B rounds and with 30 users evaluating across 5 baselines, the method yields images that are substantially closer to the target x*, according to qualitative scores and quantitative metrics.
AI.conclusion: Multi-choice human feedback can be effectively harnessed to steer personalization in image generation, enabling closer alignment to a user’s mental image despite lack of explicit target information in the model.

title: Full end-to-end diagnostic workflow automation of 3D OCT via foundation model-driven AI for retinal diseases
summary: Optical coherence tomography (OCT) has revolutionized retinal disease diagnosis with its high-resolution and three-dimensional imaging nature, yet its full diagnostic automation in clinical practices remains constrained by multi-stage workflows and conventional single-slice single-task AI models. We present Full-process OCT-based Clinical Utility System (FOCUS), a foundation model-driven framework enabling end-to-end automation of 3D OCT retinal disease diagnosis. FOCUS sequentially performs image quality assessment with EfficientNetV2-S, followed by abnormality detection and multi-disease classification using a fine-tuned Vision Foundation Model. Crucially, FOCUS leverages a unified adaptive aggregation method to intelligently integrate 2D slices-level predictions into comprehensive 3D patient-level diagnosis. Trained and tested on 3,300 patients (40,672 slices), and externally validated on 1,345 patients (18,498 slices) across four different-tier centers and diverse OCT devices, FOCUS achieved high F1 scores for quality assessment (99.01%), abnormally detection (97.46%), and patient-level diagnosis (94.39%). Real-world validation across centers also showed stable performance (F1: 90.22%-95.24%). In human-machine comparisons, FOCUS matched expert performance in abnormality detection (F1: 95.47% vs 90.91%) and multi-disease diagnosis (F1: 93.49% vs 91.35%), while demonstrating better efficiency. FOCUS automates the image-to-diagnosis pipeline, representing a critical advance towards unmanned ophthalmology with a validated blueprint for autonomous screening to enhance population scale retinal care accessibility and efficiency.

AI.tldr: End-to-end 3D OCT retinal disease diagnosis via a foundation-model-driven system (FOCUS) that aggregates 2D slice predictions into 3D patient-level diagnoses, achieving high F1 scores and robust cross-center validation, outperforming expert baselines while improving efficiency.
AI.motivation: Address limitations of multi-stage workflows and single-slice AI models in OCT-based retinal diagnosis by enabling automated, end-to-end 3D diagnosis using a foundation model.
AI.method: A sequential pipeline: 1) quality assessment with EfficientNetV2-S; 2) abnormality detection and multi-disease classification using a fine-tuned Vision Foundation Model; 3) unified adaptive aggregation to integrate 2D slice predictions into 3D patient-level diagnosis. Trained on 3,300 patients (40,672 slices) and externally validated on 1,345 patients (18,498 slices) across four-tier centers and diverse OCT devices.
AI.result: High performance across tasks: quality assessment F1 99.01%; abnormality detection F1 97.46%; patient-level diagnosis F1 94.39%. Real-world validation F1 90.22%-95.24%. In human–machine comparisons, abnormality detection F1 95.47% (AI) vs 90.91% (expert) and multi-disease F1 93.49% vs 91.35%, with improved efficiency. The system demonstrated strong generalization across devices and centers.
AI.conclusion: FOCUS provides a validated blueprint for autonomous end-to-end OCT retinal diagnostics, enabling unmanned screening and scalable, accessible retinal care.

title: PQTNet: Pixel-wise Quantitative Thermography Neural Network for Estimating Defect Depth in Polylactic Acid Parts by Additive Manufacturing
summary: Defect depth quantification in additively manufactured (AM) components remains a significant challenge for non-destructive testing (NDT). This study proposes a Pixel-wise Quantitative Thermography Neural Network (PQT-Net) to address this challenge for polylactic acid (PLA) parts. A key innovation is a novel data augmentation strategy that reconstructs thermal sequence data into two-dimensional stripe images, preserving the complete temporal evolution of heat diffusion for each pixel. The PQT-Net architecture incorporates a pre-trained EfficientNetV2-S backbone and a custom Residual Regression Head (RRH) with learnable parameters to refine outputs. Comparative experiments demonstrate the superiority of PQT-Net over other deep learning models, achieving a minimum Mean Absolute Error (MAE) of 0.0094 mm and a coefficient of determination (R) exceeding 99%. The high precision of PQT-Net underscores its potential for robust quantitative defect characterization in AM.

AI.tldr: PQT-Net uses a stripe-based data augmentation to preserve full heat diffusion evolution for pixel-wise defect depth estimation in PLA AM parts, leveraging EfficientNetV2-S and a learnable Residual Regression Head. It achieves MAE 0.0094 mm and R>0.99, indicating high accuracy and strong fit.
AI.motivation: Accurate, non-destructive pixel-wise quantification of defect depths in additively manufactured components remains challenging; conventional methods struggle with temporal heat diffusion and data efficiency. The abstract proposes a DL-based solution to exploit full temporal information for precise defect characterization in PLA.
AI.method: Proposed Pixel-wise Quantitative Thermography Neural Network (PQT-Net) with a novel data augmentation that converts thermal sequence data into two-dimensional stripe images preserving temporal evolution per pixel. Uses a pre-trained EfficientNetV2-S backbone and a custom Residual Regression Head (RRH) with learnable parameters to produce defect depth maps.
AI.result: PQT-Net outperforms other DL models on the task, achieving a minimum MAE of 0.0094 mm and R > 0.99, indicating high predictive accuracy and strong correlation with ground-truth depths across the tested PLA samples.
AI.conclusion: The approach demonstrates high precision in quantitative defect characterization for AM parts, suggesting strong potential for robust NDT applications in PLA. Further validation on diverse materials and real-world datasets could bolster generalizability.

title: Invisible Clean-Label Backdoor Attacks for Generative Data Augmentation
summary: With the rapid advancement of image generative models, generative data augmentation has become an effective way to enrich training images, especially when only small-scale datasets are available. At the same time, in practical applications, generative data augmentation can be vulnerable to clean-label backdoor attacks, which aim to bypass human inspection. However, based on theoretical analysis and preliminary experiments, we observe that directly applying existing pixel-level clean-label backdoor attack methods (e.g., COMBAT) to generated images results in low attack success rates. This motivates us to move beyond pixel-level triggers and focus instead on the latent feature level. To this end, we propose InvLBA, an invisible clean-label backdoor attack method for generative data augmentation by latent perturbation. We theoretically prove that the generalization of the clean accuracy and attack success rates of InvLBA can be guaranteed. Experiments on multiple datasets show that our method improves the attack success rate by 46.43% on average, with almost no reduction in clean accuracy and high robustness against SOTA defense methods.

AI.tldr: InvLBA introduces invisible backdoors for generative data augmentation by perturbing latent codes, with theoretical generalization guarantees; achieves higher attack success rates and robustness while preserving clean accuracy.
AI.motivation: Generative data augmentation is vulnerable to clean-label backdoor attacks, but existing pixel-level attacks (e.g., COMBAT) underperform on generated images. A latent-feature trigger could bypass human inspection and defenses.
AI.method: Propose InvLBA: an invisible clean-label backdoor attack that perturbs latent representations (latent perturbation) during generation. The authors provide theoretical guarantees for the generalization of clean accuracy and attack success, and validate on multiple datasets against SOTA defenses.
AI.result: Attacks achieve a 46.43% higher attack success rate on average, with almost no loss in clean accuracy and strong robustness to state-of-the-art defenses.
AI.conclusion: Latent-space backdoors offer an effective, theory-backed, robust approach to clean-label backdoor attacks in generative augmentation, addressing limitations of pixel-space methods.

title: MedSAM-Agent: Empowering Interactive Medical Image Segmentation with Multi-turn Agentic Reinforcement Learning
summary: Medical image segmentation is evolving from task-specific models toward generalizable frameworks. Recent research leverages Multi-modal Large Language Models (MLLMs) as autonomous agents, employing reinforcement learning with verifiable reward (RLVR) to orchestrate specialized tools like the Segment Anything Model (SAM). However, these approaches often rely on single-turn, rigid interaction strategies and lack process-level supervision during training, which hinders their ability to fully exploit the dynamic potential of interactive tools and leads to redundant actions. To bridge this gap, we propose MedSAM-Agent, a framework that reformulates interactive segmentation as a multi-step autonomous decision-making process. First, we introduce a hybrid prompting strategy for expert-curated trajectory generation, enabling the model to internalize human-like decision heuristics and adaptive refinement strategies. Furthermore, we develop a two-stage training pipeline that integrates multi-turn, end-to-end outcome verification with a clinical-fidelity process reward design to promote interaction parsimony and decision efficiency. Extensive experiments across 6 medical modalities and 21 datasets demonstrate that MedSAM-Agent achieves state-of-the-art performance, effectively unifying autonomous medical reasoning with robust, iterative optimization. Code is available \href{https://github.com/CUHK-AIM-Group/MedSAM-Agent}{here}.

AI.tldr: MedSAM-Agent reframes interactive medical image segmentation as multi-turn autonomous decision-making with hybrid prompts and a two-stage training pipeline, achieving state-of-the-art results across 6 modalities/21 datasets.
AI.motivation: Current MLLM-based interactive segmentation methods rely on single-turn, rigid interactions and lack process-level supervision, hindering adaptive use of tools and leading to redundant actions. There is a need for multi-turn reasoning and clinically faithful reward design to improve efficiency and accuracy.
AI.method: Hybrid prompting strategy to generate expert-curated trajectories; a two-stage training pipeline: (1) multi-turn end-to-end outcome verification; (2) clinical-fidelity process reward design to promote interaction parsimony and decision efficiency.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance across 6 medical modalities and 21 datasets; demonstrates effective unification of autonomous medical reasoning with iterative optimization.
AI.conclusion: MedSAM-Agent provides a framework that integrates autonomous reasoning with iterative, clinically faithful optimization for interactive medical image segmentation, with available code.

title: PWAVEP: Purifying Imperceptible Adversarial Perturbations in 3D Point Clouds via Spectral Graph Wavelets
summary: Recent progress in adversarial attacks on 3D point clouds, particularly in achieving spatial imperceptibility and high attack performance, presents significant challenges for defenders. Current defensive approaches remain cumbersome, often requiring invasive model modifications, expensive training procedures or auxiliary data access. To address these threats, in this paper, we propose a plug-and-play and non-invasive defense mechanism in the spectral domain, grounded in a theoretical and empirical analysis of the relationship between imperceptible perturbations and high-frequency spectral components. Building upon these insights, we introduce a novel purification framework, termed PWAVEP, which begins by computing a spectral graph wavelet domain saliency score and local sparsity score for each point. Guided by these values, PWAVEP adopts a hierarchical strategy, it eliminates the most salient points, which are identified as hardly recoverable adversarial outliers. Simultaneously, it applies a spectral filtering process to a broader set of moderately salient points. This process leverages a graph wavelet transform to attenuate high-frequency coefficients associated with the targeted points, thereby effectively suppressing adversarial noise. Extensive evaluations demonstrate that the proposed PWAVEP achieves superior accuracy and robustness compared to existing approaches, advancing the state-of-the-art in 3D point cloud purification. Code and datasets are available at https://github.com/a772316182/pwavep

AI.tldr: PWAVEP is a plug-and-play spectral-domain defense for 3D point clouds that purifies data by removing highly salient, nearly unrecoverable adversarial outliers and attenuating high-frequency noise on a broader set of points via graph wavelet filtering, improving robustness with non-invasive purification.
AI.motivation: Defend against imperceptible, high-performing adversarial attacks on 3D point clouds without invasive model modification or heavy training, by exploiting the link between perturbations and high-frequency spectral components.
AI.method: Compute per-point spectral graph wavelet domain saliency and local sparsity scores; use a hierarchical strategy to remove the most salient (hard-to-recover) adversarial outliers; apply graph-wavelet-based spectral filtering to a wider set of moderately salient points to suppress high-frequency adversarial noise.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show PWAVEP achieves higher accuracy and robustness than existing defenses; results demonstrate effective purification with a non-invasive approach; code and datasets are publicly available.
AI.conclusion: PWAVEP advances 3D point cloud purification by providing a non-invasive, spectral-domain defense that combines targeted removal of highly salient perturbations with broad spectral attenuation, achieving state-of-the-art robustness.

title: Composable Visual Tokenizers with Generator-Free Diagnostics of Learnability
summary: We introduce CompTok, a training framework for learning visual tokenizers whose tokens are enhanced for compositionality. CompTok uses a token-conditioned diffusion decoder. By employing an InfoGAN-style objective, where we train a recognition model to predict the tokens used to condition the diffusion decoder using the decoded images, we enforce the decoder to not ignore any of the tokens. To promote compositional control, besides the original images, CompTok also trains on tokens formed by swapping token subsets between images, enabling more compositional control of the token over the decoder. As the swapped tokens between images do not have ground truth image targets, we apply a manifold constraint via an adversarial flow regularizer to keep unpaired swap generations on the natural-image distribution. The resulting tokenizer not only achieves state-of-the-art performance on image class-conditioned generation, but also demonstrates properties such as swapping tokens between images to achieve high level semantic editing of an image. Additionally, we propose two metrics that measures the landscape of the token space that can be useful to describe not only the compositionality of the tokens, but also how easy to learn the landscape is for a generator to be trained on this space. We show in experiments that CompTok can improve on both of the metrics as well as supporting state-of-the-art generators for class conditioned generation.

AI.tldr: CompTok trains a visual tokenizer with a token-conditioned diffusion decoder, using an InfoGAN-style objective and token-swapping with an adversarial flow regularizer to promote compositionality and natural-image realism; it achieves state-of-the-art class-conditioned generation and enables semantic editing via token swapping, and introduces two metrics for the token-space landscape.
AI.motivation: To enable highly compositional, controllable visual token representations for image generation, ensuring tokens meaningfully contribute to generation and can be composed by swapping, while maintaining realism when tokens are rearranged.
AI.method: Train a tokenizer with a token-conditioned diffusion decoder. Employ an InfoGAN-style objective forcing a recognition model to predict conditioning tokens from decoded images. Train on swapped token subsets between images to promote compositional control. Use an adversarial flow regularizer as a manifold constraint to keep swapped generations on the natural-image distribution. Propose two metrics to characterize the token-space landscape.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on class-conditioned image generation. Demonstrates token-level compositional control, including swapping tokens between images for high-level semantic editing. Outperforms baselines on the proposed token-space metrics and supports state-of-the-art class-conditioned generators.
AI.conclusion: CompTok produces a tokenizer with strong compositionality and controllability, enables semantic editing via token swapping, and introduces useful metrics for evaluating token-space landscape, contributing to improved class-conditioned generation and analysis of token representations.

title: Tiled Prompts: Overcoming Prompt Underspecification in Image and Video Super-Resolution
summary: Text-conditioned diffusion models have advanced image and video super-resolution by using prompts as semantic priors, but modern super-resolution pipelines typically rely on latent tiling to scale to high resolutions, where a single global caption causes prompt underspecification. A coarse global prompt often misses localized details (prompt sparsity) and provides locally irrelevant guidance (prompt misguidance) that can be amplified by classifier-free guidance. We propose Tiled Prompts, a unified framework for image and video super-resolution that generates a tile-specific prompt for each latent tile and performs super-resolution under locally text-conditioned posteriors, providing high-information guidance that resolves prompt underspecification with minimal overhead. Experiments on high resolution real-world images and videos show consistent gains in perceptual quality and text alignment, while reducing hallucinations and tile-level artifacts relative to global-prompt baselines.

AI.tldr: Proposes Tiled Prompts for diffusion-based super-resolution, generating per-tile prompts and locally conditioned posteriors to address prompt underspecification and misguidance, improving quality and reducing artifacts in image and video SR.
AI.motivation: Global captions used in diffusion-guided SR are coarse and underspecified, causing missed local details and locally irrelevant guidance; prompt sparsity and misguidance can be amplified by classifier-free guidance, especially in high-resolution tiling.
AI.method: Generate a tile-specific prompt for each latent tile and perform SR under locally text-conditioned posteriors, enabling high-information, regionally aligned guidance with minimal overhead; unifies image and video SR.
AI.result: Experiments on high-resolution real-world images and videos show consistent gains in perceptual quality and text alignment, while reducing hallucinations and tile-level artifacts compared with global-prompt baselines.
AI.conclusion: Tile-aware prompting improves perceptual quality and alignment for high-resolution SR, with reduced hallucinations and artifacts and scalable to image and video settings.

title: Z3D: Zero-Shot 3D Visual Grounding from Images
summary: 3D visual grounding (3DVG) aims to localize objects in a 3D scene based on natural language queries. In this work, we explore zero-shot 3DVG from multi-view images alone, without requiring any geometric supervision or object priors. We introduce Z3D, a universal grounding pipeline that flexibly operates on multi-view images while optionally incorporating camera poses and depth maps. We identify key bottlenecks in prior zero-shot methods causing significant performance degradation and address them with (i) a state-of-the-art zero-shot 3D instance segmentation method to generate high-quality 3D bounding box proposals and (ii) advanced reasoning via prompt-based segmentation, which utilizes full capabilities of modern VLMs. Extensive experiments on the ScanRefer and Nr3D benchmarks demonstrate that our approach achieves state-of-the-art performance among zero-shot methods. Code is available at https://github.com/col14m/z3d .

AI.tldr: Proposes Z3D, a zero-shot 3D visual grounding framework using multi-view images, 3D instance segmentation proposals, and VLM-driven prompt-based reasoning, achieving state-of-the-art zero-shot results on ScanRefer and Nr3D.
AI.motivation: Zero-shot 3D visual grounding lacks supervision and explicit object priors; robust proposal generation and language-conditioned reasoning are needed to bridge 3D understanding with natural language.
AI.method: A universal grounding pipeline (Z3D) operates on multi-view images (optionally with camera poses and depth maps). It mitigates bottlenecks by (i) using a state-of-the-art zero-shot 3D instance segmentation method to generate high-quality 3D bounding-box proposals, and (ii) applying prompt-based segmentation to leverage full capabilities of modern vision-language models for reasoning over proposals and multi-view data.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on ScanRefer and Nr3D show Z3D achieves state-of-the-art performance among zero-shot methods; code is released at the provided GitHub URL.
AI.conclusion: Zero-shot 3D visual grounding becomes more competitive with strong proposals and VLM-based reasoning, validating a scalable, view-rich approach without geometric supervision or priors.

title: Symbol-Aware Reasoning with Masked Discrete Diffusion for Handwritten Mathematical Expression Recognition
summary: Handwritten Mathematical Expression Recognition (HMER) requires reasoning over diverse symbols and 2D structural layouts, yet autoregressive models struggle with exposure bias and syntactic inconsistency. We present a discrete diffusion framework that reformulates HMER as iterative symbolic refinement instead of sequential generation. Through multi-step remasking, the proposal progressively refines both symbols and structural relations, removing causal dependencies and improving structural consistency. A symbol-aware tokenization and Random-Masking Mutual Learning further enhance syntactic alignment and robustness to handwriting diversity. On the MathWriting benchmark, the proposal achieves 5.56\% CER and 60.42\% EM, outperforming strong Transformer and commercial baselines. Consistent gains on CROHME 2014--2023 demonstrate that discrete diffusion provides a new paradigm for structure-aware visual recognition beyond generative modeling.

AI.tldr: A discrete diffusion-based framework for handwritten math expression recognition that iteratively refines symbols and structure, achieving state-of-the-art results on MathWriting and CROHME.
AI.motivation: Autoregressive HMER models suffer exposure bias and syntactic inconsistency; there is a need for a structure-aware, non-sequential paradigm that better handles diverse 2D layouts.
AI.method: Introduce a discrete diffusion process with multi-step remasking to refine both symbol tokens and 2D structural relations. Includes symbol-aware tokenization and Random-Masking Mutual Learning to improve syntactic alignment and robustness to handwriting variation.
AI.result: On MathWriting, the method achieves CER 5.56% and EM 60.42%, outperforming strong Transformer and commercial baselines; shows consistent gains on CROHME 2014–2023.
AI.conclusion: Discrete diffusion provides a new paradigm for structure-aware visual recognition beyond traditional generative, autoregressive modeling in handwritten mathematics recognition.

title: Multi-Resolution Alignment for Voxel Sparsity in Camera-Based 3D Semantic Scene Completion
summary: Camera-based 3D semantic scene completion (SSC) offers a cost-effective solution for assessing the geometric occupancy and semantic labels of each voxel in the surrounding 3D scene with image inputs, providing a voxel-level scene perception foundation for the perception-prediction-planning autonomous driving systems. Although significant progress has been made in existing methods, their optimization rely solely on the supervision from voxel labels and face the challenge of voxel sparsity as a large portion of voxels in autonomous driving scenarios are empty, which limits both optimization efficiency and model performance. To address this issue, we propose a \textit{Multi-Resolution Alignment (MRA)} approach to mitigate voxel sparsity in camera-based 3D semantic scene completion, which exploits the scene and instance level alignment across multi-resolution 3D features as auxiliary supervision. Specifically, we first propose the Multi-resolution View Transformer module, which projects 2D image features into multi-resolution 3D features and aligns them at the scene level through fusing discriminative seed features. Furthermore, we design the Cubic Semantic Anisotropy module to identify the instance-level semantic significance of each voxel, accounting for the semantic differences of a specific voxel against its neighboring voxels within a cubic area. Finally, we devise a Critical Distribution Alignment module, which selects critical voxels as instance-level anchors with the guidance of cubic semantic anisotropy, and applies a circulated loss for auxiliary supervision on the critical feature distribution consistency across different resolutions. The code is available at https://github.com/PKU-ICST-MIPL/MRA_TIP.

AI.tldr: Proposes Multi-Resolution Alignment (MRA) to mitigate voxel sparsity in camera-based 3D semantic scene completion by auxiliary supervision across multi-resolution 3D features, via three components: Multi-resolution View Transformer, Cubic Semantic Anisotropy, and Critical Distribution Alignment; code released.
AI.motivation: Voxel sparsity in autonomous driving scenes degrades optimization efficiency and model performance when supervision is limited to voxel labels. Cross-resolution, instance-level guidance can provide richer supervision and regularization.
AI.method: 1) Multi-resolution View Transformer projects 2D image features into multi-resolution 3D features and performs scene-level alignment using discriminative seed features. 2) Cubic Semantic Anisotropy assesses instance-level semantic significance of each voxel by comparing it with neighbors within a cubic neighborhood. 3) Critical Distribution Alignment selects critical voxels as instance-level anchors guided by anisotropy and applies a circulated loss to enforce feature distribution consistency across resolutions.
AI.result: The abstract claims improved optimization efficiency and model performance through auxiliary supervision; specific quantitative results are not provided in the abstract, but code is publicly available.
AI.conclusion: MRA introduces a novel auxiliary supervision framework for camera-based SSC by leveraging multi-resolution alignment and instance-aware cues to reduce voxel sparsity and improve voxel occupancy and semantic labeling.

title: SLIM-Diff: Shared Latent Image-Mask Diffusion with Lp loss for Data-Scarce Epilepsy FLAIR MRI
summary: Focal cortical dysplasia (FCD) lesions in epilepsy FLAIR MRI are subtle and scarce, making joint image--mask generative modeling prone to instability and memorization. We propose SLIM-Diff, a compact joint diffusion model whose main contributions are (i) a single shared-bottleneck U-Net that enforces tight coupling between anatomy and lesion geometry from a 2-channel image+mask representation, and (ii) loss-geometry tuning via a tunable $L_p$ objective. As an internal baseline, we include the canonical DDPM-style objective ($ε$-prediction with $L_2$ loss) and isolate the effect of prediction parameterization and $L_p$ geometry under a matched setup. Experiments show that $x_0$-prediction is consistently the strongest choice for joint synthesis, and that fractional sub-quadratic penalties ($L_{1.5}$) improve image fidelity while $L_2$ better preserves lesion mask morphology. Our code and model weights are available in https://github.com/MarioPasc/slim-diff

AI.tldr: Compact joint diffusion model (SLIM-Diff) couples anatomy and lesion geometry for focal cortical dysplasia in epilepsy via a single shared-bottleneck U-Net using 2-channel image+mask input and tunable Lp loss; x0-prediction is strongest, L1.5 improves image fidelity, L2 preserves lesion morphology; code available.
AI.motivation: FCD lesions on FLAIR MRI are subtle and difficult to jointly model with separate image and mask generators, leading to instability and memorization. A tightly coupled, compact diffusion model aims to stabilize joint synthesis and preserve lesion geometry.
AI.method: Single shared-bottleneck U-Net processing a 2-channel image+mask representation within a diffusion framework; comparison of prediction parameterizations (x0 vs ε) and loss geometries with tunable Lp loss (e.g., L1.5 vs L2); baseline includes canonical ε-prediction with L2.
AI.result: x0-prediction consistently yields best joint synthesis; fractional sub-quadratic losses (L1.5) improve image fidelity, while L2 better preserves lesion mask morphology; code and model weights released at the provided GitHub link.
AI.conclusion: SLIM-Diff demonstrates stable, geometry-aware joint synthesis for subtle FCD lesions through shared geometry coupling and adjustable Lp loss, enabling a controllable trade-off between image fidelity and lesion morphology; resource availability supports reproducibility.

title: Unifying Watermarking via Dimension-Aware Mapping
summary: Deep watermarking methods often share similar encoder-decoder architectures, yet differ substantially in their functional behaviors. We propose DiM, a new multi-dimensional watermarking framework that formulates watermarking as a dimension-aware mapping problem, thereby unifying existing watermarking methods at the functional level. Under DiM, watermark information is modeled as payloads of different dimensionalities, including one-dimensional binary messages, two-dimensional spatial masks, and three-dimensional spatiotemporal structures. We find that the dimensional configuration of embedding and extraction largely determines the resulting watermarking behavior. Same-dimensional mappings preserve payload structure and support fine-grained control, while cross-dimensional mappings enable spatial or spatiotemporal localization. We instantiate DiM in the video domain, where spatiotemporal representations enable a broader set of dimension mappings. Experiments demonstrate that varying only the embedding and extraction dimensions, without architectural changes, leads to different watermarking capabilities, including spatiotemporal tamper localization, local embedding control, and recovery of temporal order under frame disruptions.

AI.tldr: DiM reframes watermarking as a dimension-aware mapping problem, unifying methods by treating payloads as dimensional constructs (1D, 2D, 3D). By varying embedding/extraction dimensions within a fixed architecture, it enables diverse behaviors including localization and temporal order recovery, demonstrated in video.
AI.motivation: Address fragmentation in watermarking methods by unifying them under a dimension-aware framework; show that payload dimensionality largely governs watermarking behavior and capabilities.
AI.method: Introduce multi-dimensional payloads (1D binary messages, 2D spatial masks, 3D spatiotemporal structures) and formalize dimension-aligned embedding/extraction mappings. Implement DiM in the video domain and demonstrate that changing embedding/extraction dimensions with no architectural changes yields different capabilities.
AI.result: Varying the embedding/extraction dimensions yields diverse watermarking functionalities (spatiotemporal tamper localization, local embedding control, temporal-order recovery) without altering network architecture.
AI.conclusion: Dimension-aware mapping (DiM) unifies watermarking behavior at the functional level; in video, spatiotemporal payloads unlock a broader set of dimension mappings, enabling flexible, payload-driven capabilities.

title: From Vicious to Virtuous Cycles: Synergistic Representation Learning for Unsupervised Video Object-Centric Learning
comment: ICLR 2026; Code is available at https://github.com/hynnsk/SRL
summary: Unsupervised object-centric learning models, particularly slot-based architectures, have shown great promise in decomposing complex scenes. However, their reliance on reconstruction-based training creates a fundamental conflict between the sharp, high-frequency attention maps of the encoder and the spatially consistent but blurry reconstruction maps of the decoder. We identify that this discrepancy gives rise to a vicious cycle: the noisy feature map from the encoder forces the decoder to average over possibilities and produce even blurrier outputs, while the gradient computed from blurry reconstruction maps lacks high-frequency details necessary to supervise encoder features. To break this cycle, we introduce Synergistic Representation Learning (SRL) that establishes a virtuous cycle where the encoder and decoder mutually refine one another. SRL leverages the encoder's sharpness to deblur the semantic boundary within the decoder output, while exploiting the decoder's spatial consistency to denoise the encoder's features. This mutual refinement process is stabilized by a warm-up phase with a slot regularization objective that initially allocates distinct entities per slot. By bridging the representational gap between the encoder and decoder, SRL achieves state-of-the-art results on video object-centric learning benchmarks. Codes are available at https://github.com/hynnsk/SRL.

AI.tldr: Proposes Synergistic Representation Learning (SRL) to break the encoder–decoder mismatch in unsupervised slot-based object-centric models by enabling mutual refinement, using a warm-up slot-regularization, achieving state-of-the-art on video OCC benchmarks; code available.
AI.motivation: Reconstruction-based training creates a conflict: the encoder produces sharp high-frequency attention maps while the decoder yields spatially consistent but blurry reconstructions. This yields a vicious cycle where encoder noise drives blur in reconstructions and blurry gradients fail to supervise encodings.
AI.method: Introduce SRL to create a virtuous cycle between encoder and decoder: (1) leverage the encoder’s sharpness to deblur semantic boundaries within the decoder output; (2) exploit the decoder’s spatial consistency to denoise the encoder’s features. Stabilize training with a warm-up phase using a slot-regularization objective that initially allocates distinct entities per slot, thereby bridging the encoder–decoder representational gap.
AI.result: Achieves state-of-the-art results on video object-centric learning benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: By bridging the encoder–decoder gap, SRL enables mutual refinement between components, improving unsupervised object-centric learning; code is publicly available.

title: UnHype: CLIP-Guided Hypernetworks for Dynamic LoRA Unlearning
summary: Recent advances in large-scale diffusion models have intensified concerns about their potential misuse, particularly in generating realistic yet harmful or socially disruptive content. This challenge has spurred growing interest in effective machine unlearning, the process of selectively removing specific knowledge or concepts from a model without compromising its overall generative capabilities. Among various approaches, Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) has emerged as an effective and efficient method for fine-tuning models toward targeted unlearning. However, LoRA-based methods often exhibit limited adaptability to concept semantics and struggle to balance removing closely related concepts with maintaining generalization across broader meanings. Moreover, these methods face scalability challenges when multiple concepts must be erased simultaneously. To address these limitations, we introduce UnHype, a framework that incorporates hypernetworks into single- and multi-concept LoRA training. The proposed architecture can be directly plugged into Stable Diffusion as well as modern flow-based text-to-image models, where it demonstrates stable training behavior and effective concept control. During inference, the hypernetwork dynamically generates adaptive LoRA weights based on the CLIP embedding, enabling more context-aware, scalable unlearning. We evaluate UnHype across several challenging tasks, including object erasure, celebrity erasure, and explicit content removal, demonstrating its effectiveness and versatility. Repository: https://github.com/gmum/UnHype.
