It uses a teacher-student training strategy that progressively masks surrounding agent commands during training to mimic inference where agent intentions are unavailable. The approach is architecture-agnostic and can be applied to other Transformer-based predictors.
AI.result: PTR yields 4.3%/3.5% improvements in marginal/joint mAP over the Motion Transformer baseline and achieves a 15.5% reduction in planning error at 5 seconds horizon compared with GameFormer.
AI.conclusion: PTR effectively bridges prediction and planning by integrating intent-agnostic prediction with feasibility and interaction constraints, delivering improved performance and enabling broader applicability across Transformer-based models.

title: HetroD: A High-Fidelity Drone Dataset and Benchmark for Autonomous Driving in Heterogeneous Traffic
comment: IEEE International Conference on Robotics and Automation (ICRA) 2026
summary: We present HetroD, a dataset and benchmark for developing autonomous driving systems in heterogeneous environments. HetroD targets the critical challenge of navi- gating real-world heterogeneous traffic dominated by vulner- able road users (VRUs), including pedestrians, cyclists, and motorcyclists that interact with vehicles. These mixed agent types exhibit complex behaviors such as hook turns, lane splitting, and informal right-of-way negotiation. Such behaviors pose significant challenges for autonomous vehicles but remain underrepresented in existing datasets focused on structured, lane-disciplined traffic. To bridge the gap, we collect a large- scale drone-based dataset to provide a holistic observation of traffic scenes with centimeter-accurate annotations, HD maps, and traffic signal states. We further develop a modular toolkit for extracting per-agent scenarios to support downstream task development. In total, the dataset comprises over 65.4k high- fidelity agent trajectories, 70% of which are from VRUs. HetroD supports modeling of VRU behaviors in dense, het- erogeneous traffic and provides standardized benchmarks for forecasting, planning, and simulation tasks. Evaluation results reveal that state-of-the-art prediction and planning models struggle with the challenges presented by our dataset: they fail to predict lateral VRU movements, cannot handle unstructured maneuvers, and exhibit limited performance in dense and multi-agent scenarios, highlighting the need for more robust approaches to heterogeneous traffic. See our project page for more examples: https://hetroddata.github.io/HetroD/

AI.tldr: HetroD is a large-scale drone-based dataset and benchmark for heterogeneous road traffic focusing on vulnerable road users (VRUs). It provides centimeter-accurate annotations, HD maps, and traffic signal states, plus a per-agent extraction toolkit to support forecasting, planning, and simulation tasks; baseline models show significant gaps in handling VRU-dense, unstructured, and multi-agent scenarios.
AI.motivation: To address the underrepresentation of VRUs and mixed-agent interactions in existing autonomous driving datasets, enabling robust development and evaluation of forecasting, planning, and simulation in heterogeneous traffic.
AI.method: Data collection via drone to capture holistic traffic scenes; centimeter-accurate annotations; HD maps; traffic signal states; a modular toolkit to extract per-agent scenarios; a dataset totaling 65.4k high-fidelity agent trajectories with 70% VRUs; standardized benchmarks for forecasting, planning, and simulation.
AI.result: Baseline state-of-the-art models struggle to predict lateral VRU movements, handle unstructured maneuvers, and perform in dense, multi-agent scenarios, highlighting gaps in current approaches and the need for more robust heterogeneous-traffic modeling.
AI.conclusion: HetroD provides a valuable resource for advancing perception, forecasting, and planning under heterogeneous traffic; it is expected to accelerate development of robust VRU-aware models and drive research into multi-agent interactions, domain adaptation, and more realistic simulations.

title: Scaling Continual Learning with Bi-Level Routing Mixture-of-Experts
summary: Continual learning, especially class-incremental learning (CIL), on the basis of a pre-trained model (PTM) has garnered substantial research interest in recent years. However, how to effectively learn both discriminative and comprehensive feature representations while maintaining stability and plasticity over very long task sequences remains an open problem. We propose CaRE, a scalable {C}ontinual Le{a}rner with efficient Bi-Level {R}outing Mixture-of-{E}xperts (BR-MoE). The core idea of BR-MoE is a bi-level routing mechanism: a router selection stage that dynamically activates relevant task-specific routers, followed by an expert routing phase that dynamically activates and aggregates experts, aiming to inject discriminative and comprehensive representations into every intermediate network layer. On the other hand, we introduce a challenging evaluation protocol for comprehensively assessing CIL methods across very long task sequences spanning hundreds of tasks. Extensive experiments show that CaRE demonstrates leading performance across a variety of datasets and task settings, including commonly used CIL datasets with classical CIL settings (e.g., 5-20 tasks). To the best of our knowledge, CaRE is the first continual learner that scales to very long task sequences (ranging from 100 to over 300 non-overlapping tasks), while outperforming all baselines by a large margin on such task sequences. Code will be publicly released at https://github.com/LMMMEng/CaRE.git.

AI.tldr: CaRE introduces a scalable continual learner for class-incremental learning using Bi-Level Routing Mixture-of-Experts (BR-MoE). It dynamically activates task-specific routers and experts to inject discriminative and comprehensive representations across all layers, enabling effective learning across hundreds of tasks. It also proposes a challenging evaluation protocol for very long task sequences and reports strong performance surpassing baselines.
AI.motivation: The paper addresses the instability–plasticity trade-off and representation learning quality in very long CIL sequences, aiming to maintain discriminative and comprehensive features while scaling beyond standard 5–20 task benchmarks.
AI.method: CaRE uses a bi-level routing mechanism: (1) a router selection stage that activates relevant task-specific routers, and (2) an expert routing stage that activates and aggregates experts across network layers. The approach leverages a Mixture-of-Experts framework with dynamic routing to inject representations at each intermediate layer, enabling long-horizon continual learning.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation shows CaRE achieves leading performance across multiple CIL datasets and task settings, including long sequences from 100 to >300 tasks, outperforming all baselines by a large margin. The authors also provide a code release plan.
AI.conclusion: CaRE is scalable to very long CIL task sequences and delivers strong accuracy by integrating discriminative and comprehensive representations through BR-MoE routing, representing a new benchmark for long-horizon continual learning.

title: Robust Representation Learning in Masked Autoencoders
summary: Masked Autoencoders (MAEs) achieve impressive performance in image classification tasks, yet the internal representations they learn remain less understood. This work started as an attempt to understand the strong downstream classification performance of MAE. In this process we discover that representations learned with the pretraining and fine-tuning, are quite robust - demonstrating a good classification performance in the presence of degradations, such as blur and occlusions. Through layer-wise analysis of token embeddings, we show that pretrained MAE progressively constructs its latent space in a class-aware manner across network depth: embeddings from different classes lie in subspaces that become increasingly separable. We further observe that MAE exhibits early and persistent global attention across encoder layers, in contrast to standard Vision Transformers (ViTs). To quantify feature robustness, we introduce two sensitivity indicators: directional alignment between clean and perturbed embeddings, and head-wise retention of active features under degradations. These studies help establish the robust classification performance of MAEs.

AI.tldr: MAEs learn robust, class-aware representations with early global attention, and the paper analyzes this behavior and introduces metrics to quantify robustness under degradations.
AI.motivation: Understand why masked autoencoder pretraining yields strong downstream classification performance and robustness to degradations such as blur and occlusion.
AI.method: Layer-wise analysis of token embeddings to show class-aware latent-space construction across depth; comparison with ViTs to reveal differences in attention; introduction of two robustness metrics: directional alignment between clean and perturbed embeddings and head-wise retention of active features under degradations.
AI.result: Pretraining+finetuning MAEs produce latent spaces where class embeddings lie in progressively more separable subspaces with depth; MAEs exhibit early, persistent global attention across encoder layers; robustness metrics quantify how embeddings align under perturbations and how features are retained by attention heads.
AI.conclusion: The robust classification performance of MAEs arises from class-aware latent-space organization and consistent global attention, and the proposed metrics provide a practical means to quantify feature robustness under degradations.

title: AffordanceGrasp-R1:Leveraging Reasoning-Based Affordance Segmentation with Reinforcement Learning for Robotic Grasping
summary: We introduce AffordanceGrasp-R1, a reasoning-driven affordance segmentation framework for robotic grasping that combines a chain-of-thought (CoT) cold-start strategy with reinforcement learning to enhance deduction and spatial grounding. In addition, we redesign the grasping pipeline to be more context-aware by generating grasp candidates from the global scene point cloud and subsequently filtering them using instruction-conditioned affordance masks. Extensive experiments demonstrate that AffordanceGrasp-R1 consistently outperforms state-of-the-art (SOTA) methods on benchmark datasets, and real-world robotic grasping evaluations further validate its robustness and generalization under complex language-conditioned manipulation scenarios.

AI.tldr: A CoT-guided affordance segmentation framework, AffordanceGrasp-R1, integrates reasoning with reinforcement learning and global-context grasp proposals to improve language-conditioned robotic grasping, achieving SOTA performance on benchmarks and real-world tests.
AI.motivation: Improve robotic grasping by combining chain-of-thought reasoning with affordance grounding, enabling more accurate deduction and spatial grounding in cluttered, language-conditioned scenarios.
AI.method: A reasoning-driven workflow that uses a chain-of-thought cold-start strategy paired with reinforcement learning. Grasp candidates are generated from the global scene point cloud and filtered by instruction-conditioned affordance masks, reformulating the grasping pipeline to be context-aware.
AI.result: Empirically outperforms state-of-the-art methods on benchmark datasets and demonstrates robust generalization in real-world robotic grasping under complex language-conditioned manipulation.
AI.conclusion: Integrating CoT reasoning with affordance-based segmentation and global-context grasp proposal enhances practical robotic grasping, especially for language-conditioned tasks, with strong generalization and robustness.

title: MVP-LAM: Learning Action-Centric Latent Action via Cross-Viewpoint Reconstruction
summary: Learning \emph{latent actions} from diverse human videos enables scaling robot learning beyond embodiment-specific robot datasets, and these latent actions have recently been used as pseudo-action labels for vision-language-action (VLA) model pretraining. To make VLA pretraining effective, latent actions should contain information about the underlying agent's actions despite the absence of ground-truth labels. We propose \textbf{M}ulti-\textbf{V}iew\textbf{P}oint \textbf{L}atent \textbf{A}ction \textbf{M}odel (\textbf{MVP-LAM}), which learns discrete latent actions that are highly informative about ground-truth actions from time-synchronized multi-view videos. MVP-LAM trains latent actions with a \emph{cross-viewpoint reconstruction} objective, so that a latent action inferred from one view must explain the future in another view, reducing reliance on viewpoint-specific cues. On Bridge V2, MVP-LAM produces more action-centric latent actions, achieving higher mutual information with ground-truth actions and improved action prediction, including under out-of-distribution evaluation. Finally, pretraining VLAs with MVP-LAM latent actions improves downstream manipulation performance on the SIMPLER and LIBERO-Long benchmarks.

AI.tldr: MVP-LAM learns discrete latent actions from synchronized multi-view human videos using a cross-viewpoint reconstruction objective, producing action-centric latent codes that align with ground-truth actions and improve VLA model pretraining for manipulation tasks.
AI.motivation: To scale robot learning beyond embodiment-specific datasets by extracting informative latent actions from diverse human videos, while avoiding reliance on viewpoint-specific cues and enabling better downstream manipulation performance.
AI.method: Propose Multi-View Point Latent Action Model (MVP-LAM) that learns discrete latent actions. It uses a cross-viewpoint reconstruction objective: a latent action from one view must explain future outcomes from another view, trained on time-synchronized multi-view videos.
AI.result: On Bridge V2, latent actions become more action-centric and show higher mutual information with ground-truth actions, improving action prediction, including in out-of-distribution scenarios. Pretraining vision-language-action models with MVP-LAM latent actions improves downstream manipulation performance on SIMPLER and LIBERO-Long benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: MVP-LAM yields informative, transfer-ready latent actions that enhance VLA pretraining and downstream manipulation, by emphasizing cross-view consistency and reducing view-specific cues.

title: BridgeV2W: Bridging Video Generation Models to Embodied World Models via Embodiment Masks
summary: Embodied world models have emerged as a promising paradigm in robotics, most of which leverage large-scale Internet videos or pretrained video generation models to enrich visual and motion priors. However, they still face key challenges: a misalignment between coordinate-space actions and pixel-space videos, sensitivity to camera viewpoint, and non-unified architectures across embodiments. To this end, we present BridgeV2W, which converts coordinate-space actions into pixel-aligned embodiment masks rendered from the URDF and camera parameters. These masks are then injected into a pretrained video generation model via a ControlNet-style pathway, which aligns the action control signals with predicted videos, adds view-specific conditioning to accommodate camera viewpoints, and yields a unified world model architecture across embodiments. To mitigate overfitting to static backgrounds, BridgeV2W further introduces a flow-based motion loss that focuses on learning dynamic and task-relevant regions. Experiments on single-arm (DROID) and dual-arm (AgiBot-G1) datasets, covering diverse and challenging conditions with unseen viewpoints and scenes, show that BridgeV2W improves video generation quality compared to prior state-of-the-art methods. We further demonstrate the potential of BridgeV2W on downstream real-world tasks, including policy evaluation and goal-conditioned planning. More results can be found on our project website at https://BridgeV2W.github.io .

AI.tldr: BridgeV2W unifies embodied action-to-video generation by converting coordinate-space actions into URDF-derived, pixel-aligned masks and feeding them into a pretrained video model via a ControlNet-style pathway, with a motion-loss to emphasize dynamics; it improves video quality and enables downstream planning across embodiments.
AI.motivation: Embodied world models suffer from misalignment between coordinate-space actions and pixel-space videos, sensitivity to camera viewpoint, and a lack of a unified architecture across different embodiments; a unified, view-aware pipeline leveraging large-scale video priors is needed.
AI.method: BridgeV2W converts coordinate-space actions into pixel-aligned embodiment masks rendered from URDF and camera parameters, injects these masks into a pretrained video-generation model through a ControlNet-style pathway to align action control with video predictions and add view-specific conditioning, and employs a flow-based motion loss to focus on dynamic, task-relevant regions; experiments on single-arm (DROID) and dual-arm (AgiBot-G1) datasets assess unseen viewpoints and scenes.
AI.result: BridgeV2W yields improved video generation quality over prior state-of-the-art methods and demonstrates potential for downstream tasks such as policy evaluation and goal-conditioned planning.
AI.conclusion: BridgeV2W provides a unified world-model across embodiments by aligning coordinate actions with pixel-space predictions using embodiment masks and view-conditioned control, while promoting dynamics-focused learning; this enables cross-embodiment robotics tasks and supports downstream decision-making.

title: AutoFigure: Generating and Refining Publication-Ready Scientific Illustrations
summary: High-quality scientific illustrations are crucial for effectively communicating complex scientific and technical concepts, yet their manual creation remains a well-recognized bottleneck in both academia and industry. We present FigureBench, the first large-scale benchmark for generating scientific illustrations from long-form scientific texts. It contains 3,300 high-quality scientific text-figure pairs, covering diverse text-to-illustration tasks from scientific papers, surveys, blogs, and textbooks. Moreover, we propose AutoFigure, the first agentic framework that automatically generates high-quality scientific illustrations based on long-form scientific text. Specifically, before rendering the final result, AutoFigure engages in extensive thinking, recombination, and validation to produce a layout that is both structurally sound and aesthetically refined, outputting a scientific illustration that achieves both structural completeness and aesthetic appeal. Leveraging the high-quality data from FigureBench, we conduct extensive experiments to test the performance of AutoFigure against various baseline methods. The results demonstrate that AutoFigure consistently surpasses all baseline methods, producing publication-ready scientific illustrations. The code, dataset and huggingface space are released in https://github.com/ResearAI/AutoFigure.

AI.tldr: Introduces FigureBench, a large-scale benchmark of 3,300 text-figure pairs for long-form scientific text-to-illustration tasks, and AutoFigure, an agentic framework that plans, recombines, and validates to render high-quality, publication-ready scientific illustrations; dataset and code released.
AI.motivation: Manual creation of high-quality scientific figures is a bottleneck in science and engineering; a scalable benchmark and automated generation system are needed to improve efficiency, consistency, and quality across domains.
AI.method: FigureBench aggregates 3,300 high-quality text-figure pairs from papers, surveys, blogs, and textbooks to cover diverse text-to-illustration tasks. AutoFigure employs an agentic pipeline that engages in extensive thinking, recombination, and validation before rendering a final layout, ensuring structural completeness and aesthetic refinement.
AI.result: AutoFigure consistently surpasses all baselines across tasks, producing publication-ready scientific illustrations; demonstrates strong generalization and quality on long-form textual inputs.
AI.conclusion: The FigureBench dataset and AutoFigure framework advance automated scientific illustration, providing a valuable resource for research and practical deployment; code, dataset, and HuggingFace space are released.
